34.Option: The proportion of schizonts in the control well at the time of harvest
Evidence: The proportion of parasite life cycle stages (i.e., rings, trophozoites and schizonts, as defined by Russell et al [12], [15]) in each isolate was assessed at 0 hours, 24 hours, and then at non-uniform times until 40% of the parasites in the control well reached mature schizonts. At this point, the assay was terminated, and wells under serial drug concentrations were harvested.

If a drug only acts on ring stage parasites, but the sample contains a majority of trophozoites and schizonts, the parasite is likely to be erroneously categorised as resistant simply because there were no susceptible life cycle stages present in the assay.

We recommend that P. vivax isolates should contain a minimum of 66% ring stage life cycle stages, and that assay duration should exceed 34 hours to ensure this stage-specific effect does not artificially inflate the reported EC50.
34.Rationale: While the assay is terminated when control wells reach ~40% schizonts (a procedural endpoint), the passage identifies misclassification (erroneous categorisation as resistant) as arising primarily when susceptible ring stages are not present. The proportion of schizonts at harvest is a termination criterion, not the critical determinant of misclassification when assay duration is below the threshold; the key problem is absence of ring-stage parasites (stage composition at assay start and short duration), not the schizont proportion at harvest.

34.Option: The concentration of chloroquine used in the assay
Evidence: The central tenant of the SMT is that drug activity on susceptible parasites will completely stop or slow the growth in a dose-dependent manner, with reduced susceptibility being manifest by an ability of parasites to mature to schizont stages in the presence of higher concentrations of drug.

In previous work, it has been demonstrated that the trophozoite stages of P. vivax are almost completely resistant to CQ, and continue to mature no matter how high the concentration of drug [15], [16].
Samples with less than 65% ring stage parasites at time 0 had higher and more variable EC50 values than those isolates with a greater proportion of ring stage parasites.
34.Rationale: Although drug concentration determines dose-response (EC50), the passage makes clear that trophozoites are essentially insensitive to chloroquine and will mature regardless of CQ concentration. Therefore, when assay duration is below the threshold and ring stages are underexposed, increasing or varying CQ concentration does not prevent misclassification; the critical issue is stage composition (lack of rings), not the absolute CQ concentration used.

34.Option: The proportion of ring-stage parasites present at the start of the assay
Evidence: The diversity in parasite life cycle stages in P. vivax infections creates a significant confounding factor, particularly since there is an apparent marked variation of drug susceptibility in different erythrocytic life cycle stages.

In previous work, it has been demonstrated that the trophozoite stages of P. vivax are almost completely resistant to CQ, and continue to mature no matter how high the concentration of drug [15], [16]. It follows that, if a drug only acts on ring stage parasites, but the sample contains a majority of trophozoites and schizonts, the parasite is likely to be erroneously categorised as resistant simply because there were no susceptible life cycle stages present in the assay.

The proportion of rings at the start of the assay was also significantly negatively associated with the ln(EC50) values for both P. vivax (r2 = 0.245, p<0.001) and P. falciparum isolates (r2 = 0.206, p<0.001; Figure 2).
Samples with less than 65% ring stage parasites at time 0 had higher and more variable EC50 values than those isolates with a greater proportion of ring stage parasites.

We recommend that P. vivax isolates should contain a minimum of 66% ring stage life cycle stages, and that assay duration should exceed 34 hours to ensure this stage-specific effect does not artificially inflate the reported EC50.
34.Rationale: The passage repeatedly identifies the initial proportion of ring-stage parasites as the principal determinant of whether ring-targeting drugs (like chloroquine against P. vivax) will appear effective. When assay duration is below the threshold, insufficient exposure of ring stages (or a low starting proportion of rings) leads to artificially elevated EC50 and erroneous resistance classification; hence the starting ring proportion is the most critical factor.

34.Option: The total number of parasites in the culture well at the start of the assay
Evidence: The removal of mature trophozoite parasites inevitably results in a reduction in parasite count, which is itself another important parameter for reliable quantification of parasite growth.

If a particular drug targets ring stage parasites, but is tested in an isolate predominantly containing mature stage parasites, the drug will appear to be ineffective, resulting in an overestimate of resistance.

We recommend that P. vivax isolates should contain a minimum of 66% ring stage life cycle stages, and that assay duration should exceed 34 hours to ensure this stage-specific effect does not artificially inflate the reported EC50.
34.Rationale: Although parasite count affects the reliability of growth quantification and is noted as an important parameter, the passage makes clear that the dominant cause of misclassification when assay duration is short is stage composition (lack of ring stages), not simply the absolute parasite number. Thus total parasite count is a secondary concern compared with the proportion of rings at assay start.

34.Option: The delay between venepuncture and the start of the assay
Evidence: The time between venepuncture and start of the assay (the ‘delay’) was significantly correlated with the duration of the assay for isolates of P. falciparum (correlation co-efficient (r) = −0.211, p<0.001), but not P. vivax (r = −0.065, p = 0.226).

For both P. vivax and P. falciparum isolates, there were significant negative relationships between the duration of the assay and estimated EC50 values (i.e., short duration assays were associated with reduced susceptibility to drugs).
Samples with less than 65% ring stage parasites at time 0 had higher and more variable EC50 values than those isolates with a greater proportion of ring stage parasites.
34.Rationale: While delay can influence assay timing and is correlated with assay duration for P. falciparum, the passage explicitly states delay was not significantly correlated with assay duration for P. vivax. The study identifies the starting proportion of ring-stage parasites (and assay duration) as the critical determinants of misclassification in P. vivax; thus delay between venepuncture and assay start is not the most critical factor for the scenario described.

35.Option: The absence of simple, high-throughput molecular diagnostics for metabolic resistance markers limits the scalability of resistance monitoring across diverse geographic regions
Evidence: However, such fine monitoring is not yet achievable for metabolic resistance alleles because most validated metabolic resistance markers consists of RNA markers.

Indeed, RNA markers are submitted to difficulties and flawless inherent to RNA analysis (sample storage and degradation, high variability, limited biological material, high costs …) impairing the production of individual genotyping data at a high throughput.

the detection of target-site mutations can be easily achieved through simple PCR assays on individual mosquitoes and allows monitoring the spatio-temporal dynamics of resistant alleles within and among populations.
Recently, Faucon et al. [28] identified particular CNVs as good markers of the over expression of detoxification genes in pyrethroid-resistant Ae. aegypti populations.
35.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies the lack of robust, DNA-based molecular markers for metabolic resistance and explains that most validated metabolic markers are RNA-based, which suffer from storage, degradation, variability and cost issues that impair high-throughput individual genotyping. By contrast, target-site (kdr) mutations can be tracked with simple PCR. Therefore the absence of simple, high-throughput diagnostics for metabolic resistance is described as the critical constraint on scalable monitoring across regions.

35.Option: The high cost and technical complexity of RNA-seq analysis prevent widespread adoption of resistance monitoring in low-resource settings
Evidence: Indeed, RNA markers are submitted to difficulties and flawless inherent to RNA analysis (sample storage and degradation, high variability, limited biological material, high costs …) impairing the production of individual genotyping data at a high throughput.

RNA-seq presents many advantages over microarrays in term of sensitivity and specificity of expression data and allows investigating concomitantly gene expression and polymorphism variations associated with a given phenotype across the whole transcriptome [23, 24].
RNA-seq analysis generated more than 928 million reads with an average of 116 million reads per population across the three biological replicates (Table 2).
35.Rationale: The passage does note 'high costs' and technical challenges of RNA/RNA-seq approaches which limit production of high-throughput individual genotyping data. However, it frames these as one part of the broader problem (the fundamental issue being the reliance on RNA markers rather than stable DNA markers). The question asks for the factor that most critically influences feasibility across regions; the text emphasizes the lack of simple DNA diagnostics as primary. Thus while cost/complexity of RNA-seq is a real constraint, the passage indicates it is not presented as the single most critical factor compared with the absence of simple, high-throughput molecular diagnostics for metabolic resistance.

35.Option: The presence of multiple arbovirus co-circulations in a region complicates the interpretation of resistance monitoring data and reduces program feasibility
Evidence: dengue, chikungunya and zika viruses transmitted by the mosquito Aedes aegypti represent a burden in more than 100 countries with over 2.5 billion people at risk [1–3].
However, such fine monitoring is not yet achievable for metabolic resistance alleles because most validated metabolic resistance markers consists of RNA markers.

Indeed, RNA markers are submitted to difficulties and flawless inherent to RNA analysis (sample storage and degradation, high variability, limited biological material, high costs …) impairing the production of individual genotyping data at a high throughput.
35.Rationale: Although the passage documents co-circulation of multiple arboviruses, it does not link that phenomenon to feasibility problems for insecticide resistance monitoring. Instead, the passage focuses on technical and molecular-marker limitations (RNA-marker problems, lack of DNA markers) as the critical impediment to scalable monitoring. Therefore the presence of multiple arbovirus co-circulations is not identified in the text as the principal factor undermining monitoring feasibility.

Evidence: Previous genotyping of kdr mutations revealed that resistant populations from South-America carried the V1016I and F1534C mutations at high frequencies (V1016I from 77 to 96% and F1534C from 75 to 100%) while the S989P mutation was not detected. the detection of target-site mutations can be easily achieved through simple PCR assays on individual mosquitoes and allows monitoring the spatio-temporal dynamics of resistant alleles within and among populations.
35.Rationale: The passage documents geographic variability in kdr frequencies, but it emphasizes that target-site (kdr) mutations are straightforward to detect with simple PCR assays and thus amenable to monitoring across regions. The text presents lack of suitable metabolic resistance markers (RNA vs DNA) as the major obstacle, not kdr variability. Therefore geographic variability in kdr frequency is not described as the critical factor hindering uniform implementation.

36.Option: The upregulation of genes such as CCDC168 and CLIP1 in multiple comparisons suggests a consistent molecular response to infection that may indicate a universal risk profile for severe disease

36.Option: The identification of 56 differentially expressed genes between high-intensity infected and uninfected children indicates that gene expression profiles are distinct and could reflect underlying disease risk
Evidence: We identified 56 significant DEGs (Table 2) listed in Table 4 among the children with high S. mansoni infection compared to the uninfected of which 43 (77%) were upregulated and 13 (23%) were downregulated (Fig 3B).

Thus this finding is relevant but not the strongest support for the stated hypothesis.

36.Option: The observation that six of the fibrosis-associated DEGs were previously found to be differentially expressed in liver fibrosis suggests that peripheral blood gene expression reflects tissue-level pathology and may predict disease progression

Because schistosomiasis morbidity (severe disease) is driven by hepatic fibrosis around eggs, the fact that blood expression includes genes seen in liver fibrosis provides strong support that peripheral signatures could identify individuals at higher risk of progressing to severe disease even when infection intensity is similar.

36.Rationale: The finding that gene expression differs between high and low infection intensity demonstrates that transcription varies with parasite burden, but it does not directly show that peripheral blood molecular signatures can distinguish individuals at higher risk of severe disease despite similar infection intensity. The comparison is between different infection levels rather than identifying risk heterogeneity among individuals with similar parasitaemia. Therefore this result is relevant to infection-level differences but is not the strongest direct support for the hypothesis posed.

37.Rationale: Although the passage explicitly states that the Paraguayan population was found 'in crevices and pipelines inside human dwellings' and that synanthropy increases public health risk, it also describes T. trivittatus in Argentina as a major cause of severe envenomations. Therefore presence in human dwellings is true for the Paraguayan population but does not uniquely distinguish a greater zoonotic threat compared with the Argentinean population; synanthropy is presented as a shared trait that contributes to public health risk rather than a factor that by itself makes the Paraguayan population distinctly more hazardous.

37.Option: The predicted expansion of the species' range due to climate change increases the likelihood of human exposure and thus elevates the zoonotic risk of the Paraguayan population
Evidence: The species’ range extends from central-northern and eastern Argentina to eastern Paraguay and southeast Brazil, and has been predicted to increase in response to ongoing global climate change [6].
37.Rationale: Range expansion under climate change is noted in the passage, but that statement applies to the species as a whole rather than specifically indicating that the Paraguayan population is a distinct zoonotic risk compared to the Argentinean population. The passage does not present evidence that expansion uniquely changes the Paraguayan population's risk profile relative to the Argentinean population, so this factor alone is not the strongest suggestion of a distinct zoonotic risk.

37.Option: The high venom lethality of the Paraguayan population, as measured by LD50, indicates a greater capacity to cause severe disease and thus a higher zoonotic risk compared to the Argentinean population
Evidence: Medium lethal dose in NIH Swiss mice intraperitoneally injected with T. trivittatus venom (a pool from female and male specimens) was estimated as 1.19 mg/kg (95% CI: 0.89–1.71).

Particularly, venom lethal potency for the Paraguayan population is comparable to those reported from the Argentinean provinces of Entre Ríos, Santa Fé, Córdoba, La Rioja, and Catamarca, where T. trivittatus is prevalent and associated with stings, mainly in children, with incidence rates ranging from 5.62 to 32.51 cases per 100.000 inhabitants [47] (Fig 8).
37.Rationale: The Paraguayan LD50 is reported, but the passage explicitly states that lethal potency for the Paraguayan population 'is comparable to those reported' from multiple Argentinean provinces. Thus high lethality per se does not indicate a greater zoonotic risk compared with the Argentinean population, since lethality values are similar rather than higher.

37.Option: The divergence in venom composition and antigenicity between the Paraguayan and Argentinean populations suggests that existing antivenoms may be less effective, increasing the zoonotic risk

Whereas venom from Argentina produced 18.1 ± 3.3% free antibodies at the maximal venom dose tested (10 μg/mL), incubation with venom from Paraguay produced 77.2 ± 4.2% free antibodies at the same concentration.

Thus antigenic and compositional divergence that undermines antivenom effectiveness is a strong factor suggesting a distinct zoonotic risk.

Evidence: Specimens representing the local Paraguayan population of T. trivitattus were mainly found in crevices and pipelines inside human dwellings.

37.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence that together make the strongest case: the Paraguayan population is synanthropic (found in human dwellings), has venom lethal potency within a medically significant range, and shows substantial genetic divergence (8.14% COI divergence) together with toxinological differences. The authors explicitly state these differences 'indicating that they probably comprise unique cryptic species.' The combination of urban association, medically significant venom, and deep genetic/toxinological divergence supports the conclusion that the Paraguayan population may represent a cryptic species with a distinct and potentially greater zoonotic risk.

38.Option: The reliance on GNI per capita to estimate productivity losses introduces a bias that distorts the true economic burden of the control programme
Evidence: The loss of work days was estimated from the gross national income (GNI) per capita in Morocco that shows drastic differences between women and men [29].

38.Rationale: Using GNI per capita to convert monetary losses into lost productivity is a limitation and may bias economic burden estimates (and raises ethical concerns noted in the text). However, the question asks which factor most critically undermines the validity of reported incidence trends and programme impact assessment. The passage identifies surveillance coverage (capture of cases) as the primary problem for incidence trends; the GNI-based economic conversion affects monetary and zDALY estimates but does not by itself invalidate incidence trend data. Therefore this option is not the most critical undermining factor.

DALY estimation. In accordance with other studies, YLL and YLD were considered as resulting only from surgical cases, and asymptomatic cases were considered as not having any significant impact on these two dimensions [19].
38.Rationale: The absence of post-surgery course information is a limitation for assessing long-term outcomes and for costing post-surgery complications, and the authors explicitly used literature values to fill this gap. However, the question focuses on what most critically undermines validity of reported incidence trends and programme impact assessment. The surveillance system's narrow case capture (only surgical cases) is repeatedly emphasized as causing systematic underestimation of incidence and is far more fundamental to trend validity than missing postoperative complication data. Thus this option is not the single most critical factor.

38.Option: The surveillance system captures only surgical cases and animals slaughtered in regulated facilities, leading to systematic underestimation of true disease burden and invalidating trend analysis
Evidence: In Morocco, CE in humans is a notifiable disease. However, the surveillance system, supervised by the Ministry of Health [2], is mainly based on recording the patients undergoing surgery for hydatid cysts in public hospitals [3].

ministerial data are underestimating the true prevalence of infection, because many infected individuals do not present symptoms, or because part of people with symptomatic disease will not be treated by surgery, and therefore will not be recorded in the Ministry of Health registry [5].
Therefore, data from slaughterhouses would underestimate the true CE incidence in farm animals throughout Morocco, because young animals are more frequently slaughtered [11].

Moreover, animals slaughtered at home, a common practice in Morocco, and animals slaughtered during the feast of sacrifice (i.e., 5 million small ruminants, one per household) are consumed without any sanitary control [12].
38.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that human surveillance is mainly surgical-case based and that ministerial data underestimates true prevalence because many infected people are asymptomatic or are not treated surgically. Similarly, animal surveillance relies on slaughterhouse inspection but misses home and feast-time slaughter and young animals, producing systematic underascertainment. This systematic underdetection directly undermines the validity of reported incidence trends and any assessment of programme impact because changes in surveillance capture over time (or persistent undercapture) can mask true incidence and mislead evaluation. Therefore this factor is the most critical undermining issue.

38.Option: The use of a triangular distribution to model prevalence in asymptomatic individuals introduces uncertainty that prevents reliable trend detection in the control programme's impact
Evidence: Due to the uncertainty associated with this estimate, a triangular distribution was used, with a minimum value of 0%, a maximum value of 2.2%, and a probable value of 2.05% (the mean of the extrapolated values).

To account for the uncertainty of the proposed estimates, stochastic methods were used for the overall calculations. 38.Rationale: Modeling choices (such as triangular distributions) introduce uncertainty in estimates and the authors explicitly used Monte Carlo simulation to quantify that uncertainty. While this affects precision and confidence in quantitative estimates (e.g., economic losses, prevalence among asymptomatics), it does not represent the most critical factor undermining validity of incidence trends and programme impact assessment. The core problem is incomplete case ascertainment by surveillance (surgical-only and slaughterhouse-only data), which biases trends more fundamentally than distributional choices in modeling asymptomatic prevalence.

38.Option: The absence of data on CE prevalence in camels during the feast of sacrifice undermines the accuracy of the national control programme's impact assessment
Evidence: Moreover, animals slaughtered at home, a common practice in Morocco, and animals slaughtered during the feast of sacrifice (i.e., 5 million small ruminants, one per household) are consumed without any sanitary control [12].
For camelids, data were retrieved from a study on CE prevalence in camels in Mauritania [40], a country that borders the Moroccan region where camel farming is concentrated.
Losses associated with decreased fertility were estimated as the number of lost new-borns, which are sold at birth. Decreased fertility was not estimated for camels due to the lack of data.
38.Rationale: Lack of data for camels (and for animals slaughtered outside regulated facilities) is a limitation and contributes to underestimation of animal burden. However, the more pervasive and critical validity threat described in the passage is that both human and animal surveillance systems systematically miss cases (surgical-only human reporting and slaughterhouse-only animal data). The absence of camel-specific data during feast slaughter is a narrower problem relative to the overarching surveillance capture issue that invalidates incidence trend interpretation and programme impact assessment.

strain 294H was close to a strain isolated from a person with melioidosis living in Illinois, US, who previously had travelled to Mexico.
since the US seems not endemic of melioidosis and many strains were isolated from people travelling to Mexico [41], our country might be endemic for B. pseudomallei.

39.Option: Since B. pseudomallei was isolated from a pediatric pneumonia patient in Mexico, and pneumonia is associated with environmental pollutants like dust and soot, the infection likely originated from urban air pollution in Mexico City.

The passage shows toxicity only at the highest dose (800 mg/kg) while the survival benefit occurred at lower non-toxic doses (40–80 mg/kg). The authors also document immune-related histologic changes, arguing against the improved survival being simply due to non-specific toxicity. Thus the option is not supported.

49.Option: The lack of reduction in fungal burden despite improved survival suggests that CIN-102 has a direct fungicidal effect that is not detectable by standard histological methods, indicating a need for more sensitive detection techniques.
Evidence: In vitro M. mycetomatis is inhibited by CIN-102 ... As shown in Fig 1, MIC values for itraconazole ranged from <0.008 μg/mL to 1 μg/mL and those for CIN-102 ranged from <32 μg/mL to 512 μg/mL ... The MIC for genome isolate MM55 was 256 μg/ml, this corresponds to 222 μg/mL cinnamaldehyde.
For CIN-102, the observed minimal fungicidal concentrations were similar to the respective MIC values.

In fact, the effective dosages of 40 mg/kg and 80 mg/kg CIN-102 were below the MIC of 256 μg/ml (corresponds to 256 mg/kg) obtained for genome isolate MM55 from our in vitro data. Furthermore, despite the enhanced survival, no reduction in the total number of grains or the size of the grains was noted.
Although treatment with CIN-102 did not result in a difference in the size and number of grains, differences were observed regarding the physiology surrounding the fungal grain.
49.Rationale: The proposed explanation requires that CIN-102 be exerting undetected direct fungicidal activity in vivo despite no histologic reduction in grains. However, the effective in vivo doses that improved survival were below the in vitro MIC/MFC, making a true direct fungicidal effect at those doses unlikely. Additionally, the study documents host physiological changes around grains (encapsulation, hemocyte morphology) consistent with immune effects rather than an occult fungicidal effect missed by histology. Therefore invoking an undetected direct fungicidal effect is not the most supported interpretation.

50.Option: The presence of granulomas and high fungal burden in IFA-treated mice, coupled with elevated levels of IL-4 and IL-10, supports the conclusion that a Th2 immune response promotes disease progression and tissue damage
Evidence: the lungs from IFA-treated mice presented multiple sites of focal and confluent epithelioid granulomas with lymphomonocytic halos circumscribing a high number of viable and nonviable yeast cells (Figure 2A, C and E).
treatment with CFA induced a 2.4 log reduction in the fungal burden when compared to the IFA-treated mice, corresponding to 99% less CFU (Figure 1A).
the IFA-treated group produced low levels of IFN-γ, IL-4, IL-12, TNF-α, IL-10 and TGF-β (Figure 1B–G), suggesting a suppression of the immune response in these animals.
Susceptibility has been linked to the preferential production of the Th2-type cytokines, i.e., interleukin (IL)-4, IL-5, and IL-10.
50.Rationale: While granulomas and high fungal burden in IFA-treated mice are documented and Th2 cytokines are generally associated with susceptibility, the passage does not report elevated IL-4 and IL-10 in the IFA-treated group; it explicitly states the IFA-treated group produced low levels of IFN-γ, IL-4, IL-12, TNF-α, IL-10 and TGF-β. Therefore the specific claim that elevated IL-4 and IL-10 were observed in IFA-treated mice is contradicted by the reported data, making the option incorrect as stated.

50.Option: The presence of granulomas in IFA-treated mice and their absence in CFA-treated mice indicates that granuloma formation is inherently pathological and should be avoided in all cases of PCM
Evidence: the lungs from IFA-treated mice presented multiple sites of focal and confluent epithelioid granulomas with lymphomonocytic halos circumscribing a high number of viable and nonviable yeast cells (Figure 2A, C and E).

in the P. brasiliensis-infected mice treated with CFA, no granulomas or yeast cells were seen in the pulmonary sections examined and a well-preserved alveolar architecture was observed on day 30 after treatment (Figure 2B, D and F).

The progression of P. brasiliensis infection was determined by lung histopathology and analysis of colony-forming unity (CFU), parameters that are considered trustworthy to discriminate susceptible and resistant mice to systemic fungal infection [9],[12],[19],[24].

The present study demonstrated that a single-dose administration of the Th1-inducing adjuvant (CFA) in P. brasiliensis-infected mice was sufficient to break the anergy observed in these animals restoring their ability to mount an effective immune response to the fungus.
50.Rationale: The passage shows granulomas are associated with the pathological, high-burden state in IFA-treated mice and are absent in protected CFA-treated mice, but it does not assert that granulomas are inherently pathological in all contexts. Granulomas are used here as a marker of disease severity and to discriminate susceptible versus resistant mice. The categorical statement that granuloma formation should be avoided in all PCM cases overgeneralizes beyond the data and ignores the complex role granulomas can play in host defense; therefore this option is incorrect.

50.Option: The induction of a Th1-biased immune response correlates with complete fungal clearance, absence of granulomatous pathology, and reduced fungal burden, indicating that immune polarization dictates disease outcome
Evidence: Since CFA supports a Th1 status, while incomplete Freund's adjuvant (IFA) promotes a Th2 status [23], BALB/c mice were divided into two groups and treated with CFA or IFA on day 20 after infection with P. brasiliensis.

in the P. brasiliensis-infected mice treated with CFA, no granulomas or yeast cells were seen in the pulmonary sections examined and a well-preserved alveolar architecture was observed on day 30 after treatment (Figure 2B, D and F).
Most importantly, the treatment with CFA induced a 2.4 log reduction in the fungal burden when compared to the IFA-treated mice, corresponding to 99% less CFU (Figure 1A).

The present study demonstrated that a single-dose administration of the Th1-inducing adjuvant (CFA) in P. brasiliensis-infected mice was sufficient to break the anergy observed in these animals restoring their ability to mount an effective immune response to the fungus.
Resistance to P. brasiliensis infection has been related to interferon-γ (IFN-γ) and other Th1-type cytokines [9]–[11]
50.Rationale: The passage directly links CFA (a Th1-inducing adjuvant) to loss of granulomatous pathology, dramatic reduction in lung CFU (99% reduction/2.4 log), and restoration of effective immune responses. It also cites prior evidence that resistance is related to IFN-γ and Th1 cytokines. Together these findings support the conclusion that polarization toward a Th1-type response determines a favorable outcome, whereas the Th2-associated/suppressed state corresponds to worse disease.

50.Option: The high levels of IFN-γ and TNF-α in CFA-treated mice, along with the absence of granulomas, suggest that pro-inflammatory cytokines alone are sufficient to prevent tissue damage and clear infection
Evidence: CFA-treated mice produced high levels of these pro and anti-inflammatory cytokines (Figure 1B–G).

Although many reports have demonstrated that the Th2 pattern is associated with a severe disease, whereas a Th1-biased immune response is linked to the asymptomatic and mild forms of PCM [9]–[14], others have shown that the induction of inflammatory cytokines, such as IFN-γ and TNF-α, can lead to overproduction of nitric oxide that has been associated with suppression of cell immunity [25]–[27].

we obtained an effective protection against P. brasiliensis infection even in the presence of anti-inflammatory cytokines, suggesting that, in this therapy model, the protective effect against PCM seems to be dependent on the induction of a mixed Th1/Th2 immune response pattern.
50.Rationale: Although CFA-treated mice had high levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines and protection, the authors caution that inflammatory cytokines can have deleterious effects (e.g., nitric oxide overproduction) and explicitly state the observed protection appears to depend on a mixed pro- and anti-inflammatory response rather than pro-inflammatory cytokines alone. Therefore the claim that pro-inflammatory cytokines alone are sufficient is not supported by the passage.

50.Option: The reduction in fungal burden and absence of yeast cells in CFA-treated mice indicate that the adjuvant directly kills the fungus, independent of the host immune response
Evidence: Most importantly, the treatment with CFA induced a 2.4 log reduction in the fungal burden when compared to the IFA-treated mice, corresponding to 99% less CFU (Figure 1A).

we suggest that the protection induced by CFA injection was due to a noticeable increase in the pulmonary levels of cytokines, which probably broke the immunosuppression status observed in the infected mice treated with IFA.

The present study demonstrated that a single-dose administration of the Th1-inducing adjuvant (CFA) in P. brasiliensis-infected mice was sufficient to break the anergy observed in these animals restoring their ability to mount an effective immune response to the fungus.

Nonetheless, we cannot exclude the involvement of other mechanisms, such as modulation by regulatory T cells [30], apoptosis in the antigen-specific T cells [31], and Fas-FasL and CTLA-4 expression [32].
50.Rationale: The authors attribute the reduction in fungal burden to immunomodulation (restoring cell-mediated immunity and increasing pulmonary cytokines), not to direct antifungal activity of CFA. They discuss immune-mediated mechanisms and explicitly frame CFA as an immunostimulatory adjuvant. No data are presented showing CFA has direct fungicidal action independent of host responses, so the option is incorrect.

51.Option: The demonstration that the assay can distinguish between infected and non-infected mice in both acute and chronic phases, enabling early detection of treatment failure
Evidence: The aptamers described in this report are able to detect parasite biomarkers in both the acute as well as the chronic phase of the disease.

All seven aptamers, Apt-1, 29, 71, 74, 75, 77, and 79, were able to detect significantly higher levels of parasite biomarkers in plasma of infected mice compared to the age matched non-infected control mice at 55 dpi using the ELA assay (Fig. 4B–C and S3 Fig.).

All seven aptamers were able to detect significantly higher levels of parasite biomarkers in plasma of infected mice compared to the age matched non-infected control mice at 170 dpi using the ELA assay (Figures 5B–C and S4 Fig.).

At 55 dpi, ELA assays with Apt-29 showed that the biomarker levels in 100% (9/9) of the infected drug treated mice were above the cutoff of the assay, suggesting that these animals could still be infected (Fig. 4D).
51.Rationale: A requirement for a standard endpoint across experimental models is consistent discrimination of infected versus non-infected status in the relevant disease phases. The passage explicitly states the aptamer ELA assay detects biomarkers in both acute (55 dpi) and chronic (170 dpi) phases and that it identifies treated animals that remain positive (treatment failure). Those capabilities directly support adoption as an endpoint for treatment efficacy across models.

51.Option: The ability to detect parasite DNA in blood and tissue samples using the same assay platform, allowing for direct comparison with gold-standard PCR methods
Evidence: We have recently reported an aptamer-based, non-serological, non-PCR assay, to detect T. cruzi biomarkers circulating in the blood of infected mice [19].
Real time PCR assay
T. cruzi DNA was detected in the blood, heart and skeletal muscles obtained from infected treated (I+Bz) mice at 55 dpi, by real time PCR.
51.Rationale: The assay described is an aptamer-based, non-PCR ELA that detects excreted/secreted parasite antigens (TESA), not parasite DNA. PCR-based detection of DNA is described separately in the passage as the real-time PCR assay. Therefore the ELA assay does not detect parasite DNA on the same platform and cannot be characterized as providing DNA detection comparable to PCR.

51.Option: The capacity to detect parasite-specific antigens in blood samples from mice treated with benznidazole, indicating a reduction in parasite load over time
Evidence: The fact that aptamers bound to purified trypomastigote extract and that T. cruzi infected drug treated mice showed reduced levels of biomarkers, compared to the infected group alone, indicates that the targets of the aptamers were of parasite origin.

At 55 dpi, ELA assays with Apt-29 showed that the biomarker levels in 100% (9/9) of the infected drug treated mice were above the cutoff of the assay, suggesting that these animals could still be infected (Fig. 4D).
At 170 dpi, 100% (13/13) of the benznidazole treated mice were ELA positive using Apt-29 and 71 (Fig. 5D).
51.Rationale: A key feature for an efficacy endpoint is that the assay detects parasite-specific antigens in treated animals and can reflect changes after therapy. The passage states treated mice showed reduced biomarker levels compared to untreated infected mice and reports ELA detection in benznidazole-treated animals at both acute and chronic time points, supporting that the assay measures treatment-associated changes in antigen levels (i.e., parasite burden dynamics).

51.Option: The ability to detect persistent infection in both acute and chronic phases using standard laboratory equipment
Evidence: The aptamers described in this report are able to detect parasite biomarkers in both the acute as well as the chronic phase of the disease.

ELA assays can be performed using standard ELISA equipment and do not require a highly controlled environment free of potential contaminating DNA, expensive reagents and complex instrumentation as PCR or flow cytometry.
As the animals do not need to be euthanized, a biomarker-based approach could yield continuous longitudinal data on the therapeutic effect of the drug.
51.Rationale: Adoption across diverse experimental models requires practical, scalable assays. The passage states the ELA detects persistent infection in acute and chronic phases and can be run on standard ELISA equipment without complex infrastructure, and allows longitudinal (non-terminal) sampling. Those attributes directly support its use as a standard endpoint in many lab settings.

51.Option: The use of biotinylated aptamers that bind to T. cruzi excreted secreted antigens with high affinity and specificity, enabling detection at low concentrations
Evidence: The R21 aptamer pool also showed saturable binding to trypomastigote extract and at lower levels to T. cruzi epimastigote extracts (Fig. 1B).
Aptamer pools R16, 18 and 21 showed binding at concentration as low as 32.25 nM, suggesting that they contained aptamers with higher binding affinities compared to aptamers obtained from R10.
All the selected biotinylated aptamers, Apt-1, 29, 71, 74, 75, 77 and 79, showed saturable binding to TESA but not to BSA which was used as a negative control (Fig. 2B).

Dose response curve for the R21 pool showed saturable binding to TESA at concentrations ranging between 25 to 50 nM, thus indicating that the individual aptamers in this pool were high-affinity binders with minimal binding to the BSA control (Fig. 1B).
51.Rationale: Technical performance—high affinity, specificity, and low-limit detection—is essential for a reliable endpoint. The passage documents that biotinylated monoclonal aptamers from the R21 pool bind TESA with saturable, dose-dependent kinetics, detect at low nanomolar concentrations, and show minimal non-specific binding (to BSA). These properties directly enable sensitive and specific detection of parasite antigens across models, supporting standardization.

52.Option: Use serology as the primary indicator for surveillance because it detects past exposure and can identify individuals at risk of recrudescence even after infection has resolved
Evidence: It is important to note that each of these indicators measures a different signal: clinical grading (both field-based and through photography) measures signs of inflammation, infection tests are a direct measure of current infection with Ct, and serology measures a history of previous exposure to the pathogen.

Following a WHO meeting in 2016 reviewing existing data, it was concluded that there was insufficient evidence to support routine use of Ct infection or serology tests to inform trachoma elimination programs.

WHO informal workshop in December 2021 supported the use of age-stratified data on Ct infection and serological data ... in districts where TF1–9 either remains at or above the 5% elimination threshold ... or falls <5% but subsequently returns to ≥5% during the surveillance period after cessation of MDA
52.Rationale: While serology does detect past exposure (and the passage explicitly states serology measures previous exposure), it is not suitable as the primary standalone surveillance indicator for timely detection of recrudescence. The passage notes there was "insufficient evidence to support routine use of ... serology" and that WHO only supported its use in specific scenarios (persistent or recrudescent districts) rather than as a primary routine indicator. Serology’s measurement of past exposure means it can lag behind current transmission and could misclassify current transmission status if used alone.

52.Option: Combine conjunctival photography and infection testing into a single composite indicator to increase sensitivity and reduce the need for multiple assessments
Evidence: It is important to note that each of these indicators measures a different signal: clinical grading (both field-based and through photography) measures signs of inflammation, infection tests are a direct measure of current infection with Ct, and serology measures a history of previous exposure to the pathogen.

Therefore, each of these indicators could provide different measures of the public-health threat caused by trachoma; i.e., indicators should be considered "complementary" rather than "alternative" to each other.
The most agreed-upon strength for conjunctival photography was that multiple graders can grade the same photo, increasing accuracy and helping to determine the status of borderline cases (61, 84%).

The most agreed-upon barriers for this indicator [infection testing] all pertain to challenges around the feasibility of implementation, including the extra work required to plan for logistics of field collection and sample processing (68, 96%), the challenge posed by a desire for samples to be tested by the nearest lab (63, 89%), increased costs compared to field grading of TF posed by the need to store samples (62, 87%) and purchase needed equipment (62, 87%), as well as the challenge posed by the requirement for maintaining a cold chain (61, 87%).

The most agreed-upon barrier to implementation of conjunctival photography is that it would increase the costs compared to TF field grading due to the need for camera equipment (43, 60%), followed closely by increased costs due to the need for increased personnel training and time (41, 57%).
52.Rationale: Although photography and infection testing provide complementary information, combining them into a single composite indicator does not necessarily reduce complexity or misclassification risk and would likely increase logistical burden and costs. The passage stresses that indicators measure different signals and should be considered complementary; it also highlights substantial feasibility barriers for infection testing (logistics, cold chain, lab capacity) and costs for photography. Creating a composite may exacerbate these challenges and complicate interpretation because the signals are distinct rather than directly additive.

52.Option: Use field grading alone for surveillance because it is the most feasible and widely accepted method, despite its inability to detect infection in the absence of clinical signs
Evidence: A major goal of the World Health Organization (WHO) Alliance for the Global Elimination of Trachoma is the reduction of TF prevalence in 1–9-year-olds (TF1–9) to <5% in all formerly endemic districts worldwide, contributing to the achievement of global elimination of trachoma as a public health problem.

It is important to note that each of these indicators measures a different signal: clinical grading (both field-based and through photography) measures signs of inflammation, infection tests are a direct measure of current infection with Ct, and serology measures a history of previous exposure to the pathogen.

However, TF1–9 has several limitations as the sole indicator for trachoma. First, the relationship between TF1–9 and infection is not predictable, especially post-MDA, and the reduction in TF1–9 often lags behind the reduction in Ct infection.
Almost all (79, 98%) of respondents agreed that TF grading being WHO-recommended for diagnosing trachoma is a strength of field grading of TF.
The advantages of blood testing enable programs, with appropriate ethical approvals and participant consent, to benefit from the increased efficiency of testing one sample for many diseases.
52.Rationale: Field grading is feasible, familiar, and WHO-recommended and provides immediate results, but the passage explicitly warns that TF1–9 alone has important limitations: its relationship to infection is unpredictable, reductions in TF can lag behind reductions in Ct infection, and follicles are not necessarily specific to trachoma. Using field grading alone risks missing early or low-level recrudescence (current infection without clinical signs) and misclassifying transmission status, so it does not best balance timely detection and misclassification risk.

52.Option: Prioritize infection testing for all individuals in surveillance to ensure detection of current infection, even if it delays reporting due to cold chain and lab processing constraints
Evidence: It is important to note that each of these indicators measures a different signal: clinical grading (both field-based and through photography) measures signs of inflammation, infection tests are a direct measure of current infection with Ct, and serology measures a history of previous exposure to the pathogen.
The most agreed-upon strength for infection testing was this indicator’s ability to detect infection in the absence of clinical signs (55, 81%).

The most agreed-upon barriers for this indicator all pertain to challenges around the feasibility of implementation, including the extra work required to plan for logistics of field collection and sample processing (68, 96%), the challenge posed by a desire for samples to be tested by the nearest lab (63, 89%), increased costs compared to field grading of TF posed by the need to store samples (62, 87%) and purchase needed equipment (62, 87%), as well as the challenge posed by the requirement for maintaining a cold chain (61, 87%).

In terms of feasibility, key strengths mentioned by IDI participants included the familiarity of blood spot collection and testing by health systems and the ability for high throughput of these sample types, although several participants mentioned a lack of lab capacity to store and test samples at a local level as a potential issue.
52.Rationale: While infection testing is the most direct way to detect current infection and would reduce misclassification of active transmission, the passage highlights substantial feasibility constraints (logistics, cold chain, lab capacity, costs) that could delay reporting or limit coverage. Prioritizing infection testing for all individuals could impede timeliness and be impractical; the passage suggests infection testing is valuable as a complementary, targeted tool rather than universal primary surveillance in many settings.

52.Option: Implement a tiered surveillance strategy that uses field grading for initial screening, followed by targeted infection testing or serology in ambiguous or high-risk cases to balance timeliness and accuracy
Evidence: It is important to note that each of these indicators measures a different signal: clinical grading (both field-based and through photography) measures signs of inflammation, infection tests are a direct measure of current infection with Ct, and serology measures a history of previous exposure to the pathogen. Therefore, each of these indicators could provide different measures of the public-health threat caused by trachoma; i.e., indicators should be considered "complementary" rather than "alternative" to each other.

Following a WHO meeting in 2016 reviewing existing data, it was concluded that there was insufficient evidence to support routine use of Ct infection or serology tests to inform trachoma elimination programs. However, a WHO informal workshop in December 2021 supported the use of age-stratified data on Ct infection and serological data ... in districts where TF1–9 either remains at or above the 5% elimination threshold at the second impact survey ... or falls <5% but subsequently returns to ≥5% during the surveillance period after cessation of MDA (districts with "recrudescent" TF).
Almost all (79, 98%) of respondents agreed that TF grading being WHO-recommended for diagnosing trachoma is a strength of field grading of TF.
TF field grading...the lack of pain or adverse consequences from this method, and the ability to receive results immediately.

The most agreed-upon barriers for [infection testing] all pertain to challenges around the feasibility of implementation, including the extra work required to plan for logistics of field collection and sample processing (68, 96%) ... and the challenge posed by the requirement for maintaining a cold chain (61, 87%).
52.Rationale: The passage repeatedly frames indicators as complementary and notes WHO support for using infection testing and serology in specific scenarios (persistent or recrudescent districts) rather than as routine primary measures. Field grading is rapid, familiar, and provides on-the-spot results, making it suitable for initial, timely screening; targeted confirmatory infection testing or serology in ambiguous or high-risk situations addresses the limitations of TF1–9 (poor correlation with infection, lagging reductions) while containing feasibility and cost burdens. This tiered approach therefore best balances timeliness, resource constraints, and the need to reduce misclassification from overlapping diagnostic signals.

53.Option: Since the TPTest is positive in individuals with other febrile illnesses, it should be used as a general screening tool for all acute febrile conditions in endemic areas to identify potential enteric fever cases
Evidence: The TPTest was positive in 3 out of 32 patients with other illnesses (Group III).
False positive results were seen in three patients with other illnesses.

Despite these shortcomings, our results suggest that an assay based on detecting anti-Salmonella IgA antibodies produced by transiently circulating and activated lymphocytes may hold significant promise as a platform approach to identify individuals with enteric fever, including in endemic zones.
53.Rationale: Although the TPTest was occasionally positive in patients with other febrile illnesses (three documented false positives), the passage frames the test as a targeted diagnostic for enteric fever rather than a general screening tool for all acute febrile conditions. The evidence of false positives indicates imperfect specificity, so recommending it as a broad screening test for all febrile illnesses would be inappropriate and could lead to misclassification and unnecessary treatment.

53.Option: Because the TPTest is 100% sensitive in bacteremic patients, a positive result in any febrile individual from an endemic area can be used to definitively diagnose enteric fever without further clinical evaluation
Evidence: Considering blood culture-confirmed cases as positive and the patients with other illnesses and healthy controls as negative for enteric fever , the sensitivity and specificity of the TPTest were 39/39 (100%) and 103/106 (97%; 95% confidence interval, CI: 94–100), respectively.
False positive results were seen in three patients with other illnesses.
Our study has a number of limitations.
53.Rationale: Although the TPTest showed 100% sensitivity against blood culture–confirmed cases in this analysis, the passage also documents false positives and emphasizes limitations. Thus a positive TPTest is not definitively diagnostic in all febrile individuals without clinical correlation; specificity is not perfect and the authors caution about limitations. The test should be interpreted within clinical context and not used alone as an absolute diagnostic rule-out/ rule-in without further evaluation.

53.Option: A negative TPTest result in the acute phase of illness reliably rules out enteric fever, as the assay detects only persistent immune responses that remain elevated throughout the course of infection
Evidence: To assess the duration of TPTest responses, we collected blood from later stages of illness (days 7 and 21) from 38 patients with a positive TPTest at the time of presentation. Of these, 32 (84%) patients converted to a negative result by day 21∶18 (56%) patients became negative by day 7, and the additional 14 (44%) became negative by day 21, suggested that the TPTest assesses a transient response in enteric fever.

For 28 patients, we performed the TPTest using lymphocyte culture supernatants collected at both 24 and 48 hours (p = 0.231) of incubation. Among these, 14 patients were positive for the TPTest (>10 ELISA unit) following 48 hours of incubation. Of these, 9 (64%) test results were positive following 24 hours (and remained positive after 48 hours), and 5 (36%) were negative at 24 hours but became positive at 48 hours of incubation (Figure 1).
53.Rationale: The passage indicates the TPTest measures a transient IgA response that often wanes by day 21, so it does not detect a persistent immune response throughout illness. Additionally, some positives required longer incubation (24 vs 48 hours) to be detected, implying that timing can affect detection. Therefore a single negative TPTest in the acute phase does not reliably exclude enteric fever without considering timing, clinical picture, and possibly repeat testing.

53.Option: A positive TPTest result in the acute phase of illness is likely indicative of recent infection, but false positives may occur in other febrile illnesses due to non-specific immune activation, necessitating clinical context for interpretation
Evidence: To assess the duration of TPTest responses, we collected blood from later stages of illness (days 7 and 21) from 38 patients with a positive TPTest at the time of presentation. Of these, 32 (84%) patients converted to a negative result by day 21∶18 (56%) patients became negative by day 7, and the additional 14 (44%) became negative by day 21, suggested that the TPTest assesses a transient response in enteric fever.
The TPTest was positive in 3 out of 32 patients with other illnesses (Group III).
False positive results were seen in three patients with other illnesses.
53.Rationale: The passage explicitly states the TPTest detects a transient response that converts to negative in most patients by day 21, supporting that a positive result in the acute phase reflects recent/active infection. The documented false positives in three patients with other illnesses indicate non-perfect specificity and the potential for non-specific immune activation to produce positive results. Together these facts support interpreting a positive TPTest as evidence of recent infection but in the context of clinical assessment and other information.

53.Option: The TPTest can distinguish between acute and past enteric fever infections because it detects IgA antibodies that remain elevated for months after recovery, allowing for retrospective diagnosis of prior exposure
Evidence: To assess the duration of TPTest responses, we collected blood from later stages of illness (days 7 and 21) from 38 patients with a positive TPTest at the time of presentation. Of these, 32 (84%) patients converted to a negative result by day 21∶18 (56%) patients became negative by day 7, and the additional 14 (44%) became negative by day 21, suggested that the TPTest assesses a transient response in enteric fever.

Because of the shortcomings of current enteric fever diagnostic assays, we were particularly interested in assessing whether we could pursue an alternative approach and take advantage of the fact that following recent infection activated antigen-specific lymphocytes transiently circulate in the peripheral circulation and could be detected there [28].
53.Rationale: The passage indicates the TPTest detects a transient IgA response from activated lymphocytes that circulates only briefly after recent infection and typically reverts to negative within weeks. Therefore it is not suitable for detecting past infections months after recovery, and cannot be used for retrospective diagnosis of prior exposure based on prolonged antibody persistence.

54.Option: Incorporate covariance terms for conditional dependence between test results in the latent-class model to account for potential test correlation
Evidence: Conditional dependencies between tests were assessed by separately examining the impact of each of the 4 covariance terms.
A better model fit was obtained by including a covariance term for a conditional dependence between the sensitivities of the copro-antigen ELISA and the copro-PCR for E. multilocularis.
covariance terms for conditional test dependency in the Hui-Walter latent-class model [7]–[10], have been included in a number of analyses that used a Bayesian approach
54.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes assessing conditional dependencies by including covariance terms in the Bayesian latent-class models and reports improved model fit when such a covariance term was included. Therefore incorporating covariance terms is the most direct methodological choice to address bias from conditional dependence between test results.

54.Option: Use a single diagnostic test with known high specificity as a reference standard to estimate prevalence, assuming it is a perfect gold standard
Evidence: In the absence of a perfect gold standard in surveillance studies, the accuracy of new or alternative tests cannot be estimated robustly and without bias by comparing such test results of new or alternative results against an imperfect gold standard.

Arecoline purgation, a well-established technique of high specificity, has frequently been used in the past. However, it is a laborious and potentially hazardous procedure and has been reported to show poor sensitivity [2].

Only a necropsy of dogs followed by the sedimentation and parasite-counting technique can be considered close to a perfect gold standard, i.e., a gold standard with 100% sensitivity and 100% specificity.
54.Rationale: The passage warns that using an imperfect single test as a reference leads to biased estimates. Although some tests have high specificity, they may have poor sensitivity (or other limitations), and only necropsy is close to a true gold standard. Thus assuming a single high-specificity test is a perfect gold standard is not appropriate and would risk biased prevalence estimates.

54.Option: Calculate the prevalence using the most sensitive test alone, assuming it captures all true cases
Evidence: Thus multiple tests should ideally be used routinely in the population of interest if no perfect gold standard is available.

using the true prevalence rather than the test prevalence may give different results with regard to the importance of determinants ... This is due to misclassification errors following false positive or false negative test results when using test results in a deterministic manner.
54.Rationale: Relying on a single (even highly sensitive) test ignores false positives/negatives and the absence of a perfect gold standard. The passage recommends using multiple tests and latent-class methods to avoid misclassification bias; using one test alone is therefore not an appropriate method to address bias from conditional dependence or imperfect tests.

54.Option: Stratify the population by age and sex to ensure equal distribution of covariates between test-positive and test-negative groups before estimating prevalence
Evidence: The Hui-Walter model relies on several assumptions... The third assumption is that test results are conditionally independent given the true disease status.
Age, sex and Taenia spp co-infection have been integrated as covariates in the latent-class models and their effects on the true prevalence have been assessed.
54.Rationale: While the passage notes integrating covariates (age, sex, Taenia) into latent-class models to assess effects on prevalence, stratifying by age and sex does not directly address conditional dependence (correlation) between test results. The specific issue of conditional dependence is handled by incorporating covariance terms in the model, not by simple stratification on covariates.

54.Option: Fix the specificity of all tests to 100% to eliminate uncertainty and ensure unbiased prevalence estimates
Evidence: Initial analyses with non-informative priors as beta distributions (1, 1) were used for all parameters, except for the faecal counts of adult parasites following purge, where the specificity was set at 1.

Fixing the specificity = 1 of the copro-PCR instead or in addition to the specificity of the purge did not affect the specificity of the copro-antigen ELISA. However, the sensitivities of the copro-antigen ELISA and the purge decreased (10 to 50%) (table 3). However, the deviance-information criteria indicated that this model was a poorer fit than allowing the specificity of the copro-PCR to vary.
Indeed when the PCR tests were first developed by [15], the specificity was estimated at 100%. However this estimate was based on a sample of 10 dogs from non-endemic areas.
In a naturally infected population false positive PCR results may occur due to coprophagia of faeces containing Echinococcus eggs.
54.Rationale: The authors experimented with fixing specificity to 1 for some tests but found this can lead to poorer model fit, large changes in estimated sensitivities, and may be unjustified (earlier 100% estimates were based on small samples). Fixing all specificities to 100% would not eliminate bias and can produce misleading results; the passage recommends allowing parameters to vary and modeling dependencies instead.

55.Option: The variability in treatment duration (1–3 days) across patients suggests that longer treatment duration is associated with better neurological recovery
Evidence: Dosing regimens vary from 40–60 mg/kg/day given in divided doses, and treatment duration varies widely from 1–14 days in neuroschistosomiasis.
In our series only one patient made a complete neurological recovery, with the remaining three patients demonstrating some improvement following anti-parasitic and steroid treatment.
All patients received a minimum of 3 days praziquantel and high dose steroids.
55.Rationale: Although the passage notes wide variability in praziquantel dosing and duration, it does not present data linking longer treatment duration to better neurological recovery. The series is small and uncontrolled; most patients received at least 3 days of therapy, yet only one fully recovered. There is no comparative evidence in the text showing that those who received longer courses had superior outcomes, so this option is not supported by the passage.

55.Option: The poor neurological recovery in three of four patients despite treatment suggests that delayed initiation of therapy relative to symptom onset contributes to irreversible neurological damage
Evidence: This series provides the most detailed description of a series of spinal schistosomiasis diagnosed in the UK, and highlights the delayed time from presentation to medical services to commencement of effective treatment, an important prognostic factor for long term morbidity.
Mean time from first presentation to UK health services to commencement of praziquantel was 42.15 days (range 16–74 days) (Fig 4).

In our patient with the shortest time of presentation-to-treatment (16 days), travel history was elicited early by the attending neurologist, who was then able to seek advice from specialist infectious diseases services.
In our series only one patient made a complete neurological recovery, with the remaining three patients demonstrating some improvement following anti-parasitic and steroid treatment.
55.Rationale: The passage explicitly emphasizes delays from presentation to initiation of effective treatment (mean ~42 days, range 16–74 days) and labels this delay as an "important prognostic factor for long term morbidity." The observation that three of four patients had only partial recovery despite treatment, together with the authors' statement linking delayed treatment initiation to worse long-term outcomes, supports the interpretation that delayed therapy relative to symptom onset likely contributed to irreversible neurological damage.

55.Option: The presence of peripheral eosinophilia in three patients supports the conclusion that early treatment initiation reduces the risk of irreversible neurological damage
Evidence: Peripheral blood eosinophilia was detected in three out of four patients, however detection of schistosomal ova in stool or urine was reported in no patients; rectal snips were not performed.
A raised peripheral eosinophilia in the context of neurological symptoms should prompt clinical suspicion of a parasitic infection involving the CNS.
55.Rationale: While peripheral eosinophilia was noted in three patients and is diagnostically helpful, the passage does not link eosinophilia to long-term outcomes or to the protective effect of earlier treatment. Eosinophilia indicates host response to parasitic infection but does not, in this report, provide evidence that earlier treatment reduces irreversible neurological damage. Therefore this option is not supported by the presented data.

55.Option: The presence of persistent neurological symptoms in three patients despite treatment suggests that the duration of schistosomal infection prior to treatment initiation is a key determinant of long-term outcomes
Evidence: Mean time between travel from endemic regions and presentation was 12.7 months (range 0–29 months) (Fig 4).
Length of time from initial presentation to commencing appropriate anti-parasitic treatment was 42.15 days (range 16–74 days) (Fig 4).
In our series only one patient made a complete neurological recovery, with the remaining three patients demonstrating some improvement following anti-parasitic and steroid treatment.
This series ... highlights the delayed time from presentation to medical services to commencement of effective treatment, an important prognostic factor for long term morbidity.
55.Rationale: Persistent symptoms despite treatment could be caused by either long duration of infection before symptom onset or by delays from presentation to treatment. The passage reports both time from exposure to presentation (mean ~12.7 months) and delay from presentation to treatment (~42 days), but the authors specifically emphasize the latter (delay from presentation to commencement of treatment) as an "important prognostic factor." The data in this small uncontrolled series do not isolate duration of pre-treatment infection as the key determinant apart from the acknowledged problem of delayed initiation after presentation, so this option overstates what the text supports.

55.Option: The fact that all patients had elevated CSF protein and lymphocytosis supports the hypothesis that early treatment initiation prevents chronic inflammatory changes in the CNS
Evidence: CSF analysis showed a CSF glucose within normal range at 4.2mg/dl, elevated CSF protein at 1.87g/L and a white cell count (WCC) of 12 leucocytes/mm3 (100% monocytes).
CSF microscopy revealed an elevated WCC of 297 leukocytes/mm3 which were predominantly lymphocytes (87%).
CSF microscopy showed elevated WCC of 297 (261 lymphocytes, 36 polymorphs); with elevated eosinophil count, a moderately raised protein level of 0.96g/dl and glucose within the normal range.
CSF analysis demonstrated an elevated protein of 0.7g/L and WCC of 132 (81% lymphocytes).
CSF lymphocytosis was present in all four patients.
55.Rationale: The passage documents inflammatory CSF findings (elevated protein and lymphocytosis) in these cases, consistent with an inflammatory CNS reaction to eggs. However, there is no data presented linking these CSF findings to timing of treatment or showing that earlier treatment prevents progression to chronic inflammatory changes. The presence of inflammatory CSF supports diagnosis but does not, in this uncontrolled series, demonstrate that early treatment alters the development of chronic CNS inflammation; thus the option is not supported by the text.

56.Option: The high expression of CD106 and CD54 suggests enhanced leukocyte recruitment and endothelial activation, which likely contributes to rapid parasite destruction and clinical resolution
Evidence: The immunohistochemical analysis of the brain tissues surrounding cysticerci showed a high expression of CD80, CD106, CD69, and IL-10; a moderate expression of IL4, TNF-α, and CD54, and a low expression of IL-17 and IL-6 with respect to brain tissues from sham-operated pigs (Figs 3 and 4).

The high expression of CD106 (the vascular cell adhesion molecule VCAM-1) may result from the presence of TNF-α, which is known to increase the expression of adhesion molecules in endothelial cells, favoring the adhesion of peripheral leukocytes to enter the brain and therefore promoting brain inflammation [36].
Macro- and microscopic examination revealed the presence of cysticerci in all animals.
None of the infected animals in experiment 2 exhibited neurological manifestations.
56.Rationale: While the passage documents high CD106 (and moderate CD54) expression and notes these molecules can favor leukocyte adhesion and entry into the brain, the authors describe this process as promoting brain inflammation rather than demonstrating rapid parasite clearance and clinical resolution. Additionally, parasites persisted ("Macro- and microscopic examination revealed the presence of cysticerci in all animals") and animals remained asymptomatic ("None of the infected animals in experiment 2 exhibited neurological manifestations"). Thus the evidence does not support the strong causal claim that CD106/CD54–mediated recruitment produced rapid parasite destruction and clinical resolution; if anything, the text links CD106 to inflammation and discusses regulatory mechanisms (e.g., IL-10) as more likely explanations for limited clinical severity.

56.Option: The absence of neurological symptoms in all infected pigs indicates that the immune response is effectively sterilizing the infection, preventing both parasite survival and disease manifestation
Evidence: Macro- and microscopic examination revealed the presence of cysticerci in all animals.
None of the infected animals in experiment 2 exhibited neurological manifestations.
56.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that cysticerci were present in all animals despite the absence of neurological symptoms, so the lack of clinical signs does not indicate sterilizing immunity. Presence of parasites ("Macro- and microscopic examination revealed the presence of cysticerci in all animals") shows infection persisted, so asymptomatic status cannot be taken as evidence of complete parasite elimination.

56.Option: The presence of high levels of CD69 and CD80 expression indicates strong T and B cell activation, which likely drives rapid parasite clearance and prevents clinical disease
Evidence: The immunohistochemical analysis of the brain tissues surrounding cysticerci showed a high expression of CD80, CD106, CD69, and IL-10; a moderate expression of IL4, TNF-α, and CD54, and a low expression of IL-17 and IL-6 with respect to brain tissues from sham-operated pigs (Figs 3 and 4).

In general, the expression of NeuN (viable neurons) and nestin (immature neurons) was low, particularly in tissues proximal to the parasite, and increased slightly in tissues distal to the parasite (Fig 5).
Macro- and microscopic examination revealed the presence of cysticerci in all animals.
None of the infected animals in experiment 2 exhibited neurological manifestations.
56.Rationale: Although CD69 and CD80 are markers of lymphocyte activation and are reported as highly expressed, the passage does not support the inference that this activation "drives rapid parasite clearance and prevents clinical disease." Parasites remained present in all animals, and the authors emphasize regulatory signals (high IL-10 and relatively low proinflammatory cytokines) and controlled neuroinflammation as plausible reasons for the lack of clinical signs. Therefore, the data do not justify the stronger claim that CD69/CD80-driven activation caused rapid clearance and clinical protection.

56.Option: The dominance of regulatory IL-10 over proinflammatory cytokines may suppress excessive neuroinflammation, contributing to asymptomatic disease despite parasite persistence
Evidence: The immunohistochemical analysis of the brain tissues surrounding cysticerci showed a high expression of CD80, CD106, CD69, and IL-10; a moderate expression of IL4, TNF-α, and CD54, and a low expression of IL-17 and IL-6 with respect to brain tissues from sham-operated pigs (Figs 3 and 4).
It is noteworthy that the expression of proinflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-17) was not especially high, contrasting with the high expression of the regulatory cytokine IL-10.
None of the infected animals in experiment 2 exhibited neurological manifestations.
Thus, it is possible that the absence of neurological symptoms in pigs be also due to a more active immunomodulatory process in this species.
56.Rationale: The passage directly reports a pattern of high IL-10 together with low proinflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-17) in brain tissue, and links this immunoregulatory profile to the observed absence of neurological symptoms. The authors explicitly suggest that an active immunomodulatory process, exemplified by elevated IL-10, could underlie asymptomatic infection despite parasite persistence, making this option the best-supported explanation in the text.

56.Option: The elevated levels of anti-cysticercal antibodies in serum suggest a systemic immune response that is directly responsible for controlling parasite burden and preventing clinical disease
Evidence: The level of anti-cysticercal antibodies and of the HP10 antigen in serum were measured at seven times during the experiment (Fig 7A and 7B, respectively). Samples were positive to antibodies at day 53 post-infection (PI) in all the animal groups (intramuscular and subarachnoid inoculation). Although a decrease was observed at the time of sacrifice, all sera remained positive during the experiment (Fig 7A).
The animals intramuscularly challenged exhibited higher antibody levels.
Macro- and microscopic examination revealed the presence of cysticerci in all animals.
56.Rationale: While serum anti-cysticercal antibodies increased and remained detectable, the passage shows that parasites persisted in the animals despite antibody responses ("Macro- and microscopic examination revealed the presence of cysticerci in all animals"). The authors discuss peripheral antibody production and recruitment to the CNS but do not attribute parasite control or absence of clinical disease directly to systemic antibody levels. Therefore the claim that antibodies are directly responsible for controlling parasite burden and preventing disease is not supported by the provided evidence.

57.Option: A linear model incorporating seasonal NDVI variation and animal imports best balances environmental drivers and data fit, as it provides a simpler functional form while maintaining a good fit to the IgG prevalence peak and long-term trends
Evidence: Both linear and exponential models with seasonal variations of the NDVI including animal imports (Models 1a and 1b) fitted equally well the data (Table 2, DICmodel1a = 522.8 and DICmodel1b = 523.7) and showed a good agreement with the IgG serological data (Fig 2 and Fig 3A–3H).

Since both models 1a and 1b with animal imports exhibited a similar fit, we present in the main manuscript the fitting and forecasts using the exponential model (results obtained with the linear model are similar and provided in S5 Fig and S6A–S6H Fig).

Models without any animal import (Models 2a and 2b) had the worst fit (Table 2), and failed to capture the IgG prevalence peak (S4 Fig, blue and green solid lines), suggesting that the re-emergence of RVF virus in 2008–10 may have been due to the import of infectious animals.
57.Rationale: The passage reports that both the linear (Model 1a) and exponential (Model 1b) formulations linking transmission to NDVI, when combined with animal imports, "fitted equally well the data" and agreed with the IgG serological data. That supports the correctness of a linear NDVI model with imports as a valid approach that balances environmental drivers and statistical fit. The option's point about a "simpler functional form" is consistent with a linear relationship being more parsimonious while still producing a good fit, and the passage explicitly shows that including imports was necessary to capture the IgG prevalence peak (models without imports performed poorly).

57.Option: An exponential model with constant transmission and animal imports best balances environmental drivers and data fit, as it captures the non-linear response of transmission to environmental conditions while allowing for seasonal variation in import timing
Evidence: When transmission was not dependent on NDVI seasonality (Model 3), the IgG prevalence peak was also captured (S4 Fig, black solid line), but the model did not fit the data as well as the NDVI seasonality-dependent models, the DIC being higher (Table 2, DICmodel3 = 543.6).
