Both linear and exponential models with seasonal variations of the NDVI including animal imports (Models 1a and 1b) fitted equally well the data (Table 2, DICmodel1a = 522.8 and DICmodel1b = 523.7) and showed a good agreement with the IgG serological data (Fig 2 and Fig 3A–3H).
57.Rationale: The option describes an exponential model with constant transmission plus imports (i.e., transmission not varying with NDVI). The passage shows that models in which transmission did not depend on NDVI seasonality (Model 3) performed worse (higher DIC = 543.6) than NDVI-dependent models with imports. Thus a constant-transmission model with imports does not best balance environmental drivers and fit; NDVI-dependent models (linear or exponential) performed better.

57.Option: An exponential model incorporating seasonal NDVI variation and animal imports best balances environmental drivers and data fit, as it captures both the timing of the IgG prevalence peak and the long-term serological trends with high statistical precision
Evidence: Both linear and exponential models with seasonal variations of the NDVI including animal imports (Models 1a and 1b) fitted equally well the data (Table 2, DICmodel1a = 522.8 and DICmodel1b = 523.7) and showed a good agreement with the IgG serological data (Fig 2 and Fig 3A–3H).

Since both models 1a and 1b with animal imports exhibited a similar fit, we present in the main manuscript the fitting and forecasts using the exponential model (results obtained with the linear model are similar and provided in S5 Fig and S6A–S6H Fig).

Fig 2 and Fig 3A–3H show the median and the 95% CrI of the 5000 stochastic trajectories of the proportion of IgG positive animals. The main discrepancies among model trajectories were observed for the first part of the study period (Oct 2004-Jun 2008, before the peak, Fig 2) ... In contrast, in July 2008-June 2016 the model was fitted to age-specific IgG prevalence (Fig 3A–3H), and simulations showed little variation.

The credibility intervals of the estimated parameters were relatively narrow, and impacted only on the variability of the trajectories observed in 2004–08, when monthly prevalence estimates were informed by a small number of sampled animals, generating large confidence intervals.
57.Rationale: The authors explicitly note that both NDVI-dependent models with imports fitted the serological data well, and that they presented results using the exponential model in the main manuscript because the two formulations had similar fit. The passage also documents narrow credibility intervals and little variability in fitted trajectories for the key period (July 2008–June 2016), supporting the claim that the exponential NDVI+imports model captures timing of the IgG peak and long-term trends with good statistical precision. Therefore the exponential NDVI model with imports is an appropriate choice.

57.Option: A model with constant transmission and animal imports best balances environmental drivers and data fit, as it accounts for the import of infectious animals while assuming stable transmission conditions across seasons
Evidence: When transmission was not dependent on NDVI seasonality (Model 3), the IgG prevalence peak was also captured (S4 Fig, black solid line), but the model did not fit the data as well as the NDVI seasonality-dependent models, the DIC being higher (Table 2, DICmodel3 = 543.6).

Both linear and exponential models with seasonal variations of the NDVI including animal imports (Models 1a and 1b) fitted equally well the data (Table 2, DICmodel1a = 522.8 and DICmodel1b = 523.7) and showed a good agreement with the IgG serological data (Fig 2 and Fig 3A–3H).
57.Rationale: The passage reports that a model with constant transmission (no NDVI dependence) performed worse (higher DIC) than NDVI-dependent models with imports. Although a constant-transmission model with imports could reproduce the peak, its overall fit was inferior to models that included environmental seasonality; thus it does not best balance environmental drivers and statistical fit.

57.Option: A model with seasonal NDVI variation but no animal imports best balances environmental drivers and data fit, as it captures the influence of climate on transmission while avoiding overfitting from anthropogenic factors
Evidence: Models without any animal import (Models 2a and 2b) had the worst fit (Table 2), and failed to capture the IgG prevalence peak (S4 Fig, blue and green solid lines), suggesting that the re-emergence of RVF virus in 2008–10 may have been due to the import of infectious animals.

Both linear and exponential models with seasonal variations of the NDVI including animal imports (Models 1a and 1b) fitted equally well the data (Table 2, DICmodel1a = 522.8 and DICmodel1b = 523.7) and showed a good agreement with the IgG serological data (Fig 2 and Fig 3A–3H).
57.Rationale: The passage makes clear that models omitting animal imports performed worst and specifically failed to capture the observed IgG prevalence peak. Thus a model that includes NDVI seasonality but excludes imports does not provide an adequate statistical fit to the serological data and is not the best balance between environmental drivers and data fit.

58.Option: High-level pyrethroid resistance in T. infestans is geographically restricted because of low population density in areas outside the hotspot, limiting the spread of resistant alleles.
Evidence: Resistance to insecticides is a microevolutionary process, over which the dynamics, the structure of the population and the gene flow between groups of individuals would determine the maximum geographical spread of each process of resistance evolution [27, 28].

The accumulation of evidence over the last years suggests that the occurrence of insecticide resistance in T. infestans populations is associated with the high genetic variability detected in the historical dispersion site of the species towards the southern cone of South America [21, 42, 43] and the strong spatial structure of the populations derived by low population dispersal rates [24, 44–45].
58.Rationale: The passage discusses population structure and low dispersal rates (gene flow) as determinants of the geographic spread of resistance, not low population density. It attributes limited spread to population structure/low dispersal and genetic variability rather than to low density outside the hotspot. Therefore the specific claim that low population density is the restricting factor is not supported by the passage.

58.Option: The spatial distribution of high-level pyrethroid resistance in T. infestans is determined by the presence of sylvatic populations that serve as reservoirs for resistant genotypes.
Evidence: a number of wild foci of T. infestans have been described, mainly in the Inter-Andean Valleys of Bolivia, in the Gran Chaco of Argentina, Bolivia and Paraguay [4–8] and in a Metropolitan region from Chile [9].

we propose that the occurrence of pyrethroid resistant populations in the border between Argentina and Bolivia is not a primary result of the insecticide selection pressure, but a consequence of the existence of naturally tolerant populations of T. infestans, shown by the occurrence of resistant T. infestans population of sylvatic origin.

The resistance remains high not because of an insecticide-based selection process, but as a natural selection process acting over a population having a naturally high frequency of resistant individuals.
resistant T. infestans population of sylvatic origin... even on sylvatic populations of T. infestans, never before exposed to the pyrethroids, representing natural and wild toxicological phenotypes.
58.Rationale: The passage explicitly notes resistant populations of sylvatic origin and argues these naturally tolerant (sylvatic) populations can explain high resistance in the hotspot. Sylvatic populations that already carry resistant phenotypes could act as local reservoirs maintaining high-level resistance, so this option is supported by the text.

58.Option: High-level pyrethroid resistance in T. infestans is limited to areas with the highest frequency of insecticide application, indicating that resistance evolves only under intense selection pressure.
Evidence: The repeated application of pyrethroid insecticide does not seem to be the only cause of pyrethroid resistance appearance, as resistant populations occurred in areas that received less insecticide pressure than others where resistance did not occur, and because multiple independent resistance mechanisms were detected in several populations.

Resistance in the identified hot spot is higher than in other places and it is apparently independent of the frequency of insecticide application in the area, that is not different to the frequency of insecticide application elsewhere.
58.Rationale: The passage explicitly rejects the idea that intense insecticide pressure alone explains the hotspot: resistant populations have appeared in areas with less insecticide pressure and the hotspot's resistance appears independent of application frequency. Therefore resistance is not limited only to areas with highest insecticide use, and this option is not supported.

58.Option: The limited geographic distribution of high-level pyrethroid resistance in T. infestans is due to the absence of pyrethroid insecticides in most of its historical range, preventing resistance development outside the hotspot.
Evidence: Pyrethroid insecticides were introduced into the pest control market by the end of 1970, and were rapidly identified as a major tool for the control of agricultural pests and vectors of human diseases [56].
Pyrethroids were incorporated as a tool for the control of domestic triatomines by mid 1980s.
the main tool for the elimination of house infestation by T. infestans was the application of residual insecticides (particularly pyrethroids).
58.Rationale: The passage indicates pyrethroids have been widely used across the species' range for decades (incorporated in the mid-1980s and used as a principal control tool), so the absence of pyrethroids outside the hotspot is not true. The text instead discusses other causes (genetic variability, population structure, environmental factors), so this option is contradicted by the passage.

58.Option: High-level pyrethroid resistance in T. infestans is geographically restricted due to a combination of pre-existing genetic substructure and environmental conditions that favor resistance persistence in specific regions
Evidence: The accumulation of evidence over the last years suggests that the occurrence of insecticide resistance in T. infestans populations is associated with the high genetic variability detected in the historical dispersion site of the species towards the southern cone of South America [21, 42, 43] and the strong spatial structure of the populations derived by low population dispersal rates [24, 44–45].

The spatial heterogeneity of the LD50 is associated with a combination of 3 temperature- and 2 rainfall-derived environmental variables, as shown by the significant fit of the generalized linear model developed in this study.
Resistance remains high not because of an insecticide-based selection process, but as a natural selection process acting over a population having a naturally high frequency of resistant individuals.

high level of pyrethroid resistance (and other active ingredients, such as fipronil) occurs around the putative center of dispersion of the species, where the genetic variability is very high, and a particular combination of environmental variables exists.
58.Rationale: The passage repeatedly links the hotspot of very high resistance to (1) pre-existing/high genetic variability and strong population structure (limited dispersal), and (2) a specific combination of climatic/environmental variables (temperature and precipitation) that correlate with high LD50. It also argues resistance there is not primarily due to insecticide pressure but to naturally high frequency of tolerant genotypes. Thus the combination of genetic substructure and environmental conditions is the factor best supported by the text.

59.Option: Similarity in the age distribution of hospitalized dengue cases between the two cities, suggesting comparable population susceptibility
Evidence: by using the age distribution of hospitalized cases to estimate the annual probability of infection rather than raw case counts, we can compare disease trends across such locations, even if their surveillance systems are substantially different.
Our model was able to broadly capture the observed age distribution of cases in both cities (Fig 5).
Differences in healthcare utilization by age could bias our results and result in higher or lower force of infection estimates.
59.Rationale: While similarity in the age distribution supports comparable population susceptibility and allows comparable force-of-infection estimation, it does not specifically distinguish between local environmental drivers and inter-city viral movement. The passage frames age distribution as a tool for comparison and notes potential biases (healthcare utilization by age), so age-distribution similarity is supportive but not strong evidence that local environment rather than viral flow explains synchronized epidemic patterns.

59.Option: Lack of synchronization in serotype-specific epidemic patterns despite high overall case correlation
Evidence: This was not, however, accompanied by high correlation in serotype distributions across the two cities.

These findings suggest that the similarity in the temporal patterns of dengue was not a result of significant viral flow between the two cities, which we would expect would synchronize serotype distributions.

We found widely differing levels of correlation by serotype, ranging from high correlation for DENV-1 (the dominant serotype in both locations over the time series) and DENV-3 to no correlation in DENV-2 and negative correlation for DENV-4.
59.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies discordant serotype-specific patterns despite high overall case synchrony as key evidence arguing against substantial inter-city viral movement. If viral flow synchronized epidemics, serotype distributions would be expected to be correlated; the observed lack of serotype synchronization therefore most strongly supports the conclusion that similar local environmental conditions (rather than viral importation) drive the similar epidemic patterns.

59.Option: High spatial clustering of dengue cases at distances under 500 meters in both cities, indicating focal transmission driven by local conditions
Evidence: Serotypes were found to significantly cluster at distances of under 500m in Ho Chi Minh City, similar to that previously estimated in Bangkok using hospitalized dengue cases from 1995 to 1999.

We found that in Ho Chi Minh City cases occurring within a month and 200m of each other were 1.6 times more likely to be homotypic than the probability of any two cases being homotypic at that time point (95% CI: 1.2–2.1).
The magnitude and extent of spatial dependence observed in Ho Chi Minh City were very similar to previously reported results from Bangkok.
59.Rationale: Spatial clustering under 500 m supports focal, locally driven transmission and is consistent with local environmental influences. However, although it is strong supportive evidence for local processes, the passage points to lack of serotype synchronization as the principal evidence excluding inter-city viral movement. Spatial clustering alone does not most strongly distinguish local environment from the possibility of synchronized introduction or movement of viruses, so it is supportive but not the single strongest factor.

59.Option: Similar basic reproductive numbers (R0) estimated for both cities, suggesting comparable transmission potential
Evidence: We used the average force of infection to calculate the basic reproductive number in each city: 3.2 in Bangkok (95% CI: 3.1–3.4) and 3.8 in Ho Chi Minh City (95% CI: 3.6–3.9).
The transmission parameters estimated here were consistent with those found using similar approaches from age-stratified case data from different regions of Thailand.
59.Rationale: Similar R0 values indicate comparable transmission potential but do not discriminate between causes of that similarity. Comparable R0 could result from similar local environments or from frequent viral exchange making epidemics similar; the passage does not present R0 similarity as the primary evidence against inter-city viral flow. Therefore this factor does not most strongly support the conclusion asked in the question.

59.Option: High correlation in the annual number of dengue cases between the two cities, indicating similar overall transmission intensity
Evidence: The number of annual cases was highly correlated across the two cities (correlation of 0.77, 95% CI: 0.74–0.79).
This was not, however, accompanied by high correlation in serotype distributions across the two cities.

These findings suggest that the similarity in the temporal patterns of dengue was not a result of significant viral flow between the two cities, which we would expect would synchronize serotype distributions. Instead, highly comparable local environments ... may drive these similarities.
59.Rationale: High correlation in annual case counts shows similar overall epidemic timing/intensity but is inconclusive about cause: it could reflect either similar local drivers or synchronized viral movement. The passage explicitly notes that high overall correlation without serotype synchronization is why viral flow is unlikely; thus overall case correlation alone is not the strongest evidence favoring local environmental drivers.

60.Option: The limited efficacy of albendazole against T. trichiura and S. stercoralis necessitates the use of alternative drugs, supporting a One Health strategy to improve treatment access
Evidence: albendazole is not effective for this species [8].
Ivermectin also plays an important role in improving T. trichiura control when used in combination with albendazole [9].
As a zoonotic disease, control of A. ceylanicum requires an intersectoral approach, that incorporates One Health strategies as well as preventive chemotherapy [5]
60.Rationale: The passage does report limited efficacy of albendazole for Strongyloides and that ivermectin improves control of T. trichiura, which supports changing drug regimens. However, these drug-efficacy issues point to treatment choice and program scope rather than to One Health (which addresses animal reservoirs and intersectoral measures). The passage explicitly links One Health to zoonotic transmission (A. ceylanicum), not to limitations of albendazole. Therefore the limited albendazole efficacy alone does not most strongly suggest the need for a One Health approach.

60.Option: The high prevalence of open defecation and poor sanitation across villages indicates that environmental contamination is a major driver of STH transmission, requiring integrated WASH and health interventions
Evidence: Responses to the WASH questionnaire indicated that 93.5% of participants practiced open defecation, 20.4% lived in dwellings with a household latrine, 67.9% had access to drinking water from an improved water source, and 15.7% always wore shoes outside.

These extremely high prevalences may be explained by poor access to water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH) facilities in many parts of the country, and exposure to environmental conditions that are favourable for STH development and transmission, in the absence of a national STH control programme.
Improving WASH use and access could complement deworming programmes by protecting against exposure pathways.
As a zoonotic disease, control of A. ceylanicum requires an intersectoral approach, that incorporates One Health strategies as well as preventive chemotherapy [5]
60.Rationale: High open defecation and poor sanitation strongly indicate that environmental contamination by human feces is driving transmission and point to the need for integrated WASH and health interventions. However, the question asks which factor most strongly suggests the need for a One Health approach. One Health specifically implies addressing animal reservoirs and cross-sectoral measures; the sanitation/WASH findings support environmental interventions but do not by themselves implicate animal reservoirs or intersectoral (One Health) measures as the primary driver. Thus this option, while correct in supporting integrated WASH-health interventions, is not the strongest indicator for a One Health approach.

60.Option: The high prevalence of hookworm infections in adults suggests that school-based deworming alone is insufficient, requiring community-wide treatment to control transmission
Evidence: We found that the overall STH prevalence (63.3%) is the highest reported in the country, with previous microscopy-based studies documenting prevalence of up to 52.7% [17–21]. Consistent with two previous surveys using microscopy [17,18], the leading species present was hookworm.

In the analysis of risk factors (Tables 4 and 5), males were more likely to be infected with N. americanus, than females (AOR 1.63, 95% CI 1.13–2.34, p = 0.008), and there was a pattern of increasing risk of infection with age relative to young children ...

Although school-based treatment programs will benefit children, the considerable adult reservoir in the population suggests that treatment should ideally be expanded to entire communities, perhaps integrating with mass drug administration programmes targeting other NTDs.
60.Rationale: The passage documents high hookworm prevalence and increasing infection with age, explicitly recommending expansion from school-based deworming to community-wide treatment because of an adult reservoir. That supports community-wide MDA, but it does not specifically indicate a One Health need. One Health is driven by zoonotic transmission and animal reservoirs (e.g., A. ceylanicum and possibly Strongyloides), whereas adult human reservoirs point to the scope of human-targeted treatment rather than intersectoral animal–human interventions. Therefore while this option is correct about program scope, it is not the strongest indicator for adopting a One Health approach.

60.Option: The association between improved water sources and higher A. ceylanicum infection risk suggests environmental contamination by infected animals, necessitating a One Health approach to interrupt transmission
Evidence: As a zoonotic disease, control of A. ceylanicum requires an intersectoral approach, that incorporates One Health strategies as well as preventive chemotherapy [5] particularly in endemic countries in the Asia-Pacific region where high burdens have been documented [6].

Participants whose main drinking water source was from an improved source had higher odds of A. ceylanicum infection than those whose water was from an unimproved source (AOR 2.71, 95% CI 1.41–5.24, p = 0.003).

This might be due to increased exposure to A. ceylanicum larvae near improved water sources deposited by infected dogs in communities. We did not collect data on STH burden in dogs or contamination of the environment with dog faeces, a key gap for future research to address.
60.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies A. ceylanicum as a zoonosis requiring One Health approaches, and reports a paradoxical association where improved water sources were linked to higher A. ceylanicum infection, plausibly explained by contamination from infected dogs. Those lines of evidence directly implicate an animal reservoir and environmental contamination by animals, which is precisely the rationale for a One Health approach (integrating human, animal, and environmental interventions). Therefore this factor most strongly supports the need for One Health–oriented control measures.

61.Option: The high genetic similarity between West African ZIKV strains and epidemic Asian ZIKV strains suggests a greater potential for adaptation to Aedes albopictus in new regions
Evidence: ZIKV strains are phylogenetically classified as African, Asian and now Asian/American genotypes [3].

ZIKV strains of African genotype were found to display high levels of transmission by Ae. albopictus, pointing at their elevated risk of introduction in regions where this vector has established itself [19,20].
Comparative sequence analysis revealed that ZIKV strains ZIKV-15555 and MR766-NIID share 93% nucleotide similarity and 98.5% amino-acid identity.
61.Rationale: The option posits that high genetic similarity to epidemic Asian strains is the key support for adaptation to Aedes albopictus. The passage does not state a high similarity between West African and epidemic Asian strains; it describes phylogenetic separation of African and Asian genotypes. The more direct and relevant evidence in the passage is an empirical finding that African-genotype strains themselves "display high levels of transmission by Ae. albopictus," which supports vector competence directly rather than inferring it from genetic similarity. Thus the option's specific mechanism (genetic similarity to Asian strains implying adaptation) is not supported by the cited text.

61.Option: The presence of West African ZIKV strains in human populations increases the probability of co-circulation with other flaviviruses, enhancing transmission potential in Aedes albopictus-endemic areas
Evidence: ZIKV circulates in Africa, Asia and more recently in the Americas, where an enzootic transmission cycle appears to involve primates, with the mosquitoes Aedes aegypti and less competent Aedes albopictus as primary vectors [1,2].

ZIKV strains of African genotype were found to display high levels of transmission by Ae. albopictus, pointing at their elevated risk of introduction in regions where this vector has established itself [19,20].
61.Rationale: The passage documents geographic circulation and vector involvement and explicitly notes high transmission of African-genotype ZIKV by Ae. albopictus. However, it does not provide evidence that the mere presence of West African ZIKV in human populations increases co-circulation with other flaviviruses or that such co-circulation enhances transmission by Ae. albopictus. The statement in the option introduces an unsupported causal chain (presence → increased co-circulation → enhanced transmission) that is not described in the passage.

61.Option: The fact that West African ZIKV strains replicate efficiently in human cell lines suggests they are more likely to establish transmission in regions with Aedes albopictus
Evidence: Our data indicated that GUINEA-18 was replicating more slowly, and to lower virus production, in VeroE6 cells compared to MR766MC.
In comparison with MR766MC, virus progeny production of GUINEA-18 was significantly reduced by 3 log at 48 h p.i.
The replicative ability of GUINEA-18 was also attenuated in A549 cells (S5 Fig).
In our study, a comparative analysis with historical African ZIKV strain MR766 showed the contemporary African viral strain ZIKV-15555 as attenuated in vitro.
61.Rationale: This option asserts that West African strains replicate efficiently in human cell lines, but the passage reports the opposite for the contemporary West Africa strain studied (GUINEA-18 / ZIKV-15555): it was attenuated and replicated more slowly with lower virus production in multiple cell lines. Therefore the premise of efficient replication in human cells is not supported by the passage, making the option incorrect.

61.Option: The ability of West African ZIKV strains to cause severe congenital neurological abnormalities in fetuses increases the likelihood of sustained transmission in areas with Aedes albopictus
Evidence: Emergence of Asian ZIKV strains has been associated with polyradiculoneuropathy (Guillain-Barré syndrome) and unprecedented severe complications in fetuses and neonates grouped together under the umbrella term Congenital Zika Syndrome (CZS), with teratogenic effects presenting as microcephaly and other serious neurological abnormalities [1,3–5].
It has been proposed that the greater level of pathogenicity of African ZIKV strains might be the cause of fetal loss rather than neurodevelopmental malformations [19].
61.Rationale: While the passage discusses teratogenic effects and severe fetal outcomes associated with ZIKV (primarily noting Asian-genotype associations) and raises questions about pathogenicity of African strains, it does not link congenital severity to increased likelihood of sustained transmission by vectors such as Ae. albopictus. Severe congenital disease affects disease burden and detection, but there is no evidence presented that it increases vector-borne transmission. Thus the option conflates disease severity with transmission sustainability, which is unsupported by the text.

61.Option: High transmission efficiency of West African ZIKV strains by Aedes albopictus, combined with documented human infection, suggests a heightened risk for introduction and spread in regions where this vector is established
Evidence: ZIKV strains of African genotype were found to display high levels of transmission by Ae. albopictus, pointing at their elevated risk of introduction in regions where this vector has established itself [19,20].

The contemporary West Africa ZIKV strain Faranah/18 (also called ZIKV isolate 15555 or hereafter referred as ZIKV-15555) has been obtained from an individual infected by ZIKV in Republic of Guinea in 2018.
61.Rationale: The passage directly provides the two elements invoked by the option: (1) empirical evidence that African-genotype ZIKV strains "display high levels of transmission by Ae. albopictus," and (2) documentation that the West Africa strain was recovered from a human infection (ZIKV-15555). Together these facts most strongly support the hypothesis that West African ZIKV strains pose a distinct introduction and spread risk in regions where the broadly adaptable and epidemiologically important vector Ae. albopictus is established.

62.Option: The presence of C. hominivorax in the Amazon region is sustained by the high density of human populations and the availability of open wounds in impoverished communities
Evidence: While C. hominivorax caused myiasis in both tropical and temperate regions, disease caused by this species was primarily identified among indigenous populations living in the Andean region where temperature ranges 8 to 18°C.

C. hominivorax abounds in the foothills of the western Andes up to an altitude of 1,250m where it is the primary cause of bovine and ovine infestations [14], but appears also at higher altitudes during warmer months [13].
Risk of myiasis is correlated with densities of fly populations and exposures through poor sanitation and personal hygiene, low socioeconomic status, and proximity to domestic animals.
62.Rationale: The passage indicates C. hominivorax is primarily associated with the Andean foothills and livestock (bovine and ovine infestations), not specifically sustained by high human population density in the Amazon. Although open wounds and poverty increase human risk, the text emphasizes a livestock–environment cycle in the Andean foothills rather than a human-density driven maintenance in the Amazon; thus the option overstates the role of human population density in the Amazon for sustaining C. hominivorax.

62.Option: C. hominivorax persists in the Andes due to its adaptation to high-altitude environments and its ability to infest both livestock and humans in urban centers
Evidence: C. hominivorax abounds in the foothills of the western Andes up to an altitude of 1,250m where it is the primary cause of bovine and ovine infestations [14], but appears also at higher altitudes during warmer months [13].

While C. hominivorax caused myiasis in both tropical and temperate regions, disease caused by this species was primarily identified among indigenous populations living in the Andean region where temperature ranges 8 to 18°C.
Flies of C. hominivorax were also associated with infestations in the homeless and indigent.
62.Rationale: The passage describes C. hominivorax as common in foothills up to ~1,250 m and appearing at higher altitudes during warmer months — this implies temperature-dependent presence rather than stable adaptation to high-altitude environments. The passage highlights livestock reservoirs (bovine/ovine) and associations with indigenous and indigent populations, but does not support the claim that persistence is due to adaptation to high-altitude per se or that urban centers are the primary setting for its livestock–human cycle. Therefore this option is not supported.

62.Option: D. hominis is maintained in tropical and subtropical regions due to its ability to survive in high humidity and its reliance on human hosts for transmission
Evidence: The main species involved in obligate myiasis is D. hominis, which can be found from Mexico through Central and South America. Larvae feed under the skin, causing furuncular lesions with a typical air-pore.

D. hominis was identified in almost half recorded clinical cases for whom identification was done, and occurred in tropical and subtropical regions of the Coast and Amazon. No cases caused by D. hominis were reported from the temperate Andes in accordance with the fact that D. hominis does not survive above an altitude of 1500m.
Risk of myiasis is correlated with densities of fly populations and exposures through poor sanitation and personal hygiene, low socioeconomic status, and proximity to domestic animals.
62.Rationale: While the passage documents that D. hominis occurs in tropical and subtropical regions and does not survive above certain altitudes, it does not state that survival is driven specifically by high humidity, nor that D. hominis relies primarily on human-to-human transmission. The evidence points to altitude/temperature limits and geographic distribution, and mentions proximity to domestic animals as a risk factor generally, but the specific claims about humidity and exclusive reliance on human hosts are not supported by the text.

62.Option: C. hominivorax is maintained in the Andean foothills through a cycle involving livestock infestations, temperature-dependent breeding, and human exposure in settings with poor hygiene
Evidence: C. hominivorax abounds in the foothills of the western Andes up to an altitude of 1,250m where it is the primary cause of bovine and ovine infestations [14], but appears also at higher altitudes during warmer months [13].

While C. hominivorax caused myiasis in both tropical and temperate regions, disease caused by this species was primarily identified among indigenous populations living in the Andean region where temperature ranges 8 to 18°C.
Risk of myiasis is correlated with densities of fly populations and exposures through poor sanitation and personal hygiene, low socioeconomic status, and proximity to domestic animals.
Flies of C. hominivorax were also associated with infestations in the homeless and indigent.
62.Rationale: The passage explicitly links C. hominivorax to livestock infestations in Andean foothills (bovine and ovine), notes that its presence varies with altitude and warmer months (temperature-dependent breeding/seasonality), and identifies poor sanitation/low socioeconomic status/proximity to domestic animals as risk factors for human exposure. Together these statements support the hypothesis that C. hominivorax is maintained in a local ecological cycle involving livestock reservoirs, environmental (temperature/altitude) constraints, and human exposure in settings of poor hygiene.

62.Option: D. hominis maintains its population in the Amazon through a cycle involving human-to-human transmission and seasonal temperature fluctuations
Evidence: D. hominis was identified in almost half recorded clinical cases for whom identification was done, and occurred in tropical and subtropical regions of the Coast and Amazon. No cases caused by D. hominis were reported from the temperate Andes in accordance with the fact that D. hominis does not survive above an altitude of 1500m.
Climatic changes due to global warming could modify the distribution of both flies resulting in future infestations in areas that are presently too cold to sustain these fly species.
62.Rationale: The passage supports a role for geographic/temperature limits and seasonal or climate-driven changes in distribution for D. hominis, but it does not describe human-to-human transmission as a maintenance mechanism. D. hominis is described as an obligate myiasis agent affecting humans (larvae feed under skin), but no evidence is provided for direct human-to-human transmission cycles. Thus the claim of human-to-human transmission is unsupported, making the option incorrect.

63.Option: The dynamic adjustment of release ratios and timing based on seasonal population fluctuations to maintain competitive advantage over wild males
Evidence: For the population suppression strategy to be successful, the number and ratio of released male mosquitoes carrying Wolbachia is critical, primarily due to the need for sufficient Wolbachia-transinfected males to compete with wild males.
Release frequency, distribution of wild populations, and longevity of released males also affect this release ratio.
Therefore, selecting the correct release ratio is an important factor for control strategy success.
Adjusting the number of males released according to environmental or climatic factors is also critical.

For example, during the rainy season, mosquito populations usually increase. It may be helpful to increase the number of males released a few weeks after heavy rain to increase the reproductive competitive advantage of Wolbachia-transinfected males in the wild [37].

In our semi-field experiment, a 3:1 ratio was sufficient to reach 70% suppression, although this suppression rate did not increase with ratios of 5:1 or 10:1 (Fig 7). This suggests that a 3:1 ratio will be a suitable starting point for future release trials.
63.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies the number and ratio of released males and the need to adjust releases by environmental/climatic conditions (e.g., rainy season) as critical to success. It recommends adjusting the number and timing of releases to match seasonal population fluctuations to preserve the competitive advantage of released males. Therefore, dynamically adjusting release ratios and timing is the most critical factor for designing effective suppression-release protocols.

63.Option: Maintaining a fixed release ratio of 10:1 to ensure complete sterility in wild female mosquitoes regardless of environmental conditions
Evidence: Under laboratory conditions, we determined the optimal release ratio of sterile Wolbachia-transinfected male mosquitoes to be 10:1 for wAlbB-Tw males to compete with wild type males and introduce complete or nearly complete sterility in wild type females.

In our semi-field experiment, a 3:1 ratio was sufficient to reach 70% suppression, although this suppression rate did not increase with ratios of 5:1 or 10:1 (Fig 7). This suggests that a 3:1 ratio will be a suitable starting point for future release trials.
Adjusting the number of males released according to environmental or climatic factors is also critical.
63.Rationale: Although a 10:1 ratio produced complete sterility under laboratory conditions, the passage shows that semi-field results differed (3:1 yielded substantial suppression) and emphasizes that release ratios should be selected and adjusted based on local and environmental conditions. Thus maintaining a fixed 10:1 ratio 'regardless of environmental conditions' is not supported and could be suboptimal.

63.Option: Releasing Wolbachia-infected males at a constant frequency regardless of mosquito population density to maintain consistent suppression pressure
Evidence: Release frequency, distribution of wild populations, and longevity of released males also affect this release ratio.
Adjusting the number of males released according to environmental or climatic factors is also critical.

For example, during the rainy season, mosquito populations usually increase. It may be helpful to increase the number of males released a few weeks after heavy rain to increase the reproductive competitive advantage of Wolbachia-transinfected males in the wild [37].

For the population suppression strategy to be successful, the number and ratio of released male mosquitoes carrying Wolbachia is critical, primarily due to the need for sufficient Wolbachia-transinfected males to compete with wild males.
63.Rationale: The passage indicates that release frequency and the number released must be tailored to population density and environmental changes (e.g., rainy season increases mosquito populations). Therefore a constant-frequency release regardless of density contradicts the recommended adaptive approach and is not the critical factor for effective reduction.

63.Option: Using insecticide application in conjunction with Wolbachia release to enhance suppression by killing both wild and transinfected mosquitoes
Evidence: As shown in Fig 2, we found different responses to some insecticides between mosquito lines, suggesting genetic variation.

Using high concentrations of insecticides, there were no obvious differences in knockdown rate and mortality between wAlbB-Tw and uninfected strains (WT). Using low concentrations treatments can however cause differences to appear, with wAlbB-Tw seemingly not resistant to insecticides (Fig 2).

These results suggest that Wolbachia transinfection should not influence the chemical control of Ae. aegypti. However, the current epidemic prevention policy still relies on insecticides to target mosquito populations. This is unfavorable for wAlbB-Tw, which is not resistant to insecticides.

In order for Taiwan to implement a large-scale release of wAlbB-Tw mosquitoes in the future, the spraying of insecticides in the release area may need to be at least partially restricted so that Wolbachia can effectively show long-term effects of population suppression.
63.Rationale: The passage warns that insecticide spraying can be unfavorable to the Wolbachia suppression strategy because released wAlbB-Tw males may be susceptible and spraying could reduce their effectiveness; it even suggests restricting spraying in release areas to allow Wolbachia effects to manifest. Thus combining insecticide application with releases is not presented as the critical or recommended design factor.

63.Option: Prioritizing the release of Wolbachia-infected females to maximize the spread of the symbiont and achieve population replacement
Evidence: There are two strategies for using Wolbachia in vector-borne disease control: (1) population suppression by only releasing male mosquitoes, which reduces the population density of field mosquito vectors and decreases disease transmission; and (2) population replacement by releasing males and females together to replace field mosquito vector populations with Wolbachia-transinfected populations to inhibit virus spread.

For the population suppression strategy to be successful, the number and ratio of released male mosquitoes carrying Wolbachia is critical, primarily due to the need for sufficient Wolbachia-transinfected males to compete with wild males.

releasing only male mosquitoes carrying Wolbachia would be more acceptable from a community perspective because male mosquitoes do not suck blood and can gradually reduce the number of mosquitoes in the wild. This is not the case for the replacement strategy, as female and male mosquitoes carrying Wolbachia are released together.
63.Rationale: The question specifies a method relying on CI induced by Wolbachia-infected males to produce infertile offspring (a suppression approach). Prioritizing release of infected females would shift toward a population-replacement strategy, which is a different objective and has different design considerations and acceptability issues. The passage emphasizes male-only releases for suppression, so releasing females is not the critical factor for the suppression protocol described.

64.Option: A sustained increase in the number of differentially expressed miRNAs from 1 to 12 weeks, with enrichment in immune response and inflammatory signaling pathways
Evidence: A total of 67, 154 and 53 miRNAs were differentially expressed in mice livers at 1, 4 and 12 wpe, respectively (Figs 2A, 2B, 2C and S1).

KEGG analyses indicated that 15 pathways related to neural activity, 6 pathways related to food digestion, 20 pathways related to immune response, and 17 pathways related to cancer, were subject to regulation by the DEmiRNAs (Table 2). Note that the 4 wpe group has the most DEmiRNA or DEmiRNA targets that mapped to neuro, digesting, immune and cancer pathways (Table 2 and Fig 4).
64.Rationale: The option asserts a sustained increase from 1 to 12 weeks and immune enrichment. The passage reports 67 → 154 → 53 DEmiRNAs, so there is not a sustained increase to 12 weeks (there is a decline at 12 wpe). While immune-response pathways are enriched, that enrichment is concentrated at 4 wpe (the 4 wpe group 'has the most DEmiRNA or DEmiRNA targets' mapped to immune pathways), not as a sustained pattern through 12 weeks. Therefore the stated pattern is not supported by the data.

64.Option: A decline in the number of differentially expressed miRNAs at 12 weeks, coupled with enrichment in cell cycle and DNA damage response pathways and upregulation of miRNAs linked to cellular proliferation
Evidence: A total of 67, 154 and 53 miRNAs were differentially expressed in mice livers at 1, 4 and 12 wpe, respectively (Figs 2A, 2B, 2C and S1).
After a longer period of FgESPs’ exposure (12 weeks), there were 31 miRNAs upregulated and 22 miRNAs downregulated.

In contrast, most enriched GO terms of 12 wpe were mainly involved in organelles, cellular component organization or biogenesis, DNA integrity checkpoint, DNA damage checkpoint and cell cycle process (Fig 3C).

DEmiRNAs at 12 wpe were much fewer in number, and most of them were upregulated and enriched in organelles, cellular component organization or biogenesis, cell cycle process and DNA damage related GO terms.

Only 5 miRNAs (including miR-126a-3p, miR-150-5p, miR-155-5p, miR-181a-5p and miR-362-3p) were commonly dysregulated among treated livers at all three time points compared to the untreated controls, revealing their important roles in the interplay between liver and FgESPs. One interesting finding is that all commonly dysregulated miRNA regulate the cellular proliferation. ... the upregulation of miR-150-5p, miR-181a-5p, miR-155-5p and miR-362-3p was associated with cellular migration and/or cellular proliferation.
64.Rationale: This option matches the reported pattern: the number of DEmiRNAs peaks at 4 weeks then declines by 12 weeks (67 → 154 → 53), and the authors explicitly state that 12 wpe DEmiRNAs are fewer, mostly upregulated, and enriched in GO categories involving cellular component organization, DNA damage checkpoints and cell cycle processes. They also note that commonly dysregulated miRNAs regulate cellular proliferation and several are upregulated, consistent with a shift from an acute immune response toward processes consistent with tissue remodeling and proliferative/cellular-repair programs at the later time point.

64.Option: A shift from upregulation of miRNAs at 1 week to downregulation at 12 weeks, with enrichment in cancer-related pathways, indicating a transition to oncogenic transformation
Evidence: Among these, 32 upregulated- and 35 downregulated- hepatic miRNAs showed statistically significant after one week’s exposure, while 76 upregulated miRNAs and 78 downregulated miRNAs were identified at 4 wpe. After a longer period of FgESPs’ exposure (12 weeks), there were 31 miRNAs upregulated and 22 miRNAs downregulated.

KEGG analyses indicated that 15 pathways related to neural activity, 6 pathways related to food digestion, 20 pathways related to immune response, and 17 pathways related to cancer, were subject to regulation by the DEmiRNAs (Table 2). Note that the 4 wpe group has the most DEmiRNA or DEmiRNA targets that mapped to neuro, digesting, immune and cancer pathways (Table 2 and Fig 4).
Although some DEmiRNAs participate in carcinogenic pathways, at present, no evidence is sufficient to conclude the correlation between FgESPs and carcinogenesis in the liver.
64.Rationale: The option claims a shift to downregulation at 12 weeks, but the passage reports that at 12 wpe most DEmiRNAs were upregulated (31 up vs 22 down). Cancer-related pathway enrichment is reported, but primarily in the 4 wpe group, and the authors explicitly state that the data are insufficient to conclude a causal link to carcinogenesis. Therefore the proposed pattern and inference to oncogenic transformation are not supported.

64.Option: A consistent upregulation of miRNAs involved in neural activity and synaptic function across all time points, indicating persistent neurotropic effects of FgESPs
Evidence: KEGG analyses indicated that 15 pathways related to neural activity, 6 pathways related to food digestion, 20 pathways related to immune response, and 17 pathways related to cancer, were subject to regulation by the DEmiRNAs (Table 2). Note that the 4 wpe group has the most DEmiRNA or DEmiRNA targets that mapped to neuro, digesting, immune and cancer pathways (Table 2 and Fig 4).
As shown in Fig 4A, the predicted targets of hepatic DEmiRNAs induced by 4-week exposure to FgESPs in the mice were enriched in 15 neural activity-related pathways (listed in Table 2).

PLS-DA modeling on miRNAs from three groups demonstrated that loading dots representing individual FgESPs-treated or untreated samples in 1 wpe group and 4 wpe group, were separated in different quadrants of the diagram, which suggested that the FgESPs induced distinct miRNA profiles during the early stage of immunization. In contrast, loading dots of treated samples and control samples from 12 wpe group were distributed in the close area and mapped to boundary between first quartile and third quartile.
64.Rationale: Neural/synaptic pathway enrichment is documented, but it is concentrated at 4 weeks (the 4 wpe group 'has the most DEmiRNA... mapped to' neural pathways and Fig 4A describes 4-week enrichment). The PLS-DA also indicates distinct early profiles with 12-week treated samples closer to controls. The passage does not report consistent upregulation of neural-related miRNAs across all time points, so the claim of persistent neurotropic effects across the entire time course is not supported.

64.Option: A progressive increase in the number of downregulated miRNAs from 1 to 12 weeks, with enrichment in pathways related to bile secretion and digestion, indicating worsening hepatic dysfunction
Evidence: A total of 67, 154 and 53 miRNAs were differentially expressed in mice livers at 1, 4 and 12 wpe, respectively (Figs 2A, 2B, 2C and S1).

Among these, 32 upregulated- and 35 downregulated- hepatic miRNAs showed statistically significant after one week’s exposure, while 76 upregulated miRNAs and 78 downregulated miRNAs were identified at 4 wpe. After a longer period of FgESPs’ exposure (12 weeks), there were 31 miRNAs upregulated and 22 miRNAs downregulated.

Fig 4B shows the digestion related pathways were enriched by target genes of DEmiRNAs relative to functions such as bile secretion, insulin secretion, insulin signaling pathway, gastric acid secretion, salivary secretion, and pancreatic secretion (listed in Table 2).

DEmiRNAs at 12 wpe were much fewer in number, and most of them were upregulated and enriched in organelles, cellular component organization or biogenesis, cell cycle process and DNA damage related GO terms.
64.Rationale: The option asserts a progressive increase in downregulated miRNAs through 12 weeks, but the reported counts are 35 down at 1 wpe, 78 down at 4 wpe, then only 22 down at 12 wpe—so there is not a progressive increase to 12 weeks. Digestion and bile secretion pathways are enriched (notably at 4 wpe), but the data indicate these effects are strongest at 4 weeks rather than showing progressive worsening through 12 weeks. Additionally, 12-week changes are characterized more by cell-cycle/DNA damage signals and fewer DEmiRNAs overall, inconsistent with the option's pattern.

Evidence: BMI is a measure of nutritional status in adults [11].
Undernutrition, as defined by low body mass index, showed a striking association with leprosy in this small case control study with an aOR of more than 10.
Since all these studies are taken at one point in time, it is not possible to know whether the undernutrition predated the infection or not.
65.Rationale: Low BMI is an individual-level measure of nutritional status, and the passage documents its strong association with leprosy. However, the question asks for the factor that most directly reflects the interplay between individual nutritional status and broader structural conditions. Low BMI itself reflects the individual state but does not directly capture the structural drivers (poverty, food access) that produce that state. The passage also notes the temporal ambiguity (cross-sectional design), limiting BMI’s ability to demonstrate the pathway from structural conditions to nutritional status to disease.

Evidence: food insecurity has been associated with leprosy [7–10].
Another important outcome of this study revealed that food insecurity, measured by having insufficient funds for meals, was also associated with leprosy on univariate analysis.
Low income families have little money to spend on food and consequently have a low intake of highly nutritious non-rice foods such as meat, fish, milk, eggs, fruits and vegetables.

Addressing the interplay between poverty, undernutrition, dietary habits, and nutritional deficiencies (as shown in Fig 3) may have the potential for impact on leprosy burden in this region and similar endemic areas.
65.Rationale: Food insecurity explicitly links structural conditions (insufficient funds, poverty, limited access to nutritious foods) with individual nutritional consequences and was measured and associated with leprosy in this study. The passage frames food insecurity as both a marker of broader socioeconomic constraints and a proximate determinant of dietary intake and undernutrition, making it the factor that most directly reflects the interplay asked about in the question.

Evidence: Low income families have little money to spend on food and consequently have a low intake of highly nutritious non-rice foods such as meat, fish, milk, eggs, fruits and vegetables.
Due to the fact that poverty is such a broad and complex topic to characterize, a causal relationship between poverty and leprosy is difficult to demonstrate.
we have ... controlling for age, sex, and education (as a surrogate marker of socioeconomic status).
65.Rationale: Low income is a structural determinant that contributes to food insecurity and undernutrition, and the passage explicitly links low income to limited access to nutritious foods. However, the question asks for the factor that most directly reflects the interplay between individual nutritional status and broader structural conditions; low income is a more distal structural factor. The passage treats low income/poverty as complex and often proxied (e.g., by education), whereas food insecurity is the more proximate measure tying structure to individual nutrition.

65.Option: Cutting the size of meals or skipping meals
Evidence: Cutting the size of meals/skipping meals (OR = 2.87, 95% CI 1.0, 8.32) or not having enough money to get more food (OR = 10, 95% CI 3.44, 29.06) was more common in cases of leprosy than controls.
Another important outcome of this study revealed that food insecurity, measured by having insufficient funds for meals, was also associated with leprosy on univariate analysis.
65.Rationale: Cutting or skipping meals is an individual behavior that indicates inadequate food intake and therefore relates to nutritional status. However, it is a specific manifestation of food insecurity rather than the broader structural measure itself. The passage treats such behaviors as consequences of insufficient funds/food insecurity; thus, while relevant, they are less directly inclusive of the broader structural context than the explicit food insecurity measure.

Evidence: we hypothesize that undernutrition is a risk factor for leprosy when controlling for S. mansoni co-infection (a highly prevalent helminth that also alters cell-mediated immunity) and socioeconomic status (SES).
about 21 participants (25.9%) in this study presented with S. mansoni infection.

Although our study did not find an association with S. mansoni infection and leprosy, further investigations into co-infections and the influence on immune response mechanisms is needed to better understand this interaction with leprosy
65.Rationale: S. mansoni co-infection is an immunomodulatory biological factor that could affect susceptibility to clinical leprosy, but it does not represent the structural-social drivers of nutrition or directly reflect individual nutritional status. The passage treats it as a potential confounder/effect modifier to control for, not as the structural–nutritional interplay the question asks about.

66.Option: Perceived incompatibility with school schedules reduces the likelihood of treatment receipt, particularly in low-coverage clusters
Evidence: Findings are validated by coverage surveys, which indicate that an average of 97% (Benin), 86% (India), and 77% (Malawi) of survey participants preferred door-to-door distribution over school-based or centralized distribution.
The door-to-door initiative has helped those who can’t afford to reach to schools and hospitals or even where such drug distribution events are happening especially the elderly and children…
66.Rationale: While the passage shows a general preference for door-to-door delivery over school-based approaches (suggesting school-based delivery may be less convenient for some), it does not present evidence that incompatibility specifically with school schedules is the principal driver of missed treatment in the described settings. The text emphasizes broader convenience and access barriers and gives a concrete example of missed treatment due to economic activities (people leaving early to work), not school schedule conflicts. Therefore this option is not directly supported as the most relevant factor.

66.Option: Perceived incompatibility with economic activities reduces the likelihood of treatment receipt, particularly in low-coverage clusters
Evidence: "Some participants in LCCs in Malawi indicated that cMDA was disruptive to their daily activities as it was conducted when people were unavailable due to economic activity (intervention complexity)."
"[A] lot of people leave their homes early in the morning to do business, work in the fields such that when the people come, they don’t find them." Respondent 2, Women (LCCs), Malawi

Findings that differentiate between high- and low-coverage clusters.Some participants in LCCs in Malawi indicated that cMDA was disruptive to their daily activities as it was conducted when people were unavailable due to economic activity (intervention complexity). This was a deviant finding.
66.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies economic activities (people leaving early for business/fields) as a reason individuals were unavailable for door-to-door cMDA, and it notes this finding as differentiating low-coverage clusters in Malawi. These verbatim quotes directly link perceived incompatibility with daily economic routines to reduced likelihood of receiving treatment, supporting this option as the most directly influential factor.

66.Option: Perceived incompatibility with household responsibilities reduces the likelihood of treatment receipt, particularly in low-coverage clusters
Evidence: In India and Malawi, participants reported that cMDA successfully reached women. Women were more likely to be home during distribution and to receive outreach messages.

"Some men may be missed out because they will not be there at that time in the house. When it is like that, they come and give the next day. If they are not available also at that time, some people may be left out. For ladies, no one is missed out" Respondent 1, Women (HCCs), India
66.Rationale: The passage indicates that household presence (women being at home) actually made it more likely they were reached by cMDA, and it identifies men being away for work as a common reason for missed treatment. There is no direct evidence that incompatibility with household responsibilities (e.g., caregiving tasks) reduces treatment receipt; instead, household roles are described as facilitating reach for some groups. Thus this option is not supported as the most direct factor.

66.Option: Perceived incompatibility with religious practices reduces the likelihood of treatment receipt, particularly in low-coverage clusters
Evidence: Village leaders include administrative (e.g., village chiefs) and religious leaders (e.g., Sheikhs) identified by the study team and villages and were purposively selected by the study team.

"When she refused, a traditional religious leader came and told her that no one should refuse treatment. Whatever your religion, if you are sick, you must do the treatment by taking drugs or injections." Respondent 1, Men (LCC), Benin
66.Rationale: The passage refers to religious leaders being engaged to encourage participation and gives an example where a religious leader convinced someone to accept treatment; it does not describe religious practices themselves conflicting with delivery timing or being a principal reason for missed treatment. Therefore incompatibility with religious practices is not supported as the most direct influence in the contexts described.

66.Option: Perceived incompatibility with transportation logistics reduces the likelihood of treatment receipt, particularly in low-coverage clusters
Evidence: Across all sites, participants reported that door-to-door delivery reduced time and costs associated with adults proactively seeking deworming treatment from health centers or private pharmacies.

"The door-to-door initiative has helped those who can’t afford to reach to schools and hospitals or even where such drug distribution events are happening especially the elderly and children…" Respondent 5, Men (HCCs), Malawi
66.Rationale: Transportation and access barriers are discussed and door-to-door delivery is presented as a solution that reduces such barriers. However, the specific issue highlighted as causing missed treatment when delivery timing was incompatible with daily routines was economic activity (people being away for work), not transportation logistics. Thus transportation incompatibility is not identified as the most direct factor in the passage.

67.Option: The higher seroprevalence in South India is due to a higher proportion of women with multiple pregnancies, which increases exposure to the parasite through repeated contact with infected fetuses or placental tissue
Evidence: Most (74.2%) of the 328 seropositive pregnant women were multigravida and only a quarter (25.8%) were primigravida (Figure 5, Flow chart 2).

This higher prevalence was not associated with the number of gravida per se but rather to higher mean age [27.8±5.9 (range 18–45 yr.)] of those who were married than those who were unmarried/single women [22.4±3.8 (range 18–41 yr.)].
67.Rationale: Although many seropositive women were multigravida, the authors explicitly state that higher prevalence was not associated with the number of gravida per se but rather with higher mean age. Therefore attributing regional differences primarily to multiple pregnancies is not supported by the study's findings.

67.Option: The combination of favorable environmental conditions for oocyst survival, high cat ownership, and barefoot cultural practices in South India, contrasted with arid climate and shoe-wearing norms in West India, best explains the regional disparity in seroprevalence
Evidence: The difference in the prevalence rate between women from South and West India was highly significant. It could be mainly due to socio-cultural and climatic factors.
The climatic conditions in South India favour sustenance and proliferation of Toxoplasma oocysts. Also a highly significant number of households owned cats in this region.

Moreover, as a social culture, South Indians do not wear shoes and most often are barefoot or wear sleepers only. This might increase chances of transferring T. gondii oocysts from soil and water to their food [9]–[13].

Western India, on the other hand, is a dry arid climatic zone where temperature in May-June averages 46°C. Socio-culturally also, the population in West India must wear shoes due to the high temperature and sandy soil. These climatic conditions are detrimental to T. gondii to maintain its life cycle.
67.Rationale: The authors identify environmental (climate favoring oocyst survival), high cat ownership (source of oocysts), and barefoot cultural practices (increasing fecal–oral transfer risk) in South India versus arid climate and shoe-wearing in West India as the primary explanations for the regional prevalence differences. These specific factors are presented as the main plausible causes in the text.

67.Option: The regional variation in seroprevalence is best explained by differences in socioeconomic status, with lower-income women in South India having higher exposure due to poorer hygiene practices
Evidence: Most women (97.1%) belonged to low or lower middle income groups.
The difference in the prevalence rate between women from South and West India was highly significant. It could be mainly due to socio-cultural and climatic factors.
The climatic conditions in South India favour sustenance and proliferation of Toxoplasma oocysts. Also a highly significant number of households owned cats in this region.
67.Rationale: Although socioeconomic and living-conditions are described, the study notes that nearly all participants were low or lower-middle income, and the authors emphasize socio-cultural and climatic explanations (cats, climate, barefoot practices, water/food factors) rather than socioeconomic status as the primary driver of regional differences. The passage does not support socioeconomic status as the main explanation.

67.Option: The higher seroprevalence in South India is primarily due to increased consumption of raw or undercooked meat, which is more common in that region compared to West India
Evidence: Although consumption of raw/undercooked meat and exposure to soil through farming or gardening have been associated with a higher risk of infection in various studies [1], [4], [21], no such correlation was observed in the multivariate analysis done in the present study.
In India, consumption of raw or undercooked meat is extremely rare, hence this route of infection is theoretically negligible.
67.Rationale: The authors specifically report no correlation between raw/undercooked meat consumption and infection in their multivariate analysis and state that such consumption is extremely rare in India. Thus increased meat consumption does not explain the higher seroprevalence in South India according to the study.

67.Option: The regional variation in seroprevalence is primarily driven by differences in access to healthcare and diagnostic testing, with South Indian women receiving more frequent screening and thus higher detection rates
Evidence: The difference in the prevalence rate between women from South and West India was highly significant. It could be mainly due to socio-cultural and climatic factors.

To represent 4 geographical regions of India, a total of 1772 women were enrolled. Female volunteers from the hospital staff, as well as from the local community, colleges, and antenatal clinics were recruited in the study.
Most women (97.1%) belonged to low or lower middle income groups.
67.Rationale: The passage attributes regional differences to environmental and socio-cultural factors rather than differential screening or diagnostic access. Recruitment methods were similar across regions (community and clinic volunteers), and the authors do not suggest that increased testing in South India explains higher prevalence. Therefore differences in access to healthcare/diagnostic testing are not supported as the primary driver by the study text.

68.Option: The presence of LASV-infected rodents in kitchens and bedrooms, along with a high proportion of adult animals, indicates that the virus is being maintained in a stable reservoir population with consistent human exposure risk.
Evidence: all rodents with evidence of LASV infection were captured in peridomestic settings, most frequently in kitchens and bedrooms of human dwellings.
The majority of these animals were adult (41 of 48, 85.4%).
Combining the results of serological and molecular testing, a total of 48 (19.4%) rodents, all M. natalensis, demonstrated evidence of LASV infection.
With these conditions, it seems likely that people are being exposed to LASV.
68.Rationale: The option correctly links two findings reported in the study: infected rodents were found inside human dwellings (kitchens and bedrooms), and most infected rodents were adults. Together with the quantified burden of infection (48/246, 19.4% of M. natalensis showing evidence of infection), these observations support maintenance of LASV in a stable, peridomestic reservoir population and imply consistent opportunities for human exposure. The quoted lines explicitly state the peridomestic capture locations, the adult-dominated infected group, the combined infection prevalence, and the likely human exposure, all of which substantiate the option.

68.Option: The high seroprevalence of anti-LASV antibodies in adult M. natalensis, combined with the detection of viral RNA in multiple villages, suggests that the virus is widely circulating and has established a stable endemic presence in the rodent population.
Evidence: Of the 511 serum samples from M. natalensis, a total of 35 were positive for an overall seroprevalence of 6.8%. Geographically, serological evidence of LASV infection was limited to southern Mali, specifically the villages near Soromba, where the overall prevalence increased to 14.2% (35 of 246).

Tissue samples were collected from 591 rodents and tested for the presence of LASV RNA by real-time and conventional RT-PCR assays. Similar to the serological results, molecular evidence of LASV infection was only found in rodents collected in southern Mali with a total of 19 positive animals identified for an overall prevalence of 7.7% (19 of 246).
Prevalence rates for individual villages ranged from 0 to 24% (Table 2).
Combining the results of serological and molecular testing, a total of 48 (19.4%) rodents, all M. natalensis, demonstrated evidence of LASV infection.
68.Rationale: This option is supported by the reported serological and molecular data. The study documents measurable seroprevalence concentrated in southern Mali (up to 14.2% in the Soromba area) and RT-PCR detection across villages (up to 24% in some villages), and the combined infection evidence of 19.4% in M. natalensis. These findings indicate active circulation across multiple localities and are consistent with LASV being established endemically in the local rodent population.

68.Option: The high proportion of adult M. natalensis in peridomestic settings, along with the detection of LASV in multiple villages, indicates that the virus is endemic and poses a continuous risk of human spillover due to sustained rodent-human contact.
Evidence: The overwhelming majority (>99%) of rodents captured in this area were genetically identified as M. natalensis. In contrast, little or no rodent diversity was observed in the southern regions of Mali around the village of Soromba where, during three field expeditions, all but two animals captured in these areas were M. natalensis (246/248, 99.2%).
all rodents with evidence of LASV infection were captured in peridomestic settings, most frequently in kitchens and bedrooms of human dwellings.

Tissue samples...molecular evidence of LASV infection was only found in rodents collected in southern Mali with a total of 19 positive animals identified for an overall prevalence of 7.7% (19 of 246). Prevalence rates for individual villages ranged from 0 to 24% (Table 2).
The lack of other rodents in these villages may support the endemic nature of LASV, since low biodiversity tends to increase pathogen transmission and subsequently the incidence of human disease.
68.Rationale: The option synthesizes several ecological observations reported in the study: dominance of M. natalensis in the affected villages (99.2%), peridomestic location of infected animals, and molecular detection of LASV across multiple villages. The passage explicitly notes that low rodent biodiversity may support endemic LASV circulation. Together these data justify concluding the virus is endemic in rodent hosts in southern Mali and that sustained close rodent–human contact in peridomestic settings poses ongoing spillover risk.

68.Option: The co-occurrence of RT-PCR-positive, seronegative rodents and a high proportion of adult M. natalensis in peridomestic environments indicates active viral replication and sustained transmission in a reservoir population closely associated with human habitation.
Evidence: The first group (RT-PCR positive, serologically negative) most likely represents recently infected animals in which the virus is actively replicating but the host has not yet mounted a measurable humoral response.
we anticipate this group of rodents represents the greatest threat to human exposure due to unchecked viral replication which presumably would lead to increased shedding.
all rodents with evidence of LASV infection were captured in peridomestic settings, most frequently in kitchens and bedrooms of human dwellings.
The majority of these animals were adult (41 of 48, 85.4%).
68.Rationale: The presence of RT-PCR-positive/seronegative animals is interpreted in the study as recent infections with active viral replication and higher shedding potential, directly supporting active transmission in the reservoir. Coupled with the finding that most infected animals were adults and were captured inside human dwellings, these observations indicate sustained transmission among peridomestic M. natalensis and an elevated risk for human exposure—precisely the scenario described in the option.

69.Option: The random assignment of compounds to wells to balance baseline viability across treatment groups
Evidence: The library was a subset of the National Center for Advancing Translational Sciences (NCATS) Pharmaceutical Collection of approved and investigational drugs [15]. This subset was comprised of 340 drugs approved for human use in Europe, Canada, Japan and Australia, excluding cancer drugs. The compounds were dispensed in a 384-well plate, 30 μL at 1–20 mM concentration in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) solution.
Compounds were tested in single replicates and controls in duplicates.
69.Rationale: The passage does not describe random assignment of compounds to wells as a design feature; it states compounds were dispensed in plates and explicitly notes that compounds were tested in single replicates (with controls in duplicates), which suggests no described procedure to randomize or balance baseline viability across groups. Because random assignment is not documented in the methods, we cannot treat it as the feature that enabled causal inference or control for time-dependent confounding in this study.

69.Option: The use of a single time point for viability scoring immediately after compound exposure to capture the acute effect
Evidence: On the day of testing, 21–42 day-old juveniles (depending on a study), liver stages (early and late) were scored before the addition of compounds, corresponding to time point 0 hours before drug treatment.

For primary and confirmatory screening, after the addition of compounds, worms were incubated at 37°C, 5% CO2, and scored after 3 hours (h), 24 hours, and day 7 post-treatment using an inverted Axiovert 10 microscope (Zeiss) at 10x magnification.

For primary screening, juveniles were scored before the addition of controls (= 0 hours), and 3 h and 24 h post-treatment (p.t). After 24 h incubation, controls were removed from wells and replaced with culture medium... Recovery of juveniles was assessed on day 7 (168 h) from the initial drug screening.
69.Rationale: The study did not rely on a single immediate post-exposure time point; it explicitly measured viability at multiple time points (0 h, 3 h, 24 h, and day 7). A single immediate time point would not capture recovery or delayed effects and therefore would not be the design feature used here to minimize time-dependent confounding.

69.Option: The inclusion of a washout period followed by post-exposure viability assessment at day 7
Evidence: Drug wash-out after a 24-hour incubation time

To monitor the effect of compounds on worm cultures, i.e. reversible or irreversible, compounds and controls were removed after a 24-hour exposure from juvenile and adult worm cultures during primary and confirmatory screenings.

After the third wash, the supernatant was removed, and the culture medium was added to each well. Plates were returned to the incubator at 37°C, 5% CO2. The final viability score as a measure of recovery was done on day 7 and day 3 from the initial screening for juvenile and adult worms, respectively.

Another advantage of our drug screening platform is that compounds are removed after 24 hours to be more representative of potential drug exposures, rather than continuous exposure of NTS and adults over the course of testing.
69.Rationale: Removing compounds after a fixed 24-hour exposure and then assessing viability later (day 7 for juveniles, day 3 for adults) directly isolates the effect of a defined exposure period and distinguishes reversible from irreversible effects. This washout plus post-exposure assessment minimizes confounding due to continuous or accumulating time-dependent exposure (e.g., prolonged drug accumulation or steadily changing culture conditions) because all wells experience the same controlled exposure duration and subsequent recovery interval. That design most directly supports causal interpretation of compound effects while reducing time-dependent confounding.

69.Option: The inclusion of multiple time points for viability scoring to capture the full temporal profile of compound effects
Evidence: For primary and confirmatory screening, after the addition of compounds, worms were incubated at 37°C, 5% CO2, and scored after 3 hours (h), 24 hours, and day 7 post-treatment using an inverted Axiovert 10 microscope (Zeiss) at 10x magnification.

On the day of testing, 21–42 day-old juveniles (depending on a study), liver stages (early and late) were scored before the addition of compounds, corresponding to time point 0 hours before drug treatment.
Z’ and SSMD were calculated for each screening time point (0 h, 3 h, 24 h, and day 7) for juvenile and adult worms.
