69.Rationale: While measuring multiple time points does help characterize the temporal profile and detect early versus late effects, the question asks which feature most directly enables causal assessment while minimizing confounding from time-dependent factors. Multiple time points are informative but do not by themselves remove confounding from continuous exposure or other time-dependent processes. The washout/post-exposure recovery design more directly controls exposure duration and thus better minimizes time-dependent confounding.

69.Option: The use of a positive control (PZQ) and negative control (DMSO) to standardize the assay across all time points
Evidence: PZQ (Cat. No. PHR1391 Sigma-Aldrich) served as a positive control, while DMSO (Cat. No. D8418 Sigma) was used as a negative control.

In the establishment of primary screening platform, two controls were selected. PZQ at 30 μM served as a positive control and DMSO at 0.3% v/v (representing the highest concentration of solvent). Assay performance was monitored by the inclusion of positive and negative controls on each plate and applying the same conditions as for compounds, i.e., PZQ and DMSO were added to cultures and viability scoring was monitored at 3 h and 24 h p.t (Fig 5).
Z’ ranged from 0.4–0.8 and SSMD between 7.0–19.6, depending on the parasite stage and PZQ concentration.
69.Rationale: Controls (PZQ and DMSO) are essential for assay standardization, plate quality assessment, and interpretation of signal versus background, and they help ensure comparability across time points. However, they do not by themselves eliminate confounding from time-dependent exposure or recovery dynamics; rather, they provide benchmarks and quality metrics. The washout and post-exposure recovery assessment is the feature that most directly minimizes time-dependent confounding of exposure duration.

70.Option: The presence of toxocariasis in children, combined with the high prevalence of T. canis in dogs and the fact that children play in areas where dogs defecate, supports a zoonotic transmission cycle through direct contact with infected animals
Evidence: toxocariasis is expected to have cosmopolitan distribution, since dogs and cats are companion animals with close contact with people in the world [7], [8].
Although there are no data on the prevalence of human toxocariasis in Cameroon, its prevalence in dogs in Cameroon is high [9].

As children are the most risky population for toxocariasis and the prevalence of T. canis in dogs in Cameroon was very high [9], we expected that 61 persons (36.3%) were really exposed to eggs of Toxocara [22], [23].
70.Rationale: The passage supports a zoonotic link via domestic animals by noting high prevalence in dogs, close contact between people and companion animals, and that children are the highest-risk group. However, the specific claim that children 'play in areas where dogs defecate' is not stated in the passage; there is no verbatim evidence describing children playing in contaminated areas or direct contact with animal feces. Because the option includes an unsupported specific behavioral assertion, it overreaches the passage and is therefore judged incorrect.

70.Option: The high seroprevalence of toxocariasis in children, combined with the known presence of Toxocara eggs in soil in endemic areas, supports a zoonotic transmission cycle via environmental contamination from domestic animals
Evidence: toxocariasis is expected to have cosmopolitan distribution, since dogs and cats are companion animals with close contact with people in the world [7], [8].
Although there are no data on the prevalence of human toxocariasis in Cameroon, its prevalence in dogs in Cameroon is high [9].

As children are the most risky population for toxocariasis and the prevalence of T. canis in dogs in Cameroon was very high [9], we expected that 61 persons (36.3%) were really exposed to eggs of Toxocara [22], [23].
70.Rationale: The passage explicitly links (a) toxocariasis transmission to companion animals, (b) high T. canis prevalence in local dogs, and (c) a sizable seroprevalence among the surveyed persons with the expectation that many were 'exposed to eggs of Toxocara.' These statements together most directly support a zoonotic transmission cycle mediated by environmental contamination (eggs in soil/feces) originating from domestic animals, which is what this option asserts.

70.Option: The fact that children are the most at-risk group for toxocariasis and that dogs in Cameroon have high T. canis prevalence indicates a zoonotic transmission cycle, as dogs are known to shed Toxocara eggs in their feces
Evidence: As children are the most risky population for toxocariasis and the prevalence of T. canis in dogs in Cameroon was very high [9], we expected that 61 persons (36.3%) were really exposed to eggs of Toxocara [22], [23].
toxocariasis is expected to have cosmopolitan distribution, since dogs and cats are companion animals with close contact with people in the world [7], [8].
70.Rationale: The passage states that children are the highest-risk group for toxocariasis and that T. canis prevalence in dogs in Cameroon is very high, and it explicitly interprets the seropositivity as exposure to Toxocara eggs. Those statements provide strong support for a zoonotic transmission cycle involving dogs shedding eggs in feces that lead to human exposure, making this option correct.

70.Option: The high seroprevalence of toxocariasis in children, along with their frequent consumption of raw or undercooked pork, suggests transmission via ingestion of contaminated meat from infected animals
Evidence: As children are the most risky population for toxocariasis and the prevalence of T. canis in dogs in Cameroon was very high [9], we expected that 61 persons (36.3%) were really exposed to eggs of Toxocara [22], [23].
Following the questionnaire, serum, sputum and fecal samples were collected from 168 people ... and were asked whether they consumed raw and/or undercooked fresh water crabs or pork.

We recommend highly reliable serological screening for cysticercosis for all pupils in the primary school, if possible, or all teenagers at least in highly endemic areas. As risk factors associated with human cysticercosis include the occurrence of cysticercosis in pigs, detection of adult worm carriers should be investigated.
70.Rationale: Transmission of toxocariasis occurs via ingestion of embryonated eggs from contaminated environments, not via eating undercooked pork. The passage links raw/undercooked pork to cysticercosis (Taenia solium), not toxocariasis, and explicitly interprets toxocariasis seropositivity as exposure to Toxocara eggs. Thus the pork-consumption route is not supported for Toxocara transmission and the option is incorrect.

70.Option: High seroprevalence in children coupled with high T. canis infection rates in local dogs, despite no reported consumption of contaminated soil or animal feces, suggests environmental transmission from domestic animals
Evidence: Although there are no data on the prevalence of human toxocariasis in Cameroon, its prevalence in dogs in Cameroon is high [9].

As children are the most risky population for toxocariasis and the prevalence of T. canis in dogs in Cameroon was very high [9], we expected that 61 persons (36.3%) were really exposed to eggs of Toxocara [22], [23].
70.Rationale: The first half of the option (high seroprevalence in children plus high dog infection rates) is supported by the passage and does point toward environmental transmission from domestic animals. However, the option's clause 'despite no reported consumption of contaminated soil or animal feces' is not supported by the text—the study did not report on 'no consumption' of contaminated soil/feces. Because the option introduces an unsupported negative assertion, it overstates what the passage provides and must be judged incorrect.

72.Option: The presence of amastigotes within histiocytes on histopathology confirms a direct inoculation event from a clinical setting
Evidence: Histologic findings evidenced a dermal lymphohistiocytic infiltrate with amastigote organisms within histiocytes (hematoxylin & eosin; original magnification ×40).
72.Rationale: The quoted histopathology finding confirms the diagnosis of leishmaniasis (presence of amastigotes within histiocytes) but does not by itself indicate the route of transmission. A diagnostic histologic result can be found regardless of whether infection was acquired by vector bite, environmental exposure, or direct inoculation; the passage does not state that histology distinguishes route of acquisition. Therefore this evidence does not confirm a direct inoculation event from a clinical setting.

72.Option: The healthcare worker was exposed to a patient with confirmed mucocutaneous leishmaniasis, which increases the likelihood of transmission via direct inoculation during a medical procedure
Evidence: She performed a skin biopsy on the patient and suffered an accidental needlestick injury on the left index finger.
The Haitian patient was diagnosed with mucocutaneous leishmaniasis caused by Leishmania panamensis, and treatment was established...
72.Rationale: Exposure to a patient with confirmed mucocutaneous leishmaniasis and the documented needlestick injury make direct inoculation a plausible route, as these quotes show. However, while this increases likelihood, it is not the single strongest piece of evidence distinguishing mechanical inoculation from environmental exposure—exposure alone does not prove that the infection resulted from that specific puncture rather than another source. Thus this option is supportive but not the strongest discriminator.

72.Option: The healthcare worker developed symptoms two months after exposure, which aligns with the typical incubation period for cutaneous leishmaniasis following direct inoculation
Evidence: In January 2022, 2 months after the puncture, the resident developed an erythematous, painless papule at the point of inoculation (Fig 1A).
72.Rationale: The temporal relationship (symptom onset two months after the needlestick) is consistent with the incubation period for cutaneous leishmaniasis and supports plausibility of transmission from the earlier exposure. However, timing alone is not definitive for route of acquisition because environmental or vector exposure could produce a similar incubation timeline. Therefore this factor does not most strongly support mechanical inoculation over environmental exposure.

72.Option: The lesion developed at the exact site of the needlestick injury, consistent with direct inoculation and inconsistent with environmental exposure
Evidence: She performed a skin biopsy on the patient and suffered an accidental needlestick injury on the left index finger.
In January 2022, 2 months after the puncture, the resident developed an erythematous, painless papule at the point of inoculation (Fig 1A).
Here, we present the case of a healthcare provider who suffered from an accidental autoinoculation with a needle contaminated by Leishmania organisms.
72.Rationale: The direct spatial concordance of the lesion with the documented needlestick site is the most specific evidence linking the infection to mechanical introduction via the puncture wound rather than to an environmental reservoir. The passage explicitly documents the needlestick on the left index finger and that the papule appeared 'at the point of inoculation,' and the authors characterize the event as 'accidental autoinoculation,' which together strongly indicate the puncture was the source of infection. This localization is more specific evidence of route than histology, exposure to an infected patient, or timing alone.

73.Option: The consistent temporal pattern of mortality across different hantaviruses suggests a direct viral effect rather than an artifact of immune suppression
Evidence: The onset of disease was abrupt and mortality occurred within 24–36 hours after the first signs.

For ANDV, 100% mortality was observed between 12 and 15 days post infection (dpi), for Bayou virus (BAYV) between 14 and 18 dpi, for Black Creek Canal virus (BCCV) between 12 and 15 dpi, for Caño Delgadito virus (CDV) between 13 and 20 dpi and for SNV between 11 and 14 dpi.

All hamsters of the negative control group (treated with DEX/CYP, but not infected) and the PUUV group were still alive at the termination of the experiment 35 days post infection with no noted signs of disease.
73.Rationale: The passage does report abrupt onset and specific dpi ranges for different viruses, and that DEX/CYP-only controls remained alive, which is consistent with a viral effect. However, the phrasing in this option — that a "consistent temporal pattern across different hantaviruses" is the strongest support — overstates the case. Timing data are supportive but not as direct or definitive as experimental evidence that isolates the viral factor (e.g., the passive immunization experiment). The timing varies by virus and alone cannot rule out complex effects of immunosuppression plus infection; therefore this is not the strongest single factor supporting attribution to viral pathogenesis.

73.Option: The fact that multiple New World hantaviruses induce mortality in immunosuppressed hamsters suggests a shared pathogenic mechanism independent of immune suppression
Evidence: A first experiment mortality was induced in all the infected New World hantaviruses groups with mortality rates going up to 100% for Black Creek Canal virus, Bayou virus, Caño Delgadito virus, Sin Nombre virus and Andes virus.
Several New World virus strains were able to induce mortality up to 100% indicating that disease and mortality can be induced by using immune suppression.

All hamsters of the negative control group (treated with DEX/CYP, but not infected) and the PUUV group were still alive at the termination of the experiment 35 days post infection with no noted signs of disease.
73.Rationale: That many New World hantaviruses caused high mortality in the immunosuppressed model is strong evidence that the viruses themselves can produce lethal disease under these conditions. However, this observation does not by itself distinguish between direct viral pathogenic mechanisms and effects that arise from the interaction of virus with an altered immune state; it shows only that infection plus the treatment produces disease. The passive immunization result (neutralizing antibodies preventing death despite immunosuppression) provides a more direct causal demonstration that the virus, not the drug-induced immune state alone, drives mortality. Hence this option is supportive but not the strongest single factor.

73.Option: The histological evidence of pulmonary edema and inflammation in infected hamsters, absent in controls, supports viral pathogenesis over immune suppression as the cause of disease
Evidence: Animals infected with BCCV (Fig 2A) and BAYV (Fig 2B) exhibit a diffuse acute inflammation: alveoli are filled with edema, polymorphic inflammatory cells and fibrin.
A severe hemorrhagic edema in the alveoli, spilling over in the bronchiolar lumen can also be observed (Fig 2B).
The treated control group had normal lungs with no observed abnormalities in the parenchyma and bronchioles (Fig 2C).
Histology of selected tissues showed a clear difference between the control group and the virus-infected groups. The control group received immunosuppression but did not show any signs of disease.
73.Rationale: The histopathology clearly shows lung damage in infected animals and normal lungs in DEX/CYP-treated uninfected controls, which supports that the pathology is due to infection rather than the immunosuppressive regimen alone. However, while strong supportive evidence, histology is still circumstantial compared with an experiment that functionally blocks the virus and prevents disease. The passive immunization experiment directly demonstrates that neutralizing the virus prevents mortality even in immunosuppressed animals, making that result a stronger causal indicator. Therefore histological evidence is supportive but not the single strongest factor.

73.Option: The complete protection conferred by passive immunization against BAYV and BCCV in immunosuppressed hamsters demonstrates that viral infection, not immune suppression, is the primary driver of lethal disease
Evidence: Passive immunization was carried out to confirm that the virus was responsible for the observed disease and mortality.

Two test groups received 10,000 neutralizing antibody units of BCCV or BAYV on day -1. On day 0 all groups were infected with 2,000 PFU of BCCV. In the control groups animals died between 11 and 17 days post infection. In the test groups, receiving the BAYV and BCCV antibodies, all 8 hamsters in each group were still alive at the end of the experiment on day 35 post infection (Fig 3).
The immunized groups were all alive at the end of the experiment on day 35 post infection. Both control groups showed a 100% mortality rate between 11 and 17 days post infection.

This indicates that the observed histological findings in the virus-infected groups are the result of the viral infection and not due to the treatment. This was also supported by the passive immunization, which clearly indicated that the virus is responsible for the signs of disease.
73.Rationale: The passive immunization experiment provides direct, causal evidence: immunosuppressed hamsters given neutralizing antibodies against BAYV or BCCV were fully protected from death, whereas untreated infected controls died. Because the immunosuppressive regimen was the same across groups, the only distinguishing intervention was neutralization of the virus. That specific prevention of disease by targeting the virus demonstrates that viral infection (and not the pharmacologic immune suppression itself) is the primary driver of the lethal outcome. The authors themselves state this supports that the virus is responsible for the signs of disease.

74.Option: Infection remains undetectable in all children for at least 24 months after the last treatment, indicating complete elimination of transmission potential
Evidence: Some have hypothesized that infection may never return if it is brought to a low enough level, analogous to the Allee effect found in population biology[13],[21],[22],[23],[24].
At 21 of the 128 total village visits (16 villages×8 vists), no infection could be identified in 1–5 year-old children.
If infection can be eliminated locally, then this offers a higher level of sustainability[12],[19],[22].
74.Rationale: Complete and sustained absence of detectable infection for a prolonged period (≥24 months) after stopping distributions would be the strongest empirical evidence that transmission potential in the community has been reduced to (or near) zero. The passage explicitly highlights the hypothesis that infection "may never return if it is brought to a low enough level" (Allee effect) and notes that local elimination would provide higher sustainability. Occasional short-term absence of infection is described in the study, but persistent undetectability across follow-up would most strongly support a true reduction/elimination of underlying transmission.

74.Option: Infection returns to baseline prevalence within 6 months after the last treatment, indicating that repeated treatments had no lasting effect on transmission potential
Evidence: In a previous study in southern Ethiopia, infection returned in the first 6 months after treatment at a rate that suggested that biannual treatment would be necessary for progressive reduction with each distribution[10],[14].
if infection is not locally eliminated, it returns back into a community after a single mass distribution, at least in severely affected areas
74.Rationale: Return to baseline within 6 months would indicate no lasting reduction in transmission potential and thus would not support the hypothesis that repeated distributions reduced transmission. The passage documents that return within 6 months has been observed after single treatments and that such rapid return suggests the need for repeated (e.g., biannual) distributions. Therefore this pattern argues against lasting reduction and is not supportive of the hypothesis.

74.Option: Infection returns to a level higher than baseline after treatment cessation, indicating that repeated treatments increased the pathogen's transmission potential
Evidence: The prevalence was lowered to an average of 2.6% prevalence after the four biannual treatments, but in the two years after the last treatment, infection had returned 10-fold to 25.2%.
At 24 months after the last treatment, the mean prevalence of infection was far higher than at 6 months after the last treatment (P = 0.008), but was still only 40% of that at baseline (P<0.0001).
prevalence... remained lower than it had been before the first treatment in all 16 villages.
74.Rationale: The passage provides no example of post-treatment prevalence exceeding baseline; in fact, returned prevalence remained substantially below baseline ("still only 40% of that at baseline"). A return above baseline would imply an increase in transmission potential caused by the interventions, which is not supported by the study findings. Thus this option is incorrect.

74.Option: Infection returns to a lower plateau after treatment cessation, indicating reduced but not eliminated transmission potential
Evidence: The prevalence was lowered to an average of 2.6% prevalence after the four biannual treatments, but in the two years after the last treatment, infection had returned 10-fold to 25.2%.
At 24 months after the last treatment, the mean prevalence of infection was far higher than at 6 months after the last treatment (P = 0.008), but was still only 40% of that at baseline (P<0.0001).
prevalence... remained lower than it had been before the first treatment in all 16 villages.
74.Rationale: While a return to a lower plateau (i.e., post-cessation prevalence that is persistently below baseline) would indicate that transmission potential has been reduced (but not eliminated), the question asks which pattern would most strongly support the hypothesis. A sustained undetectable infection (Option 0) is stronger evidence of elimination/reduction of transmission potential than a lower plateau. Therefore although this pattern would support reduced transmission, it is not the strongest possible support and so is categorized as incorrect in the context of the question's phrasing asking for the most convincing pattern.

74.Option: Infection returns to the same level as immediately after the final treatment, indicating that transmission potential was unchanged by repeated distributions
Evidence: The next three biannual distributions continued to reduce the prevalence to 2.6% (range 0.0% to 7.0%), a significant decrease from after a single treatment (P = 0.0004).
From 6 to 24 months after the last treatment (the 24 and 42 month visits), infection had returned to an average of 25.2% (range 2.0% to 70.0%).
74.Rationale: If prevalence after cessation stayed at the very low level observed immediately after the final treatment (e.g., ~2.6%), that would imply continued suppression equivalent to the treatment effect and might suggest an unchanged underlying transmission potential only while treatments are effectively maintaining suppression. However the study observed a substantial rise after stopping treatment (to ~25.2%), so the pattern described in this option did not occur. Moreover, returning to the same immediate-post-treatment level after stopping would be unlikely without ongoing intervention; it would not strongly indicate that transmission potential was unchanged in the community once treatments ceased.

75.Option: Leprosy risk is not spatially clustered in a predictable way, and interventions should extend beyond close contacts to include broader community screening
Evidence: There is a marked variance in PPUL among the different clusters.
A gradient along geographical lines was not found.
The clusters with a low number of newly found cases are scattered over both districts, as are the clusters with the highest numbers.

we conclude that even in the most distant category (the neighbours of the neighbours and social contacts) the PPUL rate (49/10,000) does not come down to the same level as that of the general population. It may therefore be preferable under such high-endemic circumstances to conduct full village or neighbourhood surveys instead of (close) contact surveys.

most new cases in populations where leprosy is relatively highly endemic come from the non-close contact group. Hence full village or neighbourhood surveys might be preferable to contact surveys under such circumstances.
75.Rationale: The study found no clear spatial gradient and marked heterogeneity across clusters, with high and low case clusters scattered geographically. It also found that even distant contacts had substantially elevated PPUL compared with the general population and explicitly recommended full village/neighbourhood surveys rather than only close-contact screening in this high-endemic setting. Therefore the conclusion that risk is not predictably clustered and that interventions should extend beyond close contacts to broader community screening is supported by the study findings.

75.Option: Because PPUL varies significantly across clusters, interventions should be focused on high-prevalence clusters identified through prior surveillance to maximize efficiency
Evidence: There is a marked variance in PPUL among the different clusters.
The clusters with a low number of newly found cases are scattered over both districts, as are the clusters with the highest numbers.

we conclude that even in the most distant category (the neighbours of the neighbours and social contacts) the PPUL rate (49/10,000) does not come down to the same level as that of the general population. It may therefore be preferable under such high-endemic circumstances to conduct full village or neighbourhood surveys instead of (close) contact surveys.
most new cases in populations where leprosy is relatively highly endemic come from the non-close contact group.
75.Rationale: While the study documents significant variation across clusters, it also reports that high- and low-prevalence clusters are scattered rather than geographically concentrated, and it recommends broader village/neighbourhood surveys in this high-endemic setting. The authors did not advocate focusing solely on identified high-prevalence clusters; given the scattered pattern and the high PPUL among non-close contacts, targeting only known high-prevalence clusters would risk missing many cases. Thus the option is not supported by the study's conclusions.

75.Option: The spatial distribution of leprosy risk follows a clear gradient with distance from index cases, supporting the use of targeted contact tracing for effective case detection
Evidence: As leprosy is known to occur in clusters, i.e. multibacillary (MB) leprosy, the physical distance is also an important factor determining this risk.

we conclude that even in the most distant category (the neighbours of the neighbours and social contacts) the PPUL rate (49/10,000) does not come down to the same level as that of the general population.
It may therefore be preferable under such high-endemic circumstances to conduct full village or neighbourhood surveys instead of (close) contact surveys.
There are indications that in lower endemic areas the incidence of leprosy among contacts declines faster as the physical distance to the patient increases.
75.Rationale: Although distance is generally considered important, this study did not find a clear gradient in this high-endemic setting: even distant contacts had elevated PPUL and the authors recommended broader surveys rather than relying solely on close-contact tracing. The passage does note that distance-related decline may occur in lower endemic areas, but the study's empirical findings do not support a clear distance gradient here; therefore the option is incorrect for this study's setting.

75.Option: Since the general population has a PPUL of 15.1 per 10,000, contact-based interventions should be limited to household contacts to avoid unnecessary screening of low-risk individuals
Evidence: Among these people, 27 previously undiagnosed cases of leprosy were found. The PPUL is thus 15.1 per 10,000 (95% CI = 9.4–20.8).
In the contact group of the COLEP study as a whole, the PPUL rate was 73/10,000, compared to 15.1/10,000 in the population sample.

we conclude that even in the most distant category (the neighbours of the neighbours and social contacts) the PPUL rate (49/10,000) does not come down to the same level as that of the general population.

most new cases in populations where leprosy is relatively highly endemic come from the non-close contact group. Hence full village or neighbourhood surveys might be preferable to contact surveys under such circumstances.
75.Rationale: The general population PPUL is lower than among contacts, but the study found substantial PPUL even among distant contacts (49/10,000) and that most new cases in this high-endemic area come from non-close contacts. The authors recommend broader village/neighbourhood surveys rather than restricting screening to household contacts. Therefore limiting interventions to household contacts is not supported by the study.

75.Option: The high PPUL among distant contacts indicates that transmission occurs primarily through indirect or environmental routes, justifying broad community-wide screening
Evidence: we conclude that even in the most distant category (the neighbours of the neighbours and social contacts) the PPUL rate (49/10,000) does not come down to the same level as that of the general population. It may therefore be preferable under such high-endemic circumstances to conduct full village or neighbourhood surveys instead of (close) contact surveys.
most new cases in populations where leprosy is relatively highly endemic come from the non-close contact group.
As leprosy is known to occur in clusters, i.e. multibacillary (MB) leprosy, the physical distance is also an important factor determining this risk.
75.Rationale: While the study documents elevated PPUL among distant contacts and recommends broader community surveys in this high-endemic setting, it does not provide evidence about transmission mechanisms (direct, indirect, or environmental). Elevated prevalence among distant contacts can reflect social mixing, clustered transmission, or other factors; the paper does not attribute the pattern to primarily indirect/environmental routes. Therefore asserting a primary transmission route is not supported by the study's data, even though broad screening is recommended for case-finding in this context.

76.Option: The high incidence of P. knowlesi among adult males is due to their higher likelihood of being exposed to P. knowlesi-infected macaques during forest-based work activities
Evidence: P. knowlesi infections occur in areas inhabited by natural macaque hosts where it is transmitted to humans via Anopheles Leucosphyrus Group mosquito vectors [11].
P. knowlesi is most prevalent in males aged between 20 and 40 years in Malaysia, who are the most commonly reported demographic in formal employment [15,18,19].

Males within this age group are more susceptible to acquisition of zoonotic malaria due to outdoor agricultural work, such as farming, forestry, palm oil and rubber plantations, in rural areas near forest edges [20,21].

More than half of P. knowlesi cases nationally have been attributed to agriculture and plantation-based work activities, with many of the remaining cases linked to timber logging and forestry work [22].
76.Rationale: The passage explicitly links P. knowlesi transmission to areas inhabited by macaque hosts and identifies outdoor agricultural/forest work as the reason males aged 20–40 are more susceptible. This directly supports the explanation that increased exposure during forest-based work (where macaque reservoirs exist and mosquitoes transmit infection) explains the higher incidence among adult males.

76.Option: The demographic pattern of P. knowlesi cases reflects higher rates of healthcare-seeking behavior among adult male agricultural workers in Sabah
Evidence: P. knowlesi is most prevalent in males aged between 20 and 40 years in Malaysia, who are the most commonly reported demographic in formal employment [15,18,19].

Males within this age group are more susceptible to acquisition of zoonotic malaria due to outdoor agricultural work, such as farming, forestry, palm oil and rubber plantations, in rural areas near forest edges [20,21].
Females may also be underrepresented within the sample due to gender-related socio-cultural norms that influence female’s treatment-seeking behavior [58].
76.Rationale: The passage attributes the male predominance to occupational exposure, not to greater healthcare-seeking by men. In fact, it notes possible underrepresentation of females due to socio-cultural influences on treatment-seeking, which argues against the idea that higher male healthcare-seeking drives the observed demographic pattern. Therefore this option is not supported as the primary explanation.

76.Option: Occupational exposure to forest-edge environments during agricultural work increases the risk of zoonotic transmission from macaque reservoirs to humans
Evidence: P. knowlesi infections occur in areas inhabited by natural macaque hosts where it is transmitted to humans via Anopheles Leucosphyrus Group mosquito vectors [11].

Males within this age group are more susceptible to acquisition of zoonotic malaria due to outdoor agricultural work, such as farming, forestry, palm oil and rubber plantations, in rural areas near forest edges [20,21].

More than half of P. knowlesi cases nationally have been attributed to agriculture and plantation-based work activities, with many of the remaining cases linked to timber logging and forestry work [22].
76.Rationale: The passage directly states that work in rural, forest-edge agricultural settings increases susceptibility to zoonotic transmission from macaque reservoirs via mosquito vectors, and that a majority of cases are attributed to agriculture/plantation work. This makes occupational exposure at forest edges the most direct explanation for the demographic pattern.

76.Option: The higher prevalence of P. knowlesi in males aged 20–40 is due to biological differences in immune response that increase susceptibility to zoonotic malaria in this demographic
Evidence: P. knowlesi is most prevalent in males aged between 20 and 40 years in Malaysia, who are the most commonly reported demographic in formal employment [15,18,19].

Males within this age group are more susceptible to acquisition of zoonotic malaria due to outdoor agricultural work, such as farming, forestry, palm oil and rubber plantations, in rural areas near forest edges [20,21].
76.Rationale: The passage attributes the male predominance to differential exposure from occupational activities rather than to innate biological or immune differences. There is no text presenting evidence of age- or sex-specific immune susceptibility. Therefore the immune-difference explanation is unsupported by the passage.

76.Option: Deforestation for oil-palm plantations has increased the density of Anopheles Leucosphyrus Group mosquitoes, leading to higher transmission rates in rural areas of Sabah
Evidence: large-scale deforestation primarily for oil-palm plantations and rural population growth have created an environment suitable for zoonotic malaria transmission [19,24,25].
P. knowlesi infections occur in areas inhabited by natural macaque hosts where it is transmitted to humans via Anopheles Leucosphyrus Group mosquito vectors [11].
76.Rationale: The passage links deforestation and land-use change to creation of environments suitable for zoonotic transmission, but it does not state explicitly that deforestation has increased vector density or that this is the direct cause of the observed demographic pattern among adult male agricultural workers. The demographic pattern is more directly explained by occupational exposure in forest-edge work. Thus while deforestation is a plausible contributing ecological factor, it is not the most direct explanation for the male agricultural occupational pattern described.

77.Option: Differences in the perceived risk of supernatural harm from tsetse traps were driven by variations in local religious beliefs, with HAT− villages having stronger associations with river spirits and witchcraft
Evidence: Associations with witchcraft were only mentioned by the HAT− group and by men more often than women.

Villages from HAT+ and HAT− areas lie within 10 kilometres of each other. Villagers from different villages often mix in the local markets and during social events such as celebrations of country independence, women's day, Christmas; hence the differences in views described below were surprising.

two communities belonging to the same ethnic group, with similar occupational activities and level of education differ markedly in their knowledge, attitudes and perceptions of HAT and tsetse trapping technology.
The main religious orientations are Christian (Catholic, Anglican Protestant) and Muslim.
77.Rationale: Although the passage notes that associations with witchcraft were mentioned more in the HAT− group, it also makes clear the villages are ethnically and religiously similar and geographically proximate. The authors attribute the difference to prior exposure and local experience with HAT and traps rather than to differing religious beliefs. Therefore attributing the divergence primarily to variation in local religion is not supported by the text.

77.Option: Differences in the physical design of traps used in HAT+ versus HAT− villages led to varying levels of community acceptance due to aesthetic or symbolic associations
Evidence: In HAT− villages two pyramidal traps per village were deployed in the central point of the villages 100 meters apart at the bank of the river running through the villages.

In HAT+ villages no new traps were deployed specifically for this study, but we observed about six traps along the river running through our study villages, which remained deployed from the previous control operations mentioned above.

The suspicions and fear were not reported to be in any way related to the appearance of traps, but rather its position next to the river, uncertainty about the person who carried out the deployment and the unknown purpose of this action, as one of the participants explained:
77.Rationale: The passage describes similar trap types being present and explicitly says that fear was not related to the appearance of traps but to their location, who placed them, and unknown purpose. There is no evidence that different trap designs caused the divergent community perceptions.

77.Option: Differences in prior exposure to HAT and tsetse control programs explain the divergent perceptions, as they shaped baseline knowledge and subsequent acceptance of traps
Evidence: One group comprised four villages in HAT affected areas. ... These villages are coded HAT+ for the purpose of this study and MSF detected an average prevalence of 0.5% ... The other group comprised villages with no recent or historical cases since 2000 ... In our study we coded these villages as HAT−.

Since tsetse control operations are not localized or specific to HAT endemic villages, we considered another selection criterion. The other major difference between the two groups was exposure to tsetse control operations, which had been carried out intermittently in the HAT+ area over the last decade by NGOs ... Trap deployment, however was frequent enough to enable communities in HAT+ villages to familiarize themselves with traps. In HAT− villages no previous tsetse control programs have ever been carried out, therefore, the first exposure to tsetse control baits was introduced as a part of this study.

In HAT+ areas, all participants agreed that HAT is transmitted through tsetse and were able to describe the transmission process. By contrast, villagers in the HAT− area mostly regarded HAT as disease of the “old days” and reported never having witnessed it.
In contrast, our study shows that in communities where traps have been present for a decade people knew about their purpose and perceived them as being effective and beneficial.
This figure indicates that initial acceptance of tsetse traps when they are initiated in the community is low. ... With the time this acceptance increases and is irreversible once achieved.
77.Rationale: The passage repeatedly links higher acceptance in HAT+ villages to prior experience with HAT outbreaks and past tsetse control operations that familiarized people with traps, improved knowledge of transmission, and demonstrated effectiveness. By contrast, HAT− villages lacked this exposure and showed fear and suspicion. These statements directly support prior exposure as the primary explanation for the divergent perceptions.

77.Option: Differences in the timing of trap deployment relative to community events, such as market days or religious festivals, led to varying initial reactions and perceptions of traps
Evidence: Villages from HAT+ and HAT− areas lie within 10 kilometres of each other. Villagers from different villages often mix in the local markets and during social events such as celebrations of country independence, women's day, Christmas; hence the differences in views described below were surprising.

In HAT− villages two pyramidal traps per village were deployed ... These traps were deployed about three weeks before the study was conducted and remained on the sites for the whole pre-study period. This was carried out in order to expose villagers to traps before beginning of discussions. No previous sensitization activities had been carried out prior to this deployment or data collection.

The suspicions and fear were not reported to be in any way related to the appearance of traps, but rather its position next to the river, uncertainty about the person who carried out the deployment and the unknown purpose of this action, as one of the participants explained:
77.Rationale: The text gives no indication that timing relative to community events caused the inter-village differences. Instead the authors emphasize prior exposure, lack of sensitization, and uncertainty about purpose and deployers as drivers of suspicion. Villagers also commonly mix at markets and events, arguing against isolation by timing as the main factor.

77.Option: Differences in gender roles and household responsibilities led to divergent perceptions, with women being more likely to accept traps due to their greater involvement in domestic water collection and farming
Evidence: Women mostly expressed curiosity and hence approached traps more often, while men mostly expressed feeling fear and anxiety.

More women than men reported being involved in these activities. In both groups women expressed more need for empowerment (see a quote below) and requested access to traps more often, while men expressed more need for village decision making, financial motivation and government involvement in tsetse control programs.
In contrast, our study shows that in communities where traps have been present for a decade people knew about their purpose and perceived them as being effective and beneficial.

two communities belonging to the same ethnic group, with similar occupational activities and level of education differ markedly in their knowledge, attitudes and perceptions of HAT and tsetse trapping technology. The community, who were recently initiated into the tsetse control program, reported significant gaps in knowledge of HAT symptoms and transmission.
77.Rationale: While the passage documents gendered differences in reactions (women more curious and engaged), it attributes the main inter-village difference to prior exposure and experience with HAT and trap programs rather than to gender roles. Gender likely influenced individual responses, but does not explain the village-level divergence described in the study.

78.Option: Implementation of improved sanitation infrastructure, particularly increased access to functional pit latrines, likely suppressed transmission and sustained the decline in infection
Evidence: shortly after the 17-month follow-up new pit latrines were installed in each household.
At five years 1154/1667 (69.2%; 95% CI: 67.0–71.4) of the population had access to a functional latrine (Table 2).
As this fly preferentially breeds in exposed human faeces, the construction and use of pit latrines may indirectly reduce the transmission of infection [28].
In our study latrine coverage improved from 43% at baseline to 69% at five years. At the same time fly-eye contacts dropped significantly.
In the absence of additional antibiotic treatment, this decline is likely to be attributable to favourable environmental factors.
78.Rationale: The passage documents installation of pit latrines and a marked increase in latrine coverage coincident with declining infection, and explicitly notes that pit latrines may reduce transmission by reducing fly breeding. The authors state the decline in infection is likely due to favourable environmental factors, consistent with improved sanitation being a plausible and supported contributor to the sustained decline.

78.Option: The decline in infection was maintained due to the long-term protective effect of azithromycin, which persisted in the population for over five years
Evidence: During the follow-up period no further treatment was given for active trachoma by the study team or the National Eye Care Programme (NECP).
The study population did not have ready access to antibiotics with anti-chlamydial activity.

Background antibiotic use probably contributed little to the decline in C. trachomatis, in contrast to a study from Nepal, where a much higher background use of antibiotics may have contributed to a decline in trachoma [30].
78.Rationale: Azithromycin does not have a biologically plausible protective effect lasting years after a single dose, and the paper emphasizes there was no further antibiotic treatment and low background antibiotic use. The authors attribute the sustained decline to environmental factors rather than a long-term pharmacologic effect of azithromycin, so this option is not supported.

78.Option: The reduction in Chlamydia trachomatis strain diversity following mass drug administration likely limited transmission potential and contributed to the sustained decline in infection
Evidence: It is interesting to note that there was less Ct strain diversity two-months after the mass treatment, which may have also contributed to the decline in infection [16].
The estimated community C. trachomatis burden (Williams mean) declined throughout the five-year follow-up period (Table 6).

These observations suggest that: (1) a single MDA was effective in most of the study area, although re-infection seemed to have occurred in two village; (2) the environmental conditions and/or the susceptibility of the population did not favour the transmission of C. trachomatis, leading to its decline and failure to re-emerge.
78.Rationale: The authors explicitly report reduced strain diversity after MDA and state this may have contributed to the decline. Reduced diversity can limit transmission chains or the range of strains able to persist, so this factor is plausibly and textually supported as contributing to sustained decline.

78.Option: The decline in infection was sustained because the population's immunity to Chlamydia trachomatis increased over time following the initial mass drug administration
Evidence: In the absence of additional antibiotic treatment, this decline is likely to be attributable to favourable environmental factors.

The risk of infection at any follow-up time point did not differ between those who had received treatment at baseline and those who had not or who were new residents during the follow-up (data not shown).
78.Rationale: The paper attributes the decline to environmental factors (F&E) rather than acquired population immunity, and notes similar infection risk among those treated at baseline and those not treated, which argues against a dominant role for increased immunity from the MDA in sustaining the decline. The authors do not present evidence of rising protective immunity as the explanation.

78.Option: The sustained decline in infection was likely due to the cumulative effect of improved facial cleanliness and reduced fly-eye contact over time
Evidence: Faces were assessed for fly-eye contact and the presence of nasal and ocular discharge.
There was a marked reduction in observed fly-eye contacts at the time of examination in 1 to 9 year olds from 18.0% (95% CI 14.7 to 21.5) at baseline to 1.5% (95% CI 0.5–2.5) at 5 years.

Similarly there was a large reduction in the proportion of 1 to 9 year olds with unclean faces (nasal or ocular discharge present): 38.4% (95% CI 34.1 to 42.7) at baseline and 5.0% (95% CI 3.1 to 6.8) at 5 years.
This led to the hypothesis that improving facial cleanliness may help to control trachoma by suppressing transmission of C. trachomatis in ocular secretions.
indicating the critical importance of F&E interventions that suppress transmission, in addition to antibiotic treatment.
78.Rationale: The study documents large reductions in unclean faces and fly-eye contacts and discusses the hypothesis and importance of facial cleanliness and environmental (F&E) measures in suppressing transmission. The authors consider F&E interventions critical to preventing re-emergence, supporting this factor as a likely contributor to the sustained decline.

79.Option: The presence of a male family member who can assume the role of primary caretaker during hospitalization
Evidence: In Sub-Saharan Africa, gender roles and social norms of seniority and power strongly influence how health care decisions are made [39–43]. In our research sites, most ethnic groups are patrilineal. Health care decisions that entail treatment outside of the home are made by husbands or senior members of their kin network.

Fathers who take responsibility for the treatment of a child with BU also favored decentralized care. Although not the focus of our research, we encountered one father who, having refused to send his son to the hospital for BU treatment, agreed to take him for decentralized care.
It was not possible for him to remain at the hospital, as he too had to work to sustain his household.
79.Rationale: Male presence and male decision-making are important contextual factors, and in some cases a father may assume caretaking (as in the Djalil example). However, the passage emphasizes the household's practical ability to reassign household work and childcare (substitute labor), not simply the presence of a male. The quoted material shows men make decisions and sometimes provide care, but does not support that a male caretaker is the primary or most direct determinant of feasibility; rather, broader substitution of labor and childcare capacity is emphasized.

79.Option: The availability of a nearby health station offering decentralized outpatient treatment for Buruli ulcer
Evidence: Whenever possible, decentralized outpatient treatment of BU is favored over hospital care.
Decentralized care does not disrupt a household’s daily routine by removing a mother from her household.
As a result of a mass BU outreach education program, Djalil learned about the availability of decentralized treatment at a health station a few kilometers from his house.
79.Rationale: Availability of decentralized care strongly influences the choice between outpatient versus hospital care and often leads households to prefer decentralized treatment. However, the question asks which factor most directly influences the feasibility of pursuing hospital-based care despite free treatment. The availability of an alternative (decentralized care) explains why households choose outpatient care, but the feasibility of undertaking hospital care is most directly determined by internal household capacities (substitute labor, childcare), not simply the presence of a nearby health station.

79.Option: The duration of treatment required for a patient to achieve full recovery from Buruli ulcer
Evidence: More advanced cases often require long-term hospital treatment of indeterminate duration and physical therapy to prevent disability, amputation, and functional limitations after care[26].

The amount of time needed to heal BU related wounds is difficult to predict, and hospital staff are generally not very forthcoming when it comes to communicating to a patient about how their healing process is progressing [17].
79.Rationale: Treatment duration and its unpredictability are important contributors to household strain and to decisions about care seeking. However, duration itself influences feasibility through its effect on household needs (ongoing lost labor, childcare burdens). The passage identifies the proximate feasibility constraint as whether the household can secure substitute labor and childcare during potentially prolonged hospitalization; duration is an underlying driver but not the single most direct operational factor.

79.Option: The household's ability to substitute labor and secure reliable childcare during prolonged hospitalization
Evidence: Decision makers (husbands, elder kin) took stock of available sources of substitute labor within the household as well as a wife’s social capital, her ability to mobilize support from her own kinship network.

A mother’s absence from home on a daily basis to obtain outpatient treatment for BU or her need to remain at a hospital as either a patient or caretaker was deemed feasible only when essential household duties were taken on by somebody else.
Removing a mother places the household at risk unless adequate substitute labor can be secured.
Daughters were generally turned to first to take on a mother’s responsibilities in the household or to serve as a caretaker for a hospitalized family member.
Decentralized care does not disrupt a household’s daily routine by removing a mother from her household.
79.Rationale: The passage repeatedly states that the decisive, proximate constraint on pursuing hospital-based care is the household’s capacity to replace the labor and caregiving the ill person or caretaker normally provides. Feasibility of hospital admission depends on whether substitute labor and reliable childcare can be secured so that the household can continue to function while someone is hospitalized; these verbatim statements directly support that this factor most directly determines feasibility.

79.Option: The perceived risk of social stigma and rumor associated with hospitalization for a woman or her daughter
Evidence: A second notable concern that we identified as having a big effect on health care seeking and patient caretaker decision making was social risk (risk to reputation and to present and future social relationships) [44, 45].
Fear of rumor was a constant worry for some women, adding to the stress of social isolation and trying to survive with minimal resources.
Fear of rumor influenced who was chosen to be a patient caretaker in hospital. Daughters who had not yet reached puberty were preferred.
79.Rationale: Perceived social stigma and rumor are clearly important and sometimes lead women to avoid hospitalization or to make specific caregiving choices. However, stigma affects willingness and social acceptability; the question targets the factor that most directly influences the practical feasibility of pursuing hospital care despite free treatment. The passage foregrounds substitute labor and childcare as the direct operational constraint; social stigma is a powerful motivator but not the single most direct practical determinant.

80.Option: The assumption that full access to antivenom translates into universal treatment uptake, which may not hold due to geographic and health system barriers
Evidence: Full access was modeled as all snakebite victims who were indicated for antivenom could be treated with geographically appropriate antivenoms. In the full access scenario, all snakebite victims who firstly sought traditional healers when access is now full are assumed to switch to conventional treatment.

However, this might not be possible because not all victims could have timely access to healthcare facilities, especially those who lived in the farthest rural areas. This is especially important as timely access to healthcare facilities is related to the prognosis of the victims, both in terms of mortality and long-term disability [42,43].
80.Rationale: The economic model’s core comparison (current vs full access) depends on the structural assumption that ‘‘full access’’ leads to universal uptake (victims switch to conventional treatment). If this behavioral and access assumption is not true, the projected health outcomes (deaths/DALYs averted) and indirect cost savings would be substantially overestimated, undermining validity. The passage explicitly flags this assumption and its likely violation in remote areas and ties it directly to prognosis, making it the most critical factor if not properly accounted for.

80.Option: The assumption that indirect costs from productivity losses due to premature death are discounted at 3% and adjusted for GDP growth, which may not reflect the true economic impact in low- and middle-income countries
Evidence: Costs and health outcomes that occurred after one year were discounted at the rate of 3% [29,30].
The most influential parameters for ICERs per death averted were discount rate and relative risk of death when antivenoms are not available.
80.Rationale: The discount rate and treatment of indirect costs are important model parameters (the authors note discount rate was among the most influential). However, compared with structural assumptions about actual treatment uptake (option 0), the choice of discounting primarily alters the magnitude of presented costs/outcomes rather than the fundamental validity of the modeled 'full access' intervention. The passage recognizes and tests the discount rate in sensitivity analyses, indicating it is influential but not the single most critical threat to validity.

80.Option: The exclusion of long-term disabilities such as chronic kidney disease and post-traumatic stress disorder from the health outcome measures, which could underestimate the true burden of snakebite and affect cost-effectiveness estimates
Evidence: Scenario analyses were presented in S2 and S3 Tables. Incorporating PTSD in the model, assuming that antivenom could reduce the risk of amputation, and increasing the logistic costs of antivenom resulted in similar ICERs per death averted and ICERs per DALY averted in all countries indicating that improving access will result in cost-saving.

Thirdly, other disabilities of snakebite envenoming such as blindness, malignant ulcers, and pregnancy loss were not included due to lack of empirical evidence [7]. Chronic kidney disease due to Russell’s viper (Daboia russelii) bite was not included in our study.
80.Rationale: Omitting long-term disabilities can lead to underestimation of DALYs and therefore bias cost-effectiveness estimates. The authors explicitly note these exclusions and performed scenario analyses (e.g., PTSD) which did not change conclusions, suggesting while this is a limitation it is not the single most critical factor undermining validity compared with the structural uptake assumption. Thus, it is an important but not the most critical threat.

80.Option: The exclusion of costs associated with improving the supply chain and manufacturing capacity, which could significantly increase the total cost of the intervention and invalidate the cost-saving conclusion
Evidence: Lastly, costs of strategy to improve access of antivenoms from the current level to full e.g., costs of increasing antivenom manufacturing capacity, or costs of improving the supply chain, were not included in the analysis. However, these costs were assumed to be covered by the costs of antivenom treatment.

Lastly, scenario analyses were performed by increasing the logistic costs from 5% to 10% of antivenom price because these could be higher, especially in archipelagic countries like Indonesia and Philippines.

Incorporating PTSD in the model, assuming that antivenom could reduce the risk of amputation, and increasing the logistic costs of antivenom resulted in similar ICERs per death averted and ICERs per DALY averted in all countries indicating that improving access will result in cost-saving.
80.Rationale: Excluding implementation-related costs (manufacturing, supply-chain strengthening) could indeed make the intervention less cost-saving. The authors acknowledge this omission but state these costs were assumed covered and they tested higher logistic costs in scenario analyses with similar conclusions. While important, the passage suggests this factor was explored and did not overturn the cost-saving result, and it is secondary to the fundamental uptake/behavioral assumption (option 0) in terms of threatening validity.

80.Option: The use of antivenom effectiveness data from Nigeria, which may not be generalizable to ASEAN due to differences in snake species and antivenom formulations
Evidence: We assumed antivenom effectiveness based on a study in Nigeria [11]. Most snakebites In Nigeria are inflicted by snakes of the genus Echis, for which antivenoms, in general, have high effectiveness [7]. This assumption may have a limitation as snakes in Nigeria are different from ASEAN. Nevertheless, our sensitivity analyses showed robust conclusions of the model.
The most influential parameters for ICERs per death averted were discount rate and relative risk of death when antivenoms are not available.
80.Rationale: Using effectiveness estimates from Nigeria is a legitimate limitation because species and antivenom performance differ regionally and effectiveness is a key driver of outcomes. However, the authors explicitly acknowledge this and performed sensitivity and threshold analyses; they report the model’s conclusions remained robust across plausible ranges. Thus, while this factor can influence validity if grossly mis-specified, the passage indicates it was tested and was not the most critical unaccounted-for threat compared with the structural full-uptake assumption.

81.Option: Using a red-shifted fluorophore (Cy3B) with high signal stability and specificity to distinguish true target binding from non-specific interactions in complex biological extracts
Evidence: Cy3B is a red-shifted dye that has been found to be particularly well suited for FP due to its increased fluorescence intensity, fluorescence lifetime of 2.9 ns, and stability of its signal in a variety of aqueous solvent conditions.

In addition, its use in the labeling of GA may limit the potential interference of fluorescent small-molecule library components and reduce false-positives resulting from light scattering caused by insoluble compounds.
81.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that Cy3B's photophysical properties (red-shift, higher intensity, longer lifetime, signal stability) make it well suited for FP in complex extracts, and that labeling GA with Cy3B can limit interference from fluorescent library compounds and light scattering, thereby reducing false-positives. Therefore this methodological choice most directly and explicitly addresses minimizing assay false-positives while preserving true target signal.

81.Option: Including C. elegans extracts as a negative control to confirm that the assay does not detect binding in a species with a highly conserved Hsp90 sequence
Evidence: In contrast, cy3B-GA bound the C. elegans extracts with very low affinity (Figure 1C).

The signal at each time point was virtually indistinguishable from time 0, indicating that the low increase in mP, observed especially at high extract concentrations, was caused by non-specific binding of the tracer or by autofluorescence from the worm extract.
C. elegans extracts from worms where Hsp90 levels were reduced by ∼40% by means of RNAi (data not shown), showed essentially no change in binding signal (Figure 1C).
This assay was further validated by the inability of C. elegans extracts to bind cy3b-GA.
81.Rationale: The investigators used C. elegans extracts as a negative control and demonstrated very low/no specific binding of cy3B-GA; this establishes assay specificity in a related species (despite sequence conservation) and helps distinguish true target-specific signals from non-specific or artifactual signals, thereby minimizing false positives.

81.Option: Using whole worm homogenates instead of purified recombinant Hsp90 to preserve the native conformation and post-translational modifications of the target protein
Evidence: The assay is suitable for high-throughput screening, does not require the production of purified recombinant protein and could be adapted to screen Hsp90 inhibitors against other parasites, where Hsp90 is known to be a valid target [24], [25].

Since the sensitivity to Hsp90 inhibitors is determined by a complex crosstalk between Hsp90 and the cellular environment, it is necessary to develop screening strategies that account for the endogenous presentation of Hsp90 in Brugia.
Given the format of the assay, the ability to utilise whole worm extracts and the relatively modest cost, the FP assay could now be applied to the identification of novel inhibitors of Brugia Hsp90
81.Rationale: The passage argues that Hsp90 inhibitor sensitivity depends on the protein's native cellular context (co-chaperones, post-translational modifications), and therefore using whole-worm extracts (instead of recombinant protein) preserves this endogenous presentation. That methodological choice increases biological relevance of hits and reduces identification of compounds that bind recombinant protein but not the native target (a source of false-positives), enhancing assay validity.

81.Option: Measuring FP signal stability over time to ensure that the assay window remains consistent across multiple time points
Evidence: The plate was incubated at 4°C for different time periods, and the FP in mP was measured with the Analyst GT.
The signal amplified with time, reached equilibrium at 8 h and remained stable thereafter, resulting in an excellent assay window of approximately 200 mP (Figure 1A).
The competitive assay signal remained constant over the recorded interval of 8 to 24 h (Figure 3A).
81.Rationale: Demonstrating that the FP signal reaches equilibrium and remains stable (i.e., a consistent assay window) is essential for reliable discrimination between bound and free tracer and for reproducible competition measurements. Measuring stability over time reduces time-dependent artifacts and variability that could otherwise produce spurious hits, thereby minimizing false positives and improving validity.

81.Option: Including human cancer cell lysates as a comparative control to assess cross-species binding affinity and selectivity of inhibitors
Evidence: The human breast cancer cell line SKBr3 was obtained ... Cells were collected and frozen to rupture the membranes and then dissolved in binding buffer ... to form the lysate.
cy3B-GA bound to the Brugia Hsp90 species (Figure 1A) with a high affinity, similar to the tumor Hsp90 species (Figure 1B).

To evaluate whether the assay is sufficiently sensitive to differentiate between human and Brugia Hsp90 species, and thus lead to the identification of species-selective inhibitors, we measured the affinity of GA and of several closely related PU-class derivatives, PU-24FCl, PU-H71 and PU-WS10 for the Brugia (Figure 4A) and the SKBr3 (Figure 4B) Hsp90 complexes.
a good selectivity was noted in the PU-series, with a selectivity ratio of 2, 3.5 and 9 for PU-24FCl, PU-H71 and PU-WS10, respectively (Figure 4C).
81.Rationale: Including human cancer cell lysates as a comparative control allows the assay to detect whether candidate compounds bind human Hsp90 as well as parasite Hsp90 and to quantify selectivity. Although this does not directly reduce all types of false-positives, it improves the validity of hit selection by distinguishing parasite-selective inhibitors from non-selective binders (which may be undesirable), thereby refining true-positive identification in the context of parasite-targeted drug discovery.

82.Option: The antigen is packaged into extracellular vesicles and remains undetected by antibodies from chronically infected hosts, indicating immune shielding
Evidence: Most of the TcTASV-C produced by CL Brener trypomastigotes is secreted through extracellular vesicles

Pooled sera from humans, mice or rabbits chronically infected with T. cruzi failed to efficiently detect EVs antigens, although they strongly reacted with trypomastigote antigens and with “naked” secreted proteins, both from RA and CL Brener strains (Fig 7 and S6 Fig).
These findings led us to hypothesize that immunization with TcTASV-C–which is highly expressed in bloodstream trypomastigotes and also secreted- would be a good target for immunotherapy control.
We support the hypothesis that secretion of cargo in EVs (and particularly the secretion of TcTASV-C) is another parasite-driven immune evasion mechanism.
82.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that most TcTASV-C is secreted in extracellular vesicles (EVs) and that pooled sera from chronically infected hosts "failed to efficiently detect EVs antigens" while they did detect parasite-associated or freely secreted proteins. The authors interpret this pattern as evidence that packaging into EVs hides the cargo from host antibodies and therefore acts as an immune evasion mechanism. Those lines of text directly support the option's claim that EV packaging with poor detection by chronic sera indicates immune shielding consistent with a virulence factor mediating early immune evasion.

82.Option: The antigen is secreted in large quantities and is a major component of the parasite secretome, suggesting it is a key virulence factor due to its abundance
Evidence: TcTASV-C was secreted in both EVs populations in the CL Brener strain (Fig 3B); in the small EV fraction (V16, Fig 3B, upper panel) TcTASV-C appeared as a highly abundant component, while it was almost undetectable on parasite pellets at these conditions.

we demonstrate that, despite its scarce expression on culture-derived trypomastigotes, TcTASV-C is strongly secreted, and is a major component of trypomastigote’s EVs, at least in the T. cruzi reference strain CL Brener.
82.Rationale: The passage supports that TcTASV-C is abundant in EVs and is a major component of the secretome, which implies functional importance. However, the question asks for the feature that most strongly supports a role specifically in early host immune evasion. Abundance alone suggests importance but does not by itself demonstrate immune evasion. The stronger, direct evidence for immune evasion is the EV-packaging combined with failure of chronic sera to detect EV antigens. Therefore, while abundance is supportive, it is not the most specific or strongest feature for the immune-evasion hypothesis, so this option is not the best answer to the question as phrased.

82.Option: The antigen is expressed on the parasite surface and is recognized by antibodies from chronically infected patients, indicating it is a target of the adaptive immune response and thus likely involved in immune evasion
Evidence: TcTASV-C presents a characteristic distribution pattern of scattered dots along the parasite surface and flagellum, and is spontaneously secreted to the medium.
While anti-TcTASV-C antibodies are detected in about 30% of chronically-infected patients, the seroprevalence in reservoir dogs with active infection rises to 75%.
82.Rationale: The passage does show surface localization and that anti-TcTASV-C antibodies are present in a subset (~30%) of chronically infected patients, which indicates it can be targeted by adaptive immunity. However, detection by chronic sera argues against being fully hidden from the immune system and therefore does not directly support immune evasion; indeed, immune recognition is contrary evidence to the antigen being concealed as an evasion mechanism. The strongest evasion evidence in the text is the opposite observation: EV-associated TcTASV-C is poorly detected by chronic sera. Thus this option does not correctly identify the feature that most strongly supports early immune evasion.

82.Option: The antigen is highly expressed in bloodstream trypomastigotes and secreted into the extracellular environment, suggesting it plays a direct role in host cell invasion
Evidence: TcTASV-C was detected in 1x10^6 (or even less) bloodstream parasites (Fig 5A, upper panel, BT lanes) while it was necessary to load more than tenfold of culture-derived trypomastigotes to be weakly detected (Fig 5A, Cult lanes, upper panel).
As well as detected for culture-derived trypomastigotes, bloodstream trypomastigotes were also able to strongly secrete TcTASV-C.
TcTASV-C interacts with the surface of mammalian cells but does not participate in parasite invasion in vitro.
82.Rationale: Although TcTASV-C is upregulated in bloodstream trypomastigotes and is secreted and can bind mammalian cells, the passage explicitly reports that experiments showed it "does not participate in parasite invasion in vitro." Therefore, the pattern described does not support a direct role in host cell invasion. The question asked about evidence supporting a role in early immune evasion; while bloodstream expression and secretion are consistent with an early role, the lack of effect on invasion and the more direct immune-evasion evidence (EV-packaging and poor detection by chronic sera) mean this option is not the best-supported answer.

82.Option: The antigen is secreted in a strain-specific manner, suggesting it is involved in host adaptation and immune evasion through antigenic variation
Evidence: Overall, TcTASV-C was secreted in EVs in all the strains analyzed (Fig 4). Only mild differences in TcTASV-C secretion profile were detected among strains.

In culture-derived trypomastigotes from highly virulent strains (i.e. Y, TcII and RA, TcVI) a more dynamic pattern of secretion was observed, and TcTASV-C was detected alternatively in different fractions (Fig 4 and S3 Fig).
As a whole, in all the analyzed strains, TcTASV-C was significantly more represented in the secreted than in the parasite associated fraction.
we could not link a particular secretion profile with a certain T. cruzi DTU or strain.
82.Rationale: The passage indicates that while there are some differences in secretion patterns among strains (and greater dynamic secretion in more virulent strains), TcTASV-C secretion in EVs is observed across all strains and the authors explicitly note they "could not link a particular secretion profile with a certain T. cruzi DTU or strain." Thus secretion is not described as strictly strain-specific or as evidence of antigenic variation driving immune evasion. The text does not support the claim that strain-specific secretion underlies immune evasion via antigenic variation, making this option incorrect.

83.Option: A dominant Th2 immune response driven solely by sand fly saliva exposure, which increases susceptibility to intracellular pathogens
Evidence: the human immune response to saliva of Phlebotomus duboscqi (Pd), the principal sand fly vector in Mali, was found to be dichotomously polarized with some individuals developing a Th1-dominated response and others a Th2-biased response [23].

Protective salivary proteins are characterized by their induction of a Th1 delayed-type hypersensitivity response where IFN-γ, considered a strong correlate of protection in leishmaniasis, is induced.

monocytes recovered from individuals naturally-exposed to sand fly bites were refractory to antigenic challenge, and became unresponsive to filarial or leishmanial antigens, or even potent activators such as LPS.
83.Rationale: The passage shows that exposure to sand fly saliva does not uniformly induce a dominant Th2 response; responses are dichotomous (some Th1, some Th2). Moreover, saliva-induced Th1 responses are associated with protection against Leishmania in models. In addition, the real-world effect of repeated sand fly exposure described here is monocyte hyporesponsiveness rather than a sole Th2-skewing that would universally increase susceptibility to intracellular pathogens. Therefore the statement that a dominant Th2 driven solely by saliva exposure is the key population characteristic is not supported.

83.Option: A heightened Th1 response due to prior exposure to sand fly saliva, which enhances clearance of intracellular pathogens
Evidence: adaptive immune response to whole saliva or a distinct salivary protein generally protects against cutaneous and visceral leishmaniasis in rodent models of infection [15–19].

Protective salivary proteins are characterized by their induction of a Th1 delayed-type hypersensitivity response where IFN-γ, considered a strong correlate of protection in leishmaniasis, is induced.

the human immune response to saliva of Phlebotomus duboscqi (Pd) ... was found to be dichotomously polarized with some individuals developing a Th1-dominated response and others a Th2-biased response [23].

monocytes recovered from individuals naturally-exposed to sand fly bites were refractory to antigenic challenge, and became unresponsive to filarial or leishmanial antigens, or even potent activators such as LPS.
83.Rationale: While the passage notes that Th1 responses to certain salivary proteins can be protective (mostly demonstrated in animal models), it also emphasizes that human responses are variable (Th1 or Th2) and that in endemic populations repeated exposure often leads to monocyte unresponsiveness. Thus although a heightened Th1 response could, in principle, improve clearance of some intracellular pathogens (e.g., Leishmania), the passage does not support this as the dominant population feature most strongly influencing risk in co-endemic settings. The real-world dominant modifiers described are innate dysfunction and regulatory bias from chronic filarial infection.

83.Option: A pre-existing state of innate immune exhaustion combined with a regulatory immune bias due to chronic filarial infection
Evidence: The altered function of the innate immune response, particularly the dysfunction of antigen presenting cells (APC), has been postulated to mediate some of the parasite-specific T cell unresponsiveness seen in patent filarial infection.

we have shown that circulating monocytes from filaria-infected individuals are laden with filarial antigens, exhibit diminished antigen presentation and processing, and produce fewer proinflammatory cytokines in response to surface receptor crosslinking [24].

monocytes recovered from individuals naturally-exposed to sand fly bites were refractory to antigenic challenge, and became unresponsive to filarial or leishmanial antigens, or even potent activators such as LPS.
monocytes of MF+ individuals were less responsive than monocytes of MF- individuals since LPS significantly induced the production of CCL4 and IL-10 only in MF- individuals (Fig 3).
