129.Option: The continued occurrence of neonatal tetanus is due to the ineffectiveness of the tetanus toxoid vaccine in preventing infection, as evidenced by the lack of significant association between maternal vaccination and survival
Evidence: The easiest, quickest, and most cost-effective preventive measure against NNT is vaccination of the pregnant women with the tetanus toxoid (TT) vaccine [5].

Maternal tetanus toxoid vaccination was also not significantly associated with survival in this study unlike reports from some previous studies, this probably may be because none of the mothers in this study completed the two doses of vaccination in pregnancy.
Two doses of TT in pregnancy have been reported to reduce NNT mortality by 94% [42],
129.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes tetanus toxoid vaccination as the most cost-effective preventive measure and cites strong evidence that completion of the recommended doses reduces NNT mortality substantially. The study’s failure to find a significant association between maternal vaccination and survival is explained in the text by incomplete vaccination (none completed the two doses), low coverage, and other programmatic gaps, not by vaccine ineffectiveness. Therefore, attributing persistent occurrence to vaccine ineffectiveness is not supported by the passage.

129.Option: Persistent transmission is driven by a convergence of structural inequities, cultural practices, and gaps in preventive healthcare access, despite overall declining incidence
Evidence: NNT results from unhygienic delivery practices and some harmful traditional cord care practices which include cutting the cord with non-sterile equipment such as razor blade; application of shea butter, mentholatum containing balms and animal dung to the cord [3].

Majority of the affected babies in Nigeria are delivered either at home, faith (mission) based homes, with untrained or poorly trained traditional birth attendants as only 43% of deliveries are attended by skilled birth attendant as revealed by the 2018 Nigeria Demographic and Health Survey (NDHS) report [4].
close to 60% of the mothers had no antenatal care during pregnancy and close to 60% of them did not receive tetanus toxoid vaccination during pregnancy (Table 2).
NNT is under reported in Nigeria with only about 5% of cases reporting to health facilities [13].

The burden of maternal and neonatal tetanus (MNT) also known as “silent killer” has been described as a health equity issue affecting mostly the underserved and disadvantaged population who also lack schools, employment opportunities and basic infrastructures, such as roads, health care services and communication [5].
This study reveals that neonatal tetanus is still being seen in our clinical practice and that the mortality is still high due to lack of facilities for its proper management.
129.Rationale: The passage repeatedly links ongoing NNT cases to poverty and health inequities (low education, low socioeconomic class, lack of infrastructure), harmful cultural cord-care and delivery practices, low antenatal care and tetanus vaccine coverage, home deliveries with unskilled attendants, and underreporting. These intersecting structural, cultural, and healthcare-access factors together explain why neonatal tetanus persists in resource-limited settings despite an overall decline.

129.Option: The persistence of neonatal tetanus is due to the high rate of home deliveries in rural areas, which are not adequately monitored by public health surveillance systems
Evidence: Majority of the affected babies in Nigeria are delivered either at home, faith (mission) based homes, with untrained or poorly trained traditional birth attendants as only 43% of deliveries are attended by skilled birth attendant as revealed by the 2018 Nigeria Demographic and Health Survey (NDHS) report [4].

This may probably be due to some gains of the implementation of the MNTE strategies, or it could be due to under reporting as most NNT cases do not make it to the health facilities where they could be documented. NNT is under reported in Nigeria with only about 5% of cases reporting to health facilities [13].
129.Rationale: High rates of out-of-facility deliveries and underreporting are indeed cited as contributors in the passage. However, the option frames this as the sole or primary explanation; the passage indicates multiple interacting causes (harmful cord care, low vaccination, poverty, lack of ANC, poor hygiene) rather than attribution solely to home deliveries and surveillance gaps. Thus this option is an incomplete explanation.

129.Option: The persistence of neonatal tetanus is primarily due to the high prevalence of comorbidities such as anemia, hypoglycemia, and sepsis, which increase susceptibility to infection
Evidence: 5 [26.3%] of the babies had low blood sugar (hypoglycaemia) at presentation while 7 [36.8%] of them had anaemia at presentation.
About 50% of the patients were deemed to have features suggestive of sepsis at presentation
The high mortality rate recorded may also be due to the accompanying sepsis in our patients.
129.Rationale: The passage documents that comorbidities (anemia, hypoglycaemia, sepsis) are common and may worsen prognosis, but it does not present these comorbidities as the primary drivers of continued transmission. The underlying persistence is attributed to exposure (contaminated umbilical stumps), poor cord care, low maternal vaccination, unhygienic deliveries, and systemic inequities. Therefore, comorbidities are important modifiers of outcome but not the principal cause of ongoing incidence.

129.Option: Neonatal tetanus persists because of the widespread use of unapproved medications such as shea butter and mentholatum, which directly introduce Clostridium tetani into the umbilical stump
Evidence: NNT results from unhygienic delivery practices and some harmful traditional cord care practices which include cutting the cord with non-sterile equipment such as razor blade; application of shea butter, mentholatum containing balms and animal dung to the cord [3].

Cord care was with hot water compress in most of these babies16 [48.5%], about 7 [20%] had mentholatum applied to the cord while only 1 [5.2%] mother claimed to clean the cord with only methylated spirit.
The umbilicus was the suspected/identified portal of entry of the tetanus infection in all the babies [100%].
129.Rationale: The passage identifies application of substances like shea butter and mentholatum as harmful cord care practices associated with NNT and confirms the umbilicus as the portal of entry. However, the text does not claim these substances 'directly introduce' Clostridium tetani; rather they are unhygienic practices that increase risk. Moreover, these practices are one among several interacting causes (including unskilled deliveries, low vaccination, poverty, and poor ANC). Thus the option overstates causation and is not the most complete explanation.

130.Option: Enhanced phagocytic activity of macrophages due to increased expression of FPR2 receptors on immune cells
Evidence: this aspirin-triggered lipoxin reduces the internalization of T. cruzi in macrophages [20], an effect mediated by N-formyl peptide receptor 2 (FPR2) [21,22].

Previous studies have shown that the anti-inflammatory and pro-resolving properties of AT-RvD1 are primarily mediated by FPR2 [49]. In our model, FPR2 participates in regulating pro-inflammatory and anti-inflammatory cytokines (IFNγ and IL-1Β) and reducing cellular infiltrates because its absence weakens the effect produced by AT-RvD1.
In the absence of FPR2, eliminating the parasite from cardiac tissue is not as efficient, suggesting that the pro-resolving cascade is essential for promoting parasite clearance.
we cannot exclude the fact that the observed clearance is the result of resolvin-induced efferocytosis [72].
AT-RvD1 has no trypanocidal activity. However, we observed that AT-RvD1 alone or combined with Bz reduced the heart parasite load in infected mice.
130.Rationale: The passage documents a role for FPR2 in mediating AT-RvD1 effects and notes that resolvin-induced efferocytosis (a phagocytic process) might explain parasite clearance. However, it does not state that AT-RvD1 increases expression of FPR2 on immune cells or that enhanced macrophage phagocytosis is driven by upregulated FPR2 expression. In fact, one cited effect is that an aspirin-triggered lipoxin "reduces the internalization of T. cruzi in macrophages," which is the opposite of increased parasite uptake. Because the option specifically claims increased FPR2 expression driving enhanced phagocytosis — a claim not supported by the text — the option is incorrect.

130.Option: Direct inhibition of parasite replication through suppression of mitochondrial function in T. cruzi cells
Evidence: AT-RvD1 has no trypanocidal activity. However, we observed that AT-RvD1 alone or combined with Bz reduced the heart parasite load in infected mice.
Importantly, AT-RvD1 has no trypanocidal activity.
130.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that AT-RvD1 has no trypanocidal (direct anti-parasite) activity. Therefore a mechanism invoking direct inhibition of parasite replication or mitochondrial suppression in T. cruzi is contradicted by the text and is not supported.

130.Option: Resolution of chronic inflammation restores immune system function, enabling clearance of persistent parasites that otherwise evade detection due to an immunosuppressive environment
Evidence: AT-RvD1 is a pro-resolving lipid mediator effective against inflammation, dampening pathological inflammatory responses and restoring tissue homeostasis; importantly, it contributes to clearing adverse inflammatory environments and allowing more efficient parasite elimination, with the participation of FPR2, at least partially.
the pro-resolving processes initiated by AT-RvD1 enable the immune system to eliminate the parasites, probably because chronic inflammation is an evasive mechanism used by the parasites.
we cannot exclude the fact that the observed clearance is the result of resolvin-induced efferocytosis [72].
In the absence of FPR2, eliminating the parasite from cardiac tissue is not as efficient, suggesting that the pro-resolving cascade is essential for promoting parasite clearance.
130.Rationale: The passage repeatedly links AT-RvD1's pro-resolving (anti-inflammatory) actions to improved parasite clearance: resolving the chronic inflammatory environment is proposed to allow the immune system to eliminate persistent parasites. The authors explicitly state that pro-resolving processes enable parasite elimination and that clearance may result from resolvin-induced efferocytosis, and they note that loss of FPR2 impairs parasite removal. These statements directly support the mechanism described in the option.

130.Option: Upregulation of parasite-specific antibodies that enhance opsonization and complement-mediated lysis
Evidence: the time when an adaptive antigen-specific response is established and cytotoxic and helper T lymphocytes and B cells participate [33].

AT-RvD1 is a pro-resolving lipid mediator effective against inflammation, dampening pathological inflammatory responses and restoring tissue homeostasis; importantly, it contributes to clearing adverse inflammatory environments and allowing more efficient parasite elimination, with the participation of FPR2, at least partially.
we cannot exclude the fact that the observed clearance is the result of resolvin-induced efferocytosis [72].
130.Rationale: While the passage mentions adaptive responses and B cells participate in the antigen-specific response, it provides no evidence that AT-RvD1 increases parasite-specific antibody production, opsonization, or complement-mediated lysis. Instead, the proposed mechanisms in the text focus on resolution of inflammation, modulation of cytokines, FPR2-mediated effects, and possibly efferocytosis. Because antibody upregulation is not described or supported, this option is incorrect.

130.Option: Increased production of reactive oxygen species by neutrophils leading to oxidative damage of parasite membranes
Evidence: Bz could also increase reactive oxygen species production at the tissue level [60,61], contributing to the production and establishment of a pro-inflammatory environment.

AT-RvD1 is a pro-resolving lipid mediator effective against inflammation, dampening pathological inflammatory responses and restoring tissue homeostasis; importantly, it contributes to clearing adverse inflammatory environments and allowing more efficient parasite elimination, with the participation of FPR2, at least partially.
AT-RvD1 has no trypanocidal activity. However, we observed that AT-RvD1 alone or combined with Bz reduced the heart parasite load in infected mice.
130.Rationale: The passage indicates that increased reactive oxygen species (ROS) production is associated with benznidazole (Bz) and a pro-inflammatory environment, not with AT-RvD1, which is pro-resolving and anti-inflammatory. There is no support that AT-RvD1 increases neutrophil ROS to damage parasites; indeed, the authors emphasize non-trypanocidal, immunomodulatory mechanisms (resolution of inflammation, efferocytosis, FPR2-mediated effects) to explain parasite reduction. Therefore this option is unsupported by the text.

131.Option: The presence of anti-ZIKV IgG-NS1 antibodies in mothers is a stronger predictor of microcephaly than anti-DENV IgG-NS1 levels, as it reflects recent or active infection during pregnancy
Evidence: Exposure to ZIKV was defined as the presence of plasma anti-ZIKV NS1 IgG antibodies in the mothers, accompanied by signs and symptoms of Zika disease during pregnancy at the peak of the epidemic period.
This comparison was not performed for ZIKV IgG-NS1 since positivity for this test was a study inclusion criterion.
We did not observe differences ... for ZIKV IgG-NS1 antibodies, as plasma levels of both groups were below the detection limit (Fig 4E and F).
low or undetectable levels of IgM-NS1 both in mothers and children (Figs 2A, B and S1A).
131.Rationale: The passage indicates anti-ZIKV IgG-NS1 positivity was effectively an inclusion criterion (all mothers had it by definition), so it cannot serve as a stronger predictor in this cohort. The authors did not find differences in ZIKV IgG-NS1 levels between mothers of microcephalic and non-microcephalic children, and IgM (which would indicate recent/active infection) was generally low or undetectable. In contrast, differences were observed for DENV IgG-NS1. Therefore the statement is not supported by the passage.

131.Option: A lower DENV/ZIKV IgG-NS1 ratio in mothers and children is indicative of increased risk for microcephaly, suggesting that pre-existing dengue immunity may protect against severe congenital Zika syndrome
Evidence: mothers of children with microcephaly had a lower DENV/ZIKV IgG-NS1 ratio in comparison with those without microcephaly (Fig 4C).
a ROC curve analysis indicated that the DENV/ZIKV IgG-NS1 ratio in samples from the mothers is robust in discriminating between both groups of children (Fig 4D).

We observed a significantly lower DENV/ZIKV IgG-NS1 ratio in children with microcephaly in comparison with non-microcephalic children (Fig 4G), and this ratio could discriminate between both groups of children (Fig 4H).
low levels of pre-existing DENV IgG-NS1 antibodies are associated with the development of microcephaly
These data align with the notion that the anti-orthoflavivirus IgG-NS1 response may play a protective role against ZIKV infection during pregnancy.
131.Rationale: The passage directly reports that a lower DENV/ZIKV IgG-NS1 ratio in mothers (and in children) is associated with microcephaly and that the ratio discriminates groups by ROC analysis. The authors interpret this as evidence that pre-existing DENV NS1 IgG may be protective against severe CZS (microcephaly). Thus the option accurately reflects the study findings and interpretation.

131.Option: The use of hydroxychloroquine and doxycycline in dengue patients may alter anti-NS1 antibody levels, confounding the association between DENV immunity and microcephaly risk
Evidence: Our study has some limitations. We did not prospectively measure antibody responses in pregnant women early after ZIKV exposure, which could be better correlated with clinical outcomes in newborns.

In summary, low levels of pre-existing DENV IgG-NS1 antibodies are associated with the development of microcephaly, the most severe clinical form, but not neurodevelopmental or ocular alterations in children with normocephalic CZS.
131.Rationale: The passage makes no mention of hydroxychloroquine, doxycycline, or any treatment effects on anti-NS1 antibody levels. The study focuses on measuring antibody levels and their association with outcomes; it does not identify or adjust for medication use as a potential confounder. Therefore the specific claim about those drugs altering NS1 antibody levels and confounding the association is unsupported by the passage.

131.Option: Higher levels of anti-DENV IgG-NS1 antibodies in mothers are associated with increased risk of microcephaly due to enhanced antibody-dependent enhancement of Zika virus infection
Evidence: We observed that mothers of children with microcephaly had a lower positivity rate for DENV IgG-NS1 in comparison with those without microcephaly.
low levels of pre-existing DENV IgG-NS1 antibodies are associated with the development of microcephaly

anti-ZIKV NS1 antibodies have no risk of antibody-dependent enhancement of infection [20,24], highlighting their role in antibody-mediated protection against disease and as an optimal target in vaccine development.
In contrast, pre-existing DENV-specific antibodies in mice infected with ZIKV during pregnancy may enhance vertical ZIKV transmission, resulting in microcephaly [17].
131.Rationale: The study reports the opposite association: lower, not higher, DENV IgG-NS1 levels were associated with increased microcephaly risk, suggesting a protective role for DENV NS1 antibodies. Furthermore, the passage states that anti-NS1 antibodies do not carry the risk of antibody-dependent enhancement (ADE). Although prior mouse data (not NS1-specific) showed possible enhancement, the human cohort data here contradict the option's claim. Thus the option is not supported.

131.Option: The lack of correlation between DENV and ZIKV IgG-NS1 levels in mothers suggests that pre-existing dengue immunity has no role in modulating Zika virus outcomes in pregnancy
Evidence: we did not observe a correlation between ZIKV and DENV IgG-NS1 antibody levels in plasma from mothers (rho = −0.05; p = 0.45) or children (rho = 0.004; p = 0.95).
We observed that mothers of children with microcephaly had a lower positivity rate for DENV IgG-NS1 in comparison with those without microcephaly.
low levels of pre-existing DENV IgG-NS1 antibodies are associated with the development of microcephaly
These data align with the notion that the anti-orthoflavivirus IgG-NS1 response may play a protective role against ZIKV infection during pregnancy.
131.Rationale: Although DENV and ZIKV IgG-NS1 levels are not correlated (they vary independently), the study nevertheless finds that lower DENV NS1 IgG is associated with higher microcephaly risk. Lack of correlation between antibody titres does not imply lack of effect; here, DENV NS1 antibodies appear to modulate outcome despite being uncorrelated with ZIKV NS1 levels. Therefore the option's inference is incorrect.

132.Option: The insertion of mCherry into the E3-E2 junction alters the cleavage efficiency of the furin protease, resulting in prolonged exposure of the antigen in its precursor form and enhancing antigen presentation to B cells
Evidence: Insertion of mCherry between E3 and E2 might slow down the furin cleavage of E3 because the parental CHIKV 37997 virus contains very little uncleaved E3-E2 (p62) precursor.
On each chimeric CHIKV 37997-mCherry virion, 240 copies of mCherry are presented in a repetitive pattern between viral spikes (Fig 2).

The high density of mCherry can be easily recognized by B cells and, therefore, activate B cells efficiently. Consistently, a single infection of CHIKV 37997-mCherry induced long-lasting and high titer antibody responses against mCherry...
132.Rationale: Although the passage notes that "insertion of mCherry between E3 and E2 might slow down the furin cleavage of E3," the authors do not present prolonged precursor exposure as the primary explanation for the strong, durable antibody responses. Instead, they attribute the strong single-infection antibody response to the repetitive, high-density display of mCherry on the virion surface. Therefore the specific mechanism proposed in this option (prolonged precursor exposure via altered furin cleavage) is not supported as the main feature driving sustained high-titer responses in the passage.

132.Option: Repetitive, high-density display of the foreign antigen on the viral surface enhances B cell activation and drives durable, high-titer antibody responses after a single infection
Evidence: On each chimeric CHIKV 37997-mCherry virion, 240 copies of mCherry are presented in a repetitive pattern between viral spikes (Fig 2).
The high density of mCherry can be easily recognized by B cells and, therefore, activate B cells efficiently.

Consistently, a single infection of CHIKV 37997-mCherry induced long-lasting and high titer antibody responses against mCherry that can only be reached after boosting in mice conventionally immunized with mCherry proteins (Fig 10).
132.Rationale: The passage explicitly links the structural feature of dense, repetitive antigen presentation (240 copies per virion in a repeating array) to efficient B cell recognition and activation and then to the observed durable, high-titer antibody response after a single infection. This directly supports the option as the most likely structural contributor.

132.Option: The presence of viral RNA replication intermediates during infection strongly activates innate immune responses, leading to enhanced T cell-mediated immunity and sustained antibody production against the foreign antigen
Evidence: Virus replication generates viral RNA replication intermediates and viral RNA that strongly activate host innate immune responses, which may explain the strong immunogenicity of our replication-competent chimeric virus[29].

The high density of mCherry can be easily recognized by B cells and, therefore, activate B cells efficiently. Consistently, a single infection of CHIKV 37997-mCherry induced long-lasting and high titer antibody responses against mCherry...
132.Rationale: The passage does state that viral replication intermediates strongly activate innate immunity and that this may contribute to immunogenicity. However, the question asks which feature of the viral structure most likely contributes to sustained high-titer antibody responses. Activation of innate immunity by replication intermediates is a replication/host-response property, not a structural feature; and the authors emphasize repetitive high-density antigen display on the virion surface as the structural explanation for the single-dose high-titer antibody response. Thus, while innate activation may contribute, this option does not identify the structural feature asked for and is not the primary structural mechanism described in the passage.

132.Option: The fusion of mCherry to the E2 glycoprotein enables the antigen to be incorporated into the viral nucleocapsid core, allowing for efficient delivery to antigen-presenting cells and robust B cell activation
Evidence: On each chimeric CHIKV 37997-mCherry virion, 240 copies of mCherry are presented in a repetitive pattern between viral spikes (Fig 2).
The mCherry proteins were well exposed, occupying the space between viral spikes on the particle surface (Fig 2D).
240 copies of C form an icosahedral nucleocapsid core enclosing viral genomic RNA within the lipid membrane.
132.Rationale: The passage clearly describes mCherry as being displayed on the virion surface between spikes and exposed to B cells, not incorporated into the internal nucleocapsid core. The nucleocapsid core is formed by capsid (C) proteins enclosing the RNA. Therefore the option's claim that mCherry is incorporated into the nucleocapsid core is inconsistent with the described structural data and cannot explain the observed surface-driven B cell activation.

132.Option: The chimeric virus's ability to persist in host tissues for extended periods provides continuous antigen exposure, which drives prolonged B cell stimulation and high-titer antibody production
Evidence: viral RNA in the ankles on the contralateral side of CHIKV 37997-mCherry infected mice were not significantly higher than the assay limit of detection, and viral loads in ankles on the ipsilateral side were significantly lower than those in parental CHIKV 37997 infected mice (Fig 8C).
Consistently, plasma RNA viral loads at 3 days post-infection of CHIKV 37997-mCherry or CHIKV 181/25 were significantly lower than those in CHIKV 37997 infected mice (Fig 8B).

The high density of mCherry can be easily recognized by B cells and, therefore, activate B cells efficiently. Consistently, a single infection of CHIKV 37997-mCherry induced long-lasting and high titer antibody responses against mCherry...
132.Rationale: The passage shows that the CHIKV 37997-mCherry chimeric virus is attenuated and cleared more rapidly (lower and often undetectable viral loads) than the parental virus, yet it still elicits long-lasting high-titer antibody responses after a single infection. This argues against prolonged tissue persistence being the driver; instead the authors point to dense, repetitive surface presentation of antigen as the explanation. Therefore persistent infection is not the supported mechanism.

134.Option: Fatigue is a key predictor of severe disease outcomes in individuals with a history of eye worm migration, even when microfilaremia is absent
Evidence: In comparison to LN, each of the two groups with history of eye worm (EW and EWMF) reported several signs and symptoms significantly more often.

This was the case for Calabar swelling-like manifestations (adjusted prevalence ratio [95% confidence interval]: (1.40 [1.10–1.79] for EW and 2.05 [1.56–2.69] for EWMF), paresthesia (1.51 [1.19–1.92] and 1.73 [1.32–2.26]), transient paralysis of extremities (2.63 [1.86–3.72] and 1.78 [1.15–2.76]), and fatigue (1.81 [1.43–2.30] and 1.49 [1.12–2.01]).

Comparing the EW and EWMF categories, patients in the EWMF group reported significantly fewer Calabar swelling-like manifestations (0.68 [0.51–0.91]), whereas patients in the EW category indicated more often urticaria (p = 0.062, 1.48 [0.97–2.24]).
In summary, the infection states comprising participants with history of eye worm were significantly associated with the occurrence of clinical complaints.
134.Rationale: While fatigue is reported significantly more often among individuals with a history of eye worm (EW) compared with those without signs of loiasis, the passage does not state that fatigue is a predictor of severe disease outcomes or excess mortality. The text links severe outcomes and excess mortality primarily to high microfilarial densities, not to fatigue. Therefore the claim that fatigue is a key predictor of severe disease outcomes even when microfilaremia is absent is not supported by the provided evidence.

134.Option: The presence of urticaria is strongly associated with severe disease outcomes in individuals with a history of eye worm migration, independent of microfilaremia
Evidence: Comparing the 3 LP categories among each other, EW and EWMF subjects reported some manifestations more frequently than MF individuals. This was the case for myalgia (1.79 [1.08–2.97] and 1.76 [1.04–2.99], respectively) and Calabar swelling-like manifestations (1.55 [1.00–2.40] and 2.27 [1.45–3.55]).

Further, EW subjects reported several disease manifestations more often than the MF subjects; this was the case for transient swellings of any body part (other swellings 1.81 [1.05–3.10]), transient paralysis of extremities (2.84 [1.48–5.47]), urticaria (2.38 [1.23–4.59]) and fatigue (1.53 [1.02–2.30]).

Importantly, we found that neurological symptoms, including paresthesia and transient paralysis of extremities, were strongly associated with loiasis, specifically with eye worm positive loiasis and not with microfilaremia itself.

Chronic infection has been associated with unspecific manifestations, such as itching, body pains or fatigue and importantly, individuals with high microfilarial densities are known to be at risk for spontaneous or treatment-related severe manifestations and excess mortality [1,7,12,13,18,19].
134.Rationale: Although urticaria was reported more often among EW subjects than MF subjects, the passage does not link urticaria to severe disease outcomes or excess mortality. The text explicitly links severe outcomes to high microfilarial densities. The strongest clinical features highlighted as concerning and distinct in EW are neurological symptoms (paresthesia, transient paralysis) and a broader symptom load, not urticaria as an indicator of severe outcomes. Thus the option overstates the evidence.

134.Option: Elevated absolute eosinophil counts are associated with high microfilarial density and predict severe disease outcomes, even in individuals without detectable microfilaremia
Evidence: Chronic infection has been associated with unspecific manifestations, such as itching, body pains or fatigue and importantly, individuals with high microfilarial densities are known to be at risk for spontaneous or treatment-related severe manifestations and excess mortality [1,7,12,13,18,19].
In summary, loiasis was associated with absolute and relative eosinophilia. Extent of eosinophilia was higher in microfilaremic individuals than in amicrofilaremic loiasis.

Furthermore, the extent of eosinophilia seemed to be associated with L. loa microfilarial density and, conclusively all 56 individuals with ≥8,000mf/ml, had eosinophil counts above the normal range (≤0.5x103/μL).

The here described finding of an association between extent of eosinophilia and microfilaria counts, might therefore pathophysiologically explain the previously described excess mortality in highly microfilaremic individuals [13,19].
134.Rationale: The passage clearly links elevated eosinophil counts to higher microfilarial densities and suggests this association might explain excess mortality in highly microfilaremic individuals. However, it does not support the claim that elevated eosinophil counts predict severe outcomes in individuals without detectable microfilaremia. On the contrary, eosinophilia was higher in microfilaremic individuals than in amicrofilaremic loiasis. Therefore the option is not supported by the evidence.

134.Option: A history of eye worm migration is associated with neurological symptoms and fatigue, suggesting increased risk of severe disease even without detectable microfilaremia
Evidence: Comparing the loiasis infection states between each other showed that history of eye worm migration was associated with the presence of a wide range of signs and symptoms, independently of presence of microfilaremia.

This was the case for Calabar swelling-like manifestations (adjusted prevalence ratio [95% confidence interval]: (1.40 [1.10–1.79] for EW and 2.05 [1.56–2.69] for EWMF), paresthesia (1.51 [1.19–1.92] and 1.73 [1.32–2.26]), transient paralysis of extremities (2.63 [1.86–3.72] and 1.78 [1.15–2.76]), and fatigue (1.81 [1.43–2.30] and 1.49 [1.12–2.01]).

Further, EW subjects reported several disease manifestations more often than the MF subjects; this was the case for transient paralysis of extremities (2.84 [1.48–5.47]) ... and fatigue (1.53 [1.02–2.30]).

Importantly, we found that neurological symptoms, including paresthesia and transient paralysis of extremities, were strongly associated with loiasis, specifically with eye worm positive loiasis and not with microfilaremia itself.
134.Rationale: The passage documents that a history of eye worm migration (EW) is independently associated with a wide range of clinical complaints, notably neurological symptoms (paresthesia, transient paralysis) and fatigue, and that these associations are independent of detectable microfilaremia. While excess mortality and treatment-related severe events are explicitly linked to high microfilarial densities elsewhere in the text, the presence of neurological manifestations and a high symptom burden in eye-worm positive individuals—despite absence of detectable microfilaremia—suggests these patients may have clinically important (potentially severe) disease that would be missed if only microfilaremia were used to assess risk. Thus EW history with neurological symptoms and fatigue is the feature best supported by the passage as indicating increased clinical risk even when microfilaremia is not detected.

134.Option: A history of eye worm migration is associated with myalgia and severe headache, which are strong indicators of severe disease risk even without detectable microfilaremia
Evidence: In comparison to LN, each of the two groups with history of eye worm (EW and EWMF) reported several signs and symptoms significantly more often.
In addition, individuals in the EW category reported myalgia and severe headache more often compared to LN (1.36 [1.01–1.82] and 1.33 [1.15–1.54], respectively).

Comparing the loiasis infection states between each other showed that history of eye worm migration was associated with the presence of a wide range of signs and symptoms, independently of presence of microfilaremia.

Chronic infection has been associated with unspecific manifestations, such as itching, body pains or fatigue and importantly, individuals with high microfilarial densities are known to be at risk for spontaneous or treatment-related severe manifestations and excess mortality [1,7,12,13,18,19].
134.Rationale: The passage shows that myalgia and severe headache are reported more often among those with a history of eye worm. However, there is no statement that these particular symptoms are 'strong indicators' of severe disease risk or excess mortality independent of microfilaremia. The text emphasizes neurological signs (paresthesia, transient paralysis) and links severe outcomes to high microfilarial densities rather than to myalgia or headache specifically. Therefore the option overstates what the evidence supports.

135.Option: Intradermal immunization with a moderate dose of the conjugate vaccine to promote both systemic memory B cells and mucosal IgA responses
Evidence: We intramuscularly immunized one cohort of mice with 5:1 molar ratio of OSP to rTTHc vaccine (10 μg OSP per mouse) without alum, 3 cohorts of mice (10 μg of OSP per mouse) at different molar ratios of vaccines (OSP:rTTHc ratio of 3:1, 5:1, 10:1) with alum, and 2 cohorts of mice with 5:1 molar ratio of OSP to rTTHc vaccine at different doses (20 μg and 50 μg of OSP per mouse) with alum. We also immunized 2 cohorts of mice intradermally with 5:1 molar ratio of OSP to rTTHc vaccine (10 μg OSP per mouse), with or without alum.

We found significant IgA secreting cell responses (P<0.05) in the intestinal lamina propria of mice immunized intradermally with OSP:rTTHc in the presence or absence of alum compared with responses in mice receiving alum only (Fig 7).

Following intramuscular and intradermal immunization, we detected OSP-specific IgG memory B cells in spleen of vaccinated animals at the time of sacrifice (Fig 6) in the various vaccine cohorts, although we did not detect this response in animals receiving the highest dose of vaccine (50 μg of OSP:rTTHc 5:1, intramuscularly).
Anti-TT responses were highest in mice immunized intradermally, with a significant boosting increase following the third immunization (Fig 3B).
These data suggest that intradermal immunization of conjugate may not only induce mucosal responses, but may permit dose reduction [41].
135.Rationale: The passage shows that intradermal immunization (at the 10 μg dose tested) induced significant intestinal lamina propria IgA-secreting cells and also that memory B cells were detected after intradermal immunization. Intradermal administration produced strong carrier (TT) responses and boosted with repeated doses, and the authors note intradermal dosing may permit dose reduction. Together these observations support that intradermal immunization at a moderate dose promotes both mucosal IgA (important for protection at the intestinal surface) and systemic memory B cell responses that underlie durable protection against reinfection.

135.Option: Intramuscular immunization with a high dose of the conjugate vaccine to maximize initial serum IgG antibody levels
Evidence: We found a significant increase in OSP (Fig 3A) and tetanus (Fig 3B)-specific IgG responses in serum of mice who received Inaba OSP-rTTHc vaccine intramuscularly or intradermally.

In general, immune responses were comparable across all the vaccine cohorts, although anti-OSP responses rose more rapidly in mice immunized with 20 μg of OSP (5:1) compared to responses in mice immunized with 10 μg or 50 μg.

Following intramuscular and intradermal immunization, we detected OSP-specific IgG memory B cells in spleen of vaccinated animals at the time of sacrifice (Fig 6) in the various vaccine cohorts, although we did not detect this response in animals receiving the highest dose of vaccine (50 μg of OSP:rTTHc 5:1, intramuscularly).
135.Rationale: Although intramuscular immunization produced serum IgG, the data do not support that a high intramuscular dose (50 μg) is superior for durable memory: memory B cells were not detected in animals receiving the highest intramuscular dose, and responses were comparable across doses with some measures rising faster at intermediate doses (20 μg). Thus a high IM dose to maximize initial IgG does not appear to be the best strategy for promoting long-term immune memory.

135.Option: Intradermal immunization with a low dose of the conjugate vaccine to minimize adverse reactions and promote mucosal IgA responses
Evidence: We also immunized 2 cohorts of mice intradermally with 5:1 molar ratio of OSP to rTTHc vaccine (10 μg OSP per mouse), with or without alum.

We found significant IgA secreting cell responses (P<0.05) in the intestinal lamina propria of mice immunized intradermally with OSP:rTTHc in the presence or absence of alum compared with responses in mice receiving alum only (Fig 7).
These data suggest that intradermal immunization of conjugate may not only induce mucosal responses, but may permit dose reduction [41].
135.Rationale: The passage supports that intradermal immunization at the 10 μg dose induced mucosal IgA, and the authors suggest intradermal administration may permit dose reduction. However, there is no data presented about a 'low dose' specifically improving durable memory versus the tested 10 μg intradermal cohort, nor is there evidence about adverse reaction rates to justify choosing a low dose for safety. Therefore the specific recommendation of a low-dose intradermal strategy to minimize adverse reactions and enhance durable memory is not supported by the provided data.

135.Option: Intramuscular immunization with multiple doses to boost serum IgG and memory B cell responses
Evidence: Mice were immunized on days 0, 21, and 42.

immunization of mice induced prominent anti-OSP and anti-TT serum IgG responses, with significant increases occurring following a single immunization, and additional boosting of immune responses occurring after both second and third immunizations.
Following intramuscular and intradermal immunization, we detected OSP-specific IgG memory B cells in spleen of vaccinated animals at the time of sacrifice (Fig 6) in the various vaccine cohorts...
135.Rationale: The study demonstrates that multiple immunizations (the 0, 21, 42 schedule) produced boosting of serum IgG and that memory B cells were detected after the multi-dose regimen for intramuscularly immunized animals. Boosting with repeated doses therefore supports induction of systemic memory B cells and higher antibody levels, which are mechanisms that can contribute to durable protection, making multiple-dose IM immunization a reasonable strategy to promote long-term immune memory.

135.Option: Intramuscular immunization with alum adjuvant to enhance systemic IgG and vibriocidal responses
Evidence: In general, immune responses were comparable across all the vaccine cohorts, although anti-OSP responses rose more rapidly in mice immunized with 20 μg of OSP (5:1) compared to responses in mice immunized with 10 μg or 50 μg.
We used the 5:1 version of the vaccine as our central benchmark, and found that co-administering the vaccine with alum did not significantly alter immune responses.
We found no antigen-specific responses were detected in mice immunized with alum alone.

vaccination with OSP:rTTHc resulted in the production of vibriocidal antibodies, although these responses were lower than those normally induced following immunization with currently available oral cholera vaccines [47,48].
135.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that co-administration with alum did not significantly alter immune responses, and vibriocidal responses induced by this conjugate were lower than those induced by oral vaccines. Thus intramuscular immunization with alum is not shown to meaningfully enhance systemic IgG or vibriocidal responses in this study, so it is not supported as the best strategy to enhance durable memory.

136.Option: Construct a phylogenetic tree using maximum likelihood methods on the seven MLST loci to infer geographic origin based on genetic relatedness
Evidence: Due to relatively low levels of sequence diversity and high levels of lateral gene transfer among B. pseudomallei isolates [8], [11], sequence data from only seven genes are insufficient for robust phylogenetic discrimination [11], [12].
Phylogenetic analyses show geographically correlated clusters of STs, although these analyses failed to group all samples from either region together [9] [10].

High relative rates of recombination to mutation at MLST loci for B. pseudomallei is higher than for any other bacterial species yet reported [11], meaning that allele frequency differences among populations is an appropriate method for determining population structure.
136.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that using sequence data from only seven MLST genes is insufficient for robust phylogenetic discrimination because of low sequence diversity and high lateral gene transfer (recombination). Thus constructing a maximum-likelihood phylogeny on the seven MLST loci would not directly address misclassification due to high recombination and is not the recommended approach in this context.

136.Option: Use Bayesian clustering with a high probability threshold (≥95%) to assign isolates to populations based on allele frequency patterns rather than phylogenetic trees
Evidence: The program Structure [13] is a Bayesian-based clustering algorithm that has been used to infer population structure within genetically diverse bacteria such as Helicobacter pylori [14].

Pearson et al. therefore used a population genetics approach to determine that B. pseudomallei STs form two distinct populations, conforming to the geographic regions of Southeast Asia and Australia [11].

To be conservative in our assignment of STs to a population, we suggest that a ST only be considered to be from a given population if Structure or BAPS assigned it to that population ≥95% of the time.
We therefore suggest, where possible, that Structure and BAPS are used in concert with large epidemiological datasets for highly recombinant organisms to make the most robust population assignments.

High relative rates of recombination to mutation at MLST loci for B. pseudomallei is higher than for any other bacterial species yet reported [11], meaning that allele frequency differences among populations is an appropriate method for determining population structure.
136.Rationale: The passage recommends allele-frequency based Bayesian clustering tools (Structure, BAPS) specifically for highly recombinant organisms and explicitly advises a conservative cutoff (≥95%) to reduce assignment errors. This method directly addresses misclassification risk from recombination by using population-genetic allele frequency models rather than strictly phylogenetic relationships.

136.Option: Perform whole-genome sequencing on all isolates and use core genome MLST to identify single nucleotide polymorphisms that define geographic populations
Evidence: Whole genome sequencing of this ST will help resolve uncertainties regarding Australian and Southeast Asian population assignments as phylogenetic analyses can be expected to reflect population subdivisions as they have for the Australian and Southeast Asian populations [11].

Performing MLST on large bacterial collections is a time-consuming task; however, single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) genotyping provides a streamlined way to characterize MLST populations even for recombining species [25], [26], [27].

Genotype interrogation at more loci or great numbers of STs will increase our knowledge of subpopulation dynamics, but in the meantime our current ability to differentiate between the two or three major populations is an important first step for epidemiological attribution.
136.Rationale: While the passage emphasizes allele-frequency Bayesian methods for MLST-scale data, it also notes that increasing loci (e.g., through whole-genome sequencing or SNP genotyping) and using SNP-based approaches can help resolve assignment uncertainties. Whole-genome/core-genome approaches provide many more informative loci and SNPs and therefore would directly reduce misclassification risk stemming from limited loci and recombination.

136.Option: Use a neighbor-joining tree with bootstrapping to assess the robustness of genetic clusters and assign isolates based on cluster membership
Evidence: Due to relatively low levels of sequence diversity and high levels of lateral gene transfer among B. pseudomallei isolates [8], [11], sequence data from only seven genes are insufficient for robust phylogenetic discrimination [11], [12].
Phylogenetic analyses show geographically correlated clusters of STs, although these analyses failed to group all samples from either region together [9] [10].

High relative rates of recombination to mutation at MLST loci for B. pseudomallei is higher than for any other bacterial species yet reported [11], meaning that allele frequency differences among populations is an appropriate method for determining population structure.
136.Rationale: Neighbor-joining (a phylogenetic clustering method) is a phylogenetic tree approach. The passage documents the limitations of phylogenetic methods using the seven MLST loci in the face of high recombination, indicating such tree-based cluster assignment would not adequately address misclassification risk.

136.Option: Apply a principal component analysis (PCA) to the MLST allelic profiles to visualize genetic variation and assign isolates based on proximity to population centroids
Evidence: The program Structure [13] is a Bayesian-based clustering algorithm that has been used to infer population structure within genetically diverse bacteria such as Helicobacter pylori [14].
We therefore suggest, where possible, that Structure and BAPS are used in concert with large epidemiological datasets for highly recombinant organisms to make the most robust population assignments.

High relative rates of recombination to mutation at MLST loci for B. pseudomallei is higher than for any other bacterial species yet reported [11], meaning that allele frequency differences among populations is an appropriate method for determining population structure.
136.Rationale: The passage does not endorse PCA and instead promotes allele-frequency Bayesian clustering (Structure, BAPS) for recombining organisms. PCA is a visualization/dimension-reduction tool and does not by itself implement the allele-frequency population-genetic models that the authors identify as appropriate for addressing high recombination; therefore PCA on MLST allelic profiles would not be the most direct method to reduce misclassification risk.

137.Option: The genetic relatedness between isolates from Jimma and other sites, as shown by FST and ADMIXTURE, indicates that the high DBP copy number variants are maintained through inbreeding rather than gene flow, supporting localized adaptation
Evidence: By contrast, higher differentiation was observed between Jimma and Arbaminch (FST value = 0.229) and between Jimma and Halaba (FST value = 0.327; S6 Table).

ADMIXTURE analysis showed that there were most likely two or three genetic clusters among all the Ethiopian samples based on the lowest cross validation scores of 0.75 and 0.78, respectively (Fig 7A). At K = 2, most of the samples from Badowacho, Hawassa, Arbaminch, and Halaba had predominantly the light-blue cluster, whereas samples from Jimma had the red cluster (Fig 7A).

A maximum likelihood tree constructed based on the whole genome sequences showed an admixture of P. vivax isolates with single and multiple PvDBP copy number (Fig 6A). ... Each of the subclades had samples with both one and two PvDBP copies.

Our phylogenetic analyses indicated that P. vivax with high-order DBP copies evolved from isolates with a single-copy DBP multiple times independently at different geographic locations. Isolates with high-order PvDBP copies may confer better fitness and favored by selection, resulting in a higher frequency in isolates of P. vivax from Ethiopia. Moreover, gene flow among geographical locations may allow further spread and a broad distribution of high-order PvDBP strains.
137.Rationale: The option asserts the high DBP copy variants are maintained through inbreeding/localized adaptation based on Jimma's genetic distinctness. While the passage does report higher FST and a distinct ADMIXTURE cluster for Jimma, it also shows that DBP copy-number variants occur across multiple subclades and locations and that high-order DBP copies "evolved ... multiple times independently at different geographic locations," with the authors noting that "gene flow among geographical locations may allow further spread and a broad distribution of high-order PvDBP strains." Those statements argue against the idea that the DBP expansions are solely maintained by inbreeding/local isolation. Therefore the claim that DBP high-copy variants are maintained through inbreeding rather than gene flow is not supported by the provided evidence.

137.Option: The presence of multiple DBP gene copies in Ethiopian isolates indicates a higher rate of recombination events, which may explain the observed transmission network with Arbaminch as a source hub
Evidence: A maximum likelihood tree constructed based on the whole genome sequences showed an admixture of P. vivax isolates with single and multiple PvDBP copy number (Fig 6A). ... Each of the subclades had samples with both one and two PvDBP copies.
DBP and MSP3 expansion had occurred multiple times as tandem copies.
These expanded gene copies shared identical nucleotide sequences, suggesting rapid and recent duplications within an isolate [79, 88].
The transmission network indicated that Arbaminch was the major source of infections from which the infections in Jimma, Hawassa, Badowacho, and Halaba originated (Table 3; Fig 7B).
137.Rationale: The option links multiple DBP copies to an increased rate of recombination and uses that to explain Arbaminch as a source. The passage describes DBP expansion as "tandem copies" and notes that expanded copies "shared identical nucleotide sequences, suggesting rapid and recent duplications," which points to duplication events rather than increased recombination as the mechanism. Recombination is discussed in the passage in the context of MSP3 paralogs ("likely through frequent recombination and gene conversion between the PvMSP3 paralogs"), not DBP. While Arbaminch is identified as a transmission source, the evidence attributes DBP copy-number expansions to duplication events and possible selection and gene flow, not to a higher recombination rate; therefore the statement is not supported.

137.Option: The high copy number variation in DBP genes likely reflects adaptive evolution to diverse human Duffy phenotypes, and its spread from Arbaminch suggests active dissemination of these host-adapted variants across the region
Evidence: Among the 43 erythrocyte binding genes, duplications were observed in DBP1 on chromosome 6 ... DBP1 ranged from one to as high as five copies, and MSP3.11 ranged from one to as high as three copies among our samples (Fig 5), consistent with previous findings [19, 20, 79].

Given that Duffy phenotype is more diverse among the the human populations in Ethiopia than in Asia [87], it is possible that P. vivax expands DBP to multiple copies to enhance binding affinity and invasion to different host phenotype [79].
The transmission network indicated that Arbaminch was the major source of infections from which the infections in Jimma, Hawassa, Badowacho, and Halaba originated (Table 3; Fig 7B).

Our phylogenetic analyses indicated that P. vivax with high-order DBP copies evolved from isolates with a single-copy DBP multiple times independently at different geographic locations. Isolates with high-order PvDBP copies may confer better fitness and favored by selection, resulting in a higher frequency in isolates of P. vivax from Ethiopia. Moreover, gene flow among geographical locations may allow further spread and a broad distribution of high-order PvDBP strains.
137.Rationale: The passage provides direct support for this interpretation. It documents substantial DBP1 copy-number variation ("...DBP1 ranged from one to as high as five copies"), hypothesizes that such expansion could enhance invasion across diverse Duffy phenotypes in Ethiopia ("...possible that P. vivax expands DBP to multiple copies to enhance binding affinity and invasion to different host phenotype"), and shows a transmission network with Arbaminch as a major source of infections. The authors also state that high-order DBP copies may be favored by selection and can spread via gene flow, consistent with active dissemination of host-adapted variants. Taken together, these statements support the option that DBP CNV reflects adaptation to mixed Duffy status and that such variants can be transmitted across the region (e.g., from Arbaminch).

137.Option: The low nucleotide diversity in TRAg genes across all study sites suggests that these genes are under strong purifying selection, limiting their role in host adaptation despite high copy number variation in other erythrocyte binding genes
Evidence: The TRAg gene family from the isolates from Ethiopia also showed a low level of nucleotide diversity when compared to the other EBP gene families with an average nucleotide diversity of 0.2% (Fig 3B).

Based on PAML, 17 out of the 43 erythrocyte binding genes showed evidence of positive selection (Table 2; S4 Table). The majority of these genes belong to the TRAg multigene family. The TRAg genes had an average dN/dS ratio of 2.75 across all branches and an average of 5.75 across all sites for the M2 model tested for selection (Table 2).
137.Rationale: While the TRAg family shows low nucleotide diversity numerically, the passage explicitly reports strong signals of positive selection in many TRAg genes (high dN/dS and sites under selection). Positive selection is the opposite of purifying selection; it implies adaptive change rather than constraint. Therefore it is incorrect to conclude TRAg genes are under strong purifying selection that limits their role in host adaptation. The evidence indicates TRAg genes may be under diversifying/positive selection and could contribute to host adaptation despite low overall nucleotide diversity metrics.

137.Option: The expansion of DBP gene copies in Ethiopia is likely due to relaxed selection pressure from antimalarial drugs, allowing for the accumulation of non-functional variants that are then transmitted across regions via human movement
Evidence: While DBP1 had the highest and most diverse copy number variation, no significant signal of positive selection was detected, consistent with the findings in P. vivax isolates from from Western Cambodia, Western Thailand, and Papua Province in Indonesia [19, 20].

A number of antimalarial drug resistance genes including the chloroquine resistance transporter (CRT-O) gene, the dihydropteroate synthase (DHPS) gene, and dihydrofolate reductase–thymidylate synthase (DHFR) gene were previously reported with positive selection [20, 89], but we did not detect such in this study.

Given that Duffy phenotype is more diverse among the the human populations in Ethiopia than in Asia [87], it is possible that P. vivax expands DBP to multiple copies to enhance binding affinity and invasion to different host phenotype [79].
These expanded gene copies shared identical nucleotide sequences, suggesting rapid and recent duplications within an isolate [79, 88].
The transmission network indicated that Arbaminch was the major source of infections from which the infections in Jimma, Hawassa, Badowacho, and Halaba originated (Table 3; Fig 7B).
137.Rationale: The option posits DBP expansion resulted from relaxed drug selection allowing non-functional variants to accumulate. The passage offers no support for a drug-relaxation mechanism; in fact, drug-resistance genes under selection were not detected in this dataset. The authors instead propose a functional explanation tied to host biology: DBP expansion may enhance binding to diverse Duffy phenotypes in Ethiopia. Additionally, expanded DBP copies "shared identical nucleotide sequences, suggesting rapid and recent duplications," which argues for recent functional duplications rather than neutral accumulation of non-functional copies. Human movement is cited as enabling spread, but the causal mechanism is more plausibly host-adaptive selection/duplication than relaxed drug selection based on the provided evidence.

138.Option: The agreement in classifying drug efficacy into 'satisfactory', 'doubtful', or 'reduced' is sufficient to conclude that diagnostic methods are equivalent in measuring drug efficacy, even when sensitivity varies across species
Evidence: No single diagnostic method provides ERR that are equivalent to single Kato-Katz for all STH, but they agree in classifying drug efficacy according to the WHO guidelines

for most methods there was relatively good agreement in classifying ALB efficacy according to WHO guidelines. This suggests that each method holds promise for the assessment of drug efficacy in the context of assessing drug efficacy within STH control programs.
138.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that although methods "agree in classifying drug efficacy," "No single diagnostic method provides ERR that are equivalent to single Kato-Katz for all STH." Thus agreement in classification is encouraging but not sufficient to conclude equivalence of quantitative ERR estimates, especially when sensitivity varies across species and settings. The authors distinguish agreement in classification from formal equivalence in ERR.

138.Option: The risk of differential misclassification due to variable diagnostic sensitivity must be evaluated to avoid invalid conclusions about equivalence in drug efficacy
Evidence: FECPAKG2 was previously evaluated as being less sensitive than Kato-Katz, while qPCR was found to have superior sensitivity for all STH [16, 26, 39].

When applying FECPAKG2, drug efficacy could not be measured in 438 (32.3%) of the 1,354 individuals with STH infection, because of false negative results at baseline (A. lumbricoides: 148/441, T. trichiura: 135/456 and hookworms: 155/457).

It is also important to point out that these findings are based on results obtained in sites where STH prevalence and intensities of STH infections are still relatively high. It is possible that the impact of the diagnostic sensitivity of a method on ERR calculations, as illustrated in animals, will increase when working in settings with very low infection intensities [46, 47].
138.Rationale: The passage documents substantial variability in diagnostic sensitivity (FECPAKG2 low, qPCR high) and shows that false negatives materially affected the ability to measure ERR (large proportion with no measurable efficacy using FECPAKG2). It also warns sensitivity impacts can grow in low-intensity settings. These statements support that differential misclassification from variable sensitivity is a critical factor to evaluate before concluding equivalence.

138.Option: Equivalence in drug efficacy can be concluded if the confidence intervals around the difference in egg reduction rates do not include zero, regardless of the diagnostic method's sensitivity
Evidence: This means that a method provides equivalent drug efficacy estimates as single Kato-Katz if the confidence intervals surrounding the mean difference in drug efficacy does not exceed these set of values (S1 Info).
The level of equivalence for A. lumbricoides, hookworms and T. trichiura was set arbitrarily at -/+2.5, -/+5.0 and -/+10-point percentage respectively.

As illustrated in supplementary information (S1 Info), the absence of a significant difference does not imply equivalent ERR estimates nor does the presence of a significant difference rule out equivalent ERR results.
138.Rationale: The passage defines equivalence in terms of pre-specified bounds around the difference in ERR (e.g. ±2.5%, ±5%, ±10%), not whether the CI excludes zero. Equivalence requires the CI to lie entirely within the predefined equivalence margins. The authors explicitly note that absence of significant difference is not the same as equivalence.

138.Option: High sensitivity of a diagnostic method ensures that equivalence in drug efficacy can be reliably assessed, even in settings with high drug pressure
Evidence: Although there were substantial differences in FECs across the different microscopic methods, this did not have a major impact on the equivalence of ERR.

For qPCR, there was substantial agreement for hookworms (κFleiss = 0.61, p< 0.001), moderate agreement for both A. lumbricoides (κFleiss = 0.59, p <0.001), and a fair agreement for T. trichiura (κFleiss = 0.36, p <0.001).

None of the evaluated tests provided equivalent results to those obtained by the currently recommended single Kato-Katz for all STH, but this might be due to the number of pairwise-comparisons and the strict bounds of equivalence.
138.Rationale: While high sensitivity (as with qPCR) can improve detection, the passage shows that superior sensitivity alone did not guarantee equivalence in ERR across species and sites; qPCR did not yield equivalent results for all STH and agreement varied by species. Also, factors like species-specific performance, predefined equivalence bounds, and setting (drug pressure, infection intensity) influence equivalence assessment, so high sensitivity is not sufficient by itself.

138.Option: The magnitude of the difference in egg reduction rate between methods should be compared to a fixed threshold of 10% to determine equivalence, regardless of species or setting
Evidence: The level of equivalence for A. lumbricoides, hookworms and T. trichiura was set arbitrarily at -/+2.5, -/+5.0 and -/+10-point percentage respectively.

Given the differences in drug efficacy of ALB across the STH species [8, 9] (A. lumbricoides: ~99%, hookworms: ~96%, T. trichiura: ~65%), a level of equivalence that is acceptable for T. trichiura may not be acceptable for A. lumbricoides.
138.Rationale: The passage explicitly uses species-specific equivalence thresholds (±2.5%, ±5.0%, ±10%) and argues that a single fixed threshold would be inappropriate because baseline efficacies differ markedly by species. Thus applying a universal 10% threshold regardless of species or setting is not supported.

139.Option: The proportion of the population that receives at least one dose of the drug during each annual round of MDA
Evidence: The Global Programme to Eliminate Lymphatic Filariasis (GPELF) aims to use mass drug administration (MDA) to interrupt transmission and to reduce morbidity, with annual doses of a multi-drug regimen for at least five years (www.filariasis.org) [3].

The African Programme for Onchocerciasis Control aims to establish by 2010 community-based sustainable ivermectin treatments of 50 million people in 19 African countries having meso- and hyper-endemicity (www.who.int/apoc/en/).
However, it appears unlikely that any of these MDA regimens will be sufficient to eliminate LF or onchocerciasis in all endemic areas [2].

Numerous technical challenges threaten the success of these eradication programs [2],[4], including incomplete efficacy of available drugs against adult filarial worms [5],[6], severe drug toxicity in people with heavy loiasis infections [3], and the risk that filarial worms will develop resistance to the drugs available for MDA [7],[8].
139.Rationale: While population coverage (the share treated each round) is an important operational determinant of MDA success (passage cites annual multi-dose regimens and large-scale treatment targets), the passage emphasizes several overarching technical threats (incomplete drug efficacy, toxicity, and especially emergence of resistance) that limit reliability of long-term protection. Thus, coverage alone is not presented as the most critical single factor determining the reliability of observed long-term outcomes.

139.Option: The duration of drug exposure in individual patients relative to the parasite life cycle
Evidence: Numerous technical challenges threaten the success of these eradication programs [2],[4], including incomplete efficacy of available drugs against adult filarial worms [5],[6], severe drug toxicity in people with heavy loiasis infections [3], and the risk that filarial worms will develop resistance to the drugs available for MDA [7],[8].

Doxycycline treatment has been shown to result in an almost complete loss of Mf and macrofilaricidal effects in LF and in sterilization of adult worms and macrofilaricidal effects in O. volvulus (reviewed in [16],[17]). However, the period of treatment with doxycycline at present (between 4–8 weeks) is not applicable to mass treatment strategies, due to both the logistical difficulties and contra-indication in children under eight and pregnant women [16],[17], as a result antibiotic treatment regimens require further refinement.
139.Rationale: Duration of exposure relative to parasite biology clearly matters (the passage notes doxycycline's long regimen and macrofilaricidal effects but logistical unsuitability for MDA). However, the passage frames this as a practical limitation rather than the single most critical factor for reliability of long-term protection; it highlights incomplete efficacy and, most prominently, the risk and demonstrated occurrence of drug resistance as central threats to reliable long-term outcomes.

139.Option: The consistency of drug delivery schedules across different communities in the MDA program
Evidence: The Global Programme to Eliminate Lymphatic Filariasis (GPELF) aims to use mass drug administration (MDA) to interrupt transmission and to reduce morbidity, with annual doses of a multi-drug regimen for at least five years (www.filariasis.org) [3].

The African Programme for Onchocerciasis Control aims to establish by 2010 community-based sustainable ivermectin treatments of 50 million people in 19 African countries having meso- and hyper-endemicity (www.who.int/apoc/en/).
However, it appears unlikely that any of these MDA regimens will be sufficient to eliminate LF or onchocerciasis in all endemic areas [2].

Numerous technical challenges threaten the success of these eradication programs [2],[4], including incomplete efficacy of available drugs against adult filarial worms [5],[6], severe drug toxicity in people with heavy loiasis infections [3], and the risk that filarial worms will develop resistance to the drugs available for MDA [7],[8].
139.Rationale: Consistency of delivery (timely annual rounds) is important operationally and the passage specifies annual rounds for at least five years. However, the text identifies multiple deeper biological and programmatic threats—especially drug resistance and incomplete adulticidal efficacy—that most critically undermine the reliability of long-term protection, so schedule consistency is not presented as the single most critical factor.

139.Option: The presence of co-infections with other parasitic organisms that may alter drug metabolism
Evidence: Numerous technical challenges threaten the success of these eradication programs [2],[4], including incomplete efficacy of available drugs against adult filarial worms [5],[6], severe drug toxicity in people with heavy loiasis infections [3], and the risk that filarial worms will develop resistance to the drugs available for MDA [7],[8].
However, it appears unlikely that any of these MDA regimens will be sufficient to eliminate LF or onchocerciasis in all endemic areas [2].
139.Rationale: The passage does note clinically important complications from co-endemic infections (specifically 'severe drug toxicity in people with heavy loiasis infections'), indicating co-infections can affect safety and program delivery. However, it does not emphasize altered drug metabolism from co-infections as the primary determinant of the reliability of long-term transmission outcomes. The text instead highlights incomplete efficacy and especially the risk of drug resistance as the critical biological threats to reliable long-term protection.

139.Option: The emergence and spread of drug-resistant parasite strains due to repeated exposure during mass drug administration
Evidence: Numerous technical challenges threaten the success of these eradication programs [2],[4], including incomplete efficacy of available drugs against adult filarial worms [5],[6], severe drug toxicity in people with heavy loiasis infections [3], and the risk that filarial worms will develop resistance to the drugs available for MDA [7],[8].
Ivermectin (IVM) resistance has been reported worldwide in several other parasitic nematodes [9].

The emergence of drug resistant strains of the O. volvulus parasite was initially suggested by reports of patients with onchocerciasis who failed to respond to IVM treatment [10],[11] and a recent report from Ghana has provided the first direct proof of IVM resistance in O. volvulus populations [12].

A number of studies have associated IVM resistance with genetic markers (reviewed in [13]), while a recent, more extensive study using worms taken from individuals before and after repeated IVM treatments, has demonstrated that IVM causes genetic selection on O. volvulus [14].
At present there are no alternative drugs for IVM for use in the onchocerciasis MDA programs that can reduce Mf or kill adult worms and no vaccines are available [15].
139.Rationale: The passage specifically identifies the risk and documented emergence of drug resistance (IVM resistance) as a major technical threat to the success and reliability of MDA programs. Resistance directly undermines sustained reductions in transmission and can make observed short-term treatment effects unreliable predictors of long-term protection, particularly when no alternative effective drugs or vaccines are available. Therefore emergence and spread of resistant parasite strains is the most critical factor affecting reliability of long-term outcomes.

140.Option: Use a time-to-event analysis to model the time from sample collection to deterioration, with biomarker levels as time-varying covariates
Evidence: The main limitation of our retrospective study is that our samples were collected from patients at the end of the four year period during which the extent of progression was analyzed as opposed to the beginning of the four year period, which would have been optimal for directly measuring their prognostic powers.
We expect that a prospective study in the future will reveal the answer to this question.

Of course, the patients with severe HAM/TSP symptoms began undergoing treatment soon after sample collection, rendering any observations on disease course after sample collection un-useable for analysis in this study.
140.Rationale: Time-to-event analysis with time-varying covariates requires prospective follow-up from a defined baseline (biomarker measurement) to outcome and usable post-sample observation time. The passage explicitly states samples were collected at the end of the four-year analysis window, that this retrospective timing is a main limitation, and that prospective studies are needed. Thus the study data do not support a valid time-to-event analysis here, making this option inappropriate for the present dataset.

140.Option: Compare biomarker levels between patients with long disease duration and those with short disease duration to assess whether progression rate is associated with biomarker levels
Evidence: To investigate the potential influence of disease duration as a secondary variable, a new test group was created containing only those patients for whom the disease onset date was 7–13 years prior to the sample collection day.
we confirmed that there was no significant difference in disease duration between these two groups.
The results remained consistent with our previous findings: CSF CXCL10, CXCL9, and neopterin were all elevated in deteriorating patients with respect to stable patients (p<0.01, Figure 5).
140.Rationale: Simply comparing biomarker levels between long- and short-duration patients does not directly test whether biomarkers predict progression independent of duration; it conflates duration and progression and may introduce confounding. The investigators instead restricted analysis to a subgroup with similar disease durations to control for this confounding. That subgroup-restricted approach (matching/holding duration constant) is a more direct method than splitting by long vs short durations, so this option is not the best choice.

140.Option: Stratify patients by disease duration and compare the odds of being deteriorating across strata, using biomarker levels as a predictor within each stratum
Evidence: To investigate the potential influence of disease duration as a secondary variable, a new test group was created containing only those patients for whom the disease onset date was 7–13 years prior to the sample collection day.
we confirmed that there was no significant difference in disease duration between these two groups.
The results remained consistent with our previous findings: CSF CXCL10, CXCL9, and neopterin were all elevated in deteriorating patients with respect to stable patients (p<0.01, Figure 5).
140.Rationale: Stratifying by duration and estimating biomarker effects within strata is a valid epidemiologic approach to assess independence from duration. However, the passage describes that the investigators controlled for duration by restricting to a subgroup with similar disease durations rather than stratifying across multiple duration strata. The subgroup restriction (holding duration constant) most directly demonstrates prediction independent of disease duration in this dataset. Given the study's limited sample size, stratification into multiple duration strata would be less feasible and less directly supportive than the subgroup/matching approach actually used.

140.Option: Compare biomarker levels between deteriorating and stable patients within a subgroup matched for disease duration to control for time-related confounding
Evidence: To investigate the potential influence of disease duration as a secondary variable, a new test group was created containing only those patients for whom the disease onset date was 7–13 years prior to the sample collection day.

Patients fitting this criterion were selected from the 53 total available from both the Training and Test sets: eight stable patients and ten deteriorating patients; we confirmed that there was no significant difference in disease duration between these two groups.
The results remained consistent with our previous findings: CSF CXCL10, CXCL9, and neopterin were all elevated in deteriorating patients with respect to stable patients (p<0.01, Figure 5).

The results of this study indicate that CXCL9 and/or CXCL10 may play a key role in the pathogenesis of HAM/TSP ... we concluded that of the potential biomarkers under study, CXCL10, CXCL9, and neopterin are the most fit for determining the level of spinal cord inflammation, and thus the most fit for predicting disease progression in HAM/TSP patients.
140.Rationale: The investigators explicitly performed an analysis restricting to patients with similar disease durations (7–13 years) and confirmed no significant duration difference between stable and deteriorating groups; they then showed the same biomarker associations. This approach (comparing groups matched/held constant on duration) most directly supports the conclusion that the biomarkers predict progression independent of illness duration by controlling time-related confounding in the retrospective dataset.

141.Option: Chest pain is a key early indicator of severe pulmonary complications in paragonimiasis due to its direct association with pleural invasion by migrating larvae
Evidence: There was a significant difference between the pneumothorax and non- pneumothorax groups in the number of subjects with chest pain and dyspnea (57.9% [11/19] vs 16.9% [27/160], p<0.001; 68.4% [13/19] vs 28.1% [45/160], p<0.001).

According to Yoshida A, et al. chest pain occurs when the Paragonimus larvae invades and injures the pleura, causing pneumothorax [1]. Therefore, although chest pain is less common in SSP, it is nevertheless an important clinical clue that suggests pneumothorax in SSP related to pulmonary paragonimiasis.

A subsequent multivariate analysis suggested that asthma (OR 8.10, 95% CI 1.43–45.91), chest pain (OR 8.15, 95% CI 2.70–24.58), and intrapulmonary lesions (OR 8.94, 95% CI 1.12–71.36) were independent risk factors for pulmonary paragonimiasis-associated pneumothorax (Table 5).
Most of patients with pneumothorax (89.5%, 17/19) underwent chest tube insertion as a first treatment.
141.Rationale: Chest pain is an acute clinical symptom that was significantly more common in patients with pneumothorax (57.9% vs 16.9%, p<0.001) and remained an independent predictor in multivariate analysis (OR 8.15). The authors explicitly link chest pain to pleural invasion by migrating Paragonimus larvae and state it is an important clinical clue for pneumothorax in paragonimiasis. Because pneumothorax is the severe complication that often required urgent intervention (e.g., chest tube in 89.5% of pneumothorax cases), chest pain most strongly suggests the presence of that urgent complication in this population.

141.Option: Pleural effusion is the most significant clinical feature indicating severe pulmonary complications because it is highly prevalent in patients with pneumothorax and reflects an inflammatory response to larval invasion
Evidence: The pneumothorax group showed a significantly higher prevalence of pleural effusion (78.9% vs 28.7%, p<0.001).
