Thus the direction claimed by the option (underreporting producing low reported case counts while incidence remains high) is not the most plausible single explanation for the observed discrepancy; the passage instead highlights population base differences and high Amazon incidence. Therefore this option is not the best explanation.

167.Option: The Andean region's higher socioeconomic status leads to better access to healthcare and earlier diagnosis, resulting in more reported cases, while the Amazon region's lower socioeconomic status results in delayed diagnosis and fewer reported cases despite high transmission
Evidence: social inequality, the absence of preventive measures in rural areas, deforestation, and geographical diversity allow the circulation of a wide array of species of both vector and parasite in up to 30 of the 32 departments in the country [3, 6, 8].
Andean region, which gathers over 50% of the country’s population, shows great socioeconomic diversity and highly variable geography [27].

These same departments report fewer cases to the central surveillance system than departments in other regions with more robust healthcare infrastructure. These same areas are often the most accessible to research groups, which strengthens the surveillance network, particularly in the Andean region.
167.Rationale: The passage notes socioeconomic diversity and differences in surveillance capacity, but it does not state that the Andean region uniformly has higher socioeconomic status driving more reported cases, nor does it assert that Amazon has uniformly lower SES causing delayed diagnosis and fewer reported cases. The text emphasizes population distribution and surveillance/reporting differences more than a simple SES dichotomy. Because the claim in the option is a broad SES explanation not directly supported by the passage, it is not correct as the primary explanation.

167.Option: The Andean region's higher urbanization and population density increase the number of susceptible individuals, leading to more cases, while the Amazon region's lower population density limits transmission despite high vector abundance
Evidence: Andean region, which gathers over 50% of the country’s population, shows great socioeconomic diversity and highly variable geography [27].
This was one of the most affected areas by the disease, with high incidences across all study periods.

The population in this region is relatively low, when compared with other regions with similar transmission rates (S1 Table), due in part to the large percentage of the territory occupied by tropical rainforest.
This can be attributed to the wealth of vector species and of mammals that serve as disease reservoirs, which helps maintain the epidemiological circuit and, in turn, transmission rates [8].
167.Rationale: The option's second clause is inconsistent with the passage: the Amazon region is described as having high incidence despite lower population, driven by vector/reservoir abundance. The passage does not support the idea that lower population density in the Amazon limits transmission; rather it explains high Amazon incidence by ecological factors. Therefore this option is incorrect.

167.Option: The Andean region's large population base dilutes its incidence rate despite high case counts, while the Amazon region's smaller population amplifies its incidence despite lower absolute case numbers
Evidence: Andean region, which gathers over 50% of the country’s population, shows great socioeconomic diversity and highly variable geography [27].

The population in this region is relatively low, when compared with other regions with similar transmission rates (S1 Table), due in part to the large percentage of the territory occupied by tropical rainforest.
This was one of the most affected areas by the disease, with high incidences across all study periods.

The Andean region, which gathers over 50% of the country’s population, shows great socioeconomic diversity and highly variable geography [27]. The opposite situation is seen in the Andean region, which is made up of the 11 departments that make up the majority of the country’s population, in which the large number of cases does not result in elevated incidences (Figs 1 and 5; S1 Table).
167.Rationale: The passage explicitly explains that the Andean region contains the majority of the population so that large absolute case counts there do not translate into high incidence rates, whereas the Amazon has a relatively small population but high incidence driven by vector/reservoir ecology. This directly matches the population-base dilution/amplification explanation in the option, making it the most supported factor accounting for the discrepancy.

167.Option: The Andean region's higher prevalence of multiple Leishmania species leads to more severe cases and thus higher case counts, while the Amazon region's lower species diversity results in milder disease and fewer reported cases
Evidence: Colombia is the country with the greatest number of circulating species in the world, 10 in total (L. panamensis, L. braziliensis, L. guyanensis, L. infantum chagasi, L. mexicana, L. lainsoni, L. amazonensis, L. colombiensis, L. equatoriensis, L. naiffi), and that the most prevalent species exhibit high genetic diversity [9–11].

Likewise, the great diversity of circulating Leishmania species in Colombia plays a fundamental role in the permanence and propagation of the disease, given that neither epidemiological surveillance, nor treatment, take this variable into account.
Antioquia showed the highest number of vector species, 11 overall, followed by Meta, Caldas, Boyacá, and Amazonas, with 9 species each.
167.Rationale: While the passage emphasizes species diversity as important for transmission and permanence, it does not state that the Andean region has higher Leishmania species prevalence causing more severe disease nor that the Amazon has lower species diversity. In fact, Amazonas is listed among departments with high vector/species counts. The option's claim about species-driven differences in severity and reporting between Andean and Amazon regions is not supported by the provided text, so it is incorrect.

168.Option: The co-expression of Tim-3 and Tax on CD4+ T cells in HTLV-1-infected individuals indicates that these cells are in a state of exhaustion, which contributes to disease progression
Evidence: We observed that Tax was expressed on PBMC from some subjects following 24 hours of culture and was detected on both Tim-3+ as well as Tim-3− CD4+ T cells (Figure 4A).
In this study, we report that CD8+ and CD4+ T cells in HTLV-1 infection express lower levels of Tim-3, and this was more pronounced in patients with HAM/TSP.
We propose that this low expression of Tim-3 on HTLV-1 Tax-specific T cells may lead to a persistent and deleterious effector T cell pool leading to more inflammation.
168.Rationale: The option claims that co-expression of Tim-3 and Tax indicates exhaustion. The passage instead reports that Tax is present on both Tim-3+ and Tim-3− CD4+ T cells (so co-expression is not uniquely characteristic) and emphasizes that overall Tim-3 levels are reduced in HTLV-1 infection. The authors interpret low Tim-3 as marking hyperfunctional/persistent effector T cells rather than exhaustion. Therefore the stated inference (co-expression indicates exhaustion driving disease) is not supported by the quoted text.

168.Option: The absence of co-expression of Tim-3 and PD-1 on CD8+ T cells in HTLV-1-infected individuals indicates a lack of T cell exhaustion, which supports the hypothesis of persistent T cell activation driving disease progression
Evidence: Only a few T cells co-expressed both Tim-3 and PD-1, and no differences were observed between uninfected subjects and those with HTLV-1 asymptomatic infection or HAM/TSP patients.

We observed a significant decrease in the frequency of Tim-3+ PD-1− expressing CD8+ and CD4+ T cells among HTLV-1 seropositive subjects (CD8+: median 8.01%, IQR 5.42–10.50; CD4+: median 4.3%, IQR 3.50–5.99) compared to HTLV-1 seronegative controls (CD8+ median 15.10%, IQR 10.50–17.60; CD4+: median 6.84%, IQR 5.74–7.85) (Figure 1A and B).
We propose that this low expression of Tim-3 on HTLV-1 Tax-specific T cells may lead to a persistent and deleterious effector T cell pool leading to more inflammation.
168.Rationale: While the passage notes that few T cells co-express Tim-3 and PD-1, the authors do not present the mere absence of co-expression as the primary evidence of lack of exhaustion. Instead they emphasize reduced Tim-3 expression (and an interpretation that low Tim-3 marks hyperfunctional/persistent T cells). The option's logic (absence of co-expression → lack of exhaustion → supports persistent activation) is an indirect and unsupported framing compared with the direct evidence the authors present (inverse relation of Tim-3 with function). Thus this option overstates what the co-expression data demonstrates and is not the best-supported finding.

168.Option: The higher frequency of Tim-3+ PD-1− CD8+ T cells in HTLV-1-infected individuals compared to controls would indicate a state of T cell exhaustion that contributes to disease progression
Evidence: We observed a significant decrease in the frequency of Tim-3+ PD-1− expressing CD8+ and CD4+ T cells among HTLV-1 seropositive subjects (CD8+: median 8.01%, IQR 5.42–10.50; CD4+: median 4.3%, IQR 3.50–5.99) compared to HTLV-1 seronegative controls (CD8+ median 15.10%, IQR 10.50–17.60; CD4+: median 6.84%, IQR 5.74–7.85) (Figure 1A and B).
In this study, we report that CD8+ and CD4+ T cells in HTLV-1 infection express lower levels of Tim-3, and this was more pronounced in patients with HAM/TSP.
168.Rationale: This option asserts a higher frequency of Tim-3+ PD-1− CD8+ T cells in infected individuals, but the passage explicitly reports a significant decrease in Tim-3+ PD-1− cells in HTLV-1 seropositive subjects versus controls. Furthermore, the authors interpret low Tim-3 (not high Tim-3) as consistent with a hyperfunctional, potentially pathogenic T cell population. Therefore the option contradicts the reported data and interpretation.

168.Option: Lower Tim-3 expression on HTLV-1-specific CD8+ T cells is associated with higher IFN-γ production, suggesting a hyperfunctional T cell state that may drive immunopathology
Evidence: We observed a statistically significant inverse correlation between the frequency of Tim-3 on both Tax-specific as well as CMV-specific CD8+ T cells and the amount of IFN-γ secreted (r = −0.8982; P = 0.0046; r = 0.9710; P = 0.0028; Figure 3A).
In this study, we report that CD8+ and CD4+ T cells in HTLV-1 infection express lower levels of Tim-3, and this was more pronounced in patients with HAM/TSP.
We propose that this low expression of Tim-3 on HTLV-1 Tax-specific T cells may lead to a persistent and deleterious effector T cell pool leading to more inflammation.
168.Rationale: These quoted findings directly support the hypothesis: Tim-3 frequency is inversely correlated with IFN-γ secretion (i.e., lower Tim-3 corresponds to higher cytokine production), the study documents lower Tim-3 on HTLV-1 T cells (especially Tax-specific cells), and the authors interpret low Tim-3 as marking a persistent, hyperfunctional effector pool that could mediate immunopathology. This is the strongest immunological evidence in the paper linking persistent T cell activation to disease progression.

168.Option: Elevated PD-1 expression on HTLV-1-specific CD8+ T cells is associated with increased cytokine production, indicating a state of chronic T cell activation that contributes to disease progression
Evidence: The frequency of PD-1 expression on Tax-specific or CMV-specific CD8+ T cells also did not associate with the amount of IFN-γ secreted (r = 0.1317; P = 0.7520 and r = 0.2245; P = 0.594, respectively) (Figure 3B).

The pattern of expression of PD-1 in HTLV-1 infection has recently been shown to be elevated on T cells in HTLV-1 carriers and also on CMV and EBV specific T cells in asymptomatic carriers compared to healthy controls [52].

Our data suggests that PD-1 and Tim-3 on antigen specific CD8+ T cells are functionally different, and this may reflect a distinct stage of differentiation. PD-1 appears to mark early T-cell activation and exhaustion, while Tim-3 represents a more terminal stage of impairment.
168.Rationale: Although PD-1 expression is reported as elevated in some contexts, the passage explicitly states that PD-1 frequency on Tax- or CMV-specific CD8+ T cells did not correlate with IFN-γ secretion (no association). The authors further suggest PD-1 may mark activation rather than the hyperfunctional effector phenotype linked to pathology, and they identify Tim-3 (low expression) as the marker associated with increased cytokine production. Therefore the specific claim that elevated PD-1 is associated with increased cytokine production and supports chronic activation driving disease is not supported by the data presented.

169.Option: Since ZIKV does not cause pathological damage in mosquito ovaries, it cannot be maintained in mosquito populations through vertical transmission
Evidence: No pathogenetic injury was found in the germarium, oocytes, trophocytes and follicular epithelium in the ZIKV-positive ovaries compared with the control ovaries.
Ae. albopictus of the Foshan strain can transmit ZIKV vertically from G0 to the adult F1 generation, which is clear evidence that this vector mosquito can vertically transmit ZIKV.

Vertically infected F1 female offspring transmitted ZIKV to C57BL/6 suckling mice via mosquito bites, which is the first evidence that female offspring infected through vertical transmission can initiate a new horizontal cycle.

congenitally infected females are able to carry the virus for their entire life and transmit the virus horizontally by feeding without the requirement for an extrinsic incubation period (approximately 8 to 14 days) [31].

Considering this situation, the low filial infection rates indicated that vertical transmission is an alternate transmission mechanism, and ZIKV might use this mechanism to maintain in a vector population, which could play a significant role in establishing endemicity.
169.Rationale: The option asserts that absence of pathological damage in ovaries implies inability to be maintained by vertical transmission. The passage indeed states there was no pathogenetic injury to ovaries, but it also presents direct evidence of vertical transmission to F1 eggs and adults and shows that vertically infected females can transmit to mice and potentially maintain virus in the vector population. The authors explicitly state vertical transmission could help maintain ZIKV and contribute to endemicity. Therefore the inference in the option is incorrect.

169.Option: ZIKV vertical transmission in Ae. albopictus is limited to the first gonotrophic cycle and does not support long-term persistence in mosquito populations
Evidence: Starting from 4 dpi, the ovaries showed evidence of infection and remained positive throughout the remainder of the experiment (Fig 1A).

During the FGC, the infection rate of the ovaries was 19.04% at 4 dpi, 30.43% at 7 dpi, 39.28% at 10 dpi and 75% at 14 dpi. During the SGC, the infection rates of the ovaries were similar and reached the maximum value (100%) at 33 dpi.
Ae. albopictus of the Foshan strain can transmit ZIKV vertically from G0 to the adult F1 generation, which is clear evidence that this vector mosquito can vertically transmit ZIKV.

Vertically infected F1 female offspring transmitted ZIKV to C57BL/6 suckling mice via mosquito bites, which is the first evidence that female offspring infected through vertical transmission can initiate a new horizontal cycle.
169.Rationale: The option claims vertical transmission is limited to the first gonotrophic cycle. The passage shows ovarian infection began early and persisted, with infection rates rising and reaching 100% by 33 dpi in the second gonotrophic cycle (SGC). It also documents transmission to F1 adults and onward transmission to mice. Thus vertical transmission is not limited to the first cycle and the data do not support the claim that it cannot support persistence.

169.Option: The absence of significant differences in hatching, pupation, and emergence rates between ZIKV-infected and control mosquitoes implies that vertical transmission has no ecological impact on mosquito populations
Evidence: In the control group, the rates of hatching, pupation and emergence were 61.01%, 93.08% and 95.15%, respectively, while the rates in the ZIKV group were 57.49%, 91.19% and 98.12%, respectively. No significant difference was found between them.
However, at 33 dpi of the SGC, the number of eggs in the control groups was significantly higher than that in the ZIKV groups (Fig 3B) (t = 2.767, P = 0.008).
These results suggested that ZIKV infection did not prevent the growth and development of the offspring.
These results implied that flaviviruses (ZIKV and DENV) may negatively influence oogenesis in Ae. albopictus.
169.Rationale: While the passage reports no significant differences in hatching, pupation and emergence rates, it also reports a significant reduction in number of eggs at 33 dpi and suggests a negative influence on oogenesis. Therefore it is incorrect to conclude that vertical transmission has no ecological impact; some reproductive parameters (egg number/fecundity) were affected, so the blanket statement of no ecological impact is not supported.

169.Option: Vertical transmission enables Zika virus to persist in mosquito populations across generations, allowing for re-initiation of human transmission cycles even without ongoing external exposure
Evidence: Ae. albopictus of the Foshan strain can transmit ZIKV vertically from G0 to the adult F1 generation, which is clear evidence that this vector mosquito can vertically transmit ZIKV.

Vertically infected F1 female offspring transmitted ZIKV to C57BL/6 suckling mice via mosquito bites, which is the first evidence that female offspring infected through vertical transmission can initiate a new horizontal cycle.

congenitally infected females are able to carry the virus for their entire life and transmit the virus horizontally by feeding without the requirement for an extrinsic incubation period (approximately 8 to 14 days) [31].

Considering this situation, the low filial infection rates indicated that vertical transmission is an alternate transmission mechanism, and ZIKV might use this mechanism to maintain in a vector population, which could play a significant role in establishing endemicity.
169.Rationale: The passage provides direct experimental evidence that vertically infected offspring survive to adulthood, harbor virus in relevant tissues (salivary glands), and can transmit to mammalian hosts. The authors explicitly interpret vertical transmission as an alternate mechanism that could maintain ZIKV in vector populations and help establish endemicity, enabling re-initiation of horizontal transmission cycles without continuous external exposure. Thus this option is supported by the evidence.

169.Option: The low filial infection rate of 2.06% in F1 eggs indicates that vertical transmission is insufficient to sustain ZIKV in mosquito populations over time
Evidence: In this study, the minimal filial infection rates in eggs from two gonotrophic cycles were 2.06% and 0.69%, without statistical significance (p>0.05).

the effective population transmission rate (minimum of 1.87%) was calculated in F1 adult tissues instead of F1 adult whole bodies; this result indicated that ZIKV can not only vertically transmit to F1 eggs but also persistently exist in F1 adults, even though the ratio was not high.

Vertically infected F1 female offspring transmitted ZIKV to C57BL/6 suckling mice via mosquito bites, which is the first evidence that female offspring infected through vertical transmission can initiate a new horizontal cycle.

Considering this situation, the low filial infection rates indicated that vertical transmission is an alternate transmission mechanism, and ZIKV might use this mechanism to maintain in a vector population, which could play a significant role in establishing endemicity.
169.Rationale: Although filial infection rates reported are low, the authors show that vertically infected F1 adults exist and can transmit virus to mammalian hosts. They conclude that despite low rates, vertical transmission can function as an alternate mechanism to maintain ZIKV in vector populations and contribute to endemicity. Thus the statement that low filial infection rate alone makes vertical transmission insufficient to sustain ZIKV over time is not supported by the passage.

170.Option: The higher proportion of rural health facilities in Tororo district compared to Wakiso district led to lower RHD service readiness due to geographic isolation and transportation barriers
Evidence: 50% of facilities were located in rural areas (Table 1).
Tororo district in the Eastern region generally had the lowest scores on all indicators in this study.
Compared to Lira and Mbarara, at the time of this study the Tororo district had not served as a site for any RHD-related research projects or clinical activities (such as case registers) to date.
170.Rationale: The passage does not state that Tororo had a higher proportion of rural facilities relative to Wakiso; it reports a 50% rural share overall but gives no district-specific rural proportion. Importantly, the authors explicitly link Tororo’s low scores to absence of RHD research/clinical activities rather than to rurality. Because the passage provides direct attribution to lack of prior RHD initiatives in Tororo, the rurality explanation is not supported as the most direct factor by the text.

170.Option: The absence of prior RHD-specific research and clinical programs in Tororo district likely contributed to lower health system capacity and readiness compared to districts with such initiatives
Evidence: Tororo district in the Eastern region generally had the lowest scores on all indicators in this study.
Compared to Lira and Mbarara, at the time of this study the Tororo district had not served as a site for any RHD-related research projects or clinical activities (such as case registers) to date.

We note that, though the overall level of health worker awareness was low in our sample, the proportion of health workers that had received training was higher in Lira and Mbarara districts compared to Wakiso and Tororo districts. Awareness may have been increased due to concurrent RHD research activities in those two districts.
170.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies lack of prior RHD research/clinical activity in Tororo as a distinguishing feature and links higher awareness/training in other districts to concurrent research activities. The authors state Tororo had not hosted RHD projects or case registers and suggest that districts with research/clinical programs had higher readiness; this directly explains the inter-district disparity in RHD service readiness.

170.Option: The lower number of nurses per health facility in Tororo district compared to Wakiso district resulted in reduced RHD service readiness due to workforce shortages
Evidence: A higher number of nurses in a health facility was also associated with a significantly higher RHD readiness score.
Tororo district in the Eastern region generally had the lowest scores on all indicators in this study.
Compared to Lira and Mbarara, at the time of this study the Tororo district had not served as a site for any RHD-related research projects or clinical activities (such as case registers) to date.
170.Rationale: While the passage notes that more nurses at a facility are associated with higher readiness, it does not provide district-level data showing that Tororo had fewer nurses than Wakiso. The authors directly point to absence of RHD research/clinical programs as the likely explanation for Tororo’s low scores. Because there is no explicit evidence in the text that Tororo had fewer nurses (or that that was the primary driver), this workforce-count explanation is not supported as the most direct factor by the provided passage.

170.Option: Differences in the availability of essential antibiotics like benzathine penicillin G and amoxicillin across districts explain the variation in RHD service readiness despite similar population-level risk factors
Evidence: Amoxicillin and benzathine penicillin G were widely available across all health facility levels.
Tororo district in the Eastern region generally had the lowest scores on all indicators in this study.
Compared to Lira and Mbarara, at the time of this study the Tororo district had not served as a site for any RHD-related research projects or clinical activities (such as case registers) to date.
170.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that amoxicillin and benzathine penicillin G were widely available across facility levels, so differential availability of these essential antibiotics is not indicated as the main cause of inter-district differences. Instead, the text points to lack of prior RHD research/clinical activity and lower provider training/awareness in Tororo as the explanatory factors.

170.Option: The lack of national policy prioritization for RHD in Tororo district, unlike in Wakiso, led to lower service readiness due to insufficient government funding and political will
Evidence: Participants also expressed concern over lack of national policies regarding RHD. They felt that, because of insufficient policy prioritization, funding for RHD services in their districts has remained poor:

"..right now… [the government] are not even aware that [RHD is] there… I have always sat on meetings, [and] we have always done the budget together, but there has never been any funding for RHD." (HW 008 Lira)
Compared to Lira and Mbarara, at the time of this study the Tororo district had not served as a site for any RHD-related research projects or clinical activities (such as case registers) to date.
170.Rationale: The passage describes lack of national policy prioritization as a general barrier across districts, not a Tororo-specific difference relative to Wakiso. The text instead highlights absence of RHD research/clinical projects and resulting lower training/awareness in Tororo as the salient explanation for its lower readiness. Therefore, a claim that Tororo uniquely lacked national policy compared to Wakiso is not supported by the passage.

171.Option: The detection of viral antigen and RNA in the brain and lungs of animals that reached endpoint criteria, but not in surviving animals, confirms that NiV replication was necessary for disease development
Evidence: The brains of animals that reached euthanasia criteria showed a consistently high viral load, regardless of the inoculation dose (Fig 2A).
Similarly, high virus titers were observed in the brains of these animals (Fig 2B).
In all animals that reached endpoint criteria, small foci of viral antigen were detected in type I and type II pneumocytes, and occasionally within endothelial cells (Fig 3B and 3D).
Neither lesions nor viral antigen were detected in animals who survived the intracranial challenge or mock-inoculated animals (Fig 5J and 5K).
Multifocal to regionally extensive virus antigen and viral RNA positivity was detected throughout the leptomeninges on IHC and ISH, respectively (Fig 5D and 5G).
171.Rationale: Direct evidence of viral replication (high viral RNA, high virus titers, and viral antigen and RNA localized to lesions) in animals that developed disease—and absence of antigen/lesions in survivors—supports a causal role for NiV in producing the neurological disease. In the context of an experimental framework with control groups, demonstration that pathology co-occurs with viral replication in sick animals but is absent in survivors or controls is strong mechanistic evidence that the virus, not nonspecific procedural factors, produced the disease.

171.Option: The absence of neutrophilic infiltrates in mock-inoculated animals and the identification of a non-bacterial origin for the 16S rRNA signal rule out procedural trauma and bacterial contamination as causes of the neurological disease
Evidence: One of the unusual features of neuropathological lesions observed upon intracranial inoculation is the presence of large number of neutrophils.
However, no bacteria were visible in tissue slides and neutrophils were not observed in intracranially inoculated mock animals.

To definitively prove that no bacterial contamination occurred during the inoculation procedure, RNA was extracted from brain tissues and 16S rRNA RT-PCR was performed. We detected a nonspecific amplified product of 650 bp (S4A Fig). Sanger sequencing showed the amplified product corresponds to a mitochondrial gene of the Syrian hamster (S4B Fig).
Thus, the neutrophilic infiltrate observed in intracranially inoculated hamsters was due to NiV infection.
171.Rationale: Ruling out alternative explanations (procedural trauma or bacterial contamination) is critical to isolating the causal effect of the virus. The passage explicitly states neutrophils were absent in mock-inoculated animals (so the procedure alone did not produce the neutrophilic lesions), and 16S sequencing showed the PCR product was host mitochondrial sequence rather than bacterial sequence, excluding bacterial contamination. These observations strengthen the inference that NiV infection — not non-specific procedural or bacterial causes — produced the observed neuropathology.

171.Option: The rapid progression of disease within 2–6 days post-inoculation, consistent with direct CNS access, supports that NiV was the cause of neurological disease rather than procedural effects
Evidence: Animals inoculated with 1–3125 TCID50 NiV-M uniformly displayed severe neurological signs and reached endpoint criteria within 2–6 days post inoculation (dpi) (Fig 1).
direct inoculation of NiV into the cerebellomedullary cistern results in a rapid progression to severe neurological disease.
All mock-inoculated animals survived until study endpoint at 14 dpi (Fig 1).
171.Rationale: While rapid onset of disease after inoculation is consistent with a pathogen effect, rapid progression by itself does not rule out procedural artifacts unless contrasted with appropriate controls. The passage does show rapid disease after NiV inoculation and that mock-inoculated animals survived, which together are supportive; however the option asserts that rapid progression alone is the key support. Because the faster timeline could reflect many factors, and the strongest evidence comes from comparison with mock controls and detection of virus in lesions, the statement as phrased (relying on rapid progression alone) is not the most directly supportive feature of the randomized/control framework and therefore is not correct as the best answer.

171.Option: The uniformity of neurological signs across all animals inoculated with 1–3125 TCID50 NiV-M, combined with the absence of signs in mock-inoculated animals, indicates that the disease was specifically caused by NiV
Evidence: Animals inoculated with 1–3125 TCID50 NiV-M uniformly displayed severe neurological signs and reached endpoint criteria within 2–6 days post inoculation (dpi) (Fig 1).
All mock-inoculated animals survived until study endpoint at 14 dpi (Fig 1).
Neither lesions nor viral antigen were detected in animals who survived the intracranial challenge or mock-inoculated animals (Fig 5J and 5K).
171.Rationale: The defining feature of randomized controlled frameworks that isolates causal effects is the comparison between intervention and control groups. Here, the consistent occurrence of severe neurological disease in inoculated groups and the clear absence of disease in mock-inoculated controls provides the direct between-group contrast needed to attribute the effect to NiV rather than to the procedure or other nonspecific factors. Uniformity in the exposed groups plus a disease-free control group is classic experimental evidence supporting causation.

171.Option: The dose-dependent development of neurological disease, with no signs at doses below 0.04 TCID50, supports a causal link to NiV rather than procedural or non-specific factors
Evidence: The time to euthanasia was dose-dependent, with the animals that received the highest dose reaching euthanasia criteria as early as 2 dpi, and animals that received lower doses reaching euthanasia criteria 1–2 days later.

Lowering the inoculation dose to 0.2 TCID50 resulted in only 50% of animals requiring euthanasia between 4–6 dpi; no signs of disease were observed in the surviving animals. Doses of 0.04 TCID50 or below did not result in disease signs nor lethality.
171.Rationale: A dose-response relationship is one of the strongest pieces of evidence for a causal effect: increasing exposure produces increased likelihood or severity of outcome. The passage documents dose-dependent disease and absence of disease at very low doses, indicating the observed neurological disease scales with viral dose and is therefore unlikely to be due to the inoculation procedure itself or other non-specific factors. Although dose-response is not the same experimental feature as randomization, it is a key element of causal inference in experimental designs and supports the conclusion that NiV caused the disease.

172.Option: Direct transmission from infected cat fleas to other arthropods via cofeeding on a shared vertebrate host can explain R. felis presence in non-flea vectors
Evidence: Thus, horizontal transmission of R. felis from infected to naïve fleas via cofeeding was determined to be sufficient to perpetuate the agent within populations of cat fleas [8,9].

To test the hypothesis that R. felis-infected cat fleas may contribute to dissemination of the agent to other hematophagous vectors, cofeeding bioassays were employed to examine rickettsial transmission from infected cat fleas (C. felis) to ticks (D. variabilis) and mosquitoes (Anopheles quadrimaculatus) on a vertebrate host.
The data suggest R. felis-infected cat fleas transmit rickettsiae to other hematophagous vectors via cofeeding on a vertebrate host.
R. felis was detected in 75% (15/20) D. variabilis nymphs that were forcibly removed from the host 24 hrs after cofeeding.
Naïve An. quadrimaculatus were able to acquire infection through cofeeding with R. felis-infected cat fleas.
172.Rationale: The passage explicitly demonstrates and concludes that infected cat fleas can transmit R. felis to other arthropods through cofeeding on a shared vertebrate host, and provides experimental results (ticks and mosquitoes acquiring infection after cofeeding). This mechanism directly explains detection of the pathogen in non-flea arthropods without requiring infectious vertebrate bloodborne transmission.

172.Option: Vertical transmission from infected female ticks to their offspring could account for R. felis presence in non-flea arthropods, even without vertebrate host involvement
Evidence: laboratory studies have demonstrated R. felis was vertically transmitted by the American dog tick, Dermacentor variabilis, when adult ticks were exposed via capillary feeding [14].
Detection of R. felis in male mosquitoes in China and unfed adult mosquitoes in the United States suggests vertical transmission in nature [17,18].
Under laboratory conditions, adult D. variabilis are susceptible to infection and subsequent transovarial transmission of R. felis for at least one generation [14].
172.Rationale: The passage documents laboratory evidence that vertical (transovarial) transmission can occur in non-flea arthropods (ticks) and provides field-based suggestions of vertical transmission in mosquitoes (detection in males and unfed females). Thus vertical transmission among non-flea arthropods is a plausible mechanism by which R. felis could persist and be detected in these vectors without requiring direct vertebrate-to-arthropod transmission.

172.Option: R. felis may be transmitted from infected vertebrate hosts to non-flea arthropods through blood-feeding, even if the pathogen is not detectable in the host's bloodstream
Evidence: Cofeeding transmission occurs when a pathogen is transmitted between two arthropods feeding in proximity on a vertebrate host, negating the intrinsic incubation period required for some pathogens during transmission events on a vertebrate host [39].
The cofeeding mechanism has been described for both flea- and tick- borne rickettsial pathogens, and occurs in the absence of a rickettsemic host [9,40].

There are several pieces of evidence supporting the potential for horizontal transmission of R. felis to vertebrate hosts, such as identification of R. felis in the salivary glands of cat fleas [41] and detection of R. felis in the blood of vertebrate hosts exposed to R. felis-infected fleas [5,42–44].
172.Rationale: While the passage notes R. felis has been detected in vertebrate blood in some studies, it emphasizes that cofeeding transmission can and does occur 'in the absence of a rickettsemic host.' The question states there is limited evidence for vertebrate-to-arthropod transmission; the passage presents cofeeding (arthropod-to-arthropod on the host) as the primary alternative. The specific claim that non-flea arthropods acquire infection from vertebrate blood despite the pathogen being undetectable in blood is speculative and not supported by the passage, which highlights cofeeding and vertical routes rather than occult bloodborne acquisition.

172.Option: R. felis may persist in the environment and be acquired by non-flea arthropods through contact with contaminated surfaces on the vertebrate host
Evidence: Fleas deposit rickettsiae in the dermis at the vector-host skin interface resulting in dissemination across the skin [9], presumably where other arthropods, including ticks that have mouthparts in the same tissue, can acquire the agent during feeding events;

Cofeeding transmission occurs when a pathogen is transmitted between two arthropods feeding in proximity on a vertebrate host, negating the intrinsic incubation period required for some pathogens during transmission events on a vertebrate host [39].
172.Rationale: The passage describes deposition of rickettsiae into the dermis and dissemination across the skin and emphasizes arthropod-to-arthropod acquisition via feeding proximity (cofeeding). It does not present evidence that R. felis persists on external surfaces or that superficial environmental contamination of host skin surfaces is a route leading to acquisition by other arthropods. The described mechanism is biological (dermal dissemination and cofeeding), not environmental surface persistence.

172.Option: Horizontal transmission of R. felis from infected mosquitoes to other arthropods via cofeeding on a vertebrate host could explain the presence of the pathogen in non-flea vectors
Evidence: horizontal transmission of R. felis by Anopheles gambiae was reported using a laboratory model of infection [19].

To test the hypothesis that R. felis-infected cat fleas may contribute to dissemination of the agent to other hematophagous vectors, cofeeding bioassays were employed to examine rickettsial transmission from infected cat fleas (C. felis) to ticks (D. variabilis) and mosquitoes (Anopheles quadrimaculatus) on a vertebrate host.
The current study demonstrated that R. felis-infected donor cat fleas can transmit the agent to naïve vectors via cofeeding on vertebrate hosts.
172.Rationale: Although the passage notes that Anopheles gambiae has been reported to engage in horizontal transmission in a laboratory model, the experimental evidence presented in this study shows directionality from infected cat fleas to naïve ticks and mosquitoes via cofeeding. There is no direct evidence in the passage showing infected mosquitoes act as donors to other arthropods via cofeeding; the primary demonstrated donor in cofeeding transmission is the infected cat flea. Therefore mosquito-to-other-arthropod cofeeding transmission is speculative based on this text.

173.Option: Delayed sample collection may lead to underestimation of acute infections, particularly for pathogens with short-lived biomarkers like NS1 antigen or rickettsial DNA, and reliance on serology without paired samples may misclassify past exposure as current infection
Evidence: Of 76 (30%) patients with dengue as a single disease, 71/76 (93%) had positive NS1 and/or PCR assays and, 40/58 (68%) patients had seroconversion;
Of 16 patients with scrub typhus, 8 (50%) were culture and/or PCR positive whilst 8 were diagnosed by only paired sera IFA.
In contrast, 14 (93%) of murine typhus patients were diagnosed by paired sera IFA and only one patient was PCR positive (Table 3).
Murai et al. [58] found that Orientia tsutsugamushi DNA was detectable in patient’s blood until day 3–8 after the start of chemotherapy with minocycline or doxycycline.

convalescence samples were not available for all serological investigations (17%, 37% and 56% without convalescence samples for dengue/JEV ELISA, leptospiral MAT and scrub/murine typhus IFA, respectively) and we may therefore have missed some patients who seroconverted.

Forty percent of pregnant women in this study did not have confirmed diagnoses; of these 18% received antibiotics before collecting samples for cultures, which may have reduced the frequency of positive culture results [63].
173.Rationale: The passage documents that antigen (NS1) and PCR assays detect acute dengue well when present early (71/76 positive by NS1/PCR), while many rickettsial and other infections were diagnosed only by paired sera IFA, indicating the need for convalescent samples to demonstrate seroconversion. It also cites limited windows of PCR detectability for rickettsial DNA and notes missing convalescent samples and pre-sampling antibiotics reduced detection. Therefore, delayed sample collection can underestimate acute infections with short-lived markers and reliance on single serology without paired samples can misclassify prior exposure as current infection.

173.Option: The use of PCR and antigen detection methods at admission provides the most reliable evidence of acute infection, minimizing the risk of misclassification due to timing of sample collection
Evidence: Of 76 (30%) patients with dengue as a single disease, 71/76 (93%) had positive NS1 and/or PCR assays and, 40/58 (68%) patients had seroconversion;
In contrast, 14 (93%) of murine typhus patients were diagnosed by paired sera IFA and only one patient was PCR positive (Table 3).
Murai et al. [58] found that Orientia tsutsugamushi DNA was detectable in patient’s blood until day 3–8 after the start of chemotherapy with minocycline or doxycycline.

convalescence samples were not available for all serological investigations (17%, 37% and 56% without convalescence samples for dengue/JEV ELISA, leptospiral MAT and scrub/murine typhus IFA, respectively) and we may therefore have missed some patients who seroconverted.
173.Rationale: While PCR and antigen detection at admission provided high yield for dengue in this study (NS1/PCR positive in most dengue cases) and are strong tools for identifying acute infection, the passage also makes clear that their sensitivity depends on timing—some pathogens (e.g., rickettsiae) were mostly identified by paired serology and PCR positivity can be short-lived. The statement that PCR/antigen testing 'minimizes' misclassification regardless of timing overstates the case; timing still matters and absence of early testing can yield false negatives, so the option is not fully correct as phrased.

173.Option: The absence of convalescent samples limits the ability to confirm acute infection for all pathogens, but this is not a major concern because most infections are self-limiting and severity is determined by clinical presentation alone
Evidence: convalescence samples were not available for all serological investigations (17%, 37% and 56% without convalescence samples for dengue/JEV ELISA, leptospiral MAT and scrub/murine typhus IFA, respectively) and we may therefore have missed some patients who seroconverted.

Forty percent of pregnant women in this study did not have confirmed diagnoses; of these 18% received antibiotics before collecting samples for cultures, which may have reduced the frequency of positive culture results [63].

Of 76 patients with dengue, 21 (28%) had severe outcome, including 1 maternal death, 6 miscarriages, 2 stillbirths, 9 preterm births, 1 congenital abnormality (ventricular septal defect (VSD)) and 1 vertical transmission (Table 4).

Important limitations of this study include that it was hospital, rather than community, based and therefore could not estimate the incidence of causes of fevers amongst pregnant women in the community and we were not able to recruit matched afebrile pregnant women.
173.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that missing convalescent samples likely caused missed seroconversions, so absence of paired sera is an important limitation. The claim that this is 'not a major concern' is contradicted by the documented proportion of unconfirmed diagnoses (40%) and by the fact that severity (including maternal death, miscarriages, congenital abnormalities) was observed and cannot be fully assessed by clinical presentation alone. Therefore the option is incorrect.

173.Option: Testing for all pathogens simultaneously at admission ensures accurate assessment of disease severity, as it captures the full spectrum of infections regardless of the timing of symptom onset
Evidence: convalescence samples were not available for all serological investigations (17%, 37% and 56% without convalescence samples for dengue/JEV ELISA, leptospiral MAT and scrub/murine typhus IFA, respectively) and we may therefore have missed some patients who seroconverted.

Forty percent of pregnant women in this study did not have confirmed diagnoses; of these 18% received antibiotics before collecting samples for cultures, which may have reduced the frequency of positive culture results [63].
We were not able to investigate diverse other important viral infections in pregnancy [64] or other potential pathogens such as Coxiella burnetii.
Approximately 20% of patients came to hospital with influenza-like illnesses but we were unable to test for respiratory viruses.
173.Rationale: Simultaneous testing at admission can identify many acute infections, but the passage shows that some pathogens require convalescent serology to demonstrate seroconversion, prior antibiotics can suppress cultures, and some relevant pathogens were not tested. Thus admission-only simultaneous testing does not guarantee accurate assessment of infection status or severity irrespective of symptom timing, making this option incorrect.

174.Option: The 2011–2012 cluster of genotype 1.1 isolates represents a new, locally evolved variant of S. Typhi that emerged independently in the modern era due to selective pressure from widespread antibiotic use
Evidence: The twenty isolates from 2011–2012 showed an interesting cluster with a median SNP distance of 8 SNPs which is unlikely to have evolved among acute cases, where the mutation rate is calculated to be < 1 SNP/genome/year, in one year.
A potential explanation to account for this cluster is that it was an outbreak caused by bacteria shed from a long-term carrier.
Amongst the 151 non-H58 isolates, few antimicrobial resistance mechanisms were detected.

The observation that the 1980s and 2010s collections of S. Typhi isolates do not cluster independently within our commonly found genotypes (2, 3.5 and 1.2.1) suggests that many of the sporadic cases detected in the 2010s share common ancestry with S. Typhi cases from the 1980s. These may, in turn, be linked to shedding of S. Typhi from chronic typhoid carriers.
174.Rationale: The passage explicitly states the 2011–2012 cluster is unlikely to have arisen by rapid local evolution among acute cases given the known mutation rate, and instead proposes a chronic carrier as a plausible source. Additionally, the study found few antimicrobial resistance mechanisms among non-H58 isolates, which argues against widespread antibiotic-driven selection producing a novel resistant local variant. Therefore the option asserting independent emergence driven by antibiotic selection is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

174.Option: Sporadic typhoid cases in Santiago during the 2010s are primarily due to re-emergence of environmental transmission from untreated sewage irrigation, as evidenced by the persistence of ancient S. Typhi genotypes in the modern population
Evidence: In April 1991, the government of Chile abruptly prohibited the practice of irrigating crops with untreated sewage wastewater in Santiago

Following imposition and strict enforcement of the prohibition of use of untreated sewage water for irrigation, the Santiago cholera epidemic quickly came to an end, and the incidence of typhoid dropped precipitously in subsequent years.

The current rare sporadic cases of typhoid in Santiago include illnesses among: travellers who visit countries with high incidence rates of typhoid; immigrants from typhoid-endemic countries; and transmission from Chilean chronic carriers who were originally infected decades earlier (pre-1991) during the era of hyperendemic transmission.

The observation that the 1980s and 2010s collections of S. Typhi isolates do not cluster independently ... suggests that many of the sporadic cases detected in the 2010s share common ancestry with S. Typhi cases from the 1980s. These may, in turn, be linked to shedding of S. Typhi from chronic typhoid carriers.
174.Rationale: Although untreated sewage irrigation was implicated in past amplified transmission, the passage describes a strong policy-driven interruption of that practice in 1991 with subsequent precipitous decline in typhoid incidence. The modern sporadic cases are attributed to travellers, immigrants, and chronic carriers rather than renewed environmental irrigation-based transmission. While ancient genotypes persist, the text links them to chronic human carriers rather than to re-emergent sewage irrigation as the primary driver.

174.Option: Sporadic typhoid cases in Santiago during the 2010s are caused by imported strains from high-endemic regions, as indicated by the presence of H58 genotypes in the modern isolates
Evidence: Although there have been several detected introductions of the globally dominant H58 S. Typhi clade (4.3.1) into Santiago, as occurs with most industrialized countries in North America, Europe, and Australia, this was not a frequent genotype found in the sporadic modern Chilean cases.

The observation that the 1980s and 2010s collections of S. Typhi isolates do not cluster independently within our commonly found genotypes (2, 3.5 and 1.2.1) suggests that many of the sporadic cases detected in the 2010s share common ancestry with S. Typhi cases from the 1980s.
174.Rationale: The passage acknowledges some introductions of H58 but emphasizes that H58 was not frequent among modern Chilean sporadic cases. Most modern isolates appear to share ancestry with local 1980s strains, suggesting local persistence (likely via carriers) rather than primary causation by imported H58 strains. Thus the option claiming imported H58 strains are the main cause is not supported.

174.Option: The 2011–2012 cluster of genotype 1.1 isolates likely resulted from a single environmental exposure event, such as a contaminated food source, given the high genetic similarity among isolates
Evidence: The twenty isolates from 2011–2012 showed an interesting cluster with a median SNP distance of 8 SNPs which is unlikely to have evolved among acute cases, where the mutation rate is calculated to be < 1 SNP/genome/year, in one year.
A potential explanation to account for this cluster is that it was an outbreak caused by bacteria shed from a long-term carrier.

Thus, the closely related nature of these isolates occurring mainly over a single year and during a period when typhoid was no longer hyperendemic in Santiago could be explained by an outbreak instigated by a chronic carrier who was shedding multiple highly related strains.

If this hypothetical carrier was working as a food handler, intermittently contaminating food or water consumed by subjects during 2011–12, this could result in the cluster of closely related isolates we observe.
174.Rationale: While the isolates are highly similar, the authors argue that the pattern is best explained by shedding from a long-term carrier (potentially intermittently contaminating food) rather than a single one-time environmental exposure. The text specifically discusses a chronic carrier producing a heterogeneous but closely related community of bacteria and intermittent contamination by a food handler as a plausible scenario. Therefore the single-exposure explanation is not the inference drawn by the study.

174.Option: Sporadic typhoid cases in Santiago during the 2010s are likely sustained by long-term human carriers who harbor and intermittently shed genetically stable S. Typhi lineages originating from the hyperendemic era
Evidence: The observation that the 1980s and 2010s collections of S. Typhi isolates do not cluster independently within our commonly found genotypes (2, 3.5 and 1.2.1) suggests that many of the sporadic cases detected in the 2010s share common ancestry with S. Typhi cases from the 1980s. These may, in turn, be linked to shedding of S. Typhi from chronic typhoid carriers.

Thus, the closely related nature of these isolates occurring mainly over a single year and during a period when typhoid was no longer hyperendemic in Santiago could be explained by an outbreak instigated by a chronic carrier who was shedding multiple highly related strains.

If this hypothetical carrier was working as a food handler, intermittently contaminating food or water consumed by subjects during 2011–12, this could result in the cluster of closely related isolates we observe.
as chronic carriers will continue to shed sporadically until they are treated or die out of the population [39].
In conclusion, this genomics analysis suggests that asymptomatic typhoid carriers (or other unknown reservoirs) are likely responsible for continuing sporadic typhoid cases in Chile.
174.Rationale: The passage consistently interprets genomic intermingling between 1980s and 2010s isolates, the tightly related 2011–2012 cluster, and the temporal pattern of sporadic cases as evidence that chronic human carriers are a plausible and likely reservoir sustaining sporadic typhoid in Santiago decades after hyperendemic transmission ceased. The authors explicitly conclude asymptomatic carriers are likely responsible and describe mechanisms (gallbladder carriage, intermittent shedding, possible food handler transmission) that support this inference.

175.Option: The dose-dependent and reversible nature of PZQ-induced phenotypes suggests a shared mechanism involving modulation of excitable cell function, likely through disruption of bioaminergic signaling pathways.
Evidence: Exposure of regenerating planarian (D. japonica) trunk fragments to praziquantel (PZQ) yielded two-headed worms (Fig 1A, [17])... This effect was dose-dependent (EC50 38±3.6μM, Fig 1B), with maximal doses being completely penetrant [17].
This effect was dose-dependent (EC50 38±3.6μM, Fig 1B), with maximal doses being completely penetrant [17].
This effect was dose-dependent (EC50 38±3.6μM, Fig 1B), with maximal doses being completely penetrant [17].
This effect was dose-dependent (EC50 38±3.6μM, Fig 1B), with maximal doses being completely penetrant [17].
This effect was dose-dependent (EC50 38±3.6μM, Fig 1B), with maximal doses being completely penetrant [17].
This effect was dose-dependent (EC50 38±3.6μM, Fig 1B), with maximal doses being completely penetrant [17].

Acute incubation of intact worms with PZQ caused worms to adopt a spastic, curled morphology with inhibitory effects on worm motion (Fig 4A). This effect was dose-dependent (Fig 4B)... and reversible following drug removal (Fig 4C).
The bipolarizing action of PZQ was blocked by co-incubation with 5-HT, or the analogue O-methylserotonin (O-MT), but not by co-incubation with other bioaminergic neurotransmitters (Fig 1C).
Therefore, we examined whether PZQ and the ergot alkaloids discovered to miscue polarity, impacted planarian motility.
Drugs that miscue regenerative polarity target the excitable cell niche
The observations that serotonergic activation opposes, while serotonergic inhibition mimics PZQ action, reveal that serotonergic signaling is an important modulator of PZQ efficacy in vivo.
175.Rationale: The passage demonstrates that PZQ effects on regeneration and motility are concentration-dependent (EC50s reported) and reversible on washout, arguing against simple irreversible toxicity and in favor of modulation of physiological processes. Multiple statements link PZQ phenotypes to excitable cell/muscle function and show functional interaction with bioaminergic (serotonergic) signaling (e.g., 5-HT antagonizes PZQ). Together these observations support a shared mechanism whereby PZQ perturbs excitable cell physiology via bioaminergic/Ca2+ signaling pathways, consistent with the immobilization/tegumental-damage → immune clearance model in schistosomes.

175.Option: The concentration-dependent induction of bipolarity indicates that PZQ acts as a direct agonist of serotonergic receptors, leading to aberrant head specification during regeneration.
Evidence: The bipolarizing action of PZQ was blocked by co-incubation with 5-HT, or the analogue O-methylserotonin (O-MT), but not by co-incubation with other bioaminergic neurotransmitters (Fig 1C).
This result suggests serotonergic signals functionally oppose PZQ action,

This broad efficacy of ergot alkaloids as a chemical class permitted structural-activity insight into features associated with specific polarity effects... Therefore, this structural feature of the bipolarizing ergot compounds suggests they work through serotonergic blockade.
we demonstrate the role of serotonergic antagonists as phenocopying PZQ action.
175.Rationale: The passage explicitly shows that exogenous serotonin (5-HT) blocks PZQ-evoked bipolarity and that serotonergic antagonists phenocopy PZQ. Those findings indicate PZQ does not act as a serotonergic agonist; rather, PZQ’s action is functionally opposed by serotonergic activation and resembles serotonergic blockade. Therefore the statement that PZQ is a direct 5-HT agonist is not supported by the data.

175.Option: The similar EC50 values for regenerative polarity and motility defects suggest that PZQ exerts its effects through a non-specific cytotoxic mechanism that damages cellular integrity at high concentrations.
Evidence: This effect was dose-dependent (EC50 38±3.6μM, Fig 1B), with maximal doses being completely penetrant [17].
Dose response relationship for effect of PZQ on planarian mobility. Inset, minimal intensity projections at indicated doses (μM).

Acute incubation of intact worms with PZQ caused worms to adopt a spastic, curled morphology with inhibitory effects on worm motion (Fig 4A). This effect was dose-dependent (Fig 4B), with a concentration-dependence similar to that observed for the (longer term) polarity effect (EC50 = 38±3.6μM for bipolarity in trunk fragments versus EC50 = 23±2.4μM for mobility in intact worms, Figs 1B and 4B) and reversible following drug removal (Fig 4C).

This broad efficacy of ergot alkaloids as a chemical class permitted structural-activity insight into features associated with specific polarity effects: for example, all ergots that caused bipolarity were either alkylated on the indole nitrogen or halogenated at the adjacent 2-position (Fig 3A).
we demonstrate the role of serotonergic antagonists as phenocopying PZQ action.
175.Rationale: Although EC50s for different phenotypes are similar, the passage emphasizes reversibility after washout and specific structure-activity relationships (ergot modifications) as well as pharmacologic antagonism by 5-HT. These features argue against a non-specific cytotoxic mechanism (which would be less likely reversible and less likely to show specific antagonism/structure-activity patterns) and support a targeted effect on excitable cell/bioaminergic pathways instead.

175.Option: The concentration-dependent induction of both polarity and motility defects implies that PZQ acts as a broad-spectrum neuromodulator that disrupts multiple neurotransmitter systems simultaneously.
Evidence: PZQ action in planarians has previously been shown to depend on Cav1A and tyrosine hydroxylase (TH) functionality (red, [18,20]). Activity of this pathway is opposed by Cav1B and tryptophan hydroxylase functionality (blue, [18,20]).
The observations that serotonergic activation opposes, while serotonergic inhibition mimics PZQ action, reveal that serotonergic signaling is an important modulator of PZQ efficacy in vivo.
we have previously suggested that PZQ engages dopaminergic pathways to subvert regeneration
Dopamine and serotonin regulate cAMP turnover with opposing effects on flatworm musculature [34–36].
175.Rationale: The data point to involvement of specific bioaminergic pathways (dopaminergic and serotonergic) and voltage-gated Ca2+ channel subtypes rather than indiscriminate disruption of many neurotransmitter systems. The authors describe a model of opposing, specific pathways (Cav1A/TH versus Cav1B/TPH) and functional antagonism between dopamine and serotonin. Thus, labeling PZQ as a broad-spectrum disruptor of multiple neurotransmitters is not supported by the presented evidence.

175.Option: The dose-response relationship supports the hypothesis that PZQ induces bipolarity by directly activating Wnt signaling pathways, which are known to regulate body axis formation in planarians.
Evidence: Dopamine and serotonin also are known regulators of Wnt signaling. D2Rs selectively associate with both β-catenin (to inhibit Wnt signals [40]) and Cav channels (to regulate their expression [41]).
5-HT is a well-established wounding signal [42], long range messenger involved in regenerative proliferation [43,44] and a reciprocally permissive cue for Wnt signaling [45,46].
we propose these pathways differentially regulate second messenger (cAMP) action, muscle function and—in planarians—‘head’ versus ‘tail’ specification.

PZQ action in planarians has previously been shown to depend on Cav1A and tyrosine hydroxylase (TH) functionality (red, [18,20]). Activity of this pathway is opposed by Cav1B and tryptophan hydroxylase functionality (blue, [18,20]).
175.Rationale: While Wnt signaling is central to axis specification and can be modulated by bioaminergic signals, the passage provides mechanistic evidence linking PZQ to modulation of excitable cell physiology and bioaminergic/Ca2+ pathways rather than direct activation of Wnt components. The authors frame Wnt modulation as downstream or permissive to bioaminergic/Ca2+ changes, not as the direct target of PZQ. Therefore the statement that PZQ directly activates Wnt signaling is not supported by the data.

176.Option: Use of a decision tree model that incorporates both clinical symptoms and laboratory parameters to improve diagnostic accuracy
Evidence: we show here that a decision tree approach can be useful to develop an intuitive diagnostic algorithm, using clinical and haematological parameters, that is able to distinguish dengue from non-dengue disease in the first 72 hours of illness.

The decision algorithm for the diagnosis of dengue within the first three days of illness made use of a combination of platelet count, total white cell count, body temperature, absolute lymphocyte and neutrophil counts, in sequential order (Figure 1A).
176.Rationale: Although incorporating clinical symptoms and laboratory parameters into a decision tree improves predictive or diagnostic accuracy (as documented in the passage), that feature alone does not most directly support causal inference. Causal inference requires temporality, objective measurement prior to outcome, control of confounding, and appropriate study design elements (e.g., prospective measurement and validation). The quoted text supports that the model uses clinical and lab data for prediction, but does not establish that this feature by itself provides the temporal or design safeguards needed for valid causal interpretation.

176.Option: Application of k-fold cross-validation with k=10 to assess model performance and reduce overfitting in the decision tree
Evidence: The calculated algorithms were validated using the k-fold cross-validation approach. This is considered to be a powerful methodology to overcome data over-fitting [15].
The fold value was set to k = 10.
176.Rationale: K-fold cross-validation (k=10) is correctly used in the study to assess model performance and reduce overfitting, as the passage states. While important for model robustness and internal validity of predictive performance, cross-validation does not by itself establish causal inference about early clinical predictors (it assesses predictive generalizability rather than temporality, confounding control, or causal structure). Therefore it is not the study design feature that most directly supports causal inference.

176.Option: Inclusion of both dengue and non-dengue cases from multiple geographic sites to ensure representation of diverse clinical presentations
Evidence: We constructed a decision tree for dengue diagnosis with 1,200 patients with acute febrile illness. Of these, 1,012 were recruited from the EDEN study and 188 from Vietnam.

Since the course of disease in all but three of the Singaporean adult cases were consistent with DF instead of DHF, we have included 188 DHF/DSS paediatric and adult cases from Vietnam in order to ensure that the diagnostic algorithm developed here is robust across a spectrum of dengue presentations.
176.Rationale: Including cases from multiple sites and both dengue and non-dengue presentations enhances representativeness and external validity (generalizability) and helps the algorithm perform across diverse clinical presentations. However, while valuable, this feature is not the most direct support for causal inference about early predictors of severity—causal inference more directly depends on temporality (predictors measured before outcome), objective measurement, and control of confounding rather than site diversity alone.

176.Option: Use of temporally prior, objective biomarkers collected within 72 hours of illness onset in a multi-site cohort with validation across subgroups
Evidence: Adult patients (age >18 years) presenting at selected primary care polyclinics within 72 hours of onset of acute febrile illness and without rhinitis or clinically obvious alternative diagnoses for fever were eligible for study inclusion.
A. Decision algorithm for predicting dengue diagnosis calculated on 1200 patients with data obtained in the first 72 hours of illness.

The best performing decision algorithm made used of platelet count, the crossover value (Ct) of the real-time RT-PCR for dengue viral RNA (a marker for viremia levels) and the presence of anti-dengue IgG antibodies, as the first, second and third splitting parameters, respectively (Figure 3). All 3 parameters were obtained from the first visit.
The use of such a prognostic algorithm could prove useful in segregating patients according to likely clinical outcomes to guide clinical management and follow-up visits.
176.Rationale: This option contains the key design elements that most directly support causal inference: (1) temporally prior measurement — objective biomarkers were collected within 72 hours (i.e., before the clinical outcomes unfold), establishing temporal precedence of predictors; (2) objective, measurable biomarkers (platelet count, Ct value, anti-dengue IgG) reduce measurement bias; (3) a multi-site cohort and validation across subgroups (described elsewhere in the passage) increase robustness and reduce the chance that associations are site-specific artifacts. Together these features strengthen the plausibility that identified early predictors are antecedent risk factors for severe dengue rather than consequences or artifacts, and thus most directly support causal inference when using a recursive partitioning algorithm.

176.Option: Development of a decision tree using data from hospitalized patients only to ensure accurate outcome ascertainment
Evidence: The use of data obtained from the 89 hospitalised cases alone resulted in a very similar decision algorithm, although the AUCs were substantially lower than the above analysis due largely to the smaller dataset.

Since the course of disease in all but three of the Singaporean adult cases were consistent with DF instead of DHF, we have included 188 DHF/DSS paediatric and adult cases from Vietnam in order to ensure that the diagnostic algorithm developed here is robust across a spectrum of dengue presentations.
176.Rationale: Using hospitalized patients only can improve outcome ascertainment, but it introduces selection bias (spectrum bias) because hospitalized cases are a selected subset with different severity and healthcare-seeking patterns; the passage notes that analysis limited to hospitalized cases reduced AUC and that the authors explicitly added Vietnam cases to capture a broader spectrum. Thus hospital-only sampling does not most directly support valid causal inference about early predictors in the broader population and may bias estimates of associations between early predictors and severe outcomes.

177.Option: Use mosquito surveillance as an adjunct to human testing to detect current infection and validate findings from human-based surveillance
Evidence: By contrast, mosquito surveillance gives a snapshot indication of current infection, and could serve as a useful adjunct to human surveillance methods[27, 47].

There were also examples where human testing did not detect significant transmission but xenomonitoring did. For example, the study by Ramaiah et al. reported a mosquito infection rate of 4.7% of mosquitoes when a community survey performed concurrently found no evidence of human infection on ICT testing.
Xenomonitoring surveys also appear to have utility in identifying hotspots, as in the case of Rao et al. (2018) who detected a MIR of 5.2% in an area which had recently passed TAS[38].
177.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that mosquito surveillance provides a current snapshot of infection and can act as a useful adjunct to human surveillance, and gives examples where xenomonitoring detected transmission that human testing missed. Using mosquito surveillance alongside human testing therefore directly reduces the risk of missing residual hotspots due to measurement limitations of human tests.

177.Option: Replace antigen-based testing with antibody-based testing in all surveillance activities to increase sensitivity and detect historical infections
Evidence: The studies included in this review indicate that there may be benefit in moving from the conventional rapid antigen tests to antibody tests as they increase the proportion of positive results and, hence, the likelihood that residual hotspots will be detected.

However, antibody tests are a measure of the host response to infection which can persist for some time after all antigenic material from the original infection has been eliminated. This means that antibody tests are associated with an increased false-positive rate and the detection of more historical cases, meaning there would be financial and logistical implications to switching to widespread antibody testing[13].
Antibody tests could be added to TAS without significant changes to study design[13].
177.Rationale: Although antibody tests have higher yield and may increase detection of residual hotspots, the passage warns they detect historical infections and have an increased false-positive rate and logistic/cost implications. The recommendation is cautious (adding antibody testing or targeted use), not wholesale replacement in all surveillance. Replacing antigen tests entirely would risk misclassifying historical infections as current transmission and impose costs, so it is not the best single strategy to reduce missed hotspots due to measurement error.

177.Option: Conduct repeated Transmission Assessment Surveys (TAS) at shorter intervals to improve detection of transmission hotspots despite low sensitivity
Evidence: It is acknowledged that the TAS methodology is resource-intensive and may also lack sensitivity in low-prevalence settings[5, 7].

TAS is an important programmatic tool to guide decisions on when to stop MDA but several studies report that it lacks sensitivity when used in low prevalence settings, such as a post-validation context[13, 17, 23, 38], and may not accurately describe the spatial distribution of LF at community-level[14].
177.Rationale: The passage describes TAS as resource-intensive and of limited sensitivity in low-prevalence/post-validation contexts. Repeating a method that intrinsically lacks sensitivity and is resource-heavy is unlikely to effectively reduce the risk of missing hotspots and could be inefficient. The review instead discusses alternative or adjunctive methods (e.g., antibody testing, xenomonitoring) rather than more frequent TAS.

177.Option: Use a single diagnostic test with the highest reported sensitivity across all studies to standardize surveillance and minimize measurement error
Evidence: A significant degree of heterogeneity was identified in the included studies. This included variation in study design, baseline endemicity, population sampled, use of diagnostic tests and reporting metrics.

All methods of human surveillance are affected by the persistence of the marker (antibody or antigen) in circulation. This is of variable duration for different test types, meaning that their results are not directly comparable.

The studies included in this review indicate that there may be benefit in moving from the conventional rapid antigen tests to antibody tests as they increase the proportion of positive results and, hence, the likelihood that residual hotspots will be detected.
177.Rationale: The passage emphasizes heterogeneity among tests and that different markers persist for different durations, making results not directly comparable. While some tests (e.g., antibody tests) have higher positivity, they also detect historical infection and have trade-offs (false positives, cost). Thus adopting a single most-sensitive test across all settings would not necessarily minimize measurement error or be appropriate everywhere; the review calls for more systematic evaluation and context-specific choices rather than a one-size-fits-all single test.

177.Option: Target surveillance exclusively to adult males, as they are more likely to harbor infection and show higher prevalence rates
Evidence: Studies reported that LF tests typically report higher prevalence of infection in adults than in children[14, 31] and it is thought that adults (particularly adult men) may represent the majority of the reservoir of infection for LF[37].

As prevalence reduces it may therefore also be appropriate to target surveillance to focus on these high-risk populations. Methods that have been suggested include adopting a ‘test and treat’ approach for adult males, which could focus on settings in which they may be more likely to congregate, such as marketplaces[37].
Post-validation surveillance in Togo found positive cases in low-risk areas, highlighting the importance of developing surveillance systems with nationwide coverage[40, 42].
177.Rationale: While adults—especially men—may disproportionately contribute to the reservoir and targeted surveillance of high-risk groups can improve sensitivity, the passage cautions about the need for nationwide coverage and notes positive cases in low-risk areas. Exclusively focusing on adult males would risk missing hotspots in other groups or locations. The recommended approach is targeted additional surveillance of high-risk groups alongside broader coverage, not exclusive targeting.

178.Option: The inclusion of baseline microfilaria prevalence data in the model calibration process
Evidence: The four data streams considered for investigating the value of information gained from each survey were respectively: scenario 1—baseline mf prevalence data only, scenario 2—baseline and post-MDA 3 mf prevalence data, scenario 3—baseline, post-MDA 3, and post-MDA 5 mf prevalence data, and scenario 4—baseline and post-MDA 5 mf prevalence data.

the best constraining data stream for each combination of site and model also produced as expected the lowest range in predictions of the numbers of years of annual MDA required to achieve the 1% mf prevalence in each site, with the widest ranges estimated for model-only predictions (scenario 0) and the shorter data streams (scenario 1).

data streams including post-MDA 5 mf survey data (scenarios 3 and 4) reduced model uncertainty (based on both the variance and entropy measures) most compared to data streams containing only baseline and/or post-MDA 3 mf survey data (scenarios 1 and 2) (Table 2).
178.Rationale: Although baseline mf prevalence data are part of the calibration scenarios, the passage shows that baseline-only (scenario 1) is a shorter/less informative stream and produces wider prediction ranges. The most informative scenarios included later post-MDA data (post-MDA 5). Therefore inclusion of baseline data alone is not the most critical factor for reliable forecasts.

178.Option: The use of a sequential data assimilation method rather than a batch estimation approach in model calibration
Evidence: EPIFIL used a sequential model updating procedure to iteratively modify the parameters with the introduction of each subsequent follow up data point through time [6].

LYMFASIM and TRANSFIL, by contrast, included all the data in each investigated stream together for selecting the best-fitting models for each time series–i.e. model selection for each data series was based on using all relevant observations simultaneously in the fitting process [30, 53, 54].
Note that this was also irrespective of the two types of longitudinal data assimilation procedures (sequential vs. simultaneous) used by the different models in this study.
178.Rationale: The study compared sequential (EPIFIL) and batch/simultaneous (LYMFASIM, TRANSFIL) approaches but explicitly states that the improvement in predictive precision was observed irrespective of the assimilation method used. Thus choice of sequential vs batch is not the primary critical factor determining forecast reliability.

178.Option: The use of a deterministic model structure over a stochastic one in simulating intervention outcomes
Evidence: The three existing LF models employed for this study included EPIFIL, a deterministic Monte Carlo population-based model, and LYMFASIM and TRANSFIL, which are both stochastic, individual-based models.
This gain in precision, and thus the information gained using these data streams, is, as expected, greater for the stochastic LYMFASIM and TRANSFIL models compared to the deterministic EPIFIL model.
for all three sites and models, calibration of each model by data greatly reduces the uncertainty in predictions of the years of annual MDA required to eliminate LF compared to model-only predictions
