202.Option: The use of a single time point post-infection (day 4) ensures that all biological responses are captured at a consistent stage of larval migration, minimizing variability
Evidence: We specifically used label-free quantitative (LFQ) mass spectrometry [38] on total protein extracts from the left liver lobes of C57BL/6J and CBA/Ca mice with and without Ascaris infection sampled on day 4 post-infection (p.i.). The hepatic lobe and time-point choice were based on the study of Dold et al. [32], who demonstrated relatively high and equivalent larval numbers in C57BL/6J and CBA/Ca left lobes at day 4 p.i., a time point at which they also observed the onset of a differential inflammatory response to infection between the two mouse strains.

The experimental time point examined within the present study may be too early in the infection to observe an adaptive immune response, as the adaptive immune response takes 4–7 days to engage fully [85].
202.Rationale: Although the study deliberately sampled at day 4 p.i. to capture a consistent stage of larval migration (passage supports that), the option overstates that a single time point 'ensures that all biological responses are captured.' The passage itself notes that day 4 may be too early to capture the full adaptive immune response (adaptive response takes 4–7 days). Moreover, the question asks for a strain feature that allows identification of intrinsic differences; choosing a sampling time point is an experimental choice, not an intrinsic strain feature. Therefore this option is incorrect.

202.Option: The availability of multiple inbred mouse strains with varying degrees of resistance allows for a dose-response analysis of susceptibility across a spectrum of phenotypes
Evidence: The aims of the present study were to investigate the differences in the liver proteomes between the two mouse strains, one deemed to be susceptible, the other resistant, with both control and infected groups within each strain.

C57BL/6J are uniquely susceptible to infection with the porcine ascarid, A. suum, and larval burdens recovered from the lungs of this strain, markedly exceed those recovered from similarly infected strains of which the most resistant is the CBA/Ca mouse [30]. These two strains therefore represent opposite response phenotypes [5], mimicking the extremes of predisposition detected in humans and pigs.

A clear difference in the proteomes of both strains was observed (Fig 1A) and intrinsic differences identified between the inbred strains, unrelated to Ascaris infection, which may underpin susceptibility and resistance...
202.Rationale: The passage describes the use of two inbred strains that represent opposite phenotypes (susceptible vs. resistant). It does not describe the use of multiple strains spanning a range of resistance levels, nor does it perform a dose-response style analysis across a spectrum of phenotypes. Because the option claims availability of multiple strains and a spectrum analysis, which is not supported by the text (only two strains were used), this option is incorrect.

203.Option: The presence of early thrombin generation with both venoms suggests that thrombosis is inevitable in all Bothrops envenomations, regardless of species-specific differences.
Evidence: Lag time significantly decreased with both venoms at H3 (p < 0.001 for B. atrox and p = 0.01 for B. lanceolatus) and only for B. atrox at H6 (p = 0.019) (Fig 3A). Similarly, the time to peak significantly decreased with both venoms at H3 (p = 0.001 for B. atrox and p = 0.006 for B. lanceolatus) (Fig 3B). In parallel, the peak height significantly increased with the two venoms at H3 (p = 0.04) (Fig 3C).
However, thrombosis is not specific of B. lanceolatus envenomation but may rarely occur with B. atrox bite [79,80].
203.Rationale: While the passage documents early thrombin generation with both venoms (TGA changes at H3), it explicitly states that thrombosis is not universal across species and may only rarely occur with B. atrox. The authors describe species-specific divergent clinical patterns (bleeding versus thrombotic complications) and discuss additional mechanisms (consumption, recovery, inflammation) that determine outcomes. Thus early thrombin generation alone does not make thrombosis inevitable in all Bothrops envenomations.

203.Option: The divergent clinical outcomes in Bothrops envenomation may stem from early coagulopathy followed by either persistent consumption (leading to bleeding) or recovery with inflammation-driven hypercoagulability (leading to thrombosis)
Evidence: An initial step of venom-induced hypercoagulability with increased thrombin generation could be considered followed by a secondary step of hypocoagulability with B. atrox venom (as shown at H24 with ROTEM data) or persistent hypercoagulability with B. lanceolatus venom (as shown with fibrinography data), while these two venoms both have a similar proteomic composition with a predominance of SVMPs and procoagulant activity in vitro.
An early decrease in fibrinogen plasma level was observed with the two venoms at H3 and H6, whereas it recovered at H24 with B. atrox venom.
Significant thrombocytopenia was observed with B. atrox venom at H3 (p = 0.04), H6 (p = 0.02), and H24 (p < 0.001) while B. lanceolatus venom had no effect on platelet count (Fig 2C).

Thrombosis may be due to coagulation-induced inflammatory activity of the venom. Here, we observed a systemic inflammatory response involving IL-6, TNF-α, and MCP-1 after B. lanceolatus venom injection.
203.Rationale: The passage explicitly frames a two-step model: an early procoagulant/thrombin-generating phase common to both venoms, followed by divergent trajectories—B. atrox leading to consumption and hypocoagulability (bleeding), and B. lanceolatus showing recovery of fibrinogen and inflammatory changes that favor persistent hypercoagulability and thrombosis. The cited TGA, ROTEM, platelet and fibrinogen time-course data and measured inflammatory mediators directly support this interpretation.

203.Option: The lack of change in platelet count with B. lanceolatus venom indicates that thrombocytopenia is not a factor in the pathogenesis of thrombosis in this envenomation.
Evidence: Significant thrombocytopenia was observed with B. atrox venom at H3 (p = 0.04), H6 (p = 0.02), and H24 (p < 0.001) while B. lanceolatus venom had no effect on platelet count (Fig 2C).
Despite venom-induced thrombocytopenia in mice, B. lanceolatus venom induced no direct effects on platelet aggregation in human platelet-rich plasma [41,42].

Thrombosis may be due to coagulation-induced inflammatory activity of the venom. Here, we observed a systemic inflammatory response involving IL-6, TNF-α, and MCP-1 after B. lanceolatus venom injection.
203.Rationale: In this experimental model B. lanceolatus produced no measurable thrombocytopenia while still being associated with inflammatory markers and later hypercoagulable features (fibrinogen increase, fibrinography changes). The data therefore indicate that thrombocytopenia is not a primary driver of the thrombotic phenotype for B. lanceolatus in this model. (That said, absence of thrombocytopenia does not exclude other platelet-mediated mechanisms not captured by count alone, but the experimental evidence supports the option as stated.)

203.Option: The recovery of fibrinogen at 24 hours with B. lanceolatus venom is sufficient to explain the onset of thrombosis, regardless of inflammatory or coagulation factors.
Evidence: An increase in plasma fibrinogen at H24 was remarkable in our B. lanceolatus envenomation model.

In our model, fibrinography showed an increased fibrin generation at H24 which mechanism remains unexplained, but does not seem to be linked to a delayed thrombin generation, whose level was comparable to the control at H24.
The increase in PAI-1 at H6 and H24 suggests an inhibition of the fibrinolytic pathway but ROTEM analysis may not show fibrinolysis shutdown.

Thrombosis may be due to coagulation-induced inflammatory activity of the venom. Here, we observed a systemic inflammatory response involving IL-6, TNF-α, and MCP-1 after B. lanceolatus venom injection.
203.Rationale: Although fibrinogen increase at 24 h is highlighted, the authors explicitly state the mechanism is unresolved and point to additional contributors (PAI-1–mediated fibrinolysis inhibition, inflammatory cytokines, potential factor XIII activation, and other coagulation/inflammatory interactions). Therefore recovery/increase of fibrinogen alone is not presented as a sufficient explanation 'regardless' of other factors.

203.Option: The similar proteomic composition of B. atrox and B. lanceolatus venoms explains why both species cause early coagulopathy, but differences in venom dose account for the divergent clinical outcomes.
Evidence: Surprisingly, venom composition of these two Bothrops species are relatively similar with comparable activities on coagulation in vitro in the whole blood and plasma [48].

Since preliminary in vitro experiments conducted in our laboratory using ROTEM in the rat whole blood showed that the procoagulant effect of B. atrox venom was 2-fold that of B. lanceolatus venom, we used 220% of the estimated intraperitoneal LD50 in mice for B. lanceolatus venom (i.e., 4 mg per rat), and half of this value for B. atrox venom (i.e., 2 mg per rat) to target equipotent doses.

By analogy to the Greek myth of Charybdis and Scylla, Bothrops envenomation may thus follow two opposite clinical expressions: in the event of major platelet and fibrinogen consumption promoting bleeding, hemorrhage will occur early, whereas in case of minor consumption or early restoration of platelet and fibrinogen limiting bleeding, venom-induced systemic inflammation will expose to thrombotic complications of delayed onset.
203.Rationale: While the similar proteomic composition helps explain comparable early in vitro procoagulant effects, the authors do not attribute divergent clinical outcomes primarily to differences in venom dose. In fact, the experimental design used adjusted (equipotent) doses to compare effects, and the discussion emphasizes dynamic time-course processes (consumption vs. recovery, inflammation, fibrinolysis inhibition) rather than a simple dose explanation. Thus the claim that dose differences account for divergent outcomes is not supported by the text.

204.Option: The number of microfilariae present in the specific blood spot sample used for DNA extraction
Evidence: Three false negative results were obtained with blood spot samples where mf burden was very low (20–200 mf/mL, or up to 4 organisms in a 20 µL blood spot).
These false negatives were most likely due either to sampling error (no organisms present in the processed blood spot) or to issues with the DNA extraction process.
Using DNA extracted from clinical samples as template, the LLMF72 qPCR assay was able to detect a single mf in a 20 µL dried blood spot (equivalent to a burden of 50 mf/mL).
It is nonetheless encouraging that boiling blood spots for 10 min enabled detection of as few as 180 mf/mL (7 organisms in a 40 µL blood spot).
204.Rationale: The passage explicitly attributes some false negatives to sampling error (i.e., the processed dried blood spot simply not containing any organisms) and presents detection limits in terms of number of organisms per small blood spot volumes. Therefore the actual number of microfilariae present in the particular dried blood spot used for extraction is a critical determinant of whether the qPCR will accurately estimate microfilaremia.

204.Option: The efficiency and consistency of DNA extraction methods used on dried blood spots
Evidence: These predictions were based on a standard curve derived from spiked whole blood samples, further indicating that extraction from dried blood spots is likely less efficient than extraction from fresh whole blood.

Three false negative results were obtained with blood spot samples where mf burden was very low (20–200 mf/mL, or up to 4 organisms in a 20 µL blood spot). These false negatives were most likely due either to sampling error (no organisms present in the processed blood spot) or to issues with the DNA extraction process.
Microfilaremia estimation was most accurate and most consistent with samples subjected to easyMAG extraction.

A second set of 36 blood spots was subjected to automated DNA extraction by easyMAG, and concentration of mf was again estimated by LLMF72 qPCR. Among this set, there was a strong linear correlation between predicted and observed microfilaremia (figure 3b, r2 = 0.88; P<0.0001).
204.Rationale: The authors note that dried blood spot extraction is less efficient than fresh blood extraction and that performance varied by extraction method, with the automated easyMAG yielding the most accurate and consistent estimates. They also list DNA extraction issues as a likely cause of false negatives, indicating that extraction efficiency and consistency critically influence accuracy from dried spots.

204.Option: The presence of cross-reactive filarial antigens in the blood sample that interfere with PCR amplification
Evidence: Most Loa-infected individuals will exhibit a positive IgG antibody response to crude protein extracts of Brugia malayi, but this assay is cross-reactive among all filarial pathogens as well as some intestinal helminthes.

Newer serologic assays based on the L. loa SXP-1 antigen and others reliably distinguish loiasis from other filarial and helminthic infections but cannot discern between active and prior infection or quantify microfilaremia.

Neither qPCR assay detected genomic DNA from B. malayi, O. volvulus, W. bancrofti, or M. perstans (10-fold dilutions from 10 ng to 0.1 pg), although all samples were detected in a linear fashion using the 18S rRNA primer/probe set (data not shown).
204.Rationale: Cross-reactivity is described as a limitation of serologic assays, not of the qPCR. The passage explicitly states the qPCR assays were negative with DNA from other filarial species, indicating species specificity and no evidence that cross-reactive filarial antigens interfere with PCR amplification. Thus this factor is not a critical influence on qPCR accuracy.

204.Option: The timing of blood collection relative to the peak microfilaremia period in the daily cycle
Evidence: Similar to microscopy, optimum PCR performance requires proper timing of blood collection at mid-day when microfilaremia is greatest; however, a microfilaremia kinetics study by Kamgno et al. suggests that smaller numbers of organisms may be detectable outside the window of peak microfilaremia [23], and a nomogram may be used to extrapolate qPCR results to predict true parasite burden.
204.Rationale: The authors note that, like microscopy, qPCR detection is optimal when blood is collected at mid-day, the time of peak microfilaremia, so timing of collection affects the number of organisms present in the sample and therefore accuracy of microfilaremia estimation from dried blood spots.

204.Option: The volume of blood processed during DNA extraction from dried blood spots
Evidence: For each qPCR assay of a clinical sample, the number of mf present in the template was extrapolated from a standard curve derived from blood samples spiked with limiting dilutions of mf, using SDS 2.2.2 software (Applied Biosytems, Carlsbad, CA, USA). These extrapolated values were adjusted by the ratio of the percentage of blood spot material used as template (1–10%) compared to the percentage of spiked blood sample used as template (1%) to estimate the number of mf present in each blood spot. The corrected estimates were then divided by the volume of blood processed for DNA extraction (50 µL for boiled samples vs. 20 µL for all other samples) to determine the final estimates of mf concentration for each blood spot sample.
Using DNA extracted from clinical samples as template, the LLMF72 qPCR assay was able to detect a single mf in a 20 µL dried blood spot (equivalent to a burden of 50 mf/mL).
Consequently, the qPCR assay will likely be even more sensitive, consistent, and accurate with larger volume (100 µL and above) fresh blood samples.
204.Rationale: The authors explicitly adjust estimates based on the fraction of the blood spot used and the volume of blood processed, report detection limits in terms of organisms per specific spot volumes, and state that larger sample volumes would improve sensitivity and accuracy. Thus the volume of blood processed from dried spots directly affects microfilaremia estimation accuracy.

205.Option: The fact that fructose was detected in 31.6% of male and 30.5% of female Ae. albopictus indicates that general sugar availability in the environment is the dominant factor shaping vector distribution
Evidence: Fructose positive male Ae. albopictus were more prevalent overall than female fructose positives (31.6% ± 1.4 in males and 30.5% ± 1.2 in females; GLMM, P = 0.002, Figs 1(II) and 2(II)).

The positive correlation between fructose prevalence and PC2, as opposed to the overall presence of flowers and/or fruit in the environment indicates that the composition of the vegetation structure does have importance.
This suggests that the overall measure of flower and/or fruit presence is not nuanced enough to represent the cues that to which the insect population likely responds.
205.Rationale: While the passage reports overall fructose prevalences (~31% in males, ~30% in females), the authors explicitly state that composition (PC2 and specific plant families) matters more than a crude measure of general floral presence. Thus the mere detection frequency does not imply that general sugar availability is the dominant driver; instead the text highlights that vegetation composition (not overall prevalence) better explains sugar-feeding patterns.

205.Option: The higher prevalence of flowers and/or fruit on Yorke Island compared to Hammond Island suggests that general floral abundance is the primary driver of Aedes albopictus distribution across the Torres Strait Islands
Evidence: During the vegetation survey, the prevalence of either flowers and/or fruits (χ2 = 46.11, df = 1, p < 0.001) was associated with island, with both being more prevalent on Yorke Island (S3 Fig).
Plant communities that were dominated by species from the Phyllanthaceae family were positively correlated with higher abundance and fructose prevalence of Aedes albopictus.
This plant family was only present on Hammond Island, mostly in bushland areas and comprised three species: Cleistanthus sp. (most common), Glochidion sp., and Bridelia sp.
205.Rationale: Although Yorke had higher overall flower/fruit prevalence, the passage indicates that plants in the Phyllanthaceae family (present only on Hammond) were strongly correlated with higher abundance and fructose prevalence. This contradiction shows that general floral abundance across an island does not necessarily predict mosquito distribution; specific plant community composition better explains the patterns.

205.Option: The significant association between flower and/or fruit presence and Ae. albopictus abundance in village habitats suggests that general floral availability is the main factor influencing vector distribution
Evidence: The results from the GLMM showed that Ae. albopictus abundance was significantly associated by island, sex, collection time, canopy, PC2 (variation in vegetation gradients), habitat and the presence of flowers and/or fruits (GLMM Table 1 and Figs 1(I) and 2(I)).

The positive correlation between fructose prevalence and PC2, as opposed to the overall presence of flowers and/or fruit in the environment indicates that the composition of the vegetation structure does have importance.
This suggests that the overall measure of flower and/or fruit presence is not nuanced enough to represent the cues that to which the insect population likely responds.
205.Rationale: Although flowers/fruit presence was one predictor associated with abundance, the authors emphasize that PC2 (vegetation composition) and specific plant-family associations (e.g., Phyllanthaceae) better explain fructose prevalence and abundance. The passage explicitly cautions that the simple measure of flower/fruit presence lacks nuance, undermining the claim that general floral availability is the main driver.

205.Option: The strong association between Phyllanthaceae and sugar-feeding behavior, revealed through correspondence analysis, indicates that specific plant community composition drives Aedes albopictus distribution more than general floral abundance
Evidence: Plant communities that were dominated by species from the Phyllanthaceae family were positively correlated with higher abundance and fructose prevalence of Aedes albopictus.

Phyllanthaceae was strongly associated with PC2, suggesting plant communities dominated by this family were correlated with an increased prevalence of fructose positive Ae. albopictus (Fig 3, loading PC1: 0.7569, PC2: 0.9312).
The correspondence analysis (ca) highlights meaningful ecological relationships between mosquito sugar-feeding behaviors and local floral resources.

The positive correlation between fructose prevalence and PC2, as opposed to the overall presence of flowers and/or fruit in the environment indicates that the composition of the vegetation structure does have importance.
205.Rationale: The passage directly identifies Phyllanthaceae-dominated plant communities as positively correlated with both Ae. albopictus abundance and fructose prevalence, and notes that correspondence analysis and PC2 capture these specific associations. The authors contrast this compositional signal with the coarser overall flower/fruit presence metric, supporting that specific plant community composition (e.g., Phyllanthaceae) better explains spatial distribution and sugar-feeding behavior than general floral abundance.

205.Option: The significant influence of Principal Component 2 (PC2) on fructose prevalence suggests that general vegetation complexity, rather than specific plant families, is the main determinant of Aedes albopictus sugar-feeding behavior
Evidence: The positive correlation between fructose prevalence and PC2, as opposed to the overall presence of flowers and/or fruit in the environment indicates that the composition of the vegetation structure does have importance.

Phyllanthaceae was strongly associated with PC2, suggesting plant communities dominated by this family were correlated with an increased prevalence of fructose positive Ae. albopictus (Fig 3, loading PC1: 0.7569, PC2: 0.9312).
Plant communities that were dominated by species from the Phyllanthaceae family were positively correlated with higher abundance and fructose prevalence of Aedes albopictus.
205.Rationale: Although PC2 is a multivariate axis, the passage clarifies that PC2 reflects specific vegetation composition and is strongly associated with Phyllanthaceae. Thus PC2's influence points toward particular plant-community composition effects, not merely undifferentiated vegetation complexity. The text explicitly links PC2 to a specific family, contradicting the claim that PC2 implies only general vegetation complexity.

206.Option: Higher frequency of household-level clustering of infections, indicating shared environmental exposure and increased transmission risk
Evidence: HCC is considered a “clustered” disease; some studies have pointed out the importance of the presence of tapeworm carriers in a household triggering HCC in endemic and non-endemic communities[82;83].

HCC is the result of the ingestion of eggs coming from tapeworm carriers that could have acquired the adult tapeworm from pork meat either locally produced or transported from other endemic regions [67], thus, presence of pigs in this case is not as relevant for HCC transmission than the local prevalence of tapeworm carriers.

Twenty three out of 24 studies reporting taeniasis identified at least one tapeworm carrier, however the diversity of methods used to diagnose taeniasis did not allow to make adequate comparisons between studies, even more, parasitological tests are considered having a low sensitivity, suggesting underestimation of the prevalences.
206.Rationale: Household clustering is evidence of shared or increased environmental exposure (eg, a local tapeworm carrier contaminating the household). The question asks which factor suggests a subgroup is at heightened risk for developing active infection despite similar exposure levels. Clustering indicates greater exposure or focal transmission rather than increased susceptibility given equal exposure, so it does not best answer the question.

206.Option: Higher prevalence of viable cysticerci despite similar exposure levels, suggesting reduced immune control or increased host susceptibility
Evidence: The estimated seroprevalence of anti T. solium antibodies in Africa (17.37%), Latin America (13.03%) and Asia (15.68%) was not significantly different, which could be interpreted as a similar exposure to T. solium eggs in these regions, though, within each continent a visible heterogeneity was observed...
The estimated prevalence of T. solium circulating antigens was higher in endemic areas of Africa (7.30%) compared to Latin America and Asia (4.08% and 3.98%, respectively)...

These results suggest a higher occurrence of active infection in African communities, under a similar exposure to the parasite, suggesting that in this continent there are factors enhancing the human infection regardless of the level of exposure.
It is clear that immunity plays a huge role in the host susceptibility/resistance to the parasite but the underlying mechanisms are not well understood.
A high prevalence of active infections could be as well an indicator of weak immune responses due to multiple factors as described in Table 5.
206.Rationale: The passage explicitly documents situations where antibody seroprevalence (a marker of exposure) is similar across populations while prevalence of circulating antigens (marker of viable cysticerci/active infection) is higher in one group. The authors interpret that pattern as evidence of factors such as weaker immune control or increased host susceptibility that permit more exposures to progress to established, viable infections. Thus this option directly captures the phenomenon described and best answers the question.

206.Option: Higher prevalence of taeniasis in the population, indicating increased environmental contamination and greater likelihood of exposure to infectious eggs
Evidence: Measuring the level of adult tapeworm infections in a population can be used as a support for results obtained from immunological tests to measure exposure.

HCC is the result of the ingestion of eggs coming from tapeworm carriers that could have acquired the adult tapeworm from pork meat either locally produced or transported from other endemic regions [67], thus, presence of pigs in this case is not as relevant for HCC transmission than the local prevalence of tapeworm carriers.

Twenty three out of 24 studies reporting taeniasis identified at least one tapeworm carrier, however the diversity of methods used to diagnose taeniasis did not allow to make adequate comparisons between studies...
206.Rationale: A higher taeniasis prevalence indicates more environmental contamination and therefore greater exposure risk. The question specifies 'despite similar exposure levels' — i.e., exposure is comparable across subgroups — so a higher taeniasis prevalence would not explain why one subgroup develops more active infections given similar exposure; rather it would indicate exposure differences. Therefore this option does not answer the question as posed.

206.Option: Higher prevalence of antibodies to T. solium in the absence of circulating antigens, indicating a robust immune response that prevents establishment of viable infection
Evidence: Current immunological tools used in HCC diagnosis can be classified into: 1) Tests detecting antibodies directed against T. solium cysticerci, identifying exposure to the parasite, and 2) Tests detecting circulating antigens produced by living cysticerci, identifying current infection with viable cysticerci [17].
The prevalence of circulating T. solium antigens was lower than the T. solium antibodies seroprevalence in Latin America and Asia but not in Africa.

exposure expressed by antibody seroprevalence could be interpreted as the result of a past infection, current infection or the result of a failed infection, while circulating antigens can only be detected if viable cysticerci are present.
206.Rationale: A pattern of high antibody prevalence without circulating antigens implies exposure or past/failed infections and, if anything, suggests that the population subgroup is mounting immune responses that do not progress to viable infection. The question asks which factor suggests heightened risk for developing active infection despite similar exposure — this pattern indicates the opposite (reduced progression to active infection), so it is not the correct choice.

207.Option: Testing during the first week of symptoms enables reliable differentiation between acute and chronic Q fever due to the presence of both anti-phase I and anti-phase II antibodies
Evidence: Considering that the time required for seroconversion from recent infections is at least seven days, all selected samples from the patients had more than seven days of symptoms.
According to Anderson et al. [29], 90% of patients seroconvert in the third week of illness.

Therefore, acute Q fever immune response to virulent C. burnetii is initially related to the presence of anti-phase II antibodies, while chronic Q fever is linked to the presence of high titers of anti-phase I antibodies.
207.Rationale: The passage states that seroconversion takes at least seven days and that most patients (90%) seroconvert in the third week. Anti-phase II (acute) appears initially, while anti-phase I (chronic) is linked to later/chronic disease. Therefore testing during the first week is unlikely to detect the antibody patterns needed to reliably distinguish acute versus chronic Q fever; early testing risks false negatives for serology.

207.Option: Testing within the first week of symptoms allows for the earliest detection of anti-phase I antibodies, which are indicative of chronic infection
Evidence: Therefore, acute Q fever immune response to virulent C. burnetii is initially related to the presence of anti-phase II antibodies, while chronic Q fever is linked to the presence of high titers of anti-phase I antibodies.
Considering that the time required for seroconversion from recent infections is at least seven days, all selected samples from the patients had more than seven days of symptoms.
According to Anderson et al. [29], 90% of patients seroconvert in the third week of illness.
207.Rationale: Anti-phase I antibodies are markers of chronic infection and are not the earliest antibodies detected. The passage indicates anti-phase II antibodies appear initially in acute infection and that seroconversion usually occurs after the first week (commonly by the third week). Thus detection of anti-phase I in the first week is unlikely.

207.Option: Testing at any time after symptom onset is equally effective for detecting acute Q fever, as anti-phase II antibody titers remain elevated throughout the illness
Evidence: Considering that the time required for seroconversion from recent infections is at least seven days, all selected samples from the patients had more than seven days of symptoms.
According to Anderson et al. [29], 90% of patients seroconvert in the third week of illness.
To this end, two serum samples collected at 7–21 day or 7–28 day intervals in order to observe the increase in antibody titer could ensure a more accurate diagnosis of Q fever.
207.Rationale: The passage emphasizes that timing matters: seroconversion often occurs in the second–third week and paired sera (7–21 or 7–28 day intervals) improve diagnosis. It does not claim anti-phase II titers are equally detectable at any time; rather, detection of acute infection depends on timing of seroconversion, so testing at arbitrary times is not equally effective.

207.Option: Testing after 42 days of symptoms is optimal for identifying acute Q fever because anti-phase II titers peak at this time
Evidence: According to Anderson et al. [29], 90% of patients seroconvert in the third week of illness.

A cut-off of 64, 128, and 1024 was set for past exposure (seropositivity), acute disease, and chronic disease, respectively, together with the presence of anti-phase II and anti-phase I antibodies in the sera.
Of the 46 seropositive patients by commercial immunofluorescence test, 29 (63%) had phase II antibody titers higher than 128 and were therefore defined as having acute Q fever.
207.Rationale: The passage indicates that seroconversion and detection of acute-phase (anti-phase II) antibodies typically occur by the second–third week, not specifically at >42 days. Waiting beyond 42 days risks measuring past or chronic responses rather than peak acute-phase serology; the text does not support the claim that 42 days is the optimal time for detecting acute Q fever.

207.Option: Testing during the second and third weeks of symptoms maximizes detection of acute Q fever due to peak seroconversion and anti-phase II antibody titers
Evidence: Considering that the time required for seroconversion from recent infections is at least seven days, all selected samples from the patients had more than seven days of symptoms.
According to Anderson et al. [29], 90% of patients seroconvert in the third week of illness.

Therefore, acute Q fever immune response to virulent C. burnetii is initially related to the presence of anti-phase II antibodies, while chronic Q fever is linked to the presence of high titers of anti-phase I antibodies.
To this end, two serum samples collected at 7–21 day or 7–28 day intervals in order to observe the increase in antibody titer could ensure a more accurate diagnosis of Q fever.

We noted that patients with 21–27 days of symptoms were 2.62 times more positive than patients with 7–13 days, and a similar result occurred in patients with 14–20 days, who were shown to be 2.12 times more positive than patients with 7–13 days of symptoms.
207.Rationale: The passage directly supports that seroconversion commonly occurs in the second–third week, that anti-phase II antibodies indicate acute infection, and that observed seropositivity was higher in the 14–27 day windows. Paired sera collected across 7–21 or 7–28 day intervals are recommended to observe rising titers. Thus testing in the second and third weeks maximizes detection of acute Q fever.

208.Option: The development of a mixed Th1/Th2/Th17 immune response in the cecum at 35 d.p.i.
Evidence: In the chronic phase with a high parasitic load, murine trichuriasis involves a mixed Th1/Th2/Th17 immune response; however, in the cecum, only IFN-γ levels are increased, demonstrating the immunomodulatory capacity of the parasite.
Initially, there is a predominance of Th1/Th2 cytokines in the first days of the acute phase, which then transitions to the chronic phase characterized by a mixture of Th1/Th2/Th17 responses.
the damage related to the infection, including bacterial translocation, impacted the mesenteric lymph nodes, starting at 17 d.p.i., and expanded to the spleen and intraperitoneal cells.
208.Rationale: Although the passage states that a mixed Th1/Th2/Th17 response characterizes the chronic phase (35 d.p.i.), this describes a local immune phenotype in the cecum at chronic infection rather than the primary host factor most strongly correlating with progression and systemic activation. The text indicates systemic impacts (mesenteric LN, spleen, intraperitoneal cells) begin earlier with bacterial translocation at ~17 d.p.i., so the mixed local cytokine profile at 35 d.p.i. is a feature of chronicity but is not presented as the strongest correlate of the transition to systemic immune activation.

208.Option: The increase in spleen weight and size observed at 22 d.p.i. and 35 d.p.i.
Evidence: At the end of the acute phase and during the chronic phase, on the 22nd day, we observed a greater spleen weight in the infected group than in the noninfected group. Additionally, at 22 and 35 days after infection, the spleens of infected mice were longer than those of noninfected mice. Morphometric analysis of the white pulp revealed a larger area in infected mice at 17, 22, and 35 days of infection.
208.Rationale: Spleen enlargement is clearly documented and does correlate with systemic immune activation, but it is a downstream organ-level manifestation rather than the proximate host factor driving progression from acute to chronic infection. The passage emphasizes that bacterial translocation and activation of peritoneal macrophages and blood cell changes begin earlier (around 17–22 d.p.i.) and drive systemic responses. Thus spleen enlargement is a marker of systemic activation but not the single strongest correlate identified in the study.

208.Option: A sustained pro-inflammatory and regulatory cytokine profile in peritoneal macrophages coupled with neutrophilia and lymphopenia in peripheral blood
Evidence: In the analysis of cytokines produced by peritoneal macrophages, we observed increases in TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-10 after 22 days (Fig 10).
Additionally, there was an increase in nitric oxide produced by these macrophages on the 35th day of infection when stimulated by LPS, indicating their activation (Fig 11).
At 17 d.p.i., there was an increase in IL-6 and TNF production in peritoneal macrophages, which persisted until 22 d.p.i., along with an increase in IL-10 levels.

By 22 d.p.i., there was a significant decrease in the number of lymphocytes and an increase in the number of basophils and neutrophils in the blood, indicating a response against L4 larvae and secondary infection by invasive bacteria.

The number of intraperitoneal cells, mainly monocytes and neutrophils recruited during infection, was significantly greater in the infected group than in the noninfected group, suggesting peritonitis, as expected and as previously shown in high-dose experimental trichuriasis in the chronic phase.
208.Rationale: The passage links progression from acute to chronic infection with bacterial translocation and documents activation of peritoneal macrophages (increased TNF-α, IL-6, IL-10 and nitric oxide) beginning at 17–22 d.p.i., together with peripheral blood changes (neutrophilia and lymphopenia) by 22 d.p.i. These findings together represent a sustained pro-inflammatory and regulatory innate response plus systemic leukocyte changes that most directly reflect and correlate with the transition to systemic immune activation and disease progression in this model.

208.Option: The presence of eosinophils in the intestinal mucosa and submucosa throughout the acute phase of infection
Evidence: an increase in the number of eosinophils and neutrophils starting at 10 days and persisting throughout the acute phase, up to the 22nd day after infection.

We observed eosinophilia in the mucosa and submucosa of the infected intestine at all time points of infection (10, 17, 22 and 35 d.p.i.), indicating that this infection promotes the recruitment of eosinophils to the parasite niche even at the L2 larval stage.
208.Rationale: Eosinophil recruitment to the mucosa/submucosa is a consistent local hallmark of helminth infection and is present during the acute phase, but the passage treats this as a local tissue response (parasite niche). While eosinophilia is important, the study highlights broader systemic activation (peritoneal macrophage cytokines, blood neutrophilia/lymphopenia, spleen changes) as the stronger correlate of progression to systemic involvement. Thus mucosal eosinophilia alone is not presented as the primary correlate of systemic progression.

208.Option: The increase in goblet cell numbers in the intestinal mucosa beginning at 17 d.p.i.
Evidence: For enterocyte and goblet cell quantification, we observed a significant increase in the number of these cells starting at the 10th and 17th days after infection, respectively, and remaining throughout the acute and chronic phases (Fig 6 and Table 3).
an increase in the number of goblet cells at 17 days, and remaining throughout the acute and chronic phases.
208.Rationale: Goblet cell hyperplasia is a mucosal adaptive response (increased mucin secretion) that contributes to local defense and parasite expulsion. The passage documents this as a local tissue change beginning ~17 d.p.i., but it does not present goblet cell increase as the primary correlate of progression to chronic infection or systemic immune activation. Systemic markers (peritoneal macrophage activation, peripheral blood cell shifts, and spleen changes) are emphasized as the stronger correlates.

209.Option: The bacterium's ability to be genetically modified and produce effector molecules that inhibit Leishmania development
Evidence: Paratransgenesis has been suggested as a feasible strategy for controlling the transmission of pathogens by arthropod vectors. This approach consists of the use of genetically altered symbiotic bacteria that secrete effector molecules that kill the infectious agents.

possesses the main advantages of being non-pathogenic, easy to cultivate and to perform genetic manipulation, its use for paratransgenic control of Leishmania can be challenged by its capacity to establish long-term colonies in the gut of various sand fly species.
209.Rationale: The passage states that paratransgenesis uses genetically altered bacteria that secrete effector molecules, so genetic modifiability and effector expression are necessary attributes. However, the text explicitly highlights that even when a bacterium has advantages like being amenable to genetic manipulation, its use can still be limited by its inability to establish long-term colonies in the sand fly gut. Thus while important, genetic manipulability alone is not described as the most critical single factor for candidate selection.

209.Option: The bacterium's non-pathogenicity and ease of cultivation in laboratory conditions
Evidence: we think that an exhaustive approach aimed at collecting a maximum of data on the microbiota of sand flies will give key information on the most commonly identified bacteria in sand fly species and those that are more specific.
To that end, a strain of the non-pathogenic Bacillus species (Bacillus subtilis), isolated from P. papatasi, is proposed as a possible candidate for paratransgenic approach.

possesses the main advantages of being non-pathogenic, easy to cultivate and to perform genetic manipulation, its use for paratransgenic control of Leishmania can be challenged by its capacity to establish long-term colonies in the gut of various sand fly species.
209.Rationale: The passage lists non-pathogenicity and ease of cultivation as desirable advantages (citing B. subtilis). However, it also emphasizes that these advantages do not override the critical need for the bacterium to establish and persist in the sand fly gut. Therefore non-pathogenicity/ease of culture are important but not the single most critical factor.

209.Option: The ability of the candidate bacterium to establish and persist in the midgut of the target sand fly species over time
Evidence: These bacteria should co-localize with the pathogen and be transmitted vertically to the next generation, they are introduced into vectors to block pathogen transmission.
its use for paratransgenic control of Leishmania can be challenged by its capacity to establish long-term colonies in the gut of various sand fly species.
it will be essential to probe its capacity to efficiently colonize the gut of Lutzomyia species and of other Old World sand fly species in which this bacterium has yet not been found in the gut.

Therefore, even if this bacterium possesses the main advantages of being non-pathogenic, easy to cultivate and to perform genetic manipulation, its use for paratransgenic control of Leishmania can be challenged by its capacity to establish long-term colonies in the gut of various sand fly species.
209.Rationale: The passage repeatedly stresses that candidate bacteria must co-localize with Leishmania and persist in the vector (including vertical transmission and long-term colonization). It explicitly identifies the capacity to establish long-term gut colonies and to colonize other target sand fly species as an essential requirement and a potential limitation, indicating this is the most critical consideration for selecting a paratransgenic candidate in the Tunisian context.

209.Option: The frequency of the candidate bacterium in the midgut of field-collected sand flies compared to laboratory-reared ones
Evidence: Our results suggest that lab-reared P. perniciosus display a lower bacterial richness in their midgut than in field-collected sand flies.
This difference is likely due in part to the type of food diet ingested by larvae and adults during rearing.

Of the six bacterial species identified in laboratory-reared sand flies, three are also found in the midgut of field-collected sand flies (Stenotrophomonas maltophilia, Bacillus sp., Lysinibacillus sp.).
209.Rationale: The passage documents differences between laboratory-reared and field-collected flies and reports frequency/richness differences, which are informative for candidate discovery. However, frequency in samples alone does not guarantee suitability for paratransgenesis; the text emphasizes persistence/colonization and transmission as the critical properties. Thus frequency comparisons are useful but not the single most critical influence on selection.

209.Option: The presence of the candidate bacterium in the midgut of multiple sand fly species across different geographic regions
Evidence: Bacteria belonging to the Bacillus genus seem to display a host-specific distribution, with only B. subtilis being isolated in more than one sand fly species (P. halepensis, P. papatasi, and P. perniciosus).
it will be essential to probe its capacity to efficiently colonize the gut of Lutzomyia species and of other Old World sand fly species in which this bacterium has yet not been found in the gut.
it will be of major epidemiological importance to develop a regional strategy for each endemic area with different bacterial isolates.
209.Rationale: Wider geographic and species distribution is advantageous because it may simplify regional deployment. However, the passage notes many bacteria are host-specific and emphasizes testing a candidate's ability to colonize and persist in the specific target species and area. Therefore presence across species/regions is a favorable trait but not described as the most critical single factor compared with proven establishment and persistence in the target vector.

210.Option: The presence of riparian forests along the Mouhoun River, which serve as refugia for tsetse populations and facilitate local re-colonization
Evidence: These species inhabit the riparian forests that form habitat galleries along the rivers and the flies' relative abundance is determined by forest ecotype and its level of fragmentation and destruction [2], [5].
their linear habitat that allows them to easily disperse between favourable patches, i.e. riverine forests acting as “genetic corridors” [7], [8].
riverine tsetse fly species are mainly confined to the riverbeds of the various river systems which are organised in river basins
210.Rationale: While the passage clearly shows riparian (riverine) forests are important habitat and act as 'genetic corridors' that facilitate local movement and recolonization, the study emphasizes that cross-watershed gene flow via dispersal through fragmented savannahs (i.e., the ability to cross watershed divides) is the key factor determining re-invasion risk across basins. Thus riparian forests are necessary habitat facilitating local persistence, but they are not identified as the strongest factor governing re-invasion between basins.

210.Option: The use of biconical traps in multiple river basins, which may have introduced tsetse flies from adjacent basins into the Mouhoun River Basin during sampling
Evidence: From November 2007 to March 2008 each site was sampled using 5–10 unbaited biconical traps [29].

The population genetics data presented here suggest that the savannah area of the watershed divide between two adjacent river basins does not seem to represent a significant barrier to gene flow for the two riverine tsetse species studied.

The analysis presented here showed that dispersal of G. tachinoides across river basins was ∼3 times higher than G. p. gambiensis, which suggests that G. tachinoides flies have the ability to disperse with ease despite the severe fragmentation of the riparian gallery forests in the study area.
210.Rationale: Although biconical traps were used for sampling (quoted above), there is no evidence in the passage that sampling traps introduced flies across basins. The paper attributes observed genetic mixing to natural dispersal and gene flow across watershed divides—not to sampling artifacts. Therefore the option that traps introduced flies and thus drove re-invasion risk is not supported by the text.

210.Option: Gene flow across watershed divides due to dispersal through fragmented savannah habitats
Evidence: It was proposed to use the “river basin” as a unit of operation in area-wide integrated pest management (AW-IPM) programmes [28] against tsetse in West Africa. This assumed that each primary river basin (and possibly also secondary and tertiary) contained riverine tsetse populations that were geographically isolated from those belonging to adjacent river basins. If this hypothesis proves to be correct, it would be very beneficial for the present eradication campaign since it would allow limiting the control effort to the Mouhoun River Basin.

earlier studies have indicated that riverine tsetse flies were able to disperse up to 2km into the savannah areas bordering the riparian forests [7] and a recent genetic study in Burkina Faso suggested that G. p. gambiensis was able to cross the watershed divide between the Mouhoun and the Comoe river basins that contained natural woody savannah [26].

The population genetics data presented here suggest that the savannah area of the watershed divide between two adjacent river basins does not seem to represent a significant barrier to gene flow for the two riverine tsetse species studied.
For both species, isolation by distance between sites of different river basins (or even at a micro-scale for G. p.gambiensis) was evidenced, without a particular role of river basins.

G. tachinoides dispersed ∼3 times higher than G. p. gambiensis, which suggests that G. tachinoides flies have the ability to disperse with ease despite the severe fragmentation of the riparian gallery forests in the study area.
210.Rationale: The passage repeatedly identifies natural dispersal across watershed divides via savannah areas and resulting gene flow between basins as the primary reason adjacent river basins are not biologically isolated. That lack of isolation (gene flow across basins) is explicitly cited as the reason re-invasion is likely and thus must be controlled (e.g., via buffer zones or expanded campaigns). Therefore gene flow across watershed divides due to dispersal through fragmented savannah is the strongest factor influencing re-invasion risk.

210.Option: The higher effective population density of G. p. gambiensis compared to G. tachinoides, which increases the probability of successful re-invasion from neighboring basins
Evidence: For G. tachinoides ... effective subpopulation size of ∼100. A mean sampling surface as defined above as S∼0.1 km2, resulted in an effective population density of De = Ne/S≈1000 flies per km2.

For G. p. gambiensis ... The surface defined above S∼0.01 km2 leads to an effective density of G. p. gambiensis De = Ne/S≈8000 (for Ne = 80) or De = 15000 (for Ne = 150) G. p. gambiensis per km2 in the study area.

The analysis presented here showed that dispersal of G. tachinoides across river basins was ∼3 times higher than G. p. gambiensis, which suggests that G. tachinoides flies have the ability to disperse with ease despite the severe fragmentation of the riparian gallery forests in the study area.
210.Rationale: Although G. p. gambiensis has a higher effective density in sampled areas, the passage emphasizes that re-invasion across basins is driven primarily by dispersal and gene flow across watershed divides. G. tachinoides, despite lower density, disperses more across basins and thus poses a greater re-invasion risk. Therefore higher local density of G. p. gambiensis is not identified as the most influential factor for cross-basin re-invasion in the text.

210.Option: The genetic structure of G. tachinoides populations, which shows a higher degree of local inbreeding and reduced gene flow compared to G. p. gambiensis
Evidence: G. tachinoides occurs as a panmictic population along its riverine habitat in the same area, due to its more xerophylous nature allowing it to disperse more easily between suitable habitat patches [23]–[25].

For G. tachinoides, LD tests were carried out with all the 9 loci ... there was a strong and highly significant heterozygote deficit (FIS = 0.227, 95% CI = [0.067, 0.429] in G. tachinoides due to loci pGp17, pGp20X, pGp24, pGp28 and B104X ... Consequently, it was assumed with confidence that stuttering and null alleles totally explained the heterozygote deficits observed at these five loci and we can confidently conclude that the G. tachinoides subsamples conformed to the random mating hypothesis.

The analysis presented here showed that dispersal of G. tachinoides across river basins was ∼3 times higher than G. p. gambiensis, which suggests that G. tachinoides flies have the ability to disperse with ease despite the severe fragmentation of the riparian gallery forests in the study area.

For G. p. gambiensis, two hierarchical levels appeared to contribute significantly to genetic structure, the trap in each site (FTrap/Site = 0.0117, P-value = 0.033) and the site in each river basin (FSite/Basin = 0.0379, P-value = 0.001). The analysis therefore revealed that river basins were not important for the genetic structuring of the G. p. gambiensis populations (P-value>0.6).
210.Rationale: This option states the opposite of what the passage documents. The text describes G. tachinoides as relatively panmictic with higher cross-basin dispersal, whereas G. p. gambiensis shows more local genetic structuring. Therefore the claim that G. tachinoides shows higher local inbreeding and reduced gene flow compared to G. p. gambiensis is not supported by the passage.

211.Option: Declining seroprevalence in young children signals a growing pool of susceptible individuals, which precedes and predicts the onset of cyclical EV-A71 epidemics
Evidence: We hypothesized that falls in EV-A71 seroprevalence rates were associated with new epidemics.

we showed that statistically significant decreases in population seroprevalence (as a proxy for immunity) are temporally associated with subsequent epidemics, after adjustment for age, temperature, rainfall, rain days, and ultraviolet radiance.

Every 10% decrease in EV-A71 seroprevalence between preceding and current months was not significantly associated with epidemics in univariate analysis; but there was a significant association in multivariate analysis (aOR, 1.16; CI 1.01–1.35; P<0.034). This shows that every 10% fall in monthly seroprevalence compared to the preceding month is associated with 16% higher odds of an epidemic.
211.Rationale: The passage explicitly hypothesized and demonstrated that falls in seroprevalence (used as a proxy for population immunity) are temporally associated with subsequent epidemics. The quoted multivariate result quantifies that a 10% decline in seroprevalence is associated with higher odds of an epidemic, supporting the interpretation that declining seroprevalence in young children reflects an expanding susceptible pool that precedes and helps predict cyclical EV-A71 epidemics.

211.Option: The observed increase in seroprevalence during epidemic years reflects a direct effect of the virus on immune system activation, rather than prior exposure or infection
Evidence: The higher seropositive rates and GMT levels seen during epidemic years are likely to reflect recent infection.

The overall seroprevalence and GMT were significantly higher in epidemic years (seroprevalence 67.4%, 95% CI 63.8–70.9%; GMT 23.6, 95% CI 21.8–25.5) compared to non-epidemic years (seroprevalence 56.6%, 95% CI 53.6–59.5%; GMT 17.8, 95% CI 16.7–19.0; P<0.001).
211.Rationale: The passage interprets higher seroprevalence and GMT during epidemic years as likely reflecting recent infection/exposure. The option's claim that the increase reflects a direct effect of the virus on immune activation independent of exposure/infection contradicts the authors' stated interpretation that increased seropositivity indicates recent infections during epidemics.

211.Option: The cyclical pattern of EV-A71 epidemics is best explained by seasonal climatic variation, as temperature and rain days were found to influence epidemic likelihood
Evidence: the overall likelihood of an epidemic was influenced by temperature and rain days, but not rainfall or ultraviolet radiance.

we showed that statistically significant decreases in population seroprevalence (as a proxy for immunity) are temporally associated with subsequent epidemics, after adjustment for age, temperature, rainfall, rain days, and ultraviolet radiance.

The monthly seroprevalence was positively associated with the odds of an epidemic period in the univariate analysis (OR for every 10% increase in seroprevalence, 1.40; 95% CI 1.18–1.65; P<0.001) and multivariate analysis after adjusting for plausible confounding factors such as age, temperature, rainfall, rain days, and ultraviolet radiance (adjusted OR for every 10% increase in seroprevalence, 1.45; 95% CI 1.24–1.69; P<0.001).
211.Rationale: Although the passage notes that temperature and rain days influenced epidemic likelihood, it also demonstrates that changes in seroprevalence (population immunity) are strongly and independently associated with epidemic timing after adjustment for climatic variables. The authors conclude that changes in population immunity are the major driving force of the cyclical pattern, so attributing the cycles primarily to seasonal climatic variation is not supported by the text.

211.Option: The cyclical pattern of EV-A71 epidemics is primarily driven by antigenic drift in the VP1 protein, as evidenced by the presence of positively selected codons at positions 98 and 145
Evidence: Two codons at positions 98 and 145 were under positive selective pressure, likely resulting in the emergence of new virus variants or lineage extinction.

While the increased diversity may simply reflect a larger number of infections, we cannot exclude that new variants with antigenic changes may escape population immunity and contribute to cyclical epidemics.
Evidence of strong negative selection and 2 codons with positive selection may explain the emergence of immune escape though the role in cyclical patterns of EV-A71 epidemics remains unclear.
211.Rationale: The passage acknowledges positively selected sites in VP1 and that antigenic change could possibly contribute to epidemics, but it explicitly states that the role of these genetic changes in cyclical patterns is unclear and that increased diversity may merely reflect larger epidemics. The authors emphasize changes in population immunity as the primary driver. Therefore, claiming the cycles are primarily driven by antigenic drift is not supported by the text.

211.Option: A sustained increase in seroprevalence over multiple years indicates that herd immunity is being maintained, reducing the likelihood of future epidemics
Evidence: We found clear support for our hypothesis, showing that statistically significant decreases in population seroprevalence (as a proxy for immunity) are temporally associated with subsequent epidemics.

For such a transmissible virus, the epidemic size is mainly dependent on the size of the susceptible population [53]. Following a viral epidemic, most of the population at risk would become immune. It may then take 2–3 years for the susceptible population to be replenished by newborns, and to be large enough for the R0 to increase to >1, hence leading to a cyclical pattern of EV-A71 epidemics every 2–3 years.
211.Rationale: The passage frames seroprevalence as a proxy for population immunity and explains that when many individuals are immune after an epidemic, the susceptible pool is reduced and it takes time to replenish susceptibles before another epidemic can occur. By converse reasoning, a sustained increase in seroprevalence would imply maintained herd immunity and a reduced likelihood of immediate future epidemics; this interpretation is consistent with the study's conclusions about population immunity driving epidemic timing.

212.Option: The absence of vascular leakage and paracellular barrier disruption allows for isolation of vaccine-induced immune effects from non-specific inflammatory responses
Evidence: It was found that levels of albumin in fecal fluid of both control and V. cholerae-inoculated intestinal loops were under detection limit (<0.3 g/dL). This result indicated that vascular leakage did not contribute to the development of diarrhea in this experimental model of cholera.

It was found that V. cholerae infection had no effect on serum FITC-dextran compared with saline control and CT-injected groups (Fig. 4B). Taken together, our results suggested that paracellular permeability of the mouse intestine was unaltered by V. cholerae infection and CT exposure.
212.Rationale: The passage does state that vascular leakage and paracellular permeability were not involved in pathogenesis of diarrhea in this model, which means the model isolates secretory mechanisms from vascular or barrier disruption. However, the question asks which feature would most directly support evaluating the durability of protection (i.e., how long vaccine-induced protection lasts). The absence of vascular leakage is a desirable model characteristic for mechanistic clarity, but it does not most directly enable longitudinal assessment of vaccine durability. Durability assessment requires a mature, longitudinally responsive immune system and the ability to follow immune responses or protection over time, not merely the absence of non-specific inflammatory leakage.

212.Option: The mature immune system in adult mice allows for longitudinal assessment of immune responses following vaccination and repeated challenge
Evidence: due to the maturation of immune system in the adult mice, this cholera model could be used for evaluating preventive efficacy of cholera vaccine
212.Rationale: The passage explicitly links the maturity of the adult mouse immune system to the model's suitability for evaluating vaccine preventive efficacy. A mature immune system is the key feature that permits longitudinal tracking of immune responses (antibody titers, memory responses) and performing repeated challenge experiments at different intervals post-vaccination to measure waning or durability of protection. Therefore this feature most directly supports evaluation of durability over time.

212.Option: The use of outbred ICR mice ensures genetic diversity that mimics human population variation and supports long-term immune monitoring after vaccination
Evidence: Six-week-old ICR outbred mice (weight 30–35 g) were acquired from the National Laboratory Animal Center, Bangkok, Thailand.

mice are relatively inexpensive, easy to handle and require smaller quantities of compounds/vaccines for therapeutic/preventive evaluation. Transgenic mice are also available for studying cholera pathophysiology.
212.Rationale: The passage notes the use of outbred ICR mice (indicating greater genetic diversity than inbred strains) and lists practical advantages of mice. While genetic diversity can make results more generalizable, the passage does not claim that outbred status specifically enables assessment of durability of protection over time. The most direct requirement for durability studies is a mature immune system that can be followed longitudinally (see evidence cited for Option 1). Thus, although outbred mice offer some advantage for population variability, this feature is not the most direct support for evaluating durability of vaccine-induced protection.

212.Option: The consistent induction of intestinal fluid accumulation within 12 hours post-challenge enables repeated challenge experiments to assess waning immunity over time
Evidence: intestinal fluid secretion increased over time and reached its peak at 12 h after V. cholerae inoculation (loop weight/length ratio = 0.203±0.037 g/cm). This level of intestinal fluid secretion was sustained until 18 h post-inoculation (loop weight/length ratio = 0.192±0.042 g/cm).

Diarrhea success rate, which indicated model reproducibility, and survival rate of mice were 90% and 80% at 12 h post-inoculation, and 68% and 50% at 18 h post-inoculation, respectively (Fig. 1B). Therefore, an optimal incubation period for induction of diarrhea by V. cholerae was 12 h.
212.Rationale: The passage documents that the model reproducibly induces intestinal fluid accumulation with an optimal 12 h incubation, which is valuable for standardized challenge experiments. However, the question asks which feature most directly supports evaluating the durability of vaccine protection over time. Rapid, consistent challenge endpoints are useful for measuring protection at multiple timepoints, but they do not by themselves enable longitudinal immune assessment—the mature adult immune system (Option 1) is the direct enabler of durability studies. Therefore, while the 12 h reproducible challenge is supportive, it is not the most direct feature for assessing durability.

213.Option: The presence of helminth infection may reduce the sensitivity of T. cruzi PCR by suppressing the host immune response
Evidence: Strongyloides spp infection in the murine model induces a Th2 response and regulatory cytokine induction (IL-10), leading to a suppression of pro-inflammatory cytokines and diminishing Th1 response [26].
percentage of patients with positive T. cruzi RT-PCR was higher in patients with helminth infection compared with those without helminth infection (75% versus 35.2%, p = 0.021).
Helminth infection could increase the probability of having a positive T. cruzi PCR.
213.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that helminth infection causes immunomodulation (Th2/IL-10) and is associated with a higher, not lower, proportion of positive T. cruzi PCR results (75% vs 35.2%). Thus the data and biologic rationale point toward increased detectability rather than reduced sensitivity of PCR in the presence of helminth infection, so this option is incorrect.

213.Option: The variability in T. cruzi PCR positivity across settings is primarily due to differences in helminth infection prevalence
Evidence: The percentage of positive T. cruzi PCR in peripheral blood in patients with Chagas disease in the chronic phase highly varies depending on the study: it ranges from 80% to 90% in studies performed in endemic countries, and is lower in non-endemic countries, ranging from 28% to 66% [5–10].
almost all patients in our study came from Bolivia.

the geographical distribution of the different T. cruzi discrete typing units (DTUs) differs from country to country, which may have impact in the clinical presentation or in the proportion of patients with positive T. cruzi PCR in peripheral blood [29].
213.Rationale: While helminth infection is one factor associated with higher PCR positivity in this study, the passage describes multiple contributors to variability in PCR positivity (endemic vs non-endemic settings, geographic differences in T. cruzi DTUs, study context). The passage does not state that helminth prevalence is the primary driver of variability, so this option overstates the evidence and is incorrect.

213.Option: Helminth co-infection may increase PCR detectability of T. cruzi DNA without indicating higher parasite burden or disease progression
Evidence: percentage of patients with positive T. cruzi RT-PCR was higher in patients with helminth infection compared with those without helminth infection (75% versus 35.2%, p = 0.021).
Helminth infection could increase the probability of having a positive T. cruzi PCR.

Strongyloides spp infection in the murine model induces a Th2 response and regulatory cytokine induction (IL-10), leading to a suppression of pro-inflammatory cytokines and diminishing Th1 response [26].
Further studies are needed to evaluate the impact of treating the helminth infection on the positivity of T. cruzi PCR.
213.Rationale: The passage shows helminth co-infection is associated with higher PCR positivity and describes immune modulation (Th2/IL-10) that could alter detectability. It also emphasizes uncertainty and the need for further study about the implications of this increased PCR positivity. Therefore, when interpreting a positive PCR in a co-infected patient, investigators must consider that co-infection may increase detectability (PCR positivity) and that this does not automatically equate to clinical progression or definitive higher disease burden without further evidence.

213.Option: Helminth co-infection should be treated before performing T. cruzi PCR to avoid false-positive results
Evidence: Further studies are needed to evaluate the impact of treating the helminth infection on the positivity of T. cruzi PCR.
Helminth infection could increase the probability of having a positive T. cruzi PCR.
213.Rationale: The authors note that further research is needed to understand whether treating helminth infection affects PCR positivity; they do not recommend pre-emptive treatment before PCR testing. Also, the passage describes increased true PCR positivity associated with helminth infection rather than 'false-positive' PCR results. Thus there is insufficient evidence to support the blanket recommendation to treat helminth infection before PCR, making this option incorrect.

213.Option: A positive T. cruzi PCR in co-infected patients may indicate a higher risk of treatment failure due to increased parasite load
Evidence: percentage of patients with positive T. cruzi RT-PCR was higher in patients with helminth infection compared with those without helminth infection (75% versus 35.2%, p = 0.021).

late co-infected mice (the T. cruzi infection took place 8–12 weeks after Taenia crassiceps infection, when a predominant Th2-type cytokine response is expected) showed significantly higher parasitaemia compared with non co-infected and early co-infected mice [25].

Helminth infection could increase the probability of having a positive T. cruzi PCR. Given that current clinical trials that evaluate treatment efficacy in Chagas disease are based on the positivity of T. cruzi PCR, this fact may be relevant. [13, 28].
213.Rationale: The passage documents that helminth co-infection is associated with higher PCR positivity in humans and with higher parasitaemia in animal models, and it highlights that clinical trials use PCR positivity to define treatment failure. Therefore, a positive T. cruzi PCR in co-infected patients could reflect increased parasite burden and might confound assessments of treatment failure, so this factor must be considered when interpreting PCR results.

214.Option: The case-fatality ratio trend over time is best interpreted by comparing countries with similar outbreak histories and surveillance systems, regardless of vaccination status
Evidence: JE fatality risk might change as the characteristics of JE susceptible populations evolve with the strengthened surveillance system, expanded immunization coverage, increased age of infection, advanced case confirmation methods, and improved healthcare.

The correlation of JE vaccination with reduced JE CFR would be consistent with vaccination which not only reduced infection, but also moderated the disease severity [45]. However, it is also possible that the relationship we observe with vaccination is also due to changes in surveillance which were brought with the JE vaccination program [38] or other changes in healthcare that occur concurrently with vaccination, though we did attempt to account for these in the analysis, we could not completely.
214.Rationale: The passage explicitly lists both surveillance changes and vaccination as influences on CFR over time and cautions that the observed association with vaccination may partly reflect concurrent changes in surveillance and healthcare. Therefore it is incorrect to ignore vaccination status when interpreting CFR trends; comparing only countries with similar outbreak histories and surveillance (and disregarding vaccination) would omit a key driver of changes in fatality risk.

214.Option: The decline in case-fatality ratios after 2000 is primarily attributable to the expansion of Japanese Encephalitis immunization programs in high-burden countries
Evidence: Our analysis estimated that the reduced JE CFR estimate was correlated with the expanded coverage of JE immunization (Table 2).

However, it is also possible that the relationship we observe with vaccination is also due to changes in surveillance which were brought with the JE vaccination program [38] or other changes in healthcare that occur concurrently with vaccination, though we did attempt to account for these in the analysis, we could not completely.

After accounting for the impact of vaccination status, demographic structures, surveillance scales, employed JE diagnostic methods, and other available socioeconomic features, effect of country and time still remained statistically significant (Table 2). Therefore, there are unexplained variations after addressing the current factors and indicated the presence of unobserved features, for instance, improved critical JE case management over time, differences in genotype, differences in healthcare seeking, and heterogenous case-reporting rate which were also crucial in understanding JE CFR variations across time and space.
214.Rationale: Although the passage reports a correlation between expanded immunization and reduced CFR, it explicitly warns that surveillance improvements, diagnostics, healthcare changes, and other unobserved factors could also drive the decline. Therefore it is not correct to assert that the decline is primarily attributable to immunization alone without accounting for these other influences.

214.Option: The observed trend in case-fatality ratios over time is best understood by focusing on countries with the highest vaccination coverage, as they provide the most reliable data on program effectiveness
Evidence: The proportion of JE endemic countries that conducted JE surveillance and routinely tested JE cases has increased from 21% (5/24) in 2012 to 92% (22/24) in 2016[38, 39].
This dataset included records from studies that used multiple methods of JE diagnosis and the heterogeneity across the studies might lead to a biased dataset used in the model construction.

Although 13 countries and areas were included in the modelling, 91% of analyzed JE patients came from China and India. The identified global pattern between CFR and socioeconomic features could be dominated by the results in these two countries.
214.Rationale: High vaccination coverage does not necessarily equate to the most reliable data. The passage emphasizes heterogeneity in surveillance intensity, diagnostic methods, and data concentration (e.g., dominated by China and India). Therefore focusing only on high-coverage countries would not by itself ensure the most reliable assessment of program effectiveness; surveillance and diagnostic comparability are critical.

214.Option: The decline in case-fatality ratios after 2000 is likely due to improved case confirmation methods and reduced underreporting, independent of vaccination or outbreak frequency
Evidence: JE fatality risk might change as the characteristics of JE susceptible populations evolve with the strengthened surveillance system, expanded immunization coverage, increased age of infection, advanced case confirmation methods, and improved healthcare.

The correlation of JE vaccination with reduced JE CFR would be consistent with vaccination which not only reduced infection, but also moderated the disease severity [45]. However, it is also possible that the relationship we observe with vaccination is also due to changes in surveillance which were brought with the JE vaccination program [38] or other changes in healthcare that occur concurrently with vaccination, though we did attempt to account for these in the analysis, we could not completely.
214.Rationale: Improved diagnostics and reduced underreporting are plausible contributors to lower observed CFRs, but the passage does not support the assertion that these factors act independently of vaccination. The authors report a correlation between vaccination and reduced CFR and explicitly caution that both surveillance and vaccination (and other healthcare changes) likely interplay; claiming independence from vaccination is not supported.

214.Option: The observed decline in case-fatality ratios after 2000 may reflect reduced outbreak frequency and improved surveillance more than vaccination alone
Evidence: JE fatality risk might change as the characteristics of JE susceptible populations evolve with the strengthened surveillance system, expanded immunization coverage, increased age of infection, advanced case confirmation methods, and improved healthcare.
The proportion of JE endemic countries that conducted JE surveillance and routinely tested JE cases has increased from 21% (5/24) in 2012 to 92% (22/24) in 2016[38, 39].

However, it is also possible that the relationship we observe with vaccination is also due to changes in surveillance which were brought with the JE vaccination program [38] or other changes in healthcare that occur concurrently with vaccination, though we did attempt to account for these in the analysis, we could not completely.

After accounting for the impact of vaccination status, demographic structures, surveillance scales, employed JE diagnostic methods, and other available socioeconomic features, effect of country and time still remained statistically significant (Table 2). Therefore, there are unexplained variations after addressing the current factors and indicated the presence of unobserved features, for instance, improved critical JE case management over time, differences in genotype, differences in healthcare seeking, and heterogenous case-reporting rate which were also crucial in understanding JE CFR variations across time and space.
214.Rationale: The passage documents major improvements in surveillance and diagnostics over time and explicitly cautions that the apparent association between vaccination and lower CFR could reflect concurrent surveillance and healthcare improvements. It further notes unexplained temporal/country effects (including case-reporting heterogeneity and improved case management).
