Thus, when comparing countries with different surveillance and vaccination levels, changes in surveillance/outbreak frequency and reporting practice are critical influences on death-to-case ratio trends and can explain much of the observed decline alongside (or even more than) vaccination alone.

215.Option: The significantly higher parasite load in VK247-infected individuals compared to those with VK210 or mixed infections provides direct evidence of increased disease severity due to a more virulent variant
Evidence: The parasite loads were higher in individuals infected by VK247 (median of 3.35 log parasites/μL; IQR 3.1–3.8 log parasites/μL) compared to patients infected by VK210 (median of 2.5 log parasites/μL; IQR 1.8–3.1 log parasites/μL) (p = 0.05) and patients with mixed infection (median of 1.8 log parasites/μL; IQR 0.52–3.1 log parasites/μL) (p = 0.01) (Fig 3A).

VK247 induced a more prominent inflammatory profile and the highest parasite loads, suggesting that these CSP polymorphisms have systemic effects changing variables that may influence the course of infection.

However, in patients with VK247, this was not observed and no correlation between the two cytokines was found (r = 0.3 and p = 0.68) (Fig 3D), which indicates that the increase in inflammatory cytokines was not followed by the regulatory effect of IL-10 in these patients.
215.Rationale: The quoted data do show VK247 infections have higher parasite loads and a stronger inflammatory profile, which supports greater virulence/severity. However, the question asks which finding most strongly supports that the parasite contributes to more severe disease by disrupting immune regulation. Demonstrating higher parasite load alone does not prove disruption of immune regulation. The more direct evidence for disrupted regulation is the lack of IL-6/IL-10 correlation in VK247 (quoted above). Therefore, although higher parasite load is consistent with increased severity, it is not the strongest single finding to support the specific hypothesis about disruption of immune regulation.

215.Option: The lack of correlation between IL-6 and IL-10 in VK247-infected individuals, despite high inflammatory response and parasite load, indicates disrupted immune regulation
Evidence: The variants did not influence the IL-10 concentrations (Fig 3C), but in patients infected with VK210, the IL-10 levels correlated with IL-6 concentrations (r = 0.92 and p<0.0001) (Fig 3E). However, in patients with VK247, this was not observed and no correlation between the two cytokines was found (r = 0.3 and p = 0.68) (Fig 3D), which indicates that the increase in inflammatory cytokines was not followed by the regulatory effect of IL-10 in these patients.
The parasite loads were higher in individuals infected by VK247 ...
A significant increase of IL-6 in patients with VK247 infection was observed when compared to VK210 and mixed infections (Fig 3B).
215.Rationale: The hypothesis concerns a parasite that causes more severe disease by disrupting immune regulation. The most direct evidence of disrupted regulation in the passage is the absence of the normal positive relationship between inflammatory cytokine IL-6 and regulatory IL-10 in VK247 infections, despite VK247 having higher IL-6 and higher parasite loads. The passage explicitly states that in VK247 there was no IL-6/IL-10 correlation (r = 0.3, p = 0.68) and interprets this as the inflammatory increase not being followed by IL-10 regulatory effect, which directly supports the idea that VK247 disrupts immune regulation and could thereby contribute to more severe clinical course.

215.Option: The absence of detectable IFN-γ in plasma of infected individuals suggests that the immune response is suppressed, which may contribute to uncontrolled parasite growth and severe disease
Evidence: Only eight patients had detectable IFN-γ concentrations with a median of approximately zero; however, there was no significant difference compared to the healthy group (Fig 1D).
Initially, it was observed that patients infected with P. vivax showed increased plasma IL-10 and IL-6 levels which was associated with the parasite load.
215.Rationale: While IFN-γ was low in many patients, the passage notes there was no significant difference from healthy controls, and this finding is not linked specifically to the VK247 variant or to increased severity. The hypothesis asks for evidence that a globally distributed, Duffy-dependent parasite variant contributes to more severe disease by disrupting immune regulation; a low/undetectable IFN-γ overall does not specifically indicate disrupted regulation by VK247 nor explain the variant-specific pattern (VK247 shows high IL-6 and lack of IL-10 response). Therefore this option does not strongly support the stated hypothesis.

215.Option: The observation that IL-10 concentrations were higher in plasma than in PBMC culture supernatants suggests that systemic immune regulation is active in vivo, which may explain the higher parasite load in VK247-infected individuals
Evidence: The median IL-10 concentration was 367 times higher in the plasma from patients when compared to that of the culture supernatant.
Despite the PBMC from patients produced more IL-10 than healthy individuals (Fig 4B), the IL-10 production was almost abolished in the absence of systemic influence of parasite (Fig 4D).
The variants did not influence the IL-10 concentrations (Fig 3C).
215.Rationale: The quoted results show systemic (plasma) IL-10 levels are much higher than PBMC supernatant levels, indicating in vivo regulatory signals. However, the passage also states that IL-10 concentrations were not different across variants, so this plasma-versus-ex vivo difference is a general feature of infection and does not explain why VK247-infected individuals have higher parasite loads or disrupted regulation. The key variant-specific evidence for disrupted regulation is the absent IL-6/IL-10 correlation in VK247; the global plasma-versus-supernatant IL-10 observation does not specifically support VK247 causing disrupted immune regulation and thus is not the strongest supporting finding.

216.Option: High viremia levels in the first few days of illness, which exceed the threshold for mosquito infection
Evidence: most ambulatory dengue cases were revealed to have viremia levels that exceed the MID50 in the first few days of their illness [11], indicating that most are still infectious to Ae. aegypti when they return to their homes.

Nguyet et al. [11] and Whitehorn et al. [12] have identified the plasma DENV viremia levels in Vietnamese dengue patients needed to successfully infect 50% of exposed Ae. aegypti or Ae. albopictus mosquitoes (50% mosquito infectious dose; MID50).
216.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies viremia relative to the mosquito infectious dose (MID50) as the biological determinant of whether a mosquito becomes infected when it feeds. It states that most ambulatory cases have viremia exceeding the MID50 in the first few days of illness, which ‘‘indicat[es] that most are still infectious to Ae. aegypti when they return to their homes.’’ Therefore high viremia in early illness is the primary factor driving likelihood of transmission to mosquitoes.

216.Option: The use of DEET-based repellent, which provides longer protection in febrile patients than in healthy individuals
Evidence: we found no difference in the duration of protection from the DEET-based repellent between dengue patients and healthy control participants.

The effectiveness of 13% DEET-based repellent, as measured by the median difference between landing times of repellent-treated and non-treated arms, was 49m32s (IQR = 16m01s–01h19m29s) and 56m43s (IQR = 15m55s–02h20m00s) for healthy controls and patients, respectively.
216.Rationale: The passage reports no difference in repellent duration of protection between febrile dengue patients and healthy controls. Thus the specific claim that DEET provides longer protection in febrile patients is not supported by the text and cannot be regarded as the factor that most strongly influences transmission likelihood.

216.Option: The presence of symptomatic illness, which correlates with higher viral load and increased mosquito biting frequency
Evidence: Symptomatic dengue patients had been found to transmit DENV to Aedes spp. mosquitoes from ~1.5–2 days prior to fever onset until day 3–6 of illness [8–10].

most ambulatory dengue cases were revealed to have viremia levels that exceed the MID50 in the first few days of their illness [11], indicating that most are still infectious to Ae. aegypti when they return to their homes.

The capacity of asymptomatically-infected individuals to transmit to Ae. aegypti has been recently highlighted, but more work is needed to understand their prevalence and importance to the overall epidemiology of dengue [10].

dengue patients, like other febrile patients, may be more attractive to mosquitoes compared to non-febrile individuals due to their higher body temperature, and therefore higher production of carbon dioxide and possibly other volatile compounds [30–32].
216.Rationale: While symptomatic (febrile) patients can transmit and fever may increase mosquito attraction, the passage makes two important points that undercut the option's blanket claim: (1) transmissibility is tied to viremia relative to MID50 (not simply symptom presence), and (2) asymptomatic individuals can also transmit. The passage does not assert that symptomatic status per se ‘‘correlates with higher viral load’’ universally; it instead emphasizes viremia levels in early illness. Therefore the option overstates the role of symptomatic illness as the strongest determinant.

216.Option: Increased mosquito attraction due to higher body temperature and elevated carbon dioxide production during fever
Evidence: dengue patients, like other febrile patients, may be more attractive to mosquitoes compared to non-febrile individuals due to their higher body temperature, and therefore higher production of carbon dioxide and possibly other volatile compounds [30–32].

we found no clear evidence that the host’s tympanic temperature affected the duration of protection from Ae. aegypti landing, irrespective of the hosts’ febrile status (Hazard ratio 0.89; 95% CI 0.61–1.29; p = 0.53 per +1°C increase in tympanic temperature).
216.Rationale: The passage supports the biological plausibility that fever can increase attractiveness to mosquitoes via higher temperature and CO2 production, so this is a contributing factor. However, it is not presented as the primary determinant of transmission risk; the text emphasizes viremia relative to MID50 as the key driver of mosquito infection. Moreover, the study found no clear effect of measured tympanic temperature on mosquito landing times. Thus increased attraction from fever is a relevant but not the strongest single influence.

216.Option: The duration of the febrile phase, which extends from day 1 to day 6 of illness and increases the window of exposure
Evidence: Symptomatic dengue patients had been found to transmit DENV to Aedes spp. mosquitoes from ~1.5–2 days prior to fever onset until day 3–6 of illness [8–10].

most ambulatory dengue cases were revealed to have viremia levels that exceed the MID50 in the first few days of their illness [11], indicating that most are still infectious to Ae. aegypti when they return to their homes.
216.Rationale: The duration of infectiousness (roughly spanning the period given) does increase the window during which transmission can occur, and the passage cites the period of transmissibility. However, the question asks which factor most strongly influences the likelihood of transmission during early illness. The passage identifies viremia magnitude relative to the MID50 as the proximate biological determinant of whether a mosquito becomes infected. Duration of fever/window of exposure is important but secondary to the viral load that determines infectivity at any given time.

217.Option: Widespread detection of antifilarial antibodies across multiple villages with no significant association to environmental or occupational factors
Evidence: The maps confirm that villages with high prevalence of Bm33 Ab were more widespread across the territory, while the distribution of villages with high prevalence of Ag, Wb123 and Bm14 Ab was more confined to the north-west of the territory.
The spatial models for all infection makers indicated that there was a positive association between the prevalence of LF and the extent of tree coverage in the 20 m buffers.
There was a consistent positive association between all Abs and tuna cannery workers. Also, a positive association between Bm14 and Bm33 Abs and individuals working in outdoor locations.
217.Rationale: Although Bm33 Ab was more widespread, the passage documents clear and statistically significant associations between infection markers and environmental (tree coverage) and occupational (tuna cannery, outdoor work) factors. The presence of these associations contradicts the phrasing "no significant association to environmental or occupational factors," so this option does not indicate localized drivers over widespread community exposure.

217.Option: Uniform distribution of infection markers across all villages with no evidence of clustering or environmental gradients
Evidence: The maps confirm that villages with high prevalence of Bm33 Ab were more widespread across the territory, while the distribution of villages with high prevalence of Ag, Wb123 and Bm14 Ab was more confined to the north-west of the territory.
The highest predicted probability of all infection makers (≥0.61) was mainly confined in the north-west part, an area that corresponds largely to the coastal villages of Fagali’i and Fagamalo.

In this study, the Bayesian geostatistical models incorporating sociodemographic and environmental covariates showed that the predicted prevalence of LF was not homogeneous in American Samoa. Small-scale spatial variation in LF prevalence was observed which indicates that there is scope for further spatial analyses to help inform spatially-targeted interventions in American Samoa.
217.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes clustering and non-homogeneous (non-uniform) spatial distributions—particularly high-probability foci in the north-west (Fagali’i, Fagamalo) and small-scale spatial variation—so a uniform distribution with no clustering or gradients is contradicted by the data and thus incorrect.

217.Option: High prevalence of antigenemia in areas with low tree cover and minimal outdoor occupational activity
Evidence: Tree coverage was also positively associated with Ag-positivity, with an estimated increase of 0.4% (95% CrI: 0.1–0.7%) in the odds of Ag-positivity for each 1% increase in the extent of tree coverage in the 20 m buffers.
There was a consistent positive association between all Abs and tuna cannery workers. Also, a positive association between Bm14 and Bm33 Abs and individuals working in outdoor locations.
The spatial models for all infection makers indicated that there was a positive association between the prevalence of LF and the extent of tree coverage in the 20 m buffers.
217.Rationale: The passage reports a positive association between tree cover and antigen positivity, and higher infection among certain occupational groups (tuna cannery, outdoor work). Therefore high antigenemia in areas with low tree cover and minimal outdoor activity contradicts the reported associations; the evidence indicates the opposite pattern.

217.Option: Infection risk is primarily driven by age and sex, with no significant role for environmental or occupational factors
Evidence: There were consistent associations between the infection markers and the sociodemographic variables included in the models. The observed differences among females and males and the positive association with age is most likely to be exposure-related.
There was a consistent positive association between all Abs and tuna cannery workers. Also, a positive association between Bm14 and Bm33 Abs and individuals working in outdoor locations.
The spatial models for all infection makers indicated that there was a positive association between the prevalence of LF and the extent of tree coverage in the 20 m buffers.
217.Rationale: While age and sex were significant predictors, the passage also identifies statistically significant environmental (tree coverage) and occupational (tuna cannery, outdoor) associations. Thus infection risk is not primarily driven exclusively by age/sex with no role for environment/occupation; environmental and behavioral factors are important.

217.Option: High infection risk concentrated in specific villages with strong associations to tree cover and outdoor occupational exposure
Evidence: The highest predicted probability of all infection makers (≥0.61) was mainly confined in the north-west part, an area that corresponds largely to the coastal villages of Fagali’i and Fagamalo.

In this study, the Bayesian geostatistical models incorporating sociodemographic and environmental covariates showed that the predicted prevalence of LF was not homogeneous in American Samoa. Small-scale spatial variation in LF prevalence was observed which indicates that there is scope for further spatial analyses to help inform spatially-targeted interventions in American Samoa.

Tree coverage was also positively associated with Ag-positivity, with an estimated increase of 0.4% (95% CrI: 0.1–0.7%) in the odds of Ag-positivity for each 1% increase in the extent of tree coverage in the 20 m buffers.
There was a consistent positive association between all Abs and tuna cannery workers. Also, a positive association between Bm14 and Bm33 Abs and individuals working in outdoor locations.
The spatial models for all infection makers indicated that there was a positive association between the prevalence of LF and the extent of tree coverage in the 20 m buffers.
217.Rationale: The strongest signal that residual transmission is localized is the concentration of high predicted probabilities in specific villages (Fagali’i, Fagamalo) together with statistically significant positive associations between LF markers and local environmental (tree cover) and behavioral/occupational exposures (tuna cannery, outdoor work). These findings indicate focal, environment- and behavior-driven transmission rather than uniform community-wide exposure.

218.Option: Misdiagnosis by healthcare providers is a systemic failure that reflects inadequate training and contributes to prolonged health system delay, thereby indirectly affecting patient delay
Evidence: We therefore conducted our study with the aim of exploring factors that contribute to the overall delay in diagnosis of leprosy. Such factors can be divided into two categories: those contributing to ‘patient delay’ (defined as the time between symptom onset and patients consulting a medical doctor) and those contributing to ‘health-system delay’ (defined as the time between first consulting a medical doctor and receiving a diagnosis of leprosy).

Reported misdiagnosis remained statistically significantly associated with a longer health-system delay in multivariate analysis (Table 5). The likelihood of misdiagnosed patients receiving a later diagnosis was nearly three times higher than those who were not misdiagnosed (those who were either correctly diagnosed or not given any diagnosis on their first visit) (OR 2.867, 95% CI 1.288–6.384, p = 0.01).
The principal association with longer health system delays was found to be patient reported misdiagnosis.
218.Rationale: While misdiagnosis is clearly a structural/systemic problem and is associated with longer health-system delay (time from first consultation to diagnosis), the question asks specifically about factors that influence the timing of medical consultation (i.e., when patients decide to seek care), independent of clinical presentation. The passage defines misdiagnosis as affecting health-system delay rather than patient delay, so it does not most directly reflect influences on the timing of initial medical consultation.

218.Option: Fear of social isolation due to leprosy-related stigma is a key structural barrier to timely medical consultation
Evidence: For participants who thought their symptoms were due to leprosy but feared they would be isolated from their community, the odds of them waiting longer before consulting was ten times higher than those who did not fear isolation (OR 10.37, CI 2.18–49.45, p = 0.003).
The principal factors found to be associated with patient delay were participants who feared community isolation and those who visited a traditional healer.

These findings indicate that stigma towards leprosy sufferers remains despite the availability of a cure... Fear of isolation was a strong predictor of longer patient delay, implying that community isolation still exists in Brazil... Some patients seem to conceal their symptoms and delay in seeking treatment due to fear of social exclusion [32].
218.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that fear of community isolation (stigma) greatly increases the odds of waiting longer before consulting a medical doctor (a ten-fold effect). This is a social/structural factor that directly influences the timing of seeking care (patient delay), independent of the clinical features present.

218.Option: The presence of insensitive skin lesions is a clinical marker that is more likely to prompt early diagnosis, indicating that structural factors such as clinician training influence diagnostic timing
Evidence: The only presenting symptom associated with health system delay was hypo-pigmented, insensitive skin lesions. Patients presenting with insensitive skin lesions had a 55% higher chance of being diagnosed quicker than those who did not present with this symptom (OR 0.463, CI 0.222–0.964, p = 0.039).

The principal association with longer health system delays was found to be patient reported misdiagnosis. Higher numbers of clinical examinations (pertaining to leprosy diagnosis) performed by doctors were associated with a quicker diagnosis...
218.Rationale: Insensitive skin lesions are a clinical presentation that makes earlier diagnosis more likely; this reflects diagnostic sensitivity to clinical signs rather than a social or structural determinant of when patients seek care. The question asks for the factor that most directly reflects social/structural influences on timing of medical consultation (i.e., patient delay). Because insensitive skin lesions pertain to clinical presentation and affect diagnosis after consultation, this option does not best answer the question.

218.Option: Perception that symptoms are not serious reflects a lack of health literacy, which is shaped by socioeconomic conditions and educational access
Evidence: Fifty-five participants, (45.1%), said they waited before consulting because they did not believe their symptoms were serious. The odds of them waiting longer was three times higher compared to those who did consider their symptoms serious (OR 3.114, 95% CI 1.235–7.856, p = 0.016).

Many patients appear to still be ignorant regarding leprosy symptoms. Nearly half of the participants in this study (45.1%) waited before consulting a doctor because they did not believe their symptoms were serious.
As leprosy is a disease of the poor and literacy rates are low among this population in Brazil, it could be useful to incorporate graphics or animations into patient education tools...
218.Rationale: The passage documents that a large proportion of participants delayed care because they did not think symptoms were serious and links this to lack of knowledge/health literacy. It also situates leprosy among poorer, lower-literacy populations, indicating socioeconomic and educational context shape this perception. Thus this factor directly reflects social/structural influences on the timing of seeking care (patient delay).

218.Option: Use of traditional healers is a culturally embedded practice that reflects limited access to formal healthcare systems and systemic inequities in health service availability
Evidence: Participants who visited a traditional healer before a qualified medical doctor had 10 times higher odds of waiting longer before consulting a trained medical doctor than those who did not (OR 10.386, CI 1.092–98.784, p = 0.042).

Visiting a traditional healer was not found to be as clinically relevant as found in Nepal, Nigeria and Ethiopia, in contributing to patient delay... only a small proportion (2.5%) of participants in this study reported visiting a traditional healer.
This percentage may be higher in poorer, more rural areas where access to healthcare is limited; future research should be done in such areas to explore this further [28].
218.Rationale: The study shows that visiting traditional healers before seeking formal medical care is associated with substantially longer patient delay. The authors explicitly link this practice to rural, poorer areas with limited access to healthcare, framing it as a culturally and structurally mediated pathway that delays formal consultation. Therefore it directly reflects social/structural influences on timing of medical consultation.

219.Option: The shift toward human feeding in April 2018, particularly in An. coustani and An. rufipes, indicates that these species have become primary vectors, implying that current vector control strategies targeting An. funestus and An. gambiae s.l. are no longer sufficient to interrupt transmission.
Evidence: The propensity for human feeding increased significantly from the December 2017 (27% human positive) to the April 2018 (44% human positive) collection (χ2 = 44.8, 1 DOF, p < 0.0001), with anthropophilic shifts observed for An. coustani (32% to 60%), An. funestus (9% to 50%), An. rufipes (23% to 44%), and An. arabiensis (38% to 53%).
Anopheles coustani, comprising 37% of our mosquito collection, was recently implicated as a vector of malaria [10].

By analyzing all of our captured Anopheline mosquitoes, as opposed to focusing only on the predetermined important vectors, we detected a significant parasite reservoir in the abdomens of secondary vectors.

Consequently, targeted efforts focused on the suppression of An. funestus and An. gambiae s.l., which primarily include indoor interventions, may not be sufficient to effectively reduce malaria transmission.
Plasmodium positivity in this assay is observed in the abdomen of the mosquito, and does not indicate the status of sporozoites in the mosquito’s salivary glands.
219.Rationale: The passage documents an increased human-feeding propensity for species such as An. coustani and An. rufipes and identifies Plasmodium in the abdomens of many 'secondary' vectors; it also warns that focusing only on traditional primary-vector interventions may be insufficient. However, the option overstates the evidence by declaring these species have 'become primary vectors' and therefore definitively rendering current control inadequate. The study detected abdominal Plasmodium (not necessarily infectious sporozoites) and describes a potential reservoir in secondary vectors, but conversion to 'primary vector' status requires additional evidence (e.g., sporozoite rates in head/thorax, vectorial capacity, transmission attribution). Thus the strong causal claim in the option is not supported by the passage.

219.Option: The presence of human-only bloodmeals in all quadrants of the traps in April 2018 indicates that mosquito feeding behavior is uniformly distributed across the study area, suggesting a consistent and widespread risk of malaria transmission regardless of location.
Evidence: The presence of human-only and pig-only bloodmeals was restricted to traps from Amparihy. Furthermore, human-only bloodmeals were observed across all traps and all quadrants in the April 2018 collection.

The distribution of host bloodmeals identified on the QUEST varied significantly (when constraining the analysis to hosts with sufficient sample size: human, cow, and pig positive bloodmeals) between village (AMB vs AMP in 2017: χ2 = 23.5, 2 DOF, p < 0.005, AMB vs AMP in 2018: χ2 = 25, 2 DOF, p < 0.005) and timepoint (Amparihy 2017–2018: χ2 = 33.3, 2 DOF, p < 0.005, Ambolodina 2017–2018: χ2 = 56.3, 2 DOF, p < 0.005).

The distribution of host choice across mosquitoes on the nets (Fig 6) may be influenced by the proximity of hosts to the net. It is also likely influenced by the composition of mosquito species on individual QUESTs, each of which displayed an individual hierarchy of host choice (Fig 5).
219.Rationale: While the passage explicitly notes that human-only bloodmeals were observed across all traps and quadrants in April 2018, it simultaneously documents statistically significant spatial variation in host choice between villages and emphasizes that distributions are influenced by local factors (host proximity, species composition). Therefore the observation of human-only meals in every quadrant does not imply uniform feeding behavior or homogeneous transmission risk across the study area; the broader data indicate heterogeneity. The option thus overgeneralizes a specific observation to claim uniform spatial risk, which the passage does not support.

219.Option: The spatial variation in host choice between villages, particularly the higher proportion of bovine blood in Ambolodina and porcine blood in Amparihy, suggests that malaria transmission risk is primarily driven by livestock density rather than human-mosquito contact.
Evidence: Three mammalian hosts were detected across our blood meal samples, with the predominant constituents including bovine (90.3%), followed by human (39.5%) and porcine (15.3%).
The presence of human-positive bloodmeals increased in April 2018 for both villages, from 4% to 48% in Amparihy and 32% to 41% in Ambolodina.
The distribution of host bloodmeals identified on the QUEST varied significantly ... between village ... and timepoint ...
The zoophilic preferences of the mosquitoes in this sample, however, may temper their impact on malaria transmission by dedicating potentially infective bites to non-human hosts.
The combined impact of An. coustani and the remaining secondary vectors may be responsible for sustained malaria transmission in these villages, which have low primary vector density.
219.Rationale: The passage documents strong livestock feeding (high bovine proportion) and spatial differences in host choice between villages, but it does not conclude that transmission risk is primarily driven by livestock density rather than human-mosquito contact. Indeed, the study emphasizes increases in human feeding (noted between timepoints) and notes that human-positive bloodmeals are associated with Plasmodium (Plasmodium prevalence was higher in human positive than human negative bloodmeals). The authors discuss that zoophily could reduce human transmission risk, but they do not assert livestock density is the primary driver. Therefore the option's categorical attribution to livestock density is not supported by the passage.

219.Option: The observed increase in human feeding by multiple mosquito species in April 2018 suggests a heightened risk of malaria transmission during the latter part of the rainy season, potentially driven by shifts in vector behavior rather than just vector abundance.
Evidence: The propensity for human feeding increased significantly from the December 2017 (27% human positive) to the April 2018 (44% human positive) collection (χ2 = 44.8, 1 DOF, p < 0.0001), with anthropophilic shifts observed for An. coustani (32% to 60%), An. funestus (9% to 50%), An. rufipes (23% to 44%), and An. arabiensis (38% to 53%).

Considering that our timepoints occur at the beginning and end of the 2017–2018 rainy season (considered to be the malaria transmission season in Madagascar [35]), ... we might naturally expect to see seasonal variation in species occurrence and abundance.
An increase in human feeding could lead to an increase in the presence of Plasmodium positive mosquitoes, though evidence of such from this study was variable between villages (Fig 7B).

The December 2017 collection contained 18 of the 19 overall observed P. vivax infected mosquitoes. The abundance of positive samples, and diversity of Plasmodium spp. within them, shifted in these two villages, with P. falciparum comprising nearly all of the Plasmodium positive bloodmeals in April 2018.
219.Rationale: The passage documents a clear, significant increase in human-feeding across species between the early and late rainy-season timepoints, links the sampling times to the rainy/transmission season, and states that increases in human feeding could lead to more Plasmodium-positive mosquitoes. These points support the interpretation that transmission risk may be heightened later in the rainy season and that behavioral shifts (increased anthropophagy) likely contribute in addition to changes in abundance. The option accurately reflects the study observations and reasonable epidemiologic inference conveyed by the authors.

219.Option: The high prevalence of multiple blood meals in April 2018 suggests that mosquitoes are more likely to transmit malaria between individuals, increasing the risk of secondary transmission events within the human population.
Evidence: The BLOODART analysis revealed multiple blood feeding behavior (46.4%) in our mosquito sample.

Individual mosquitoes frequently harbored blood from two or three different host species: single host = 53.6%, two hosts = 42.1%, three hosts = 4.3%. The incidence of multiple blood feeding was significantly different (χ2 = 42.3, 2 DOF, p < 0.0001) from December 2017 (single host = 41.8%, two hosts = 52.3%, three hosts = 5.8%) to April 2018 (single host = 60.9%, two hosts = 35.7%, three hosts = 3.2%).

High incidence of multiple blood feeding has important epidemiological implications. Numerous mosquitoes showed evidence of feeding on humans and one or two additional mammal species. This may increase the probability that a mosquito is also feeding on multiple individuals within a species, amplifying the vectorial capacity of the potential malaria vectors in these villages.
219.Rationale: Although the passage notes a high overall prevalence of multiple blood feeding and explains that multiple feeds can amplify vectorial capacity, the month-specific data show that multiple blood feeding was actually more common in December 2017 (two-host meals 52.3%) and declined in April 2018 (two-host 35.7%). Thus the option's assertion that April 2018 had a high prevalence of multiple blood meals is incorrect. Moreover, while multiple feeding can increase transmission potential, the passage carefully frames this as one of several factors and does not provide direct evidence that secondary human-to-human transmission events increased in April. Therefore the statement is not supported as written.

220.Option: The differences in microbial diversity may indicate recent acute infection with helminths, as evidenced by the rapid shifts in gut and oral microbiota composition
Evidence: Participants in the original study were recruited without the presentation of symptoms.
The chronic nature of many of the helminth morbidities present make the identification of symptomatic individuals difficult, the collection of further clinical parameters may be useful in the future.
220.Rationale: The passage specifies participants were asymptomatic and emphasizes the chronic nature of many helminth morbidities, arguing against interpreting the observed differences as indicators of recent acute infection or 'rapid' shifts. There is no text asserting recent/acute timing; rather the study context supports chronic, often asymptomatic infections.

220.Option: The observed microbial changes reflect a direct pathogenic effect of helminths on host microbiota, with helminths acting as primary drivers of dysbiosis in both gut and oral environments
Evidence: Many of these helminth infections have a systemic impact on human health via interactions with the host immune response [7].
It is hypothesised that when a helminth establishes infection it alters the gut microbiota by both excretory and secretory molecules [52].
However the data between and within studies can be highly variable and no clear consensus is evident [10].
220.Rationale: While the passage notes helminths can alter the microbiota (via excretory/secretory products and immune interactions), it also emphasizes high variability and lack of consensus across studies. The authors do not claim helminths are unequivocal primary pathogenic drivers of dysbiosis in both gut and oral sites; thus the strong, singular causal claim in this option is not supported by the text.

220.Option: The observed microbial changes likely reflect long-term immunomodulatory adaptation to chronic, asymptomatic helminth exposure rather than acute infection responses
Evidence: Participants in the original study were recruited without the presentation of symptoms.
The chronic nature of many of the helminth morbidities present make the identification of symptomatic individuals difficult, the collection of further clinical parameters may be useful in the future.
Many of these helminth infections have a systemic impact on human health via interactions with the host immune response [7].
It is hypothesised that when a helminth establishes infection it alters the gut microbiota by both excretory and secretory molecules [52].
220.Rationale: The passage describes the infections as often chronic and asymptomatic in this cohort, and explicitly discusses helminth interactions with the host immune system and their capacity to alter the microbiota. These points support an interpretation that observed microbiota differences more plausibly reflect longer-term immunomodulatory adaptation to chronic exposure rather than short-term acute infection responses.

220.Option: The microbial differences are likely due to confounding by recent antibiotic use or dietary changes, which could alter microbiota composition independently of helminth infection
Evidence: This approach allows mitigation of many of the variable factors associated with human studies including diet, geographical location, the duration of helminth infection, and the presence of co-morbidities.
Age, genetics, diet, and parasitic infections are just a few of these factors that can lead to both moderate and drastic alterations of the gut microbiota composition and abundance [50].
Other important factors that could influence an individual’s microbiome include socio-economic status, profession, diet and genetic markers.
220.Rationale: The passage acknowledges potential confounders such as diet and other host factors and recommends considering them in larger studies, so confounding is a possible concern. However, the specific claim about 'recent antibiotic use' is unsupported because antibiotics are not mentioned in the text, and the study context and authors' discussion focus more on chronic exposures and other environmental/demographic confounders rather than recent antibiotic effects. Therefore the option as stated is not supported by the passage.

220.Option: The increased alpha diversity in saliva and reduced Haemophilus in the gut suggest a generalized inflammatory response to helminth infection, consistent with systemic immune activation
Evidence: Chao 1 metric was divergent at both the genus (p-value = 0.042) and phylum (p-value = 0.026) level, where in both taxonomic ranks, increased alpha diversity was associated with an active helminth infection (SI- saliva infected) (Fig 5).
In this study, analysis of the faecal microbiota demonstrated a reduction in Haemophilus in helminth infected people.
Many of these helminth infections have a systemic impact on human health via interactions with the host immune response [7].

Much interest has developed from these studies in the potential of adapting helminth derived molecules (ES or excreted/secreted) as therapeutics to reduce both inflammatory and fibrotic diseases [53–55].
220.Rationale: While the data show increased saliva alpha diversity and reduced gut Haemophilus in infected individuals, the authors do not interpret these findings as evidence of a generalized inflammatory response. In fact, the passage notes helminth interactions with the immune system and even potential anti-inflammatory therapeutic interest in helminth-derived molecules, indicating helminths can modulate immunity in complex, not uniformly pro-inflammatory, ways. Thus the specific interpretation of a generalized inflammatory response is not supported by the text.

221.Option: The observed metabolic flux patterns suggest that the reverse activity of phosphofructokinase (PFK) is the primary mechanism enabling gluconeogenesis in the absence of FBPase.
Evidence: Fernandes and colleagues [17] reported reverse activity of trypanosomal and mammalian PFKs in vitro, but the physiological relevance is unclear.
Crucially, RNAi induced cells with decreased PFK expression still utilised glycerol via GNG and incorporated it into hexose phosphate sugars.
we saw clear evidence for diminished glycolysis, but not GNG.
Altogether, our data do not support participation of PFK in GNG.
221.Rationale: Although reverse PFK activity was reported in vitro, the passage presents direct experimental evidence from PFK RNAi that decreasing PFK expression caused reduced glycolytic flux but did not reduce gluconeogenic (GNG) flux from glycerol. The authors explicitly conclude their data do not support participation of PFK in GNG. Therefore, it is incorrect to interpret reverse PFK activity as the primary mechanism enabling GNG in the absence of FBPase.

221.Option: A non-canonical enzyme with fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase activity is responsible for gluconeogenic flux in the absence of FBPase and SBPase
Evidence: GNG was not abolished after deletion of the FBPase gene indicating an involvement of another, so far unknown, enzyme [6,7].
To our surprise, double knock-out cell lines for FBPase and SBPase (Δfbp.sbp) ... retained GNG flux, suggesting an unknown enzyme performing the FBPase reaction.
Deletion of the FBPase gene does not eliminate the enzymatic activity responsible for dephosphorylation of F1,6bP [6,7], indicating that other enzymes capable of this activity are also present.
Taken together, our results indicate that (an)other enzyme(s), currently unrecognized by in silico searchers, is/are responsible for the activity.
221.Rationale: The passage documents that deletion of canonical FBPase (and SBPase) did not abolish conversion of F1,6bP to F6P or overall GNG flux, and the authors repeatedly infer the existence of an unknown enzyme(s) with FBPase-like activity. Thus the most appropriate interpretation is that a non-canonical enzyme with fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase activity is responsible for the observed gluconeogenic flux.

221.Option: The metabolic flux patterns indicate that the non-oxidative pentose phosphate pathway is fully functional and compensates for the loss of canonical gluconeogenesis.
Evidence: The arrangement and function of the PPP is not fully resolved in T.brucei BSF because transketolase is not expressed in this stage, but transaldolase is, resulting in an incomplete non-oxidative branch of the PPP (Fig 1A) [20, 21].

our experiments with the Δtal and Δfbp.sbp.tal mutants show that carbons from glycerol are still incorporated into F1,6bP, F6P, and other metabolites, demonstrating continued flux though GNG, and precluding the existence of the alternative pathway.

Although O8P has previously been shown to be synthesized in vitro from F6P and R5P by TAL [29], there appears to be a separate route for synthesis in the parasites, as evidenced by the presence of this metabolite in TAL-depleted cell lines.
221.Rationale: The passage states the non-oxidative PPP is incomplete in bloodstream-form T. brucei (transketolase absent) and that deletion of transaldolase (TAL) did not abolish GNG; indeed, the authors conclude the proposed alternative PPP-based pathway does not explain the persistence of GNG. Therefore it is incorrect to claim the non-oxidative PPP is fully functional and compensating for loss of canonical GNG.

221.Option: The lack of growth defect in nutrient-rich medium indicates that the metabolic adaptations observed are not essential for viability under standard conditions.
Evidence: The Δfbp.sbp cell line showed no growth defect in the standard nutrient-rich HMI-11 medium (Fig 1E).
The Δfbp.sbp.tal cells displayed no growth defect in HMI-11 medium supplemented either with glucose or glycerol as the main carbon source (Fig 5C).

When Creek’s Minimal Medium (CMM), which better approximates the composition of blood serum, was used for cell culture, Δfbp.sbp cells suffered a mild growth defect with higher variability, possibly due to different metabolic adaptations required for growth in CMM (Fig 1E) [33].
221.Rationale: The passage reports that strains lacking FBPase and SBPase (and even the triple knock-out including TAL) grow without defect in nutrient-rich HMI-11 medium, while showing mild defects in nutrient-restricted CMM. This supports the interpretation that the metabolic adaptations (and compensatory pathways) are not essential for viability under standard nutrient-rich culture conditions, though they may matter under nutrient limitation.

221.Option: The persistence of gluconeogenesis indicates that glycerol is being used primarily for energy production through glycolysis rather than for biosynthetic pathways.
Evidence: The data demonstrated that glycerol was utilised as the main carbon source under the experimental conditions, since the majority of glycolytic intermediates were labelled from 13C3-glycerol.
In addition to use as a substrate for GNG, glycerol was used for synthesis of S7P, which may reflect a novel variation of the non-oxidative PPP.

13C3 labelling was observed in phosphoenolpyruvate (PEP), pyruvate, fumarate, malate, aspartate, and alanine (Figs 3C and S2). 72–78% of PEP is 13C3, demonstrating a high level of utilisation of labelled glycerol in glycolysis.
In addition to use as a substrate for GNG, glycerol was used for synthesis of S7P and to synthesize large sugar phosphates such as S7P.
221.Rationale: While the passage shows glycerol is used in glycolysis (energy production), it also provides clear evidence that glycerol carbons are incorporated into biosynthetic metabolites (e.g., S7P, PPP intermediates, hexose phosphates). The authors explicitly note glycerol is used both as a substrate for GNG and for synthesis of larger sugar phosphates. Thus it is incorrect to state glycerol is being used primarily for energy production rather than for biosynthesis.

222.Option: Helminth-induced immunomodulation, indicated by elevated MXD values, may attenuate harmful inflammatory responses during ART initiation, explaining the observed protective effect despite lack of expected immunological deterioration
Evidence: The statistically significant association of elevated MXD values with a reduced risk of death and LFU may support this assumption.

MXD value is composed of absolute cell counts of monocytes, eosinophilic, and basophilic granulocytes, which are independent from HIV infection and disease status [39–41]. In the current setting in a primarily rural area of south-central Tanzania, elevated MXD values are most likely driven by helminth-induced eosinophilia and may be construed as a surrogate marker of helminth-induced immunomodulation in the HIV-infected host.
Schistosoma-induced immunomodulation may have an attenuating effect on IRIS-related morbidity and mortality and by this means, improve the clinical outcome of PLWH starting ART.

Because immunomodulatory effects are a consistent feature of helminth infection, we believe that the weaker effect of schistosomal infection on survival/retention in care in our study may possibly be explained by competing immunomodulatory effects of other undetected helminths...
222.Rationale: The passage explicitly links elevated MXD values to helminth-induced eosinophilia and describes MXD as a surrogate marker of helminth-induced immunomodulation. It further hypothesizes that Schistosoma-induced immunomodulation could attenuate IRIS (an excessive inflammatory response during ART initiation) and thereby improve clinical outcomes. Those statements provide direct biological plausibility for a mechanism in which helminth-driven immunomodulation (reflected by MXD) reduces harmful inflammation during ART initiation, explaining better survival/retention despite no clear immunological deterioration on standard markers.

222.Option: The presence of Schistosoma infection may enhance antiretroviral drug metabolism, leading to improved virological control and reduced clinical risk
Evidence: Our study did not show an impact of schistosomal infection on virological treatment failure (OR: 1.63, 95% CI: 0.53–4.95, p = 0.39, Table 4).

Because helminth-induced immunomodulatory effects are associated with elevated HIV RNA levels in ART-naïve individuals, helminth infection could theoretically be associated with an elevated risk of virological failure [11].
222.Rationale: The passage provides no evidence that Schistosoma alters antiretroviral drug metabolism. Instead, it reports no association between schistosomal infection and virological treatment failure and even discusses the theoretical possibility that helminths could increase HIV RNA in ART-naïve individuals. There is no data or statement supporting enhanced drug metabolism or improved virological control due to Schistosoma, so this option is not supported by the study.

222.Option: Schistosoma coinfection may reduce HIV viral load through direct inhibition of viral replication via parasite-derived molecules
Evidence: Our study did not show an impact of schistosomal infection on virological treatment failure (OR: 1.63, 95% CI: 0.53–4.95, p = 0.39, Table 4).

Because helminth-induced immunomodulatory effects are associated with elevated HIV RNA levels in ART-naïve individuals, helminth infection could theoretically be associated with an elevated risk of virological failure [11].
222.Rationale: The authors do not present data or a mechanistic argument that Schistosoma produces molecules that directly inhibit HIV replication. On the contrary, they note no beneficial virological effect and mention that helminth effects have been associated with higher HIV RNA in ART-naïve persons. Therefore the option proposing direct parasite-mediated inhibition of viral replication is not supported by the passage.

222.Option: The observed protective effect may be due to improved adherence to ART among Schistosoma-infected individuals, resulting in better clinical outcomes
Evidence: confounding factors cannot be excluded. In particular, schistosomiasis and elevated MXD values could be associated with other characteristics of the study population, which reduce mortality or LFU, remained unrecognized for which we could not control for (e.g., distance to the clinic or migratory mobility).

This counterintuitive association is a well-known phenomenon in European and sub-Saharan cohort studies and is generally explained by a closer clinical monitoring and better adherence of patients with lower CD4 cell count who are at an increased risk for opportunistic infections [31–33].
222.Rationale: While differences in adherence or other unmeasured confounders are discussed as possible non-biological explanations, the question asks for the factor that most strongly suggests a biological mechanism. The passage emphasizes helminth-induced immunomodulation and MXD as biological explanations. Improved adherence is mentioned only as a general explanation for other associations and as an unmeasured confounder possibility, not as the primary biological mechanism supported by data. Thus this option is not the best-supported biological explanation.

222.Option: Elevated MXD values may reflect a general state of immune activation that enhances antiviral defense and contributes to the observed clinical benefit
Evidence: MXD value is composed of absolute cell counts of monocytes, eosinophilic, and basophilic granulocytes, which are independent from HIV infection and disease status [39–41]. In the current setting in a primarily rural area of south-central Tanzania, elevated MXD values are most likely driven by helminth-induced eosinophilia and may be construed as a surrogate marker of helminth-induced immunomodulation in the HIV-infected host.

As both infections induce chronic modulations of the host’s immune system, interactions on this level are of particular interest [7]. Schistosomiasis and other helminth infections lead to an upregulation of T-helper cell type 2 (Th-2) immune response and a downregulation of T-helper cell type 1 (Th-1) immune response and of cytolytic activity of CD8 T-cells [8,9].
The statistically significant association of elevated MXD values with a reduced risk of death and LFU may support this assumption.
222.Rationale: Although elevated MXD is linked to reduced death/LFU, the passage interprets elevated MXD not as a marker of enhanced antiviral activation but as evidence of helminth-induced immunomodulation (Th2 bias and downregulation of Th1/cytolytic activity). The authors propose that this immunomodulation may attenuate harmful inflammatory responses (e.g., IRIS), not that it enhances antiviral defense. In fact, Th1 responses are described as essential for control of viral infections, so a Th2-biased state would not be expected to enhance antiviral defense. Therefore this option mischaracterizes the biological implication of elevated MXD in the study.

223.Option: The fact that pigs spend most of their time within 50 meters of their home supports the hypothesis that transmission occurs through direct contact with tapeworm carriers rather than environmental contamination
Evidence: A GPS tracking study of pigs in rural Peru found that pigs spent an average of 70% of their time within 50 meters of their residence and interacted with human defecation areas nearly 30 minutes per day inside these 50 meters rings (compared to just 7 minutes per day outside of 50 meters) [26].
Based on these findings, we propose that 50 meter rings accurately represent T. solium transmission foci in endemic areas of rural Peru.

The prevalence of T. solium cysticercosis (at least one viable cyst on necropsy) was greatest among pigs living within 50 meters of a tapeworm carrier, and decreased proportionally at greater distances.
223.Rationale: The quoted evidence does show pigs spend most of their time within 50 meters of the home and that infection is concentrated within 50 m. However, the passage links pig behavior to interaction with human defecation areas (environmental contamination), not to direct contact with tapeworm carriers. Thus the option's claim that this supports direct contact rather than environmental contamination is not supported by the passage. The passage emphasizes environmental exposure near households (defecation areas) as the plausible mechanism.

223.Option: The strong association between infection and proximity to tapeworm carriers, even after adjusting for pig age, indicates that environmental contamination is the only possible transmission route
Evidence: After adjusting for pig age, pigs living less than 50 meters from a human tapeworm carrier were 4.56 times (95% CI: 1.33, 15.6) more likely to be infected with at least one cyst than pigs living more than 500 meters from a tapeworm carrier.
Although distance to human tapeworm carriers was an important predictor for T. solium infection among pigs, many infected pigs in our study did not reside in close proximity to a tapeworm carrier.

It is also possible that pigs appearing distant from tapeworm carriers were infected through egg dispersion mechanisms, such as dung beetles and flies, which have been identified as possible mechanical vectors capable of dispersing T. solium eggs over long distances [27–31].
223.Rationale: While the adjusted association supports localized environmental contamination as an important transmission route, the passage explicitly acknowledges alternative explanations (mechanical vectors, undetected carriers, infections acquired earlier) and does not claim environmental contamination is the only possible route. Therefore the absolute claim 'only possible transmission route' is unsupported.

223.Option: A sharp decline in infection prevalence beyond 50 meters, coupled with pigs' high activity in this zone, indicates that transmission occurs primarily through localized environmental contamination near human dwellings
Evidence: The prevalence of at least one viable cyst was 15.6% (12 out of 77) at < 50 meters from a tapeworm carrier, 8.3% (27 out of 325) between 50 and 500 meters, and 4.4% (5 out of 113) at > 500 meters (p < 0.01 for trend) (Fig 2).

A GPS tracking study of pigs in rural Peru found that pigs spent an average of 70% of their time within 50 meters of their residence and interacted with human defecation areas nearly 30 minutes per day inside these 50 meters rings (compared to just 7 minutes per day outside of 50 meters) [26].
Based on these findings, we propose that 50 meter rings accurately represent T. solium transmission foci in endemic areas of rural Peru.
223.Rationale: The data show a clear decline in infection prevalence with increasing distance, with the highest prevalence within 50 m and statistically significant trend. Combined with evidence that pigs spend most of their time and interact more with human defecation areas within this 50 m zone, this pattern most strongly supports localized environmental contamination near human dwellings as the primary transmission driver.

223.Option: The absence of infection in pigs located more than 500 meters from a tapeworm carrier suggests that T. solium eggs cannot survive or remain infectious beyond this distance in the environment
Evidence: The prevalence of at least one viable cyst was 15.6% (12 out of 77) at < 50 meters from a tapeworm carrier, 8.3% (27 out of 325) between 50 and 500 meters, and 4.4% (5 out of 113) at > 500 meters (p < 0.01 for trend) (Fig 2).

Although distance to human tapeworm carriers was an important predictor for T. solium infection among pigs, many infected pigs in our study did not reside in close proximity to a tapeworm carrier. In fact, only 27% (12 out of 44) of the infected pigs in this study lived within 50 meters of a tapeworm carrier, and some infected pigs lived more than 1 km from it’s the nearest identified tapeworm carrier.

It is also possible that pigs appearing distant from tapeworm carriers were infected through egg dispersion mechanisms, such as dung beetles and flies, which have been identified as possible mechanical vectors capable of dispersing T. solium eggs over long distances [27–31].
223.Rationale: This option is incorrect because infections were observed beyond 500 m (4.4% prevalence), so it is false to claim an absence of infection there. Moreover, the authors acknowledge possible long-distance dispersal mechanisms (dung beetles, flies) and undetected past carriers, so the data do not support the strong conclusion that eggs cannot survive or remain infectious beyond 500 m.

223.Option: The lack of a significant association between infection and distance beyond 50 meters suggests that long-distance dispersal mechanisms are not involved in transmission
Evidence: Pigs living more than 50 meters from a tapeworm carrier, including pigs living between 50 and 100 meters from a tapeworm carrier, did not have an increased odds of infection at any cyst burden analyzed.

Although distance to human tapeworm carriers was an important predictor for T. solium infection among pigs, many infected pigs in our study did not reside in close proximity to a tapeworm carrier. In fact, only 27% (12 out of 44) of the infected pigs in this study lived within 50 meters of a tapeworm carrier, and some infected pigs lived more than 1 km from it’s the nearest identified tapeworm carrier.

It is also possible that pigs appearing distant from tapeworm carriers were infected through egg dispersion mechanisms, such as dung beetles and flies, which have been identified as possible mechanical vectors capable of dispersing T. solium eggs over long distances [27–31].
223.Rationale: While the study found no significant increased odds of infection for pigs beyond 50 m in the adjusted models, the presence of infected pigs far from identified carriers and the explicit mention of possible long-distance dispersal mechanisms mean the lack of a statistically significant association beyond 50 m does not rule out long-distance dispersal. The authors discuss alternative explanations (mechanical vectors, undetected carriers, historical infections), so this option overinterprets the absence of significance.

224.Option: W. bancrofti infection is uniquely associated with increased effector memory CD4 T cells, a subset highly susceptible to HIV, in multivariable analysis, unlike other helminth species
Evidence: W. bancrofti infection was associated with significantly increased frequencies of effector memory (CD27negCD45ROpos) CD4 T cells, in univariable (mean 24.1% versus 20.0%, p = 0.0096) as well as multivariable analysis (Tables 3 and S5).
This was not observed for other analyzed helminth species.

we have previously shown in the same cohort that both CD27 negative effector memory CD4 T cells and HLA-DR+ memory CD4 T cells express significantly higher levels of the HIV co-receptor CCR5 compared to CD27+ central memory or naive CD4 T cells [21], which may facilitate better HIV entry into these specific cell subsets.
An increase of systemically activated CD4 T cells is likely to support early HIV dissemination because they are cellular targets for HIV replication [45, 46].
224.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that W. bancrofti infection (but not other helminths) is associated with significantly increased effector memory CD4 T cells in both univariable and multivariable analyses. The authors also note that effector memory and HLA-DR+ memory CD4 subsets express higher CCR5 and that activated CD4 T cells facilitate HIV replication. Together these statements support the conclusion that W. bancrofti uniquely associates with an increase in a CD4 T cell subset particularly permissive to HIV, beyond a general helminth co-infection effect.

224.Option: W. bancrofti infection is associated with increased eosinophil counts, which are known to correlate with systemic immune activation and may contribute to HIV susceptibility
Evidence: We found a median number of 130x103/μl eosinophils in helminth free individuals compared to 400x103/μl in W. bancrofti-infected individuals and 260x103/μl in participants harboring other helminths.
As expected, there was a difference in eosinophil count between helminth free individuals and participants with one of the diagnosed helminth species.
An increase of systemically activated CD4 T cells is likely to support early HIV dissemination because they are cellular targets for HIV replication [45, 46].
224.Rationale: While the passage documents substantially higher eosinophil counts in W. bancrofti-infected individuals, it does not state that eosinophil counts are known to correlate with systemic T cell activation or that eosinophils contribute to HIV susceptibility. The authors focus on T cell activation and effector memory CD4 T cells as mechanistic links to HIV risk. Therefore the portion of the option asserting that eosinophils are known correlates of systemic immune activation driving HIV susceptibility is not supported by the passage.

224.Option: W. bancrofti infection is associated with increased expression of CCR5 on CD4 T cells, which directly facilitates HIV entry and explains the increased susceptibility
Evidence: Addressing the expression of HIV entry receptor CCR5, no difference was found regarding the percentage of CCR5 positive CD4 T cells, CCR5 positive regulatory CD4 T cells, or the mean fluorescence intensity of CCR5 on memory CD4 T cells between the different helminth infections (Tables 3, S6, S7 and S8).

we have previously shown in the same cohort that both CD27 negative effector memory CD4 T cells and HLA-DR+ memory CD4 T cells express significantly higher levels of the HIV co-receptor CCR5 compared to CD27+ central memory or naive CD4 T cells [21], which may facilitate better HIV entry into these specific cell subsets.
224.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports no difference in CCR5 expression on CD4 T cells between helminth infections, including W. bancrofti. Although effector memory/activated CD4 cells are known to express higher CCR5 (from prior work), the present study did not find a direct increase in CCR5 expression attributable to W. bancrofti compared with other helminths. Thus the claim that increased CCR5 expression on CD4 T cells explains the increased susceptibility in W. bancrofti-infected subjects is not supported by the data presented.

224.Option: W. bancrofti infection leads to a higher overall burden of helminth co-infection, which may drive increased immune activation and thus HIV susceptibility
Evidence: Almost all W. bancrofti-infected subjects (32 of 33) were also infected with other helminths.
A mean of 2.3 helminths species were detected in W. bancrofti-infected individuals.
The composition of the “additional” species in W. bancrofti-infected individuals was almost identical to that in the group harboring other helminth species (Table 1).

we clearly showed that the described CD4 T cell activation in W. bancrofti- (but also in A. lumbricoides and T. trichiura) -infected individuals was independent of concomitant infection with other helminths.
224.Rationale: Although W. bancrofti-infected individuals were frequently co-infected, the passage states the composition and number of additional helminths were similar to the 'other helminths' group. Moreover, the multivariable analyses adjusted for other helminth infections and concluded the W. bancrofti-associated CD4 T cell activation was independent of concomitant helminths. Therefore the proposition that a higher overall helminth burden unique to W. bancrofti drives the increased activation and HIV susceptibility is not supported.

224.Option: W. bancrofti infection is associated with increased HLA-DRpos CD8 T cells, which are also targets for HIV replication, suggesting a similar mechanism to CD4 T cell activation
Evidence: Frequencies of HLA-DRpos CD8 T cell were increased in subjects with W. bancrofti (mean 29.5%) infection compared to subjects without filarial infection (mean 20.8%). However, this difference was not significant after adjusting for study site and other confounding factors in the multivariable analysis (Tables 3 and S2).

In addition we measured increased percentage of HLA-DRpos/CD38pos CD8 T cells in W. bancrofti-infected individuals, however, there is no evidence that CD8 T cell may contribute to increased HIV susceptibility.
Activated CD4 T cells are a major cellular reservoir for continuous HIV replication in vivo, as shown in several studies [17–20].
224.Rationale: Although unadjusted comparisons showed higher HLA-DR+ CD8 frequencies in W. bancrofti-infected individuals, the difference was not significant in multivariable analysis. The authors explicitly state there is no evidence that CD8 T cells contribute to increased HIV susceptibility, and HIV primarily targets CD4 T cells. Thus attributing the increased HIV susceptibility to HLA-DR+ CD8 T cells is not supported by the study.

225.Option: A rapid decline in platelet count observed between day 1 and day 3 of illness, as it reflects early immune-mediated thrombocytopenia and predicts progression to DHF
Evidence: All spectra of dengue also had lower median peripheral platelet count during the later days of illness, although the median value in DF and UF did not meet the WHO's criterion for thrombocytopenia, i.e. lower than 100,000/mm3.

Moreover, the level of platelet count showed reverse correlation with the disease severity, i.e. highest in UF and lowest in DHF. The median platelet count in DHF was significantly lower than the other 2 groups during day 3–6 of illness, while DF had significantly lower median platelet count compared to UF only during day 3–5 of illness.
This study shows that a decrease in peripheral platelet count occurs not only in DHF, but also in DF and UF, with a respectively less degree.
225.Rationale: The passage indicates platelet declines occur during the later days of illness (not specifically day 1–3) and that platelet decreases happen across all disease spectra (UF, DF, DHF). The median platelet count differences predictive of DHF are evident around days 3–6. Because declines are neither specific to DHF early (day 1–3) nor unique to severe disease, a rapid decline between day 1 and day 3 is not supported by the data as the strongest early indicator of progression to severe dengue.

225.Option: Persistent or increasing gastrointestinal symptoms (anorexia, nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain) beyond day 2 of illness
Evidence: It was noted that the prevalence of most of the symptoms was highest during the first 2 days of illness and then slowly declined. The exceptions are for anorexia, nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain and diarrhea that tended to increase in prevalence in DHF during day 3–5 of illness.

It was also revealed that most symptoms were more common in DHF and, specifically, DHF cases had significantly higher prevalence of anorexia, nausea/vomiting and abdominal pain during day 3–6 and diarrhea during day 4–6 of illness (Chi-square test; p<0.05).

This study, to the best of our knowledge, is the first study suggesting that diarrhea may be a predictor for DHF. Its highest prevalence just before the stage of shock suggests that it may be related to plasma leakage that may cause malabsorption.
The finding in this study that DHF cases had higher prevalence of nausea/vomiting and abdominal pain supports this guideline.
225.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that anorexia, nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain (and diarrhea) tend to increase in prevalence in DHF during day 3–5 and that DHF cases had significantly higher prevalence of these gastrointestinal symptoms in early-mid illness (day 3–6). The authors even suggest diarrhea may be a predictor for DHF and note these symptoms align with WHO warning signs. Therefore persistent or increasing GI symptoms beyond day 2 are supported by the study as an early feature warranting heightened vigilance for progression to severe dengue.

225.Option: A positive tourniquet test observed on day 1 of illness, as it indicates early plasma leakage and is a reliable predictor of severe dengue
Evidence: Positive tourniquet test was found in 72% and flushed face was found in approximately half of the dengue infected patients.

Table 6 shows the occurrence of selected clinical manifestations and their predictive value for DHF among symptomatic dengue infected cases. It was found that all of these clinical manifestations had low positive predictive value but high negative predictive value for DHF.

It is worth noting that some clinical manifestations (e.g. rash, positive tourniquet test) were considered as more specific to DF/DHF and are included in clinical diagnostic criteria for DF. This can explain why these clinical manifestations were more common in DF/DHF.
225.Rationale: Although the tourniquet test was common and is included among DF/DHF clinical features, the passage reports that these clinical manifestations have low positive predictive value for DHF. Thus a positive tourniquet test on day 1 is not described as a reliable early predictor of severe dengue in this study; it is more useful for ruling out severe disease (high negative predictive value) than for positively predicting progression.

225.Option: The occurrence of drowsiness or lethargy during day 2–5 of illness, as it is a common symptom in both mild and severe dengue and may indicate early neurological involvement
Evidence: Conversely, drowsiness/lethargy was significantly more common in UF during day 2–5 of illness.
We do not have an explanation for this finding. Further studies are needed to clarify this issue.

we found that the presence of lethargy or drowsiness in the first few days of illness may not necessarily indicate severe dengue infection. The significance of drowsiness/lethargy needs to be further defined.
225.Rationale: The passage indicates drowsiness/lethargy was actually more common in undifferentiated fever (UF) cases during days 2–5 and explicitly states that early lethargy does not necessarily indicate severe dengue. Therefore this feature does not, per the study's findings, strongly signal imminent progression to DHF.

225.Option: The presence of rhinorrhea during the first 2 days of illness, as it may indicate a co-infection with a respiratory virus that could worsen dengue outcomes
Evidence: It is interesting that rhinorrhea was commonly found in this study. Whether this symptom is one manifestation of dengue infection or is caused by co-incidental respiratory infection is still unclear.
Mild dengue infection is quite similar to many other infections in that the prevalence of most of the symptoms was highest during the first few days of illness and then slowly declined.
225.Rationale: The authors note rhinorrhea was common but explicitly state it is unclear whether it is due to dengue or a coincident respiratory infection; they do not present evidence that rhinorrhea predicts worse outcomes or progression to severe dengue. Thus the study does not support rhinorrhea as a strong early indicator necessitating heightened vigilance for progression to severe disease.

226.Option: Pigs serve as the primary reservoir host for T. penetrans in this setting, given their high infection prevalence and intensity, strong correlation with human cases, and significant bidirectional association in multivariate analysis
Evidence: The study revealed that the prevalence of animal tungiasis was high and that the disease prevalence and parasite loads in humans and animals correlated. Pigs were identified as the most important domestic animal hosts for T. penetrans.
Pigs had the highest parasite load followed by goats and dogs: median = 8 lesions (inter-quartile range (IQR) = 3–30; range of 1–246 lesions per pig);
The prevalence of human tungiasis at household and village levels correlated strongly with the number of lesions in pigs at the respective levels (rho = 0.5, p<0.0001; rho = 0.8, p = 0.002).
Animal tungiasis increased the odds of the occurrence of human tungiasis in households by six times (OR = 6.1, 95% CI 3.3–11.4%; p<0.001) and vice versa.
In multivariate analysis, presence of animal tungiasis in households was strongly influenced by presence of human tungiasis (OR = 6.5, p < 0.0001) and presence of pigs (OR = 5.9, p = 0.0002).
226.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies pigs as the most important domestic hosts and provides multiple lines of evidence: pigs had the highest parasite loads, pig lesion counts correlated strongly with human tungiasis at household and village levels, and multivariate models show a strong association between presence of pigs and animal tungiasis. Together these observations (high prevalence/intensity in pigs, strong correlation with human cases, and significant associations in adjusted analyses) support the inference that pigs serve as the primary reservoir in this setting.

226.Option: Pigs are the primary reservoir host because they are the only species with a significant association between infection and the presence of other ectoparasites, suggesting shared environmental risk factors
Evidence: The reasons for the association between T. penetrans and other pig ectoparasites such as lice in this study are not known as the ecological interactions between these parasites have not been studied yet.
Of the infected 121 pigs, only 18 (15%) had ever received ectoparasite treatment and none of these had a well-defined interval of treatment.
Pig tungiasis occurred in strong association with dog tungiasis (OR = 7.4, 95% CI 1.5–36.9; p = 0.02) and human tungiasis (OR = 7.0, 95% CI 3.4–14.7; p<0.0001)
226.Rationale: While the paper notes an association between T. penetrans and other pig ectoparasites, it explicitly states the reasons for this association are unknown. It does not claim pigs are uniquely the only species with such an association, nor that this association is the basis for identifying pigs as the primary reservoir. The evidence for pigs as primary reservoirs is based on prevalence, intensity and correlation with human cases, not solely on co-occurrence with other ectoparasites. Therefore the option's specific causal reasoning is unsupported.

226.Option: Pigs are the most important reservoir host for T. penetrans because they have the highest number of lesions per infected animal and are the only species with viable lesions detected in the study
Evidence: Pigs had the highest parasite load followed by goats and dogs: median = 8 lesions (inter-quartile range (IQR) = 3–30; range of 1–246 lesions per pig);
Of the 3357 lesions in pigs, 2243 (66.8%) were viable (Fortaleza stage IIa-IIIb) and 1114 (33.3%) were non-viable (Fortaleza stage IV).
The 20 infected dogs had a total of 53 lesions of which 32 (60.4%) were viable while 21 (39.6%) were non-viable.
Of the total 40 lesions found on the two goat kids, 27 (67.5%) were viable while the other 13 (32.5%) were non-viable.
226.Rationale: Although pigs did have the highest lesion counts (supporting their epidemiologic importance), the assertion that pigs were the only species with viable lesions is false: dogs and goats also had viable lesions. Thus the reasoning in this option is factually incorrect even though the conclusion (pigs are important reservoirs) is consistent with other evidence; the stated justification is not supported by the passage.

226.Option: Pigs are the primary reservoir host because they are the only species with a significant association between infection prevalence and household size, suggesting greater exposure in larger households
Evidence: The strong association of animal infections with large household sizes is probably also indirect and may be due to the fact that such households have many children below 15 years who constitute the most vulnerable group of humans to T. penetrans.
number of animal species reared in the household (OR = 1.6, p = 0.02) and the size of the homestead (OR = 1.4, p = 0.02) significantly increased the odds detecting animal tungiasis among households.

For pig tungiasis in households with pigs, the variables; “presence of tungiasis in humans”, “number of animal species”, “number of pigs”, “distance of pigs from human housing”, “presence of other ectoparasites in pigs”, “number of dogs”, “presence of tungiasis in dogs”, “number of cats”, “number of goats”, “number of cattle”, “number of chicken”, “presence of other poultry” and “sanitation of the pig dwellings (clean vs. dirty)” were initially considered.
226.Rationale: The passage reports an association between larger household/homestead size and animal tungiasis generally, but it does not state that this association is unique to pigs nor that pigs are the only species linked to household size. The pig-specific multivariate model emphasizes other variables (human tungiasis, herd size, dog tungiasis, pig dwelling sanitation), not household size as a sole or unique predictor.
