249.Option: The observed pattern of higher infection intensity in adults suggests that the current mass drug administration schedule is too frequent, leading to increased parasite resistance and selection for more virulent strains in untreated populations.
Evidence: Annual school-based deworming with albendazole has been implemented successfully since 2012 (with a prior round in 2009) with programmatic coverage of 82% of pre-school and school-age children (2–14 years) in 2014 [19, 20].

For the ten years prior to this survey, Child Health Days (Malezi Bora) have been implemented on a biannual basis, targeting children under five years with benzimidazoles—either albendazole or mebendazole—and vitamin A [22].
The data reported here show that even after repeated rounds of school-based deworming, hookworm remains prevalent throughout Kwale County, with highest prevalence and intensity observed in adult men.
School-based deworming alone is insufficient to reduce hookworm transmission within communities [6, 36].
249.Rationale: The passage documents repeated and frequent anthelmintic interventions (annual school-based deworming since 2012 and biannual Child Health Days) and yet reports persistent high prevalence and intensity in adults. The authors interpret this pattern as failure of school-based strategies to control community-wide hookworm (i.e., insufficient targeting of adults or coverage gaps), not as evidence that too-frequent MDA produced selection for resistance or more virulent strains. The passage does not provide any evidence of drug resistance or of selection for virulent strains driven by MDA frequency; instead it emphasizes inadequate impact on adults and the need to target adults. Therefore the statement about frequency-driven resistance is not supported by the passage.

249.Option: The higher infection intensity in adults compared to children reflects a delayed immune response, suggesting that adults have not yet developed protective immunity, which may explain why they remain susceptible despite repeated treatment.
Evidence: Hookworm prevalence increased with age across both sexes, and was higher in males (Fig 3a).
The data reported here show that even after repeated rounds of school-based deworming, hookworm remains prevalent throughout Kwale County, with highest prevalence and intensity observed in adult men.
This suggests that targeting adults for deworming may have important consequences for both morbidity and transmission control in areas where STH transmission is dominated by hookworm.
249.Rationale: The passage describes age-related increases in prevalence and intensity and interprets these findings in terms of exposure patterns and programme reach (adults not reached by school-based deworming), not as evidence of a delayed or absent immune response in adults. The authors recommend targeting adults for deworming because adults harbour most moderate-to-heavy infections and can sustain transmission. There is no discussion or evidence in the text that adults lack protective immunity or that immune maturation explains the observed pattern; instead the text implicates programmatic coverage and exposure as drivers. Thus the immunity-based explanation is not supported by the passage.

249.Option: The higher intensity of infection in adults compared to children indicates that adults have a greater intrinsic susceptibility to hookworm, which may necessitate higher-dose treatment regimens for this group.
Evidence: The data reported here show that even after repeated rounds of school-based deworming, hookworm remains prevalent throughout Kwale County, with highest prevalence and intensity observed in adult men.
This suggests that targeting adults for deworming may have important consequences for both morbidity and transmission control in areas where STH transmission is dominated by hookworm.
The poorest people in communities may also have low adherence to repeated rounds of deworming and low coverage at each round.
249.Rationale: The passage attributes higher adult intensity to residual transmission and insufficient impact of school-based strategies on adults (coverage/exposure), not to intrinsic increased biological susceptibility of adults requiring higher drug doses. The recommended programmatic implication given is to target adults (community-wide treatment) and improve coverage/compliance, rather than to change dosing. There is no evidence in the text that adults are intrinsically more susceptible or that current dosages are inadequate for adults, so the option is not supported.

249.Option: The persistence of high-intensity infections in adults despite low overall prevalence suggests that school-based deworming has failed to interrupt transmission, and adults may serve as a reservoir for reinfection in treated populations.
Evidence: The data reported here show that even after repeated rounds of school-based deworming, hookworm remains prevalent throughout Kwale County, with highest prevalence and intensity observed in adult men.

These observations suggest that despite notable declines in STH infection in school-going children following three years of school-based deworming, the programme appears to have had less of an impact on prevalence of hookworm in adults and non-school-going children.
This suggests that targeting adults for deworming may have important consequences for both morbidity and transmission control in areas where STH transmission is dominated by hookworm.
school-based deworming alone is insufficient to reduce hookworm transmission within communities [6, 36].
249.Rationale: The passage explicitly presents the pattern described in this option: after repeated school-based deworming, children show declines but adults retain higher prevalence and intensity, implying adults act as a reservoir that sustains transmission and can reinfect treated children. The authors conclude that school-based programmes alone have been insufficient and that targeting adults (community-wide treatment) may be necessary to reduce transmission. Therefore this option accurately reflects the interpretation supported by the passage.

249.Option: The concentration of moderate-to-heavy intensity infections in adults indicates that the current deworming strategy is effective at reducing transmission, as it has shifted the burden to older age groups who are less likely to transmit the infection.
Evidence: The data reported here show that even after repeated rounds of school-based deworming, hookworm remains prevalent throughout Kwale County, with highest prevalence and intensity observed in adult men.
This suggests that targeting adults for deworming may have important consequences for both morbidity and transmission control in areas where STH transmission is dominated by hookworm.
school-based deworming alone is insufficient to reduce hookworm transmission within communities [6, 36].
249.Rationale: The passage does not interpret adult concentration of moderate-to-heavy infections as a desirable shift indicating effective transmission reduction. On the contrary, the authors interpret it as evidence that school-based deworming has not controlled community transmission because adults remain infected and therefore can maintain transmission. There is no claim that adults are less likely to transmit; indeed adults are implicated as important contributors to community infection burden. Thus the option's conclusion that the strategy is effective and that burden shifting is beneficial is not supported by the text.

251.Option: The persistence of R. conorii conorii in wild populations is limited by the high rate of horizontal transmission to non-host animals, leading to pathogen dilution
Evidence: the prevalence in the wild of ticks infected by R. conorii is low (usually less than 1%).
Interestingly, it has been suggested that R. conorii conorii has a negative effect on the survival of its tick vector when Rh. sanguineus ticks are experimentally infected [8]–[12].
infected ticks (nymphs and adults) had a comparatively higher rate of mortality at 37°C than at 25°C, and after a 4°C cooling when compared to non-infected ticks.
251.Rationale: The passage does not present evidence that high horizontal transmission to non-host animals (and consequent dilution) limits persistence. Instead, it emphasizes direct biological harms to infected ticks (reduced survival/mortality) and low prevalence in the wild. The cited sentences point to negative effects on tick survival and higher mortality of infected ticks under temperature stress as the proposed explanations for low wild prevalence, not dilution through horizontal transmission to non-hosts.

251.Option: The combined biological and environmental disadvantages of infected ticks prevent sustained population-level persistence despite high transovarial transmission efficiency
Evidence: These data suggested that transstadial transmission (transfer of bacteria from stage to stage) but also transovarial transmission (TOT, the transfer of bacteria from adult female ticks to the subsequent generation of ticks via the eggs) of R. conorii conorii occurs in ticks

Twelve successive generations were obtained, and the infected colony is still growing in our laboratory as of this writing (Figure 2). The PCR assay was positive for specimens of all stages of these generations for rickettsial detection. ... These data suggest 100% transstadial and transovarial transmission of R. conorii conorii in Rh. sanguineus ticks.

No difference was observed between the duration of life cycle of R. conorii conorii-infected and non-infected ticks held at 25°C, but the average weight of engorged females and eggs was found to be significantly lower in infected ticks.
This implies that the prevalence of infection in a tick population should gradually decline and disappear without periodical augmentation.
infected ticks (nymphs and adults) had a comparatively higher rate of mortality at 37°C than at 25°C, and after a 4°C cooling when compared to non-infected ticks.
In conclusion, infected ticks might not survive the winter. This could help to explain the scarcity of infected ticks found in the wild.
251.Rationale: The study documents very efficient vertical transmission (TOT ~100% and high FIR) but also demonstrates biological disadvantages of infected ticks (reduced female and egg weight implying lower fecundity) and increased mortality of infected nymphs and adults when exposed to temperature extremes (4°C and 37°C). The authors explicitly state these disadvantages would cause prevalence to decline without periodic augmentation and suggest infected ticks may not survive the winter. Together these biological and environmental disadvantages best explain why an obligate intracellular bacterium with efficient vertical transmission does not persist at high prevalence in wild tick populations.

251.Option: The limited geographic spread of MSF foci is due to the inability of infected ticks to survive in urban environments with high human population density
Evidence: Rh. sanguineus has become one of the most globally widespread ticks because of its specialized feeding and association with domestic dogs [3].
This tick is highly adapted to warm climates but also thrives in dog kennels and human homes.
It can be imported with dogs to the cooler regions and survive in peridomestic environments, provided that it encounters acceptable conditions.
251.Rationale: The passage indicates Rh. sanguineus thrives in dog kennels and human homes and has spread widely; it does not suggest inability to survive in urban/high-human-density environments. The study attributes limited foci to low prevalence of infected ticks and factors like decreased fecundity and temperature-related mortality, not to an inability to survive in urban settings. The quoted sentences show the tick actually does well in peridomestic/human-associated environments.

251.Option: The high transovarial transmission rate of R. conorii conorii is counterbalanced by a high rate of spontaneous bacterial clearance in tick eggs
Evidence: Twelve successive generations were obtained, and the infected colony is still growing in our laboratory as of this writing (Figure 2). The PCR assay was positive for specimens of all stages of these generations for rickettsial detection.
These data suggest 100% transstadial and transovarial transmission of R. conorii conorii in Rh. sanguineus ticks.

The filial transmission rate (FIR, proportion of infected eggs or larvae obtained from an infected female) of R. conorii conorii was 99.07% (107/108), 94.3% (66/70) and 95.5% (21/22) in larvae from several infected females of the 3rd, 4th, and 11th generations, respectively.
This study confirms the vertical transmission of R. conorii conorii in naturally infected Rh. sanguineus ticks over twelve generations with a TOT rate of 100% and an FIR of up to 99%.
251.Rationale: The data show very high and sustained vertical transmission across many generations, with PCR positivity in eggs and larvae and FIR values near 95–99%. There is no evidence in the passage of a high rate of spontaneous bacterial clearance in eggs; on the contrary, the results indicate persistence of infection through eggs and subsequent stages. Thus this option contradicts the study findings.

251.Option: The low prevalence of infected ticks in the wild is primarily due to insufficient host availability for tick feeding during the winter months
Evidence: Rh. sanguineus overwinter as engorged nymphs or unfed adults [35], so our preliminary results suggest that infected ticks might not survive the winter. This could help to explain the scarcity of infected ticks found in the wild.

infected eggs, larval and nymphal unfed stages are unable to survive at a cold temperature in laboratory conditions, and the temperature exerts considerable influence on the length of their life cycle [27], [35].

In conclusion, under laboratory conditions we did not find any difference in the duration of developmental stages of the life cycle of R. conorii conorii-infected Rh. sanguineus when compared to non-infected ticks [27].
251.Rationale: While winter is discussed, the passage attributes low prevalence to infected ticks' decreased survival during cold (and heat) exposures and lower fecundity rather than to lack of hosts for feeding in winter. The authors suggest infected ticks may not survive the winter (temperature-driven mortality), not that host scarcity for feeding is the primary limiting factor. The quoted lines emphasize temperature effects and survival rather than host availability.

252.Option: The presence of cross-reactive antibodies against other alphaviruses, which may provide indirect protection against CHIKV reinfection
Evidence: it has been shown that ECSA strain based vaccines are able to cross-neutralize against other CHIKV strains and even other alphaviruses from the same serogroup [12], [14], [19]. Therefore, our recombinant CHIKV VLPs would be expected to provide protection against most, if not all, CHIKV strains.
252.Rationale: The passage does state that ECSA-strain vaccines can cross-neutralize other CHIKV strains and even other alphaviruses, so cross-reactive antibodies could contribute to observed protection. However, the question asks which factor most critically influences interpretation of long-term protection after a single dose with no boosters. Cross-reactivity can confound interpretation but is secondary to whether protective immune effectors persist over time. The critical issue for interpreting durability of protection is the persistence (magnitude and neutralizing capacity) of the antibody response itself rather than whether some of those antibodies cross-react with other alphaviruses. Therefore this option is not the best single, most critical factor.

252.Option: The antigenic stability of the CHIKV VLPs during storage, which may affect the integrity of the vaccine and thus the duration of protection
Evidence: Conditions for optimal stability of VLPs produced under serum-free conditions should be determined when this vaccine candidate is further developed by the industry, but so far we have no indications that storage at −80°C is detrimental to VLP integrity.
252.Rationale: Storage stability and antigenic integrity of the vaccine formulation matter for manufacturing, distribution, and the ability to deliver an effective initial dose. The passage even notes no indications that −80°C storage is detrimental. However, once a single dose has been administered, interpretation of long-term protection in the absence of boosters hinges primarily on host immune durability (persistence of neutralizing antibodies and functional immunity), not on how well the vaccine was stored after administration. Thus antigenic stability during storage is important for vaccine quality control, but it is not the most critical factor for interpreting long-term protection post-vaccination without boosters.

252.Option: The rate of viral clearance in the host, which may reduce the likelihood of reinfection and thus influence the observed duration of protection
Evidence: All animals vaccinated with 1 µg VLPs were completely protected against viraemia (Fig. 6A).
Mice vaccinated with 0.1 µg VLPs, showed a ∼7 log reduction in viraemia on day 2, with virus undetectable on the other days (Fig. 6A).
252.Rationale: The passage documents that vaccination reduces or eliminates viraemia after challenge, demonstrating an effect on viral replication/clearance during acute challenge. However, the question concerns interpreting long-term protection in the absence of boosters. Rate of viral clearance following an acute challenge is an outcome that reflects protection at the time of challenge, but it does not directly determine how long that protection will last in an unchallenged host over months. The critical interpretive factor is whether protective immune effectors (especially neutralizing antibodies) persist over time; viral clearance rate is a consequence of existing immunity rather than the primary metric for projecting duration of protection.

252.Option: The dose of the vaccine administered, as higher doses are known to induce longer-lasting immunity in many vaccine platforms
Evidence: To assess the immunogenicity of the CHIKV-VLPs, C57/BL6 mice were vaccinated once with 0.1 µg or 1 µg of the VLPs, 1 µg of the VLPs formulated with Quil A adjuvant or 10 µg inactivated CHIKV (positive control).
The 1 µg VLP dose induced neutralizing antibody titers comparable with those observed after vaccination with 10 µg of inactivated CHIKV (Fig. 5A).
A single vaccination with 1 µg of non-adjuvanted CHIKV VLPs provided complete protection against viraemia and foot swelling caused by CHIKV infection.
252.Rationale: Vaccine dose clearly influences the magnitude of the immune response in this study (0.1 µg versus 1 µg produced different antibody titers and protection), and dose can influence durability. However, when interpreting long-term protection after a single dose with no boosters, the most critical factor to assess is the durability and functional persistence of the immune response (e.g., neutralizing antibodies) elicited by that dose. Dose is upstream of that durability (it influences it), but the direct determinant for interpreting whether protection persists at day 183 is the measured persistence/magnitude of neutralizing antibodies and other immune correlates, not dose per se.

252.Option: The durability of the humoral immune response, particularly the magnitude and persistence of neutralizing antibodies, is the most critical factor in interpreting long-term protection after a single dose
Evidence: VLPs mimic the native virus surface architecture and protein conformation, which often makes them potent inducers of protective antibody responses in the absence of adjuvants [21].

DNA vaccines have so far not been particularly effective at generating antibody responses in humans [26], which is a concern as antibodies are believed to be required for protection against CHIKV infections [12], [27].

All groups receiving CHIKV antigens generated neutralizing antibody titers that are significantly different (P<0.01) from both control groups (PBS and Ac-GFP) but not significantly different from one another (Fig. 5A).
The 1 µg VLP dose induced neutralizing antibody titers comparable with those observed after vaccination with 10 µg of inactivated CHIKV (Fig. 5A).
All animals vaccinated with 1 µg VLPs were completely protected against viraemia (Fig. 6A).
a single vaccination with 1 µg of non-adjuvanted CHIKV VLPs provided complete protection against viraemia and foot swelling caused by CHIKV infection.
252.Rationale: The passage repeatedly links neutralizing antibody responses with protection: VLPs are 'potent inducers of protective antibody responses', 'antibodies are believed to be required for protection', and neutralizing titers correlate with complete protection against viraemia and disease after a single dose. Therefore, when assessing long-term protection (e.g., persistence of protection at ≥183 days without boosters), the critical consideration is whether neutralizing antibodies (and other relevant humoral effectors) remain at protective levels over that interval. Durability of the humoral response is the most direct and informative factor for interpreting long-term protection in the absence of booster doses.

253.Option: H. ducreyi persists in the community through asymptomatic genital colonization, which allows the pathogen to evade systemic azithromycin and re-infect skin after minor trauma
Evidence: Asymptomatic carriage of H. ducreyi was demonstrated by PCR in the genital mucosa of about 2% of sex workers in The Gambia [14], but no studies address whether either H. ducreyi or T. p. pertenue potentially colonize non-genital skin.
This is the first report of asymptomatic carriage of H. ducreyi DNA on skin.

By exposure status, 8 (24%) of 34 asymptomatic participants in the group exposed to an H. ducreyi positive ulcer, and 8 (18%) of 44 participants not exposed to an H. ducreyi positive ulcer were H. ducreyi DNA positive (OR 1.38; 95%CI 0.46–4.17; p = 0.76)
253.Rationale: While genital mucosal carriage has been documented in a specific high-risk group (2% of sex workers), the passage emphasizes that skin (non-genital) asymptomatic carriage was newly demonstrated and was common in the study community. The observed similar prevalence of H. ducreyi DNA among contacts in households with and without ulcer cases argues against genital colonization of a few individuals being the primary explanation for community persistence after MDA. The study's findings point to non-genital skin carriage and environmental sources rather than a dominant genital reservoir in this population.

253.Option: H. ducreyi persists in the community through a reservoir in the gut of infected individuals, which is not affected by systemic azithromycin and allows for re-infection via fecal-oral transmission
Evidence: Both infections are thought to be exclusively transmitted through non-intact skin contact with infectious lesions.
Asymptomatic colonization of exposed skin may serve as a reservoir for transmission to family members, classmates, or playmates.
Flies (n = 10) were caught outside 10 houses of patients with ulcers; 9 (90%) of 10 flies had H. ducreyi DNA and 5 (50%) had amplifiable T. p. pertenue DNA
253.Rationale: The passage frames transmission as occurring via non-intact skin contact and explores skin carriage, flies, and fomites as plausible reservoirs. There is no mention or evidence of a gastrointestinal reservoir or fecal-oral transmission route for H. ducreyi. The data instead support cutaneous and environmental reservoirs, so a gut reservoir is unsupported by the text.

253.Option: H. ducreyi persists in the community through a reservoir in the respiratory tract of asymptomatic individuals, which is not targeted by systemic azithromycin and allows for airborne transmission
Evidence: Both infections are thought to be exclusively transmitted through non-intact skin contact with infectious lesions.
Asymptomatic colonization of exposed skin may serve as a reservoir for transmission to family members, classmates, or playmates.
There is no clear evidence of H. ducreyi or yaws transmission by insects or fomites.
253.Rationale: The passage specifies cutaneous transmission (non-intact skin contact) and investigates skin, flies, and fomites as possible reservoirs. There is no evidence or suggestion of respiratory tract carriage or airborne transmission for H. ducreyi in the text; therefore a respiratory reservoir is not supported.

253.Option: H. ducreyi persists in the community through direct transmission from symptomatic cases via skin-to-skin contact, which remains frequent despite mass drug administration
Evidence: By exposure status, 8 (24%) of 34 asymptomatic participants in the group exposed to an H. ducreyi positive ulcer, and 8 (18%) of 44 participants not exposed to an H. ducreyi positive ulcer were H. ducreyi DNA positive (OR 1.38; 95%CI 0.46–4.17; p = 0.76)
This is the first report of asymptomatic carriage of H. ducreyi DNA on skin.
The minimal effect of MDA with azithromycin on the prevalence of skin ulcers due to H. ducreyi, compared to the profound effect on the prevalence of yaws, is puzzling.
253.Rationale: If ongoing transmission were driven primarily by symptomatic cases via skin-to-skin contact, one would expect higher carriage among contacts of cases. Instead, carriage rates were similar in households with and without ulcer cases, and the study documents widespread asymptomatic skin carriage. The authors infer that asymptomatic carriers and environmental reservoirs (flies, linens) better explain persistence after MDA than continued direct transmission from symptomatic cases alone.

253.Option: H. ducreyi persists in the community through asymptomatic skin carriage and environmental reservoirs such as flies and contaminated linens, which are not effectively targeted by systemic azithromycin
Evidence: Asymptomatic colonization of exposed skin may serve as a reservoir for transmission to family members, classmates, or playmates.
This is the first report of asymptomatic carriage of H. ducreyi DNA on skin.
Overall, 16 (21%, 95%CI 12–31) of 78 asymptomatic participants had H. ducreyi bacterial DNA on the skin, and 1 (1%) had T. p. pertenue DNA.
Flies (n = 10) were caught outside 10 houses of patients with ulcers; 9 (90%) of 10 flies had H. ducreyi DNA and 5 (50%) had amplifiable T. p. pertenue DNA
Environmental contamination was observed in 3 (50%) of 6 bed sheets sampled from 6 households with index cases; 2 sheets had detectable H. ducreyi DNA and 1 had detectable T. p. pertenue DNA.
If azithromycin does not reach the outer surface of skin, it may not interrupt colonization, and these sources may perpetuate the infection in the community.
The minimal effect of MDA with azithromycin on the prevalence of skin ulcers due to H. ducreyi, compared to the profound effect on the prevalence of yaws, is puzzling.
253.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence that asymptomatic skin carriage is common (≈20% of asymptomatic children), that flies and bed linens frequently carry H. ducreyi DNA, and that MDA had limited impact on H. ducreyi despite reducing yaws. The authors explicitly propose that colonization of asymptomatic villagers, flies, and fomites could explain persistence and note that systemic azithromycin might not reach the skin surface to clear such colonization. Together, these observations directly support this explanation.

254.Option: Exposure to bat bodily fluids through contact with surfaces where bats have roosted
Evidence: Bats can harbor zoonotic pathogens that may be shed in excreta and bodily fluids (eg., feces, urine, saliva, blood, etc.) [19–20].

In developing settings, buildings, like family homes, places of worship, and schools, can be highly permeable to bats, and with ongoing habitat loss bats are increasingly using these structures as roosts [26–27].
Over 90% of respondents reported indirect contact with bats, mostly through interactions with their feces and urine (n = 98).
bats often roost in livestock enclosures in this region and may deposit feces or bodily fluids in spaces frequently used by domestic animals [43].
254.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that bats shed pathogens in bodily fluids and that bats frequently roost in buildings, depositing feces and bodily fluids on surfaces. Contact with surfaces contaminated by those bodily fluids in shared built environments is a clear form of environmental contamination and an indirect transmission route to humans. Therefore, exposure to bat bodily fluids on roosting surfaces directly increases the likelihood of indirect pathogen transmission.

254.Option: Consumption of fruit pulp contaminated by bat saliva in shared indoor environments
Evidence: Many frugivorous bat species chew and eject saliva-covered fruit pulp below their roosts, which domestic animals may then consume and become exposed to shed pathogens [44].
Indeed, it is thought that Nipah and Hendra virus, both paramyxoviruses with high human mortality, emerged in pigs and horses in this fashion, respectively [25].
Over 90% of respondents reported indirect contact with bats, mostly through interactions with their feces and urine (n = 98).
254.Rationale: While contaminated fruit pulp is a plausible indirect transmission route, the passage describes this pathway primarily in relation to domestic animals consuming saliva-contaminated fruit below roosts, not humans in shared indoor environments. The study emphasizes feces/urine and environmental contamination inside buildings as the primary indirect exposure routes. Thus, consumption of contaminated fruit pulp indoors is not the most directly supported environmental contamination pathway in this context.

254.Option: Contact with bat feces and urine in shared indoor environments
Evidence: Over 90% of respondents reported indirect contact with bats, mostly through interactions with their feces and urine (n = 98).
Daily occurrences of indirect contact were reported by most participants (78%, χ2 = 285.06, P < 0.01, Fig 2B).
Indirect contacts were frequently reported in our study and are also common pathways for zoonotic pathogen spillover [39].

bat excreta reported in these indirect interactions, mostly feces, can contain pathogens shed by bats, including coronaviruses, rotaviruses, and paramyxoviruses that are viral families of human health concern [20].
254.Rationale: The passage directly identifies contact with bat feces and urine inside buildings as the most commonly reported form of indirect interaction and links bat excreta to pathogens of human health concern. These facts make contact with feces and urine the most direct and well-supported environmental contamination pathway for indirect transmission in shared built environments.

254.Option: Inhalation of bat fecal dust from contaminated surfaces in shared indoor environments
Evidence: Fungal pathogens, like Histoplasma capsulatum, the causative agent of histoplasmosis, may also be inhaled from bat fecal dust and have infected people living in buildings with bat roosts in Africa [40].
Indirect contacts were frequently reported in our study and are also common pathways for zoonotic pathogen spillover [39].

bat excreta reported in these indirect interactions, mostly feces, can contain pathogens shed by bats, including coronaviruses, rotaviruses, and paramyxoviruses that are viral families of human health concern [20].
254.Rationale: The passage gives a specific example of inhalation of bat fecal dust leading to human infection (histoplasmosis) and emphasizes that bat feces in buildings can harbor pathogens. Aerosolization/inhalation of fecal dust from contaminated surfaces is therefore a clear and directly supported mechanism of indirect environmental transmission in shared built environments.

254.Option: Direct contact with live bats during attempts to remove them from buildings
Evidence: Close to half of participants (39%) reported direct contact with bats, including people touching bats (n = 40) and one report of being bitten.
Attempts to remove bats from buildings were reported by almost 80% of participants (n = 81). Of those reporting removal efforts, almost half reported direct contact with bats (n = 34).
Paradoxically, these activities often led to direct human contact with bats, creating additional opportunities for pathogen exposure.
Indirect contacts were frequently reported in our study and are also common pathways for zoonotic pathogen spillover [39].
254.Rationale: Direct contact with live bats is a route for direct transmission (e.g., bites, touches) rather than environmental contamination. The question specifically asks about indirect pathogen transmission through environmental contamination in shared built environments, so direct contact during removal attempts does not most directly represent the environmental contamination pathway being asked about.

255.Option: The combined model uses QMRA to estimate exposure and SIR to predict the number of new infections per day, allowing for direct inference of the proportion of diarrhea cases attributable to waterborne transmission without considering the probability of symptomatic illness
Evidence: In our QMRA, we used information about the hazard (i.e. contaminated drinking water), the exposure (i.e. amount drunk, amount treated, etc.), the target pathogen (i.e. Cryptosporidium and Giardia) and the host (i.e. children < 5), described by statistical distributions or point estimates, to estimate the probability that exposure resulted in ingestion of a target pathogen, and that the estimated amount ingested resulted in an infection for the host.

To determine if a susceptible child transitioned into the infected state on a given day, first the QMRA model was used to assign a probability of infection, P(i), modeled as a Bernoulli variable (1 = infection, 0 = no infection), to each child. If P(i) = 1, the probability that the infection resulted in a diarrheic symptom (P(s)) was modeled based on published studies of the diarrhea morbidity ratio for children diagnosed with Cryptosporidium [45, 47] or Giardia [9, 46] in developing countries.

Using results from the SIR model, we estimated the diarrheal disease burden associated with drinking tubewell water during each monsoon season (mid-June to mid-September 2012 and 2013) for the population of < 5 children in the Sanitation Trial (population burden) and compared modeling estimates to observed levels of child diarrhea measured in the Sanitation Trial over the same periods.
255.Rationale: Although the combined QMRA+SIR approach does use QMRA to estimate exposure/infection probability and SIR to simulate infections over time, the option is incorrect because the study explicitly models the probability that an infection becomes symptomatic (P(s)). The authors did not infer symptomatic case counts 'without considering the probability of symptomatic illness'—they explicitly incorporated morbidity ratios (P(s)) into the SIR transitions and used those to estimate diarrhea burden.

255.Option: The combined model uses QMRA to estimate pathogen concentration and SIR to simulate the spread of infection, allowing for direct comparison of model outputs to observed diarrhea rates without adjusting for household-level water treatment behaviors
Evidence: As tubewell samples may have contained a mixture of human and non-human shed parasites, we simulated three different scenarios to estimate the fraction of parasites detected in a tubewell sample that was a species infectious-to-humans (Sp)...

To estimate the waterborne child diarrhea disease burden in the study population attributable to Cryptosporidium and Giardia protozoal contamination of tubewell drinking water, we used an SIR model coupled with the QMRA model, above, to longitudinally track the daily infection status of each individual child in our study population over time (Fig 2), assigning each to a tubewell type (TW) and to boiling (B) before drinking as a function of the household fractions for each practice in the population (see TW and B in Table 1).

To account for the effects of boiling water (the only point of use treatment method used by the study population) on parasite viability, we used WHO reported [39] 6 log10 reduction values for Cryptosporidium and Giardia from boiling ... and used household survey data collected from study households (n = 355) to estimate the fraction of households that boiled their drinking water (9% and 16% of deep and shallow tubewell users, respectively, reported boiling; see Table 1, B, boiling rates)
255.Rationale: The claim that the model did not adjust for household-level water treatment is false. The study explicitly incorporated household boiling behavior (rates by tubewell type based on survey data) into exposure/dose calculations and the SIR assignments. Therefore this option misrepresents the methods and is incorrect.

255.Option: The combined model simulates how daily exposure leads to cumulative illness, recovery, and immunity, enabling estimation of time-varying diarrhea prevalence that can be compared to observed surveillance data
Evidence: To estimate the infection risk and associated child diarrhea disease burden from drinking groundwater and compared estimates to independently measured child diarrhea prevalence rates observed in the same population over the same period when contamination was observed.

To estimate the waterborne child diarrhea disease burden in the study population attributable to Cryptosporidium and Giardia protozoal contamination of tubewell drinking water, we used an SIR model coupled with the QMRA model, above, to longitudinally track the daily infection status of each individual child in our study population over time (Fig 2), assigning each to a tubewell type (TW) and to boiling (B) before drinking as a function of the household fractions for each practice in the population (see TW and B in Table 1).

SIR models track the daily infectious status of individuals in three states; susceptible to acquiring an infection when exposed (S), infected from an exposure event (I), and recovered from an infection event with some degree of immunity (R).
The SIR model was run on a daily time step for 98 days each year (allowing for eight days of model warm-up to establish baseline distributions of SIR states)...
Once the illness event ended a child was assumed to transition into the recovered state and be temporarily immune to reinfection for a duration of seven days post-infection [17].

Thus, to properly compare our QMRA-SIR modeled daily average prevalence over the monsoon period simulation (mid-June to mid-September) to the Trial’s observed 7-day recall period prevalence on a single day during each monsoon season, we sampled the simulated child population and their daily diarrhea prevalence time series over the 90-day monsoon period as they were sampled in the Trial’s surveillance.
255.Rationale: This accurately describes what the integrated approach does in the study: QMRA provides daily infection probabilities from exposure, and the SIR framework accumulates infections, models illness duration, recovery, and temporary immunity on a daily timescale, producing time-varying prevalence that is then sampled to compare directly to surveillance (7-day recall) data. That integration enables a more realistic, temporally resolved estimate of the waterborne contribution to diarrhea than either component alone.

255.Option: The combined model applies QMRA to estimate daily infection risk and SIR to calculate the average duration of illness, enabling estimation of the total number of diarrhea episodes per child over the study period
Evidence: In our QMRA, we used information about the hazard (i.e. contaminated drinking water), the exposure (i.e. amount drunk, amount treated, etc.), the target pathogen (i.e. Cryptosporidium and Giardia) and the host (i.e. children < 5), described by statistical distributions or point estimates, to estimate the probability that exposure resulted in ingestion of a target pathogen, and that the estimated amount ingested resulted in an infection for the host.

To estimate the waterborne child diarrhea disease burden in the study population attributable to Cryptosporidium and Giardia protozoal contamination of tubewell drinking water, we used an SIR model coupled with the QMRA model, above, to longitudinally track the daily infection status of each individual child in our study population over time (Fig 2)...

Not all cases of symptomatic Cryptosporidium and Giardia infection persist for the same duration of time. To model this variability, we used a duration of illness parameter (D) (see Table 1) fit from published sources.... From these studies, the mean duration of cryptosporidiosis and giardiasis is 10 (IQR 3–14) and 11 (IQR 6–14) days respectively. Once the illness event ended a child was assumed to transition into the recovered state and be temporarily immune to reinfection for a duration of seven days post-infection [17].

Using results from the SIR model, we estimated the diarrheal disease burden associated with drinking tubewell water during each monsoon season (mid-June to mid-September 2012 and 2013) for the population of < 5 children in the Sanitation Trial (population burden) and compared modeling estimates to observed levels of child diarrhea measured in the Sanitation Trial over the same periods.
255.Rationale: The statement reflects key components of the study: QMRA provides daily infection probabilities and the SIR model incorporates illness duration (drawn from distributions) to generate symptomatic episodes over time. Those elements together allow the authors to estimate cumulative burden (episodes/prevalence) across the study period. Although the duration parameter was an input rather than being 'calculated' de novo by SIR, the combined framework does produce estimates of total episodes over the monsoon period, so the option captures the substantive mechanism and is therefore correct.

255.Option: The combined model estimates the probability of infection from a single exposure event and directly calculates the total number of symptomatic cases over the study period without accounting for individual immunity or reinfection
Evidence: To determine if a susceptible child transitioned into the infected state on a given day, first the QMRA model was used to assign a probability of infection, P(i), modeled as a Bernoulli variable (1 = infection, 0 = no infection), to each child.
Once the illness event ended a child was assumed to transition into the recovered state and be temporarily immune to reinfection for a duration of seven days post-infection [17].

Only children in the susceptible state could transition into the infected state and only if their infection resulted in diarrheic symptoms (i.e. both P(i) and P(s) equal to 1 in the model); susceptible children who were infected without symptoms (P(i) = 1 but P(s) = 0) remained in the susceptible state, while children in the infected and recovered states were protected from infection in the model.
255.Rationale: This option is incorrect because the combined model does more than a single exposure estimate: it assigns daily infection probabilities and explicitly models symptomatic probability, illness duration, recovery, and a temporary immunity period (thus accounting for reinfection dynamics). The model therefore does account for individual immunity and prevents immediate reinfection, contrary to the option's claim.

256.Option: Combined facial defects involving the cheek, nose, and upper lip, as these complex lesions are associated with the highest risk of postoperative complications and poor aesthetic outcomes
Evidence: Nearly 56% (n = 91) of the total facial defects reported in the overall medical records are combined in form.
Most defects resulting from Noma involve the lateral and anterolateral aspects of the face and are often combined with severe functional deficits [19].

A retrospective study that investigated the long-term results of trismus release among Noma patients reported a poor prognosis. This study showed that trismus is one of the most disabling sequelae among Noma survivals [41].
256.Rationale: While combined facial defects are common and produce severe functional and aesthetic problems (supported by quotes showing high frequency and associated functional deficits), the passage specifically highlights trismus as a particularly disabling sequela with a reported poor prognosis for surgical release. Thus, combined defects are important but are not presented in the passage as the single clinical feature most strongly predictive of poor prognosis after surviving the acute phase.

256.Option: Complete loss of the nose or lips, as these anatomical defects are associated with the most severe facial disfigurement and social stigma
Evidence: Furthermore, a complete absence of facial tissues such as the nose, upper lip, and lower lip has been reported.

Most of the surveyed patients suffered from extensive Noma-induced facial disfigurements, which exposed them to eminent functional limitations, social discrimination, and negative psycho-social effects.

A retrospective study that investigated the long-term results of trismus release among Noma patients reported a poor prognosis. This study showed that trismus is one of the most disabling sequelae among Noma survivals [41].
256.Rationale: Complete loss of nose or lips clearly indicates severe disfigurement and social stigma (supported by quoted text), but the passage emphasizes that trismus (ankylosis of the TMJ) is uniquely disabling and has a documented poor prognosis for corrective surgery. Therefore, complete tissue loss indicates severe morbidity but is not singled out in the passage as the feature most strongly associated with poor longer-term prognosis compared with trismus.

256.Option: Severe trismus, as it leads to impaired mastication and swallowing, which can perpetuate malnutrition and stunting in survivors
Evidence: Most survivors present with prominent facial deformities, trismus or ankylosis of the temporomandibular joint, and extensive muscle and skin contracture, which leads to the difficulty of opening and closing of the mouth, thereby, trouble in mastication and swallowing, oral incontinence, and speech difficulties [12].
The problems in mastication and swallowing can also further exacerbate or cause malnutrition and as a result, many young patients experience significant stunting of growth [13].

A retrospective study that investigated the long-term results of trismus release among Noma patients reported a poor prognosis. This study showed that trismus is one of the most disabling sequelae among Noma survivals [41].
256.Rationale: The passage explicitly connects trismus with severe functional impairment (difficulty opening the mouth, impaired mastication and swallowing), documents that these problems perpetuate malnutrition and stunting, and cites a study reporting poor prognosis after trismus release, calling trismus one of the most disabling sequelae. These statements directly support that severe trismus most strongly suggests a poor prognosis despite survival from the acute phase.

256.Option: Grade-4 facial tissue damage (75–100% anatomical damage), as it represents the most severe structural destruction and is associated with the highest mortality risk
Evidence: The NOIPTUS classifies facial anatomical tissue damage into four classes. The classes are graded from 1 to 4, depending on the severity of facial tissue damage. Grade-1, Grade-2, Grade-3 and Grade-4 imply 0–25%, 25–50%, 50–75%, and 75–100% facial anatomical damages respectively.
As Table 2 describes, the NOIPTUS classifies facial anatomical tissue damage into four classes.
Nearly 8% (n = 13) have reported Grade-4 tissue damages respectively.

A retrospective study that investigated the long-term results of trismus release among Noma patients reported a poor prognosis. This study showed that trismus is one of the most disabling sequelae among Noma survivals [41].
256.Rationale: Grade-4 indicates the most extensive tissue loss and is associated with severe morbidity, but the passage does not specifically state that Grade-4 damage is the single best predictor of poor prognosis among survivors. Instead, it highlights functional sequelae—especially trismus—with documented poor prognosis for remediation. Therefore, although Grade-4 damage denotes severe structural destruction, the passage points to trismus as the clinical feature most strongly linked to poor longer-term prognosis.

256.Option: The presence of multiple missing teeth, as this indicates extensive dental destruction and is linked to poor oral hygiene and malnutrition
Evidence: Out of the total 163 patients reviewed, 7.4% (n = 12) had no dental 266 information. On the other hand, 25.8% (n = 42) are reported to have healthy teeth, while the remaining 66.9% (n = 109) have reported a varying number of missing teeth.
The number of missing teeth ranges from 1 to 19 in patients affected by the disease.
The problems in mastication and swallowing can also further exacerbate or cause malnutrition and as a result, many young patients experience significant stunting of growth [13].

A retrospective study that investigated the long-term results of trismus release among Noma patients reported a poor prognosis. This study showed that trismus is one of the most disabling sequelae among Noma survivals [41].
256.Rationale: Multiple missing teeth are common and reflect extensive dental involvement, which contributes to functional problems. However, the passage emphasizes that trismus—causing inability to open the mouth and resultant feeding problems—is the decisive disabling sequela with documented poor prognosis. Thus, missing teeth are an important morbidity marker but are not presented as the single strongest indicator of poor prognosis after surviving the acute phase.

257.Option: High levels of synonymous polymorphism across multiple lineages of the pathogen, indicating relaxed selection on the gene
Evidence: The neutral theory of evolution states that most evolutionary change at the molecular level is caused by the fixation of neutral alleles through random genetic drift [1].
To be considered as being under positive selection, these genes had to exhibit a dN/dS ratio larger than 1 and a p-value for the Z-test below 0.05.
Thirty-two housekeeping genes were randomly selected ... None of these genes showed evidence of being under positive selection in the P. brasiliensis branches, as illustrated in Table 2.
257.Rationale: High synonymous polymorphism is a pattern consistent with neutrality or relaxed selection (neutral theory), not with adaptive change driven by immune evasion. The passage contrasts neutral expectations with signals of positive selection (dN/dS > 1 and significant tests). Therefore, observing high levels of synonymous (silent) variation would argue against an immune-driven adaptive process and is not the pattern that would most strongly suggest immune-escape driven evolution.

257.Option: A significant excess of non-synonymous substitutions in the gene's coding region compared to synonymous substitutions, detected using the M-K test
Evidence: Deviations from this model form the basis for various tests of natural selection, such as the HKA test [17], and the M-K test [18].

M-K tests.M-K tests [18] between the P. brasiliensis sensu lato and H. capsulatum, using the aligned regions previously sequenced as well as sequences retrieved from GenBank, were calculated using the DNASP analysis program [42].
M-K tests were significant for p27, cdc42 and fks (p27: P=0.043594; cdc42: P=0.000993; fks: P=0.000017; Table 3) when H. capsulatum was used as an outgroup.

Judging by the significant results of the M-K tests, positive selection has played an important role in the history of these three genes and the depletion of genetic variation within P. brasiliensis (at these three loci) is a consequence of positive selection.
257.Rationale: An excess of non-synonymous substitutions relative to synonymous substitutions in the McDonald–Kreitman (M-K) framework is a standard and well-supported signal of positive (adaptive) selection. If the gene encodes a surface antigen, such a pattern would indicate that amino acid–changing mutations have been favored and fixed due to selective pressures (for example, host immune pressure), consistent with immune-escape driven evolution. The passage explicitly uses significant M-K test results to infer positive selection for virulence-related genes.

257.Option: Persistent non-synonymous polymorphisms in antigenic regions predicted to be recognized by host immune cells
Evidence: The persistence of non-synonymous intra- and trans-specific gp43 polymorphisms within and between lineages of the P. brasiliensis complex suggests they have been maintained by historical or contemporary selection [51].
Using the Bayesian Empirical Bayes (BEB) analysis, 19 sites with a posterior probability greater than 95% of having a greater than 1 value were identified.

In order to predict potential antigenic determinants for HLA recognition, we used the program SYPFETHI [44]. As illustrated in Figure 3, seven of the sites under positive selection were located as potential epitopes as predicted with SYFPEITHI.
257.Rationale: Persistent non-synonymous polymorphisms in regions that are predicted epitopes (i.e., potential targets of host immune recognition) are a classic signature of diversifying or balancing selection driven by host immune pressure. The passage gives gp43 as an explicit example: multiple non-synonymous polymorphisms have been maintained across lineages and several positively selected sites map to predicted epitopes, which the authors interpret as evidence of selection related to host–pathogen interaction. This pattern most directly implicates antigenic change and immune evasion as drivers of molecular evolution.

257.Option: A high dN/dS ratio in a gene with no known antigenic function, suggesting general adaptive evolution unrelated to host immunity
Evidence: To be considered as being under positive selection, these genes had to exhibit a dN/dS ratio larger than 1 and a p-value for the Z-test below 0.05.
Thirty-two housekeeping genes were randomly selected ... None of these genes showed evidence of being under positive selection in the P. brasiliensis branches, as illustrated in Table 2.

A comparison of these virulence factors with housekeeping genes in P. brasiliensis showed that a higher proportion of virulence genes evolve under positive selection (37.5% vs. 0%), suggesting that at least some of these genes have an adaptive role.
257.Rationale: A high dN/dS indicates adaptive evolution, but if the gene has no known antigenic or surface function, that signal does not specifically implicate immune-escape through changes in surface antigens. The question asks for the pattern that most strongly suggests immune-evasion by altering surface antigens; adaptive change in a non-antigenic gene is consistent with general adaptation but is not the most direct or specific evidence for immune-driven antigenic evolution.

257.Option: A recent fixation of a single non-synonymous mutation in a virulence gene across all pathogen lineages, indicating strong directional selection
Evidence: Positive directional selection occurs when successive amino acid changes make a protein better adapted in a particular biological context, and as a result the changes will tend to be fixed in future lineages.
Conversely, the TRMCAs for cdc42, p27 and fks were significantly lower than the other genes as is expected if a gene is under positive directional selection.

The TRMCA for the gp43 alleles is longer than for any other gene in P. brasiliensis (Table 6), indicating that the polymorphism in gp43 is significantly older than the polymorphism in the other genes ... This constitutes evidence for balancing selection [49],[50].
257.Rationale: A recent fixation of a single non-synonymous change indicates a strong directional selective sweep, which can reflect adaptation. However, immune-escape via surface antigen variation is most often associated with diversifying or balancing selection that maintains multiple alleles (persistent polymorphism) rather than fixation of a single allele across lineages. The passage contrasts directional sweeps (short TMRCA) with balancing selection (long TMRCA with persistent polymorphism) and provides gp43 as an example of the latter being indicative of immune-related pressures. Thus fixation of a single mutation is less diagnostic of ongoing immune-escape processes than persistent antigenic polymorphism.

258.Option: The use of blood samples collected close to the time of exposure reduced misclassification of pre-existing immunity, enhancing the accuracy of the exposure-outcome relationship
Evidence: Two major strengths of this study design likely explain why a significant association between NTs and protection from infection was observed in contrast to previous prospective cohort studies. Whereas earlier cohort studies typically analyzed NTs in blood samples collected up to six months or more prior to the incident DENV infection, our study provided blood samples from within two weeks prior to virus exposure, thus minimizing the confounding factor of variations in antibody kinetics.
In all subjects, the day 0 blood sample was presumed to reflect the neutralizing antibody status just prior to exposure or infection.
This presumption is further supported by the fact that DENV IgM ELISA was negative at day 14/15 indicating that infection was still early in its course.
258.Rationale: The authors explicitly state that obtaining day 0 samples within two weeks of exposure minimized misclassification due to antibody waning and variability, and that day 0 samples were presumed to reflect pre-exposure NTs. Reducing exposure misclassification increases measurement accuracy of the exposure (pre-existing NTs) and therefore increases the ability to detect an association even with small numbers.

258.Option: The use of PCR-positive cases and PCR-negative controls from the same household ensured that both groups were exposed to the same viral strain, reducing confounding by serotype variation
Evidence: we identified contact subjects from the two geographic cluster studies who lived in the same house as another subject who was DENV positive on day 0, but were themselves DENV negative by nested PCR and IgM ELISA on both days 0 and 15 (KPSII study), or by NS1 ELISA and IgM ELISA on both days 0 and 14 (DEVOL study). These DENV negative “non-susceptible” subjects were presumed to have a high likelihood of exposure to DENV during the cluster investigation, yet still did not become infected.
In both the KPSII and DEVOL studies, the exposure serotype for all “non-susceptible” subjects was presumed to be the same as the infecting serotype for the index case from the same cluster.
By requiring documentation of a DENV infection in the same house, however, our selection criteria for “non-susceptible” subjects maximized the likelihood of such exposure.
258.Rationale: Selecting PCR-positive and PCR-negative subjects from the same household/cluster, and presuming the same exposure serotype for non-susceptible subjects, reduces confounding by differing serotype exposures and increases comparability between groups. This stronger exposure ascertainment enhances the chance of observing a true association despite limited sample size.

258.Option: The use of a two-week window for blood collection allowed for the detection of both pre-existing and newly formed neutralizing antibodies, increasing the likelihood of identifying protective immunity
Evidence: In order to assess neutralizing antibody status just prior to virus exposure, we first identified contact subjects from the two geographic cluster studies who were DENV positive by nested PCR at day 15 (KPSII study) or day 14 (DEVOL study).
In all subjects, the day 0 blood sample was presumed to reflect the neutralizing antibody status just prior to exposure or infection.
No data or blood was collected after day 15.
258.Rationale: The study used day 0 samples specifically to capture pre-existing NTs just prior to exposure; they did not collect post-exposure samples (no blood after day 14/15) to measure newly formed neutralizing antibodies. Thus the two-week window was used to ensure pre-exposure measurement, not to detect newly formed antibodies, so this option is incorrect.

258.Option: The use of multiple DENV serotypes in the PRNT assay allowed for the detection of cross-protective immune responses, increasing the sensitivity of the association analysis
Evidence: day 0 blood samples were tested by an in-house plaque reduction neutralization test (PRNT) using all four DENV serotypes and JEV as previously described [24], [25].

Homotypic NTs were more strongly associated with protection than heterotypic NTs except in the case of DENV-2 infections, in which pre-existing heterotypic DENV-4 NTs were also positively associated with protection against DENV-2.

Two major strengths of this study design likely explain why a significant association between NTs and protection from infection was observed in contrast to previous prospective cohort studies. Whereas earlier cohort studies typically analyzed NTs in blood samples collected up to six months or more prior to the incident DENV infection, our study provided blood samples from within two weeks prior to virus exposure... By requiring documentation of a DENV infection in the same house, however, our selection criteria for “non-susceptible” subjects maximized the likelihood of such exposure.
258.Rationale: While the PRNT panel across serotypes was useful for characterizing homotypic versus heterotypic responses, the authors attribute the main reasons they could detect an association despite small numbers to (1) timely pre-exposure blood sampling and (2) household-based selection maximizing exposure. The use of multiple serotypes aided interpretation but is not presented as the primary factor that increased ability to detect the association with small sample size.

258.Option: The inclusion of both children and adults in the study increased the diversity of immune responses, improving the generalizability of the association between neutralizing antibodies and protection
Evidence: Although age was incorporated into the statistical analyses, the inclusion of adults in the DEVOL study compared to only children in the KPSII study could have introduced an additional confounding factor in interpreting NT values.

Because of the small numbers, we used data from two different geographic cluster studies over multiple years. The inclusion of both adults and children with likely differing immunological backgrounds may have affected our findings for the DENV-2 analysis, possibly accounting for the higher DENV-2 NTs associated with protection.
258.Rationale: The passage indicates that including adults (DEVOL) alongside children (KPSII) may have introduced confounding and affected interpretation, not that it increased the ability to detect the association. Thus this option is incorrect.

259.Option: In high-transmission settings, the high proportion of sub-microscopic infections indicates that routine surveillance using light microscopy is sufficient to detect most P. vivax cases and guide interventions effectively
Evidence: A negative relationship between LM P. vivax prevalence and proportion of SM P. vivax infections was identified (Fig. 7), with LM P. vivax prevalence identified as a significant factor for predicting the proportion of samples that are PCR positive/LM negative (SM) in cross-sectional surveys where the LM P. vivax prevalence is less than 45% (P<0.001, Deviance  = 7.32, df  = 29).

This suggests that a relatively larger proportion of P. vivax infections are SM in areas with low transmission intensity compared to areas with high transmission intensity, although SM P. vivax is prevalent in both type of settings.

In summary, SM P. vivax is prevalent across different geographic areas with varying transmission intensities constituting, on average, 69.5% of all P. vivax infections. The relative proportion of SM P. vivax is significantly higher than that of the sympatric P. falciparum in these settings and is higher in areas with relatively low transmission intensity.
259.Rationale: The option asserts that in high-transmission settings there is a high proportion of SM infections and that LM is therefore sufficient. The passage demonstrates the opposite: the proportion of SM infections is larger in low-transmission settings (negative relationship between LM prevalence and SM proportion). Thus the premise is incorrect and the conclusion about sufficiency of routine LM in the stated context is not supported.

259.Option: Since sub-microscopic P. vivax infections are asymptomatic, they do not contribute to transmission and can be safely ignored in elimination planning
Evidence: Mature gametocytes are present earlier in P. vivax infections meaning they can infect mosquitoes at an early stage of infection, with SM P. vivax gametocytes shown to be able to successfully infect mosquitoes [8], [9].
This means that between 88.6 and 100.0% (average 97.1%) of individuals with SM P. vivax were asymptomatic.

SM P. vivax infections not only have negative health impact on the infected individual, but will also contribute to P. vivax transmission both from the current infection and subsequent relapses, and thus present a major challenge for malaria elimination programs.
259.Rationale: Although SM P. vivax infections are largely asymptomatic, the passage explicitly states they can infect mosquitoes and contribute to transmission (including future relapses). Therefore they cannot be ignored in elimination planning; they represent a hidden reservoir that undermines elimination unless addressed.

259.Option: Because SM P. vivax infections are more common in adults, surveillance should focus on adult populations to detect the majority of transmissible infections
Evidence: SM P. falciparum is more common in adults, in areas with low transmission intensities and in chronic infections [6].

Of the 16 surveys that analysed the relationship between P. vivax LM or PCR prevalence and age, seven publications (eight surveys) found no significant association between the relative proportion of SM P. vivax infection and host age [14], [15], [17], [22], [27], [28], [30], while five publications (eight surveys) reported that the relative proportion of SM P. vivax increased with age [12], [23], [31], [32], [34].

This difference may be related to the transmission intensities in study regions, however a comparison of the average LM P. vivax prevalence between these two groups did not show a significant difference.
259.Rationale: The evidence for an age association in P. vivax SM infections is inconsistent across studies: about half found no association and half found increased SM with age. Thus it is not valid to universally prioritize adult surveillance as the primary approach; surveillance should be informed by local data rather than assuming adults carry the majority of SM P. vivax.

259.Option: The negative relationship between LM prevalence and SM proportion suggests that increasing the sensitivity of light microscopy would reduce the proportion of sub-microscopic infections across all transmission settings
Evidence: Many factors can contribute to the occurrence and level of SM P. vivax. These include technical, parasite and host factors. From a technical point of view, the quality of microscopy is an important factor that can contribute to the level of LM P. vivax, and thus SM P. vivax.

SM P. vivax infections are not detectable using LM or RDTs, as such these infected individuals would not receive any anti-malarial therapy for P. vivax blood or liver stages and thus continue to transmit P. vivax.
In order to maximize PCR sensitivity in detecting SM parasite infections, >150 µL of whole blood should be used when possible.
mass screening using PCR-based or comparably sensitive methods and radical cure approach would be required to identify and treat all SM P. vivax infected subjects.
259.Rationale: While improving microscopy quality could detect some additional low-density infections, the passage emphasizes that SM infections are below LM detection limits and that PCR or comparably sensitive methods are required to identify the large hidden reservoir—especially in low-transmission settings. Biological and methodological detection limits mean simply improving LM sensitivity will not eliminate SM infections across all settings; more sensitive diagnostics (PCR-like) are indicated.

259.Option: In low-transmission settings, surveillance based on light microscopy will substantially underestimate the true burden of P. vivax and fail to identify a major reservoir of transmissible infections, necessitating more sensitive diagnostics for elimination success
Evidence: There has been increased reporting of malaria infections in communities which are detected by PCR, but not by LM. A negative relationship between LM and PCR determined P. vivax prevalence was observed (Fig. 6). LM P. vivax prevalence was a significant factor for predicting PCR P. vivax prevalence in community surveys (P<0.001, R2 = 0.675).

SM P. vivax infections not only have negative health impact on the infected individual, but will also contribute to P. vivax transmission both from the current infection and subsequent relapses, and thus present a major challenge for malaria elimination programs.
mass screening using PCR-based or comparably sensitive methods and radical cure approach would be required to identify and treat all SM P. vivax infected subjects.
259.Rationale: The passage documents that PCR detects substantially more P. vivax than LM, that the proportion of SM infections is larger in low-transmission settings, and that these SM infections are transmissible and lead to relapses. Therefore in elimination settings relying on LM alone will substantially underestimate burden and miss a key reservoir; more sensitive diagnostics (or strategies such as PCR-based mass screening or MDA guided by PCR-informed estimates) are warranted to achieve elimination.

260.Option: The lack of integration of NTDs into the core curriculum's transmission-based classification may result in students failing to recognize the shared epidemiological patterns among these diseases
Evidence: The epidemiology of communicable diseases course for medical and nursing students is divided into general epidemiology (infectious cycle, pattern of disease occurrence, and general measures of prevention and control of communicable diseases) and specific epidemiology.

Neither the term "neglected tropical disease" nor the abbreviation "NTDs" was mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specifications.

This gap of epidemiological knowledge about NTDs is consistent with the content analysis of the epidemiology course which indicates that the term “NTDs” was not mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specification.
260.Rationale: The passage states that while many individual NTDs were presented within the course's transmission-based organization, the unifying label "NTDs" was not used in course materials. The quoted lines document both the transmission-based structure and the absence of an explicit NTD conceptualization, supporting this explanation.

260.Option: The absence of a unifying conceptual framework for NTDs in the curriculum may prevent students from recognizing these diseases as a distinct public health priority despite exposure to individual diseases
Evidence: Most of the NTDs are included in the course such as schistosomiasis, lymphatic filariasis, leishmaniasis, rabies, soil transmitted helminthiasis (STH) as ascariasis and hookworm infections.
Neither the term "neglected tropical disease" nor the abbreviation "NTDs" was mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specifications.

This gap of epidemiological knowledge about NTDs is consistent with the content analysis of the epidemiology course which indicates that the term “NTDs” was not mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specification.

The quoted text links the curricular omission to the observed low awareness.

Evidence: Most of the NTDs are included in the course such as schistosomiasis, lymphatic filariasis, leishmaniasis, rabies, soil transmitted helminthiasis (STH) as ascariasis and hookworm infections.
Neither the term "neglected tropical disease" nor the abbreviation "NTDs" was mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specifications.

260.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence that the main problem is lack of clinical-phase coverage; instead it highlights that many NTDs were taught within the epidemiology course but the explicit NTD concept and control activities were not emphasized. The authors link the awareness gap to course content/wording (absence of the term "NTDs" and poor coverage of specific prevention/control measures) rather than to omission from clinical training. Because the passage does not support the specific claim about clinical-phase noncoverage, this option is not supported by the provided evidence.

Evidence: Neither the term "neglected tropical disease" nor the abbreviation "NTDs" was mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specifications.

The gap of epidemiological knowledge about NTDs is consistent with the content analysis of the epidemiology course which indicates that the term “NTDs” was not mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specification.

Therefore attributing the problem to absence of standardized national teaching guidelines is speculative and not supported by the quoted material.

260.Option: The limited exposure to NTDs in the epidemiology course may be due to the course's focus on high-profile diseases such as HIV and tuberculosis, which overshadow less common but endemic conditions
Evidence: The specific epidemiology of infectious diseases is divided according to the 4 main modes of transmission, namely: droplet, foodborne, contact, and arthropod borne infections.
Most of the NTDs are included in the course such as schistosomiasis, lymphatic filariasis, leishmaniasis, rabies, soil transmitted helminthiasis (STH) as ascariasis and hookworm infections.
Neither the term "neglected tropical disease" nor the abbreviation "NTDs" was mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specifications.
