261.Option: Perform the assay within 1 hour of drug addition to minimize the impact of time-dependent changes in parasite motility and drug metabolism
Evidence: The activity of the Biospeckle pattern as a function of benznidazole concentration shows curves that fit similar Polynomial regressions at all the tested times (Fig 13) ...
As shown in Fig 15, there is an instantaneous effect at 1min that remains with similar activity values at least for one hour for all the benznidazole concentrations.
This effect is better detected after 1 hour of drug action.
261.Rationale: While the passage documents rapid (1 min) effects and stability up to ~1 hour—i.e. biological time-dependent changes in drug action and parasite response—these are biological kinetics rather than the ‘‘physical process’’ confounder emphasized in the text. The question asks about controlling a confounding physical process; the passage instead highlights desiccation/evaporation as the physical confounder. The quoted lines show the biological/time course observations but do not address the physical confounder (desiccation/evaporation) that the authors identify as needing control.

261.Option: Limit the assay duration to less than 200 minutes to prevent desiccation from altering drug concentration and confounding the interpretation of parasite activity
Evidence: The results indicate that although there is a marked desiccation effect, the Biospeckle pattern remains stable up to around 200 minutes, indicating that as was seen in Figs 2, 3 and 4 (Effect of Parasite Quantity), the Biospeckle pattern under the conditions of this work, reveals the total number of parasites within the well of the VDRL plate.
However it should be noted that desiccation may interfere by increasing drug concentration at the longer times.

We selected the following conditions: 100μL in the test well, parasites in exponential phase, 4x10^4 parasites per well, a maximum time of evaluation of the activity of the Biospeckle pattern of 204 minutes.
261.Rationale: The authors measured desiccation over time and explicitly state that although the biospeckle signal is stable up to ~200 minutes, desiccation at longer times can alter drug concentration and thus confound interpretation. Limiting assay duration to a period before substantial desiccation (i.e., <~200 minutes) is therefore a valid control to ensure observed changes reflect drug effect rather than concentration changes from evaporation.

261.Option: Use a sealed chamber or humidified environment to prevent evaporation and maintain constant liquid volume throughout the assay
Evidence: The design of in vitro assays must take into account that parasites are very sensitive to physical, chemical and biological factors such as handling, temperature, evaporation, osmolarity changes, nutritional factors, doubling time, etc.
The desiccation of the liquid in the air exposed VDRL wells and its implications on the activity of the Biospeckle pattern was evaluated in several ways.
However it should be noted that desiccation may interfere by increasing drug concentration at the longer times.
261.Rationale: The passage identifies evaporation/desiccation as a physical factor that affects the assay and can increase drug concentration over time, thereby confounding results. Preventing evaporation (for example by using a sealed or humidified chamber) directly controls this physical process and preserves constant liquid volume and drug concentration, addressing the confounder the authors highlight.

261.Option: Standardize the initial volume of liquid in each well to ensure consistent baseline conditions across all experimental groups
Evidence: Unless otherwise stated, all the assays were performed with an initial volume of 100μL.
In order to test reproducibility, the internal diameter of the cavities, 60 wells (5 plates) were measured ...

With these dimensions and the approximate size of the parasite ... the object’s plane ... has a mean volume of 1.66μL and contains approximately 670 parasites when the density is 4x10^5 parasites per mL (4x10^4 parasites per well).
261.Rationale: The authors explicitly standardize the initial assay volume (100 μL) and discuss how volume affects the object’s plane and the number of parasites sampled. Standardizing initial volume across wells controls a physical source of variability (baseline concentration, optical geometry, and percent object’s plane) so that changes in parasite activity are attributable to the drug rather than differences in starting liquid volume.

261.Option: Match the concentration of benznidazole across all wells to ensure that differences in activity are due to parasite viability rather than drug concentration variation
Evidence: Each well of the VDRL plate was filled with 100μL of LIT medium in the presence or absence of 4x10^4 epimatigotes per well, containing 7 different concentrations of benznidazole: 0, 1.95x10^-2, 7.81 x10^-2, 31.25 x10^-2, 1.25, 5 and 20 μg/mL ... and at different times (1, 61, 128 and 204 minutes) a video was recorded

261.Rationale: While matching drug concentration is basic experimental control, in this study the investigators intentionally vary benznidazole concentration to measure dose–response. The question asked specifically about a confounding physical process; the passage emphasizes evaporation/desiccation as that physical confounder. Matching concentrations does not address evaporation-driven concentration changes over time (the primary physical confounder discussed), so this option does not directly answer the question as posed.

262.Option: Living in homes with earthen/mud floors, which reflects poor housing conditions and limits access to protective footwear
Evidence: Factors that have been reported to aggravate the problem include earthen and dusty floors, non regular use of closed footwear, common resting place outside the house and pigs on the compound [10].

Others retained in the model though not significant were earthen floor, toilet availability, occasional or not using closed footwear and common resting place inside the house.
262.Rationale: Earthen/mud floors are clearly a structural risk factor mentioned repeatedly in the passage, but the question asks for the factor that most directly reflects the interplay between individual behavior and structural conditions. Earthen floors are primarily a structural condition; while they interact with behavior (e.g., exposure while barefoot), they do not by themselves capture the behavioral choice element as directly as inconsistent footwear use does.

262.Option: Inconsistent use of closed footwear, driven by economic constraints and shaped by high child population density
Evidence: Factors that have been reported to aggravate the problem include earthen and dusty floors, non regular use of closed footwear, common resting place outside the house and pigs on the compound [10].

Others retained in the model though not significant were earthen floor, toilet availability, occasional or not using closed footwear and common resting place inside the house.
This can be attributed to the fact that they are more exposed due to them playing barefoot [10] and also getting reinfected at school in endemic areas.
262.Rationale: The passage identifies 'non regular use of closed footwear' and 'occasional or not using closed footwear' as risk factors and links barefoot play among children to exposure. Inconsistent footwear use sits at the intersection of individual behavior (choosing/being able to wear shoes) and structural/economic constraints (poverty, housing, and access), making it the factor that most directly reflects the behavior-structure interplay that increases risk of ectoparasite presence on the skin.

262.Option: Age ≤14 years, which reflects both developmental vulnerability and high exposure due to outdoor play in endemic areas
Evidence: During peak transmission, children and the elderly bear the brunt of the infection.
In the bivariate analysis, the age group ≤14, school going, self-treatment, occasional or not using closed footwear were shown to be significantly associated with tungiasis (Table 2).

In the multivariate analysis, the occurrence of tungiasis was significantly associated with variables that indicated low economic status (like monthly income <1000 adjusted OR 27.85 (4.13–187.59) and age (≤14 years) of participant adjusted OR 27.414 (10.02–74.99) (Table 3).
This can be attributed to the fact that they are more exposed due to them playing barefoot [10] and also getting reinfected at school in endemic areas.
262.Rationale: Age ≤14 is an important demographic and reflects higher exposure (e.g., playing barefoot), but it is primarily an intrinsic risk group variable rather than a direct indicator of the interplay between behavior and structural conditions. The question asks for a factor that most directly embodies behavior interacting with structural constraints; age indicates who is at risk but not the behavioral choice constrained by socioeconomic conditions as directly as footwear use.

262.Option: Lack of electricity and lack of a toilet, which are indicators of low economic status and contribute to poor hygiene practices
Evidence: Others were being illiterate or low literacy level, bathing without soap, common resting place inside the house, having rats in the compound, lack of electricity and lack of a toilet.

Other variables that were seen to be significant in the bivariate analysis were the age group ≤14, school going, occasional or not using closed footwear, being illiterate or having not completed primary school, bathing without soap, having a common resting place inside the house, lack of electricity and lack of a toilet.
This corroborates the finding of [5, 10]. Tungiasis is certainly a poverty related disease [12].

In the multivariate analysis only the age and the monthly income bracket of <1000 remained significant. The type of floor, not using closed footwear regularly, lack of a toilet and having a common resting place inside the house were retained in the model indicating they had a contribution though not significant.
262.Rationale: Lack of electricity and toilets are structural indicators of poverty referenced in the passage. While they reflect important socioeconomic context that can affect hygiene and vulnerability, they are primarily structural and do not directly capture individual behavioral choices that mediate exposure to ectoparasites on the host's surface as clearly as footwear use.

262.Option: Bathing without soap, which reflects poor hygiene and is associated with low literacy and lack of health education
Evidence: Others were being illiterate or low literacy level, bathing without soap, common resting place inside the house, having rats in the compound, lack of electricity and lack of a toilet.
In the bivariate analysis, the age group ≤14, school going, self-treatment, occasional or not using closed footwear were shown to be significantly associated with tungiasis (Table 2).
Bathing without soap is a factor that has been elucidated in previous studies as well [7, 13].
Almost a half, 45% of the participants interviewed had no knowledge on the transmission of tungiasis.
262.Rationale: Bathing without soap is a behavioral/hygiene factor linked in the passage to lower knowledge and poverty. It does reflect behavior shaped by structural factors (education, resources), but compared with inconsistent use of closed footwear, bathing without soap is less directly tied to exposure of the external surface to the soil-borne flea (transmission is strongly linked to skin contact with contaminated ground and footwear). Thus it is not the most direct single reflection of the behavior-structure interplay that increases risk of ectoparasitic presence on the host's external surface.

263.Option: The delayed peak in dengue cases is caused by a lag in public health reporting, with more cases being diagnosed and recorded in October due to increased surveillance during the peak transmission season
Evidence: The annual outbreaks typically begin around July, peak in October, and then decrease towards the end of the year, corresponding with the wet and hot periods (figure 1b).

Dengue is one of 24 infectious diseases in Vietnam for which there is monthly mandatory reporting from all administrative levels, which, in increasing size, are communes, districts, provinces, and regions.
263.Rationale: The passage explicitly links the timing of outbreaks to climatic seasons (begin July, peak October) rather than to a reporting artifact. Reporting is described as a monthly mandatory system, not a seasonally intensified surveillance program that would produce an artificial October spike. Therefore a reporting lag is not the most likely explanation for the observed late-summer/early-autumn peak.

263.Option: The peak in dengue cases in October is due to increased susceptibility among children who return to school after summer break, leading to higher transmission in densely populated urban areas
Evidence: The annual outbreaks typically begin around July, peak in October, and then decrease towards the end of the year, corresponding with the wet and hot periods (figure 1b).
The majority of notified cases (85%) occurred in individuals aged over 15 years (figure 2A).
263.Rationale: The passage attributes the seasonality to wet and hot periods rather than school-related transmission, and most notified cases were adults (85% over 15 years), which argues against a children/school-driven explanation for the October peak.

263.Option: The peak in dengue cases occurs in October because of increased human mobility during the autumn festival season, which enhances transmission in urban centers
Evidence: The annual outbreaks typically begin around July, peak in October, and then decrease towards the end of the year, corresponding with the wet and hot periods (figure 1b).

In Hanoi dengue transmission demonstrates clear annual cycles that are associated with a lag of around two months with seasonal increases in mean temperatures and rainfall. This pattern reflects climate driven changes in vector abundance, survival, and biting frequency; and the time it takes for a newly infected mosquito to become infectious to humans (the extrinsic incubation period) [17]–[20].
263.Rationale: The passage emphasizes climate-driven vector dynamics and the extrinsic incubation period as the mechanism producing an annual peak with a lag, not increased human mobility due to festivals. There is no discussion of festival-related mobility as a driver of the seasonal peak, making this explanation unlikely relative to the documented climatic explanation.

263.Option: The seasonal peak in dengue cases is due to a higher proportion of secondary dengue infections in October, which are more likely to result in severe clinical illness and thus be reported
Evidence: The annual outbreaks typically begin around July, peak in October, and then decrease towards the end of the year, corresponding with the wet and hot periods (figure 1b).

In Hanoi dengue transmission demonstrates clear annual cycles that are associated with a lag of around two months with seasonal increases in mean temperatures and rainfall. This pattern reflects climate driven changes in vector abundance, survival, and biting frequency; and the time it takes for a newly infected mosquito to become infectious to humans (the extrinsic incubation period) [17]–[20].

As dengue disease severity is modulated by age, prior dengue exposure history, and the infecting dengue serotype, the incidence of clinically apparent dengue cases is unlikely to have a linear relationship with transmission intensity [38], [39], [53].
263.Rationale: While severity and reporting can be influenced by secondary infections, the passage attributes the seasonal timing to climate-driven increases in vector abundance and the extrinsic incubation period. There is no evidence in the text that the proportion of secondary infections specifically increases in October to drive the observed seasonal peak; the dominant explanation provided is climatic effects on transmission.

263.Option: Seasonal increases in temperature and rainfall enhance mosquito abundance and virus transmission, leading to a delayed peak in cases due to the extrinsic incubation period
Evidence: The annual outbreaks typically begin around July, peak in October, and then decrease towards the end of the year, corresponding with the wet and hot periods (figure 1b).

In Hanoi dengue transmission demonstrates clear annual cycles that are associated with a lag of around two months with seasonal increases in mean temperatures and rainfall. This pattern reflects climate driven changes in vector abundance, survival, and biting frequency; and the time it takes for a newly infected mosquito to become infectious to humans (the extrinsic incubation period) [17]–[20].

This pattern reflects climate driven changes in vector abundance, survival, and biting frequency; and the time it takes for a newly infected mosquito to become infectious to humans (the extrinsic incubation period) [17]–[20].
263.Rationale: The passage directly states that seasonal increases in temperature and rainfall are associated with a roughly two-month lag leading to peaks in dengue cases, and explicitly links this to increases in vector abundance and the extrinsic incubation period. This mechanistic explanation best accounts for the observed late-summer/early-autumn peak despite no consistent rise in the susceptible population.

264.Option: The morphological similarity between Amphimerus sp. and Haplorchis pumilio metacercariae allows for cross-protection in human hosts, reducing the need for frequent exposure to maintain infection
Evidence: Eggs from the families Opisthorchiidae and Heterophyidae measuring <30 μm in length have been labeled as small trematode eggs because their morphological similarities prevent detailed discrimination via light microscopic diagnosis [17–19].

Because we can barely rely on morphology to distinguish eggs or metacercariae from members of the Opisthorchiidae and Heterophyidae families, we recommend to implement the PCR-RFLP molecular approach presented in this study in epidemiological surveys...
264.Rationale: The passage documents morphological similarity that complicates diagnosis and species discrimination, but it contains no evidence of immunological cross-protection between Amphimerus sp. and Haplorchis pumilio in humans or that such cross-protection would reduce exposure frequency. The cited text supports diagnostic confusion, not host cross-immunity. Therefore the option's assertion of cross-protection is unsupported by the passage.

264.Option: The widespread presence of morphologically indistinguishable metacercariae in multiple edible fish species undermines accurate detection and enables sustained transmission even in low-fish-consumption communities
Evidence: All fish species were positive for Amphimerus sp. metacercariae.

Because we can barely rely on morphology to distinguish eggs or metacercariae from members of the Opisthorchiidae and Heterophyidae families, we recommend to implement the PCR-RFLP molecular approach presented in this study in epidemiological surveys aiming to identify fish species involved in Amphimerus sp. life cycle

The high prevalence of 35.8% in the present study corresponds to a population of mestizo inhabitants ... who instead of being connected by rivers, live in a human-populated, dry forest landscape ... where use of fish as a daily food source is secondary.
264.Rationale: The passage shows (1) multiple locally edible fish species were infected with Amphimerus metacercariae, (2) morphology alone cannot reliably distinguish these larvae from other small trematode metacercariae (undermining detection), and (3) a high human prevalence exists even where fish are not a primary daily food source. Together these statements support the conclusion that widespread, morphologically similar metacercariae in many fish species can sustain transmission and make detection harder, enabling continued human infection despite lower overall fish consumption.

264.Option: The presence of multiple fish species as second intermediate hosts increases the likelihood of zoonotic spillover, enabling transmission even in the absence of definitive animal hosts
Evidence: All fish species were positive for Amphimerus sp. metacercariae.

we have identified molecularly adult Amphimerus sp. from feces of humans and bile ducts of naturally infected domestic animals, whose DNA sequences match those of Amphimerus sp. from the province of Esmeraldas
Opossums play a role in the sylvatic maintenance of A. neotropicalis and A. pseudofelineus in Latin America [42] and might be incriminated in the life cycle of Amphimerus sp. in Ecuador as well.
264.Rationale: While multiple fish species as second intermediate hosts do increase opportunities for zoonotic exposure, the statement that this enables transmission 'even in the absence of definitive animal hosts' is not supported. The life cycle described requires definitive hosts (humans, domestic animals or wildlife) to harbor adult flukes and produce eggs that continue the cycle. The passage documents adult infections in humans and domestic animals and suggests wildlife hosts may contribute; it does not support sustained transmission without definitive hosts.

264.Option: The consumption of raw fish, even in small quantities, ensures consistent exposure to infective metacercariae and maintains transmission regardless of fish species diversity
Evidence: however, when freshwater fish are eaten, they are prepared as raw fish with salt and lemon juice (Fig 5).

Consumption of infected fish to sustain or complement diets for the populations in Esmeraldas and Manabí, respectively, is an ideal means of maintaining the life cycle of Amphimerus sp. and potentially other trematodes [13,45].
The high prevalence of 35.8% in the present study corresponds to a population ... where use of fish as a daily food source is secondary.
264.Rationale: The passage indicates that residents prepare freshwater fish as raw dishes and explicitly states that consumption of infected fish is an ideal means of maintaining transmission. The observation of high prevalence even where fish are secondary suggests that even intermittent or small-quantity consumption of raw fish can provide sufficient exposure to sustain human infections. Combined with the fact many fish species are infected, raw fish consumption—however infrequent—critically sustains transmission.

264.Option: The high prevalence of small trematode eggs in human populations is primarily due to the use of contaminated water sources for fish collection, which sustains the life cycle across communities
Evidence: Briefly, eggs arriving into freshwater water sources (e.g., streams, lakes, ponds) are ingested by prosobranch molluscs, the first intermediate hosts...

Consumption of infected fish to sustain or complement diets for the populations in Esmeraldas and Manabí, respectively, is an ideal means of maintaining the life cycle of Amphimerus sp. and potentially other trematodes [13,45].
The high prevalence of 35.8% in the present study corresponds to a population of mestizo inhabitants ... where use of fish as a daily food source is secondary.
264.Rationale: Although the parasite life cycle involves eggs entering freshwater and snail and fish intermediate hosts, the passage attributes sustained human infection primarily to consumption of infected fish and the presence of metacercariae in edible fish species. The text does not present use of 'contaminated water sources for fish collection' as the primary driver of high human prevalence; rather, it emphasizes fish consumption practices (raw preparation) and infected fish availability as central factors. Therefore the option's claim that contaminated water for fish collection is the primary cause is not supported by the passage.

265.Option: The geographic overlap of human and animal cases in Sudan provides epidemiological evidence for cross-species transmission of actinomycetoma
Evidence: The patients were from Sudan, except for a patient from Yemen.

These sequences also formed strongly supported clades with reference rRNA sequences in the phylogenetic analyses (Fig 1A and 1C). ... Isolates related to S. somaliensis (Fig 1A), as well as potentially novel actinomycetoma agents MRC002 and MRC012, were part of clades with 16S rRNA identified in Sudan soil samples [48], consistent with the described primary route of infection by traumatic inoculation of bacteria from the environment.

The clade including the potentially novel MRC012, and the clade including S. sudanensis isolates, both also included 16S rRNA sequences from strains isolated by Sengupta, Goodfellow and Hamid from lesions of donkeys with fistulous withers in Sudan (Fig 1C and 1E; NCBI accessions: EU544241.1, EU544239.1), suggesting that these strains may be capable of infecting multiple hosts thereby raising the possibility of cross-species transmission of actinomycetoma.
265.Rationale: While the passage documents that both human cases and donkey isolates come from Sudan, geographic co-occurrence alone is weak epidemiological evidence for cross-species transmission. Stronger inference requires direct evidence of shared strains (genetic similarity) or temporally/space-linked transmission chains. The passage itself uses genetic/clade similarity to raise the possibility of cross-species transmission rather than relying on geography alone.

265.Option: The ability of S. sudanensis and S. kordofanensis to cause disease in both humans and animals indicates that they are naturally adapted to multiple hosts
Evidence: The clade including the potentially novel MRC012, and the clade including S. sudanensis isolates, both also included 16S rRNA sequences from strains isolated by Sengupta, Goodfellow and Hamid from lesions of donkeys with fistulous withers in Sudan (Fig 1C and 1E; NCBI accessions: EU544241.1, EU544239.1), suggesting that these strains may be capable of infecting multiple hosts thereby raising the possibility of cross-species transmission of actinomycetoma.

Streptomyces kordofanensis (kor.do.fan.en’ sis. N. L. masc. adj. kordofanensis, belonging to Kordofan, the source of the isolate). ... The type and only strain, MRC013T, was isolated from granulomatous material of mycetoma lesions of a patient in the Sudan.
265.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that some Streptomyces lineages (e.g., S. sudanensis-related clades) include sequences from donkey lesions, supporting their potential to infect multiple hosts. However, there is no evidence in the text that S. kordofanensis (MRC013) has been isolated from animals. The statement therefore overgeneralizes beyond the data; only certain lineages show cross-host genetic matches, not demonstrated for S. kordofanensis.

265.Option: The presence of identical antibiotic resistance genes in actinomycetoma isolates from humans and donkeys suggests a shared transmission route across species
Evidence: we scanned the whole genome sequences for known antibiotic resistance determinants using AMRfinderPLUS (S3 Fig). This indicated the presence of rifampicin resistance genes in A. madurae, a result in agreement with the observed IC profiles.

The clade including the potentially novel MRC012, and the clade including S. sudanensis isolates, both also included 16S rRNA sequences from strains isolated by Sengupta, Goodfellow and Hamid from lesions of donkeys with fistulous withers in Sudan (Fig 1C and 1E; NCBI accessions: EU544241.1, EU544239.1), suggesting that these strains may be capable of infecting multiple hosts thereby raising the possibility of cross-species transmission of actinomycetoma.
265.Rationale: The passage describes antibiotic-resistance gene screening for the clinical isolates sequenced (human isolates) but does not report antibiotic resistance gene data for the donkey isolates. There is no quoted evidence of identical resistance genes shared between human and donkey isolates. Therefore, the presence of identical AMR genes across species is not supported by the provided text and cannot be taken as evidence of cross-species transmission here.

265.Option: The shared phenotypic characteristics of actinomycetoma in humans and donkeys, such as granulomatous lesions and sinus formation, support the hypothesis of cross-species transmission
Evidence: Mycetoma is characterised by a painless mass with multiple sinuses which discharge material including grains which are colonies of the causal agents.
There is also evidence that Streptomyces can cause mycetoma in animals [25,26], as well as fistulous withers in donkeys [27].

The clade including the potentially novel MRC012, and the clade including S. sudanensis isolates, both also included 16S rRNA sequences from strains isolated by Sengupta, Goodfellow and Hamid from lesions of donkeys with fistulous withers in Sudan (Fig 1C and 1E; NCBI accessions: EU544241.1, EU544239.1), suggesting that these strains may be capable of infecting multiple hosts thereby raising the possibility of cross-species transmission of actinomycetoma.
265.Rationale: Phenotypic similarity (granulomatous lesions, sinuses) is consistent with similar disease manifestations across hosts, but such shared clinical features are not strong evidence of cross-species transmission because many different organisms can produce comparable lesions. The passage relies on genetic sequence similarity between human and donkey isolates as the more compelling line of evidence, not phenotype alone.

265.Option: Genetic similarity between actinomycetoma isolates from human patients and those from donkeys with fistulous withers supports the possibility of cross-species transmission
Evidence: The clade including the potentially novel MRC012, and the clade including S. sudanensis isolates, both also included 16S rRNA sequences from strains isolated by Sengupta, Goodfellow and Hamid from lesions of donkeys with fistulous withers in Sudan (Fig 1C and 1E; NCBI accessions: EU544241.1, EU544239.1), suggesting that these strains may be capable of infecting multiple hosts thereby raising the possibility of cross-species transmission of actinomycetoma.

The identification of isolates of S. sudanensis and S. warraenensis with similarity to strains previously isolated from donkey withers by Sengupta, Goodfellow and Hamid (NCBI accessions EU544231.1 to EU544241.1) suggests that some actinomycetoma causing strains may have the potential to infect multiple host species.
265.Rationale: The strongest evidence presented in the passage for cross-species transmission is the genetic/phylogenetic similarity: human clinical isolates cluster in clades that include sequences from donkey lesions. The authors explicitly interpret these shared sequences as suggesting the strains "may be capable of infecting multiple hosts," making genetic similarity the most direct and persuasive line of evidence for potential cross-species transmission.

266.Option: Temporal clustering of infections in the same year led to genetic homogenization, preventing the detection of geographic differentiation in northern Ethiopia
Evidence: There was however, no indication for temporal clustering as we still found high inbreeding for NE strains isolated in the same year but with no significant differentiation between different years of isolation.

When the strains from SE were divided into two groups, SE-2007 and SE-2008, and strains from NE into three groups, NE-2007, NE-2008 and NE-2009, according to the year of their isolation, the FIS for each division remained clearly positive.
266.Rationale: The passage explicitly states there was no indication of temporal clustering and that high inbreeding persisted for NE strains isolated in the same year. Thus temporal clustering causing homogenization is contradicted by the authors' analyses and statements and is not the most likely explanation for the inability to detect differentiation.

266.Option: The use of microsatellite markers with limited discriminatory power prevented the detection of genetic differences between strains isolated in the same year and geographic focus in northern Ethiopia
Evidence: Microsatellite markers have proved to be the most powerful tools for population genetic studies in Leishmania [16], [26], [27].

Highly discriminatory methods differentiating the parasites at the strain level are needed to answer key epidemiological and clinical questions associated with VL in East Africa. The MLMT approach has been shown to combine high discriminatory power with good reproducibility allowing the comparison of profiles identified in different laboratories and at different times [26], [27].
266.Rationale: The passage argues the opposite: microsatellite-based MLMT is highly polymorphic and has strong discriminatory power and reproducibility. Therefore, limited marker resolution is not the likely reason for failure to detect within‑year, within‑focus differentiation in the north.

266.Option: The presence of high inbreeding in northern Ethiopia led to reduced genetic diversity, which masked detectable genetic differentiation between strains isolated in the same year and geographic focus
Evidence: In both East African populations identified herein, a considerable deficit of heterozygotes (He > Ho, FIS >0) was observed for both single-locus and multilocus data (Table S3).

When the strains from SE were divided into two groups, SE-2007 and SE-2008, and strains from NE into three groups, NE-2007, NE-2008 and NE-2009, according to the year of their isolation, the FIS for each division remained clearly positive.
266.Rationale: Although high inbreeding (positive FIS) is documented, the authors treat inbreeding as a phenomenon to explain heterozygote deficits rather than as the primary reason for inability to detect geographic differentiation within NE. The passage points instead to issues of sample origin misclassification (travel) and reports that subdivision analyses did not reveal significant differentiation between the NE subdivisions. Thus high inbreeding alone is not presented as the main cause masking geographic differentiation.

266.Option: Misclassification of infection location due to frequent travel between foci obscured true genetic differentiation in northern Ethiopia
Evidence: We did not re-calculate FIS values for the NE strains isolated in the same year in same focus. Because most of our patients were soldiers and immigrant workers and had to travel frequently the two foci Humera and Metema, it was impossible to accurately record where they got infected.
no correlation could be found between the geographical origin of strains and the two subpopulations and the 5 clusters in population NE/SD.

Strong genetic differentiation (FST = 0.56; p<.0001) was only found between NE and SE similar to what was obtained for NE/SD and SE/KE. The FST values between the different subdivisions were not significant (data not shown).
266.Rationale: The authors explicitly state that frequent travel by patients (soldiers and immigrant workers) made it impossible to accurately record infection location between the two northern foci (Humera and Metema). This misclassification of infection origin would obscure true geographic patterns and plausibly explain the inability to detect significant differentiation among strains purportedly from the same year and focus in the north.

266.Option: The Wahlund effect due to unmeasured micro-geographic sub-structuring was responsible for the lack of detectable genetic differentiation in northern Ethiopia
Evidence: To determine whether heterozygote deficiency could be attributed to population sub-structuring, the Wahlund effect, the FIS values were re-calculated for each subpopulation and cluster defined by STRUCTURE (Table S4). This resulted in only a slight reduction of FIS values for the subpopulations NE/SD-A and –B.

For the BAPS clusters of the population NE/SD the FIS values were lower than those estimated for the NE/SD subpopulations and clusters inferred by STRUCTURE, indicating that the latter were at least partly due to population subdivision (Wahlund effect).

We did not re-calculate FIS values for the NE strains isolated in the same year in same focus. Because most of our patients were soldiers and immigrant workers and had to travel frequently the two foci Humera and Metema, it was impossible to accurately record where they got infected.
266.Rationale: While the Wahlund effect (population sub-structuring) was considered and appears to partly explain some heterozygote deficits in NE/SD (BAPS clusters reduced FIS), the authors emphasize that misclassification of infection location due to frequent travel between foci prevents accurate assignment of geographic origin. Thus Wahlund effect is not described as the primary reason for the lack of detectable differentiation among strains from the same year and focus in the north.

267.Option: The increased risk of severe infection among individuals with intermediate and high IgG levels reflects a higher likelihood of re-infection with a more virulent strain of O. tsutsugamushi, which is more likely to cause severe disease regardless of prior immunity.
Evidence: Given the great strain diversity of O. tsutsugamushi, repeated infection with a variety of genetic variants may be a common phenomenon, possibly explaining some recent findings regarding the sero-epidemiology of scrub typhus.

The study is further limited by lack of data on which strains of O. tsutsugamushi caused scrub typhus in this study. Variations in strain diversity across settings, and the sequence of strains causing primary infection and re-infection may influence the risk of severe infection [32]
267.Rationale: While the passage explicitly acknowledges that strain diversity and sequence of strains could influence risk of severe infection (supporting the plausibility of the ‘‘more virulent strain’’ explanation), this option does not represent an immune-enhancement mechanism analogous to antibody-dependent enhancement described for other infections. The question asks for the interpretation most consistent with immune enhancement mechanisms; the strain-virulence explanation is an alternative (non–immune-enhancement) hypothesis and therefore not the best fit to the specific concept of immune enhancement.

267.Option: The observed association between higher initial IgG levels and increased severity is best explained by a stronger immune response triggered by more severe infection, leading to higher antibody production as a consequence rather than a cause of disease severity.
Evidence: Despite these considerations, the absence of data on pre-infection IgG levels in the cases included in this study makes it difficult to exclude the possibility of reverse causality as the main limitation of this study.

High bacterial loads in severe cases may accelerate IgG response even in those who have never been infected with O. tsutsugamushi before, which would lead to an observed association between IgG levels and risk of severe infection.
267.Rationale: The passage explicitly raises reverse causality—severe infection causing a stronger/faster antibody response—as a credible alternative explanation, and provides mechanistic text about high bacterial loads accelerating IgG. However, this interpretation is not an immune-enhancement mechanism (it treats antibodies as a consequence of severe disease, not a prior factor that enhances severity). The question asks which interpretation aligns with immune enhancement phenomena seen in other diseases, so reverse causality is an alternative explanation but not the immune-enhancement interpretation requested.

267.Option: The association between higher IgG levels and increased severity is due to the presence of maternal antibodies in young children, which may interfere with protective immune responses and lead to more severe disease upon infection.
Evidence: These may include in vitro studies, for example by using cell culture. Clinical studies exploring IgG antibody levels in mothers of very young scrub typhus cases may be used to estimate the effect of maternal antibodies on the severity of infection in the child.

In cross-sectional surveys conducted in endemic areas in south India, IgG antibody levels against O. tsutsugamushi show a bimodal distribution with peaks at very low and very high levels (the latter are often regarded as “sero-positive”)
267.Rationale: The passage suggests that maternal-antibody studies could be useful future work, indicating the authors considered maternal antibodies as a possible area to explore. However, the study findings and the discussion emphasize prior personal infection and antibody-dependent enhancement (ADE) analogies (dengue), not maternal antibody interference as the primary biologically plausible explanation. There is no direct evidence in the text that maternal antibodies are driving the observed association in this dataset, so this option is not the interpretation most consistent with immune enhancement mechanisms discussed in the paper.

267.Option: The association between higher initial IgG levels and increased severity is most plausibly explained by antibody-dependent enhancement from prior infection, similar to mechanisms observed in dengue fever
Evidence: If IgG antibodies to O. tsutsugamushi in the early phase of illness largely reflect pre-infection levels, then our findings may suggest that a scrub typhus infection in the past may increase the risk of severe infection in subsequent episodes.
We therefore believe that the data are compatible with prior scrub typhus infection enhancing the severity of subsequent infection.
In dengue fever, prior infection is known to cause more severe subsequent infections, through a mechanism called antibody-dependent enhancement.
Whether scrub typhus infection is modified by antibody-dependent enhancement or other mechanisms enhancing subsequent episodes has not been systematically studied.
267.Rationale: The passage explicitly frames the observed association in terms compatible with immune-enhancement phenomena: if early IgG largely reflects prior infection, prior infection could increase the risk of severe subsequent infection. The authors draw a direct analogy to dengue's antibody-dependent enhancement and state the data are compatible with prior infection enhancing severity, while acknowledging the mechanism has not been proven for scrub typhus. Thus, interpreting the association as potentially due to antibody-dependent enhancement best matches the biological plausibility discussed in the text.

267.Option: The higher IgG levels observed in severe cases are a result of delayed diagnosis and prolonged antigen exposure, leading to a more robust and sustained humoral response that correlates with disease severity.
Evidence: Both the 33rd and the 67th fitted OD percentiles showed a gradual increase with longer duration of fever (Fig 2B). For the median percentile, there was initially a gradual increase with an accelerated increase after day 7.

Because of higher IgG OD values in cases presenting later for testing (Fig 2B), we conducted a subgroup analysis in those where the ELISA test was done within 7 days of fever onset vs those in whom the test was done later.

In those tested within 7 days, the difference in risk between the lowest and the other two categories was higher than in those tested after 7 days of fever (Table 3). Overall, the effect of initially intermediate or high IgG levels on the risk of severe infection appeared attenuated in those tested after 7 days of fever
267.Rationale: The passage documents that IgG rises with longer duration of fever and that the association between initial IgG and severity is attenuated in those tested later, consistent with delayed testing/prolonged antigen exposure producing higher IgG (an alternative explanation). However, this explanation attributes higher IgG to the timing and severity of the current infection (reverse causality), not to a prior immune-enhancement mechanism. The question asked which interpretation aligns with immune enhancement mechanisms; therefore, while delayed diagnosis/prolonged exposure is plausible and supported by the data, it is not the immune-enhancement interpretation and so is not the best answer to the question.

268.Option: The expression of Rp-piwi2, Rp-piwi3, and Rp-ago3 in both germline and somatic tissues suggests a broader functional role that may increase vulnerability to environmental stressors in Rhodnius compared to species with germline-restricted expression
Evidence: Rp-piwi2 transcripts can be detected both in the somatic as well as in the germ cells and seem to display an asymmetric localization pattern during oocyte development.

the Rp-piwi3 and Rp-ago3 transcripts are detected in the region of the tropharium hosting the polyploid Ts and in the ooplasm of newly formed and mature egg chambers, but not in the follicle cells (Fig 4D and 4E).
268.Rationale: The option asserts that all three genes (Rp-piwi2, Rp-piwi3, and Rp-ago3) are expressed in both germline and somatic tissues; the passage shows only Rp-piwi2 is detected in both somatic and germ cells, whereas Rp-piwi3 and Rp-ago3 are reported as germline-specific and “not in the follicle cells.” Therefore the premise of the option is factually incorrect based on the passage, weakening its conclusion about broad functional role across all three genes and any inferred increased vulnerability.

268.Option: The presence of a lineage-specific PolyQ tract in Rp-Piwi2 may confer unique functional dependence on this protein for fertility, increasing susceptibility to reproductive failure in Triatomine species compared to those lacking the tract
Evidence: the N-terminal region of the putative Rp-Piwi2 protein features a stretch of 18 Glutamine residues (i.e. polyQ tract) (Fig 3A and S2 Fig).

Using NCBI Blast search analyses, we found that the polyQ tract is present in Piwi-like proteins encoded in the genome of Rhodnius neglectus, Triatoma infestans and Triatoma dimidiata (Fig 3B), but it is absent in PIWI proteins of Drosophila and other animals (Fig 3A and S2 Fig).
Our functional studies using pRNAi against the Rp-piwi2 gene resulted in oogenesis arrest and complete female adult sterility.

Thus, the PolyQ tract is likely a recent acquisition in the evolution of the PIWI proteins and, based on the available sequenced genomes, appears to be restricted to blood-feeding insects of the Triatomine family.
268.Rationale: The passage documents (1) a lineage-restricted PolyQ tract unique to Rp-Piwi2 and conserved among Triatomine relatives, and (2) that Rp-piwi2 knockdown causes oogenesis arrest and essentially complete female sterility. Together these facts strongly support that this lineage-specific molecular feature (the PolyQ tract in Rp-Piwi2) corresponds to a unique functional dependence of fertility on Rp-Piwi2 in Triatomines and thus plausibly underlies differential susceptibility to reproductive failure across species that lack this feature.

268.Option: The absence of the RDC complex in Rhodnius, except for a putative Rp-Rai1l protein, suggests that the piRNA pathway is less robust in Triatomine species, making them more susceptible to reproductive failure than Drosophila
Evidence: We did not find evidence of an RDC complex in Rhodnius except for a putative protein (i.e. Rp-Rai1l) with similarity to Cuff.
If piRNA clusters exist in this species, it is likely that their regulation relies on a set of proteins different from the one described in Drosophila.
268.Rationale: While the passage states the canonical RDC complex components described for Drosophila are not evident in Rhodnius (except a Rai1-like gene), it does not claim that this makes the piRNA pathway less robust or that it increases susceptibility to reproductive failure. Instead, the authors suggest the regulatory machinery may differ: “their regulation relies on a set of proteins different from the one described in Drosophila.” The passage provides no direct evidence that absence of the Drosophila-style RDC complex causes greater susceptibility, so the option over-interprets the data.

268.Option: The high expression of hsp83 and uap56 in Rhodnius ovaries indicates a reliance on chaperone and RNA export functions that may make these species more vulnerable to proteotoxic stress during oogenesis compared to Drosophila
Evidence: we immediately noticed that the heat shock protein 83 (hsp83) and uap56 gene, which encode a nuclear-cytoplasmic RNA export factor, are expressed at higher levels (>500 RPKM) than other piRNA pathway components in Rhodnius ovaries (Fig 2A).
268.Rationale: The passage documents high expression of hsp83 and uap56 in Rhodnius ovaries, but it does not link this observation to an increased vulnerability to proteotoxic stress or reproductive failure relative to other species. High expression could reflect importance or adaptation rather than susceptibility. There is no direct evidence in the text that reliance on these factors confers differential susceptibility, so the option is speculative and not strongly supported by the provided passage.

268.Option: The presence of a mariner-like transposase in the intron of Rp-vas suggests that genomic instability may be a key factor in reproductive failure, making Triatomine species more susceptible than those without such elements
Evidence: this arrangement is absent in Rhodnius, where the Rp-vas intron 1 harbors an Open Reading Frame (ORF) encoding a transposase enzyme from a mariner-like element (Fig 2D). Transcripts of this ORF are readily detected in our ovarian transcriptome datasets.
The percentage of transposable elements in the Rhodnius genome is approximately 6% and two thirds of the transposons in this species belong to the mariner family [41,50,51].
It is tempting to speculate that the DNA damage and the loss of Ts observed in the Rp-piwi2 KD ovaries might be caused by the deregulation of transposable elements.
268.Rationale: The passage notes the presence and transcription of a mariner-like transposase within Rp-vas intron and that mariner elements are abundant in the Rhodnius genome; the authors also speculate that deregulation of transposons could underlie DNA damage and germ cell loss observed after Rp-piwi2 knockdown. However, this is presented as a hypothesis rather than demonstrated evidence, and the passage does not show that such intronic transposase insertions are unique to Triatomines or directly link them to differential susceptibility across related species. Compared with the direct functional evidence linking Rp-piwi2 to sterility (and its lineage-specific PolyQ), the transposase presence is a plausible but weaker and more speculative mechanism; therefore this option is not the strongest-supported choice.

269.Option: Airborne transmission of C. burnetii via windborne aerosols enables long-distance spread independent of local study coverage
Evidence: Transmission to human most frequently occurs through inhalation of aerosolized bacteria that are spread in the environment by infected animals after delivery or abortion. Such windborne outbreaks can affect dozens to hundreds of people who may not have direct exposure to the infected animals.

The ability of Q fever pathogenic agents (Coxiella burnetii) to be transmitted from animal reservoirs to humans by inhalation of infected aerosols makes coxiellosis easily transmissible among different organisms over large geographical areas [165].
Tissot-Dupont [166] concurs and opines that some Q fever outbreaks are related directly to the speed and frequency of wind.
Ticks are considered second only to mosquitoes as important vectors of human infectious diseases across the world [99].
269.Rationale: The passage clearly documents airborne, windborne spread for Coxiella burnetii and notes that infected aerosols can affect people far from the source—so airborne spread supports wide dissemination of Q fever specifically. However, the question asks about transmission of diseases transmitted by ixodid ticks and caused by diverse pathogens (bacteria, viruses, protozoa). The cited airborne mechanism is specific to C. burnetii and its environmental aerosol route, not a general transmission route for the wide set of tick-borne pathogens carried by ixodid ticks. Therefore while the quoted text supports long-distance spread of C. burnetii, it does not generalize to the suite of tick‑borne agents the question addresses, so this option is not the strongest supporting factor.

269.Option: The presence of multiple tick species in Iran, including those capable of transmitting pathogens, indicates that transmission can occur across diverse ecological zones despite limited study coverage
Evidence: Ticks are considered second only to mosquitoes as important vectors of human infectious diseases across the world [99].
The obligate hematophagous arthropods parasitize every class of vertebrates in almost every region of the world [15].

According to Shemshad et al. [103], a number of Ixodid tick fauna obtained from cattle, sheep, goats, dogs and even shrubbery have been identified in different parts of Iran. The ticks include: Haemaphysalis concinna, H. sulcata, Hyalomma anatolicum, Hy. asiaticum, Hy. detritum, Hy. dromedarii, Hy. marginatum, Hy. schulzei, Rhipicephalus bursa, R. sanguineus, Ixodes ricinus and Dermacentor spp.

The presence of a number of Ixodid ticks in Iran therefore implies that there could be many natural foci of tickborne zoonoses in the country from which they may spread to other areas under changing socio-economic and climatic conditions.
269.Rationale: The passage documents a diverse and geographically distributed ixodid tick fauna in Iran and explicitly links that presence to the existence of multiple natural foci and spread potential. Because ixodid ticks are the primary vectors for many bacterial, protozoal and viral pathogens of vertebrates, broad tick species distribution across ecological zones is the most general ecological/epidemiologic factor supporting widespread transmission potential despite limited study coverage.

269.Option: The detection of C. burnetii in animal products such as milk and eggs suggests that the pathogen can be widely disseminated through food chains, enabling transmission across regions despite limited study coverage
Evidence: As regards animal products, a total of 5 (9.4%) of all the Q fever studies investigated C. burnetii antibodies in bulk milk samples collected from commercial dairy cattle and goats, while one study (1.9%) reported on poultry eggs collected from chicken, ducks, goose, quails and ostriches drawn from three provinces (Isfaham, Gilan and Mazandaran) in Iran.

This makes it possible for the pathogen to survive for days and weeks in animal products such as milk, cream, butter and cheese, posing a risk of pathogen transmission to humans through consumption of raw animal products like milk and eggs [215].
Cases of human Q fever outbreaks associated with consumption of dairy products have been reported in many parts of the world.
269.Rationale: The passage supports dissemination of C. burnetii via contaminated animal products and documents its survival in milk and eggs, which can spread Q fever to humans. However, this mechanism is specific to C. burnetii and food-borne exposure, and does not apply broadly to the full range of ixodid-tick–transmitted agents (many of which require vector bite transmission rather than foodborne routes). Thus while relevant for Q fever, it is not the strongest general factor supporting widespread transmission of diverse tick-borne pathogens.

269.Option: The presence of multiple tick-borne pathogens in the same geographic regions suggests that transmission foci are interconnected and capable of spreading across Iran through shared vector populations
Evidence: 9 of the 22 provinces had up to three diseases (anaplasmosis, ehrlichiosis and coxiellosis) reported jointly within a single province.

The presence of various pathogens was confirmed by molecular, serological and microscopic techniques conducted on specimens obtained from humans, ticks, domestic animals and animal products (milk and eggs) drawn from a number of provinces in Iran.
For each bacterial disease, one or several tick vectors and one or several reservoirs may exist [100].

Pathogens that transmit tickborne zoonoses rely on a number of ticks in the family Ixodidae (the hard ticks) among them: Amblyomma, Dermacentor, Haemaphysalis, Hyalomma and Rhipicephalus as vectors and vertebrates such as small mammals, sheep or deer as their reservoir hosts [101].
269.Rationale: The passage documents co‑occurrence of multiple tick‑borne diseases within the same provinces and emphasizes that multiple pathogens share tick vectors and reservoir hosts. That spatial overlap of pathogens and vectors supports the interpretation that transmission foci are interconnected via shared vector populations, enabling multi‑pathogen transmission potential to expand across regions even if formal studies are geographically limited. Therefore this option is a valid supporting factor.

269.Option: The high seroprevalence of C. burnetii in domestic ruminants across multiple provinces suggests that the pathogen is uniformly distributed and likely to be present in all regions of Iran
Evidence: Individual pooled prevalence among domestic ruminants was lowest (0.83–22.3%) in cattle ... and highest (22.4–65.78%) in goats ... individual pooled prevalence among sheep was intermediate of the two at 19.5–36.5%.

Geographically, over half (57.1%) the studies in this review reported on coxiellosis in 19 of the 31 provinces in Iran. However, the distribution of these studies was not uniform as most studies were concentrated in two provinces (Kerman and Sistan & Baluchestan), both of which are located to the southeastern part of Iran. This skewed distribution excludes 12 provinces which collectively cover a substantial portion of the country.
This makes it difficult to generalize the findings emanating from the reviewed articles may not reflect the actual epidemiology and prevalence of Q fever in Iran.
269.Rationale: While some studies report high C. burnetii seroprevalence in certain domestic ruminants and provinces, the review emphasizes the skewed and limited geographic sampling and explicitly cautions that findings cannot be generalized to the whole country. Thus high localized seroprevalence does not prove uniform distribution nationwide, so this option overstates what the data support.

270.Option: The parasite relies on high-fidelity RNA editing to preserve mitochondrial gene integrity during periods of metabolic inactivity in the mammalian host
Evidence: During this stage of the life cycle, the mitochondrion is down-regulated, lacking both Krebs cycle enzymes and a functional electron transport chain (ETC) [4].
This unique lifecycle leads to a major problem: when the mitochondrial genes are unused, they are not under selection, hence the integrity of these genes are threatened by genetic drift [5, 6].
This sequential dependence means that with even high accuracy rates for each gRNA, the overall fidelity of the process is astonishingly low.
270.Rationale: The option claims RNA editing is high-fidelity and preserves mitochondrial gene integrity during mammalian metabolic inactivity. The passage explicitly states the mitochondrion is down-regulated in the mammalian bloodstream and that unused mitochondrial genes are threatened by genetic drift. Crucially, it also states that "the overall fidelity of the process is astonishingly low," which contradicts the claim of high-fidelity RNA editing as the preserving mechanism. Thus the option is not supported by the passage.

270.Option: The parasite uses antigenic variation to maintain mitochondrial gene stability during the mammalian phase of its life cycle
Evidence: The salivarian trypanosomes (e.g. T. brucei, T. vivax) are especially interesting, because they are exclusively extracellular in their mammalian hosts, continuously replicating within the bloodstream over periods of months.

These parasites undergo multiple bottlenecks at each antigenic switch, as they evade the host immune system [7]. Such bottlenecks create additional forces of genetic drift, where genes can be lost even if their deleterious fitness effect is considerable.
270.Rationale: Antigenic switching is described in the passage as producing bottlenecks that "create additional forces of genetic drift," which would threaten, not maintain, mitochondrial gene stability. The passage does not present antigenic variation as a mechanism for maintaining mitochondrial gene integrity; rather it increases drift pressure. Therefore this option is incorrect.

270.Option: The parasite maintains mitochondrial gene function by expressing ETC genes only in the tsetse fly vector, avoiding mutational drift in the mammalian host
Evidence: During this stage of the life cycle, the mitochondrion is down-regulated, lacking both Krebs cycle enzymes and a functional electron transport chain (ETC) [4].
Successful transition to the fly vector, requires activation of the ETC and ATP synthesis via oxidative phosphorylation.
This unique lifecycle leads to a major problem: when the mitochondrial genes are unused, they are not under selection, hence the integrity of these genes are threatened by genetic drift [5, 6].
270.Rationale: While it is true that the ETC is active in the fly and down-regulated in the mammalian bloodstream, the passage explicitly states that when mitochondrial genes are unused they are "not under selection" and thus "threatened by genetic drift." Simply restricting ETC expression to the vector does not avoid mutational drift; on the contrary, that pattern creates the non-selective periods that permit drift. The passage instead explores mechanisms (e.g., dual-coding, alternative editing, gene fragmentation) that protect genes despite those non-selective periods. Thus the option's implication that restricting expression avoids drift is incorrect.

270.Option: The parasite maintains essential mitochondrial genes through dual-coding architecture, where non-selected ETC genes are preserved by being embedded within a reading frame under selection in both host environments
Evidence: This unique lifecycle leads to a major problem: when the mitochondrial genes are unused, they are not under selection, hence the integrity of these genes are threatened by genetic drift [5, 6].
Another level of complexity in the kinetoplastids RNA editing process was the detection of an alternative editing event that leads to the production of a functionally discrete protein isoform.
We hypothesize that trypanosomes use dual-coding genes to protect genetic information by essentially hiding a non-selected (ETC) gene within one that remains under selection.

The work presented here, suggests that as many as six of the extensively edited mRNAs in T. brucei are dual-coding and that it is alternative editing using different terminal gRNAs that allows access to the two different reading frames.
COIII... contains the information for two distinct proteins, COIII and AEP-1. AEP-1 is important for kinetoplastid DNA maintenance...
270.Rationale: The passage directly frames the problem (mitochondrial genes not under selection during mammalian parasitemia and thus vulnerable to genetic drift) and states the central hypothesis that dual-coding genes protect genetic information by "hiding a non-selected (ETC) gene within one that remains under selection." It presents evidence of alternative editing producing distinct proteins (e.g., COIII/AEP-1) and identifies multiple pan-edited mRNAs as dual-coding, with alternative terminal gRNAs enabling access to different reading frames. This mechanism matches the option's description and is supported by multiple explicit statements in the passage.

270.Option: The parasite uses RNA editing to generate multiple protein isoforms from a single gene, allowing functional redundancy that buffers against genetic drift
Evidence: Another level of complexity in the kinetoplastids RNA editing process was the detection of an alternative editing event that leads to the production of a functionally discrete protein isoform.
Alternative editing of Cytochrome Oxidase III (COIII) is reported to generate a novel DNA-binding protein, AEP-1, that functions in mitochondrial DNA maintenance [11, 12].
We hypothesize that trypanosomes use dual-coding genes to protect genetic information by essentially hiding a non-selected (ETC) gene within one that remains under selection.

Maintaining dual-coding genes is costly, as it constrains the flexibility of the amino acid composition of both proteins. Hence, it is thought that dual-coding genes can survive long evolutionary spans only if the overlap is advantageous to the organism [15].
270.Rationale: The passage does support that RNA editing can generate multiple, functionally distinct protein isoforms (for example COIII and AEP-1). However, the option claims this creates 'functional redundancy' that buffers against genetic drift. The passage describes the alternative isoforms as 'functionally discrete' (distinct functions) and emphasizes that dual-coding is costly and maintained only if advantageous, not that it provides redundant backup. The primary protective model proposed is that dual-coding 'hides' non-selected ETC information within a reading frame under selection, not that isoform redundancy buffers drift. Therefore the statement about functional redundancy as the buffering mechanism is not supported by the passage.

271.Option: Steady, low-level transmission with occasional localized outbreaks in temperate regions, particularly in Europe and North America
Evidence: These three mosquito-borne diseases are mainly endemic in tropical and subtropical regions.
European Region had the lowest seroprevalence of DENV (4%, 95% CI: 0–7).
Additionally, countries without Aedes mosquitoes were excluded, such as Russia, Australia, and countries in North Europe, North America, and etc.
As the most common mosquito-borne disease, dengue has become endemic in 129 countries and has caused almost 390 million infections per year.
271.Rationale: The passage states these arboviruses are mainly endemic in tropical and subtropical regions rather than temperate regions, and gives evidence of very high global burden (endemic in 129 countries, ~390 million infections/year). Europe (a temperate region) is reported to have low seroprevalence, and countries without Aedes (including North America regions) were excluded. Thus the pattern of steady low-level transmission centered in temperate regions is not supported by the cited data.

271.Option: Uniform, moderate transmission across all regions with no significant differences in seroprevalence between countries or populations
Evidence: There were heterogeneous results across regions, with the highest seroprevalence in South-East Asian Region for DENV and CHIKV, and Regions of the Americas for ZIKV.
The seroprevalence of DENV infection was reported in 102 studies consisted of 124,013 individuals from 44 countries and areas. And the pooled seroprevalence was 38% (95% CI: 32–43).

Among regions tested, the South-East Asia Region had the highest seroprevalence among all WHO regions (56%, 95% CI: 39–73); In contrast, European Region had the lowest seroprevalence of DENV (4%, 95% CI: 0–7).
271.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports heterogeneity across regions with large differences in seroprevalence (e.g., 56% in South-East Asia vs. 4% in Europe). Therefore the claim of uniform moderate transmission with no significant regional differences is contradicted by the presented data.

271.Option: Recurring large-scale epidemics with persistent high transmission in tropical and subtropical regions, particularly in South-East Asia
Evidence: As the most common mosquito-borne disease, dengue has become endemic in 129 countries and has caused almost 390 million infections per year.
According to the Global Burden of Disease study, dengue is spreading more rapidly than any other infectious diseases, with a 4-fold increase within 13 years (2000–2013).
Among regions tested, the South-East Asia Region had the highest seroprevalence among all WHO regions (56%, 95% CI: 39–73);
A progressively growing DENV seroprevalence has been observed since 2000, indicating a continuous dengue outbreak around the world.
271.Rationale: The passage documents very large and increasing burden of dengue, high pooled seroprevalence (and especially high seroprevalence in South‑East Asia), and notes a progressive increase since 2000. These findings best match a pattern of recurring large-scale epidemics with persistent high transmission in tropical/subtropical areas (especially South‑East Asia).

271.Option: Cyclical epidemics with long inter-epidemic periods and low seroprevalence in most regions, except for isolated high-burden areas
Evidence: The pooled seroprevalence was 38% (95% CI: 32–43).

Among regions tested, the South-East Asia Region had the highest seroprevalence among all WHO regions (56%, 95% CI: 39–73); In contrast, European Region had the lowest seroprevalence of DENV (4%, 95% CI: 0–7).
A progressively growing DENV seroprevalence has been observed since 2000, indicating a continuous dengue outbreak around the world.
271.Rationale: The data show substantial and broadly distributed seroprevalence (pooled ~38%) and a trend of increasing DENV seroprevalence since 2000, inconsistent with a pattern of generally low seroprevalence and long quiescent inter-epidemic periods. Instead, the evidence supports frequent/recurrent transmission and large outbreaks in multiple endemic regions.

271.Option: Sustained, high-level transmission in urban areas with declining prevalence in rural settings due to improved public health infrastructure
Evidence: Urban area had a higher DENV seroprevalence (39%, 95% CI: 30–49) than that of rural areas (23%, 95% CI: 15–32), but did not achieve a statistical significance based on their 95% CIs.
However, there was no difference between rural and urban areas for the overall seroprevalence of DENV and CHIKV.
Nowadays, with rapid urbanization and accelerated population mobility, the gap between urban and rural areas has narrowed.
271.Rationale: While urban seroprevalence is reported higher numerically, the difference did not reach statistical significance and the authors state no overall rural–urban difference; they also note the rural–urban gap is narrowing. The passage does not provide evidence that rural prevalence is declining due to improved public health infrastructure, so the specific claim of sustained high urban transmission coupled with declining rural prevalence from improved infrastructure is not supported.

272.Option: Media coverage is driven more by the perception of sudden, dramatic events than by the steady, high burden of chronic diseases affecting marginalized populations
Evidence: In general, it is an approach with a marketing bias and one decontextualized from the political and social reality in which it operates and interferes [3].

The words of one journalist interviewed corroborate this affirmation: “[…] We need to sell newspapers. Although we know the importance that is given to the news, of the importance of promoting and discussing it, we also need to sell newspapers."

The health/disease news covered included recent news with a negative slant, news with pessimistic character, and diseases that were most serious and those that affected and killed more people, as evidenced in the speech below: [...] "The parameter [to cover the news] is: is the news recent? Is the news appealing? Will it reach many people?"

In this context of growing interest devoted to media sources, health is largely addressed by the reporting of the extraordinary, disasters, denunciations, advertisements, technological and scientific innovations, diseases of celebrities, and prescriptions of beauty treatments or healthy lifestyles involving individual responsibility and the consumption of goods and services.

Traquina (2008) [23] defined events that are catastrophic, unusual, tragic, and capable of dramatization, personalization, and death itself, to have important news value in the process of the selection and development of news articles.
272.Rationale: The passage repeatedly states that news selection is dominated by newsworthiness criteria favoring recent, dramatic, catastrophic, or emotionally appealing events ("extraordinary, disasters, ... diseases of celebrities") and by market pressures to "sell newspapers." Diseases that are consistently present but lack sudden, dramatic outbreaks therefore attract less coverage despite high burden, making this factor the primary explanation for the reporting–burden discrepancy.

272.Option: The media prioritizes diseases with high case fatality rates, regardless of their overall burden in the population
Evidence: According to Romeyer (2014) [32], the higher the disease severity, the higher its media coverage.

Traquina (2008) [23] defined events that are catastrophic, unusual, tragic, and capable of dramatization, personalization, and death itself, to have important news value in the process of the selection and development of news articles.
A low mortality rate often reduces the impact of the media coverage and its political weight [24].
272.Rationale: The passage notes that severity and deaths can increase news value ("the higher the disease severity, the higher its media coverage") but does not support the claim that the media prioritizes high case-fatality diseases "regardless of overall burden." In fact, the text emphasizes dramatic, recent events and other factors (market logic, novelty, emotional appeal) over simple fatality metrics. Thus while fatality can influence coverage, the option overstates its role and is not the best single explanation.

272.Option: Diseases that are frequently reported in international news are more likely to receive local media attention, regardless of their local burden
Evidence: “[…] no one knew what this disease was (ALS), and then people came to know about it after the ice bucket story."

This example underscores the influence of media coverage of certain diseases on the public health agenda. The case of the “ice bucket challenge”, which resulted in more than 440 million views on social networks, collected USD 115 million in donations for the association that represents patients with ALS and other motor neuron diseases (ALS Association, United States) and tripled investments in research on ALS (BBC News. Available at: http://www.bbc.com/news/health-33640896. Accessed on October 26, 2015.), which is beginning to produce promising results [33].
the media reported 253,442 news stories about swine flu but only 6,501 news stories about tuberculosis.
272.Rationale: The passage gives illustrative examples (the ALS "ice bucket" viral campaign and global swine flu coverage) showing that viral/international attention can drive local coverage. However, it does not claim this is the dominant or general explanation "regardless of local burden;" rather, these are specific instances of alert providers and social amplification. The main explanatory theme in the text is newsworthiness tied to drama/novelty and market logic, so this option is not the best general explanation.

272.Option: Media coverage is primarily influenced by the availability of expert sources and medical professionals willing to comment on the disease
Evidence: In addition, the coverage of diseases that are more well known by journalists prevails in the agendas because of the availability of relevant information and expert sources.

"[…] we have a gynaecologist who is our favourite and who speaks very well, and he is able to ask the patient to wait five minutes in the chair to talk to us. In some cases, he says: ‘I cannot meet with you because this is a more severe case but I will talk to you in 15 minutes.’ So, these doctors end up having priority."
272.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that availability of expert sources affects coverage (the "favorite" gynaecologist example), so this is a contributing factor. However, it is presented as one among several mechanisms (market logic, drama, emotional appeal, editorial routines) and not identified as the primary reason diseases with steady high burden are neglected. Therefore, while true as a partial explanation, it is not the most likely main factor asked for in the question.

272.Option: Diseases with high notification rates are more likely to receive media attention due to their formal reporting requirements
Evidence: It is known that the mandatory reporting of diseases is essential to promote health surveillance and to encourage protective and preventive interventions [27].

The death-related news index and its derivations, the hospitalization-related news index and the notification-related news index, indicate discrepancies that were determined by the number of news stories for every death, hospitalization, or notification relevant to the diseases in question.

Schistosomiasis (1075 notifications) and leishmaniasis (265 notifications and also cited by respondents as important) are endemic in several municipalities in the state [12], which increases political concerns; even then, these diseases were covered by the newspapers only three and two times, respectively.
272.Rationale: While mandatory reporting and notification metrics are discussed and the authors constructed notification-related indices, the passage demonstrates that high notification counts do not necessarily translate into media coverage (e.g., schistosomiasis had many notifications but was covered only a few times). Thus formal reporting requirements alone do not explain the discrepancy and are not the most likely factor.

274.Option: The inability of IL-27 and IL-7 to restore polyfunctional T-cell responses in patients with severe cardiomyopathy
Evidence: IL-27 and IL-7 improve T. cruzi-responsive polyfunctional T cells

In contrast to that observed in subjects without cardiomyopathy, the addition of IL-27 or IL-7 in T. cruzi-stimulated cell cultures of PBMCs derived from those with heart disease (i.e., the G2 group) did not improve the frequencies of monofunctional or polyfunctional CD4+ T-cell responses specific for T. cruzi.

CD4+ T cell responses improved after in vitro treatment with IL-27 and IL-7 only in subjects without signs of heart disease who have a more functional IL-27 and IL-7 axis and less exhausted immune system compared with those in patients with severe cardiomyopathy.
274.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that IL-27 and IL-7 increased polyfunctional and monofunctional T-cell responses in subjects without heart disease (G0) but failed to do so in patients with cardiomyopathy (G2). This inability to restore parasite-specific polyfunctional CD4+ T cells in severe cardiomyopathy is therefore a distinguishing immunological feature between mild and severe disease.

274.Option: The increased frequency of CD8+ T cells expressing both chains of IL-27R in patients with mild disease compared to uninfected controls
Evidence: subjects in the G0 group had an increased frequency of CD8+CD45RA—WSX-1+CD130+ T cells (Fig 1C) along with a decrease in the frequency of CD8+CD45RA—WSX-1—CD130+ T cells (Fig 1D) compared with uninfected subjects.
