An increasing trend by number of infections is direct evidence that pathology is linked to cumulative exposure rather than being attributable solely to a single infection event.

314.Option: Higher mean placental lesion scores in women with P. vivax infection compared to uninfected women, regardless of the number of infections
Evidence: By developing a histology score based on the most significant placental histology parameters associated with P. vivax, we show that women from an endemic region of the Brazilian Amazon, who were diagnosed with P. vivax during pregnancy, had higher levels of placental lesions than non-infected pregnant women and that these lesions increase in frequency with increasing exposure to the parasite.

This formula was applied to all of the samples in the study (Fig. 4A). Placentas belonging to the “vivax” group exhibited higher scores than those from the “no plasmodium” group (Mann-Whitney, P = 0.027).
314.Rationale: While the passage does report that placentas from the vivax group had higher mean scores than uninfected women (Mann-Whitney P=0.027), the option's wording 'regardless of the number of infections' does not address cumulative exposure. The study further reports that scores increase with the number of infections (Cuzick's trend-test), indicating that the number of infections matters. Therefore the mere overall higher mean score versus uninfected does not most strongly support a cumulative-exposure association; the trend by number of infections is the stronger evidence.

314.Option: A statistically significant increase in placental barrier thickness and syncytial knotting in women with P. vivax infection compared to uninfected women, with no evidence of confounding by parity
Evidence: When univariate analyses of each parameter against the species of Plasmodium infection diagnosed during pregnancy were performed (Table 3, and Figs. 1 and 2), a significant increase in the percentage of syncytial knots (ANOVA, P = 0.006) (Figs. 1A and 1B) was observed in the placentas from the P. falciparum-exposed women. The values of syncytial knotting were increased in women in the “falciparum” group versus women in the “no-plasmodium” group, while women in the “vivax” group exhibited intermediate syncytial knotting values.

This same pattern was also observed for the presence of syncytial rupture (ANOVA, P = 0.003) (Figs. 1C and 1D) and the thickness of the placental barrier (ANOVA, P = 0.08) (Figs. 1E and 1F), although no statistical significance was obtained with this last parameter.
No significant differences were observed between the “no plasmodium” and “vivax” groups when they were sub-divided according to parity (data not shown).
Both syncytial knots (z = 2.35; P = 0.019) and placental barrier thickness (z = 2.63; P = 0.008) were elevated with increased frequency of P. vivax infections.
314.Rationale: The passage does report significant increases in syncytial knots and barrier thickness across increasing numbers of P. vivax infections (trend tests), and it notes no significant differences when groups were sub-divided by parity. However, the univariate ANOVA results show that syncytial knotting increases are most pronounced in P. falciparum (vivax values were intermediate) and placental barrier thickness did not reach statistical significance in that ANOVA (P=0.08). Thus the specific claim of a statistically significant increase comparing P. vivax-infected versus uninfected women is not clearly supported; the stronger evidence is the trend with number of infections rather than a simple vivax vs no-plasmodium pairwise significance. Therefore this option overstates the direct vivax vs uninfected pairwise significance.

314.Option: A significant association between P. vivax infection and lower infant birth weight in primigravidae, suggesting that repeated exposure may be more harmful
Evidence: When women in the “no plasmodium” and “vivax” groups were sub-divided into those with one pregnancy (primigravidae) and those with two or more pregnancies (multigravidae) the only significant difference was of birth weight of the primigravidae, with the mean birth weight of infants in the “no plasmodium” group 350 g higher than that of the “vivax” group (student's t-test, P = 0.03)

The biological significance of this finding, although similar to previous results [5], [7], [27], could not be determined in this study since birth weight is a multidimensional outcome that depends not only on exposure and immunity to malaria [28], [29], but also on anthropometric features of both the parents and the infant [30], [31].
314.Rationale: The passage does report a significant lower birth weight among primigravidae with P. vivax infection versus uninfected primigravidae (350 g difference, P=0.03). However, this finding concerns primigravidae (first pregnancy), not repeated exposures; it does not indicate that repeated (cumulative) exposure is more harmful. In fact, the association in primigravidae suggests vulnerability in first pregnancies rather than an effect of cumulative exposure. The authors also caution about interpreting biological significance of birth weight differences because birth weight is influenced by multiple factors. Therefore this option does not correctly identify evidence that placental pathology is linked to cumulative exposure.

314.Option: A significant difference in placental lesion scores between women with one P. vivax infection and those with two or more infections, but not between uninfected women and those with one infection
Evidence: Women were divided according to the number of P. vivax (and not P. falciparum) infections experienced during pregnancy and the following group order was established: “no infection”, “1 infection” and “2+ infections”. All histological parameters were evaluated between these groups and Cuzick's trend test across ordered groups was performed to assess presence of a trend with any of the parameters according to the number of P. vivax infections (Table 4). Both syncytial knots (z = 2.35; P = 0.019) and placental barrier thickness (z = 2.63; P = 0.008) were elevated with increased frequency of P. vivax infections.

This formula was applied to all of the samples in the study (Fig. 4A). Placentas belonging to the “vivax” group exhibited higher scores than those from the “no plasmodium” group (Mann-Whitney, P = 0.027). Also, applying the score to the different groups, according to the number of P. vivax infections experienced during pregnancy, showed an increase in score values with increasing numbers of P. vivax infections (Cuzick's trend-test: z = 2.76; P = 0.006) (Fig. 4B)
314.Rationale: The passage reports a statistically significant monotonic trend across 'no infection', '1 infection', and '2+ infections' groups (Cuzick's test) and a significant overall difference between vivax-exposed and unexposed placentas (Mann-Whitney P=0.027). However, the text does not present pairwise significance results specifically showing that 1 vs 2+ is significant while no infection vs 1 is not. Only the overall trend and the vivax vs no-plasmodium comparison are reported. Because the option asserts a specific pattern of pairwise significance that is not stated in the passage, it cannot be supported.

315.Option: Neutrophil recruitment is the primary determinant of worm burden, with S100A8/A9 acting as a chemotactic signal to enhance early larval clearance
Evidence: S100A8/A9 exhibits chemotactic properties on neutrophils [60] and is expressed in almost 20% of the cytosolic protein content in murine neutrophils and 45% in human neutrophils, and is increasingly expressed in lungs following L. sigmodontis L3 larvae migration [38],

S100A8/A9-/- mice were characterized by an increased number of thoracic cavity cells and elevated concentrations of neutrophil-associated chemokines such as CXCL-1, CXCL-2, CXCL-5, and elastase, which may contribute to the elimination of L. sigmodontis in S100A8/A9-/- mice.
Our study demonstrates that S100A8/A9 facilitates L3 migration.

Comparable total numbers of neutrophils in the thoracic cavity and BAL of S100A8/A9-/- mice upon L. sigmodontis infection is in contrast to previous studies demonstrating a S100A8 and S100A9-dependent recruitment of murine and human phagocytes to the site of inflammation.
315.Rationale: Although S100A8/A9 has chemotactic properties for neutrophils, the passage shows that the presence of S100A8/A9 actually facilitates larval migration and that S100A8/A9-/- mice have decreased worm burden with increased neutrophil-associated chemokines and neutrophil activation. Total neutrophil counts in pleura/BAL were comparable between strains, so simple recruitment driven by S100A8/A9 is not the primary determinant here. Thus the statement that S100A8/A9 acts to enhance early larval clearance via chemotaxis contradicts the observed data that S100A8/A9 presence promotes migration and that its deficiency yields enhanced clearance associated with neutrophil activation.

315.Option: S100A8/A9 promotes vascular permeability to facilitate larval migration through the lung endothelium during early infection
Evidence: Intradermal injection of filarial adult worm extract (LsAg) led to an increased permeability in WT and S100A8/A9-/- animals compared to PBS controls (Fig 6C). However no statistically significant differences were observed between LsAg-treated WT and S100A8/A9-/- mice, suggesting that the absence of S100A8/A9 does not impact vascular permeability.
315.Rationale: The authors directly measured vascular permeability and report no significant difference between WT and S100A8/A9-/- mice after LsAg treatment, concluding that absence of S100A8/A9 does not impact vascular permeability. Therefore, promoting vascular permeability is not supported as the mechanism by which S100A8/A9 facilitates larval migration in this model.

315.Option: The protective effect of S100A8/A9 deficiency is mediated by enhanced eosinophil activation and increased mucus production in the lung
Evidence: Total cell counts of CD4+ T cells, CD8+ T cells, neutrophils, macrophages, and eosinophils in the thoracic cavity were not different between the two mouse strains (Fig 1D).

Lung pathology in S100A8/A9-/- mice contained by trend less inflamed/congested blood vessels (p = 0.14; S7E Fig) and less bronchi with goblet cells in comparison to infected WT animals (p = 0.22; S7F Fig).

S100A8/A9-/- mice had a higher total cell count after intradermal injection... L3 inoculation in S100A8/A9-/- mice resulted in a significantly increased total cell number of eosinophils (Fig 2B), neutrophils (Fig 2C) and macrophages (Fig 2D) in comparison to PBS-injected S100A8/A9-/- mice.

Depletion of neutrophils led to a significantly increased worm recovery in S100A8/A9-/- mice compared to isotype-treated S100A8/A9-/- controls, resulting in a worm burden that was comparable to isotype-treated WT controls (Fig 5).
315.Rationale: While eosinophil numbers increased in the skin after intradermal challenge, eosinophil counts in thoracic cavity/BAL were not different between WT and knockouts, and S100A8/A9-/- lungs actually showed a trend toward fewer goblet-cell–containing bronchi (less mucus), not more. The protective phenotype of S100A8/A9 deficiency was abrogated by neutrophil depletion, implicating neutrophils rather than eosinophils or increased mucus production as the key mediator of enhanced clearance.

315.Option: S100A8/A9 acts as a systemic immunosuppressant that dampens all innate immune responses, leading to increased larval survival in wild-type mice
Evidence: Collectively our data suggest that S100A8/A9 inhibits L3-induced inflammation by decreasing chemokine production and neutrophil activation.
the impact of S100A8/S100A9 may differ depending on the inflammatory milieu and location [2,3].

Comparable total numbers of neutrophils in the thoracic cavity and BAL of S100A8/A9-/- mice upon L. sigmodontis infection is in contrast to previous studies demonstrating a S100A8 and S100A9-dependent recruitment of murine and human phagocytes to the site of inflammation.
315.Rationale: The data support a modulatory, anti-inflammatory effect of S100A8/A9 on neutrophil activation and local chemokine responses during larval migration, but do not support the claim of a blanket systemic immunosuppression of all innate responses. The authors explicitly note context- and location-dependent effects; some innate parameters were unchanged between strains. Therefore the phrasing 'systemic immunosuppressant that dampens all innate immune responses' is overly broad and not supported by the evidence.

315.Option: S100A8/A9 modulates neutrophil activation to balance protective immunity and excessive inflammation during early larval migration

CD10 is significantly lower in both groups at ToD as compared to controls (p<0.0001, Table 5). CD10 expression increased at EoT in VL patients and was restored at 3m (Fig 3A, Table 5). In contrast, in VL/HIV patients, CD10 levels remained similar at EoT, and only increased marginally over time.
46 VL/HIV patients were on antiretroviral therapy (ART) at the time of VL diagnosis, the remaining three started ART at the end of the anti-leishmanial treatment.
321.Rationale: The passage provides clear evidence that higher CD10 expression post-treatment is associated with not relapsing among VL/HIV patients (quoted above), so CD10 distinguishes relapsers from non-relapsers. However, the question asks specifically about distinguishing individuals with high HIV RNA copies/mL versus those who are not. The passage does not report HIV RNA (viral load) measurements or relate CD10 to HIV RNA levels; it only reports ART status and relapse comparisons. Because there is no verbatim evidence linking CD10 to HIV RNA in the text, this option cannot be confirmed for the question asked.

321.Option: Higher production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) by neutrophils post-treatment is associated with reduced risk of relapse in VL/HIV patients despite clinical cure
Evidence: The ability of neutrophils to produce ROS was similar (p = 0.1777) in VL/HIV patients who relapse (206464 [119571]) and those who didn’t relapse during follow-up (227781 [155669–259241]).
Whereas ROS production in response to pyocyanin was impaired at ToD in both VL and VL/HIV patients, it was restored faster in VL/HIV patients.
321.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that ROS production was similar between VL/HIV patients who relapsed and those who did not (quoted above), so higher ROS post-treatment does not distinguish reduced relapse risk. In addition, no data are provided linking ROS levels to HIV RNA (viral load), so this option is not supported for the question asked.

321.Option: Higher phagocytic capacity of neutrophils post-treatment is associated with reduced risk of relapse in VL/HIV patients despite clinical cure
Evidence: This was restored to levels similar to those of controls at EoT in VL patients, but remained impaired at all time points in VL/HIV patients (Table 7).
In the latter group, the ability of neutrophils to phagocytose bioparticle was similarly impaired in patients who relapse and those who didn’t during follow-up (Fig 5B, Table 7).
321.Rationale: The passage reports that phagocytic capacity remained impaired in VL/HIV patients and was similarly impaired in those who relapsed and those who did not (quoted above). Therefore higher phagocytic capacity post-treatment does not distinguish reduced relapse risk in this cohort. The passage also contains no measurements or associations with HIV RNA (viral load), so this option is not supported for the specific question.

321.Option: Higher expression of CD62L on neutrophils post-treatment is associated with reduced risk of relapse in VL/HIV patients despite clinical cure
Evidence: There were no significant differences (p = 0.1750) in CD62L MFI between VL/HIV patients who relapsed (13380 [7986–17068]) and those who didn’t (10022 [7155–13582]).
CD62L MFI were restored to levels similar to those of controls at EoT in both groups (Fig 2B, Table 3).
321.Rationale: The passage states explicitly that CD62L levels did not differ significantly between VL/HIV patients who relapsed and those who did not (quoted above), so higher CD62L post-treatment is not shown to distinguish reduced relapse risk. As with the other options, the study does not report HIV RNA (viral load) data or link CD62L to viral load, so this option is not supported for the question as phrased.

322.Option: Antigenic divergence in non-lethal venom components, particularly PLA2 and β-cardiotoxin, likely prevents effective neutralization despite strong immunoreactivity to neurotoxins
Evidence: O. hannah venom represents the only venom that could not be neutralized by FNAV in current study, although FNAV did cross-reacted intensely with the major lethal toxins (neurotoxins, which formed 50% of proteome based on our venom proteome and immunoprofiling analyses as shown in Figs 3 and 6 and S5 Table).
It was found that β-cardiotoxin, identified in the HPLC fractions 20–23, is one of venom proteins weakly recognized or even non-recognized by FNAV.
In addition, PLA2 in O. hannah venom also showed poor immunoreactivity to FNAV.

This analysis revealed that the sequence similarity is quite low between O. Hannah PLA2 and N. atra PLA2 (64%) or between O. Hannah PLA2 and B. multicinctus PLA2 (59%). ... this alignment analysis together with our immunological profiling data suggest that O. Hannah PLA2 might have distinct antigenic site(s) as compared with PLA2 from venoms of N. atra, N. kaouthia and B. multicinctus.
322.Rationale: The passage documents that FNAV cross-reacts strongly with O. hannah neurotoxins but still fails to neutralize O. hannah venom. It specifically identifies β-cardiotoxin and PLA2 as components that are weakly or not recognized by FNAV and shows they have lower sequence similarity (i.e., antigenic divergence) compared with the immunogen-derived proteins. Antigenic divergence of these components provides a plausible explanation for lack of neutralization of those toxin contributors despite good immunoreactivity to neurotoxins. The cited text explicitly links poor immunoreactivity of β-cardiotoxin and PLA2 and low sequence similarity as reasons these components may not be neutralized by FNAV.

322.Option: The lack of neutralization is due to the high concentration of non-neutralized neurotoxins in O. hannah venom, which overwhelms the antivenom's binding capacity despite strong immunoreactivity
Evidence: O. hannah venom represents the only venom that could not be neutralized by FNAV in current study, although FNAV did cross-reacted intensely with the major lethal toxins (neurotoxins, which formed 50% of proteome based on our venom proteome and immunoprofiling analyses as shown in Figs 3 and 6 and S5 Table).

Hence, the conflicting finding that FNAV could react strongly to the major neurotoxins yet failed to neutralize the lethality at the challenge dose used remains unresolved here and warrants further study.
322.Rationale: The passage states FNAV cross-reacted intensely with neurotoxins (which make up ~50% of the proteome) and explicitly notes the paradox that strong reactivity to neurotoxins co-occurred with failure to neutralize lethality. The authors do not propose that an overwhelming concentration of non-neutralized neurotoxins explains the failure; instead they point to poor recognition of other components (β-cardiotoxin, PLA2). There is no passage evidence that antigen binding capacity was exceeded by neurotoxin quantity, so this option is not supported.

322.Option: The antivenom's failure to neutralize O. hannah venom is due to the degradation of neurotoxins during storage, despite strong immunoreactivity to intact neurotoxins in the assay
Evidence: However, there were not any LAAO proteins identified in our O. hannah venom proteome. The exact reason(s) for this discrepancy is (are) currently unknown. One of the possible reasons is that the amount of LAAO protein in our O. hannah venom might be too low to be detected after the whole process for venom sample preparation and fractionation. Another possibility is that LAAO protein might have been degraded in the venom after long-term storage and thus could not be detected.
322.Rationale: The passage discusses possible degradation during storage only in reference to LAAO proteins (and as a possible reason for their absence from the proteome), not for neurotoxins. In fact, the authors report strong cross-reaction to neurotoxins, which argues against degradation of neurotoxins being responsible. Thus the passage provides no support that neurotoxin degradation during storage explains the failure to neutralize.

322.Option: The poor neutralization of O. hannah venom is due to the presence of a novel, non-recognized toxin that is not detected by proteomic analysis, despite strong immunoreactivity to known neurotoxins
Evidence: Raw MS data files were analyzed using Proteome Discoverer Software (version 1.3.0.339; Thermo Fisher, San Jose, CA, USA) and searched against an in-house–generated Squamata database originated from the UniProt database using the MASCOT search engine (version 2.2; Matrix Science, London, UK).
The decoy database search approach was assessed for peptide identification, and the criteria of target false discover rate (FDR) was estimated to be <0.01 in this study.
It was found that β-cardiotoxin, identified in the HPLC fractions 20–23, is one of venom proteins weakly recognized or even non-recognized by FNAV.
In addition, PLA2 in O. hannah venom also showed poor immunoreactivity to FNAV.
322.Rationale: The investigators performed thorough proteomic analyses with rigorous peptide-identification criteria (FDR <0.01) and identified β-cardiotoxin and PLA2 as existing venom components that are weakly recognized by FNAV. The passage does not provide evidence for an undetected novel lethal toxin; instead it identifies specific, detected components with poor immunoreactivity. Therefore, the explanation invoking an undetected novel toxin is not supported by the reported data.

322.Option: The high sequence similarity between O. hannah neurotoxins and those in the immunogen (N. atra) ensures complete neutralization, but the antivenom's inability to bind to non-toxic venom proteins limits its overall efficacy
Evidence: This analysis revealed that the sequence similarity is quite low between O. Hannah PLA2 and N. atra PLA2 (64%) or between O. Hannah PLA2 and B. multicinctus PLA2 (59%). However, the sequence similarity between N. atra PLA2 and N. kaouthia PLA2 is much higher (up to 95%).

O. hannah venom represents the only venom that could not be neutralized by FNAV in current study, although FNAV did cross-reacted intensely with the major lethal toxins (neurotoxins, which formed 50% of proteome based on our venom proteome and immunoprofiling analyses as shown in Figs 3 and 6 and S5 Table).

Although we observed that the immunorecognition of FNAV toward β-cardiotoxin and PLA2 is weakly, the pharmacological activities of both β-cardiotoxin and PLA2 seem not to correlate with the major symptom (neurotoxicity) caused by O. hannah venom.
322.Rationale: The option misstates several points. The passage does not claim high sequence similarity between O. hannah neurotoxins and N. atra (it documents low similarity for PLA2 specifically). It also does not state that non-toxic proteins limit efficacy; in fact, β-cardiotoxin and PLA2 are biologically active (β-cardiotoxin can cause labored breathing and death at high doses), so calling them 'non-toxic' is incorrect. Finally, the authors report strong reactivity to neurotoxins but incomplete neutralization and identify poor recognition of certain toxin families (PLA2, β-cardiotoxin) as a more likely issue. Thus this option is not supported by the passage.

323.Option: Persistent elevation of serum IgG levels that remain significantly elevated at week 11 p.e. compared to baseline
Evidence: Increases in IgG were detected in 7/12 animals p.e., peaking between weeks 4 and 6 (p<0.0001 between weeks 0 and 4 and between weeks 0 and 6) and slightly decreasing by week 11 (p = 0.04 between weeks 6 and 11).
These results demonstrate that conjunctival Ct exposure elicits systemic circulation of Ct-specific IgA and IgG, which remained elevated to the latest time-point tested.
Serum IgA and IgG increases were correlated with increased bacterial loads
323.Rationale: Although the passage states serum Ct-specific IgG remained elevated to the latest time-point tested and only slightly decreased by week 11, the text also indicates that serum IgG increases correlated with increased bacterial loads. That pattern is more consistent with an exposure/response marker than a marker that temporally associates with disease resolution. Therefore the passage does not support serum IgG as the immunological parameter most strongly indicating a correlate of protection tied to resolution.

323.Option: Conjunctival monocyte proportion that increases significantly from week 6 p.e. and peaks at week 11 p.e., coinciding with the resolution of clinical signs
Evidence: their proportion among leucocytes steadily increased from 6 weeks p.e. and peaked at 11 weeks p.e. (53 ± 9% of all immune cells, p = 0.0078 for week 11 (final time tested) vs week 1 p.e.).
local monocytes are characteristic of the continuance of clinical signs of inflammation once bacterial infection resolves.
323.Rationale: While monocyte proportions do increase from week 6 and peak at week 11, the authors explicitly interpret local monocytes as being characteristic of the continuance (persistence) of clinical signs after infection resolves, not as markers of resolution or protection. Thus the pattern does not support monocyte proportion as a correlate of protection associated with disease resolution.

323.Option: Early and sustained increase in conjunctival T cell proportions, which peak at 3 weeks p.e. and remain elevated through the convalescent phase
Evidence: During the first 2 weeks p.e., the proportion of T cells increased slightly from baseline (57 ± 8% to 67 ± 9%, p = 0.1210), and remained stable (Fig 4B). Between weeks 3 and 4, T cells proportions fluctuated and by week 6 the proportions had decreased.
These data demonstrate the local fluctuations of key immune subsets responding to acute Ct infection.
323.Rationale: The passage describes an early rise in conjunctival T cells peaking around weeks 2–3, but reports that T cell proportions declined by week 6. They do not remain elevated through the convalescent phase. Therefore the described timing/pattern does not support a sustained T cell proportion as a correlate of protection tied to resolution.

323.Option: Delayed peak in pro-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-17A and IL-1β, which coincides with the resolution of clinical signs and bacterial clearance
Evidence: Cytokines VEGF, IL-12/23, IL-13, IL-17α, IL-10, IFN-y, IL-2, IL-15, sCD40L and IL-1β decreased between weeks 4 or 6, or during both timepoints, but rebounded at week 11 p.e., to levels higher than baseline, and this effect was observed in more macaques.
In contrast, Th1 cytokines evaluated in our panel (IL-1β, TNF-α, and GM-CSF) did not significantly increased p.e.
The increase in IL-17A was delayed, (week 11 p.e.), along with elevated levels of other pro-inflammatory (VEGF, IL-1B, IL-15, and GM-CSF).
323.Rationale: Although some pro-inflammatory cytokines (including IL-17A and sometimes IL-1β) rebound or increase late (around week 11), the passage does not link these late increases to bacterial clearance or to resolution of clinical signs. In fact, these late pro-inflammatory changes are discussed in the context of convalescence and possible chronic/Th17-related pathology. Furthermore, IL-1β did not show significant early increases. Thus the evidence does not support these delayed pro-inflammatory cytokines as correlates of protection tied to resolution.

323.Option: Sustained tear IgA response that persists after bacterial clearance and coincides with the resolution phase of disease
Evidence: IgG increased comparably at 2 weeks p.e. (p = 0.021), then both IgG and IgA peaked between 4 and 6 weeks p.e. (p = 0.001 and 0.002 between weeks 0 and 6 for IgG and IgA respectively), and then decreased at 11 weeks p.e. (p = 0.0098 and 0.0039 for IgG and IgA respectively) (Fig 5B).
we observed a tendency toward decreased ocular signs around 6 weeks p.e. corresponding to a peak in specific anti-Ct IgA in tears, concomitant with a higher proportion of B cells.
In 4/12 macaques, the bacterial load decreased to undetectable levels at 6 weeks p.e. In the 8 remaining macaques the bacterial load decreased to undetectable levels by week 9, which was 2 weeks p.t.
323.Rationale: The passage provides direct support that a peak in tear (local) anti-Ct IgA at ~4–6 weeks coincides with a tendency toward decreased ocular signs, which is the timing most consistent with disease resolution. However, the option's claim that tear IgA is 'sustained' and 'persists after bacterial clearance' is contradicted by the text: tear IgA (and IgG) decreased by week 11 p.e., whereas bacterial loads were undetectable in most animals by week 9. Therefore the specific assertion of persistence after clearance is not supported. Although local tear IgA timing most closely aligns with clinical improvement, the option's additional claim about sustained persistence after clearance is inaccurate, so the option as written is incorrect.

324.Option: Using a crossover design where parasites are exposed to drug and no-drug conditions in alternating periods to control for individual parasite variability
Evidence: Due to significant decline in Wolbachia content with time in culture due to loss of mf production (Fig 4), vehicle treated cultures were ran simultaneously and sampled at 7 or 14 days as matched controls (Fig 5A).
In certain cultures, drugs were removed following 7-day exposure, parasites washed, and cultures extended for a further 7-day period.
324.Rationale: The passage describes parallel control cultures and occasional drug washout periods, but does not describe a crossover design in which the same individual parasites are alternately exposed to drug and no-drug conditions. The text specifically states vehicle controls were run simultaneously as matched controls rather than individual parasites being crossed over between treatments. Therefore a crossover design was not used and is not the feature that enabled attribution of Wolbachia changes to drug exposure.

324.Option: Comparing Wolbachia load in treated parasites to baseline levels measured immediately after isolation from in vivo hosts
Evidence: Wolbachia yields within female B. malayi after 1, 2 or 3 weeks in LEC+LEC insert co-cultures were compared with those from parasites immediately retrieved from IL-4Rα-/-IL-5-/- mice (Fig 4B).

a sample of 62 adult female worms were isolated from CB.17 SCID mouse infections, and approximately equal batches were either processed immediately for metabolic activity and Wolbachia molecular assays, set up into culture within LEC bilayers, or implanted into naïve CB.17 SCID recipients (Fig 4C).

Due to significant decline in Wolbachia content with time in culture due to loss of mf production (Fig 4), vehicle treated cultures were ran simultaneously and sampled at 7 or 14 days as matched controls (Fig 5A).
324.Rationale: The researchers did measure baseline Wolbachia levels immediately after isolation and compared cultured parasites to that baseline, which provides useful context. However, baseline comparison alone cannot distinguish drug-induced changes from natural time-dependent declines in Wolbachia that occur in culture (for example due to cessation of embryogenesis). The investigators therefore ran concurrent vehicle-treated controls sampled at the same time points to control for natural temporal changes. Because baseline comparisons do not control for parallel time effects, this option is not the most direct design feature for attributing changes to drug exposure.

324.Option: Using a within-subjects design where each female B. malayi is exposed to multiple drug treatments in sequence to compare effects directly
Evidence: a sample of 62 adult female worms were isolated from CB.17 SCID mouse infections, and approximately equal batches were either processed immediately for metabolic activity and Wolbachia molecular assays, set up into culture within LEC bilayers, or implanted into naïve CB.17 SCID recipients (Fig 4C).
In certain cultures, drugs were removed following 7-day exposure, parasites washed, and cultures extended for a further 7-day period.
324.Rationale: The study used parallel groups (freshly processed, cultured, or implanted) and included washout groups for some treatments, but it did not describe exposing the same individual female worms sequentially to multiple different drug treatments (a within-subjects repeated-treatment sequence). The design is primarily parallel-group with some treatment/removal (washout) arms rather than a within-subject multiple-treatment crossover. Thus this option is not an accurate description of the design feature used to attribute Wolbachia changes to drug exposure.

324.Option: Including concurrent vehicle-treated control cultures sampled at identical time points to account for natural decline in Wolbachia over time
Evidence: Due to significant decline in Wolbachia content with time in culture due to loss of mf production (Fig 4), vehicle treated cultures were ran simultaneously and sampled at 7 or 14 days as matched controls (Fig 5A).
An equivalent percentage of DMSO used in the drug groups was added to vehicle control groups.
Vehicle treated cultures were ran simultaneously and sampled at 7 or 14 days as matched controls (Fig 5A).
324.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that vehicle-treated control cultures were run simultaneously and sampled at the same time points as drug-treated cultures to serve as matched controls. This concurrent control strategy directly controls for natural temporal declines in Wolbachia (e.g., due to cessation of embryogenesis and loss of mf production) and therefore most directly enables attribution of additional changes in Wolbachia load specifically to the drug exposure rather than to background physiological changes over time.

325.Option: Reduced predation pressure in oil palm plantations due to lower invertebrate diversity
Evidence: Based on our data, vectorial capacity was estimated to be greater in oil palm plantation than in forest, largely driven by greater mosquito abundance in oil palm.
as forest conversion to oil palm plantation reduces invertebrate diversity and abundance [76,77], predation and competition may also be reduced.
325.Rationale: The passage states the higher vectorial capacity in plantations was driven by greater mosquito abundance, and explicitly proposes that conversion reduces invertebrate diversity and abundance such that predation and competition may be reduced. Reduced predation/competition offers a plausible ecological mechanism to increase adult mosquito abundance even when key life-history traits (survival, biting rate, etc.) appear similar between land-use types; therefore this explanation is supported by the authors' text.

325.Option: Greater daily temperature fluctuations in oil palm plantations leading to increased mosquito reproductive output
Evidence: Temperature data indicated that although mean daily temperatures did not differ significantly between land-use types during the sampling period, daily temperature fluctuations were greater in oil palm plantations than in logged forest [55].

Despite warmer temperatures in plantation sites, none of the parameters estimated using the temperature-dependent responses from Mordecai et al. [22] differed significantly between the land-use types for either sampling year (Wilcox, Z = 0.43, p = 0.663; Fig 1).
This increased abundance was not estimated from TPCs, as diurnal temperatures did not differ sufficiently between habitat types to produce divergent responses in the underlying demographic traits.

In a previous study conducted at these same field sites, we found that differences in daily temperature fluctuations resulted in faster larval development rates in oil palm plantations [55], an effect not predicted by published TPCs.
325.Rationale: While the passage notes greater daily temperature fluctuations in plantations and references prior findings of faster larval development, the authors report that temperature-dependent trait estimates did not differ significantly between land-use types in this study and that diurnal differences were insufficient to explain the abundance difference. Thus, although temperature fluctuations could influence traits in some contexts, the study's own results do not support temperature fluctuations as the most likely explanation for the observed higher weekly trap counts.

325.Option: Higher ambient temperatures in oil palm plantations enhancing mosquito development and survival rates
Evidence: Temperature data indicated that although mean daily temperatures did not differ significantly between land-use types during the sampling period, daily temperature fluctuations were greater in oil palm plantations than in logged forest [55].

Despite warmer temperatures in plantation sites, none of the parameters estimated using the temperature-dependent responses from Mordecai et al. [22] differed significantly between the land-use types for either sampling year (Wilcox, Z = 0.43, p = 0.663; Fig 1).
325.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that mean daily temperatures did not differ significantly between land-use types during sampling and that temperature-driven parameter estimates did not differ significantly. Therefore higher ambient temperatures are not supported by the study as the explanation for the greater trapped mosquito numbers.

325.Option: Increased availability of artificial breeding sites due to higher human activity and infrastructure investment in oil palm plantations
Evidence: The homogenisation of vegetation associated with monoculture may reduce breeding habitats by removing trees, but may also increase the availability of artificial breeding sites due to increases in human activity.

The plantation and forest sites sampled in this study were all characterised by labour-intensive economies, but the level of investment in housing infrastructure and public services was considerably higher in the former.
Variation in water storage, waste management and vector control would all have impacted mosquito abundance, but characterising these factors were beyond the scope of this study.
Additionally, we did not collect data on human density, however it was likely higher in plantations than in logged forest and may contribute to the higher mosquito density there.
Based on our data, vectorial capacity was estimated to be greater in oil palm plantation than in forest, largely driven by greater mosquito abundance in oil palm.
325.Rationale: The authors point to increased artificial breeding sites associated with higher human activity/infrastructure in plantations (water storage, waste management, etc.) and note that human density and infrastructure investment are likely higher in plantations and could contribute to higher mosquito density. This mechanism directly explains greater trap counts despite similar life-history trait estimates and is explicitly discussed by the authors as a plausible driver.

325.Option: Increased availability of natural breeding sites due to forest degradation in logged forest sites
Evidence: The homogenisation of vegetation associated with monoculture may reduce breeding habitats by removing trees, but may also increase the availability of artificial breeding sites due to increases in human activity.
Oil palm plantations were established in 2006 ... The plantation sites have considerably lower plant biomass than the forest sites (ABG = 38.1 t/ha;[54]).
Conversion has been at the expense of either selectively logged or old growth forest [41].
325.Rationale: The passage does not suggest that logged forest degradation increased natural breeding site availability relative to plantations; rather, conversion and monoculture can reduce natural tree-associated habitats. The authors emphasize artificial breeding site availability and changes in human activity as plausible drivers of increased mosquito abundance in plantations. Therefore the option claiming increased natural breeding sites in logged forest as the cause is not supported by the text.

326.Option: The specificity of pyriproxyfen to mosquito species ensures that non-target organisms remain unaffected despite its long-term presence in the environment
Evidence: PPF is not mosquito specific and has been shown to inhibit the development of other insects [13–15] and to impact the physiology of non-target invertebrates [16, 17].
The effects of PPF on pollinators such as honey bees (Apis mellifera) are of particular concern [18–20].
PPF has previously been shown to impact adult and immature honey bees if brought back to the colony and could be a potential contributing factor to colony collapse [18, 21].
326.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that pyriproxyfen (PPF) is not mosquito-specific and documents effects on non-target insects, including honey bees, so the assertion of species specificity protecting non-targets is false. Therefore this option is incorrect.

326.Option: The self-delivery mechanism of autodissemination reduces the need for repeated applications, thereby decreasing the total amount of insecticide released and minimizing ecological risk
Evidence: There are a couple of advantages to using autodissemination techniques: (1) less labor is required because mosquitoes self-deliver insecticide, and (2) the need for less active ingredient relative to conventional control measures.

However, while direct targeting of cryptic locations seems like a significant advantage over large scale applications of insecticides, this could actually be detrimental to nontarget organisms by delivering these highly potent long-lasting growth inhibitors to the exact places that other beneficial insects visit, such as locations where many anthophilous insects seek nectar.
This study suggests a potential risk to insect pollinators using autodissemination approaches utilizing highly potent insect growth regulators.
326.Rationale: Although the passage acknowledges operational advantages of autodissemination (less labor, less active ingredient), it also warns that these same features can concentrate potent, persistent IGRs in cryptic sites used by non-targets (e.g., nectar sources), creating ecological risk. The option's claim that ecological risk is minimized is contradicted by the text, so it is incorrect.

326.Option: The use of male mosquitoes as delivery agents enhances targeted control of breeding sites while reducing the chance of contamination in non-target habitats due to their limited foraging range
Evidence: adult male mosquitoes can be treated with an Insect Growth Regulator (IGR) and the males deliver lethal doses to breeding containers, which has been dubbed ‘Autodissemination Augmented by Males’ (ADAM) [5, 6]

We know that male and female mosquitoes seek out nectar sources, and if exposed to a growth inhibitor they could be delivering lethal doses where important pollinators such as honey bees can be contaminated with PPF.
A. mellifera will travel longer distances to forage compared to Ae. albopictus, so the potential for vectoring PPF across landscapes is high with the observed indirect transfer from nectar sources.
326.Rationale: While males can be used as delivery agents (ADAM), the passage documents that both male and female mosquitoes visit nectar and that treated males can contaminate nectar sources and indirectly contaminate pollinators. The text even highlights the potential for long-distance vectoring via pollinators. Thus the claim that male use reduces contamination risk due to limited foraging is unsupported and incorrect.

326.Option: The long environmental persistence of pyriproxyfen enables sustained mosquito control but increases the likelihood of unintended contamination of pollinator habitats
Evidence: PPF has been shown to persist up to four months in the environment and is incredibly potent [8, 9].

As currently practiced, autodissemination consists of ... placing artificial adult resting sites (‘dissemination stations’) in the field, which are attractive to adult mosquitoes, and are treated with a persistent IGR [7].

However, while direct targeting of cryptic locations seems like a significant advantage over large scale applications of insecticides, this could actually be detrimental to nontarget organisms by delivering these highly potent long-lasting growth inhibitors to the exact places that other beneficial insects visit, such as locations where many anthophilous insects seek nectar.
Results suggest A. mellifera can be contaminated with PPF indirectly through the visitation of artificial nectar sources in the presence of PPF treated male Ae. albopictus mosquitoes.
Mass spectrometry data and bioassays confirm the presence of PFF on A. mellifera from cages with PPF treated Ae. albopictus males (Fig 6 and S6 Data).
326.Rationale: The passage explicitly links PPF's environmental persistence and use in dissemination stations to sustained control potential, and simultaneously documents indirect contamination of nectar sources and honey bees. Therefore persistence is the factor most likely to both prolong intervention effect and increase risk to pollinators, making this option correct.

326.Option: The high potency of pyriproxyfen at low concentrations ensures effective mosquito control while minimizing exposure to non-target species through rapid degradation in the environment
Evidence: PPF is a juvenile hormone mimic that disrupts the hormonal system of insects resulting in the arrested development and morphogenesis of the adult stage. PPF has been shown to persist up to four months in the environment and is incredibly potent [8, 9].
The concentration required to prevent mosquito development (LC50) is only 0.012 parts per billion [10].
PPF is not mosquito specific and has been shown to inhibit the development of other insects [13–15] and to impact the physiology of non-target invertebrates [16, 17].
326.Rationale: While PPF is highly potent at low concentrations, the passage states it persists for months rather than degrading rapidly, and it is not mosquito-specific with documented non-target impacts. The claim that rapid degradation minimizes exposure is contradicted by the evidence, so this option is incorrect.

327.Option: The ability to detect and resolve multiple melting curve peaks allows identification of mixed blood meals, revealing simultaneous host use by vectors
Evidence: Therefore, we evaluated the possibility of detecting at least two different blood meal sources. In this case, the melt curve showed two peaks belonging to mouse and chicken amplicons, with their respective Tm. In addition, the dissociation curve had a shape with two falls (Figure S2). These types of curves were analyzed by modifying the normalization area to every fall of the curve to recognize genotypes separately with their respective standard genotype.

Unspecific peaks led to testing mixed feedings, showing that the technique detects both Tm sources. This allows identification of many species from which the insects have been fed, even when there are no standards for those species.
327.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes HRM melting curves that show two distinct peaks for mixed feeds (mouse and chicken) and explains how those dual peaks are analyzed to recognize the separate genotypes. Detecting and resolving multiple melting-curve peaks therefore enables identification of mixed blood meals, which reveals simultaneous host use by vectors and thus reveals more complex vector–host interactions than single-host identification alone.

327.Option: The technique's sensitivity to nucleated erythrocytes allows it to detect blood from species with higher DNA content, thereby revealing the most frequent host species in a vector's diet
Evidence: Chicken blood has nucleated erythrocytes, so a large amount of DNA should remain in the intestine for a long time.
This could explain the presence of unspecific peaks in the melt curve when insects were fed with human blood because the insects are usually fed with chicken blood in laboratory conditions.
Thus, the present results suggest that chicken blood remains in the intestinal tract longer than mouse and human blood (data not shown).
327.Rationale: While the passage notes that chicken blood (nucleated erythrocytes) persists longer and can produce detectable signal (even causing 'unspecific peaks'), it does not claim HRM's sensitivity to nucleated erythrocytes uniquely reveals the most frequent host species. In fact, the persistence can bias detection toward species with more durable DNA rather than accurately reflecting frequency of feeding. The text presents this as a caveat (possible explanation for spurious peaks), not as a feature that reliably infers the most frequent host in a diet.

327.Option: The high confidence percentage values generated by the software allow researchers to assign genotypes with certainty, even when multiple host species are present, enabling accurate reconstruction of transmission networks
Evidence: Genotypes were defined by selecting a sample from each standard species as a reference control to identify unknown samples. The software then auto-called the genotype and melting temperatures of each amplicon and provided a confidence percentage based on the square root of the correlation coefficient between samples and the reference genotypes.

A specific profile for each species analyzed was observed (Figures 2 and S1), with confidence percentages (%C) ranging from 78.81% and 98.69% (Table 2), indicating that samples were correctly identified in each of the experiments conducted in triplicate.
These types of curves were analyzed by modifying the normalization area to every fall of the curve to recognize genotypes separately with their respective standard genotype.
we suggest using sequencing of amplicons only when unidentified samples by HRM analysis are present.
327.Rationale: The passage supports that the HRM software produces confidence percentages that help assign single-species genotypes with high reliability. However, when multiple host species are present (mixed feeds), the method requires special handling (modifying normalization areas) and sometimes sequencing to resolve identities. Thus, the claim that high confidence percentages alone allow certain assignment 'even when multiple host species are present' overstates the method's standalone certainty and the ability to reconstruct transmission networks without additional analysis.

327.Option: The use of a single pair of primers for amplification enables detection of a broad range of host species, facilitating the identification of novel or unexpected blood sources in complex transmission settings
Evidence: the technique used herein only needs a single pair of primers, making it easy to include other species according to every epidemiological scenario with no other standardizations of PCR conditions.

Unspecific peaks led to testing mixed feedings, showing that the technique detects both Tm sources. This allows identification of many species from which the insects have been fed, even when there are no standards for those species.

When the sample is not recognized, the next step is sequencing, as reported by other authors [29]–[31]. Based on these results, we recommend the use of Tm values as a first approach to identify a sample from insects collected in natural populations. Then the species selected based on the Tm could be used as a standard sample for HRM analysis, and finally the species not identified could be sequenced.
327.Rationale: The passage states the method relies on a single primer pair for Cyt b amplification, simplifying inclusion of additional species without restandardizing PCR conditions. It also notes that unspecific peaks can indicate species not included among standards and that HRM can detect many species (even without prior standards), with sequencing as a follow-up. Thus the single-primer approach supports broad host detection and discovery of novel or unexpected blood sources in complex transmission settings.

327.Option: The ability to analyze both intestinal content and feces samples ensures that the timing of blood meals can be estimated, allowing researchers to reconstruct the sequence of host use over time
Evidence: The HRM technique proved to work in both types of samples (intestinal and fecal) at least until 30 days after insects were fed.
Intestinal content and feces samples showed successful amplification and detection of the blood-meal source after 30 days post-feeding, indicating the high sensitivity of Cyt b-HRM at this time.

The HRM technique proved to work in both types of samples (intestinal and fecal) at least until 30 days after insects were fed. ... many factors have an influence on the presence of blood in the intestinal tract over time, as described by Lee et al. [30]. Chicken blood has nucleated erythrocytes, so a large amount of DNA should remain in the intestine for a long time.

no misidentification was recorded with DNA from insect feces, low confidence percentages were reached and other samples did not amplify, showing that the quality of the sample is a limitation of the technique, as reported for real-time PCR [50].
327.Rationale: While HRM can analyze both intestinal contents and feces and detect blood sources up to 30 days post-feeding, the passage emphasizes that persistence of DNA (e.g., chicken blood) and multiple influencing factors complicate temporal interpretation. Examples of prior meals persisting (leading to mixed or unspecific peaks) and limitations in sample quality indicate that reliably estimating precise timing or reconstructing the chronological sequence of host use is not supported as a general capability. The technique provides presence/identity information over a window of time but does not reliably resolve the exact timing sequence without additional data.

328.Option: Increased D-dimer levels after ivermectin treatment suggest that microfilarial killing triggers acute coagulation activation and fibrinolysis
Evidence: Among hemostatic parameters, median D-dimers significantly increased, from 582 ng/mL to 745 ng/mL after IVM (p = 0.024) with a median relative variation of +34.7% (interquartile range: 1.44–72.5%).
The increase in D-dimers observed on D4 post-IVM suggests both enhanced fibrinolysis and coagulation activation which happen simultaneously.

This could be attributed to the close relationship between hemostasis and inflammation, where inflammation induces fibrin formation in the extravascular space, facilitating immune cells functions [20].
328.Rationale: The quoted passages describe an acute rise in D-dimers occurring after ivermectin (post-IVM D4), consistent with treatment-triggered coagulation/fibrinolysis. The question asks for mechanisms linking infection intensity to severe events independent of acute treatment effects; since these D-dimer changes are observed after IVM, they reflect an acute treatment-associated phenomenon rather than a chronic infection-intensity mechanism, so this option does not answer the question.

328.Option: Elevated gamma-globulins across all microfilarial density groups indicate a systemic immune response that may contribute to chronic inflammation and vascular damage
Evidence: Furthermore, our study highlighted a polyclonal elevation of gamma-globulins across all MFD groups, suggesting a systemic immune response.
This phenomenon is consistent with a previous study in Ghana, were 75% of participants had gamma-globulins > 16 g/L [17].
ESR were increased in all groups, possibly driven by hypergammaglobulinemia, which is known to accelerate erythrocyte sedimentation.
328.Rationale: Elevated gamma-globulins and raised ESR across all MFD groups do indicate chronic systemic immune activation, which plausibly could contribute to chronic inflammation and vascular damage. However, compared with other findings in the paper (notably eosinophilia with a direct procoagulant mechanism), the evidence linking hypergammaglobulinemia to a specific biological pathway causing severe thrombotic or vascular events is more indirect. The question asks which factor most strongly suggests such a mechanism; while gamma-globulins support chronic inflammation, they are a less specific mechanistic link to severe clinical events than eosinophil-associated tissue factor expression.

328.Option: Elevated eosinophil counts in individuals with high microfilarial density suggest a chronic prothrombotic state driven by tissue factor expression
Evidence: Eosinophilia exceeding 5,000 /μL is common among subjects with loiasis.
Eosinophils, known to express tissue factor, may trigger blood coagulation [5], leading to thrombosis.
There was a trend towards increased leucocyte, granulocyte and eosinophil counts with increasing MFD (p = 0.011, p = 0.013 and p<0.001, respectively, Cuzick’s tests for trend).
Median eosinophil counts were significantly higher in patients with a MFD exceeding 20,000 mf/mL compared to those with no mf (1,016/μL vs 111/μL, p<0.001).
328.Rationale: These quotes document a clear, dose-related association between higher L. loa microfilarial density and elevated eosinophil counts at baseline (i.e., independent of treatment), and they state that eosinophils express tissue factor and may trigger coagulation leading to thrombosis. That combination — quantitative infection intensity linked to chronic eosinophilia, plus a known procoagulant property of eosinophils — provides the most direct and specific biological mechanism connecting worm burden/prevalence measures to increased risk of severe thrombotic or vascular clinical events, independent of acute ivermectin effects.

328.Option: The significant increase in eosinophils after ivermectin treatment supports a mechanism of immune-mediated vascular injury due to microfilarial death
Evidence: Median eosinophil counts rose from 171/μL to 1,095/μL (p<0.001).
We observed a marked increase in eosinophils, consistent with previous reports of interleukin-5 (IL-5)-driven eosinophilia following microfilarial killing by IVM [19].

This could be attributed to the close relationship between hemostasis and inflammation, where inflammation induces fibrin formation in the extravascular space, facilitating immune cells functions [20].
328.Rationale: While the observed post-IVM eosinophil surge and the linkage to microfilarial killing support a plausible mechanism for immune-mediated vascular injury, these changes are explicitly treatment-associated (post-IVM) rather than demonstrating a baseline, infection-intensity–driven mechanism. The question specifically asks for a factor that links infection intensity to increased risk independent of acute treatment effects, so a post-treatment phenomenon does not satisfy the requirement.

328.Option: The association between high microfilarial density and increased platelet counts suggests a prothrombotic state mediated by platelet activation during chronic infection
Evidence: Similarly, median platelet counts were also significantly higher in the same group (254,500/μL vs 180,000/μL, p = 0.044).
The notable increase in platelets with increasing MFDs suggests their probable role in filarial infections.

Prior research has established that platelets mediate the immunological killing of L. loa mfs [14,15], while data in lymphatic filariasis also demonstrated mfs’ ability to modulate platelet functions [16].
328.Rationale: Higher platelet counts with increasing MFD at baseline provide evidence consistent with a chronic, infection-intensity–related alteration of hemostasis that could favor thrombosis. However, the mechanistic link is less direct than that for eosinophils expressing tissue factor: the passage notes platelet involvement and modulation but does not state a direct prothrombotic molecular pathway (e.g., platelet activation markers or direct evidence of thrombosis driven by platelets) as clearly as it does for eosinophil tissue factor. Therefore, while supportive, this factor is a weaker and less specific indicator of the strongest mechanism sought by the question.

329.Option: The use of whole blood versus buffy coat as a sample type may introduce variability in test performance due to differences in parasite load distribution
Evidence: The sensitivity was also high, with the best results obtained with whole blood processed either by the Boil & Spin method (97.62%) or by the QIAgen columns (100%).

There were no significant differences between the sensitivities or specificities of LAMP in whole blood vs. buffy coat, being these processed either with the Boil & Spin method (WB and BC) or with the QIAamp DNA Mini Kit (WB QIA and BC QIA), McNemar's p > 0.1.
329.Rationale: The passage reports that whole blood and buffy coat produced similarly high sensitivity and specificity for LAMP, with no significant differences (McNemar's p > 0.1). Therefore, variability due to sample type was not observed in this study and is not the most critical factor influencing interpretation in real-world use according to the study data.

329.Option: The stability of the kit at 30°C for 12 months may lead to degradation of reagents over time, reducing test sensitivity in prolonged field use
Evidence: The kit has a shelf life of one year if stored between 1 and 30°C, and stability testing has shown that it remains stable for 12 months at 30°C.
It is in a ready-to-use format, with an additional advantage of being based on dried reagents, and therefore does not need a cold chain for transport and storage.
329.Rationale: The passage states the kit was stability-tested and remains stable for 12 months at 30°C and uses dried reagents that do not require a cold chain. While real-world cold-chain breaches could always be a concern, the study specifically documents that the kit is designed and tested for field stability, so reagent degradation under the specified storage conditions is not presented as the most critical factor in interpreting performance.

329.Option: The use of a chimeric antigen in the rK28 RDT may lead to cross-reactivity with other parasitic infections, affecting the validity of the LAMP test's reference standard
Evidence: Lymph node aspirate microscopy (LNA-M) was considered as the reference test of the study.
The rK28 RDT included in this study (OnSite Leishmania rK39-Plus, CTK Biotech, Inc., USA) presented a very good diagnostic performance, 98.81% sensitivity and 100% specificity.
329.Rationale: The rK28 RDT is not the reference standard in this study—the authors used lymph node aspirate microscopy (LNA-M) as the reference. Therefore any cross-reactivity of the rK28 RDT would not 'affect the validity of the LAMP test's reference standard' within this study. The reference standard is microscopic parasite confirmation, so concerns about rK28 antigen cross-reactivity do not critically influence interpretation of LAMP performance here.

329.Option: The genetic diversity of Leishmania strains across endemic regions may affect the test's sensitivity in real-world settings
Evidence: The Loopamp Leishmania Detection Kit uses primers targeting two different regions of the Leishmania genome: the 18S rRNA gene and the kDNA minicircles, and is specific to the Leishmania genus.

It contains a primer combination targeting two different regions of the Leishmania genome, which makes this test Leishmania genus specific and can be used across the different endemic regions to diagnose all forms of leishmaniasis.
329.Rationale: Although genetic diversity can be a theoretical concern for molecular tests, the passage explains that the kit targets two separate genomic regions and is designed to be Leishmania genus-specific so it can be used across different endemic regions. The study authors therefore indicate that strain diversity was addressed in the assay design, and they present this as a feature allowing broad applicability rather than a principal limitation influencing interpretation in the field.

329.Option: The presence of anti-Leishmania antibodies in patients after treatment may lead to false-positive results in the LAMP test
Evidence: Parasite confirmation by tissue aspirate microscopy is also used for test-of-cure, since serology is useless for this purpose as anti-Leishmania antibodies remain detectable up to several years after cure.
Loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) is a nucleic acid amplification test that combines rapidity, simplicity, and high specificity.
The Loopamp Leishmania Detection Kit uses primers targeting two different regions of the Leishmania genome: the 18S rRNA gene and the kDNA minicircles, and is specific to the Leishmania genus.
329.Rationale: LAMP detects parasite DNA, not host antibodies. The passage notes that serology is unsuitable for test-of-cure because antibodies persist for years, but that is a limitation of antibody-based tests, not nucleic acid tests. Therefore the presence of anti-Leishmania antibodies after treatment would not cause false-positive LAMP results; antibodies and DNA detection are different modalities.

330.Option: The fact that 22 participants were referred for treatment, as this indicates that seropositivity was interpreted as clinically significant regardless of symptom status
Evidence: Twenty-two [22] slaughterhouse workers were evaluated as probable brucellosis cases, prescription was obtained from the nearest government health facility, drugs purchased by the research team and patients referred to the same health facilities for management.
Participants who were evaluated as probable brucellosis cases were referred to the nearest government health facilities for management.
330.Rationale: While the passage states that 22 workers were evaluated as probable cases and referred for management, referral alone does not by itself demonstrate that seropositivity reflected active or recent infection rather than past exposure. The text indicates these were "probable brucellosis cases," implying assessment included clinical evaluation; it does not show that all seropositive persons were treated regardless of symptoms. Thus referral documents clinical concern but is not the strongest direct evidence of active/recent infection compared with combined high titres plus compatible symptoms.

330.Option: The high seroprevalence of 9.0% among ruminant slaughterhouse workers, as this indicates widespread active transmission in the population
Evidence: Among the ruminant slaughterhouse workers, the sero-prevalence according to results of the standard RBT protocol was 9.0% (31/344) (95% CI: 6.3–12.7).
Positive results also need to be carefully evaluated because antibodies to Brucella may result from contact without clinical disease and may remain after successful therapy.
330.Rationale: A cross-sectional seroprevalence (9.0%) documents past or current exposure at the population level but does not distinguish active/recent infection from remote, clinically irrelevant exposure. The passage explicitly warns that antibodies "may result from contact without clinical disease and may remain after successful therapy," so prevalence alone is insufficient to indicate active infection.

330.Option: High antibody titers in both RBT and BrucellaCapt combined with self-reported symptoms consistent with acute brucellosis
Evidence: Out of the 31 sera positive in the standard RBT protocol, eight had a titre ≥ 1:8 in the modified RBT protocol, a very high titre in BrucellaCapt and seven self-reported symptoms (one case did not return this information) compatible with brucellosis.
Of these, serological evidence shows that at least 1 out 3 (12/31), possibly twice as much (25/31), were actively infected (Table 3).

Considering that for this test, ≥ 1:160–320 diagnostic titers are recommended [29,48], comparison of the results of this study strongly suggest that a RBT titer ≥ 1:4 is diagnostic under local conditions, making unnecessary a significant proportion of additional tests.
330.Rationale: The strongest evidence that seropositivity reflects active or recent infection is the combination of high diagnostic antibody titres (both RBT dilution titres and high BrucellaCapt titres) together with compatible clinical symptoms. The authors use exactly this combination to infer that a substantial fraction were "actively infected" and refer to recommended diagnostic titre cutoffs (BrucellaCapt ≥160–320, RBT dilutions) to support that high titres plus symptoms indicate active disease rather than past exposure.

330.Option: Seropositivity detected by the RBT alone without confirmation by BrucellaCapt, as this test is highly specific in endemic settings
Evidence: Normally used with plain serum, RBT can be positive in persons from endemic areas and risk groups that do not have clinical symptoms, a problem largely solved by testing serum dilutions.
Positive results also need to be carefully evaluated because antibodies to Brucella may result from contact without clinical disease and may remain after successful therapy.
Provided antigen quality is good, the standard RBT shows excellent performance ... and the specificity in persons from endemic areas and risk groups can be optimized by testing serum dilutions.
330.Rationale: The passage states that RBT alone can be positive in asymptomatic persons in endemic settings and that serum dilutions or complementary testing (e.g., BrucellaCapt) are needed to distinguish clinically meaningful titres. Thus RBT alone without confirmation is not the strongest indicator of active/recent infection.

330.Option: The presence of fever, headache, and arthralgia in seropositive individuals, as these are pathognomonic for acute brucellosis and distinguish it from other febrile illnesses
Evidence: The symptoms reported by the serologically positive patients were variable but most reported with pyrexia (38.7%), headache (22.6%), arthralgia (22.6%), chills (16.1%), fatigue (12.9%), myalgia (9.7%) and swollen joints (6.5%).

brucellosis signs and symptoms are unspecific, protean and can be confused with those of several other febrile illnesses, including malaria, tuberculosis and typhoid fever, all endemic in many African countries [6,15].

Brucellosis is a complex zoonotic disease lacking specific symptoms, making it easy to confuse with fevers of unknown origin (FUO) and because of the low index of suspicion by clinicians it often goes undetected and untreated [9,30,58].
330.Rationale: Although fever, headache and arthralgia were common among seropositive individuals, the passage explicitly states that brucellosis symptoms are nonspecific and can resemble many other febrile illnesses. Therefore these symptoms are not pathognomonic and by themselves do not reliably distinguish active brucellosis from other causes or past exposure.

331.Option: Using a fluorescence-based detection system with a 450 nm emission filter allows for real-time monitoring of NADPH consumption and reduces interference from non-fluorescent compounds
Evidence: Because NADPH is naturally fluorescent, emitting at ∼450 nm, while its oxidized counterpart NADP is not, we switched the detection platform for the coupled reaction from absorbance to fluorescence, a step that parallels the application of profluorescent substrates in assays for phosphatases and proteases [23], with the main difference being the fluorescence change trending from high to low in this reaction.

The plate was transferred to ViewLux high-throughput CCD imager (Perkin-Elmer, Wellesley, MA) where kinetic measurements (16 reads, one read every 30 sec) of the NADPH fluorescence decrease were acquired using 365 nm excitation/450 nm emission filter set.

Because of the anticipated fluorescence interference from compound library members in this blue-shifted detection region and because the output generated from NADPH is not very strong (due to the combination of low extinction coefficient and quantum yield), we further modified the detection format of the assay to measure the reaction progress in kinetic mode as opposed to collecting a single end-point read.
331.Rationale: Although the assay did use fluorescence detection at ~450 nm (365 nm excitation/450 nm emission) to monitor NADPH consumption, the passage explicitly states that fluorescence interference from library members in this detection region was anticipated and problematic. The authors therefore implemented additional measures (kinetic reads and storing initial fluorescence values) to identify fluorescent artifacts. Thus the mere use of a fluorescence-based detector/emission filter does not by itself reduce interference from fluorescent compounds and is not the feature that most directly supports distinguishing true activity from fluorescent artifacts.

331.Option: The use of a dual-enzyme assay system increases signal robustness and allows for direct comparison of inhibition profiles between TGR and Prx2
Evidence: By performing the high-throughput screen against both enzymes (present at equivalent levels in the assay), we were able to address both novel targets simultaneously while also combining target deconvolution and confirmation at the post-screen stage.

When tested against Prx2 in a GR-coupled hydrogen peroxide reduction assay none of the selected compounds showed activity up to the 50 µM top concentration tested (and by extension, none were active against GR, an enzyme related to TGR). Prx2 was therefore ruled out as the target of any of the actives identified in the screen.
Such kinetic mode data acquisition not only secured a more robust assay signal but minimized the interfering effects of dust and fluorescent but otherwise inert library members (see Discussion).
331.Rationale: Screening both enzymes together provided the ability to address two targets and later deconvolute which enzyme was actually inhibited, which increases assay usefulness and robustness. However, the question asks specifically about distinguishing true biological activity from fluorescent artifacts (compounds that emit light at longer wavelength). The passage attributes the ability to identify fluorescent artifacts to kinetic/time-course data and inspection of initial fluorescence (y-intercept), not to the dual-enzyme design. Thus the dual-enzyme system is not the most direct feature for distinguishing fluorescent artifacts.

331.Option: The high Z′ factor of 0.76 indicates that the assay has sufficient signal-to-noise ratio to reliably detect true inhibitors without requiring additional artifact filtering
Evidence: The assay performed robustly, yielding an average Z′ value of 0.76 [30].
Compounds which showed activity but also had elevated y-intercept values were flagged as potential fluorescent artifacts.

Because of the anticipated fluorescence interference from compound library members in this blue-shifted detection region and because the output generated from NADPH is not very strong (due to the combination of low extinction coefficient and quantum yield), we further modified the detection format of the assay to measure the reaction progress in kinetic mode as opposed to collecting a single end-point read.
331.Rationale: A high Z' indicates good assay performance and signal-to-noise, but the passage makes clear that fluorescent artifacts were still a concern and required explicit post-hoc identification (e.g., flagging elevated y-intercepts). Therefore, the Z' alone did not obviate the need for artifact filtering, and it is not the feature most directly responsible for distinguishing fluorescent artifacts.

331.Option: The inclusion of detergent in the assay buffer prevents compound aggregation and reduces false positives from non-specific inhibition
Evidence: Additionally, in order to minimize the interfering effect of promiscuous inhibitors acting via colloidal aggregate formation [34],[35], we included detergent in the assay buffer.
Compounds which showed activity but also had elevated y-intercept values were flagged as potential fluorescent artifacts.
Such kinetic mode data acquisition not only secured a more robust assay signal but minimized the interfering effects of dust and fluorescent but otherwise inert library members (see Discussion).
331.Rationale: Including detergent was intended to reduce false positives due to compound aggregation (colloidal aggregates), which addresses a different class of artifacts (non-specific inhibition). It does not directly address fluorescent compounds that emit light and thus is not the feature most directly supporting distinction between true biological inhibition and fluorescence-based artifacts.

331.Option: Retaining kinetic time-course data with initial fluorescence values enables post-hoc identification of fluorescent artifacts
Evidence: Thus, for each plate the reaction progress was recorded for 8 minutes at the rate of one fluorescence read every 30 seconds. Such kinetic mode data acquisition not only secured a more robust assay signal but minimized the interfering effects of dust and fluorescent but otherwise inert library members (see Discussion).
For activity calculations, delta values were chosen while the calculated slope, intercept, and the raw time-course data were stored in the database.

In addition, the y-intercept of the reaction progress plot, typically equal to fluorescence at the first time point, was stored in the database and was used to further scrutinize purported actives. Compounds which showed activity but also had elevated y-intercept values were flagged as potential fluorescent artifacts.

Because the first time-point values (when the enzymatic reaction has produced minimal amount of product) associated with each compound well are stored in the database, a further analysis can be performed to flag interfering fluorescent library members [32].
331.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that kinetic (time-course) measurements were collected and that the y-intercept/initial fluorescence values were stored and used to flag interfering fluorescent compounds. This approach allows discrimination between true enzyme-driven decreases in NADPH fluorescence and wells that start with high fluorescence (fluorescent artifacts), making the retention of kinetic data with initial fluorescence the most direct feature enabling identification of fluorescent artifacts.

332.Option: The age range of participants (18–76 years) ensures that immune responses are not influenced by developmental stage differences
Evidence: Ten volunteers between the ages of 18 and 76 were recruited over the course of two months (Table 1).

While our results shed light on the patent mechanisms of immunosuppression present in hookworm infection, it is important to mention that the sample size and age variation of our studied population might be considered as possible limiting factors of our study.

although effort was made to match the nutritional status of all participants (endemic and non-endemic areas), unfortunately perfect matching of control individuals and infected patients by age and sex was not always possible.
332.Rationale: Although the study enrolled adults (ages 18–76), the authors explicitly note age variation as a potential limitation and that perfect matching by age and sex was not always possible.
