Therefore the age range does not ensure that developmental-stage or age-related immune differences are excluded as confounders; the passage itself warns that age variation could affect interpretation.

332.Option: The use of both crude and excreted-secreted antigens in stimulation assays increases the sensitivity of detecting antigen-specific immune responses
Evidence: Whole blood was stimulated in vitro with ES and crude antigens (5 µg/well) in RPMI 1640 media supplemented with ... for 24 hours at 37°C with 5% CO2.
PBMCs from infected individuals pulsed with excreted/secreted products or hookworm crude antigens presented an impaired cellular proliferation, which was augmented after the depletion of Treg cells.
332.Rationale: Using both crude and excreted/secreted (ES) antigens supports detection of hookworm-specific cellular responses and thus helps link observed immunologic effects to parasite antigens. However, this methodological point alone does not most strongly exclude general health or demographic confounding — it shows antigen specificity of measured responses but does not by itself rule out that baseline health, nutrition, age, or other demographic factors caused the differences. Therefore it is not the strongest single factor supporting that changes are specifically due to chronic parasitic exposure.

332.Option: The absence of other helminth infections in both groups, confirmed by multiple diagnostic techniques, rules out confounding by co-infections
Evidence: Individuals were selected on the basis of not having any other helminth infection (mono-infected after analysis of 6 slides of Kato-Katz fecal thick-smear and Baermann-Moraes techniques).

None of these individuals had a history of Necator infection and all presented with egg-negative stool (6 slides of Kato-Katz fecal thick-smear and Baermann-Moraes techniques) and no specific antibodies to Necator crude antigen extracts.
332.Rationale: The study explicitly confirmed mono-infection status using multiple diagnostic methods and ensured controls were egg-negative and antibody-negative. This directly rules out co-infecting helminths as alternative explanations for the altered Treg frequencies and function, making this the strongest factor in attributing the observed immunological changes to chronic hookworm exposure rather than to other parasitic co-infections.

332.Option: The use of individuals from the same geographic region as controls ensures that environmental exposures other than hookworm are similar between groups
Evidence: Ten hookworm-naive individuals were enrolled as healthy non-infected individuals from Belo Horizonte, Minas Gerais State, Brazil, where no transmission occurs.

The absence of negative individuals from endemic areas as controls was preferred once it is not possible to guarantee the absence of infection in these donors (due to the limited sensitivity of fecal exams or long pre-patency period).
332.Rationale: Controls were recruited from Belo Horizonte, a non-endemic area, not from the same endemic geographic region as the infected individuals. The authors explicitly explain that they did not use negative individuals from endemic areas. Therefore the study did not match geographic region, and this option is incorrect as a factor supporting that immunological differences are not due to other environmental exposures.

332.Option: The comparable nutritional status between infected individuals and hookworm-naive controls from a non-endemic area reduces the likelihood that observed immune differences are due to malnutrition or socioeconomic factors
Evidence: The nutritional status of non-infected volunteers (controls) was similar to those presented by hookworm-infected individuals as determined by anthropometric measurements.
The nutritional status of adults was determined using the absolute body mass index and classified as eutrophic (18.5–24.9 kg/m2), underweight (<18.5 kg/m2) or overweight (≥25 kg/m2) [21].

although effort was made to match the nutritional status of all participants (endemic and non-endemic areas), unfortunately perfect matching of control individuals and infected patients by age and sex was not always possible.
332.Rationale: By measuring and reporting similar nutritional status between infected and control groups, the investigators reduced the likelihood that malnutrition or related socioeconomic differences explain the immune alterations. While not perfect (authors acknowledge imperfect matching on other demographics), comparable nutrition is a direct control for a major potential confounder (iron/protein deficiency) relevant to hookworm pathology and immune function, and thus strongly supports parasite-specific attribution of the findings.

333.Option: Use a combination of multiple parasitological methods and antigen detection to establish a composite reference standard
Evidence: In order to evaluate the performance of the different diagnostic tests, a “Reference Standard” was established, which included all positive results (visible eggs) from any of the parasitological methods used (18 KK slides, saline gradient, and Helmintex).

In the present study, we performed a combination of alternative parasitological methods to detect more precisely intestinal schistosomiasis in an endemic area in Brazil. The thorough parasitological investigation allowed us to implement a new reference standard to detect active S. mansoni infection and to evaluate each of the parasitological methods for its performance and accuracy.

We believe that a combination of methods has to be implemented since the schistosomiasis control programs in different regions of the world are moving from morbidity control towards transmission control and elimination.

The combination of two KK slides from one fecal sample (1SPL K1-K2) with Helmintex or of six slides from three fecal samples (SPL1-3 K1-K2) with POC-CCA resulted in the highest prevalence rates (45.4% and 60.3%) and highest sensitivity rates (90.0% and 88.3%) (Table 6).
333.Rationale: The study itself established and used a composite reference standard combining multiple parasitological methods (18 KK slides, saline gradient, Helmintex) to reduce misclassification from low-sensitivity single tests. The authors explicitly state they performed a combination of methods, created a reference standard from all positive results, and conclude that combining methods yields the highest prevalence detection and sensitivity. Therefore using a combined/paralleled diagnostic approach to form a composite reference standard is the most appropriate strategy to minimize misclassification bias in this low-endemicity setting.

333.Option: Use the POC-CCA rapid urine test as the primary diagnostic tool due to its high sensitivity and ease of use in field settings
Evidence: The POC-CCA identified 108 out of a total of 228 individuals as infected, which resulted in a prevalence of 47.4% and a sensitivity of 64.9%, when compared with the reference standard (18 Kato-Katz slides, saline gradient, and Helmintex).

The sensitivity of the POC-CCA was superior to the saline gradient and comparable to the results obtained with six KK slides from three fecal samples. However, the kappa index of the urine test was considerably lower than that obtained with the other parasitological tests (Table 5).

In our study, the main shortcoming of the rapid urine test (POC-CCA) was a low concordance with the reference standard, since we found 30 and 35% of false positive and false negative results, respectively.

The POC-CCA showed a sensitivity of approximately 65%, which is superior to that obtained with the saline gradient method, comparable with the results obtained with KK variant using six KK slides from three fecal samples, and inferior to the sensitivity found with Helmintex, when the criteria of evaluation were used as indicated by the manufacturer that is, if ‘trace’ was considered a positive result.
333.Rationale: Although POC-CCA is easier to use in the field and showed moderate sensitivity (~65%), the study found substantial false positives and false negatives and low concordance with the composite reference standard. The authors caution that POC-CCA alone is insufficient in this low-endemicity Brazilian setting. Thus it is not appropriate as the sole primary diagnostic to minimize misclassification bias.

333.Option: Increase the number of Kato-Katz slides examined from one fecal sample to 14 to improve sensitivity and reduce false negatives
Evidence: The inclusion of additional parasitological methods for schistosome diagnosis resulted in the detection of a much higher number of infected individuals within the study population (Table 3). The apparent prevalence rose from 20.4 to 29.9%, when the number of KK slides was increased from two to 14 slides,
In comparison, if only one fecal sample was processed, the sensitivity remained around 60%, even when the number of examined slides was increased to 12 or 14 (Table 3 and Fig 3).
The analysis also demonstrated that the sensitivity of the KK method increased from 41.4% with two slides from one fecal sample to up to 66.7% with six slides from three fecal samples.

we showed that even using the superior version of the KK technique, which involves analyzing two slides from three different fecal samples, we still missed more than one third of the infected population.
333.Rationale: Increasing the number of KK slides from a single fecal sample (even up to 14) does raise apparent prevalence but, when only one fecal sample is used, sensitivity plateaued around ~60% and still misses many infections. The study shows that better gains come from multiple fecal samples and from combining methods (e.g., Helmintex), so simply increasing to 14 slides from one sample is insufficient to minimize misclassification bias.

333.Option: Match cases and controls on age and sex to reduce confounding in the estimation of prevalence
Evidence: Most of the infected individuals in endemic areas harbor low parasite loads and are very unlikely to be detected with the commonly used parasitological methods.
The KK method (Kato-Katz) ... shows reduced sensitivity in individuals with low parasite loads.

An important result of the NSCP was a significant reduction in the number of severe clinical cases and deaths due to S. mansoni infection. However, the failure to correctly identify all or most of the individuals with low parasite burden by the standard parasitological approach (1 or 2 KK slides) has contributed to the continuation of S. mansoni infection...
333.Rationale: Misclassification here arises from diagnostic test sensitivity (false negatives/positives), not from confounding by demographic variables. Matching cases and controls on age/sex is a design tool for analytic studies to control confounding, not a strategy to reduce diagnostic misclassification in prevalence estimation. The passage emphasizes poor test sensitivity and the need for improved diagnostics, not matching to address misclassification.

333.Option: Use a single fecal sample with six Kato-Katz slides and one POC-CCA test to balance sensitivity and operational feasibility
Evidence: The analysis also demonstrated that the sensitivity of the KK method increased from 41.4% with two slides from one fecal sample to up to 66.7% with six slides from three fecal samples.
In comparison, if only one fecal sample was processed, the sensitivity remained around 60%, even when the number of examined slides was increased to 12 or 14 (Table 3 and Fig 3).

The combination of two KK slides from one fecal sample (1SPL K1-K2) with Helmintex or of six slides from three fecal samples (SPL1-3 K1-K2) with POC-CCA resulted in the highest prevalence rates (45.4% and 60.3%) and highest sensitivity rates (90.0% and 88.3%) (Table 6).
We found that the combination of the KK method with the POC-CCA test produced better results when the number of slides and fecal samples was increased (Table 6).
333.Rationale: The study indicates that six Kato-Katz slides are helpful when distributed across three fecal samples (not from a single sample), and that combining multiple-sample KK variants with POC-CCA yields high sensitivity. A single fecal sample with six KK slides plus one POC-CCA would likely have limited sensitivity (~60%) and miss many infections. Thus this single-sample approach is not the best strategy to minimize misclassification bias compared with multi-sample and multi-method combinations.

334.Option: The presence of Translationally Controlled Tumor Protein (TCTP), which is involved in cell proliferation and immune modulation, suggests the spider may act as a reservoir for pathogens whose spread is enhanced by venom activity
Evidence: Toxins including hyaluronidase, proteases, low molecular mass insecticidal peptides, Translationally Controlled Tumor Protein (TCTP) and phospholipases-D have been identified [1], [2], [13], [14], [15], [16], [17].

By degrading glycosaminoglycans, the hyaluronidase reduces the viscosity of hyaluronic acid and renders the extracellular matrix less rigid. This change makes the matrix more permeable to other toxins and facilitates the spread of other venom constituents and inflammatory cell mediators [21], [22].
For the first time in the literature, experimental results strongly indicate that the hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom is in fact a “spreading factor” for this venom.
334.Rationale: While the passage lists TCTP among identified venom proteins, it provides no evidence that TCTP promotes dissemination of microbes or acts as a spreading factor. The passage explicitly attributes matrix degradation and facilitation of spread to hyaluronidase (with experimental proof), not to TCTP. Therefore TCTP does not most strongly support the reservoir/zoonotic dissemination hypothesis based on the provided text.

334.Option: The presence of low molecular mass insecticidal peptides, which are structurally similar to known bacterial toxins, suggests the spider may act as a reservoir for pathogens whose spread is enhanced by venom activity
Evidence: Toxins including hyaluronidase, proteases, low molecular mass insecticidal peptides, Translationally Controlled Tumor Protein (TCTP) and phospholipases-D have been identified [1], [2], [13], [14], [15], [16], [17].

The idea that brown spider venom hyaluronidases play a role in the gravitational spreading of dermonecrosis and/or systemic diffusion of venom toxins, and then act as “spreading factors” comes from its degradative activity on hyaluronic acid and other glycosaminoglycans that mediate tissue integrity and stability.
For the first time in the literature, experimental results strongly indicate that the hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom is in fact a “spreading factor” for this venom.
334.Rationale: The passage names low molecular mass insecticidal peptides as part of the venom composition but does not state they are structurally similar to bacterial toxins or that they facilitate pathogen dissemination. The text specifically points to hyaluronidase as the enzyme that degrades extracellular matrix and acts as a spreading factor; therefore the insecticidal peptides are not the strongest support for the pathogen-reservoir hypothesis in this passage.

334.Option: The presence of hyaluronidase, which degrades extracellular matrix components and facilitates microbial dissemination, suggests the spider may act as a reservoir for pathogens whose spread is enhanced by venom activity
Evidence: The delivery of venom toxins to neighboring bite sites and into systemic circulation is assisted by molecules that degrade extracellular matrix constituents such as proteases and hyaluronidases [9], [10], [11], [12].

The idea that brown spider venom hyaluronidases play a role in the gravitational spreading of dermonecrosis and/or systemic diffusion of venom toxins, and then act as “spreading factors” comes from its degradative activity on hyaluronic acid and other glycosaminoglycans that mediate tissue integrity and stability.

By degrading glycosaminoglycans, the hyaluronidase reduces the viscosity of hyaluronic acid and renders the extracellular matrix less rigid. This change makes the matrix more permeable to other toxins and facilitates the spread of other venom constituents and inflammatory cell mediators [21], [22].
Macroscopically, Dietrich's Hyaluronidase increases the erythema, ecchymosis and dermonecrotic lesion area induced by recombinant phospholipase-D along exposure times observed.
For the first time in the literature, experimental results strongly indicate that the hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom is in fact a “spreading factor” for this venom.
Based on the spreading property, we extrapolated that this molecule would be primarily responsible for spreading other venom toxins from the bite site into the systemic circulation [1], [2].
334.Rationale: The passage provides direct mechanistic and experimental evidence that hyaluronidase degrades extracellular matrix glycosaminoglycans, reduces matrix viscosity, and acts as a spreading factor that facilitates diffusion of venom components into neighboring tissue and the systemic circulation. Those properties most directly support the hypothesis that the spider could act as a reservoir for a pathogen whose dissemination is aided by venom-mediated tissue barrier disruption.

334.Option: The presence of phospholipases-D, which induce cell membrane disruption and systemic inflammation, suggests the spider may act as a reservoir for pathogens whose spread is enhanced by venom-induced tissue damage
Evidence: Toxins including hyaluronidase, proteases, low molecular mass insecticidal peptides, Translationally Controlled Tumor Protein (TCTP) and phospholipases-D have been identified [1], [2], [13], [14], [15], [16], [17].
Experimental inoculation of purified recombinant dermonecrotic toxins (phospholipases-D, non-proteolytic or hyaluronidase activities), evokes gravitational spreading of skin lesion on rabbits [34].
Macroscopically, Dietrich's Hyaluronidase increases the erythema, ecchymosis and dermonecrotic lesion area induced by recombinant phospholipase-D along exposure times observed.
334.Rationale: Phospholipase-D toxins clearly cause dermonecrosis and systemic inflammation, but the passage emphasizes hyaluronidase as the specific component that degrades the extracellular matrix and facilitates spread of other toxins. Phospholipase-Ds produce tissue damage but the text provides stronger, direct evidence that hyaluronidase (not phospholipase-D) enhances dissemination—so phospholipase-D is not the most convincing feature supporting a reservoir-for-pathogen hypothesis.

334.Option: The presence of proteases, which degrade host proteins and induce systemic inflammation, suggests the spider may act as a reservoir for pathogens whose spread is enhanced by venom activity
Evidence: The delivery of venom toxins to neighboring bite sites and into systemic circulation is assisted by molecules that degrade extracellular matrix constituents such as proteases and hyaluronidases [9], [10], [11], [12].

Toxins including hyaluronidase, proteases, low molecular mass insecticidal peptides, Translationally Controlled Tumor Protein (TCTP) and phospholipases-D have been identified [1], [2], [13], [14], [15], [16], [17].
For the first time in the literature, experimental results strongly indicate that the hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom is in fact a “spreading factor” for this venom.
334.Rationale: Proteases are noted as extracellular-matrix–degrading components and could plausibly aid dissemination, but the passage provides explicit experimental demonstration and mechanistic emphasis on hyaluronidase as the spreading factor. Because hyaluronidase is directly shown to render tissue more permeable and to facilitate systemic diffusion, proteases are not presented as the strongest supporting feature for the reservoir/zoonotic dissemination hypothesis in this text.

335.Option: Intermediate levels of flooding agricultural and grassland land cover are associated with optimal environmental conditions for Taenia egg survival, increasing cysticercosis risk independently of direct host contact
Evidence: A non-linear relationship (quadratic) was indicated between the percentage of land that was flooding agricultural and grassland, and presence of Taenia spp. antigen in humans (p = 0.04).

The percentage of land that was flooding agricultural or grassland had a quadratic relationship with presence of Taenia spp. antigen in humans: lower odds of antigen presence were associated with very low or very high percentages of this land cover class, while the odds of antigen presence were highest in areas with intermediate percentages of the land cover class (OR = 1.09 and 0.998; p = 0.03 and 0.03 for the linear and squared terms respectively).
Flooding agricultural land and grassland was also (non-linearly) associated with presence of antigens in humans, indicating a role for landscape factors in cysticercosis.
335.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports a statistically significant quadratic association between percentage flooding agricultural/grassland and human Taenia spp. antigen presence, with highest odds at intermediate coverage. This finding indicates that landscape-level environmental conditions (flood-prone agricultural/grassland) are associated with infection risk, supporting the interpretation that environmental factors affecting egg survival and deposition—rather than only direct contact with infected hosts—contribute to human cysticercosis risk.

335.Option: Increased cysticercosis risk in areas with high rainfall, as greater moisture supports egg survival and environmental transmission
Evidence: Precipitation was negatively associated with the outcome (OR = 0.998, p = 0.02).
Based on the previous discussion regarding flooding and access to ground water, this relationship is not as expected.
335.Rationale: Although moisture can support egg survival in theory, the study data do not show higher cysticercosis risk with greater rainfall; instead precipitation was significantly negatively associated with antigen presence. The authors explicitly note this was unexpected and provide contextual explanations (e.g. overland flow washing eggs away, spatial patterns of flooding and water source use). Thus the option claiming increased risk with high rainfall is not supported by the study findings.

335.Option: Cysticercosis risk is highest in areas with high pig density, indicating that animal reservoirs are the primary determinant of environmental transmission
Evidence: Of the 416 households, 56.2% kept cattle and 16.9% kept pigs.

The final model for presence of Taenia spp. antigen in pigs (Table 5) indicated that breeding sows had significantly higher odds of antigen presence compared to male pigs (OR = 10.35, p = 0.01), and flooding agricultural land and grassland demonstrated a positive association with the outcome (OR = 1.04, p = 0.004).

The results presented here indicate endemic transmission of Taenia spp. in the study area, and demonstrate significant associations between assumed cysticercosis in both pigs and humans (based on presence of Taenia spp. HP10 antigen in sera), and land cover, after accounting for other known risk factors.
335.Rationale: The study does not report pig density as the primary predictor of human cysticercosis risk. Instead, porcine infection was associated with breeding sows and flooding land cover; human infection showed associations with environmental variables (land cover, precipitation) and water source. The evidence emphasizes environmental (landscape) influences rather than simple pig density as the dominant driver of human tissue infection risk in this analysis.

335.Option: Cysticercosis risk increases with the use of unprotected water sources, as this facilitates ingestion of eggs from contaminated soil
Evidence: The use of wells as a water source was associated with a higher odds of antigen presence (OR = 2.76, p = 0.02).

Wells, although they may be improved (e.g. covered to prevent surface water influx), are generally at higher risk of contamination as they are often left uncovered or inadequately covered, allowing potentially contaminated surface water to enter.

Within the study area, well water is more common in areas with frequent flooding, presumably due to the requirement of a high water table. This combination of flooding and vulnerable water supplies may enhance contamination, thus increasing the risk of infection.
335.Rationale: The study found a significant association between use of wells and higher odds of Taenia spp. antigen in humans, and the authors describe wells as vulnerable to faecal contamination—linking water source quality and environmental contamination to human infection risk. This supports the option that unprotected or vulnerable water sources facilitate environmental transmission (ingestion of eggs), independent of direct contact with infected individuals or animals.

335.Option: Higher prevalence of cysticercosis in households with pigs kept in close proximity to human dwellings, suggesting direct transmission through animal contact
Evidence: Of the 55 pig-keeping households, 13 had at least one pig with detected Taenia spp. antigen.

The proximity to tapeworm carriers has also been identified as an important factor determining the occurrence of cysticercosis [15,16]. As this study was based on a sample of individuals from the study area, information on the locations of all tapeworm carriers was not available and, thus, it was not possible to include this aspect in our analysis.

Sanitation related covariates (frequency an individual uses a latrine; presence of latrine within household; type of latrine; evidence of latrine use; or evidence of scavenging around the latrine by pigs), which have previously been shown to be associated with cysticercosis occurrence, were not significantly correlated with the presence of Taenia spp. antigen in pigs or humans.
335.Rationale: The study does not present evidence that proximity of pigs to dwellings (direct animal contact) was the primary driver of human cysticercosis in their data. The authors note they lacked comprehensive data on locations of tapeworm carriers and could not include proximity to carriers in analyses. Moreover, key sanitation variables and pig-scavenging indicators were not significantly associated in this dataset. Therefore the option asserting direct transmission via proximate pigs as the strongest suggestion is not supported by the study's findings.

336.Option: Individuals with high perceived susceptibility and severity are more likely to maintain high willingness to pay regardless of income level
Evidence: For HBM constructs, similar to vaccination intention, a higher marginal WTP was significantly associated with the items in the perception of susceptibility and severity of COVID-19, barriers and cues to action constructs.

A significant positive moderating effect of income level on the relationship perceived susceptibility and WTP was found (β = 3.007, p = 0.045). Income level was also found significantly moderated the relationship between perceived severity and WTP (β = 3.980, p = 0.026).
336.Rationale: While the passage states that perceived susceptibility and severity are positively associated with higher marginal WTP, it explicitly reports that income level significantly moderates those relationships. The presence of a positive moderating effect of income on the susceptibility–WTP and severity–WTP relationships contradicts the claim that high perceived susceptibility/severity would maintain high WTP 'regardless of income level.' Therefore the option's 'regardless of income level' condition is not supported by the passage.

336.Option: Younger individuals will maintain high willingness to pay over time due to higher baseline WTP and greater trust in new vaccines
Evidence: Younger aged participants had a higher WTP for an amount of CNY¥300/400 [US$43/57] and ≥CNY¥500 [US$72] over CNY¥100/200[US$14/28].
Perceived benefits have a strong and positive effect on vaccination intention
336.Rationale: The passage does report that younger participants had higher baseline WTP, which supports the first half of the option. However, it does not provide evidence that younger people have greater trust in new vaccines (the study addresses confidence in domestic vs foreign vaccines but not age-related trust), nor does it indicate that age is the strongest determinant of maintaining WTP over time. The passage emphasizes HBM constructs and income as key correlates/moderators of WTP maintenance rather than age, so the asserted causal mechanism ('greater trust in new vaccines') and the claim that age is the strongest influence are unsupported.

336.Option: The perceived benefit of vaccination is the strongest predictor of sustained willingness to pay over time, even as protection wanes
Evidence: Perceived benefits have a strong and positive effect on vaccination intention

For HBM constructs, similar to vaccination intention, a higher marginal WTP was significantly associated with the items in the perception of susceptibility and severity of COVID-19, barriers and cues to action constructs.
336.Rationale: Although perceived benefits are described as a strong predictor of vaccination intention, the passage specifically identifies perceived susceptibility and severity (plus barriers and cues to action) as the HBM constructs significantly associated with higher marginal WTP. It does not state that perceived benefits are the strongest predictor of WTP. Therefore claiming perceived benefit is the strongest predictor of sustained WTP is not supported by the passage, especially the qualifier 'even as protection wanes' which is not addressed.

336.Option: Individuals who perceive high economic barriers are more likely to increase their willingness to pay if they believe the vaccine is effective and safe
Evidence: Perceived susceptibility and severity of COVID-19 correlated positively with higher marginal WTP, while the perceived economic barrier was negatively correlated with higher marginal WTP.
Although many were concerned about the cost of the COVID-19 vaccine, the affordability barrier was nonetheless not a significant predictor of definite vaccination intention.
336.Rationale: The passage reports that perceived economic barriers were negatively correlated with higher marginal WTP, meaning those who perceive economic barriers tend to have lower WTP, not higher. While belief in effectiveness and safety influence vaccination intention (and perceived barriers include concerns about efficacy and side effects), there is no evidence in the passage that individuals with high perceived economic barriers would increase WTP when convinced of effectiveness/safety. On the contrary, economic barriers are portrayed as reducing WTP.

336.Option: High-income individuals' willingness to pay is more responsive to changes in perceived disease severity or susceptibility over time
Evidence: For HBM constructs, similar to vaccination intention, a higher marginal WTP was significantly associated with the items in the perception of susceptibility and severity of COVID-19, barriers and cues to action constructs.

A significant positive moderating effect of income level on the relationship perceived susceptibility and WTP was found (β = 3.007, p = 0.045). Income level was also found significantly moderated the relationship between perceived severity and WTP (β = 3.980, p = 0.026).
There was a gradual increase in the odds of WTP for an amount of CNY¥500 [US$72] over CNY¥100/200 [US$14/28] with an increase in household income.
336.Rationale: The passage directly reports that perceived susceptibility and severity are associated with higher marginal WTP and that income level positively moderates those relationships, indicating the effect of perceived severity/susceptibility on WTP is stronger at higher income levels. It also notes higher household income is associated with greater odds of higher WTP. Thus, high-income individuals' WTP being more responsive to changes in perceived disease severity or susceptibility is the interpretation best supported by the passage.

337.Option: A positive correlation between DHF incidence and vector abundance in the intermediate age group, with no significant relationship in younger or older individuals
Evidence: Univariate analysis revealed that incidence and HI were positively correlated in the youngest age class (Figure 2C); whereas, DHF incidence and HI were negatively correlated in the oldest age class (Figure 2E). A non-monotonic relationship between DHF incidence and HI was detected within the intermediate age class (Figure 2D).

As shown in Figure 6, averaging only the last three years of each simulation resulted in a negligibly detectable relationship between DHF incidence and R0, which greatly resembled the empirical relationship (compare with Figure 2B).
337.Rationale: The option states positivity only in the intermediate age group and no relationship in younger or older groups. The passage explicitly reports a positive correlation in the youngest age class, a negative correlation in the oldest age class, and a non-monotonic relationship in the intermediate class. Thus the asserted pattern (positive in intermediate only, null in others) contradicts the empirical age-stratified findings.

337.Option: A positive correlation between DHF incidence and vector abundance across all age groups, with the strongest association observed in the oldest age group
Evidence: Univariate analysis revealed that incidence and HI were positively correlated in the youngest age class (Figure 2C); whereas, DHF incidence and HI was negatively correlated in the oldest age class (Figure 2E). A non-monotonic relationship between DHF incidence and HI was detected within the intermediate age class (Figure 2D).

Analysis of DHF incidence among the entire Thai population revealed that incidence rose up to HI of about 30 and gradually declined thereafter. This non-monotonic relationship appears to be consistent with a state of endemic stability.
337.Rationale: The option claims a uniformly positive correlation across all ages, strongest in the oldest. The passage reports the opposite for the oldest age class (a negative correlation) and a non-monotonic pattern in the intermediate class. Therefore this option is not supported by the data.

337.Option: A non-monotonic relationship between DHF incidence and vector abundance in the intermediate age group, with positive correlation in young children and negative correlation in older individuals
Evidence: Univariate analysis revealed that incidence and HI were positively correlated in the youngest age class (Figure 2C); whereas, DHF incidence and HI were negatively correlated in the oldest age class (Figure 2E). A non-monotonic relationship between DHF incidence and HI was detected within the intermediate age class (Figure 2D).

This model hypothesized that, due to the presence of transient cross-serotype immunity, the incidence of DHF and transmission intensity will be correlated negatively at high transmission intensities... the incidence of DHF could be correlated negatively to transmission intensity at areas of intense transmission, while the correlation is positive only at low levels of transmission. In the present study, such a complex correlation structure mixed with positive and negative correlations will be called “non-monotonic”, hereafter.
GAM detected a non-monotonic relationship in the simulations with cross-immunity (Figure 7A)...

Cross-immunity explains not only this non-monotonic relationship (Figure S2 in Protocol S1), but also the wide variation in incidence of DHF, as well as its complex response to the duration of window for averaging (Figure S8 in Protocol S1).
337.Rationale: The hypothesis that transient cross-protective immunity modulates severity at high transmission predicts a complex (non-monotonic) population-level relationship and divergent age patterns. The passage shows exactly this: positive correlation in youngest, negative in oldest, and a non-monotonic pattern in the intermediate age group, and further notes that simulations with cross-immunity produced a non-monotonic relationship. Therefore the described empirical pattern in the option best supports the cross-immunity hypothesis.

337.Option: A monotonic negative relationship between DHF incidence and vector abundance in all age groups, with no variation by age
Evidence: Univariate analysis revealed that incidence and HI were positively correlated in the youngest age class (Figure 2C); whereas, DHF incidence and HI were negatively correlated in the oldest age class (Figure 2E). A non-monotonic relationship between DHF incidence and HI was detected within the intermediate age class (Figure 2D).

Analysis of DHF incidence among the entire Thai population revealed that incidence rose up to HI of about 30 and gradually declined thereafter. This non-monotonic relationship appears to be consistent with a state of endemic stability.
337.Rationale: The option asserts a consistent negative (monotonic) relationship across all ages. The data and analyses in the passage explicitly show age-dependent differences (positive in youngest, negative in oldest, non-monotonic in intermediate) and an overall non-monotonic pattern, so a uniform monotonic negative relationship is contradicted by the empirical findings.

337.Option: A negative correlation between DHF incidence and vector abundance in the youngest age group, with a positive correlation in the oldest age group
Evidence: Univariate analysis revealed that incidence and HI were positively correlated in the youngest age class (Figure 2C); whereas, DHF incidence and HI were negatively correlated in the oldest age class (Figure 2E).

Stratification of data according to age revealed a positive association between DHF incidence and HI among the youngest population. In contrast, a negative association was observed in the oldest population.
337.Rationale: This option reverses the observed age-specific directions. The passage reports a positive correlation in the youngest age class and a negative correlation in the oldest, so the pattern asserted by the option is opposite to the empirical results and therefore incorrect.

338.Option: Use of a case-control design with matched controls from the same geographic region to minimize selection bias
Evidence: Phase I and phase II follow a case-control design and deliver the proof-of-principle and intrinsic sensitivity and specificity of a diagnostic test, which are mostly defined by the antigen and the assay format.

To validate the diagnostic accuracy of a test, it should undergo an evaluation in a prospective phase III study conducted on a larger number of representative samples of consecutively enrolled or randomly selected patients within the population [18,19].
Our analysis shows that 89% of the tests were evaluated in phase I and/or phase II.
338.Rationale: While matching case-control studies and using geographically co-located controls can reduce selection bias within early-phase evaluations, the passage emphasizes that phase I/II case-control designs provide proof-of-principle but do not validate diagnostic accuracy for real-world use. External generalizability to East African clinical settings is achieved primarily by progressing to prospective phase III studies on representative populations. Therefore reliance on case-control design—even with matched regional controls—does not most directly ensure generalizability.

338.Option: Progression to phase III studies with representative East African populations
Evidence: To validate the diagnostic accuracy of a test, it should undergo an evaluation in a prospective phase III study conducted on a larger number of representative samples of consecutively enrolled or randomly selected patients within the population [18,19].

Especially in Eastern Africa where antigen diversity is high compared to South-Asia [126] we consider it essential to conduct a large-scale evaluation on a broad panel of sera from different geographic origin, before concluding on the diagnostic potential of a test.
We recommend including a sufficient number of specimens from Eastern Africa in the downstream evaluation of any new POC test for VL showing high diagnostic accuracy in early phases of development.
338.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that validation requires prospective phase III studies on representative, consecutively enrolled or randomly selected patients, and it strongly recommends large-scale evaluations including sufficient East African specimens. Thus progression to phase III with representative East African populations most directly influences whether early-phase findings generalize to real-world clinical settings in East Africa.

338.Option: Inclusion of a diverse panel of Leishmania species and strains in the test evaluation to account for antigenic variation
Evidence: Especially in Eastern Africa where antigen diversity is high compared to South-Asia [126] we consider it essential to conduct a large-scale evaluation on a broad panel of sera from different geographic origin, before concluding on the diagnostic potential of a test.
We recommend including a sufficient number of specimens from Eastern Africa in the downstream evaluation of any new POC test for VL showing high diagnostic accuracy in early phases of development.
There is 1 non-native, defined antigen with high diagnostic accuracy on East African specimens: the rK28, which incorporated in an ICT has been evaluated on 285 persons in phase III.
338.Rationale: Accounting for antigenic variation by including diverse species/strains is important for test performance and is mentioned as a concern (high antigen diversity in East Africa). However, the question asks which methodological feature most directly influences generalizability of early-phase findings to real-world East African settings. The passage stresses that the key step is large-scale, prospective phase III evaluation with representative East African specimens. Inclusion of diverse strains is a necessary component of such evaluations but by itself is not presented as the single most direct methodological determinant of generalizability.

338.Option: Evaluation of diagnostic accuracy using a meta-analysis of multiple phase I and II studies to increase statistical power
Evidence: We did not perform a meta-analysis on the diagnostic sensitivity and specificity of the tests, which reduces the potential bias of the individual studies.

Phase I and phase II follow a case-control design and deliver the proof-of-principle and intrinsic sensitivity and specificity of a diagnostic test, which are mostly defined by the antigen and the assay format.

To validate the diagnostic accuracy of a test, it should undergo an evaluation in a prospective phase III study conducted on a larger number of representative samples of consecutively enrolled or randomly selected patients within the population [18,19].
338.Rationale: A meta-analysis of phase I/II studies can increase statistical power and summarize existing evidence, but the passage notes that phase I/II (case-control) work does not replace the need for prospective phase III validation. Meta-analysis does not by itself ensure the representativeness or prospective enrollment needed for real-world generalizability in East Africa, so it is not the methodological feature that most directly influences external validity.

338.Option: Use of recombinant antigens with eukaryotic post-translational modifications to enhance antigenic fidelity
Evidence: Fifty three antigens (59%) were expressed in E. coli or on phages or were synthetic peptides and thus do not contain eukaryotic glycosylated or lipid moieties.

For well identified antigens or antigen mixtures, eukaryotic expression systems such as developed in Leishmania tarentolae, Pichia pastoris or Spodoptera frugiperda should be envisioned, as they allow to produce recombinant proteins with eukaryotic post-translational modifications, including glycosylation [127–129].

Especially in Eastern Africa where antigen diversity is high compared to South-Asia [126] we consider it essential to conduct a large-scale evaluation on a broad panel of sera from different geographic origin, before concluding on the diagnostic potential of a test.
338.Rationale: Using recombinant antigens with eukaryotic post-translational modifications can improve antigenic fidelity and potentially test sensitivity/specificity, and the passage recommends considering eukaryotic expression systems. However, this is a feature of assay design that affects intrinsic accuracy rather than the methodological element that most directly determines generalizability of early-phase results to East African clinical practice. The passage identifies large-scale, prospective phase III studies with representative East African specimens as the primary determinant of generalizability.

339.Option: The duration of acute-phase symptoms, particularly fever and joint swelling, during the initial illness
Evidence: The acute phase commonly presents with a sudden onset of fever and severe musculoskeletal pain lasting up to twelve days [8].
Persistent joint or musculoskeletal pain and weakness are characteristic in its chronic presentation, and may be accompanied by fatigue, loss of vitality and neurologic manifestations [10].
Most of these studies associate chronic chikungunya with reduced HRQoL-scores on both physical and mental components [6,15–17].

Problem-focused coping strategies aimed at physical healing induced a high uptake of (alternative) medicines and consultation of physicians, whereas emotional coping strategies focused on acceptance of the situation were linked to lower perceived impacts on HRQoL and less uptake of medical care.
339.Rationale: While the passage describes the acute phase ("lasting up to twelve days"), it emphasizes that long-term impairment is driven by chronic persistent musculoskeletal symptoms and their severity rather than simply the short duration of acute fever or swelling. Moreover, the text highlights coping strategy as a strong determinant of perceived long-term HRQoL (see evidence). Therefore, duration of acute-phase symptoms is not presented as the factor that most strongly influences long-term physical and emotional HRQoL impairment.

339.Option: The presence of a prodromal period characterized by vague symptoms such as feeling strange or fatigue before the onset of acute illness
Evidence: Before onset of the disease, some individuals reported to have perceived a prodromal period in which they had ‘felt strange’ for days or weeks.

This study describes several ways of how participants coped with the effects of chikungunya. A notable coping strategy was high uptake of (natural) medicines or frequent physician visits, which was typically performed by subjects with major impacts on HRQoL and difficulties to accept their situation.
Persistent joint or musculoskeletal pain and weakness are characteristic in its chronic presentation, and may be accompanied by fatigue, loss of vitality and neurologic manifestations [10].
339.Rationale: The passage mentions that some participants perceived a prodromal period, but it does not link the mere presence of a prodrome to greater long-term HRQoL impairment. The narrative and conclusions focus on chronic persistent symptoms, severity, and coping strategies as the drivers of long-term impact. Therefore the prodromal period is not shown to be the strongest influencing factor.

339.Option: The level of access to formal healthcare services, including physician visits and prescription medications
Evidence: Within the community, problem-focused coping was commonly performed by using (natural) medicines and by visiting a physician to cope with the physical impact of chikungunya.
Frequent physician visits were linked to desperation, a desire for solace, persistence of symptoms, and a desire to try new treatment.
Frequent physician visits as coping strategy led to unmet expectations for cure or treatment, resulting in stress (negative impact on QoL).

Problem-focused coping strategies aimed at physical healing induced a high uptake of (alternative) medicines and consultation of physicians, whereas emotional coping strategies focused on acceptance of the situation were linked to lower perceived impacts on HRQoL and less uptake of medical care.
339.Rationale: Access to healthcare and physician visits are discussed, but the passage frames frequent visits and high treatment uptake as part of a problem-focused coping pattern that can be associated with greater unmet expectations and worse perceived HRQoL. The authors emphasize the type of coping strategy (problem-focused vs emotional/acceptance) as more directly linked to long-term HRQoL than mere access to services. Thus level of access alone is not presented as the strongest influence.

339.Option: The individual’s perception of chikungunya as a potentially fatal disease, especially among those with comorbidities or older age
Evidence: Within the community, it was believed that chikungunya might be lethal for people with a ‘weaker resistance [immune system]’ like little children, older people, or those with comorbidities.
The acute phase was described as highly debilitating in which patients perceived strong anxious emotions, sometimes to the extent that they thought they might be dying.
The unpredictable lingering nature of mainly musculoskeletal manifestations and its subsequent difficulties in normal daily life activities were linked to several consequences for emotional HRQoL.
339.Rationale: Perceiving the disease as potentially fatal clearly contributes to acute emotional distress and anxiety, but the passage does not indicate this perception as the primary determinant of long-term physical and emotional HRQoL impairment. Instead, persistent physical sequelae and the coping responses to them are emphasized as principal drivers of prolonged HRQoL impact. Therefore this option is not the strongest influence per the text.

339.Option: The type of coping strategy employed in response to persistent musculoskeletal symptoms
Evidence: Problem-focused coping strategies aimed at physical healing induced a high uptake of (alternative) medicines and consultation of physicians, whereas emotional coping strategies focused on acceptance of the situation were linked to lower perceived impacts on HRQoL and less uptake of medical care.
Frequent physician visits as coping strategy led to unmet expectations for cure or treatment, resulting in stress (negative impact on QoL).
Participants showing coping strategies focused on acceptation of circumstances or on social support showed better HRQoL, which is in line with literature on coping strategies [24,36].

Problem-focused coping strategies aimed at physical healing induced a high uptake of (alternative) medicines and consultation of physicians, whereas emotional coping strategies focused on acceptance of the situation were linked to lower perceived impacts on HRQoL and less uptake of medical care.
339.Rationale: The passage explicitly links the type of coping strategy to differences in long-term HRQoL: problem-focused coping (high treatment seeking) is associated with unmet expectations and greater negative impact, whereas emotional/acceptance coping and social support are associated with lower perceived impacts and better HRQoL. This direct, repeated statement in the text identifies coping strategy as a key determinant of long-term physical and emotional HRQoL among affected individuals.

340.Option: The reduction in hospitalizations was offset by a higher proportion of patients requiring extended care due to comorbidities, maintaining average length of stay
Evidence: The LOS in hospital remained stable over the years; both the annual mean and median LOS were 3 to 4 days.
The proportion of dengue cases hospitalized plummeted from 97.3% in 2003 to an all-time low of 25.6% in 2014 (p < 0.0005).
The factors contributing to prolonged LOS were dengue severity, age, comorbidity and HAI [29].
We revealed that a higher proportion of older dengue cases (aged ≥45 years) were admitted, and that older patients experienced more severe disease.
340.Rationale: While the passage explicitly lists comorbidity as one factor contributing to prolonged LOS ("The factors contributing to prolonged LOS were dengue severity, age, comorbidity and HAI [29]."), it does not present direct evidence that an increasing proportion of hospitalized patients had more comorbidities over time. The text emphasizes age and disease severity (and demographic shift toward older patients) as explanations for severity among admissions. Thus attributing the stable mean LOS primarily to rising comorbidity prevalence among those hospitalized is not directly supported by the passage and is less likely than explanations based on age/severity.

340.Option: The proportion of patients admitted to the ICU remained stable, indicating consistent severity of illness across the study period
Evidence: About 0.6% to 1.5% of dengue patients in hospital were admitted to the ICU during the study period (Table 1).
Over the 15-year period, the proportion of patients admitted to the ICU remained below 2%, and the proportion who died in hospital was less than 1%.
The LOS in hospital remained stable over the years; both the annual mean and median LOS were 3 to 4 days.
340.Rationale: Although the passage notes that ICU admissions remained low and below 2% ("Over the 15-year period, the proportion of patients admitted to the ICU remained below 2%"), this does not by itself explain why average hospital LOS stayed stable despite far fewer admissions. The more direct explanation in the text is that hospitals became more selective and admitted a higher concentration of older/more severe cases (see Evidence for Option 3). The small, stable ICU proportion could reflect that most admitted severe cases still did not require ICU care; it is not presented as the primary driver of stable LOS.

340.Option: The use of rapid diagnostic tests improved early detection and allowed for timely discharge, preventing prolonged hospitalization
Evidence: Early diagnosis of dengue was facilitated by NEA’s EHI, which offered NS1 antigen testing to primary care clinics at no cost from 2006 [25].
These initiatives facilitate early diagnosis and close monitoring of dengue in the primary healthcare setting [23,26].
The implementation of more selective admission criteria by the hospitals had not led to longer LOS; the overall mean was 3.8 days and the overall median was 3 days (IQR 3 days) from 2003 to 2017.
340.Rationale: The passage supports that rapid diagnostics (NS1 testing) improved early detection and contributed to more outpatient management and fewer admissions. However, it does not state that improved diagnostics led to shorter hospital stays for those admitted. Instead, the passage attributes the stable LOS to selective admission of patients who were more likely to require inpatient care (especially older patients). Therefore, while rapid tests likely reduced admissions, they are not presented as the primary explanation for why LOS among hospitalized patients remained stable.

340.Option: The hospitalization of more severe cases, particularly among elderly patients, maintained average length of stay despite fewer overall admissions
Evidence: The proportion of dengue cases hospitalized plummeted from 97.3% in 2003 to an all-time low of 25.6% in 2014 (p < 0.0005).
The LOS in hospital remained stable over the years; both the annual mean and median LOS were 3 to 4 days.
We revealed that a higher proportion of older dengue cases (aged ≥45 years) were admitted, and that older patients experienced more severe disease.
Hospital stays among elderly patients ≥65 years of age were significantly longer: the annual mean LOS was 5 to 8 days and the annual median was 4 to 6 days (p < 0.0005).
This was likely contributed to by both the MOH guideline to refer for hospital evaluation those aged ≥65 years as well as a shift in demographic profile of dengue cases towards older adults [17–19].
340.Rationale: The passage documents a large reduction in the proportion of cases hospitalized while noting that LOS stayed stable. It also documents a shift toward admitting a higher proportion of older patients who have longer hospital stays and more severe disease (e.g., "Hospital stays among elderly patients ≥65 years of age were significantly longer..."). Together these statements support the interpretation that hospitals admitted fewer but sicker/older patients after refining admission criteria, and that concentration of more severe admissions (especially elderly) would keep the average LOS roughly unchanged despite fewer overall admissions. This explanation is directly supported by multiple passage statements and is the most likely factor.

340.Option: The implementation of standardized treatment protocols across hospitals led to consistent management and reduced variability in length of stay
Evidence: The Singapore Ministry of Health (MOH) sent out circulars to hospitals, government primary care clinics and medical practitioners to apprise them of the dengue situation during epidemic periods, and to provide periodic updates on guidelines for the management of dengue [14–19].

In 2007, a medical expert committee established the criteria for immediate referral to hospitals, which comprised objective criteria such as significant bleeding, fall in blood pressure, dehydration and/or postural hypotension, a rise in the haematocrit ≥ 20% above the baseline and platelet count < 80 000 cells / mm3, as well as subjective criteria such as severe vomiting or diarrhoea, severe abdominal pain, and elderly patients with medical co-morbidities who are unwell [15].
The LOS in hospital remained stable over the years; both the annual mean and median LOS were 3 to 4 days.
340.Rationale: While the passage describes MOH guidance and updated referral/admission criteria, it does not provide direct evidence that standardized inpatient treatment protocols across hospitals were implemented and were the cause of stable LOS. The text emphasizes selective admission (triage) and a shift toward older/severe admissions as explanations. Therefore, attributing stability in LOS primarily to standardized treatment protocols is not directly supported by the passage.

341.Option: Differential antibody clearance rates between age groups can lead to overestimation of ongoing transmission if not accounted for in serological interpretation
Evidence: Anti-filarial antibodies are a marker for past, present or exposure to filarial infections.

Antibody clearance tends to occur faster after treatment in children than in adults, since children tend to have shorter term exposure and lighter infections. Therefore, adults may have persistent antibodies years after effective therapy [9], [10].
antibody prevalence decreased faster in children than in adults [8].

The observation that it took 6 rounds of MDA and additional 22 months until the filarial antibody rate as determined by the Brugia Rapid test dropped below 5% (upper 95% confidence limit 10%) in children aged between 3 and 10 years suggests that the antibody rate is a sensitive marker for previous exposure to B. timori.
341.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that antibody kinetics differ by age — antibodies clear faster in children than in adults and adults may retain antibodies for years after successful therapy. Because seropositivity can reflect past exposure as well as current infection, failure to account for slower antibody clearance in adults (and differential clearance by age) can make antibody-based measures appear to signal ongoing transmission when they instead reflect historical exposure. Thus differential antibody clearance by age is a critical factor in interpreting post-MDA serology.

341.Option: The use of a rapid antibody test with a recombinant antigen may lead to false-positive results in individuals with prior exposure to other filarial species, affecting the accuracy of transmission assessment
Evidence: We used the Brugia Rapid dipstick test as a marker for ongoing or prior infection, because there is no adult worm antigen detection test for Brugia spp. This test employs a recombinant B. malayi antigen (BmRI) that has been extensively studied [19].
Anti-filarial antibodies are a marker for past, present or exposure to filarial infections.
341.Rationale: While the passage describes the Brugia Rapid test and notes it uses a recombinant B. malayi antigen, it does not provide any information about cross-reactivity or false-positives from other filarial species. The text emphasizes that antibodies indicate past or present exposure and discusses antibody decline, but it does not state that the assay produces species-cross-reactive false positives that would critically influence interpretation. Because the passage lacks evidence about cross-reactivity or false-positive problems with the recombinant antigen, this option is not supported by the provided text.

341.Option: The rebound in soil-transmitted helminth prevalence after MDA cessation suggests that the population may have been re-exposed to filarial parasites, confounding serological interpretation
Evidence: hookworm rates rebounded to 7% at 34 months after the last MDA.
STH rates rebounded quickly after MDA was discontinued.
MF rates fell from a baseline of 26% to less than 1% after round 4, and this decrease was maintained for the duration of the study including the period following cessation of MDA.
Only one woman was found to be MF-positive with 192 MF/ml and 115 MF/ml, respectively. It was the same young lady who had been found to be MF-positive all the years before.
341.Rationale: The passage documents rebound of soil-transmitted helminths (STH) after MDA cessation, but it separately shows that microfilaremia (MF) for filariasis fell dramatically and remained very low, with essentially no new MF carriers detected. The rebound in STH prevalence reflects re-infection with intestinal helminths, not evidence of renewed filarial transmission. Therefore STH rebound does not imply re-exposure to filarial parasites and is not the primary factor confounding antibody-based interpretation of filarial serology in this study.

341.Option: A high baseline antibody prevalence in children suggests that the population was already exposed to filariasis before the start of MDA, which complicates interpretation of post-MDA serological trends
Evidence: As reported previously, antibody rates were high (about 80%) in all age groups in 2001.

In 2009 the majority of children aged between 3 and 10 years were born after the initiation of MDA, but many of these children might have been exposed to B. timori during the early years of the MDA program.
Anti-filarial antibody rates in the general population fell from 80% in 2001 to 6.4% in 2010.

The observation that it took 6 rounds of MDA and additional 22 months until the filarial antibody rate as determined by the Brugia Rapid test dropped below 5% ... suggests that the antibody rate is a sensitive marker for previous exposure to B. timori.
341.Rationale: The passage indicates very high baseline antibody prevalence (about 80%) including in children, meaning many individuals had prior exposure before MDA began. Because antibodies reflect past as well as current exposure and decay slowly, a high baseline seroprevalence complicates interpretation of subsequent declines: observed antibodies post-MDA may represent historic exposure and slow decay rather than ongoing transmission, making baseline prevalence a critical factor in interpreting post-MDA serological trends.

341.Option: The presence of antibodies in individuals who have never received treatment may indicate ongoing transmission, even if overall prevalence is low
Evidence: Anti-filarial antibodies are a marker for past, present or exposure to filarial infections.

Antibody clearance tends to occur faster after treatment in children than in adults, since children tend to have shorter term exposure and lighter infections. Therefore, adults may have persistent antibodies years after effective therapy [9], [10].

While all examined children in that age group were constantly MF negative following the first two rounds of MDA, the antibody prevalence dropped quickly but remained higher than 2% for the entire study period.
This means MF rates fell more rapidly after MDA than antibody rates. For this reason, it is not possible to use antibody rates to estimate MF prevalence rates following MDA.
341.Rationale: The passage makes clear that antibodies can indicate past exposure and may persist long after transmission has ceased, particularly in adults, and that antibody prevalence declines more slowly than microfilaremia. Therefore, finding antibodies in an untreated individual does not necessarily signify ongoing transmission — it may reflect prior exposure or slower antibody clearance. The text even cautions that antibody rates cannot be straightforwardly used to estimate MF prevalence post-MDA, so the statement that antibodies in never-treated individuals necessarily indicate current transmission is not supported.

342.Option: Despite strong genetic divergence indicating long-term host adaptation, cross-infection between human and pig Trichuris populations occurs in endemic regions, though infections in the heterologous host are typically transient.
Evidence: A substantial level of nucleotide difference (32.9%) was detected in the complete mt genome between an individual of human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris from China.

Taken together, the molecular evidence presented here supports the hypothesis that the gene pools of human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris have been isolated for a substantial period of time and that they represent distinct species.

In spite of the genetic distinctiveness recorded here between them, host affiliation is not strict [47]. Cross-infection of Trichuris between humans and pigs (both directions) has been described, but infection in the heterologous host is usually abbreviated [47].
342.Rationale: The passage documents marked genetic divergence consistent with long-term isolation and species-level distinction (e.g., "A substantial level of nucleotide difference (32.9%)..." and "...they represent distinct species"). It also explicitly states that cross-infection has been observed but infections in the heterologous host are typically abbreviated/short-lived ("...Cross-infection... has been described, but infection in the heterologous host is usually abbreviated"). Thus the option accurately integrates the genetic evidence for host adaptation with the epidemiologic observation of occasional, transient cross-infection.

342.Option: The genetic similarity in ribosomal RNA genes between human- and pig-Trichuris indicates that cross-species transmission is common and stable in endemic regions.
Evidence: Comparison of the mt genomes of human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris showed that the rrnS and rrnL were the two most conserved genes (Table 5).
The alignment of the partial rrnL sequences revealed that all individuals of human-Trichuris differed at 89 nucleotide positions when compared with pig-Trichuris.

Phylogenetic analyses of the rrnL sequence data revealed strong support for the separation of human-Trichuris from pig-Trichuris individuals into two distinct clades, and the trees produced using the three different methods were essentially the same in topology (Fig. 3).
342.Rationale: Although rrnS and rrnL are relatively more conserved than some protein genes ("...rrnS and rrnL were the two most conserved genes"), the passage shows substantial rrnL differences ("...differed at 89 nucleotide positions") and phylogenetic separation into distinct clades. The data therefore do not support the claim that rRNA similarity implies common, stable cross-species transmission; instead they support genetic distinctiveness and limited cross-host persistence.

342.Option: The high genetic divergence between human- and pig-Trichuris suggests that cross-species transmission is biologically impossible, even in regions where humans and pigs live in close proximity.
Evidence: A substantial level of nucleotide difference (32.9%) was detected in the complete mt genome between an individual of human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris from China.

Taken together, the molecular evidence presented here supports the hypothesis that the gene pools of human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris have been isolated for a substantial period of time and that they represent distinct species.

In spite of the genetic distinctiveness recorded here between them, host affiliation is not strict [47]. Cross-infection of Trichuris between humans and pigs (both directions) has been described, but infection in the heterologous host is usually abbreviated [47].
342.Rationale: The passage documents strong genetic divergence, but it also explicitly states that cross-infection between humans and pigs has been described, although infections in the heterologous host tend to be abbreviated. Therefore it would be incorrect to conclude cross-species transmission is biologically impossible; the data indicate it can occur, albeit usually transiently and not leading to sustained transmission.

342.Option: Because cross-infection between human and pig Trichuris has been documented, the genetic differences observed are likely due to sampling bias rather than true species-level divergence.
Evidence: A substantial level of nucleotide difference (32.9%) was detected in the complete mt genome between an individual of human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris from China.

Taken together, the molecular evidence presented here supports the hypothesis that the gene pools of human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris have been isolated for a substantial period of time and that they represent distinct species.

only a small number of specimens from one country (Spain) was used in this study, and amplicons (from the first and second internal transcribed spacers, ITS-1 and ITS-2, of nuclear ribosomal DNA) were subjected to cloning prior to sequencing, which has significant potential to lead to artefacts [15], [16]. Therefore, the findings from this study [14] need to be interpreted with some caution at this stage.
342.Rationale: The passage presents whole mitochondrial genome data showing large (≈32.9%) divergence and concludes this supports distinct species, which argues against attributing the differences merely to sampling bias. The quoted caution about small sample size and artefacts refers to a prior, different study, not the present mt genome evidence. Thus the option's assertion that documented cross-infection implies sampling bias for the observed divergence is not supported by the data presented.

342.Option: The transient nature of cross-infection in the heterologous host implies that human and pig Trichuris populations are not genetically distinct, as sustained transmission would be required for genetic exchange.
Evidence: Taken together, the molecular evidence presented here supports the hypothesis that the gene pools of human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris have been isolated for a substantial period of time and that they represent distinct species.

In spite of the genetic distinctiveness recorded here between them, host affiliation is not strict [47]. Cross-infection of Trichuris between humans and pigs (both directions) has been described, but infection in the heterologous host is usually abbreviated [47].
342.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports both substantial genetic distinctiveness (supporting species-level separation) and the epidemiologic observation that cross-infections, while documented, are usually abbreviated. The transient nature of heterologous infections is consistent with lack of sustained transmission and genetic exchange, which supports — not undermines — the conclusion that populations are genetically distinct. Therefore the option's inference is incorrect and reversed relative to the evidence.

343.Option: The presence of concurrent opportunistic infections in the Pos-group suggests that co-infections are the main reason for delayed clearance, independent of antifungal susceptibility
Evidence: There were no significant differences regarding age, sex, ART history, and concurrent opportunistic infections (OIs) between the two groups.
We found that the delayed clearance group had higher AST level and lower CD4+T cells, also with higher MIC values for fluconazole and voriconazole.
343.Rationale: The passage explicitly states there were no significant differences in concurrent opportunistic infections between the Pos- and Neg-groups, so co-infections are not supported as the main reason for delayed clearance. Instead, the study highlights higher MIC values for azoles (voriconazole and fluconazole) and higher AST/lower CD4 in the delayed-clearance group, with MICs identified as an independent factor in multivariate analysis. Thus concurrent OIs do not most strongly suggest a pathogen-intrinsic influence on outcome.

343.Option: Elevated AST levels in the Pos-group are the strongest predictor of delayed clearance, indicating that liver dysfunction is the primary mechanism of treatment failure
Evidence: Pos-group had a higher baseline Aspartate aminotransferase (AST, 78.5 vs. 105 U/L; P = 0.073) and lower CD4+ T cells level (11 vs. 7 cells/μl; P = 0.061) (shown in Table 1).

Multivariate analysis using the logistic regression model involving the 2 significant factors (AST and CD4+T levels)determined by univariate analysis and the MIC values for VOR and FLU. The MIC value for VOR was identified as the prognostic variable derived from binary logistic regression analysis that influence the clearance of T. marneffei in blood culture after antifungal therapy among AIDS patients with talaromycosis (shown in Table 4).

The MIC for voriconazole was determined as the independent factor by binary logistic regression analysis, which indicates that the susceptibility of T. marneffei strain to antifungal agents could affect the antifungal efficacy.
343.Rationale: Although AST was higher in the Pos-group, the difference was not strongly significant (P = 0.073) and in multivariate logistic regression the MIC for voriconazole—not AST—was identified as the independent prognostic variable influencing clearance. Therefore elevated AST is not shown to be the strongest predictor, nor does it indicate the primary mechanism independent of pathogen properties.

343.Option: The use of voriconazole as the initial antifungal agent in the Pos-group indicates that treatment choice directly causes delayed clearance, independent of pathogen resistance
Evidence: AMB, ITRA, or VOR were used as antifungal agents, and the therapeutic regimen exhibited no significant difference between the two groups.

Multivariate analysis using the logistic regression model involving the 2 significant factors (AST and CD4+T levels)determined by univariate analysis and the MIC values for VOR and FLU. The MIC value for VOR was identified as the prognostic variable derived from binary logistic regression analysis that influence the clearance of T. marneffei in blood culture after antifungal therapy among AIDS patients with talaromycosis (shown in Table 4).

The MIC for voriconazole was determined as the independent factor by binary logistic regression analysis, which indicates that the susceptibility of T. marneffei strain to antifungal agents could affect the antifungal efficacy.
343.Rationale: The passage states there was no significant difference in therapeutic regimen between groups, undermining the claim that treatment choice (use of VOR) explains delayed clearance. The analysis instead found the MIC for voriconazole (pathogen susceptibility) to be the independent factor associated with delayed clearance, indicating intrinsic pathogen properties rather than initial drug choice drive the association.

343.Option: A higher proportion of patients in the Pos-group with CD4+ T cell counts below 10 cells/μL suggests that severe immunosuppression is the primary driver of delayed clearance, regardless of pathogen susceptibility
Evidence: Pos-group had a higher baseline Aspartate aminotransferase (AST, 78.5 vs. 105 U/L; P = 0.073) and lower CD4+ T cells level (11 vs. 7 cells/μl; P = 0.061) (shown in Table 1).

Multivariate analysis using the logistic regression model involving the 2 significant factors (AST and CD4+T levels)determined by univariate analysis and the MIC values for VOR and FLU. The MIC value for VOR was identified as the prognostic variable derived from binary logistic regression analysis that influence the clearance of T. marneffei in blood culture after antifungal therapy among AIDS patients with talaromycosis (shown in Table 4).
We found that the delayed clearance group had higher AST level and lower CD4+T cells, also with higher MIC values for fluconazole and voriconazole.
343.Rationale: While CD4 counts tended to be lower in the Pos-group, the difference was not statistically definitive (P = 0.061), and multivariate analysis that adjusted for CD4 identified voriconazole MIC as the independent prognostic factor. Therefore the data do not support severe immunosuppression as the primary driver independent of pathogen susceptibility.

343.Option: Higher voriconazole minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) in T. marneffei isolates is independently associated with delayed clearance of the pathogen in blood culture, even after adjusting for host immune status
Evidence: Significant differences were found in the overall distribution of MIC value between the two groups both for VOR (Z = 1.762, P < 0.001) and FLU (Z = 1.211, P = 0.006). The MICs of VOR and FLU in the Pos-group were significantly higher than those in the Neg-group (the proportion of isolates with MICs above 0.008 for VOR: 69/101 (68.3%) vs. 38/89 (42.7%), respectively, χ2 = 12.623, P < 0.001; the proportion of isolates with MICs above 4 for FLU: 28/101 (27.7%) vs. 9/89 (10.1%), respectively, χ2 = 9.356, P = 0.002) (shown in Table 2).

Multivariate analysis using the logistic regression model involving the 2 significant factors (AST and CD4+T levels)determined by univariate analysis and the MIC values for VOR and FLU. The MIC value for VOR was identified as the prognostic variable derived from binary logistic regression analysis that influence the clearance of T. marneffei in blood culture after antifungal therapy among AIDS patients with talaromycosis (shown in Table 4).

The MIC for voriconazole was determined as the independent factor by binary logistic regression analysis, which indicates that the susceptibility of T. marneffei strain to antifungal agents could affect the antifungal efficacy.

The cumulative negative conversion rate of blood cultured fungi among three groups after 2, 4, 6 weeks and more than 6 weeks of antifungal treatment was significantly different (Shown in Fig 2), indicating that the MIC values of voriconazole significantly affects the clearance of T. marneffei in blood.
343.Rationale: The study found significantly higher voriconazole MICs among isolates from patients with delayed clearance, and multivariate logistic regression that included host markers (AST and CD4+ T cells) identified voriconazole MIC as the independent prognostic variable influencing blood-culture clearance. The cumulative negative-conversion analysis also showed differing clearance trajectories by VOR MIC group.
