These findings directly support that pathogen intrinsic susceptibility (higher VOR MIC) influences treatment outcome independent of host immune status.

344.Option: The vaccine may cause teratogenic effects in the fetus due to the accumulation of viral RNA in maternal tissues, even if the vaccine is otherwise safe in non-pregnant hosts
Evidence: Remarkably, inoculation during gestation resulted in vertical transmission of the virus to the fetus, which was associated with malformed lambs, stillbirths and precolostral antibodies. Untoward events were most prominent in ewes inoculated during the first trimester of gestation.

Analysis of the lamb organs and plasma samples revealed that ten lambs were positive for Clone 13 RNA in the blood or one or more organ samples, six in the 50 days group and four in the 120 days group (Fig 3).
As expected, almost all blood samples (22 out of 24) were tested positive for RVFV RNA two days post Clone 13 inoculation.

These malformations are consistent with the consequences of intra-uterine virus infections and have been reported during RVF outbreaks in the field [37] and after vaccination with the live-attenuated RVF vaccine MP-12 [17].
344.Rationale: The passage documents that following maternal inoculation Clone 13 RNA was detectable in maternal blood and that vertical transmission occurred, with fetal infection and malformations. That chain of findings supports the notion that presence/accumulation of viral RNA (i.e., maternal viremia/dissemination) can be associated with teratogenic effects in the fetus even when the vaccine is tolerated in non-pregnant hosts.

344.Option: The vaccine's safety profile may be compromised in pregnant animals due to altered immune responses, even if it is safe in non-pregnant hosts
Evidence: Extensive studies have demonstrated that the NSs protein counteracts host innate immune responses at different levels, e.g. by suppression of type-I interferon responses, and is thereby considered the major virulence factor [19–22].

The combined results of all four studies demonstrate that Clone 13 is very well tolerated and safe for young lambs. However, inoculation of pregnant ewes was associated with foetal infections, malformations and stillbirths.
344.Rationale: While the passage describes that the NSs protein modulates host innate immunity and that pregnant ewes experienced fetal infection and malformations, it does not provide explicit evidence that altered maternal immune responses in pregnancy (per se) are the reason safety is compromised. The text points to transplacental infection, fetal susceptibility and teratogenesis rather than documenting pregnancy-specific immune modulation as the causal factor. Therefore this option is not directly supported by the passage.

344.Option: The developing fetus may be uniquely vulnerable to live-attenuated vaccine components, especially during early gestation, even if the vaccine is safe in non-pregnant hosts
Evidence: the ovine fetus is the most susceptible.

Remarkably, inoculation during gestation resulted in vertical transmission of the virus to the fetus, which was associated with malformed lambs, stillbirths and precolostral antibodies. Untoward events were most prominent in ewes inoculated during the first trimester of gestation.
As many as 7 lambs of 5 different ewes inoculated with Clone 13 at 50 days after mating showed malformations of the central nervous system ...
No malformations were observed in lambs of ewes inoculated with Clone 13 at 120 days after mating.
344.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the ovine fetus is highly susceptible, that vertical transmission led to malformations and stillbirths, and that adverse outcomes were concentrated when inoculation occurred in early gestation (50 days) rather than later (120 days). This directly supports the need to consider fetal vulnerability, especially during early gestation.

344.Option: The vaccine may lead to prolonged shedding in pregnant animals, increasing the risk of fetal infection, even if it is otherwise safe in non-pregnant hosts
Evidence: No adverse reactions to the Clone 13 inoculation, such as reduced appetite, depression or elevated temperatures were observed and no Clone 13 RNA was detected in saliva, nasal mucus, lacrimal fluid, urine or faeces samples.
As expected, Clone 13-specific RNA was detected in plasma samples collected one and two days post inoculation.
Viremia was very short-lived and of very low level, suggesting that the risk of Clone 13 transmission by mosquito vectors is negligible.
All sentinel animals remained free of Clone 13 RNA and did not develop RVFV-specific antibodies.
344.Rationale: The passage reports no evidence of secretion or excretion (no RNA detected in saliva, nasal mucus, urine, feces) and describes viremia as short-lived and low-level, with no spread to sentinels. There is no support for prolonged shedding in pregnant animals; instead the data indicate limited shedding and short viremia, so this option is not supported by the passage.

344.Option: The vaccine's ability to induce viremia in the mother may increase the risk of transmission to the fetus, even if the vaccine is otherwise safe in non-pregnant hosts
Evidence: As expected, almost all blood samples (22 out of 24) were tested positive for RVFV RNA two days post Clone 13 inoculation.
Remarkably, inoculation during gestation resulted in vertical transmission of the virus to the fetus, which was associated with malformed lambs, stillbirths and precolostral antibodies.

Analysis of the lamb organs and plasma samples revealed that ten lambs were positive for Clone 13 RNA in the blood or one or more organ samples, six in the 50 days group and four in the 120 days group (Fig 3).

transplacental infection of Clone 13 took place in at least 6 out of the 12 ewes vaccinated approximately 50 days after mating and in 7 out of 12 ewes in the group vaccinated during the third trimester of pregnancy.
344.Rationale: The passage documents maternal viremia shortly after inoculation and documents vertical (transplacental) transmission with fetal infection and malformations. This supports the consideration that maternal viremia following live-attenuated vaccination can increase the risk of transmitting the vaccine virus to the fetus, producing adverse outcomes in pregnancy.

345.Option: The laboratory technician’s experience in interpreting Western blot band patterns affects the classification of results as indeterminate
Evidence: While offering high diagnostic performance, results can be difficult to interpret, and this platform remains expensive [28–31].

Assay results were interpreted by captured images of membrane strips on an ImageQuant LAS 4000 digital imaging system (General Electric, Boston, MA, USA) after 30 seconds of exposure to the light produced during the chemiluminescence reaction.
Samples were deemed inconclusive when RBI values fell into an indeterminate zone: 0.90 ≤ RBI ≤ 1.10.
345.Rationale: Although the passage explicitly notes that some immunoblot platforms can be "difficult to interpret" and describes the image-based interpretation workflow, it emphasizes assay-design-related factors (cutoffs, antigen composition, and RBI thresholds) as the basis for indeterminate results. The question asks which factor most critically influences interpretation when results fall in the indeterminate zone; operator experience can influence reading, but the passage frames the indeterminate classification around predefined cutoff/indeterminate RBI thresholds and antigen performance rather than technician experience. Thus technician experience is not presented as the most critical factor.

345.Option: The immunodominance and epitope diversity of the chimeric antigens used in the assay determine the likelihood of results falling within the indeterminate zone
Evidence: Chimeric antigens, composed of linear immunodominant conserved and repetitive epitopes of structural, surface and cytosolic T. cruzi antigens, offer increased epitope diversity, and have thusly been proposed as an appropriate tool to enhance immunoassay diagnostic parameters [13–15].
Following WHO recommendations, around 5% of samples submitted to dual-serological assay testing return discordant or doubtful results (values falling within the cutoff range or indeterminate zone).

Cut-off point analysis was used to identify the optimal pixel intensity that differentiated between T. cruzi-positive and negative samples. Threshold values were established based on the area under ROC curve (AUC). Assay results were normalized by determining the relative band intensity (RBI) representative of the ratio between each sample’s final pixel intensity and its respective cutoff value. Samples that returned RBI < 1.00 were considered negative. Samples were deemed inconclusive when RBI values fell into an indeterminate zone: 0.90 ≤ RBI ≤ 1.10.
The availability of multiple epitopes also increases assay sensitivity, as the same amount of surface area will therefore capture a greater number of antibodies from each sample [51–53].
345.Rationale: The passage identifies antigen choice and epitope diversity as major determinants of serologic assay performance and explicitly proposes chimeric antigens to "enhance immunoassay diagnostic parameters." Because the indeterminate (cutoff) zone is defined by signal intensity relative to thresholds, the antigen's ability to present immunodominant epitopes (increasing signal for true positives and reducing borderline values) most directly influences whether results fall below positive but above negative. Therefore immunodominance/epitope diversity is the critical factor affecting interpretation of borderline/indeterminate confirmatory results.

345.Option: The timing of sample collection relative to the onset of chronic symptoms influences whether a result falls within the indeterminate zone
Evidence: Parasite replication becomes controlled by cell-mediated immune response, symptoms resolve spontaneously, and parasites are undetectable by direct parasitological methods. While most individuals remain asymptomatic, infection persists throughout their lifetime.

Laboratory diagnosis during the chronic phase is based on the detection of anti-T. cruzi antibodies by indirect immunoassays, including enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), indirect immunofluorescence (IIF), indirect hemagglutination (IHA), and chemiluminescent (CLIA) methods.
Samples were deemed inconclusive when RBI values fell into an indeterminate zone: 0.90 ≤ RBI ≤ 1.10.
345.Rationale: The passage describes chronic infection as one in which antibodies are detectable and infection persists for life, and diagnostic approaches in the chronic phase rely on antibody detection. It does not describe timing within the chronic phase (relative to symptom onset) as a driver of indeterminate results. Thus timing of sample collection relative to chronic symptom onset is not presented as a primary factor determining whether a result lands in the indeterminate zone.

345.Option: The prevalence of Chagas disease in the population being tested directly affects the probability of a result being classified as indeterminate
Evidence: operational and technical issues hamper the performance of serological assays, which has been attributed to variations in disease prevalence [7,8], the choice of antigens employed to sensitize the solid phase of immunoassays [9], variable immune responses in T. cruzi-infected individuals [10], and extensive T. cruzi genetic and phenotypic intraspecific diversity [11].
Following WHO recommendations, around 5% of samples submitted to dual-serological assay testing return discordant or doubtful results (values falling within the cutoff range or indeterminate zone).
345.Rationale: The passage acknowledges that variations in disease prevalence have been associated with operational and technical issues affecting assay performance. However, prevalence affects predictive values (PPV/NPV) and general assay performance at a population level rather than directly determining whether an individual sample's signal falls into the indeterminate RBI zone. The text more directly links antigen choice, immune response variability, and assay cutoffs to indeterminate/doubtful results, making prevalence a secondary influence rather than the most critical factor.

345.Option: The presence of co-infections such as visceral leishmaniasis increases the likelihood of a result being classified as indeterminate
Evidence: Although cross-reactivity with species of the Leishmania genus present significant limitations for conventional serological diagnosis of chronic CD [22,46,67], this is largely mitigated when using WB.
To evaluate cross-reactivity, three serum samples from individuals serologically diagnosed with visceral leishmaniasis were also included.
Herein, no cross-reactivity was seen in three positive samples for visceral leishmaniasis evaluated using WB-IBMP.

For visceral leishmaniasis, cross-reactivity was observed in 3.49% and 0.58% of samples evaluated by IBMP-8.2 and IBMP-8.3, respectively. No cross-reactions were detected in any of the 829 samples tested by IBMP-8.4.
345.Rationale: Co-infections (e.g., Leishmania) can cause cross-reactivity and thus affect serologic specificity in some assays, and the authors evaluated this explicitly. However, the passage emphasizes that Western blot using IBMP chimeric antigens largely mitigates cross-reactivity and showed no cross-reaction in the evaluated leishmaniasis samples. The primary determinant of borderline/indeterminate results in the passage is antigen design and cutoff definition rather than co-infection status, so co-infection is not presented as the most critical factor for indeterminate classification.

346.Option: The absence of respiratory signs and oral lesions is associated with a favorable prognosis because it indicates a less severe systemic infection
Evidence: Patients with a positive identification from the gastrointestinal tract were less likely to have respiratory signs, oral signs, and superficial adenopathies.

Table 1 shows there were significantly fewer deaths at one month in patients with a digestive presentation and positive identification of H. capsulatum than in patients without a gastrointestinal presentation.
346.Rationale: The passage does state that patients with gastrointestinal identification were less likely to have respiratory and oral signs and that those with a digestive presentation and positive identification had fewer deaths at one month. However, the study explicitly links the favorable prognosis to the combination of gastrointestinal symptoms with confirmed identification and a shorter interval to diagnosis (i.e., earlier diagnosis via endoscopy/biopsy), not simply the absence of respiratory/oral signs alone. The absence of respiratory/oral lesions is therefore an indirect correlate but not presented as the principal feature most strongly suggesting a favorable prognosis.

346.Option: A longer interval between symptom onset and diagnosis is associated with a favorable prognosis because it allows for more comprehensive clinical evaluation
Evidence: The interval between symptom’s onset and diagnosis was shorter and the risk of death at one month was lower in those with gastrointestinal symptoms and a positive identification of H. capsulatum than in those without gastrointestinal symptoms.
A possibility would be that a quicker diagnosis through endoscopy eventually explained a more limited dissemination and a lower case-fatality.
346.Rationale: The passage reports the opposite: a shorter (not longer) interval between symptom onset and diagnosis was observed in patients with gastrointestinal symptoms and confirmed H. capsulatum, and the authors propose that quicker diagnosis (not delayed evaluation) may explain the lower case-fatality. Thus the statement that a longer interval is associated with better prognosis contradicts the findings.

346.Option: Gastrointestinal symptoms with confirmed H. capsulatum identification is associated with a favorable prognosis due to earlier diagnosis and reduced disease dissemination
Evidence: Table 1 shows there were significantly fewer deaths at one month in patients with a digestive presentation and positive identification of H. capsulatum than in patients without a gastrointestinal presentation.
Patients with a positive identification from the gastrointestinal tract were less likely to have respiratory signs, oral signs, and superficial adenopathies.
They had a shorter delay between symptoms onset and diagnosis and were more likely to be diagnosed through pathology than those without gastrointestinal manifestations.

The interval between symptom’s onset and diagnosis was shorter and the risk of death at one month was lower in those with gastrointestinal symptoms and a positive identification of H. capsulatum than in those without gastrointestinal symptoms. A possibility would be that a quicker diagnosis through endoscopy eventually explained a more limited dissemination and a lower case-fatality.
endoscopies, notably colonoscopy, allow biopsies and opportunities to identify the pathogen either through mycological methods or histopathology.
346.Rationale: The passage directly indicates that patients with gastrointestinal signs and laboratory/pathologic confirmation of H. capsulatum had lower one-month mortality, were diagnosed sooner, and were more often identified via endoscopic biopsy. The authors propose that earlier diagnosis via endoscopy/biopsy likely limited dissemination and reduced case-fatality. Thus gastrointestinal symptoms with confirmed identification best explain the favorable prognosis and the mechanism is earlier detection and likely less advanced dissemination.

346.Option: The use of abdominal CT-scan is associated with a favorable prognosis because it enables earlier detection of disseminated disease
Evidence: There was a linear trend for the odds of having gastrointestinal signs and symptoms which increased significantly with time (P<0.0001). There was no such temporal trend for gastrointestinal endoscopies but there was a significant trend for an increase in the proportion of patients benefitting from and abdominal CT-scan, a gastrointestinal biopsy, and a decrease in abdominal ultrasonography.
The most frequently prescribed procedure was by far abdominal ultrasonography (71%), far more prescribed than gastrointestinal endoscopy (47.7%) and abdominal CT-Scan (30.7%).
endoscopies, notably colonoscopy, allow biopsies and opportunities to identify the pathogen either through mycological methods or histopathology.
346.Rationale: Although abdominal CT use increased over time, the passage does not link CT-scanning to the observed lower mortality. Instead, the study emphasizes that endoscopy/colonoscopy with biopsy provides opportunities to identify the pathogen and likely expedited diagnosis, which the authors suggest explains the lower case-fatality. CT-scan is less emphasized as the diagnostic modality leading to earlier definitive diagnosis.

346.Option: The presence of hepatomegaly and splenomegaly is associated with a favorable prognosis because it indicates a robust immune response to the infection
Evidence: A significant proportion of patients also had hepatomegaly and, to a lesser degree, splenomegaly.
Those with gastrointestinal H. capsulatum had lower TGO, LDH, Ferritin, prothrombin time than those without digestive signs.

The interval between symptom’s onset and diagnosis was shorter and the risk of death at one month was lower in those with gastrointestinal symptoms and a positive identification of H. capsulatum than in those without gastrointestinal symptoms.
346.Rationale: The passage notes hepatomegaly and splenomegaly as common findings but does not state that their presence predicts a better outcome; nor does it present a biologic rationale that these findings reflect a protective immune response. The study instead links favorable prognosis to gastrointestinal presentation with confirmed identification and earlier diagnosis, and reports lower laboratory markers of severity among those with gastrointestinal identification. Therefore the claim that hepatosplenomegaly indicates a favorable prognosis is unsupported by the text.

347.Option: Conduct a spatial cluster analysis of Anopheles occurrence records to identify high-risk zones for targeted vector control during peak transmission months
Evidence: In this study, we applied an ecological niche model to predict the distribution and seasonal variation of three major malaria vectors in China, including An. sinensis, An. lesteri, and An. minimus.

As precipitation and temperature are important climatic factors influencing mosquito population dynamics, we explored seasonal trends in the distribution range of Anopheles mosquitoes based on the habitat suitability maps output by the maxent model, using fluctuations in the mean monthly temperature and monthly precipitation to obtain monthly habitat suitability maps for three species of Anopheles (Figs 3, 5 and 7).
347.Rationale: The passage describes using a time-resolved ecological niche modeling approach (MaxEnt) with monthly environmental variables to generate seasonal habitat suitability maps. A pure spatial cluster analysis of occurrence records can identify geographic clusters but does not itself integrate environmental variability over time to produce dynamic, month-by-month habitat suitability maps. Therefore it does not best meet the stated goal of integrating environmental variability with temporal dynamics to inform seasonal, targeted vector control.

347.Option: Use a time-resolved ecological niche model with monthly environmental thresholds to generate dynamic habitat suitability maps for seasonal vector control planning
Evidence: In this study, we applied an ecological niche model to predict the distribution and seasonal variation of three major malaria vectors in China, including An. sinensis, An. lesteri, and An. minimus.

The survival of mosquitoes is mainly controlled by temperature and precipitation, so we predict the seasonal variation trend of the distribution range of Anopheles mosquitoes by the fluctuation of monthly mean temperature and monthly precipitation.

Using the raster calculator, raster maps of the suitable monthly mean temperature and suitable monthly precipitation for each species of Anopheles from January to December were obtained. The habitat suitability maps were overlaid with these two raster maps and the intersection was taken to produce a monthly habitat suitability map for each species of Anopheles.
MaxEnt 3.4.4 was employed to predict the potential distribution of An. lesteri, An. minimus, and An. sinensis.
347.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes exactly this approach: using an ecological niche model (MaxEnt) together with monthly mean temperature and monthly precipitation to derive monthly habitat suitability maps. This method directly integrates environmental variability (temperature, precipitation, land cover, elevation) with temporal dynamics (monthly maps) and is used by the authors to inform seasonal vector control planning, making it the best fit for the question.

347.Option: Use a machine learning model trained on satellite-derived land cover and temperature data to classify vector presence into fixed seasonal categories (e.g., high, medium, low) for annual control planning
Evidence: Maxent, one of the most widely used models in ecology, has excellent predictive performance and can produce distribution maps of comparable accuracy to ensemble methods [39].

Habitat suitability represents an index of the suitability of the area for the species, which reveals the probability that the species be present in the area. habitat suitability values range from 0–1, with values closer to 1 indicating that the area is more suitable for the species and closer to 0 indicating that the area is less suitable for the species.

As precipitation and temperature are important climatic factors influencing mosquito population dynamics, we explored seasonal trends in the distribution range of Anopheles mosquitoes based on the habitat suitability maps output by the maxent model, using fluctuations in the mean monthly temperature and monthly precipitation to obtain monthly habitat suitability maps for three species of Anopheles (Figs 3, 5 and 7).
347.Rationale: While machine learning on satellite-derived predictors is consistent with methods used in the passage (MaxEnt is a machine learning-type ENM), the option proposes classifying presence into fixed seasonal categories (high/medium/low). The passage emphasizes continuous, month-by-month habitat suitability maps (values 0–1) and deriving monthly thresholds rather than discretizing into fixed seasonal bins. Fixed categorical classification reduces temporal resolution and nuance compared with time-resolved monthly suitability maps and is therefore not the best approach for integrating environmental variability with fine temporal dynamics for targeted seasonal control.

347.Option: Develop a dynamic transmission model that simulates the spread of Plasmodium parasites through human and mosquito populations to predict seasonal risk windows for intervention
Evidence: Linking historical disease data to vector data may be more meaningful than simply predicting the distribution of vectors.
Where data allow, the addition of detailed information on imported malaria cases helps to further accurately model the risk of the spread of imported malaria in China.

As precipitation and temperature are important climatic factors influencing mosquito population dynamics, we explored seasonal trends in the distribution range of Anopheles mosquitoes based on the habitat suitability maps output by the maxent model, using fluctuations in the mean monthly temperature and monthly precipitation to obtain monthly habitat suitability maps for three species of Anopheles (Figs 3, 5 and 7).
347.Rationale: Dynamic transmission models that couple hosts and vectors are powerful for predicting seasonal risk windows and assessing interventions and could, in principle, incorporate environmental drivers. However, the passage focuses on integrating environmental variability directly into time-resolved habitat suitability mapping (ENM) as the principal tool to inform targeted vector control. The transmission model is not the approach used or demonstrated in the passage; it also typically requires additional epidemiological parameters and case data. Given the question’s emphasis on modeling spatial and temporal patterns of invertebrate vectors and integrating environmental variability to inform targeted vector control, the time-resolved ecological niche modeling approach is the better match in this context.

347.Option: Apply a time-series regression model to predict monthly malaria incidence based on historical case data and climate variables to guide vector control timing
Evidence: Linking historical disease data to vector data may be more meaningful than simply predicting the distribution of vectors.

As precipitation and temperature are important climatic factors influencing mosquito population dynamics, we explored seasonal trends in the distribution range of Anopheles mosquitoes based on the habitat suitability maps output by the maxent model, using fluctuations in the mean monthly temperature and monthly precipitation to obtain monthly habitat suitability maps for three species of Anopheles (Figs 3, 5 and 7).
The habitat suitability areas for the three species of Anopheles peaked at different times, with An. lesteri and An. minimus in the summer (June-August) and An. sinensis in the autumn (September).
347.Rationale: Time-series regression on malaria incidence and climate can certainly help guide timing of control by predicting case counts, and the passage acknowledges value in linking historical disease data to vector data. However, such models predict disease outcomes rather than directly modeling spatial habitat suitability of vectors across landscapes. The question centers on integrating environmental variability with temporal dynamics of the invertebrate vectors themselves to inform targeted vector control strategies; the passage shows that monthly habitat suitability mapping via ecological niche modeling is the direct approach for that purpose, making time-series incidence models a less optimal choice for the specific goal described.

348.Option: The rVSV filovirus GP vaccine provides long-term protection by inducing a strong immune response that clears the vector from the CNS before neuroinvasion can occur.
Evidence: In the current study, we evaluated the NV potential of the rVSV-ZEBOV-GP and the rVSV-MARV-GP vectors in cynomolgus macaques. The NV tests were modeled on previous examinations of the yellow fever virus vaccine [55] and rVSV HIV vector variants [59] where cynomolgus macaques were given an IT inoculation with 10^7 PFU of the vaccine and closely monitored over the course of 21 days.
None of the animals in the rVSV filovirus GP vaccine or vehicle control groups showed any evidence of neurodegeneration or neuroinflammation.

One animal from the rVSV-ZEBOV-GP group (358-09) had low levels of rVSV RNA detectable at day 21 post inoculation but no virus was isolated from the tissues (Table 2), while an animal from the rVSV-MARV-GP group (61-09) had low levels of detectable rVSV RNA in axillary lymph node (LN) taken at day 21 post inoculation with no virus isolated from this tissue (Table 2).
To date, the rVSV filovirus GP vectors have been used in over 80 NHPs with no signs of toxicity as a result of vaccination [8], [9], [11], [12], [18], [35].
348.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence that vaccine-induced immune responses 'clear the vector from the CNS before neuroinvasion' or that they confer long-term protection. The study was an intracerebral neurovirulence (NV) test with 21 days follow-up, not a long-term clearance study. Results show mostly no detectable rVSV RNA or infectious virus in neural tissues for the vaccine vectors and no neurodegeneration over 21 days, but that is not proof of an immune-mediated clearance mechanism from the CNS nor of long-term protection. Therefore this mechanistic and long-term claim is not supported by the presented data.

348.Option: The replacement of VSV glycoprotein with filovirus GP confers both strong immunogenicity and CNS safety in a replication-competent vector, supporting durable protection without risk of neuroinvasion
Evidence: Initial studies showed that a single intramuscular (i.m.) vaccination of cynomolgus macaques with a rVSV vector expressing the ZEBOV GP or MARV GP induced strong humoral and/or cellular immune responses and elicited complete protection against a high dose (1000 plaque forming unit [PFU]) i.m. challenge of homologous ZEBOV or MARV given 28 days later [8].

Vaccination by these different routes resulted in complete protection against homologous challenge and elicited robust ZEBOV GP-specific humoral responses and T-cell responses that were induced after vaccination and also 6 months after ZEBOV challenge [18].
None of the animals in the rVSV filovirus GP vaccine or vehicle control groups showed any evidence of neurodegeneration or neuroinflammation.
The clinical, histological, and virus replication data from this study indicate that the rVSV-ZEBOV-GP and rVSV-MARV-GP vectors lack the NV properties of the rVSV-wt vector.
Although these data suggest that the vectors are innocuous, the use of the rVSV filovirus GP vectors as vaccines and/or postexposure treatments in humans requires further safety testing.
348.Rationale: The passage supports that replacing VSV G with filovirus GP is associated with strong immunogenicity in prior protective studies and with substantially reduced neurovirulence in this intracerebral NV test. However, the option overstates conclusions by asserting 'durable protection without risk of neuroinvasion.' The NV study had 21 days follow-up and was designed to assess neurovirulence, not to demonstrate lifelong durability or absolute absence of risk. The authors explicitly note further safety testing is required. Thus the broad claim of no risk and durable protection is not supported by the presented data.

348.Option: The rVSV filovirus GP vaccine induces robust immune responses and prevents neuroinvasion by directly inhibiting viral replication in neural tissues through the expression of filovirus GP.
Evidence: Initial studies showed that a single intramuscular (i.m.) vaccination of cynomolgus macaques with a rVSV vector expressing the ZEBOV GP or MARV GP induced strong humoral and/or cellular immune responses and elicited complete protection ...

The lack of NV in the rVSV filovirus GP vaccine vectors when compared to rVSV-wt is encouraging considering that the vaccines are not attenuated through mutation of known virulence markers such as methionine 51 of the VSV M protein [66], [67].

The glycoprotein(s) of a virus generally contributes to cell tropism, and when compared to VSV, the filoviruses and influenza virus do not show a neural tropism. The deletion of VSV G and replacement of this protein with glycoproteins from foreign viruses that do not have a neural tropism would suggest the rVSV-(foreign glycoprotein) would not be NV.
One animal from the rVSV-ZEBOV-GP group (358-09) had low levels of rVSV RNA detectable at day 21 post inoculation but no virus was isolated from the tissues (Table 2).
348.Rationale: While the passage documents robust immune responses in prior vaccine efficacy studies and shows reduced neurovirulence for the filovirus GP vectors, it attributes the reduced neurovirulence to altered cell tropism after replacing VSV G, not to a mechanism of 'directly inhibiting viral replication in neural tissues' mediated by filovirus GP. There is no direct evidence presented that filovirus GP expression actively inhibits replication within neural cells; rather, the data are consistent with reduced neural tropism and lack of productive replication in neural tissues. Therefore the mechanistic claim in the option is not supported.

348.Option: The immunogenicity of the rVSV filovirus GP vaccine is directly linked to its ability to replicate in the CNS, which triggers protective immune responses without causing neuroinflammation due to the absence of viral spread.
Evidence: Each animal received two inoculations, one per hemisphere, with the left hemisphere receiving the experimental inoculation and the right hemisphere receiving the vehicle control.
None of the animals in the rVSV filovirus GP vaccine or vehicle control groups showed any evidence of neurodegeneration or neuroinflammation.
None of the other macaques had detectable rVSV RNA in neural tissues, spleen, liver, heart, kidney, axillary LN, or adrenal gland (data not shown for non-neural negative tissues).

In particular, it was interesting that one of these animals (56-09) also had rVSV and rVSV RNA detectable in the right frontal cortex, considering that the experimental inoculation was in the left hemisphere thalamus suggesting spread of rVSV-wt through the neural tissues.
348.Rationale: The data do not show that the vaccine vectors replicate efficiently in the CNS; in fact, rVSV RNA and infectious virus were largely absent from neural tissues in the vaccine groups, and no neuroinflammation was observed. The option incorrectly asserts that immunogenicity is linked to CNS replication triggering protective responses. The passage instead supports that lack of neural tropism (and lack of replication/spread in neural tissues) is associated with absence of neurovirulence. Therefore this option is not supported.

348.Option: The immunogenicity of the rVSV filovirus GP vaccine is maintained over time because the vector replicates efficiently in the CNS, which enhances long-term immune memory without causing neuroinflammation.
Evidence: In the current study, we evaluated the NV potential of the rVSV-ZEBOV-GP and the rVSV-MARV-GP vectors in cynomolgus macaques. The NV tests were modeled ... where cynomolgus macaques were given an IT inoculation with 10^7 PFU of the vaccine and closely monitored over the course of 21 days.
None of the animals in the rVSV filovirus GP vaccine or vehicle control groups showed any evidence of neurodegeneration or neuroinflammation.
None of the other macaques had detectable rVSV RNA in neural tissues, spleen, liver, heart, kidney, axillary LN, or adrenal gland (data not shown for non-neural negative tissues).

Vaccination by these different routes resulted in complete protection against homologous challenge and elicited robust ZEBOV GP-specific humoral responses and T-cell responses that were induced after vaccination and also 6 months after ZEBOV challenge [18].
348.Rationale: The passage does not support the claim that the vector replicates efficiently in the CNS; rather, it shows minimal or no replication in neural tissues for the vaccine vectors and absence of neuroinflammation over the 21-day observation period. While prior studies indicate durable immune responses after peripheral vaccination, there is no evidence that CNS replication is responsible for maintaining immunogenicity or long-term memory. Thus the assertion here is unsupported by the presented data.

349.Option: Infection with L. mexicana prevents the nuclear translocation of NF-κB, which is required for DC maturation and T cell activation
Evidence: Infection of DC 2.4 cells led to a reduction in the nuclear translocation of both AP-1 and NF-κB as soon as 1 hr post-infection (Figure 3A).

We also analyzed the expression of CD40. CD40 is an important co-stimulatory molecule involved in antigen presentation [40]. Even though CD40 expression was not altered by Leishmania infection, it could not compensate for the diminished expression of other co-stimulatory molecules (B7.1, B7.2), as we observed a significant decrease on IL-2 production by T cells.

Together, our data suggest that alterations in DC maturation and cytokine production upon infection with L. mexicana promastigotes involve activation of PTPs that can dephosphorylate important MAP kinases and inhibit nuclear translocation of AP-1 and NF-κB, resulting in DCs with a suppressed activation phenotype, characterized by decreased expression of MHC and costimulatory molecules, decreased IL-12 production and increased production of IL-10
349.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that L. mexicana infection reduces nuclear translocation of NF-κB (and AP-1). NF-κB nuclear translocation is a key step in DC activation/maturation and in driving cytokine and costimulatory molecule expression necessary for effective T cell activation. The described inhibition of NF-κB translocation is therefore a direct mechanistic explanation for the observed failure of infected DCs to support T cell activation.

349.Option: L. mexicana infection reduces the expression of MHC class II molecules, impairing antigen presentation to CD4+ T cells
Evidence: L. mexicana infection significantly decreased the basal levels of CD11b and B7.2, and slightly decreased MHC II and B7.1 expression (Figures 4 and 5).

infection for 24 hr prior to LPS stimulation significantly reduced the levels of MHC I, MHC II, CD11b and B7.1 in comparison with LPS-matured cells (Figures 4 and 5), suggesting that addition of LPS after infection is not sufficient to reverse the suppressing effect of L. mexicana promastigotes on the expression of these molecules.
349.Rationale: The passage documents that MHC II expression is reduced by L. mexicana infection, particularly when infection precedes LPS stimulation. Reduced MHC class II on DCs would impair peptide presentation to CD4+ T cells, providing a plausible explanation for the diminished T cell activation observed.

349.Option: Failure to upregulate CD40 expression in infected DCs prevents effective co-stimulation of T cells
Evidence: Interestingly, CD40 expression was increased in LPS-matured DCs, and its expression was not affected by L. mexicana.
Similarly to what we observed in DC2.4 cells, expression of CD40 was not altered in BMDC cells in either condition of infection.
349.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that CD40 expression was increased by LPS maturation and was not altered by L. mexicana infection. Therefore, a failure to upregulate CD40 is not occurring and cannot explain the impaired T cell activation. The authors even note that unchanged CD40 could not compensate for losses in other co-stimulatory molecules, indicating CD40 is not the primary failing factor.

349.Option: Impaired MAPK signaling prevents IL-12 production, leading to failure in T cell activation despite a strong maturation signal
Evidence: Our study revealed impairment of MAPK signaling in L. mexicana-infected DCs.
Infection with L. mexicana promastigotes inhibited basal phosphorylation of ERK, JNK and p38, without affecting total protein expression (Figure 2A right panels).

when DCs were first infected and then stimulated with LPS, IL-12 mRNA expression was reduced more than 30 fold, indicating that L. mexicana infection renders DCs refractory to further LPS stimulation, in line with the demonstration that LPS-signaling in infected cells was altered.
Together, these results demonstrate that L. mexicana infection impairs IL-12 production in DCs, which inhibits their ability to promote Th1 cell development.
349.Rationale: The passage links L. mexicana-mediated impairment of MAPK phosphorylation (ERK, p38) to a marked reduction in IL-12 production when infection precedes LPS stimulation. IL-12 is critical for Th1 differentiation and effective T cell responses; thus impaired MAPK signaling leading to reduced IL-12 provides a clear mechanistic explanation for failure of DCs to support T cell activation despite LPS.

349.Option: Increased IL-10 production by infected DCs directly suppresses T cell activation even after LPS stimulation
Evidence: We hypothesized that the decrease in DC activation could also be due to increased production of the suppressive cytokine IL-10, as IL-10 is known to suppress the expression of co-stimulatory molecules and cytokine production.

L. mexicana infection resulted in a modest increase in IL-10 production, similar to that induced by LPS stimulation (Figure 8D). However, DCs infected with L. mexicana and then stimulated with LPS, produced much higher levels of IL-10.
L. mexicana infected BMDCs stimulated with LPS showed reduced IL-12p70, increased IL-10 and a diminished ability to promote Th1 responses.
349.Rationale: The authors state that IL-10 is a suppressive cytokine and show that infected DCs, particularly when infected prior to LPS stimulation, produce much higher IL-10. Increased IL-10 can suppress co-stimulatory molecule expression and cytokine production, and the passage links elevated IL-10 with reduced IL-12 and impaired Th1 induction. Thus increased IL-10 is a plausible and supported contributor to the failure of T cell activation.

350.Option: Resistance emerged through a combination of recurrent mutation and migration of resistant haplotypes, with migration being the dominant driver of diversity
Evidence: We propose a model for anthelmintic resistance in which multiple independent resistance mutations recurrently arise in parasite populations and are then spread by migration.
There is a large amount of movement of sheep between farms in the UK and so it is likely that migration of resistance haplotypes plays an important role in the spread of resistance.
The striking differences we see in the selective sweeps between farms is consistent with the hypothesis that recurrent mutations occur.

T. circumcincta populations on farms 37 and 95 have little genetic differentiation based on neutral markers (FST; -0.0023) but have dramatically different selective sweeps; ten different resistance haplotypes for farm 37 and a four different resistance haplotypes on farm 95 without a single haplotype being shared.
350.Rationale: The passage explicitly proposes a model combining recurrent de novo mutations and migration: "multiple independent resistance mutations recurrently arise... and are then spread by migration." Thus a combination mechanism is supported. However the option adds that "migration [is] the dominant driver of diversity." The text emphasizes recurrent mutation as a key generator of diversity (the striking between-farm differences despite little neutral differentiation are cited as evidence that recurrent mutations occur). While migration is important for spread (e.g., Hr1 predominating on multiple farms likely due to movement), the authors do not state that migration is the dominant driver of diversity overall; rather they emphasise recurrent mutation as a principal source. Therefore the statement that migration is the dominant driver is not supported by the passage and makes the option incorrect.

350.Option: Resistance emerged through a single hard selective sweep that occurred before the onset of drug use and was maintained in the population through long-term selection
Evidence: In the classical model of adaptation, a beneficial mutation arises once and increases in frequency under the influence of selection. This is known as a "hard" selective sweep and is characterised by a single haplotype increasing in frequency.
we report a remarkably high diversity of benzimidazole resistance haplotypes with both hard and soft selective sweeps occurring in different parasite populations.
We propose a model for anthelmintic resistance in which multiple independent resistance mutations recurrently arise in parasite populations and are then spread by migration.
In the case of H. contortus, we identified resistance mutations from at least four independent origins and almost certainly more for T. circumcincta.
350.Rationale: A single hard sweep model (one origin before drug use) predicts a single predominant resistant haplotype. The passage documents high diversity of resistance haplotypes and explicitly reports both hard and soft sweeps and multiple independent origins (e.g., at least four independent origins for H. contortus). The authors propose recurrent independent mutations rather than a single ancestral sweep; thus the single hard-sweep-before-drug-use hypothesis is contradicted by the data and text.

350.Option: Resistance emerged through the spread of a single resistant haplotype via migration between farms, followed by recombination that generated new resistance variants
Evidence: There is a large amount of movement of sheep between farms in the UK and so it is likely that migration of resistance haplotypes plays an important role in the spread of resistance.

For H. contortus, just one haplotype (Hs10) of the total number of isotype-1 β-tubulin haplotypes (15) was identified as a recombinant (S7A Fig.). Although recombination was detected, it does not account for the majority of the haplotypic diversity for H. contortus.
In contrast, for T. circumcincta 22 of the total number of isotype-1 β-tubulin haplotypes (43) were identified as possible recombinants resulting from just 5 unique recombination events.
We propose a model for anthelmintic resistance in which multiple independent resistance mutations recurrently arise in parasite populations and are then spread by migration.
350.Rationale: While migration and recombination are both discussed and can contribute to diversity, the passage does not support a scenario where a single resistant haplotype spread and then recombined to produce the observed diversity. Instead, the authors argue for multiple independent origins (recurrent mutations) as a major source of diversity. Recombination is noted to be limited for H. contortus (one recombinant haplotype) and although more recombination was detected in T. circumcincta, the authors state that "Both re-current mutation and recombination contribute to the haplotypic diversity." The text therefore rejects the idea that a single lineage plus recombination alone explains the pattern; recurrent independent mutations are central. Thus this option is incorrect.

350.Option: Resistance emerged through recurrent de novo mutations rather than the spread of a single resistant lineage
Evidence: We propose a model for anthelmintic resistance in which multiple independent resistance mutations recurrently arise in parasite populations and are then spread by migration.
The striking differences we see in the selective sweeps between farms is consistent with the hypothesis that recurrent mutations occur.

T. circumcincta populations on farms 37 and 95 have little genetic differentiation based on neutral markers (FST; -0.0023) but have dramatically different selective sweeps; ten different resistance haplotypes for farm 37 and a four different resistance haplotypes on farm 95 without a single haplotype being shared.
In the case of H. contortus, for farms 37 and 95 there are completely different resistance haplotypes present in spite of very little genetic differentiation for neutral markers (FST 0.0087).
350.Rationale: The passage explicitly argues that multiple independent (recurrent) resistance mutations arise and that this recurrent mutation process explains the observed high diversity of resistance haplotypes despite minimal neutral genetic differentiation between farms. The authors use the contrast of low FST at neutral loci but very different resistance haplotype spectra across farms as strong evidence that new resistance mutations have arisen independently rather than being solely spread from a single lineage. Therefore this option correctly summarizes the mechanism the authors infer.

350.Option: Resistance emerged from pre-existing standing genetic variation that was selected for in multiple locations simultaneously
Evidence: Soft selective sweeps are characterised by the presence of multiple resistance haplotypes in the population(s) and can arise in two ways. First, from recurrent de novo mutations appearing on different susceptible haplotype backgrounds after the onset of the selection. Second, from polymorphisms already present in the standing genetic variation before the onset of selection.
However, the striking differences we see in the selective sweeps between farms is consistent with the hypothesis that recurrent mutations occur.

T. circumcincta populations on farms 37 and 95 have little genetic differentiation based on neutral markers (FST; -0.0023) but have dramatically different selective sweeps; ten different resistance haplotypes for farm 37 and a four different resistance haplotypes on farm 95 without a single haplotype being shared.
In both cases, with the exception of the P198L substitution, our analysis detects few recent recombination events consistent with mutations appearing on divergent susceptible haplotypes.
350.Rationale: Although soft sweeps can arise from standing variation, the authors argue that standing variation alone would produce more similar selective-sweep patterns across farms that have little neutral differentiation. The observed dramatic differences in resistance haplotypes between farms that are genetically similar at neutral loci are interpreted by the authors as evidence favoring recurrent de novo mutation (not solely selection on shared standing variation). Thus the option claiming emergence solely from standing genetic variation is not supported by the passage.

351.Option: High levels of systemic IL-10 are most strongly associated with severe urogenital pathology and may mask the true extent of Th1 immune suppression in individuals with high parasite burden
Evidence: IL-10 has both immunomodulatory and anti-inflammatory properties [43] that appear to be important during Sh infection.

Two studies showed that blockade of IL-10 with monoclonal antibodies of isolated PBMCs led to increased IFN-γ production and enhanced lymphocyte proliferation following stimulation with schistosome antigens SEA and AWA in people with Sh infection [68,69].

A Zimbabwean study assessing the cytokine response to AWA stimulation of PBMCs revealed that IL-10 was most strongly correlated with Sh infection intensity, and that participants with high intensity infection also tended to produce lower IL-5 levels [70].
Increased seminal eosinophils and IL-10 were seen in men with high egg burden when compared to men without Sh infection [157].
The intensity of Sh infection appears to be important, with a higher burden of disease triggering a stronger local immune response.
351.Rationale: The passage documents that IL-10 is an important regulatory cytokine in S. haematobium infection and that IL-10 levels correlate with infection intensity and can suppress IFN-γ (evidence above). However, the review emphasizes that parasite burden (infection intensity, egg burden) — not systemic IL-10 per se — is the factor most consistently linked to severe genitourinary pathology (e.g., higher egg burdens correlate with more local pathology and altered local cytokines). While IL-10 can down-modulate Th1 responses (blockade increases IFN-γ), the passage does not indicate that systemic IL-10 is the single strongest predictor of severe urogenital pathology. Therefore the assertion that high systemic IL-10 is the most strongly associated host factor is not supported by the review.

351.Option: High parasite burden is most strongly associated with severe urogenital pathology and may confound systemic immune response measurements by introducing a strong pro-inflammatory signal independent of infection intensity
Evidence: The intensity of Sh infection appears to be important, with a higher burden of disease triggering a stronger local immune response.

A study in Kenyan individuals with Sh infection and concomitant bladder pathology assessed by ultrasonography reported a 12-fold higher serum TNF-α production at baseline and 13-fold higher production in response to schistosome and purified protein derivative antigen stimulation than in persons with Sh infection without bladder pathology [74].

Sh urinary egg burden correlated inversely with IFN-γ levels in PBMCs after AWA or SEA stimulation from individuals who developed Sh reinfection two years after PZQ treatment, suggesting a lower pro-inflammatory immune environment in those with the highest Sh burden [75].

S. haematobium disease burden was reported to impact urinary cytokine expression in children with higher Sh urinary egg burden, who had higher urinary levels of IL-6 and lower IL-10, compared to children with lower disease burden [67].

Collectively, the above studies demonstrate that chronic Sh infection with ova in genitourinary tissues leads to a local inflammatory reaction ... The intensity of Sh infection appears to be important, with a higher burden of disease triggering a stronger local immune response.
351.Rationale: The review repeatedly identifies parasite burden / egg burden as the key factor linked to severe urogenital pathology and stronger local immune changes (quotes above). High burden is associated with marked local inflammatory markers (e.g., higher IL-6, TNF-α in those with pathology) and thus can alter systemic or measured immune profiles. That means infection intensity can confound interpretation of immune-readouts in cross-sectional studies: differences in cytokines or cell populations may reflect differences in burden rather than a baseline host trait. (Note: the option's phrase 'independent of infection intensity' is self-contradictory — the intended point is that high burden itself introduces strong inflammatory signals that must be accounted for when interpreting immune data. The central claim that parasite burden is the strongest host-associated predictor of severe urogenital pathology and that it can distort immune measurements is supported by the passage.)

351.Option: Female sex is most strongly associated with severe urogenital pathology and may lead to overestimation of systemic immune activation due to hormonal influences on cytokine production
Evidence: Sex hormones affect immune function and lead to differences in the immune phenotype and disease response between women and men [76,77]. Furthermore, sex differences in immune response also vary over the lifespan and with reproductive status, which is particularly pronounced in women with fluctuations due to the menstrual cycle as well as during pregnancy, breastfeeding, and menopause [78–82].

In a Senegalese cohort, women with Sh infection produced lower levels of TNF-α and IFN-γ following SEA stimulation of PBMCs, while they produced more TGF-β and IL-10 and had higher expression of the apoptosis marker nuclear matrix protein, compared to men with Sh infection with comparable egg burdens and schistosome circulating anodic antigen (CAA) levels [83].

Studies investigating local mucosal changes are critically important ... there are numerous additional potential confounders, including physiologic changes occurring throughout the menstrual cycle [78,151,152], effect of recent pregnancy [153] or current breastfeeding [79], recent sexual contact, and vaginal cleansing practices [154,155], which were not consistently assessed in the above studies.
351.Rationale: Although the review documents sex-related differences in immune responses and notes that female-specific factors (menstrual cycle, pregnancy, hormones) can influence mucosal immunity, it does not state that female sex is the strongest predictor of severe urogenital pathology. Instead, infection intensity/egg burden is emphasized as the principal factor associated with local pathology. Thus the claim that female sex is most strongly associated with severe pathology is not supported. The comment about hormonal influences potentially affecting immune readouts is valid as a caveat, but it does not make female sex the primary risk factor according to the passage.

351.Option: Reinfection status is most strongly associated with severe urogenital pathology and may result in misclassification of immune response data due to transient cytokine spikes after treatment
Evidence: individuals living in endemic areas frequently become reinfected with Sh after successful treatment with praziquantel (PZQ) [13] and can experience additional DALYs lost after reinfection.

PZQ treatment increased the production of IFN-γ, IL-2, IL-5, IL-10, and TNF-α in whole blood of previously infected children in response to schistosome antigens AWA and SEA and also increased production of the immunosuppressive cytokine, IL-10, but not the other cytokines, in response to generic antigen stimulation [58].
These studies suggest a time dependent cytokine response post-PZQ treatment likely due to an initial surge of Sh antigen release from dying adult worms which diminishes over time [86].
Repeated treatment led to high IL-5 and low IFN-γ production in response to AWA in comparison to no or single treatment, but was not protective against Sh reinfection.
351.Rationale: The passage notes that reinfection is common and that praziquantel treatment can provoke transient changes in cytokines (a post-treatment surge), which indeed can complicate interpretation of immune data collected near treatment. However, the review consistently emphasizes parasite burden / egg burden as the key correlate of severe genitourinary pathology; reinfection contributes to cumulative exposure but is not singled out as the strongest immediate correlate of severe pathology. Therefore the statement that reinfection status is the most strongly associated host factor is not supported, even though reinfection and post-treatment cytokine dynamics are relevant methodological considerations.

351.Option: Age is most strongly associated with severe urogenital pathology and may lead to underestimation of immune response magnitude due to age-related immune senescence
Evidence: Cytokine patterns varied with age, with increasing IL-4 and IL-10 levels, and decreasing IL-5 levels in plasma, in those with Sh infection as age increased [72].
Adolescents with Sh infection aged 15–16 years showed the highest IL-4 and IL-5 responses to SWAP stimulation of whole blood, and similarly IL-5 peaked in this age group after ConA stimulation [70].
Younger men had higher egg secretion in both urine and semen compared to men above age 39 [157], possibly reflecting partial immunity to the helminth with increasing age.
A weakness of this study was lack of data analysis by host sex, despite its potential impact on the immune response.
351.Rationale: Age clearly modifies immune responses to S. haematobium and infection intensities vary by age (younger individuals often have higher egg excretion), but the review does not identify age as the single strongest predictor of severe urogenital pathology. Instead, infection intensity / egg burden is emphasized repeatedly as the principal determinant of local pathology. The specific claim about 'immune senescence' causing underestimation of immune response magnitude is not supported by the provided text (the passage documents age-related shifts but not generalized immune senescence as the main interpretation issue). Thus the option overstates the role of age relative to parasite burden.

352.Option: The vaccine's limited duration of protection allows non-targeted serotypes to re-emerge and dominate after vaccine immunity wanes
Evidence: Phase III clinical trials demonstrated that the three inactivated EV-A71 vaccines showed over 90% efficacy against EV-A71-associated HFMD and nearly 100% efficacy in preventing severe manifestations of the disease [17,18,20].
The implementation of the EV-A71 vaccine has resulted in a significant reduction of over 90% in EV-A71-associated HFMD cases in China since 2016 [47].

monovalent EV-A71 vaccines do not offer cross-protection against other serotypes and have low coverage rates in the province. These factors may contribute to the persistent epidemic of HFMD in Guizhou and inform enhancements to pathogen surveillance for HFMD.
352.Rationale: The passage emphasizes high efficacy of the EV-A71 vaccine and attributes continued circulation of other serotypes to lack of cross-protection and low coverage, not to brief duration of protection. There is no discussion of rapid waning of vaccine-induced immunity as the driver; instead the text links the persistence of other serotypes to absence of cross-protection and ecological/epidemiologic shifts after vaccine introduction. Therefore the specific claim that limited duration of protection is the most likely cause is not supported by the passage.

352.Option: The vaccine alters the host microbiome in a way that promotes colonization and transmission of non-targeted serotypes
Evidence: monovalent EV-A71 vaccines do not offer cross-protection against other serotypes and have low coverage rates in the province. These factors may contribute to the persistent epidemic of HFMD in Guizhou and inform enhancements to pathogen surveillance for HFMD.

the antibodies induced by EV-A71 and CV-A16 do not provide protection for susceptible populations against infections caused by other EVs serotypes, which may exert selective pressure on infections from other EVs serotypes [46].
high rates of gene mutations and recombinations, as well as the launch of the EV-A71 vaccine [6].
352.Rationale: The passage attributes ongoing circulation of other serotypes to lack of cross-protection, selective pressure, and viral genetic change (mutation/recombination). It does not mention effects of vaccination on the host microbiome or microbiome-driven promotion of other serotypes. There is no evidence in the passage supporting a microbiome-mediated mechanism, so this option is not supported.

352.Option: The absence of cross-protection allows non-targeted serotypes to fill the ecological niche left by declining EV-A71 transmission
Evidence: monovalent EV-A71 vaccines do not offer cross-protection against other serotypes and have low coverage rates in the province. These factors may contribute to the persistent epidemic of HFMD in Guizhou and inform enhancements to pathogen surveillance for HFMD.

The implementation of the EV-A71 vaccine has resulted in a significant reduction of over 90% in EV-A71-associated HFMD cases in China since 2016 [47]. However, the antibodies induced by EV-A71 and CV-A16 do not provide protection for susceptible populations against infections caused by other EVs serotypes, which may exert selective pressure on infections from other EVs serotypes [46].
Other EVs have replaced EV-A71 and CV-A16 as the main serotypes of HFMD in recent years.
352.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the monovalent EV-A71 vaccine does not provide cross-protection against other enterovirus serotypes and that EV-A71 incidence fell after vaccine introduction while other EVs became predominant. This describes exactly the ecological niche-filling process: targeted-serotype declines due to vaccination, and non-targeted serotypes continue circulating because vaccine-induced immunity does not protect against them. Thus absence of cross-protection is the most directly supported factor.

352.Option: The monovalent vaccine induces immune suppression in the host, increasing susceptibility to other enterovirus serotypes
Evidence: Phase III clinical trials demonstrated that the three inactivated EV-A71 vaccines showed over 90% efficacy against EV-A71-associated HFMD and nearly 100% efficacy in preventing severe manifestations of the disease [17,18,20].

monovalent EV-A71 vaccines do not offer cross-protection against other serotypes and have low coverage rates in the province. These factors may contribute to the persistent epidemic of HFMD in Guizhou and inform enhancements to pathogen surveillance for HFMD.
352.Rationale: There is no mention in the passage that the EV-A71 vaccine causes immune suppression; on the contrary, trials showed high efficacy and near-complete prevention of severe disease, which is inconsistent with a vaccine-induced generalized immune suppression. The passage identifies lack of cross-protection and viral genetic changes as contributors to persistence of other serotypes, not vaccine-mediated immune suppression.

352.Option: The high mutation rate of non-targeted serotypes enables them to evade immune responses generated by the monovalent vaccine
Evidence: this epidemiological pattern is gradually changing due to high rates of gene mutations and recombinations, as well as the launch of the EV-A71 vaccine [6].

recombination has long been regarded as a driving force in the evolution of EVs, potentially leading to the emergence of new recombinants with increased virulence and transmissibility [48]. Additionally, this process may facilitate EVs in acquiring advantageous traits from diverse genomes throughout their evolutionary journey [37].

the antibodies induced by EV-A71 and CV-A16 do not provide protection for susceptible populations against infections caused by other EVs serotypes, which may exert selective pressure on infections from other EVs serotypes [46].
352.Rationale: While the passage documents high mutation/recombination rates and notes that viral evolution can increase transmissibility or virulence, the core reason other serotypes continue to circulate despite EV-A71 vaccination is that vaccine-induced antibodies do not cross-protect against those serotypes. The phrasing of this option implies non-targeted serotypes must 'evade' vaccine-generated immune responses; however, they are antigenically distinct and thus unaffected by EV-A71-specific immunity regardless of mutation. Mutation/recombination may contribute to emergence or increased spread of certain non-targeted serotypes, but evasion of a vaccine directed at a different serotype is not the main mechanism described in the passage.

353.Option: The high frequency of genetic admixture across all sampled locations indicates that natural dispersal is sufficient to maintain connectivity among populations
Evidence: Ae. aegypti dispersal can occur in one of two ways: the slower, active dispersal of flying adult females in search of bloodmeals or oviposition sites, or the faster, passive human-mediated dispersal, by which humans unintentionally transport mosquitoes via vehicle traffic (boats, cars, planes, etc).
Ae. aegypti genetic differentiation across various spatial scales is likely due to its limited flight range (rarely exceeding 100m under natural conditions [35–37])
Structure results showed a clear pattern of genetic admixture from all the sample populations, suggesting steady gene flow within this region
Our results support the hypothesis that Ae. aegypti long-distance dispersal is driven by human activity, with particular emphasis on boat traffic.
353.Rationale: Although the study observes genetic admixture, the passage explicitly states that natural (active) dispersal is limited by a very short flight range ("rarely exceeding 100m"), making natural dispersal unlikely to explain the long-distance genetic mixing observed. The authors interpret the admixture as evidence of human-mediated (passive) transport (boats, barges), not sufficient natural movement. Therefore the statement that admixture indicates natural dispersal is sufficient is not supported by the passage.

353.Option: The high genetic differentiation in Barrio Florida, a town with minimal large-vehicle traffic, compared to low differentiation in Iquitos and surrounding towns with frequent boat traffic, indicates that human transportation networks are the primary driver of gene flow
Evidence: Barrio Florida populations, in contrast, were the most genetically isolated compared to other towns.
Mosquitoes collected from Iquitos A and B (the major and secondary port for fluvial transit) had lower FST values than mosquitoes collected from the interior of Iquitos.

Iquitos is the major regional transportation hub, with frequent visits of barges and other types of boats from surrounding areas, whereas Barrio Florida is a small town (pop: ~800), receiving most of its traffic from vehicles unlikely to be infested with Ae. aegypti (e.g., speed boats and small water taxis) [24].
Our results support the hypothesis that Ae. aegypti long-distance dispersal is driven by human activity, with particular emphasis on boat traffic.
353.Rationale: The contrast between high genetic isolation in Barrio Florida (which receives little traffic from infested large vehicles) and low differentiation in Iquitos and nearby towns (which are well connected by boat traffic and have low FST at port sites) directly supports the conclusion that human transportation networks—especially boat traffic—drive gene flow. The authors explicitly link these patterns to transportation differences and conclude that human-mediated dispersal explains long-distance connectivity.

353.Option: The absence of significant genetic differentiation between Iquitos port sites and surrounding towns suggests that natural dispersal is not limited by distance in this region
Evidence: Ae. aegypti genetic differentiation across various spatial scales is likely due to its limited flight range (rarely exceeding 100m under natural conditions [35–37])
Mosquitoes collected from Iquitos A and B (the major and secondary port for fluvial transit) had lower FST values than mosquitoes collected from the interior of Iquitos.
Iquitos A (Puerto Masusa) serves as a major hub of fluvial transit and predominantly harbors large barges, which carry cargo and passengers throughout the Peruvian Amazon distances up to ~500km.
Our results support the hypothesis that Ae. aegypti long-distance dispersal is driven by human activity, with particular emphasis on boat traffic.
353.Rationale: The lack of differentiation between port sites and surrounding towns is better explained by human-mediated transport (boats carrying mosquitoes long distances) than by unrestricted natural dispersal. The passage states active flight is typically <100 m and describes large barges traveling hundreds of kilometers; the authors attribute low FST at ports to boat traffic. Thus the observed genetic similarity does not indicate that natural dispersal is not limited by distance.

354.Option: The elevated synonymous divergence in specific lectin genes is most likely due to a higher mutation rate in those genomic regions compared to the rest of the genome
Evidence: We would expect, under positive selection, that the number of nonsynonymous differences per nonsynonymous site (dN) would be high, but that dS would not differ from dS in neighbouring genes.

The pattern we observed for the igl genes EHI_006980:EDI_276450 and EHI_065330:EDI_244250 and lgl genes EHI_049690:EDI_071530, where dS was notably higher than for neighbouring genes, suggested a mechanism generating diversity other than positive selection on nonsynonymous mutations. The pattern indicated gene conversion, in which gene regions are changed to match a paralogue that may be more divergent than a gene's true orthologue, hence the elevated dS.

While it should be noted that the lower sequence coverage of E. dispar, compared to E. histolytica, could result in more errors in its sequence, such errors would inflate the estimated divergence (d) for all genes. Thus, the unusually high dS in lectin genes relative to their neighbours and the the genomic average would not be affected.
354.Rationale: The passage attributes the unusually high, localized dS to gene conversion rather than a localized higher mutation rate. If a higher mutation rate were responsible one would expect broader regional effects or evidence in neighboring genes; the authors explicitly note the pattern suggests a mechanism other than positive selection and state it "indicated gene conversion." They also note sequencing errors would inflate divergence genome-wide, not produce a localized spike, arguing against a local mutation-rate explanation.

354.Option: The elevated synonymous divergence in specific lectin genes is best explained by gene conversion between paralogous gene copies within the same family
Evidence: The pattern we observed for the igl genes EHI_006980:EDI_276450 and EHI_065330:EDI_244250 and lgl genes EHI_049690:EDI_071530, where dS was notably higher than for neighbouring genes, suggested a mechanism generating diversity other than positive selection on nonsynonymous mutations. The pattern indicated gene conversion, in which gene regions are changed to match a paralogue that may be more divergent than a gene's true orthologue, hence the elevated dS.

To confirm that dS values between positional orthologues were unusually high, they were compared to a genomic average (Figure 3). The median dS between orthologous genes of E. histolytica and E. dispar was 0.38, and 99% of dS values were below 0.73. The igl genes EHI_006980:EDI_276450 and EHI_065330:EDI_244250 and the lgl genes EHI_049690:EDI_071530 all had dS values in the top 1%, strongly supporting the hypothesis that gene conversion has occurred among these genes.
Phylogenetic analysis supported the hypothesis that gene conversion had made paralogous igl genes more similar than orthologous igl genes.
354.Rationale: The authors explicitly conclude that the localized, unusually high dS is best explained by gene conversion among paralogues. They compare lectin-gene dS to a genome-wide distribution, find these genes in the top 1% of dS, and state that this pattern "indicated gene conversion," with phylogenetic trees showing paralogues clustering—consistent with conversion making paralogues more similar than orthologues.

354.Option: The elevated synonymous divergence in specific lectin genes is best interpreted as evidence of horizontal gene transfer from a distantly related organism
Evidence: The pattern we observed for the igl genes EHI_006980:EDI_276450 and EHI_065330:EDI_244250 and lgl genes EHI_049690:EDI_071530, where dS was notably higher than for neighbouring genes, suggested a mechanism generating diversity other than positive selection on nonsynonymous mutations. The pattern indicated gene conversion, in which gene regions are changed to match a paralogue that may be more divergent than a gene's true orthologue, hence the elevated dS.
Phylogenetic analysis supported the hypothesis that gene conversion had made paralogous igl genes more similar than orthologous igl genes.
The expected pattern, given no gene conversion, is that orthologues should cluster together, yet the tree shows strong bootstrap support for the clustering of paralogous igl genes (Figure 4A).
354.Rationale: The passage explicitly attributes the unexpected high dS to gene conversion among paralogues, supported by phylogenetic patterns. There is no discussion or evidence presented for horizontal gene transfer from a distantly related organism; instead the data (paralogous clustering and high local dS relative to neighbours) are consistent with intragenomic gene conversion rather than HGT.

354.Option: The high synonymous divergence in specific lectin genes likely results from incomplete lineage sorting, where ancestral polymorphisms have not yet fixed in the two species
Evidence: The pattern we observed for the igl genes EHI_006980:EDI_276450 and EHI_065330:EDI_244250 and lgl genes EHI_049690:EDI_071530, where dS was notably higher than for neighbouring genes, suggested a mechanism generating diversity other than positive selection on nonsynonymous mutations. The pattern indicated gene conversion, in which gene regions are changed to match a paralogue that may be more divergent than a gene's true orthologue, hence the elevated dS.
Phylogenetic analysis supported the hypothesis that gene conversion had made paralogous igl genes more similar than orthologous igl genes.
Both igl pairs and one lgl pair fall within the top 1% of dS values.
354.Rationale: Incomplete lineage sorting is not invoked or supported in the passage. The authors compare local dS to a genomic background and interpret the highly elevated, localized dS together with paralogue clustering in phylogenies as evidence of gene conversion. The pattern (paralogues clustering and top 1% dS) is more parsimoniously explained by gene conversion rather than ancestral polymorphism persisting only at these loci.

354.Option: The unusually high synonymous divergence in specific lectin genes likely reflects positive selection acting on synonymous sites to increase genetic diversity for immune evasion
Evidence: We would expect, under positive selection, that the number of nonsynonymous differences per nonsynonymous site (dN) would be high, but that dS would not differ from dS in neighbouring genes.

The pattern we observed for the igl genes EHI_006980:EDI_276450 and EHI_065330:EDI_244250 and lgl genes EHI_049690:EDI_071530, where dS was notably higher than for neighbouring genes, suggested a mechanism generating diversity other than positive selection on nonsynonymous mutations. The pattern indicated gene conversion, in which gene regions are changed to match a paralogue that may be more divergent than a gene's true orthologue, hence the elevated dS.
354.Rationale: The passage explains that positive selection on protein function would raise dN rather than dS, and that elevated dS is not the expected signature of positive selection; instead the authors interpret high dS as evidence of gene conversion. Therefore positive selection acting on synonymous sites is not the favored interpretation in the text.

355.Option: Pathogenic Leptospira spp. avoid detection by neutrophils through downregulation of surface lipopolysaccharide, reducing immune activation and NET formation
