Nonetheless, the question asks which factor most critically undermines the ability to assess whether observed differences are due to treatment versus unmeasured biological variability. While inconsistent outcome measures are a serious problem for synthesis and interpretation, the passage emphasizes missing reporting of key biological and environmental variables (Option 1) as the chief reason researchers cannot separate treatment effects from underlying biological variability. Thus lack of standardized outcomes is important but not the single most critical factor for the specific issue posed.

388.Option: The high prevalence of asymptomatic infections is due to a delayed seroconversion process, which results in a lag between infection and detection, thereby reducing the apparent incidence of symptomatic cases

388.Rationale: While the passage documents delays between PCR-positivity and antibody/LST markers (e.g., PCR becomes positive before seroconversion; LST-positivity develops months later), the model and conclusions attribute the discrepancy not to detection lag but to a true epidemiologic pattern: most infections remain asymptomatic and these asymptomatic hosts sustain transmission. Thus although delayed seroconversion is factual, it does not explain why symptomatic case incidence remains low despite widespread treatment; the primary explanation given in the passage is the reservoir and transmission role of asymptomatic infections rather than mere detection lag.

388.Option: Treatment failure rates are so high that most treated patients relapse into symptomatic disease, which maintains the incidence of KA despite widespread treatment

For KA patients surviving treatment, a fraction of treatment failure of 5% is assumed [9], [21].

These results suggest that transmission of KA is predominantly driven by asymptomatically infected hosts who are not eligible for treatment.
388.Rationale: Although the passage notes localized high treatment failure (e.g., up to 60% in some foci) and discusses treatment failure parameters, the model's main explanation for persistent incidence is that asymptomatically infected hosts—who are not identified or treated—drive transmission. The M-shaped curves have two peaks. The first peak of P/P (0.51) occured in November followed by a serological peak of IgM in December (7.89%). The second and largest peak of IgM was in July (8.14%).

non-locals typically work and dwell closer to sandfly habitats like the burrows of rodents (reservoirs of disease) and are consequently plagued by biting sandflies.

Evidence: In Al Ahsa, we found that non-local patients (visiting labour) had significantly higher levels (P<0.001) of anti-PpSP32 compared to the local residents (Fig. 5A).
non-locals typically work and dwell closer to sandfly habitats like the burrows of rodents (reservoirs of disease) and are consequently plagued by biting sandflies.
nearly three quarters of the non-local patients developed more than three lesions compared to only 40% in the local group (Fig. 5B).
Ph. papatasi was the only vector species found in Al Ahsa and Al Madinah

nearly three quarters of the non-local patients developed more than three lesions compared to only 40% in the local group (Fig. 5B).

IL-6 levels were more significantly elevated in the patients with severe VL (Fig 3A; p = 0.03). Remarkably, severe VL patients that died had IL-6 serum levels of 11,443 ± 15,388 pg/ml, whereas the surviving severe VL patients had 2,848 ± 7,512 pg/ml (Fig 3B; p = 0.01). All of the patients that died had circulating IL-6 levels ≥ 200 pg/ml, while only 2 patients that survived (1 SVL, 1 classical VL) presented with IL-6 levels above this threshold.
393.Rationale: IL-6 shows the clearest and most direct link to the worst clinical outcomes: (1) elevated in active disease, (2) strongly correlated with organomegaly and other severity markers, and (3) markedly higher in fatal cases. Those facts make elevated IL-6 the strongest single immunological/clinical feature in the paper supporting the hypothesis that systemic inflammation contributes directly to disease severity and mortality.

393.Option: The strong positive correlation between IL-10 levels and both spleen/liver enlargement and elevated liver enzymes, despite IL-10 being an anti-inflammatory cytokine
Evidence: Spleen and liver enlargement, the classical manifestation of VL, both strongly and positively correlated with the circulating levels of IL-10, IL-6, IL-27 and TNF-α (all p≤0.0005).
Similarly, serum levels of ALT and AST, which are also considered as markers of disease severity, positively correlated with TNF-α, IL-27 and IL-10 (p ≤0.0005).
High levels of IFN-γ, IL-10, IL-6, IL-27 and TNF-α cytokines were observed in sera of VL patients before treatment when compared with healthy control and DTH+ individuals (Fig 1).
Several studies have associated the presence of IL-10 in the serum, PBMCs, spleen, liver or lymph nodes of VL patients with disease severity (reviewed in [15])...
393.Rationale: Although IL-10 is conventionally anti-inflammatory, the passage shows IL-10 is elevated in active disease and strongly correlates with organomegaly and liver enzyme elevations—key clinical severity markers. This association supports the idea that systemic immune mediators (including regulatory cytokines) are tightly linked to disease severity, either as contributors to pathogenesis (e.g., by permitting parasite proliferation) or as markers of dysregulated systemic inflammation that accompanies severe disease.

393.Option: High levels of IFN-γ and TNF-α in active VL patients, which are positively correlated with spleen and liver enlargement and liver enzyme elevation
Evidence: High levels of IFN-γ, IL-10, IL-6, IL-27 and TNF-α cytokines were observed in sera of VL patients before treatment when compared with healthy control and DTH+ individuals (Fig 1).
Spleen and liver enlargement, the classical manifestation of VL, both strongly and positively correlated with the circulating levels of IL-10, IL-6, IL-27 and TNF-α (all p≤0.0005).
The present study does not indicate significant differences in IFN-γ levels between classical (VL D0) and severe (SVL) VL patients.
393.Rationale: While TNF-α is elevated and correlates with clinical severity, IFN-γ—though elevated in active disease—was not shown to differ between classical and severe VL in this study. The option claims both IFN-γ and TNF-α are positively correlated with organomegaly and liver enzyme elevation; the passage supports TNF-α but does not support IFN-γ having a strong correlation with severity. Therefore the combined statement as written is incorrect.

393.Option: The elevated levels of IL-27 and its strong correlation with IL-6, IL-10, and TNF-α, suggesting a central role in orchestrating the cytokine storm
Evidence: In this study, we observed high circulating levels of IFN-γ, IL-10, IL-6, TNF-α and IL-27 during active VL disease.
We observed a strong correlation between IL-27 and IL-6 (r = 0.69), IL-10 (r = 0.85) and TNF-α (r = 0.86) (Fig 2).

we not only confirm the presence of high serum levels of inflammatory and anti-inflammatory cytokines in active VL, but describe for the first time high concentrations of IL-27 and its correlation with clinical profile...
393.Rationale: IL-27 is elevated in active disease and shows very strong correlations with multiple other key cytokines implicated in severity (IL-6, IL-10, TNF-α). The passage also links IL-27 to disease-promoting functions (e.g., stimulating IL-10–producing T cells). Together, these points support IL-27 as a central orchestrator of the systemic inflammatory milieu ('cytokine storm') that the authors propose contributes directly to disease severity.

394.Option: A deceleration of growth parameter (p) less than 1, indicating sub-exponential or linear growth, suggests that transmission is constrained by factors such as intervention timing or population heterogeneity, which delays the observable impact of control measures
Evidence: We utilize two phenomenological growth models, the generalized growth model (GGM) and the generalized logistic growth model (GLM) that have been validated by deriving short-term forecasts for multiple infectious diseases in the past, including SARS, pandemic Influenza, Ebola, and Dengue [52,53].

We generate short term forecasts using the generalized growth model (GGM) that characterizes the early ascending phase of the epidemic by estimating two parameters: (1) the intrinsic growth rate, r; and (2) a dimensionless “deceleration of growth” parameter, p.

This equation becomes constant incidence over time if p = 0 and an exponential growth model for cumulative cases if p = 1. Whereas if p is in the range 0< p <1, then the model indicates sub-exponential growth dynamics [54,55].

The GLM model ... The deceleration parameter modulates the epidemic growth patterns, including the sub-exponential growth (0< p <1), constant incidence (p = 0) and exponential growth dynamics (p = 1).

The incidence curve displays sub-exponential growth dynamics with the scaling of growth parameter (deceleration of growth parameter), p, estimated at 0.77 (95% CI: 0.73, 0.81) and the intrinsic growth rate, r, estimated at 0.81 (95% CI: 0.67, 1.00).

We also estimate the reproduction number from May 9th- July 7th, 2020, ... The incidence curve displays a nearly linear growth trend with the deceleration of growth parameter, p, estimated at 0.51 (95% CI: 0.47, 0.56).

The 20-day ahead forecast suggested that the early social distancing measures significantly slowed down the early spread of the virus in Chile, whose effect is noticeable about two weeks after implementing an intervention, as shown in Fig 6.

The commixture of interventions, including localized lockdowns, night-time curfew, school closures, and the ban on social gatherings in Chile, can probably be attributed to preventing the disease trend from growing exponentially during the early growth phase, as has occurred elsewhere [3,4].
394.Rationale: The passage explicitly defines the deceleration parameter p and states that p<1 indicates sub-exponential growth (including near-linear growth when p≈0.5). The authors observe p≈0.77 early and p≈0.51 later, interpreting these as sub-exponential/linear growth and linking the pattern to interventions and other constraints. Thus, observing p<1 on the epidemic curve is the graphical/modeling feature that most strongly indicates non-exponential transmission, and it implies that transmission is being constrained (by interventions, heterogeneity, behavior), which also affects interpretation of control measures (e.g., effects may appear with delay and the epidemic may grow slower than pure exponential).

394.Option: A sharp decline in case counts immediately after the implementation of interventions, indicating that the epidemic is under control and that no further measures are needed
Evidence: The 20-day ahead forecast suggested that the early social distancing measures significantly slowed down the early spread of the virus in Chile, whose effect is noticeable about two weeks after implementing an intervention, as shown in Fig 6.

The 20-day ahead forecast suggested that the early social distancing measures significantly slowed down the early spread of the virus in Chile, whose effect is noticeable about two weeks after implementing an intervention, as shown in Fig 6.
This study has some limitations. First, our study analyzes cases by the dates of reporting while it is ideal to analyze the cases by the dates of onset or after adjusting for reporting delays.

Therefore, in the absence of pharmacological interventions and considering the advent of second waves in Asia and Europe, non-pharmacological interventions such as the ones implemented in Chile are the available options for countries to address the pandemic before large segments of the population are immunized with effective and safe vaccines.
This study ... underscores the need for persistent isolation and social distancing measures to stomp all active disease transmission chains in Chile.
394.Rationale: The passage indicates intervention effects are generally not immediate—"effect is noticeable about two weeks after implementing an intervention"—and it emphasizes the need for persistent measures. Therefore, an immediate sharp decline after interventions is neither the expected graphical signature of non-exponential dynamics nor a reason to stop interventions. The statement that no further measures are needed is contradicted by the authors' emphasis on continued social distancing and limits caused by reporting delays and resurgence risk.

394.Option: A consistent doubling time across the epidemic curve, which would indicate that transmission is not affected by interventions and that control measures have no impact on the trajectory
Evidence: This equation becomes constant incidence over time if p = 0 and an exponential growth model for cumulative cases if p = 1. Whereas if p is in the range 0< p <1, then the model indicates sub-exponential growth dynamics [54,55].

The GLM model ... The deceleration parameter modulates the epidemic growth patterns, including the sub-exponential growth (0< p <1), constant incidence (p = 0) and exponential growth dynamics (p = 1).

The commixture of interventions, including localized lockdowns, night-time curfew, school closures, and the ban on social gatherings in Chile, can probably be attributed to preventing the disease trend from growing exponentially during the early growth phase, as has occurred elsewhere [3,4].
394.Rationale: A consistent doubling time is the hallmark of exponential growth (p≈1), not of non-exponential dynamics. The passage describes that p=1 corresponds to exponential growth and p<1 to sub-exponential. Moreover, the passage documents that interventions altered the trajectory (preventing exponential growth), so a constant doubling time would not indicate interventions had no impact. Thus the option's assertion is incorrect for identifying non-exponential dynamics and for interpreting control measures.

394.Option: A rapid increase in case counts with a doubling time of less than 3 days, indicating exponential growth, which would suggest that control measures are ineffective if not implemented immediately
Evidence: This equation becomes constant incidence over time if p = 0 and an exponential growth model for cumulative cases if p = 1. Whereas if p is in the range 0< p <1, then the model indicates sub-exponential growth dynamics [54,55].

The commixture of interventions, including localized lockdowns, night-time curfew, school closures, and the ban on social gatherings in Chile, can probably be attributed to preventing the disease trend from growing exponentially during the early growth phase, as has occurred elsewhere [3,4].

The 20-day ahead forecast suggested that the early social distancing measures significantly slowed down the early spread of the virus in Chile, whose effect is noticeable about two weeks after implementing an intervention, as shown in Fig 6.
394.Rationale: A very rapid doubling time indicates exponential growth (p≈1), the opposite of the non-exponential feature the question asks about. While rapid exponential growth raises urgency for control, the passage shows that well-timed interventions can alter trajectories and their effects may appear after a delay (~two weeks), so the statement that such growth would imply control measures are ineffective unless immediate is an overgeneralization and not the best descriptor for non-exponential dynamics.

394.Option: A plateau in case counts after an initial rise, indicating that the epidemic has reached herd immunity, which would mean that further control measures are unnecessary
Evidence: This simple logistic growth type model typically supports single peak epidemics in the number of new infections followed by a burnt-out period, unless external driving forces such as the seasonal variations in contact patterns exist.

The 20-day ahead forecast suggested that the early social distancing measures significantly slowed down the early spread of the virus in Chile, whose effect is noticeable about two weeks after implementing an intervention, as shown in Fig 6.
Our current findings point to sustained transmission of SARS-CoV-2 in the early phase of the outbreak, with our estimate of the reproduction number at R~1.8.
This study ... underscores the need for persistent isolation and social distancing measures to stomp all active disease transmission chains in Chile.

The high positivity rate at the beginning of the epidemic indicates that the government failed to cast a wide enough net to test the masses early in the pandemic, and there were probably many more active cases than those detected by epidemiological surveillance, underestimating the epidemic growth curve [90–92].
394.Rationale: A plateau can arise for reasons other than herd immunity (e.g., interventions, behavior change, testing limitations). The passage notes that interventions caused slowed spread and emphasizes ongoing need for measures; it also warns of under-ascertainment and resurgence. Therefore, interpreting a plateau as evidence of herd immunity and abandoning control measures is not supported by the passage or by sound epidemiologic interpretation.

395.Option: The lower culling rate in Sikka compared to Manggarai reduced the perceived risk of rabies, leading to higher vaccination uptake in Sikka
Evidence: The proportion of dog owners who had vaccinated their dogs was significantly (p<0.001) higher in Sikka (65%) than in Manggarai (39%) (Table 5).
In contrast, the proportion of dog owners that had culled their dogs was significantly higher in Manggarai (45%) than in Sikka (27%) (p<0.001).

The animal health authority of Sikka has focused on vaccination of dogs as the main approach to control rabies in the regency, which is in line with the national campaign. Whereas the authority in Manggarai implemented culling of roaming dogs as an additional control measure alongside the national campaign.
The difference in vaccination uptake between the regencies might also be due to the intensity of local community support for the control campaign.
395.Rationale: While the passage documents that culling was more common in Manggarai and vaccination uptake higher in Sikka, there is no evidence in the text that a lower culling rate in Sikka reduced perceived risk and thereby increased vaccination. The passage attributes differences to differing control approaches and community support (e.g., active involvement of local leaders) rather than a reduced perceived risk because of lower culling. Therefore the specific causal mechanism proposed (lower culling → reduced perceived risk → higher vaccination) is not supported by the quoted evidence.

395.Option: The higher proportion of dog owners in Sikka who kept female dogs for breeding increased their motivation to vaccinate, explaining the disparity in vaccination uptake between regencies
Evidence: The proportion of vaccination uptake was also significantly higher for owners of female dogs (59%; 87/147) than male dogs (49%; 147/303) (p<0.05).
The dog owners who held female dogs for breeding had significantly higher odds to vaccinate their dogs (OR = 2.07; 95%CI = 1.31–3.27) compared with those who did not.
Dog owners from Sikka were more likely to vaccinate their dogs (OR = 4.07; 95% CI = 2.30–7.20) than those from Manggarai.
395.Rationale: The passage shows that owning female dogs for breeding is associated with higher individual uptake and that Sikka had higher overall uptake. However, the text does not provide evidence that Sikka had a higher proportion of owners of female breeding dogs relative to Manggarai. Without explicit data showing a higher prevalence of female-breeding-dog ownership in Sikka, we cannot conclude that this factor explains the inter-regional disparity. Thus the option asserts a between-regency difference that is not supported by the quoted evidence.

395.Option: The difference in vaccination uptake is primarily due to the higher economic value of dogs in Sikka, which increased owners' willingness to vaccinate their animals
Evidence: Dog owners who owned dogs with an economic value ranging between Rp250,000–500,000 tended to vaccinate their dogs (OR = 2.38; 95%CI = 1.36–4.17) compared with owners who valued their dogs at less than or equal to Rp250,000.
Dog owners from Sikka were more likely to vaccinate their dogs (OR = 4.07; 95% CI = 2.30–7.20) than those from Manggarai.
The vaccination uptake was significantly associated with the knowledge of the dog owners about rabies (p<0.001) and its control measures (p<0.05) (Table 6).
395.Rationale: Economic value of dogs is associated with higher likelihood of vaccination at the owner level, but the passage does not present data demonstrating that dogs in Sikka systematically had higher economic value than in Manggarai, nor that this is the primary driver of the inter-regional difference. The text highlights multiple predictors (regency, accessibility, female-breeding dogs, income) and emphasizes accessibility and community engagement as important reasons for the regional disparity. Therefore attributing the difference primarily to higher dog economic value in Sikka is not supported by the provided evidence.

395.Option: Differences in regional control strategies led to disparities in infrastructure access and community engagement, which directly influenced vaccination uptake despite similar knowledge levels
Evidence: The animal health authority of Sikka has focused on vaccination of dogs as the main approach to control rabies in the regency, which is in line with the national campaign. Whereas the authority in Manggarai implemented culling of roaming dogs as an additional control measure alongside the national campaign.
Religious and village leaders in Sikka participated actively in encouraging dog owners to vaccinate their dogs, whereas this was not the case in Manggarai.
Geographical accessibility is one of the important predictors of vaccination uptake among dog owners.

This finding suggests that targeted distribution of vaccination campaign information within these villages is an effective and practical way to increase the uptake of rabies vaccination in the future.
395.Rationale: The passage supports that regional control strategies differed (Sikka emphasized vaccination, Manggarai used culling) and that community engagement and geographical accessibility influenced uptake. However, the option claims that regional control strategies 'led to disparities in infrastructure access'—a causal link that the passage does not make. Infrastructure/accessibility is described as an independent predictor (villages differ in accessibility), not as a consequence of control strategy. While differences in strategy and community engagement are relevant, the statement that strategies produced disparities in infrastructure access is unsupported. Therefore the option is not correct as written.

395.Option: The higher vaccination uptake in Sikka is due to greater religious influence from Catholic leaders, who actively promoted the campaign compared to Manggarai
Evidence: Religious and village leaders in Sikka participated actively in encouraging dog owners to vaccinate their dogs, whereas this was not the case in Manggarai.
The encouragement of community leaders may have stimulated dog owners to increase their efforts to catch and restrain the dog to be vaccinated.
Our findings suggest that the involvement of local communities in rabies control activities can be important to implement rabies control measures successfully in a regency.
395.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that religious and village leaders in Sikka actively encouraged vaccination and that this community leader involvement likely stimulated higher owner participation, whereas such involvement did not occur in Manggarai. This provides a direct, text-supported explanation for higher uptake in Sikka despite similar knowledge levels. Thus this option correctly identifies community religious leadership promotion as a key factor explaining the inter-regional disparity.

396.Option: The spatial distribution of dengue incidence is primarily driven by human population density, with weather conditions acting as a secondary modifier of transmission risk
Evidence: We conclude that a coupling of temperature-humidity is an important and sensitive determinant of DENV transmission-potential regulating the location, timing and intensity of risk.
With 30 million people in Peru, the largest population centers are mostly clustered along the western coast.
Numerous factors, in addition to weather, influence the size of epidemics locally. Spatial distribution and movement of people have been associated with the spatial distribution of disease [45,46].
396.Rationale: Although the passage acknowledges that human population distribution and movement influence dengue spread, the study's empirical results and conclusions emphasize that weather — specifically coupled temperature-humidity thresholds and durations — is the fundamental regulator of where, when, and how large transmission occurs. Thus the claim that spatial distribution is "primarily driven by human population density" contradicts the authors' main conclusion that weather is the primary determinant; population is an important covariate but not presented as the primary driver in the study.

396.Option: The spatial patterns of dengue incidence are best explained by the frequency of extreme weather events, such as heatwaves or heavy rainfall, rather than sustained temperature and humidity thresholds
Evidence: Possible thresholds per weather component for predicting transmission were tested based on the full range of weather conditions observed across Peru, 2005–2012 with stepping intervals of 0.5°C for temperature and 2% for humidity. At each threshold of each weather component tested, the mean duration in weeks spent above that threshold annually was assessed for each district.
We did not include rainfall because data of sufficient quality were not available.
Epidemic magnitude was highly sensitive to duration in specific areas of temperature-humidity weather-space, increasing exponentially with longer duration in optimal conditions.

Previously, in an analysis of the weather-dengue link in Thailand, rainfall was examined in conjunction with temperature and humidity [3]. Results of that study indicated that local seasonal onset of dengue occurred after the rainy season began, but no correlation was observed between the amount of rainfall and the timing or magnitude of dengue disease.
396.Rationale: The study explicitly focused on sustained thresholds and duration above temperature and humidity cutoffs (not isolated extreme events) and quantified annual duration above thresholds as key predictors. Rainfall (an extreme-event proxy) was excluded due to data limitations and prior cited work did not show amount of rainfall correlated with magnitude. Therefore the assertion that extreme events rather than sustained thresholds best explain spatial patterns contradicts the study's empirical approach and results.

396.Option: Disease incidence is directly proportional to the annual duration of time spent above mean temperature >22°C, with no additional influence from humidity or other weather factors
Evidence: Out of more than 2 trillion classification tests performed to fully explore Peruvian weather-space, only one set of weather inputs achieved a classification of districts as dengue-positive or negative with 95% sensitivity and specificity. The combined criteria: mean temperature >22°C for 7 or more weeks per year and minimum temperature >14°C for 33 or more weeks per year provided a classification of districts with 95% sensitivity and 95% specificity, and overall 5% misclassification rate (Fig 2).

In level 2 of the classification tree, true positive districts were categorized into 5 groups ... The most important observation from level 2 classifications was that although temperature alone identified positive vs. negative districts in level 1, mean humidity in conjunction with temperature was necessary for differentiating groups by transmission intensity, indicating a coupling of temperature-humidity in the dynamics of large epidemics.
Epidemic magnitude was highly sensitive to duration in specific areas of temperature-humidity weather-space, increasing exponentially with longer duration in optimal conditions.
396.Rationale: While mean temperature >22°C for a minimum duration identifies areas where transmission is possible (presence/absence), the study shows humidity is an additional and necessary factor for explaining differences in epidemic magnitude. The authors explicitly note that temperature alone separated positives from negatives but humidity combined with temperature was necessary to differentiate transmission intensity. Thus the statement that incidence is driven solely by duration above 22°C with no additional influence from humidity contradicts the study's findings.

396.Option: Transmission potential is determined by temperature thresholds for initiating transmission, but epidemic magnitude is exponentially amplified by sustained high humidity during optimal temperature periods
Evidence: Dengue-positive districts were identified by mean temperature >22°C for 7+ weeks and minimum temperature >14°C for 33+ weeks per year, defining environmental conditions needed to sustain biological processes that enabled transmission.
Mean humidity in conjunction with temperature was necessary for differentiating groups by transmission intensity, indicating a coupling of temperature-humidity in the dynamics of large epidemics.
Epidemic magnitude was highly sensitive to duration in specific areas of temperature-humidity weather-space, increasing exponentially with longer duration in optimal conditions.

High dengue impact was concentrated in a small area of weather-space above 25°C mean temperature and above 75% mean humidity. Impact was highest as humidity increased above 80% when mean temperature was 25–29°C.
396.Rationale: This option accurately synthesizes the study's central empirical findings: specific temperature thresholds and durations define the potential for transmission (i.e., where transmission can occur), while high and sustained humidity concurrent with optimal temperatures amplifies epidemic magnitude — with the study noting an exponential relationship between duration in optimal temperature-humidity space and incidence. The quoted sentences are direct statements from the study supporting this two-part relationship.

396.Option: High dengue incidence occurs only in areas where mean temperature exceeds 25°C for more than 20 weeks per year, regardless of humidity or other factors
Evidence: Annual duration above 22°C was the best predictor of incidence magnitude in lower incidence districts; duration above 25°C was the best predictor in higher incidence districts.

Annual duration above mean humidity levels of 70%, 75%, and 80% when mean temperature was concurrently above 25°C were associated with the highest rate of increasing cumulative incidence (p<0.0001 in each scenario), producing the largest epidemics when humidity >80% for >6 weeks per year or >75% for >19 weeks per year, while concurrently mean temperature >25°C.
High dengue impact was concentrated in a small area of weather-space above 25°C mean temperature and above 75% mean humidity.
396.Rationale: The study does identify mean temperature around 25°C as part of the optimal range for high impact, but it does not assert that >25°C for >20 weeks alone (and "regardless of humidity") is sufficient or the only condition for high incidence. Instead, the authors emphasize the joint role of humidity — specifying that high humidity concurrent with >25°C (e.g., >80% for >6 weeks or >75% for >19 weeks) is associated with the largest epidemics. Thus the option's exclusivity ("only" and "regardless of humidity") is contradicted by the empirical findings.

397.Option: The parasite's surface proteins are conserved across trematode species and are expressed in both fish and mammalian hosts, allowing for cross-species targeting in reservoirs
Evidence: Metacercariae of O. viverrini were collected from infected fishes obtained from reservoirs in Khon Kaen province, Thailand.
Ov-tsp-1 is expressed in all stages; expression was highest in metacercariae followed by two-week old juveniles, adults and eggs in descending order (Figure 3).

Ov-TSP-1 shares 97% identity with a TSP of its close relative, Clonorchis sinensis (GAA49954.1) calculated from the alignment over 229 amino acid (amino acid position 19–247 of Ov-TSP-1 to 87–315 of C. sinensis), and 74% identity with Sm-TSP-1 of Schistosoma mansoni (XP_002580456.1) (Figure 1).
397.Rationale: The passage documents that Ov-tsp-1 is highly expressed in the metacercarial stage (the stage present in infected fish reservoirs) and that Ov-TSP-1 sequence is highly similar to tetraspanins from other trematodes. Those two facts together support the feasibility of cross-species targeting in reservoir hosts: the antigen is present in the fish-associated stage (metacercariae) and the protein family is conserved across related parasites, which favors interventions in non-human reservoirs to interrupt transmission to humans.

397.Option: The parasite's tegumental proteins are expressed throughout all life stages and are essential for survival in both fish and mammalian hosts, enabling broad-spectrum targeting in reservoirs
Evidence: Ov-tsp-1 is expressed in all stages; expression was highest in metacercariae followed by two-week old juveniles, adults and eggs in descending order (Figure 3).

Suppression of Ov-tsp-1 in adult flukes impacts on proper tegument formation and results in increased vacuolation, implying that this protein is essential for fluke development and survival and is therefore worthy of consideration as a vaccine and/or drug target.
397.Rationale: While the passage supports that Ov-tsp-1 is expressed across life stages and that suppression in adults impairs tegument formation and implies essentiality for development and survival in the mammalian (adult) stage, it does not provide evidence that the protein is essential for survival within the fish (intermediate) host. The claim that it is essential in both fish and mammalian hosts is not established by the data presented (RNAi effects were demonstrated in adult flukes), so the option overstates what the passage supports.

397.Option: The parasite's tegumental proteins are highly expressed during the metacercarial stage in fish and are essential for excystation, making them ideal targets for intervention in the reservoir host
Evidence: Ov-tsp-1 is expressed in all stages; expression was highest in metacercariae followed by two-week old juveniles, adults and eggs in descending order (Figure 3).
RNAi targeting Ov-tsp-1 resulted in deformities of the tegument of adult worms within 24 hours of exposure to the dsRNA.
397.Rationale: The passage confirms high expression of Ov-tsp-1 in the metacercarial stage (the fish-associated stage), but it does not provide any evidence that Ov-tsp-1 is essential for excystation (the process by which metacercariae emerge in the definitive host). The functional data shown are for adult tegument malformation after RNAi; excystation is not addressed. Therefore the specific claim about essentiality for excystation is unsupported.

397.Option: The parasite's tegumental proteins are highly expressed during early developmental stages in mammalian hosts and are accessible to immune responses, making them viable targets for intervention in reservoir hosts
Evidence: Ov-tsp-1 is expressed in all stages; expression was highest in metacercariae followed by two-week old juveniles, adults and eggs in descending order (Figure 3).
Anti-Ov-TSP-1 serum localized Ov-TSP-1 to the tegument of adult worms in the liver of hamsters infected with O. viverrini.
Ov-TSP-1 is recognized by sera from O. viverrini-infected humans and hamsters, indicating that the LEL is accessible to antibodies and is indeed immunogenic during natural infection.
397.Rationale: The passage documents substantial expression in early mammalian-stage juveniles and shows that the protein is localized to the tegument and is accessible to and recognized by host antibodies. Those features (expression in early intra-mammalian stages plus immune accessibility) support the idea that targeting such tegument proteins in reservoir hosts via vaccination or other immunological interventions could interrupt transmission.

397.Option: The parasite's surface antigens are recognized by host antibodies during natural infection, indicating immune exposure and enabling vaccine development in reservoir hosts
Evidence: Anti-Ov-TSP-1 serum localized Ov-TSP-1 to the tegument of adult worms in the liver of hamsters infected with O. viverrini.
Immunoblot analysis showed that sera from humans and hamsters infected with O. viverrini both reacted with rOv-TSP-1 (Figure 5).
Ov-TSP-1 is recognized by sera from O. viverrini-infected humans and hamsters, indicating that the LEL is accessible to antibodies and is indeed immunogenic during natural infection.
397.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that Ov-TSP-1 is accessible to antibodies and is recognized by sera from naturally infected humans and experimentally infected hamsters, and it notes tetraspanins as vaccine candidates (including work aimed at reservoir hosts). Recognition by host antibodies during natural infection is a key feature supporting feasibility of vaccine-based interventions in reservoir hosts to interrupt transmission.

398.Option: The use of praziquantel in both humans and animals in the same region suggests that treatment of both populations is necessary to interrupt transmission
Evidence: Nissen et al. found that half of the North Vietnamese farm dogs that had been given a single dose of praziquantel were reinfected with FZT within three months [24]. Hence they could not recommend anthelmintic treatment as the only intervention to control FZT in dogs.
Two intervention groups were studied; one farm management group with control of snail vectors and fecal pollution of pond, and one group with drug treatment of humans and animals.
398.Rationale: While the passage documents use of praziquantel in animals and humans and reports rapid reinfection of treated dogs (supporting the idea that treating animals alone may not be sufficient), it does not state that the mere use of praziquantel in both populations is the strongest factor indicating human-only interventions will fail. The passage more directly attributes the risk of continued transmission to the presence of animal reservoirs and environmental egg contamination rather than to the fact that praziquantel happens to be used in both populations. Thus this option overstates what the cited treatment practice alone implies.

398.Option: The presence of multiple parasite species in humans and animals indicates that cross-species transmission is common and may sustain infection in the community
Evidence: FZT include the liver flukes Clonorchis sinensis and Opisthorchis viverrini as well as a large number of minute intestinal fluke (MIF) species.
FZT infections in Northern Vietnam are a mix of C. sinensis and MIF. C. sinensis seems to be much less common in fish than in humans.

Studies from the same area have revealed high prevalence of different MIF-species, especially Haplorchis spp., in dogs, cats and pigs, suggesting that domestic animals could play an important role as reservoir hosts and in the transmission of FZT [9], [10].
398.Rationale: The passage documents multiple parasite species and co-occurrence in humans and animals, and it raises cross-species issues. However, the strongest factor arguing that interrupting human infection alone may not reduce community transmission is the high prevalence of infection in domestic animals serving as reservoirs (explicitly stated). The mere presence of multiple parasite species does not by itself most strongly indicate sustained transmission independent of humans; the reservoir role of infected animals is the more direct and emphasized reason.

398.Option: High prevalence of the same parasite species in domestic animals suggests that animal reservoirs maintain transmission even if human infections are controlled
Evidence: Studies from the same area have revealed high prevalence of different MIF-species, especially Haplorchis spp., in dogs, cats and pigs, suggesting that domestic animals could play an important role as reservoir hosts and in the transmission of FZT [9], [10].
Nguyen et al. [9], [10] found that dogs, cats and pigs are important sources of FZT eggs, in particular minute intestinal fluke eggs, to the environment in two endemic areas in Northern Vietnam.

As humans are only one of several reservoirs that contribute to the transmission, decreasing the egg output through preventive chemotherapy of humans may not have a lasting effect on reducing transmission and risk of FZT infection.
398.Rationale: These quotes explicitly state that domestic animals are frequently infected, contribute eggs to the environment, and are one of several reservoirs. That directly supports the conclusion that high prevalence of infection in animals can maintain transmission even if human infections are reduced, making this the strongest factor in the passage.

398.Option: The high rate of reinfection in humans after treatment suggests that behavioral risk factors are strong and may undermine any intervention targeting only human infection
Evidence: In the present study, we found a high reinfection rate of FZT within one year following treatment with praziquantel.

When analyzing gender, age and previous infection as risk factors for reinfection at 60 weeks of follow up, we see in the single logistic regression model that significantly more men than women become reinfected. Increasing age is also associated with reinfection, as is previous infection. However, in the multiple logistic regression model only previous infection remains associated with reinfection, indicating confounding.

This indicates that the seemingly close association between male gender, increasing age and reinfection we observed (crude numbers), is possibly confounded by risk behavior of eating raw fish, a tradition mostly common among men [27].
398.Rationale: The passage supports that behavioral risk (eating raw fish) drives rapid human reinfection and that prior infection (a proxy for risky behavior) predicts reinfection. However, the question asks which factor most strongly suggests that interrupting human infection alone may not reduce community transmission in a setting where both humans and animals are infected. Behavioral reinfection is important, but the passage emphasizes animal reservoirs and environmental egg contamination as the primary reason human-only interventions may not have lasting impact. Therefore this option is not the strongest single factor.

398.Option: The long lifespan of adult liver flukes in humans suggests that reinfection may be masked by persistent infection, making it difficult to assess the impact of human-only interventions
Evidence: One possible explanation is that it is a relatively rare event for a human to acquire an infection with C. sinensis compared to a MIF infection, but once acquired, C. sinensis infections are long lived, 26 years has been described [29], [30].
Annual preventive chemotherapy may then decrease the proportion of C. sinensis infections in humans, as by far most of the reinfection will be due to MIF.
398.Rationale: The passage notes that C. sinensis can be long-lived in humans, which could complicate assessment of interventions. However, the question concerns why interrupting human infection alone may not reduce transmission community-wide when domestic animals are also infected. The long lifespan of liver flukes in humans speaks more to challenges in measuring intervention impact and species composition changes, not to the core transmission-maintaining role of animal reservoirs. Thus it is not the most direct or strongest factor cited in the passage.

399.Option: The frequency of mixed blood meals involving both humans and animals would most strongly influence the relative contribution of each species to Zika virus spread in co-occurring populations.
Evidence: other parameters such as the feeding behaviour and mosquito densities can modulate pathogens transmission in nature.

Indeed, preliminary studies in Central Africa showed that Ae. aegypti occurs across the region and Ae. albopictus is found under 6°N latitude. Meanwhile, in the sympatric areas, Ae. albopictus is almost dominant except in some rare locations [22–24], and irrespective to the season in the areas with short dry season [48].
In this study, mixed blood meals animal-human were detected, confirming that this species could act also as a bridge vector for zoonotic pathogens [49].
399.Rationale: Mixed (animal-human) blood meals can matter for zoonotic spillover and indicate the potential for a species to act as a bridge vector, but the question asks which factor most strongly influences the relative contribution to spread among humans beyond intrinsic transmission ability. The passage emphasizes feeding behaviour (human preference) and mosquito densities/population dominance as key modulators of transmission. While mixed blood meals are relevant, they are not portrayed as the primary driver of human epidemic spread in the passage, so this option overstates their relative importance.

399.Option: The rate of viral replication in the mosquito midgut, as measured by viral titres in the body, would most strongly determine each species' relative contribution to Zika virus spread in co-occurring areas.
Evidence: Overall, ZIKV titres were significantly higher in Ae. aegypti compared to Ae. albopictus (Chi-squared test: χ2 = 6.4527, df = 1, P = 0.01).

In this study, we assessed for the first time the ability of Ae. aegypti and Ae. albopictus collected in different ecological settings in Central Africa to transmit a ZIKV strain ... We demonstrated that this ZIKV strain was able to replicate, disseminate, and be secreted in saliva of both Ae. aegypti and Ae. albopictus.
other parameters such as the feeding behaviour and mosquito densities can modulate pathogens transmission in nature.
399.Rationale: Viral replication and titres are measures of intrinsic vector competence (how well a mosquito can support and transmit virus). The question asks for a factor beyond intrinsic ability to transmit. The passage explicitly points out that non-intrinsic factors such as feeding behaviour and mosquito densities modulate transmission. Therefore, while midgut replication influences competence, it is not the factor 'beyond intrinsic ability' that the question seeks.

399.Option: The presence of refractory genes in mosquito populations would most strongly influence the relative contribution of each species to Zika virus spread, even in areas with high vector competence.
Evidence: Nevertheless, lower transmission rates and saliva ZIKV titres in Ae. aegypti were found for two populations from northern part of Cameroon, Benoué and Maroua, probably due to the presence of specific refractory genes [42, 43].
This result is consistent with previous studies showing the level of infection varied among mosquito populations [28, 30].
other parameters such as the feeding behaviour and mosquito densities can modulate pathogens transmission in nature.
399.Rationale: Refractory genes are an intrinsic genetic determinant of vector competence and can reduce transmission in particular populations (as the passage notes). The question asks for the strongest influence beyond intrinsic transmission ability; refractory genes do not fit that category because they are part of intrinsic variation. The passage emphasizes behavioral and ecological factors (feeding preference, densities, distribution) as modulators beyond intrinsic competence.

399.Option: Human blood feeding preference and population dominance in urban areas would likely outweigh intrinsic transmission efficiency in determining each species' role in Zika virus spread
Evidence: other parameters such as the feeding behaviour and mosquito densities can modulate pathogens transmission in nature.

Indeed, preliminary studies in Central Africa showed that Ae. aegypti occurs across the region and Ae. albopictus is found under 6°N latitude. Meanwhile, in the sympatric areas, Ae. albopictus is almost dominant except in some rare locations [22–24], and irrespective to the season in the areas with short dry season [48].
It was also demonstrated that in Yaoundé (Cameroon), Ae. albopictus preferentially fed on humans rather than on available domestic animals.
399.Rationale: The passage identifies feeding behaviour (human preference) and mosquito densities/population dominance as key non-intrinsic determinants of transmission. Aedes species that bite humans more frequently and are locally dominant—especially in urban settings where human-mosquito contact rates are high—will contribute more to human transmission even if intrinsic transmission efficiencies are similar. The cited sentences directly support that human feeding preference and dominance in given settings strongly influence each species' epidemiological role.

399.Option: The ability of each species to survive in diverse ecological settings, including both urban and rural environments, would most strongly determine their relative contribution to Zika virus spread.
Evidence: Indeed, preliminary studies in Central Africa showed that Ae. aegypti occurs across the region and Ae. albopictus is found under 6°N latitude. Meanwhile, in the sympatric areas, Ae. albopictus is almost dominant except in some rare locations [22–24], and irrespective to the season in the areas with short dry season [48].
other parameters such as the feeding behaviour and mosquito densities can modulate pathogens transmission in nature.

In addition, as our results showed that the transmission rate in both species vary according to the population origin, mosquito microbiome composition, genetic structure and gene flow of both species across the central African region, should be further explored to determine their impact on ZIKV transmission.
399.Rationale: Ability to survive across diverse ecological settings (distribution, persistence) influences where a species is present and thus its potential contribution. The passage documents differing distributions of the two species. However, the question states the species 'coexist and share breeding sites,' focusing on situations where presence overlaps. In that context, behavioral factors (human feeding preference) and local population dominance/density are more directly determinative of transmission to humans. Thus while ecological breadth matters at larger scales, it is not the single strongest factor 'beyond intrinsic ability' in the sympatric, shared-breeding context described.

400.Option: Use a single-dose challenge model in mice to determine the minimum effective antibody titer required for protection and extrapolate duration from that threshold
Evidence: Intracerebral infection poses an extremely harsh challenge that is artificial, but accepted by the scientific community as a stringent experimental test.
We found a clear correlation between the induction of protective virus neutralization titers and survival upon infectious challenge.

This might indicate that the titer of 0.5 IU/ml is rather a surrogate marker for protection for a natural rabies infection (e.g. intramuscular in the periphery) than for the laboratory intracerebral infection in our experimental setting.
400.Rationale: While the passage shows intracerebral challenge in mice can identify a protective titer and that VN titers correlate with survival, it also cautions that the i.c. challenge is an artificial, harsh test and that the standard 0.5 IU/ml correlate may not reliably predict protection in that model. Extrapolating duration of protection from a single-dose challenge-derived threshold ignores kinetics of antibody decay and the biological differences between challenge conditions and natural exposure. The passage therefore supports using longitudinal measurement rather than a single-dose challenge to infer duration.

400.Option: Administer a booster dose at the end of the seasonal risk period and measure the rise in antibody titers to infer the duration of the primary immune response
Evidence: The third vaccination at day 49 did not further increase RABV-G specific neutralizing antibody levels.

We tested the longevity of the humoral immune response. BALB/c mice treated with two vaccinations on days 0 and 21 ... were observed for about one year and antibodies in the serum of vaccinated mice were analyzed monthly.
400.Rationale: Measuring the response to a booster given at the end of the risk period tells you about the ability to be boosted and the secondary response, but does not directly demonstrate whether the primary immune response remained protective throughout the seasonal risk window. The passage shows the investigators directly measured antibody longevity with repeated sampling over time (monthly for about a year), and also notes that an additional (third) vaccination did not necessarily raise titers, illustrating that a booster-response approach is an indirect and potentially misleading method to infer primary-duration coverage.

400.Option: Calculate the half-life of virus-neutralizing antibodies in vaccinated individuals using a single post-vaccination measurement and extrapolate to the full seasonal period
Evidence: We tested the longevity of the humoral immune response. BALB/c mice treated with two vaccinations on days 0 and 21 ... were observed for about one year and antibodies in the serum of vaccinated mice were analyzed monthly.
The kinetic of virus-neutralizing antibodies in BALB/c mice from (D). Immunization points are indicated by arrows. Mean an SD is presented (n = 8/group).
400.Rationale: Antibody half-life and kinetics cannot be estimated from a single post-vaccination measurement; they require multiple timed measurements to observe decay. The passage describes exactly this approach (monthly sampling over ~1 year and reporting kinetics), indicating that serial measurements are necessary to determine duration of protection. Thus the single-measurement extrapolation is not appropriate.

400.Option: Compare the incidence of rabies cases in vaccinated versus unvaccinated populations during peak transmission months to assess whether protection lasts through the seasonal risk period
Evidence: a widely recognized and standardized functional correlate of protection (virus neutralizing titers).
A clinical phase I study to test safety and immunogenicity of this RABV-G mRNA vaccine in healthy volunteers has been initiated (EudraCT no. 2013-002171-17, NCT02241135).
400.Rationale: Comparing incidence in vaccinated versus unvaccinated populations during peak months can provide real-world effectiveness data, but it is subject to confounding (differences in exposure, access to care, etc.) and does not directly measure biological duration of protection. The passage emphasizes virus-neutralizing titers as a standardized correlate of protection and highlights immunogenicity studies and longitudinal serology as the methods used to assess duration. Therefore, while incidence comparisons may be useful for effectiveness, they are not the most direct or practical method to determine whether immunologic protection persists through a prolonged seasonal risk period.

400.Option: Conduct a longitudinal study measuring virus-neutralizing antibody titers over time in vaccinated individuals to determine if protection persists beyond the expected duration of seasonal exposure risk
Evidence: We tested the longevity of the humoral immune response. BALB/c mice treated with two vaccinations on days 0 and 21 and doses of 1.25 μg, 5 μg, 20 μg, and 80 μg RABV-G mRNA vaccine or 0.1 human dose Rabipur were observed for about one year and antibodies in the serum of vaccinated mice were analyzed monthly.
We found that after vaccination with 20 μg and 80 μg RABV-G mRNA or with Rabipur antibody titers remained stable throughout the observation period with mean titers between 17 and 65 IU/ml (Fig 1E).
Two weeks after the second immunization, all RABV-G immunized mice developed relevant virus neutralizing (VN) titers of ≥0.5 IU/ml (...), considered as protective in humans, dogs and cats [34].
bona fide assessments of vaccine immunogenicity in naïve individuals on the basis of a widely recognized and standardized functional correlate of protection (virus neutralizing titers).
400.Rationale: The passage demonstrates that longitudinal measurement of virus-neutralizing (VN) titers (monthly sampling over ~1 year in mice) was used to assess durability and that VN titers are an accepted correlate of protection. For a public health intervention in a region with prolonged seasonal exposure, serially measuring VN titers in vaccinated persons (or sentinel animals) over the risk period directly answers whether antibody levels remain above the protective threshold for the entire season and is the most appropriate, biologically informative method.

401.Option: The detection of Neoehrlichia mikurensis in bat ticks that fed on domestic dogs and bats indicates that these ticks are primary vectors for zoonotic transmission of this pathogen to humans, warranting immediate public health intervention.
Evidence: We detected Neoehrlichia mikurensis in both I. simplex (n = 1, EW16, Romania) associated with M. schreibersii and I. vespertilionis (n = 3, EW54, Romania; EW75, EW159, Hungary), previously fed on C. lupus familiaris, M. schreibersii, and M. myotis (Fig 5).
These results suggest that bat-associated ticks may play a role in the transmission and maintenance of this pathogen, not only between bat hosts but among other domestic and wildlife species.

However, this probably has moderate epidemiological significance, similar to the role of the hedgehog tick (I. hexagonus), which is taxonomically closely related to bat-associated ticks (subgenus Pholeoixodes) and usually carries N. mikurensis with low prevalence [49].
Until the frequency of non-bat host feeding behavior of bat-associated ticks is quantified and their vectorial capacity is determined, their epidemiological significance cannot be fully assessed.
presence of pathogens in field-collected ticks does not provide evidence of vectorial capacity, as they may simply originate from the host’s blood meal.
401.Rationale: Although the study detected N. mikurensis DNA in bat-associated ticks that had fed on dogs and bats, the authors explicitly caution that detection of pathogen DNA in field-collected ticks does not prove vectorial capacity and that epidemiological significance is not yet quantifiable. They also characterize the likely significance as probably moderate and call for quantification of non-bat feeding frequency and determination of vector competence. Therefore it is not supported to conclude these ticks are primary vectors to humans or that immediate public-health interventions are warranted on that basis alone.

401.Option: Because bat-associated ticks have been found to feed on humans and domestic animals, and harbor zoonotic pathogens like Neoehrlichia mikurensis, they should be considered a primary vector for emerging infectious diseases in human populations, especially in regions with high bat and dog density.
Evidence: the latter two ixodid species, as well as soft ticks (Ixodida: Argasidae) of bats, such as Chiropterargas boueti, Carios kelleyi, C. vespertilionis, Secretargas transgariepinus, and Reticulinasus salahi occasionally feed on humans [24–26],
This is the first study providing molecular evidence that bat-associated ixodid ticks opportunistically feed on several non-bat hosts, such as dogs, horses, and wild boars.
We detected Neoehrlichia mikurensis in both I. simplex ... and I. vespertilionis ... previously fed on C. lupus familiaris, M. schreibersii, and M. myotis.
Until the frequency of non-bat host feeding behavior of bat-associated ticks is quantified and their vectorial capacity is determined, their epidemiological significance cannot be fully assessed.
The methods used here cannot accurately determine the frequency of non-bat feeding.
401.Rationale: The passage documents that bat-associated ticks sometimes feed on humans and domestic animals and can carry zoonotic pathogens. However, the authors emphasize limited sample sizes, unknown frequency of non-bat feeding, and that detection of pathogen DNA does not establish vectorial capacity. They explicitly state that epidemiological significance cannot be fully assessed without further data. Thus labeling bat ticks as a primary vector for emerging infectious diseases in humans is not supported by the study's findings.

401.Option: Bat-associated ticks may act as bridge vectors facilitating zoonotic pathogen transmission between bats, domestic animals, and humans in shared environments, increasing the risk of disease emergence
Evidence: bat-associated ticks have been found to harbor a wide variety of pathogens, some with high veterinary and public health importance, including species from the following genera: Anaplasma, Bartonella, Borrelia, Mycoplasma, Rickettsia and Babesia [9–15].

the latter two ixodid species, as well as soft ticks (Ixodida: Argasidae) of bats, such as Chiropterargas boueti, Carios kelleyi, C. vespertilionis, Secretargas transgariepinus, and Reticulinasus salahi occasionally feed on humans [24–26],
these data suggest that bat-associated tick species may opportunistically feed on non-chiropteran hosts.
bat-associated ticks may play a hitherto neglected link between bats and non-chiropteran hosts, implying their potential role in the zoonotic transmission of bat-derived vector-borne pathogens.
This is the first study providing molecular evidence that bat-associated ixodid ticks opportunistically feed on several non-bat hosts, such as dogs, horses, and wild boars.
These results suggest that bat-associated ticks may play a role in the transmission and maintenance of this pathogen, not only between bat hosts but among other domestic and wildlife species.
401.Rationale: The study provides molecular evidence that bat-associated ticks sometimes feed on non-bat hosts (dogs, horses, wild boars, and occasionally humans) and that they harbor zoonotic agents (e.g., Neoehrlichia mikurensis, Bartonella). The authors explicitly propose that bat ticks may form a previously underappreciated link between bats and non-chiropteran hosts and suggest they may contribute to transmission and maintenance of pathogens across species. Together, these findings support the inference that bat-associated ticks can act as bridge vectors in shared environments, increasing potential zoonotic risk and disease emergence concern.

401.Option: The presence of Bartonella in a tick that fed on a bat and a Rhinolophus species suggests that bat-associated ticks are efficient transmitters of this pathogen across bat species and to other mammals, increasing the risk of cross-species infection in shared habitats.
Evidence: Bacterial pathogen screening detected the presence of Bartonella sp. in a single I. vespertilionis (n = 1, EW83, Hungary) in the nymph stage that had fed on R. ferrumequinum (Figs 2 and 3).
Sequence data showed the highest similarity with a Bartonella sequence found in R. ferrumequinum in Georgia (KX420722, percentage identity: 100%, query coverage: 97%) [44] (Fig 4).

The presence of pathogen DNA may result from the blood meal source of the previous life cycle stage (e.g. in males), however the vectorial potential of ticks (implying transstadial survival of the relevant pathogen) cannot be excluded.
presence of pathogens in field-collected ticks does not provide evidence of vectorial capacity, as they may simply originate from the host’s blood meal.
401.Rationale: While Bartonella DNA was detected in a single bat-associated tick that contained Rhinolophus DNA, the authors caution that pathogen detection in ticks could reflect residual blood from a prior host rather than active infection of the tick or its ability to transmit the agent. They explicitly state that presence of pathogen DNA does not prove vectorial capacity and that transstadial survival and transmission remain unproven. Therefore it is not supported to conclude that bat-associated ticks are efficient transmitters of Bartonella across species based on this single detection.

401.Option: Since bat-associated ticks have been found to feed on wild boars and horses, they are likely to be major contributors to the spread of zoonotic pathogens in agricultural and rural ecosystems, especially where livestock and wildlife interact.
Evidence: I. vespertilionis indicated the broadest spectrum of host feeding, including ... C. lupus familiaris (n = 6), Equus caballus (n = 1), and Sus scrofa (n = 1) (Fig 1).
This is the first study providing molecular evidence that bat-associated ixodid ticks opportunistically feed on several non-bat hosts, such as dogs, horses, and wild boars.
However, two of the tick species studied had small sample sizes, and the methods used here cannot accurately determine the frequency of non-bat feeding.
Until the frequency of non-bat host feeding behavior of bat-associated ticks is quantified and their vectorial capacity is determined, their epidemiological significance cannot be fully assessed.

This finding adds to the already documented epidemiological connections between bats and horses, all of which might be relevant to the transmission of zoonotic pathogens (e.g., Hendra virus) between these hosts [53].
401.Rationale: The study documents isolated instances of ticks with horse and wild boar blood, indicating potential contact, but these were few (single samples) and the authors emphasize small sample sizes and inability to quantify frequency of non-bat feeding. They also state that vectorial capacity and epidemiological significance remain undetermined. Therefore it is not justified by the presented evidence to label bat-associated ticks as likely major contributors to pathogen spread in agricultural/rural ecosystems without further data.

402.Option: Hybridization is most likely to occur when both species are exposed to the same environmental conditions in the intermediate host
Evidence: These parasites have a two-host life cycle, which includes a mammalian definitive host, in which sexual reproduction occurs and a mollusc intermediate host in which asexual multiplication takes place.

For schistosome species that randomly pair with no mate preference and for many related parasitic species capable of hybridizing, final host specificity may be the sole barrier preventing interbreeding
This isolation mechanism “by the host” may be so efficient that species may lack any post-zygotic or other pre-zygotic mechanisms ultimately allowing them to hybridize when the opportunity arises.
402.Rationale: The passage emphasizes that sexual reproduction (and thus the opportunity for hybridization) occurs in the mammalian definitive host and that final host specificity is the primary barrier to interbreeding. While the intermediate (mollusc) host is part of the life cycle, the text points to meeting in the same definitive host as the critical condition for hybridization rather than shared environmental conditions in the intermediate host.

402.Option: Hybridization is most likely when both species are exposed to the same chemotherapeutic agent, such as Praziquantel
Evidence: In particular hetero-specific male S. haematobium under-expressed a fair amount of genes involved in response to drugs compared to homo-specific ones (e.g., Multidrug and toxin extrusion, Multidrug and toxin extrusion 2, Multidrug resistance or Multidrug resistance-associated).

However, it is important to pinpoint that any changes associated to PZQ response in hybrids is still theoretical and there is not current evidence that there is any difference in drug response in natural infections.
402.Rationale: Although the authors note differential expression of drug-response genes in some comparisons, they explicitly state that any association between Praziquantel response and hybrids is theoretical and unproven in natural infections. The passage does not present chemotherapeutic exposure as a driver of hybridization; rather, it highlights host specificity and co-occurrence in the definitive host as determinants.

402.Option: The frequency of hybridization is determined by the relative abundance of each species in the definitive host population
Evidence: We showed that S. haematobium and S. bovis mate in a random fashion and depend only on the presence and the relative abundance of each species in the definitive host.
Overall, the data shows that S. haematobium and S. bovis mate in a random fashion and depend only on the presence and the relative abundance of each species in the definitive host.
402.Rationale: The passage directly states that, given random (non-selective) mating, hybridization depends on the presence and relative abundance of the species within the definitive host. If both species are present and their relative numbers determine encounter probabilities, then hybrid frequency will be driven by those abundances.

402.Option: The occurrence of hybridization depends on whether both species infect the same definitive host
Evidence: For schistosome species that randomly pair with no mate preference and for many related parasitic species capable of hybridizing, final host specificity may be the sole barrier preventing interbreeding

lack of pre-zygotic barriers does imply that in areas where S. haematobium and S. bovis are sympatric and infect the same definitive hosts, hybrids and introgressed individuals should be more likely to be found.
This isolation mechanism “by the host” may be so efficient that species may lack any post-zygotic or other pre-zygotic mechanisms ultimately allowing them to hybridize when the opportunity arises.
402.Rationale: The passage repeatedly identifies definitive (final) host specificity as the main pre-zygotic barrier: if both species infect the same definitive host individuals (i.e., they co-occur within the same host), opportunities for mating and hybridization increase despite the absence of mate recognition.

402.Option: Hybridization is primarily driven by the presence of a shared intermediate host species that supports both parasite life cycles
Evidence: These parasites have a two-host life cycle, which includes a mammalian definitive host, in which sexual reproduction occurs and a mollusc intermediate host in which asexual multiplication takes place.

For schistosome species that randomly pair with no mate preference and for many related parasitic species capable of hybridizing, final host specificity may be the sole barrier preventing interbreeding

The most significant reproductive isolation mechanisms preserving the genetic integrity between these species would be habitat isolation, including geographical location and definitive host specificity.
402.Rationale: Although sharing an intermediate host is part of the parasites' life cycles, the passage stresses that sexual mating occurs in the definitive host and that definitive host specificity (and geographical sympatry) is the key reproductive isolation mechanism. Thus, presence of a shared intermediate host alone is not presented as the primary driver of hybridization.

403.Option: The current study's smaller sample size may have reduced statistical power to detect TT, leading to a false-negative result
Evidence: Following data cleaning, 1009 children were included in the study, which is very close to the targeted sample size of 1018.
No cases of TT were observed in 928 examined participants aged 15 and over. The age-adjusted prevalence of TT in those aged 15 years and above was 0% (95% CI 0–0.2%).
The number of children per household was higher in this survey than in the 2007 national census, and we therefore reached our sample size in a lower number of households than expected.
our individual participation rate was over 99%, therefore the risk of attrition bias is considered to be low.
403.Rationale: The passage indicates the current survey nearly achieved its target sample size and had very high participation, with 928 adults examined for TT and narrow confidence bounds (0% with upper 95% CI 0.2%). These facts argue that the study was not markedly underpowered to detect TT at the population level; therefore a simple lack of sample size/power is an unlikely primary explanation for the large discrepancy with the 2012 estimate.

403.Option: Misclassification of eyelash epilation as trachomatous trichiasis due to cultural practices likely contributed to overestimation of TT prevalence in the 2012 survey
Evidence: we have observed social practices of eyelash epilation in Fiji that may have been misdiagnosed as trachomatous trichiasis.

We did not find any cases of TT in this population. This is in contrast to the previous (2012) PBPS, which found a high prevalence of TF (19.6% in 1–9 year-olds) and TT (8.7% in ≥15 year-olds) in this Division [13,36].
The reported prevalence of TF and TT in Fiji varies significantly between studies.
403.Rationale: The authors explicitly identify local eyelash epilation practices that can be mistaken for trachomatous trichiasis as a plausible source of misclassification in prior surveys. This specific, observed cultural practice provides a direct and credible explanation for an overestimate of TT in the 2012 PBPS and is therefore the most likely explanation mentioned in the passage.

403.Option: Differences in clinical grading criteria between the two surveys may have led to underestimation of TF and TT in the current study
Evidence: A single GTMP-certified [7] trachoma grader examined both eyes of each participant using a 2.5× binocular loupe and sunlight. Each eye was assessed for the presence or absence of TT, TF and TI, according to the WHO simplified grading system [4,7].

The difference in TF prevalence between the present data and the 2012 PBPS could be due to poor consensus between graders, seasonal variation in trachoma prevalence or an artefact of the cross-sectional study designs.
403.Rationale: The current study used a GTMP-certified grader and the WHO simplified grading system, implying standardised clinical criteria. The passage does mention 'poor consensus between graders' as a possible reason for differences, which points to inter-grader variability rather than a change in formal grading criteria. There is no explicit evidence that the two surveys used different clinical grading criteria; thus the option asserting differences in criteria as the main cause is not supported by the text.

403.Option: The use of PCR testing in the current study may have led to underestimation of Ct infection, contributing to the lower prevalence of active trachoma
Evidence: For logistical reasons, swabs that were collected during this survey were not frozen during storage.
Evidence from our positive control swabs indicates that a substantial proportion of chlamydial and human DNA is lost during storage over a few months at 5°C.

Some commentators have suggested the sensitivity of this ddPCR assay may be too low for trachoma programs [29] ... the discrepant results occurred in a mathematically predictable manner related to the analyte concentration ... and we therefore believe the test was appropriate in this setting.
loss of sensitivity is however a limitation when considering the findings of this cross-sectional prevalence study.
We found no association between infection and active trachoma.
403.Rationale: The passage documents potential loss of PCR sensitivity due to storage conditions and low-load infections, so PCR could underestimate ocular Ct infection prevalence. However, the main discrepancy cited between surveys is in clinical outcomes (TF and especially TT), and PCR-based under-detection of infection does not directly explain why clinical signs—particularly TT in adults—would be observed in 2012 but not now. The authors themselves emphasise misclassification (epilation) and other survey design/grader issues as explanations. Therefore PCR underestimation of infection is an unlikely primary explanation for the TF/TT discrepancy.

403.Option: Seasonal variation in trachoma transmission may have led to a lower prevalence in the current study compared to the 2012 survey
Evidence: The difference in TF prevalence between the present data and the 2012 PBPS could be due to poor consensus between graders, seasonal variation in trachoma prevalence or an artefact of the cross-sectional study designs.

The 2012 survey followed a PBPS protocol with random selection at village and household level with a comparable number of children sampled overall. The survey presented here sampled lower numbers of villages and households, but this is unlikely to sufficiently explain the large difference between the resultant TF prevalences found.
403.Rationale: The authors list seasonal variation as one possible contributor to differences between surveys, so it is plausible. However, they present it as one of several generic explanations and give stronger, specific emphasis to misclassification from eyelash epilation and grader/sample differences. Given the explicit statement about epilation and the lack of evidence that seasonality alone would account for the large discrepancy in TT prevalence, seasonal variation is less likely to be the primary explanation.

404.Option: The presence of O. moubata in both wild and domestic animal reservoirs ensures continuous spillover of pathogens into human populations
Evidence: Ornithodoros moubata is an argasid tick widely distributed throughout many countries of central, eastern and southern mainland Africa and Madagascar [4], where it constitutes an important medical and veterinary problem because it transmits lethal diseases such as human relapsing fever (HRF) and African swine fever (ASF) [5,6].

O. moubata is found in nature, associated to warthogs and other wild hosts inhabiting burrows, as well as in anthropic environments, living inside human dwellings and domestic animal premises, which greatly contributes to the transmission and persistence of HRF and ASF in the affected areas.
This presence of this argasid in the anthropic environment makes it difficult to eradicate these diseases from endemic areas.
404.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that O. moubata occurs both in natural (warthogs and other wild hosts) and in anthropic/domestic settings (human dwellings and domestic animal premises) and that this dual association "greatly contributes to the transmission and persistence of HRF and ASF". That pattern of maintenance in wild and domestic reservoirs supports continuous spillover into humans and is therefore a critical factor enabling sustained transmission.

404.Option: The high genetic diversity of salivary proteins in O. moubata allows for immune evasion and sustained transmission across diverse host populations
Evidence: All these tick salivary protein sequences have been compiled and classified into a recently constructed database (TickSialoFam) [20].
